### [1] [Learning 3D Affordances for Blade Insertion in Cluttered Stowing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02549)
*Tianyu Li,Harpreet Sawhney,Minju Jung,Aditya Mehrotra,Kunal Mehrotra,Mudit Agrawal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VulcanVoxel uses a masked autoencoder over 3D occupancy fields to predict free-space affordances for blade stowing in cluttered bins, outperforming pose-based baselines by keeping inference spatial rather than parametric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic stowing requires reasoning about free-space affordances where a rigid tool can navigate, but standard approaches infer affordances as SE(3) pose distributions, which lack structure for reasoning about unobserved placements and cannot recover geometric alternatives from unimodal data.

Method: VulcanVoxel uses a masked autoencoder over 3D occupancy fields to reconstruct blade occupancy conditioned on scene geometry, computing feasibility locally at each voxel and recovering multi-modal predictions from unimodal data.

Result: Trained on 10,000 real warehouse stow episodes without human annotation, VulcanVoxel achieves top-5 coverage of 0.89 versus 0.71 for the best pose-based baseline. A distilled student provides RGB-to-voxel inference in 30 ms vs 1.4 s for voxel-to-voxel.

Conclusion: Blade affordances are better treated as spatial objects (subsets of 3D space defined by geometric feasibility) rather than pose parameters, and the method enables efficient, multi-modal free-space reasoning for robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Many manipulation tasks require reasoning about free-space affordances: discovering volumes where an extended rigid tool can safely navigate, complementary to surface contact affordances for grasping. Robotic stowing is a canonical instance, where a blade must sweep items aside inside cluttered fabric bins to create insertion space. Production stow systems generate millions of such episodes, but standard approaches with unimodal data infer affordances as SE(3) pose distributions, a geometric question asked in the wrong domain. VulcanVoxel keeps inference spatial: a masked autoencoder over 3D occupancy fields reconstructs blade occupancy conditioned on scene geometry, computing feasibility locally at each voxel and recovering multi-modal predictions from unimodal data. Blade affordances are spatial objects, subsets of 3D space defined by geometric feasibility. Pose parameters carry no structure for reasoning whether unobserved placements are feasible, and standard generative objectives including flow matching faithfully learn the unimodal distribution produced by execution policies and cannot recover geometric alternatives. Trained on 10,000 real warehouse stow episodes without human annotation, VulcanVoxel achieves top-5 coverage of 0.89 versus 0.71 for the best pose-based baseline, with a distilled student providing RGB-to-voxel inference in 30 ms. vs. 1.4 s. for voxel-to-voxel. We have released a dataset of real blade insertion cycles with RGB-D observations and pose trajectories at https://www.armbench.com/blade_insertion. html.

</details>


### [2] [DELTAVID: Enhancing Fine-Grained Spatiotemporal Perception with Cross-Video Differences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02551)
*Yankai Yang,Yancheng Long,Bin Wen,Fan Yang,Tingting Gao,Han Li,Shuo Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DELTAVID is a verifiable proxy-task framework that enhances fine-grained spatiotemporal perception in Video MLLMs by turning cross-video spot-the-difference into a trainable perception signal, supported by DELTAVID-10K and DELTAVID-Bench datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Video MLLMs excel at open-ended understanding but lack precise local spatiotemporal perception, failing to detect changes when two videos share nearly identical global semantics but differ only in short time spans or small regions.

Method: DELTAVID transforms cross-video spot-the-difference into a trainable perception signal where the model identifies local changes, judges temporal boundaries, and organizes spatial evidence. It is trained on DELTAVID-10K and evaluated on DELTAVID-Bench with controllable local differences in real videos.

Result: DELTAVID substantially improves cross-video difference understanding and transfers learned local evidence ability to eight general video understanding benchmarks: MMVU, MLVU, Video-MME, VideoHolmes, VideoMMMU, LVBench, TempCompass, and LongVideoBench.

Conclusion: Cross-video differences serve both as a diagnostic tool for fine-grained perception failures and as scalable proxy supervision to move Video MLLMs from coarse semantic understanding toward fine-grained spatiotemporal evidence reasoning.

Abstract: Video multimodal large language models have made strong progress on open-ended video understanding, but they still lack precise local spatiotemporal perception. When two videos share almost the same global semantics and differ only in a short time span or a small region, current models often fail to find the change and provide reliable evidence. We propose DELTAVID, a verifiable proxy-task framework that enhances fine-grained spatiotemporal perception with cross-video differences. The key idea is to turn cross-video spot-the-difference into a trainable perception signal, where a model identifies local changes, judges temporal boundaries, and organizes spatial evidence by comparing similar videos. To make this signal scalable to train and reliable to evaluate, we further introduce DELTAVID-10K and DELTAVID-Bench, which convert controllable local differences in real videos into evidence-labeled training and test samples. Experiments show that DELTAVID substantially improves performance on cross-video difference understanding and transfers the learned local evidence ability to general video understanding benchmarks, including MMVU, MLVU, Video-MME, VideoHolmes, VideoMMMU, LVBench, TempCompass, and LongVideoBench. These results show that cross-video differences are not only an effective way to diagnose fine-grained perception failures, but also a scalable proxy supervision that moves Video MLLMs from coarse semantic understanding toward fine-grained spatiotemporal evidence reasoning.

</details>


### [3] [Interpretable machine learning predicts Parkinson's disease severity using motion-corrected QSM MRI and multiband multiecho fMRI features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02553)
*Aixa X. Andrade*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study uses interpretable machine learning to predict Parkinson's disease motor severity from QSM and resting-state fMRI features. The best model (selected QSM + clinical variables) explained 45.4% of variance in motor severity and had 75% of participants predicted within ±5 MDS-UPDRS Part III points, with SHAP highlighting cerebellar, thalamic, striatal, insular, and motor cortical regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop objective neuroimaging biomarkers that capture brain variation not observable from clinical examination, improving Parkinson's disease motor assessment beyond traditional clinical methods.

Method: 28 participants (24 PD, 4 controls); extracted regional QSM and ReHo features; 13 feature-set experiments; trained SVR, Elastic Net, Random Forest, and XGBoost with nested cross-validation; evaluated by pooled held-out R², RMSE, MAE, Pearson correlation, permutation testing, and proportion within ±5 UPDRS points.

Result: Imaging-only models outperformed clinical-only models. The best models (full fMRI + full QSM + clinical) explained 45.4% variance. Selected QSM + clinical variables yielded 75% of predictions within ±5 points and lowest MAE. SHAP revealed cerebellar, thalamic, striatal, insular, and motor cortical importance.

Conclusion: QSM and multiband multi-echo fMRI-derived ReHo capture distinct, interpretable dimensions of PD motor severity. Structural and functional imaging contribute differently depending on the clinical prediction goal.

Abstract: Introduction: Objective neuroimaging biomarkers may improve Parkinson's disease motor assessment by capturing brain variation not directly observable from clinical examination. We used interpretable machine learning to predict current motor severity, measured by MDS-UPDRS Part III, from QSM and multiband multi-echo resting-state fMRI-derived ReHo features.
  Methods: Regional QSM and ReHo features were extracted from 28 participants, including 24 individuals with Parkinson's disease and 4 controls. Thirteen feature-set experiments evaluated imaging-only, clinical-only, imaging-plus-clinical, full, reduced, and multimodal inputs. Support vector regression, Elastic Net, Random Forest, and XGBoost models were trained using nested cross-validation. Performance was assessed using pooled held-out R^2, RMSE, MAE, Pearson correlation, permutation testing, and the proportion of participants predicted within +/-5 MDS-UPDRS Part III points.
  Results: Imaging-only models carried meaningful predictive signal, whereas the clinical-only model performed weakly. Full fMRI, full QSM, and clinical variables provided the strongest global fit, explaining 45.4% of variance in motor severity. Selected QSM plus clinical variables produced the most clinically close predictions, with 75.0% of participants predicted within +/-5 points and the lowest MAE among top-performing models. SHAP highlighted cerebellar, thalamic, striatal, insular, and motor cortical features.
  Conclusion: QSM and multiband multi-echo fMRI-derived ReHo capture distinct, interpretable dimensions of Parkinson's disease motor severity. These findings show that structural and functional imaging contribute differently depending on the clinical prediction goal.

</details>


### [4] [Reliability-Aware Monocular Depth Supervision for Sparse-View Neural Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02554)
*Wei-Teng Chu,Yashasvini Gopalan,Changju Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates selective monocular depth supervision (using Depth Anything V2 with photometric masks) for sparse-view neural reconstruction in outdoor driving scenes, finding it benefits Splatfacto (PSNR +1.029, RMSE 0.542→0.100) but not Mip-NeRF-360, with effectiveness tied to reliable region selection rather than mask quantity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse-view neural reconstruction in outdoor driving scenes is challenging because cameras move along narrow forward-facing trajectories with limited multi-view overlap. Monocular depth estimators provide dense geometric priors, but their predictions are noisy and not uniformly reliable across image regions.

Method: Use Depth Anything V2 as dense monocular depth prior, align predictions to metric depth via scale-shift fitting, apply depth supervision selectively through photometric masks from an RGB-only baseline model. Evaluate on Mip-NeRF-360 and Splatfacto representations.

Result: Masked monocular depth supervision gives marginal rendering gains for Mip-NeRF-360 and no metric geometry improvement. Splatfacto improves PSNR 14.903→15.932 and RMSE 0.542→0.100. Gains stem from selecting reliable low-error regions. On Bicycle scene with strong multi-view coverage, depth supervision improves geometry but hurts RGB rendering.

Conclusion: Monocular depth priors are useful for under-constrained sparse-view reconstruction but should be applied selectively and with moderate weighting.

Abstract: Sparse-view neural reconstruction is challenging in outdoor driving scenes, where cameras usually move along a narrow forward-facing trajectory and provide limited multi-view overlap. Although monocular depth estimators can provide dense geometric priors, their predictions are noisy, and not uniformly reliable across image regions. In this work, we study monocular depth supervision for sparse-view neural reconstruction. We use Depth Anything V2 as a dense monocular depth prior, align its predictions to metric depth using scale-shift fitting, and apply depth supervision selectively through photometric masks generated from an RGB-only baseline model. We evaluate this strategy on two representative scene representations: Mip-NeRF-360 and Splatfacto. On KITTISeq02 under an every2 sparse-view setting, masked monocular depth supervision gives only marginal rendering gains for Mip-NeRF-360 and does not improve metric geometry. In contrast, Splatfacto benefits more clearly, improving PSNR from 14.903 to 15.932 and reducing RMSE from 0.542 to 0.100. Additional KITTISeq05 experiments and matched-ratio mask ablations further show that the gains for Splatfacto come from selecting reliable low-error regions rather than simply reducing the number of depth-supervised pixels. Additional experiments on the Bicycle scene show that depth supervision can improve geometry while hurting RGB rendering quality when multi-view coverage is already strong. Overall, our results suggest that monocular depth priors are useful for under-constrained sparse-view reconstruction, but should be applied selectively and with moderate weighting.

</details>


### [5] [Do Diabetic Foot Ulcer Segmentation Models Generalize? A Cross-Dataset Benchmark of CNN and Transformer Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02555)
*Abderrahmane Benfatah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Deep learning models for diabetic foot ulcer segmentation achieve high in-domain accuracy but degrade substantially on external datasets. SegFormer-B2 (Transformer) generalizes best across hospitals, outperforming U-Net and DeepLabV3+, with architecture family (not complexity) driving cross-dataset performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most DFU segmentation models are trained and tested on the same dataset, leaving cross-hospital generalization unmeasured. The authors aim to quantify how well these models perform on unseen data from different clinical sources.

Method: Benchmark U-Net, DeepLabV3+ (convolutional), and SegFormer-B2 (Transformer) under identical leakage-screened protocol. Train on combined FUSeg/AZH data, evaluate without fine-tuning on DFUC2022 and Medetec. Use per-image failure analysis (2,160 images) and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests.

Result: In-domain Dice 0.80–0.83 for all models. External: SegFormer-B2 (0.557/0.786) > U-Net > DeepLabV3+. Failure rates: SegFormer-B2 (31.1%) < U-Net (38.5%) < DeepLabV3+ (43.0%). Wilcoxon tests confirm significance (p<0.001).

Conclusion: Architecture family, not model complexity, drives cross-hospital generalization in DFU segmentation. Transformer-based models generalize better than convolutional ones on external data.

Abstract: Deep learning models for diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) segmentation routinely report high accuracy, but they are almost always trained and tested on the same dataset, leaving their behaviour on data from a different clinical source largely unmeasured. We benchmark three representative segmentation architectures -- U-Net and DeepLabV3+ (convolutional) and SegFormer-B2 (Transformer) -- under an identical, leakage-screened protocol: training on the combined FUSeg/AZH wound data and evaluating, without fine-tuning, on two independent external datasets (DFUC2022 and Medetec). All models achieve strong in-domain performance (Dice 0.80--0.83) but degrade substantially across datasets. The degradation is, however, architecture-dependent: SegFormer-B2 generalizes best on both external sets (DFUC2022 Dice 0.557, Medetec Dice 0.786), outperforming both convolutional models, while the more complex DeepLabV3+ generalizes worse than the simpler U-Net. Per-image failure analysis on 2,160 images across both external test sets confirms that SegFormer-B2 produces the fewest catastrophic failures on DFUC2022 (31.1%), compared with U-Net (38.5%) and DeepLabV3+ (43.0%). The consistent ranking across two independent external sources, confirmed by Wilcoxon signed-rank tests (p < 0.001 on both datasets), indicates that architecture family, not model complexity, drives cross-hospital generalization.

</details>


### [6] [How many labels do you need? A decision framework for cross-habitat marine species recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02559)
*Alzayat Saleh,Mostafa Rahimi Azghadi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A decision framework quantifies the trade-off between labelling effort and recognition accuracy when transferring vision systems across marine habitats, finding that frozen DINOv2 with a linear classifier and only 10-20 labelled images per species achieves reliable recognition at new sites, cutting annotation effort by ~10x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ecologists lack evidence-based guidance on how much labelling effort is needed for reliable deployment of automated image recognition at new sites, especially across diverse marine habitats.

Method: Benchmark across 5 datasets, 3 oceans, 3 taxonomic groups (fish, corals, invertebrates). Evaluated 4 models (DINOv2, CLIP, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B4) under 4 adaptation strategies (linear probing, LoRA, VPT, full fine-tuning) across 3 protocols: within-habitat transfer (240 runs), cross-dataset geographic transfer (40 runs), few-shot adaptation with 0-100 labelled samples (648 runs).

Result: Frozen DINOv2 + linear classifier (1,538 params) generalised as well as fully fine-tuned baselines 10,000x larger. Only 10-20 labelled images per species sufficed for reliable deployment at new sites (~1-4 hours annotation per site).

Conclusion: Programmes expanding to new sites should pair a frozen foundation model (DINOv2) with a simple linear classifier and annotate only 10-20 images per species, enabling efficient budgeting of labelling effort against expected accuracy.

Abstract: Automated image recognition is increasingly used to scale ecological monitoring beyond manual annotation, yet ecologists lack evidence-based guidance on how much labelling effort reliable deployment at new sites requires. We present a decision framework quantifying the trade-off between labelling effort and recognition accuracy when transferring vision systems across marine habitats. The benchmark spans five datasets, three oceans, and three taxonomic groups (fish, corals, invertebrates), from tropical reefs in the Great Barrier Reef and French Polynesia to a temperate Danish fjord. We evaluated four recognition models (DINOv2, CLIP, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B4) under four adaptation strategies (linear probing, LoRA, Visual Prompt Tuning, full fine-tuning) across three protocols: within-habitat transfer across 20 reef sites (240 runs), cross-dataset geographic transfer along a difficulty gradient (40 runs), and few-shot adaptation curves with 0-100 labelled samples per class (648 runs). Frozen self-supervised foundation features (DINOv2 + linear classifier, 1,538 trainable parameters) generalised to unseen reef sites at least as well as fully fine-tuned convolutional baselines four orders of magnitude larger; they learned species-diagnostic, habitat-invariant representations, whereas baselines encoded habitat-specific shortcuts that fail at new sites. As few as 10-20 labelled images per species sufficed to deploy reliable recognition at a new site, cutting annotation effort by roughly an order of magnitude.
  Solution. Programmes expanding to new sites can deploy reliable recognition by pairing a frozen, open foundation model (DINOv2) with a simple linear classifier and annotating only 10-20 images per species - roughly 1-4 hours per site. The framework lets programmes budget labelling effort against expected accuracy across sites, ecosystems, and platforms.

</details>


### [7] [Inpainting U-Net for seamless pedestrian-level wind prediction across urban morphologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02560)
*Jingzi Huang,Claire E. Heaney,Tao Li,Xinzhe Li,Graham O. Hughes,Maarten van Reeuwijk*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-stage U-Net framework for efficient pedestrian-level wind speed prediction. Stage 1 (M1) predicts patch-by-patch, and Stage 2 (M2) refines using inpainting with a Gauss-Seidel scheme to reduce boundary artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-fidelity simulations like LES are computationally expensive for rapid pedestrian-level wind assessment, motivating a need for efficient surrogate models applicable to realistic urban morphologies.

Method: A two-stage U-Net framework: M1 predicts wind fields patch-by-patch from building height and fetch data; M2 refines predictions via inpainting with larger contextual windows and iterative Gauss-Seidel convergence.

Result: M1 captures overall wind patterns well in low/moderate regions but underestimates peaks. M2 reduces patch-boundary artifacts and improves spatial coherence. Unseen urban cases show reasonable mean and spatial variability but underestimated maxima.

Conclusion: The framework provides an efficient, flexible surrogate for high-resolution pedestrian-level wind prediction across realistic urban morphologies, suitable for rapid wind-comfort assessment.

Abstract: Pedestrian-level wind prediction is essential for urban design and wind-comfort assessment, but high-fidelity simulations such as LES remain computationally expensive for rapid evaluation. This study develops a two-stage U-Net framework for efficient prediction of time-averaged pedestrian-level wind speed over realistic urban morphologies. The model is trained and evaluated using the UrbanTALES dataset, which contains realistic city configurations under different approaching wind directions. In the first stage, a baseline U-Net model (M1) predicts wind fields patch-by-patch from normalised building height and fetch information. This formulation allows application to urban domains of arbitrary size, but independent patch inference can introduce discontinuities at patch boundaries. To address this, a second U-Net model (M2) is introduced as an inpainting-based refinement model. M2 uses a larger contextual window containing the initial M1 prediction and local morphology to reduce discontinuities using neighbouring flow information. During full-field inference, M2 is applied iteratively using a Gauss-Seidel scheme until convergence. Results show that M1 captures the main spatial distribution of pedestrian-level wind speed and performs well in low- and moderate-velocity regions, although high-velocity peaks are less accurate. M2 substantially reduces patch-boundary artefacts and improves spatial coherence. Across unseen urban cases, the framework reproduces mean velocity and spatial variability reasonably well, while maximum velocities remain underestimated. Overall, the proposed framework provides an efficient and flexible surrogate model for high-resolution pedestrian-level wind prediction across realistic urban morphologies.

</details>


### [8] [Double-Helix Active Geometry: LiDAR-Anchored Multi-View Depth with Selective Abstention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02561)
*Jinwen Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DH-Active is a lightweight, training-free geometry back-end that uses LiDAR as a metric ruler by anchoring poses with near-field depth returns and triangulating visually trackable samples, achieving fast (1.11ms CPU), sparse depth recovery with explicit abstention instead of forced estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Consumer depth sensors (e.g., iPhone LiDAR) have short range and sparse returns, limiting their usefulness as a sole depth source. The authors aim to treat the sensor as a metric ruler rather than the primary depth provider.

Method: Uses near-field depth returns to anchor metric relative poses via PnP; visually trackable samples without depth are triangulated under that pose. A parallax/reprojection gate abstains where geometry is ill-conditioned, leaving explicit holes instead of forcing estimates.

Result: 1.11 ms median CPU latency (14 threads), ~38x faster than DINOv2-L on GPU. Median relative error 1.4-6.7%. In ARKitScenes: 64.2% coverage at 13.4% median relative error. Alternatives (ICP, defocus fusion, learned models) failed or were less efficient.

Conclusion: The contribution is narrower: metric sparse depth, explicit abstention, zero learned parameters, near-millisecond CPU cost. A learned 1.26B model is more accurate after scale alignment, but the proposed method offers a practical trade-off.

Abstract: Consumer depth sensors such as the LiDAR scanner on recent iPhones provide metric range, but their useful range is short and their returns are sparse. We present DH-Active, a lightweight, training-free geometry back-end that treats the sensor as a metric ruler rather than the sole source of depth. Near-field returns anchor the metric relative pose of two views through PnP; visually trackable samples without a valid depth return are then triangulated under that pose. A parallax/reprojection gate abstains wherever the geometry is ill-conditioned, leaving an explicit hole and a selective score instead of forcing an estimate. The measured core front end, including spiral sampling, sparse back-projection, and hole taxonomy but excluding preprocessing and multi-view recovery, runs at 1.11 ms median latency on CPU (OpenCV using 14 threads), about 38 times faster than a DINOv2-L visual branch on GPU in our timing setup. Across two iPhone captures and the public TUM RGB-D and ARKitScenes benchmarks, held-out depth is recovered at 1.4 to 6.7 percent median relative error. In a controlled ARKitScenes protocol that uses only returns within 2 m to set scale and an independent laser scan as ground truth, DH-Active achieves 64.2 percent scene-median coverage of evaluable far-field candidates at 13.4 percent scene-median relative error; direct triangulation from the device trajectory is not usable. We also report the alternatives that failed in our tests: single-frame defocus, classical focus-stack depth, defocus-LiDAR fusion, point-to-point ICP over a good visual-inertial track, and attention-to-holes resampling. A 1.26 B learned model remains more accurate after oracle scale alignment. The contribution here is narrower: metric sparse depth, explicit abstention, zero learned parameters, and near-millisecond CPU cost.

</details>


### [9] [Entropy-Coded MS-VQ-VAE with Learned Priors for Ultra-Low Bitrate Video Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02562)
*Manikanta Kotthapalli,Banafsheh Rekabdar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper shows that discrete latent representations (vector-quantized codecs) overcome the low-bitrate limitations of continuous latent video codecs. By exploiting power-law code usage and autoregressive priors, the MS-VQ-VAE achieves 0.043-0.064 bpp on UCF101, outperforming H.264 and H.265 in perceptual quality (LPIPS) while using 5-7.6× fewer bits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous latent video codecs fail below 0.1 bpp because Lagrangian optimization cannot effectively balance quality and bitrate without a differentiable rate signal. The authors aim to overcome this fundamental limitation.

Method: Uses MS-VQ-VAE architecture with vector quantization, sweeping codebook sizes K∈{128,256,512,1024}. Applies EMA-stabilized codebook updates with dead-code restart to prevent training collapse. Exploits power-law distributed code usage via learned autoregressive prior to push bitrates below the log₂K ceiling.

Result: At 0.043-0.064 bpp on UCF101, MS-VQ-VAE outperforms H.265 CRF 36 on LPIPS by up to 0.072 absolute, using 5-7.6× fewer bits. Codebook analysis shows 70-85% entropy efficiency and power-law index distributions.

Conclusion: Discrete latent representations with vector quantization and autoregressive priors provide a principled learned entropy coding pipeline that operates far below conventional codec bitrate floors while maintaining perceptual quality.

Abstract: Learned video codecs based on continuous latent representations struggle to operate reliably below 0.1 bits per pixel~(bpp): without a differentiable rate signal, Lagrangian optimisation cannot effectively trade reconstruction quality for bitrate at extreme compression ratios. We demonstrate that discrete latent representations sidestep this limitation entirely. In a vector-quantized~(VQ) codec, the codebook size~$K$ imposes a hard information ceiling of $\log_2 K$ bits per symbol; a learned autoregressive prior then exploits the non-uniform distribution of code usage -- which we show follows a power law -- to push actual bitrates well below this ceiling, without any rate-penalty tuning.
  Building on the MS-VQ-VAE architecture introduced in~\cite{kotthapalli2026msvqvae}, we sweep $K \in \{128, 256, 512, 1024\}$ under a uniform training protocol to trace four operating points on the rate-distortion~(RD) curve. We identify and resolve a critical training instability: gradient-based VQ collapses catastrophically at $K \leq 512$, whereas EMA-stabilised codebook updates with dead-code restart maintain full utilisation across all configurations. On 500 UCF101 test clips ($64\!\times\!64$, 32~frames), our models operate at 0.043-0.064~bpp -- 3.3-5$\times$ below H.264's practical floor and $5$-$7.6\times$ below H.265's floor at this resolution. Every MS-VQ-VAE configuration outperforms H.265 CRF\,36 on perceptual quality (LPIPS) despite using $5$-$7.6\times$ fewer bits. At $K{=}1024$, the model surpasses H.265 CRF\,36 on LPIPS by a margin of 0.072 absolute while using $5.1\times$ fewer bits. Codebook analysis confirms power-law index distributions and 70-85\% entropy efficiency, establishing the pipeline as a principled learned entropy coder.

</details>


### [10] [Attention Dynamics in Diffusion Models: A Visual Analytics Framework for Human-AI Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02563)
*Yiran Xiao,George Legrady*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a visual analytics framework for exploring cross-attention map dynamics in diffusion-based text-to-image models, enabling structured analysis of token-level attention evolution across generation steps through linked temporal and spatial views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing workflows rely on aggregated attention or scalar summaries that fail to capture the temporal evolution and spatial relationships of semantic structure in diffusion models, making them difficult to interpret.

Method: A visual analytics framework that integrates quantitative measures with data-driven stage identification, enabling step-indexed tracking of token-level cross-attention maps, temporal concentration, and spatial relationships in an interactive workflow.

Result: Case studies on a structured 60-prompt Stable-Diffusion-class benchmark show recurring, interpretable patterns, and demonstrate that linked temporal and spatial views facilitate observation and discussion of generative processes.

Conclusion: The framework supports more effective human-AI collaboration by making attention dynamics interpretable and enabling structured analysis of generative processes.

Abstract: Diffusion-based text-to-image models can synthesize complex and highly structured visual content, yet the emergence and evolution of semantic structure remain difficult to interpret. Many existing workflows rely on aggregated attention or scalar summaries that separate temporal change from image-space evidence. To address this gap, we present a visual analytics framework for exploring attention dynamics in diffusion models: the step-indexed evolution of token-level cross-attention maps, their temporal concentration, and their spatial relationships. Our approach enables structured analysis of attention behavior across generation steps by integrating quantitative measures with data-driven stage identification in an interactive workflow. Case studies on a structured 60-prompt Stable-Diffusion-class benchmark illustrate recurring, interpretable patterns within this setting and show how linked temporal and spatial views facilitate the observation and discussion of generative processes, supporting more effective human-AI collaboration.

</details>


### [11] [From Raw Segmentations to Simulation-Ready Cardiac Meshes: An Automated Framework for Anatomical Reconstruction and Virtual Cohort Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02564)
*Francesco Fabbri,Martino Andrea Scarpolini,Paolo Ciancarella,Francesco Tudisco,Roberto Verzicco,Alessandro Ricci,Francesco Viola*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A semi-automatic pipeline that converts CT-based segmentations into topology-preserving, simulation-ready cardiac meshes within minutes, validated on 58 healthy hearts, enabling statistical shape modeling and virtual cohort generation for in silico studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cardiac models are limited to single or patient-specific anatomies, lacking population-level variability for uncertainty quantification. Translating medical-image segmentations with artifacts into topologically coherent geometries for multiphysics simulations remains a key challenge.

Method: A semi-automatic pipeline combining deep learning segmentation, template-based registration to regularize artifacts, and Chamfer-distance morphing to deform a high-quality template toward each segmented heart while preserving topology.

Result: Validated on 58 healthy cardiac CT scans; produces watertight, isotopological meshes with consistent point-to-point correspondence. PCA shows low-dimensional latent space captures population variability; GMM enables synthetic anatomy generation.

Conclusion: The open-source framework provides a pathway from raw segmentations to simulation-ready cardiac geometries, enabling anatomically consistent virtual cohorts for large-scale in silico studies.

Abstract: Computational models of the human heart are widely used to study electromechanical and fluid-dynamical cardiac function and to support applications such as in silico clinical trials. However, most studies remain limited to single or patient-specific anatomies, restricting the inclusion of population-level variability required for uncertainty quantification. A key challenge is translating medical-image segmentations, which may contain artifacts, mesh defects or disjoint domains, into topologically coherent geometries suitable for multiphysics simulations.
  In this work, we present a semi-automatic pipeline that converts CT-based segmentations into simulation-ready cardiac meshes within a few minutes while preserving anatomical and topological consistency. Building on modern deep learning segmentation methods, the framework incorporates a template-based registration stage to regularize artifacts and enforce mesh-quality constraints. A Chamfer-distance morphing strategy deforms a high-quality template toward each segmented heart, matching individual chambers while preserving topology.
  The resulting meshes are watertight, isotopological, and endowed with consistent point-to-point correspondence. The pipeline is validated on 58 healthy cardiac CT scans, including all cardiac chambers and proximal vessel segments. The resulting meshes can be represented in a unified shape space, enabling the construction of a statistical shape model of the heart and major vessels. Principal Component Analysis shows that a low-dimensional latent space efficiently captures population variability, while Gaussian Mixture Modeling enables synthetic anatomy generation. Overall, the proposed framework (released open-source) provides a pathway from raw segmentations to simulation-ready cardiac geometries, enabling anatomically consistent virtual cohorts for large-scale in silico studies.

</details>


### [12] [Coordinate Singularities Break Conformal Coverage for Gaze and Head Pose](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02565)
*Mohammadreza Jamalifard,Yaxiong Lei,Parastoo Azizinezhad,Javier Andreu-Perez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Conformal prediction using flat coordinate scoring (e.g., yaw-pitch, Euler angles) suffers systematic coverage collapse near coordinate singularities (large pitch, gimbal lock), with coverage dropping 30-50 percentage points. This structural issue is diagnosed via Riemannian volume density and fixed by coordinate-free geodesic scoring without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conformal prediction guarantees depend on how prediction errors are measured. Many vision tasks (gaze, head pose) produce outputs on curved spaces (e.g., sphere, SO(3)), but common scoring uses flat coordinate charts, introducing geometric distortion near singularities that breaks coverage guarantees in those regions.

Method: The paper empirically evaluates slice-conditional coverage on four datasets using flat-coordinate scoring vs. geodesic scoring. It theoretically proves that scalar thresholding on chart-based scores preserves distorted axis ratios. It proposes using Riemannian volume density as a diagnostic and geodesic scoring as a fix.

Result: When targeting 90% marginal coverage, slice-conditional coverage drops to 38.9% (ETH-XGaze) and 42.0% (Gaze360) for gaze pitch >70°, and to 57.5% (BIWI) and 55.2% (AFLW2000-3D) for head pose pitch >60°. Riemannian volume density strongly correlates with coverage collapse. Geodesic scoring restores valid coverage.

Conclusion: Flat-coordinate scoring in conformal prediction for curved output spaces causes systematic, geometry-induced coverage failure near singularities. This is structural, diagnosable via Riemannian volume density, and fixable by geodesic scoring at negligible cost.

Abstract: Conformal prediction provides distribution-free reliability guarantees for vision systems, but these guarantees depend on how prediction errors are measured in the output space. Many vision tasks produce outputs on curved spaces (e.g. gaze directions on the sphere or 3D head rotations), yet intermediate prediction heads, residuals, uncertainty estimates, or conformal scores are often defined in flat coordinate charts such as yaw-pitch or Euler angles. We show that this scoring choice introduces systematic geometric distortion near coordinate singularities (large pitch angles on the sphere and poses approaching gimbal lock in 3D rotations). Across four datasets (ETH-XGaze, Gaze360, BIWI, AFLW2000-3D), slice-conditional coverage at a nominal 90% target drops by 30-50 percentage points in these regions, falling to 38.9% on ETH-XGaze and 42.0% on Gaze360 at gaze pitch above 70 degrees, and to 57.5% on BIWI and 55.2% on AFLW2000-3D at head pose pitch above 60 degrees near gimbal lock, despite marginal coverage remaining near 90%. We prove that this is structural. Scalar thresholding changes the size of chart-coordinate prediction sets but leaves their distorted axis ratios unchanged. To diagnose this hidden failure mode, we show that a simple geometric quantity, the Riemannian volume density, strongly correlates with where coverage collapse occurs. Finally, we show that coordinate-free geodesic scoring removes this distortion. It requires no retraining and adds negligible computational cost.

</details>


### [13] [MAGE: View-guided Point Cloud Completion with Efficient Modality Alignment and Adaptive Geometry Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02568)
*Weize Quan,Zhengwei Wu,Kai Wang,Dong-Ming Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes MAGE, a unified geometry-aware framework for view-based point cloud completion, featuring modality alignment via shared self-attention Transformer, adaptive geometry-aware self-attention, and geometry-perceptive anchor refinement, achieving state-of-the-art results on synthetic and real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing view-based point cloud completion methods suffer from weak modality alignment between image and point cloud features, and limited self-geometry enhancement, leading to cross-modal geometric inconsistency.

Method: (1) Geometry-aware modality alignment using shared self-attention Transformer and cross-modality reconstruction supervision to align image and point cloud features in a shared latent space. (2) Adaptive geometry-aware self-attention module for local geometry-aware attention and spatially-variant feature fusion. (3) Geometry-perceptive anchor refinement module with appropriate supervision to reorganize anchor points.

Result: Superior performance over existing approaches on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed unified geometry-aware framework effectively addresses cross-modal geometric inconsistency and achieves state-of-the-art view-guided point cloud completion.

Abstract: View-based point cloud completion aims to recover a complete 3D shape from a partial point cloud, guided by a single-view image. However, existing approaches often suffer from limited performance due to weak modality alignment and limited self-geometry enhancement. To overcome these challenges, we propose a unified geometry-aware framework that integrates efficient modality alignment and adaptive geometry enhancement, mainly to address cross-modal geometric inconsistency of view-guided point cloud completion. Specifically, we propose a geometry-aware modality alignment by integrating a shared self-attention Transformer and cross-modality reconstruction supervision, which aims to bring features of the image and point cloud close to each other in a shared latent space describing the 3D object. To enhance the perception of global shape and local geometric details, we propose an adaptive geometry-aware self-attention module, which simultaneously considers local geometry-aware attention computation and the spatially-variant feature fusion. In addition, we apply a geometry-perceptive anchor refinement module to reorganize the anchor points (representing a local region of the shape) under appropriate supervision, further boosting the completion performance of our method. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance over existing approaches. Our code will be available at https://github.com/weizequan/MAGE.

</details>


### [14] [Uncertainty-Aware Last-Layer Adaptation of RETFound for Referable Diabetic Retinopathy Screening Under Dataset Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02569)
*Karim Mardhani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates uncertainty-aware last-layer adaptation methods for diabetic retinopathy screening using RETFound, finding that while these methods improve sensitivity and selective-referral behavior on the APTOS dataset (reducing false negatives to zero by deferring ~20% of cases), results vary significantly across datasets (DDR), with Bayesian methods not uniquely responsible for false-negative reduction, and transferred models failing to generalize well.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether uncertainty-aware last-layer adaptations can improve safety in referable diabetic retinopathy screening beyond what conventional accuracy metrics capture, and to evaluate their reliability across different datasets and distribution shifts.

Method: Compares multiple uncertainty-aware last-layer approaches (cached-feature softmax head, post-hoc temperature scaling, variational Bayesian last-layer heads, diagonal Laplace last-layer approximation, SNGP-style cached-feature head) using RETFound as a frozen feature encoder on APTOS 2019 and DDR datasets.

Result: On APTOS, uncertainty-aware methods improved sensitivity and selective-referral; the best result deferred ~20% of cases with zero false negatives and high specificity. However, false-negative reduction was achievable via threshold tuning and not unique to Bayesian methods. On DDR, Bayesian heads showed weaker tradeoffs, and APTOS-trained SNGP transferred poorly.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware last-layer heads can improve safety-oriented operating points, but trustworthy claims require explicit safety-coverage evaluation and validation on a second dataset under distribution shift.

Abstract: This paper presents a safety-centered empirical evaluation of uncertainty-aware last-layer adaptation for referable diabetic retinopathy screening using RETFound, a self-supervised vision-transformer retinal foundation model used here as a frozen feature encoder, and the public APTOS 2019 and DDR diabetic retinopathy fundus image datasets. We compare a cached-feature softmax head, post-hoc temperature scaling, variational Bayesian last-layer heads, a diagonal Laplace last-layer approximation, and an SNGP-style cached-feature head. On APTOS, uncertainty-aware operating points improved sensitivity and selective-referral behavior. The strongest APTOS selective-referral result deferred approximately 20 percent of cases and reduced accepted-case false negatives to zero while preserving high accepted-case specificity. However, threshold tuning also reduced false negatives at high false-positive cost, so false-negative reduction alone was not unique to Bayesian modeling. On DDR, native Bayesian heads qualitatively reproduced the APTOS direction but with weaker tradeoffs, while the APTOS-trained SNGP checkpoint transferred poorly and failed to provide useful external selective-referral behavior. These results highlight the value of safety-centered evaluation beyond aggregate accuracy: uncertainty-aware last-layer heads can improve internal safety-oriented operating points, but trustworthy retinal screening claims require explicit safety-coverage evaluation and second-dataset validation under shift.

</details>


### [15] [Dual-Adaptive SAM3: Hierarchical Routing over Low-Rank Expert Layers for Parameter-Efficient Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02571)
*Ying Chen,Jinyue Li,Kun Wang,Qiankun Li,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DA-SAM3 is a dual-adaptive framework for medical image segmentation that combines a Dynamic Expert Router (task-aware) and Decomposed Parameterized Experts (parameter-aware) to achieve high accuracy with extreme parameter efficiency, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 5% while reducing MoE overhead by 80%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adapting the powerful SAM3 vision-language model to diverse medical imaging is challenging: naive fine-tuning is parameter-inefficient, and standard MoE methods introduce prohibitive computational overhead, limiting clinical applicability.

Method: Two key components: (1) **Dynamic Expert Router (DER)** — task-aware adaptation that sparsely activates the most relevant experts by jointly reasoning over visual input and text concept prompts; (2) **Decomposed Parameterized Experts (DPE)** — parameter-aware adaptation that represents each expert as a shared frozen base plus a lightweight trainable low-rank delta, reducing MoE parameters by over 80%.

Result: Matches or exceeds fully fine-tuned SAM3 and standard MoE baselines; achieves a notable 5% gain over current state-of-the-art methods on multiple public medical segmentation benchmarks; parameter reduction of over 80%.

Conclusion: DA-SAM3 effectively balances accuracy and efficiency for medical image segmentation, with interpretable results validating its clinical viability.

Abstract: The Segment Anything Model with Concepts (SAM3) heralds a new paradigm for open-vocabulary segmentation through natural language interaction, offering significant potential for medical image analysis. However, effectively adapting such a powerful vision-language model to the diverse and nuanced domain of medical imaging remains a key challenge. Naive fine-tuning is parameter-inefficient, while standard Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) methods introduce prohibitive computational overhead, limiting their clinical applicability. To address this, we propose Dual-Adaptive SAM3 (DA-SAM3), a novel framework that achieves both high segmentation accuracy and extreme parameter efficiency via a dual-adaptive specialization mechanism. Our first adaptation is task-aware: a Dynamic Expert Router (DER) that sparsely activates the most relevant experts by jointly reasoning about the visual input and the textual concept prompt, mimicking a clinical consultation process. Our second adaptation is parameter-aware: a Decomposed Parameterized Experts (DPE) design that represents each expert as a shared frozen base (inherited from the pretrained SAM3) and a lightweight trainable low-rank delta, reducing MoE parameter overhead by over 80\%. Extensive experiments on multiple public medical segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that Dual-Adaptive SAM3 not only matches or exceeds the accuracy of fully fine-tuned SAM3 and standard MoE baselines, but also achieves a notable 5\% gain over current state-of-the-art methods, with interpretable results validating its effectiveness. The code is available at: https://github.com/Reconsider80/DA-SAM3.

</details>


### [16] [PixCon: Clean-Positive Contrastive Learning for Foundation-Model Semi-Supervised Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03068)
*Ebenezer Tarubinga*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PixCon is a clean-positive pixel-contrastive framework for semi-supervised semantic segmentation that guarantees contamination-free memory banks by using only correctly-classified labeled pixels, outperforming prior methods that rely on confidence-filtered pseudo-labels, especially when foundation model teachers are used.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional SSSS methods focus on filtering pseudo-labels by confidence, but foundation backbones like DINOv2 already produce 98%-clean pseudo-labels, shifting the problem from filtering to structuring the embedding space. Additionally, prior contrastive SSSS methods (ReCo, U²PL) suffer from contamination in their memory banks due to reliance on confidence-filtered pseudo-labels.

Method: PixCon uses a per-class memory bank that only admits labeled pixels the student already classifies correctly, guaranteeing zero false-positive contamination (ρ_F=0) by construction. It is a single branch over a consistency backbone with no inference-time parameters and no bank-specific threshold. The authors also provide a first-order analysis of the supervised-InfoNCE gradient showing the false-positive term scales as ρ_F/(1-ρ_F).

Result: Across Pascal VOC, Cityscapes, and ADE20K, PixCon matches or improves a strong DINOv2-based UniMatch V2 baseline. It improves every Pascal-1/8 seed (about +0.2 mIoU per seed) and reaches 87.90 three-seed mean mIoU (matching published UniMatch V2-B). Contamination rates are measured at 0.018 on Pascal and 0.106 on ADE20K.

Conclusion: With foundation-model teachers where contamination is already rare, the ρ_F=0 guarantee mainly provides robustness as teachers weaken, while accuracy gains stem from cleaner positive supervision. Clean-positive contrast offers a robust, low-cost default for foundation-model SSSS.

Abstract: Semi-supervised semantic segmentation (SSSS) has long turned on one question, which pseudo-labels to trust, and answered it with ever more careful confidence filtering. Foundation backbones change the regime: with a DINOv2 teacher a strict threshold already retains a measured 98%-clean pseudo-label set, so the accuracy that remains lives not in the filter but in how the embedding space is structured by class. We propose PixCon, a clean-positive pixel-contrastive framework. PixCon maintains a per-class memory bank that admits only labeled pixels the student already classifies correctly, guaranteeing a contamination-free positive set ($ρ_F=0$) by construction, unlike prior contrastive SSSS banks (ReCo, U$^2$PL) built from confidence-filtered pseudo-labels. It is a single branch over a consistency backbone, adds no inference-time parameters, and needs no bank-specific threshold. A first-order analysis of the supervised-InfoNCE gradient explains why contamination hurts: its false-positive term scales as $ρ_F/(1-ρ_F)$, which we measure (0.018 on Pascal, 0.106 on ADE20K) rather than assume. Across Pascal VOC, Cityscapes, and ADE20K, PixCon matches or improves a strong DINOv2-based UniMatch V2 baseline in a compute-matched one-switch protocol: it improves every Pascal-1/8 seed (a per-seed gain of about +0.2 mIoU) and its three-seed mean reaches 87.90, the published UniMatch V2-B figure. Because contamination is already rare under foundation-model teachers, our analysis indicates the $ρ_F=0$ guarantee acts chiefly as robustness as teachers weaken, while the accuracy gain comes from cleaner positive supervision, making clean-positive contrast a robust, low-cost default for foundation-model SSSS.

</details>


### [17] [Additive Causal Construction for Transferable and Reconfigurable Cross-System Learning in Multi-Source Image Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02572)
*Zhizhong Fu,Wei Zhou,Zhaoyang Jiang,Yulong Lin,Yifu Hou,Xiaorong Ding,Qiang Yan,Yifan Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes an Additive Causal Construction (ACC) framework to address cross-system discrepancy and entanglement in multi-source image fusion, achieving robust out-of-distribution generalization via causal representation learning with uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-source image fusion suffers from cross-system discrepancy (CSD) and cross-system entanglement (CSE), causing significant performance degradation under out-of-distribution (OOD) predictions.

Method: Proposes ACC framework with two levels: (1) causal graph transferability (CGT) via intervention consistency establishing shared causal anchors; (2) causal graph reconfigurability (CGR) via uncertainty quantification. Instantiates as ACC-CRL using content-mechanism decoupling and response alignment under shared anchors, with structural uncertainty to regulate fusion.

Result: Experiments on synthetic ColorMNIST and real-world multi-center medical imaging (MVI prediction) show significant OOD generalization improvement while maintaining in-distribution performance.

Conclusion: The ACC-CRL strategy based on mechanism alignment and uncertainty modeling is effective and robust for open-environment multi-source image fusion.

Abstract: In multi-source image fusion scenarios, heterogeneous inputs are typically driven by distinct generative mechanisms and can be viewed as a composition of multiple causal systems. However, cross-system discrepancy (CSD) and cross-system entanglement (CSE) commonly arise during the fusion process, often leading to significant performance degradation under out-of-distribution (OOD) predictions. To address the CSD and CSE issues, we propose the additive causal construction (ACC) framework, which characterizes information fusion at two levels: firstly, it establishes causal "anchors" shared among multiple systems through intervention consistency to enable causal graph transferability (CGT); and secondly, it formalizes the fusion process as causal construction and models the reliability of constructed paths through uncertainty quantification to ensure causal graph reconfigurability (CGR). Building upon this, we revisit the traditional causal representation learning (CRL) with ACC and propose ACC-CRL as a learnable instantiation of the framework. The method explores joint causal content representations across systems via content-mechanism decoupling, and performs response alignment under shared anchors to mitigate CSD. Furthermore, it incorporates structural uncertainty to adaptively regulate the fusion process, thereby suppressing unstable CSE. We conduct systematic experiments on synthetic data (ColorMNIST) and real-world multi-center medical imaging tasks (microvascular invasion (MVI) prediction). The results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves OOD generalization while maintaining in-distribution (ID) performance, validating the effectiveness and robustness of the ACC-CRL strategy based on mechanism alignment and uncertainty modeling in open environments.

</details>


### [18] [Towards Standardized Light Field Quality Assessment: Hybrid Subjective Benchmarking and Objective Metric Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03494)
*Saeed Mahmoudpour,Mylene C. Q. Farias,Gi-Mun Um,Myllena A. Prado,Ismael Seidel,Leonardo de Sousa Marques,Leonardo Andrade,Shengyang Zhao,Carla L Pagliari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a standardized workflow for light field quality assessment, integrating benchmark generation, hybrid subjective evaluation, and objective metric analysis. It finds that current objective metrics perform well on coding-only distortions but fail when view synthesis distortions are introduced, highlighting the need for better view-pooling strategies in future metrics. The annotated dataset is publicly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a reliable, reproducible, and standardized workflow for light field quality assessment that can handle both coding-only artifacts and distortions from view synthesis and reconstruction pipelines, addressing the lack of comprehensive evaluation frameworks in JPEG Pleno standardization.

Method: A standardized workflow integrating benchmark generation, a hybrid subjective evaluation method (combining reference-anchored rating with pairwise refinement in ambiguous regions), and systematic evaluation of objective metrics using statistical consistency analyses across two observer cohorts.

Result: Several objective metrics achieve strong agreement for coding-only stimuli, but performance consistently drops when view synthesis distortions are included. View-pooling strategy is identified as critical for future light field quality metric design.

Conclusion: The work provides a reproducible, standardization-ready framework for fine-grained light field QA and identifies key limitations of current objective metrics under emerging coding pipelines.

Abstract: Benchmarking immersive media coding solutions, especially in the standardization context, requires reliable and reproducible subjective quality assessment (QA) procedures, along with objective quality metrics that remain accurate across different distortion types. This paper presents a standardized workflow for light field QA, developed and deployed in the context of JPEG Pleno standardization activities, which integrates benchmark generation, a hybrid subjective evaluation, and objective metric analysis into a common workflow. The benchmark is designed to encompass not only traditional coding-only artifacts but also distortions that arise in processing pipelines in which light field encoding is accompanied with view synthesis and reconstruction techniques. A hybrid subjective method is proposed enabling fine-grained assessment by combining reference-anchored quality rating with targeted pairwise refinement in perceptually ambiguous regions. The reliability of subjective scores is verified using statistical consistency analyses between observers of two cohorts. Finally, a large set of objective metrics is systematically evaluated in terms of global prediction accuracy, local agreement in ambiguous quality regions, and robustness across distortion families. The results show that several metrics achieve strong agreement for coding-only stimuli, but their performance consistently drops when view synthesis distortions are included. The analysis further highlights the importance of view-pooling strategy in the design of future light field quality metrics. The work provides a reproducible and standardization-ready framework for fine-grained light field QA, while identifying key limitations of current objective metrics under emerging coding pipelines. The subjectively annotated dataset is publicly available at https://plenodb.jpeg.org/lfqa/objectivecfp.

</details>


### [19] [Symmetry-Structured Neural Completion of Islamic Geometric Patterns from Sparse Control Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02573)
*Hassan Ugail,Irfan Mehmood*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper embeds exact rotational symmetry rules into a neural completion framework for Islamic geometric patterns, producing vector graphs with guaranteed N-fold symmetry, unlike purely data-driven approaches that lose validity under corruption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Islamic geometric patterns require exact rotational symmetry and strict construction rules, which purely data-driven neural methods cannot guarantee. The authors aim to provide a knowledge-constrained, guarantee-backed approach.

Method: The system treats symmetry rules as formal geometric knowledge embedded in a neural completion framework. It uses a candidate lattice with edges organized into rotational orbits under the cyclic group, enforcing symmetry via orbit-tied constraints or projections during inference.

Result: Orbit-tied variant guarantees exact N-fold symmetry, preserves anchor points, and keeps refinements within bounds. On clean inputs, no fidelity loss; under corruption, unstructured decoder loses fidelity and breaks symmetry, while symmetry-structured inference keeps violations at zero.

Conclusion: Augmentation improves fidelity under corruption; symmetry structure guarantees validity. The framework provides a guarantee-backed approach for scalable vector ornaments requiring exact geometric structure.

Abstract: Islamic geometric patterns are governed by exact rotational symmetry and strict construction rules. This paper treats these rules as formal geometric knowledge and embeds them in a neural completion framework, rather than leaving them to be learned statistically from data. Given sparse control geometry and a target symmetry order, the system completes the pattern as a vector graph by predicting edges and refinements of bounded curves over a candidate lattice whose edges are organised into rotational orbits under the cyclic group. Symmetry is enforced either by constraining predictions within these orbits or by projecting them onto them during inference. The orbit-tied variant provides a constructive guarantee: for any input and any orbit-level selection rule, it produces exact N-fold symmetry, preserves anchor points, and keeps all refinements within prescribed bounds. These properties are verified numerically. The study focuses on rotational symmetry, and all quantitative results are obtained from procedurally generated graphs inspired by Islamic geometric design rather than from a historical corpus. On clean inputs, enforcing exact validity produces no measurable loss in fidelity. When control geometry is missing, an unstructured decoder loses fidelity and breaks symmetry; retraining on corrupted inputs recovers much of the fidelity but not exact validity. Symmetry-structured inference, by contrast, keeps violations at zero throughout. The results show that augmentation and symmetry structure address distinct failure modes: augmentation improves fidelity under corruption, while symmetry structure guarantees validity. The framework therefore provides a knowledge-constrained, guarantee-backed approach to neural completion for scalable vector ornaments whose validity depends on exact geometric structure.

</details>


### [20] [Probabilistic Robustness in Medical Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03797)
*Yi Zhang,Siddartha Khastgir,Xingyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates probabilistic robustness (PR) as a more practical measure of trustworthiness for deep learning models in medical image classification, evaluating common models on MedMNIST v2 under natural corruption settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing studies focus on adversarial robustness from a worst-case perspective, which may be less representative of real medical applications where prediction errors under perturbations can be severe.

Method: Constructed a set of natural corruption settings for medical image classification and systematically evaluated commonly used DL models on the MedMNIST v2 dataset.

Result: Provided a statistically grounded assessment of model trustworthiness through probabilistic robustness evaluation under natural corruptions.

Conclusion: Probabilistic robustness offers a more practical measure than adversarial robustness for trustworthy deployment of DL models in medical imaging.

Abstract: Deep learning (DL) has shown strong performance in medical image classification, but its trustworthy deployment remains challenging in safety-critical clinical settings, where prediction errors under perturbations may lead to severe consequences. Existing studies mainly focus on adversarial robustness (AR) from a worst-case perspective; however, such settings may be less representative of real medical applications. In this work, we investigate probabilistic robustness (PR) as a more practical measure of model trustworthiness. To this end, we construct a set of natural corruption settings for medical image classification and systematically evaluate commonly used DL models on MedMNIST v2 dataset. Our study provides a statistically grounded perspective on assessing the trustworthiness of DL models, thereby supporting their more trustworthy deployment in medical imaging applications.

</details>


### [21] [Criterion-Conditional In-Context Learning: Evaluating Criterion-Shift Adaptation in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02575)
*Kaiyun Yang,Ruilin Yang,Zhimin Yao,J. Wang,Wei Ge*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new ICL setting (CC-ICL) where vision-language models must adapt to shifting decision criteria while maintaining fixed task semantics, along with a benchmark (CC-Bench) and metrics to evaluate this capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world tasks often have stable high-level intents but shifting decision criteria, which standard ICL fails to handle. The paper aims to address this gap by introducing criterion-conditional reasoning.

Method: The authors (1) define CC-ICL with two metrics (Criterion Invariance &amp; Sensitivity), (2) construct CC-Bench with a dual-level data hierarchy for ground-truth variation under fixed tasks, and (3) propose a multi-criterion training strategy.

Result: Most models exhibit rigid boundary bias under criterion shifts. Multi-criterion training significantly reduces this bias, and 7B-scale models can surpass proprietary models without degrading general multimodal performance.

Conclusion: CC-ICL is a crucial but previously overlooked capability; simple training strategies can effectively mitigate criterion-related biases and improve model adaptability.

Abstract: Vision-language models can perform new tasks without parameter updates through in-context learning (ICL), whose core mechanism is utilizing the support set for task induction. In the standard ICL setting, once the task is induced, its decision criterion remains fixed. However, in real-world applications, many tasks exhibit a stable high-level intent, while their decision criteria shift according to specific requirements. Thus, we introduce a new setting, denoted as Criterion-Conditional In-Context Learning (CC-ICL), where models must infer the latent criterion from context and adjust predictions accordingly under fixed task semantics. To evaluate this capability, we propose two complementary metrics, Criterion Invariance and Criterion Sensitivity, capturing the model's robustness and adaptability under criterion shifts. We further construct CC-Bench, a multi-domain benchmark that supports evaluation under the CC-ICL setting. By employing a dual-level data hierarchy, CC-Bench enables legitimate ground-truth variation conditioned on the active criterion even when the task remains fixed. Experiments on CC-Bench reveal that most models exhibit a rigid boundary bias, struggling to align their decisions with the latent criterion. We also find that even a simple multi-criterion training strategy can significantly reduce this bias, improving Criterion Sensitivity and enabling 7B-scale models to surpass proprietary models without degrading general multimodal performance.

</details>


### [22] [ContiStain: Cross-Domain Relation-Preserving Distillation for Continual Multi-Domain Virtual IHC Staining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03851)
*Fuqiang Chen,Yifeng Wang,Hongpeng Wang,Yongbing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ContiStain is an IHC multi-domain relational distillation framework that addresses catastrophic forgetting in continual virtual staining by using a mixture-of-experts feature extractor and relation-preserving distillation to maintain cross-domain structural coherence when new biomarkers are added sequentially over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multiplex virtual staining models face performance degradation on previously learned biomarkers when fine-tuned sequentially on new biomarker data, as sequential optimization disrupts structured relationships among biomarker representations in the latent space.

Method: ContiStain proposes (i) a domain-aware structured feature space using mixture-of-experts (MoE) feature extractor to reduce representation interference, and (ii) a relation-preserving distillation strategy that enforces consistency of cross-domain token-level cosine similarity matrices between learned biomarker domains during continual adaptation.

Result: On the MIST dataset under four-domain sequential virtual IHC staining, ContiStain reduces FID by 11.1 and ConchFID by 60.9 compared to sequential fine-tuning, demonstrating improved stability and reduced forgetting.

Conclusion: ContiStain effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting while retaining adaptability to new domains, enabling scalable and robust multi-domain virtual staining for clinical practice where biomarker data arrives sequentially.

Abstract: A unified multiplex virtual staining model enables scalable and non-destructive multiplex analysis from H&E slides while promoting parameter efficiency, shared pathological knowledge, and consistent cross-biomarker representations. However, in clinical practice, data for new biomarkers are typically acquired sequentially over time. Fine-tuning on such temporally arriving data leads to severe performance degradation on previously learned biomarkers, as sequential optimization disrupts the structured relationships among biomarker representations in the latent space. To address this issue, we propose ContiStain, an IHC multi-domain relational distillation framework for continual virtual staining. We first (i) construct a domain-aware structured feature space using a mixture-of-experts (MoE) feature extractor to reduce representation interference across biomarker domains. Based on this stabilized feature space, we then (ii) propose a relation-preserving distillation strategy that explicitly enforces the consistency of cross-domain token-level cosine similarity matrices between learned biomarker domains during continual adaptation. By maintaining cross-domain structural coherence, ContiStain mitigates forgetting while retaining adaptability to new domains. Experiments on the MIST dataset under a four-domain sequential virtual IHC staining setting show improved stability, reducing FID and ConchFID by 11.1 and 60.9 compared to sequential fine-tuning, enabling scalable and robust multi-domain virtual staining. Code is released at https://github.com/ccitachi/ContiStain.

</details>


### [23] [Classroom Behavior Monitoring with YOLO An Empirical Study in Higher Education Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02580)
*Sinh Vu Trong,Dung Nguyen Manh,Hieu Hoang Minh,Hieu Pham Trung,Thu Pham Ha,Nhu Le Hoang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a real-world classroom video dataset (BAV-Classroom) with nine behavioral categories, evaluates computer vision models (YOLOv11 best), and finds student concentration drops significantly during lecture endings, demonstrating feasibility of automated monitoring for academic quality management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional classroom behavior monitoring methods are subjective and lack scalability, motivating the need for automated, objective solutions to evaluate student engagement and improve teaching effectiveness.

Method: Created the BAV-Classroom dataset with videos annotated across nine behavioral categories, then evaluated and compared state-of-the-art Computer Vision models, with YOLOv11 performing best.

Result: YOLOv11 achieved the best performance among evaluated models; students' concentration notably decreases during the final part of lectures, revealing sustained engagement challenges.

Conclusion: Computer vision is feasible for automated classroom monitoring, offering valuable insights for academic quality management and addressing scalability limitations of traditional methods.

Abstract: Classroom behavior monitoring plays a vital role in evaluating student engagement and improving teaching effectiveness. Traditional observation methods remain subjective and lack scalability. This study introduces a real-world dataset of classroom videos collected at the Banking Academy of Vietnam (BAV-Classroom dataset), annotated with nine distinctive behavioral categories. State-of-the-art Computer Vision models were evaluated and compared, with YOLOv11 achieving the best performance. Experimental results indicate that students' concentration often decreases notably during the final part of lectures, highlighting challenges in sustaining engagement. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility of applying computer vision for automated classroom monitoring, providing valuable insights for academic quality management.

</details>


### [24] [Evaluating Intellectual Property Guardrails of Generative Image Models: A Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02582)
*Austin T. Hoag,Apostolos Modas,Yunhao Ba,Julienne M. LaChance,Jinru Xue,Wiebke Hutiri,Jan Simson,Tiffany Georgievski,Alex Towli,Joseph Smith,Yuki Mitsufuji,Alice Xiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks generative image models for IP guardrails, testing 14 models (3 open-weight, 11 private) on fictional characters, celebrity likenesses, and commercial logos. Results show all models generate recognizable IP as of March 2026, with commercial logos generated most frequently and refusal rates varying substantially across models and categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative image models can produce images that replicate recognizable intellectual property (e.g., characters, celebrities, logos), raising legal and ethical concerns. The authors aim to systematically measure how effectively current models refuse such generation.

Method: The authors built a benchmark and automated evaluation pipeline covering three IP categories: fictional characters, celebrity likenesses, and commercial logos. They evaluated 14 text-to-image models (3 self-hosted open-weight, 11 private) for IP guardrail effectiveness and generation propensity.

Result: All 14 models generated recognizable IP as of March 2026. Private models showed varying refusal rates; commercial logos had the lowest refusal rate and highest generation success. Refusal rates varied considerably across IP categories and models.

Conclusion: Despite some IP guardrails, all tested text-to-image models still produce recognizable IP, especially commercial logos. The findings highlight the need for stronger, more consistent IP protection mechanisms in generative image models.

Abstract: Generative image models are capable of producing images that bear a strong resemblance to, or replicate, recognizable intellectual property (IP). In this technical report, we present a benchmark and automated evaluation pipeline to test for evidence of IP guardrails in generative image models along with the propensity for these models to generate images with recognizable IP. The IP categories we tested include fictional characters, celebrity likeness, and commercial logos and do not encompass the full range of IP which may be implicated by image generation models. We evaluated fourteen widely used text-to-image models, including three self-hosted open weights models and eleven private models. While all of the private models were observed to refuse generations at some level due to IP guardrails, the frequency of generation refusals varied substantially among models. The refusal rates also varied considerably across the different IP categories tested. Commercial logos were refused least frequently and were successfully generated at the highest rate, on average. Though the rate varies, all models tested readily generated images containing recognizable IP as of March 2026.

</details>


### [25] [RotateAttention: RoPE-Aware Rotation and Range Rectification for INT4 Quantized Attention in Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02584)
*Yaofu Liu,Wanli Lan,Jinxi Li,Binhang Yuan,Harry Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes RotateAttention, a mixed-precision INT4 FlashAttention framework for DiT-based video generation with 3D RoPE, addressing RoPE-compatible rotation and asymmetric quantization of attention matrices to achieve up to 1.68× end-to-end speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attention is the main bottleneck in DiT-based video models due to quadratic complexity. Existing low-bit quantization fails to handle: (1) RoPE-incompatible online rotation for outlier mitigation, and (2) wasted INT4 range from symmetric quantization of non-negative attention matrices.

Method: Two core techniques: (1) RoPE-aware Rotation — merges rotation matrices into RoPE or uses low-overhead matrices to suppress Q/K outliers; (2) Range-optimized P Quantization — applies fixed scales/zero-points to fully use INT4 range for non-negative attention matrices. Uses selective FP16 fallback for sensitive blocks/steps.

Result: Matches full-precision generation quality, achieves up to 1.68× end-to-end speedup and 2.2× kernel-level acceleration.

Conclusion: RotateAttention effectively enables INT4 FlashAttention for 3D RoPE-based video DiTs, overcoming key quantization challenges while delivering significant speedups without quality loss.

Abstract: In \textbf{DiT-based video generation models equipped with 3D Rotary Position Embeddings (3D RoPE)}, the attention mechanism remains a primary computational bottleneck due to its quadratic complexity with respect to sequence length. While quantized \textbf{FlashAttention} offers a promising path toward hardware acceleration, existing low-bit quantization methods overlook two critical challenges in this setting: \textbf{1)} applying online rotation matrices -- a widely used technique for mitigating outliers in Queries ($Q$) and Keys ($K$) -- is difficult to reconcile with \textbf{RoPE}; and \textbf{2)} the non-negative attention matrix $P = \exp(QK - \max(QK))$ makes symmetric quantization waste half of the 4-bit dynamic range. In this work, we observe that the outlier distributions of $Q$ and $K$ are strongly affected by the dimensional partitioning of \textbf{3D RoPE}. Based on this finding, we propose \textbf{RotateAttention}, an efficient \textbf{mixed-precision INT4 FlashAttention} framework tailored for \textbf{DiT-based video generation models with 3D RoPE}, using selective \textbf{FP16 fallback} for accuracy-sensitive attention blocks and denoising steps. RotateAttention introduces two core techniques: \textbf{1) RoPE-aware Rotation}, which employs either mergeable rotation matrices that can be fused into RoPE or negligible-overhead matrices to mitigate RoPE-induced outliers in $Q$ and $K$; and \textbf{2) Range-optimized $P$ Quantization}, which uses fixed scales and zero-points to fully exploit the \textbf{INT4 numerical range} with minimal computational overhead.
