*Jiaqi Tang,Shaoyang Zhang,Fandong Zhang,Shu Zhang,Yang Liu,Qingchao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a non-parametric, topology-driven framework for transferability estimation of medical vision foundation models on 3D segmentation tasks, achieving SOTA results with 56x speedup over fine-tuning-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing transferability estimation metrics are designed for image-level classification and fail to preserve spatial relationships and fine-grained boundary details crucial for segmentation. Fine-tuning-based model selection is computationally expensive.

Method: Proposes a topology-driven framework using Minimum Spanning Trees (MST) to align sparse 1-skeleton graphs of dense features with semantic labels. Decouples alignment into Local Boundary-Aware Topological Consistency (LBTC) for boundary separability and Global Representation Topology Divergence (GRTD) for anatomical layout. Uses a randomly initialized decoder as a topology-preserving spatial projector, with a task-adaptive gating mechanism.

Result: Evaluated on 114,000 3D medical volumes across diverse anatomical tasks. Achieves state-of-the-art transferability estimation with average weighted Kendall outperforming by 0.36, while accelerating evaluation by 56 times compared to fine-tuning-based methods.

Conclusion: The proposed topology-driven framework effectively bridges the gap in transferability estimation for dense segmentation tasks in 3D medical imaging, offering both superior accuracy and dramatic computational efficiency.

Abstract: The growing number of medical vision foundation models highlights the need for effective model selection. However, mainstream selection methods rely on exhaustive fine-tuning, which is computationally expensive. Most of the existing Transferability Estimation (TE) metrics are primarily designed for image-level classification. They fail to preserve spatial relationships and fine-grained boundary details, which are crucial for the segmentation task. Additionally, while image-level tasks typically process a single feature vector per input, dense prediction tasks in 3D medical imaging require voxel-wise evaluation against dense annotations. To bridge these gaps, we propose a \textit{non-parametric, topology-driven} framework that estimates transferability directly from the alignment between the sparse 1-skeleton graph of dense features and semantic labels via Minimum Spanning Trees (MST). We decouple the alignment into two complementary geometric scales: Local Boundary-Aware Topological Consistency (LBTC) to assess boundary separability, where we prove that the MST leakage rate serves as a finite-sample lower bound on the Bayes error; and Global Representation Topology Divergence (GRTD) to evaluate the overall anatomical layout. Crucially, we formally justify a counterintuitive mechanism: Although without fine-tuning, the randomly initialized segmentation decoder acts as a topology-preserving spatial projector, reducing the variance of pairwise distance estimates and stabilizing global alignment evaluation. Fused via a task-adaptive gating mechanism, these dual metrics adapt to diverse clinical complexities. Evaluated on a large-scale benchmark of 114,000 3D medical volumes across diverse anatomical tasks, our topological framework achieves state-of-the-art transferability estimation with an average weighted Kendall (outperforming by 0.36) while accelerating evaluation by 56 times.

</details>


### [210] [Hierarchical Multi-to-Single-Modal Knowledge Distillation for Disruption Prediction in EAST](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04241)
*Qiang Chen,Xiao Wang,Hao Si,Qingquan Yang,Meiwen Chen,Jianhua Yang,Xiaofeng Han,Yunhu Jia,Ran Chen,Liang Wang,Jin Tang,Guosheng Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a hierarchical knowledge distillation framework that transfers multimodal (image + time-series) knowledge to a time-series-only student model for tokamak disruption prediction, achieving comparable accuracy with lower inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visible images improve disruption prediction accuracy but substantially increase computational cost during inference; the goal is to preserve multimodal advantages while reducing inference overhead.

Method: Uses a multimodal teacher (Transformer encoders + prototype-guided spatiotemporal hypergraph) trained on synchronized images and time-series signals, then distills knowledge into a time-series student via graph-structure-level, representation-level, and decision-level distillation.

Result: On 640-discharge EAST dataset, the student model retains discriminative advantages of multimodal learning while significantly reducing inference cost.

Conclusion: The framework provides an effective route for efficient disruption prediction in tokamaks by balancing accuracy and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Plasma disruption is a critical threat to tokamak safety. Existing data-driven predictors mainly rely on time-series diagnostic signals, while visible images provide complementary spatial cues including plasma deformation, local brightening, and radiation-structure evolution. Although the image modality improves the model's discriminative capability, it also substantially increases the computational cost during inference. To address this issue, we propose a hierarchical multi-to-single-modal knowledge distillation framework for disruption prediction on a synchronized EAST multimodal dataset. During training, visible images and time-series signals are used to train a multimodal teacher, which learns disruption precursor representations through Transformer-based encoders and a prototype-guided spatiotemporal hypergraph module. During inference, only the time-series student is retained, with multimodal knowledge transferred through graph-structure-level, representation-level, and decision-level distillation. On the 640-discharge EAST dataset, the results demonstrate that the proposed framework can preserve the discriminative advantages of multimodal learning while substantially reducing inference cost, and providing an effective route for efficient disruption prediction in EAST. The source code of this paper will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenFusion.

</details>


### [211] [HeartVolMesh: Cardiac Volumetric Mesh Reconstruction via Covariance-Guided Graph Deformation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04243)
*Fengming Lin,Arezoo Zakeri,Haoran Dou,Zherui Zhou,Shaokun Lan,Jinming Duan,Alejandro Frangi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HeartVolMesh uses a 3D CNN-GNN to predict anisotropic Gaussian kernels for each template vertex, enabling accurate patient-specific tetrahedral cardiac meshes from volumetric images while preserving cross-case correspondence via deformable template warping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing segmentation-then-modelling pipelines blur thin-wall anatomy and offer limited cross-case correspondence, which is problematic for in-silico trials requiring accurate patient-specific tetrahedral cardiac meshes.

Method: Lifts each template vertex to an anisotropic Gaussian kernel; uses a 3D CNN-GNN to predict per-vertex displacements and Cholesky-parameterized covariances from volumetric images; training uses covariance-aware negative log-likelihood loss with mesh regularization; warps a fixed tetrahedral template via staged alignment, non-rigid registration, and deformation propagation.

Result: Consistent improvements over deformation-based baselines in both surface mesh accuracy and volumetric mesh fidelity.

Conclusion: HeartVolMesh effectively generates accurate, correspondence-preserving tetrahedral cardiac meshes, suitable for in-silico trials.

Abstract: Accurate patient-specific tetrahedral cardiac meshes are essential for in-silico trials, yet common segmentation-then-modelling pipelines can blur thin-wall anatomy and offer limited cross-case correspondence. We propose HeartVolMesh, which lifts each template vertex to an anisotropic Gaussian kernel and uses a 3D CNN-GNN to predict per-vertex displacements and Cholesky-parameterized covariances from volumetric images. Training is guided by a covariance-aware negative log-likelihood loss with lightweight mesh regularization. For volumetric meshing, we warp a fixed tetrahedral template to the reconstructed surface via staged alignment, non-rigid registration, and deformation propagation, preserving connectivity and correspondence by construction, with resolution controlled by template density. Experiments show consistent gains over deformation-based baselines in surface mesh accuracy and volumetric mesh fidelity.

</details>


### [212] [Beyond Random Sampling: Distribution-Aware Alignment for Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04249)
*Weihao Yan,Yeqiang Qian,Yi Dong,Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A semi-supervised medical image segmentation framework using distribution alignment via VFM-guided sample selection and Memory-guided Copy-Paste to overcome representation bias in low-data regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SSMIS assumes i.i.d. data and random sampling, which causes severe representation bias in low-data regimes, failing to capture heterogeneous medical data manifolds.

Method: (1) Distribution-Aware Sample Selection using VFMs + Density-K-Center algorithm to find representative anchors. (2) Memory-guided Copy-Paste (MCP) module leveraging semantic memory for cross-domain alignment with class imbalance handling, plus an easy-to-hard progressive schedule.

Result: Strong segmentation performance across six diverse 2D/3D datasets, especially in extremely low-labeled scenarios (e.g., 1/16 ratio).

Conclusion: The proposed distribution-alignment framework is highly data-efficient for SSMIS, effectively addressing representation bias and pseudo-label noise.

Abstract: Precise medical image segmentation is crucial for clinical diagnosis and treatment planning, yet relies heavily on expensive expert annotations. Semi-supervised medical image segmentation (SSMIS) offers a cost-effective solution but typically operates under the assumption of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data, defaulting to random sampling. While statistically valid at scale, this strategy suffers from severe representation bias in low-data regimes, failing to capture the heterogeneous medical data manifold. To address this, we propose a highly data-efficient framework driven by distribution alignment. First, we introduce an offline Distribution-Aware Sample Selection strategy. By leveraging Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) and our designed Density-K-Center algorithm, we explicitly identify representative structural anchors, establishing a more representative labeled domain. Second, to bridge the remaining distribution gap, we propose the Memory-guided Copy-Paste (MCP) module. Tailored for the inherent class imbalance in medical scans, MCP leverages a semantic memory mechanism to retrieve historically consistent priors for cross-domain alignment, encouraging semantic consistency. Coupled with an easy-to-hard progressive schedule, this framework effectively mitigates early-stage pseudo-label noise. Extensive experiments on six diverse 2D and 3D datasets demonstrate strong segmentation performance, particularly in extremely low-labeled scenarios (\eg, 1/16 ratio).

</details>


### [213] [AdaptiveSplat:Texture Aware Controllable 3D Gaussian Allocation for Feed-Forward Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04256)
*Badrinath Singhal,Srihari K G,Sreehari Iyer,Ankit Dhiman,Venkatesh Babu Radhakrishnan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A feed-forward 3D reconstruction method that prunes redundant Gaussians using local texture information, reducing representation redundancy without breaking the feed-forward paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods produce highly redundant pixel-aligned Gaussian primitives; naive pruning causes artifacts and requires fine-tuning, breaking the feed-forward paradigm.

Method: Three components: (1) texture estimation to capture spatial variation, (2) texture-aware pruning to remove redundant Gaussians in low-frequency regions, and (3) an adaptive Gaussian head to predict modified attributes of retained primitives.

Result: Validated on RE10K, ACID, DL3DV, Tanks and Temples, and DTU datasets; ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of key components.

Conclusion: Explicitly controlling Gaussian count via local texture information effectively reduces redundancy while maintaining quality in a feed-forward manner.

Abstract: Current feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods predict pixel aligned Gaussian primitives, resulting in highly redundant representations. A natural solution is to prune the redundant Gaussians, but naive pruning introduces severe artifacts and often requires inference time fine-tuning, breaking the feed-forward paradigm. Based on previous works, high frequency regions require more Gaussian primitives, while low frequency regions can be represented with significantly fewer primitives. Motivated by this, we propose a novel approach to explicitly control the number of Gaussians by leveraging local texture information. Our approach achieves this through three key components: (1) texture estimation to capture spatial variation in scene detail, (2) texture-aware pruning that removes redundant Gaussians from low frequency regions, and (3) an adaptive Gaussian head that predicts the modified attributes of the retained primitives without breaking the feed-forward paradigm. Experiments on RE10K, ACID, DL3DV, Tanks and Temples, and DTU demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, while ablation studies validate the contributions of its key components.

</details>


### [214] [EMPURPLE: A Free Lunch for Diffusion Distillation based on the Information Bottleneck](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04276)
*Zilai Li,Lujia Bai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion distillation models suffer from degraded FID due to distribution mismatch from aggressive early-step velocity redirection; EMPURPLE, a training-free method recycling intermediate latents, improves FID by 7-20%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models are expensive at inference; distillation reduces steps but causes FID degradation. The paper aims to analyze and fix this issue.

Method: PAC-style generalization bound analysis to explain FID degradation; propose EMPURPLE, a training-free method that recycles intermediate latents from the original model.

Result: EMPURPLE improves FID by 7% to 20% across DMD2, Hyper-SD, FlashSD, and SDXL-Lightning.

Conclusion: Aggressive early-step velocity redirection causes distribution mismatch in distilled diffusion models; EMPURPLE effectively mitigates this without additional training.

Abstract: Diffusion models achieve impressive image-generation quality but remain expensive at inference time. Diffusion distillation reduces sampling steps, yet many distilled models, including SDXL-Lightning and distribution matching distillation methods, suffer from degraded Fréchet Inception Distance (FID). We analyze this phenomenon through a PAC-style generalization bound. Our analysis suggests that aggressive early-step redirection of the velocity field makes the distillation target harder to learn, enlarging the train-test gap. As a result, early-step output distributions differ between training and inference, causing distribution mismatch in the intermediate noisy latent used as next-step inputs. We empirically validate this mechanism by showing reduced diversity in both intermediate features and final outputs. To address this issue, we propose EMPURPLE, a simple training-free method that recycles intermediate latents sampled from the original model. EMPURPLE is model-agnostic and improves FID by 7\% to 20\% across DMD2, Hyper-SD, FlashSD, and SDXL-Lightning. The repo is: https://github.com/TheLovesOfLadyPurple/Empurple-Training-Free-Algorithm-To-enhance-Diversity-of-The-Diffusion-Distillation-Model

</details>


### [215] [AquaStereo: Enabling Underwater Stereo Matching via Depth-Conditioned Diffusion and Geometry Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04303)
*Qizhe Wei,Yingping Liang,Shaodi You,Ying Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AquaStereo is a stereo matching framework for underwater environments that uses a depth-conditioned diffusion pipeline for data simulation and a self-distillation strategy with perception-enhanced features to address data scarcity and feature degradation, showing improved robustness and zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning-based stereo matching models struggle in underwater environments due to scarce in-domain data and difficulty extracting discriminative correspondences from degraded imagery.

Method: 1) A depth-conditioned diffusion pipeline renders underwater stereo pairs with a left-right consistency module for geometric alignment. 2) A self-distillation strategy where a frozen binocular teacher (trained on clean terrestrial pairs) guides a student exposed to rendered underwater pairs with perturbations, using stage-weighted sequence loss and clean-branch supervision. 3) Learnable perception frames that fuse temporal cues from auxiliary views (encoded by a video backbone) with semantic features from a strong image encoder to build robust matching descriptors.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate substantially improved robustness and zero-shot generalization in challenging underwater scenarios.

Conclusion: AquaStereo effectively narrows the terrestrial-underwater domain gap and enhances feature stability under turbidity and low texture, enabling reliable stereo matching in underwater environments.

Abstract: Learning-based stereo matching models struggle in underwater environments due to scarce in-domain data and the difficulty of extracting discriminative correspondences from degraded imagery. In this work, we present $\textbf{AquaStereo}$, a perception-enhanced framework with a data simulation pipeline and a self-distillation strategy that jointly address data scarcity and feature degradation in underwater stereo matching. First, a depth-conditioned diffusion pipeline renders underwater stereo pairs while preserving binocular geometry, with a lightweight left-right consistency module ensuring geometric alignment. Training on this synthetic corpus effectively narrows the terrestrial-underwater gap and improves zero-shot robustness. Second, a frozen binocular teacher trained on clean terrestrial pairs guides a student exposed to rendered underwater pairs with perturbations. A stage-weighted sequence loss is performed to align the student's disparities with the teacher's geometry, while a clean-branch supervision with shared pseudo targets prevents scale drift. To further enhance feature stability under turbidity and low texture, we introduce learnable perception frames, a perception-enhanced feature formulation that constructs robust matching descriptors by fusing temporal cues from two auxiliary views encoded by a video backbone with semantic features extracted by a strong image encoder. Extensive experiments demonstrate that $\textbf{AquaStereo}$ substantially improves robustness and zero-shot generalization in challenging underwater scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/qz-wei/AquaStereo.

</details>


### [216] [Road-Aware Anomaly Segmentation with Query-Guided Polygons and CLIP in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04304)
*Zhiran Yan,Gordon Elger*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a lightweight, postprocessing anomaly segmentation framework for autonomous driving that requires no retraining, no OOD data, and no auxiliary supervision. It builds on mask transformers with query-level confidence, polygonal road priors, and CLIP-based zero-shot filtering to detect road anomalies. The method outperforms the training-free baseline Maskomaly on most metrics and achieves state-of-the-art AP on Fishyscapes LostAndFound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional semantic segmentation models fail to recognize unknown or unexpected objects in open-world autonomous driving scenarios, leading to safety risks from misclassified or overlooked out-of-distribution road anomalies.

Method: A lightweight, postprocessing framework combining: (1) mask transformer query-level confidence analysis, (2) polygonal road prior to detect gap regions, and (3) CLIP-based zero-shot semantic filtering using in-distribution and optional OOD prompts to suppress false positives.

Result: Outperforms training-free baseline Maskomaly on most metrics across Fishyscapes, SMIYC, and RoadAnomaly benchmarks; achieves highest AP on Fishyscapes LostAndFound.

Conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates strong practicality and deployability for real-world autonomous driving systems, offering robust and interpretable anomaly predictions without requiring retraining or additional data.

Abstract: Traditional semantic segmentation models operate under a closed-set assumption and struggle to recognize unknown or unexpected objects-an essential capability for autonomous driving. As a result, such models often misclassify or overlook out-of-distribution (OOD) road anomalies, posing safety risks in open-world environments. We present a lightweight, postprocessing, road-aware anomaly segmentation framework that requires no retraining, no OOD data, and no auxiliary supervision. Our approach builds on a mask transformer-based segmentation network by exploiting query-level mask confidence and deriving a polygonal road prior to detect gap regions that may correspond to anomalies. To further suppress false positives, we introduce a CLIP-based zero-shot semantic filtering module using in-distribution prompts, with optional generalized OOD prompts. By jointly leveraging spatial priors and semantic verification, our framework produces robust and interpretable anomaly predictions. Evaluation on three public benchmarks-Fishyscapes, SMIYC, and RoadAnomaly-shows consistently strong performance. In particular, our method outperforms the training-free baseline Maskomaly on most metrics and achieves the highest AP on Fishyscapes LostAndFound. These results demonstrate the practicality and deployability of our approach for real-world autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [217] [Aura: Consistent Multi-Subject Video Generation via VLM-Grounded Semantic Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04311)
*Zixiang Zhou,Zhentao Yu,Yifeng Ma,Hongmei Wang,Wenqing Yu,Cong Wang,Zilin Yang,Rui Chen,Jiarong Ou,Yezhou Liu,Yuan Zhou,Qinglin Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Aura is a unified framework for identity-consistent, multi-subject video generation using AI director-level captions, VLM with learnable queries, two-stage feature alignment, subject-aware RoPE-Shift, and Progressive-APG, achieving SOTA results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video generation methods struggle to preserve identity consistency and model complex relationships among multiple subjects in controllable video synthesis.

Method: Aura introduces (1) AI director-level captions for dense scene descriptions, (2) VLM with learnable queries for multimodal feature extraction, (3) two-stage alignment strategy bridging VLM and DiT features, (4) token concatenation for visual conditioning, (5) subject-aware RoPE-Shift mechanism to distinguish heterogeneous subjects, (6) subject-aware learnable tokens, (7) Memory Tokens for balanced training, and (8) Progressive-APG for improved inference.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on both single-subject generation and multi-element video scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed unified framework effectively addresses identity consistency and complex subject interaction challenges in multi-element video generation.

Abstract: Subject-driven and multi-element video generation are central to controllable video synthesis, but existing methods still struggle to preserve identity consistency and model complex relationships among multiple subjects. In this paper, we propose Aura, a unified framework for high-fidelity and identity-consistent video generation. To better capture scene dynamics and subject interactions, we introduce AI director-level captions that provide dense and structured descriptions of video content. We further leverage a vision-language model (VLM) with learnable queries to extract multimodal semantic features from textual and visual references, covering both global semantics and fine-grained visual cues. To bridge the representational gap between the VLM and the Diffusion Transformer (DiT), we design a two-stage alignment strategy that progressively maps VLM features into the DiT feature space. For visual conditioning, we adopt token concatenation to inject reference information directly into the generation process. To distinguish heterogeneous subject types and reduce common copy-paste artifacts, we develop a subject-aware RoPE-Shift mechanism. To further differentiate reference images of different categories, we introduce subject-aware learnable tokens. In addition, we introduce Memory Tokens to balance the training signal across examples with different numbers of reference subjects. During inference, Progressive-APG (Adaptive Prompt Guidance) further alleviates oversaturation and improves semantic alignment with user prompts. Finally, we build a high-quality video-subject image dataset through a dedicated data construction pipeline. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both single-subject generation and more challenging multi-element scenarios.

</details>


### [218] [Framework and Multi-modal Dataset for Roadwork Zone Detection and Geo-localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04330)
*Zhiran Yan,Yutong Xin,S Shyam Shenoi,Rui Song,Gordon Elger*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces the RZDG dataset (simulated + real-world) for detecting and geo-localizing roadwork zones in autonomous driving, along with a tracker-based pipeline (extended AB3DMOT) that achieves reasonable accuracy (F1: 0.597 real, 0.665 simulated) for converting local object positions to global coordinates within 1-meter error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HD maps used by autonomous vehicles are outdated and lack semi-static roadwork zone information; no public dataset exists for evaluating roadwork zone detection and geo-localization models.

Method: They created the RZDG dataset (simulated + real multimodal data) and proposed a tracker-based pipeline extending AB3DMOT for roadwork zone detection and geo-localization.

Result: Precision: 0.565 (real) / 0.615 (sim); Recall: 0.898 (real) / 0.809 (sim); F1-score: 0.597 (real) / 0.665 (sim) at 1-meter TP threshold.

Conclusion: The approach effectively detects roadwork zones and transforms positions from local to global coordinates, with higher F1 on simulated data but better recall on real data.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles often rely on high-definition (HD) maps for navigation; however, these maps are not frequently updated and often lack semi-static information, such as temporary roadwork zones, which can significantly alter the road network. This limitation underscores the urgent need for an accurate global position of roadwork zones. However, the absence of publicly available datasets for evaluating roadwork zone detection and geo-localization models has hindered the development of reliable autonomous driving systems. To address this challenge, we propose the Roadwork Zone Detection and Geo-localization (RZDG) dataset, which includes both simulated and real-world data, providing multimodal sensor inputs along with comprehensive annotations. The dataset supports multiple perception tasks, including image semantic segmentation, 3D object detection, and object geo-localization. In addition, we introduce a tracker-based roadwork zone detection and geo-localization (RZDG) pipeline, an extension of AB3DMOT, for accurate object geo-localization in roadwork zones. We benchmark our approach on the RZDG dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting roadwork zones and transforming object positions from the local coordinate system to the global coordinate system. A prediction is considered a true positive (TP) if its estimated position falls within one meter of the ground truth. Our experimental results show that our approach achieves high accuracy on both real and simulated data. Specifically, we report: Precision: 0.565 (real) / 0.615 (simulated) Recall: 0.898 (real) / 0.809 (simulated) F1-score: 0.597 (real) / 0.665 (simulated).

</details>


### [219] [IRIS: An Intelligent Vision-Language System for Ocular Surface Diseases via Topic Tree and Scene-Driven VQA Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04344)
*Hao Wei,Wenjin Qi,Dasen Dai,Minqing Zhang,Wu Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IRIS introduces a dual-branch data generation paradigm (Topic Finding Tree + Scene-driven dialogues) to create IRIS-120K, a high-quality OSD VQA dataset, and achieves SOTA performance with a compact 4B VLM by injecting structured clinical knowledge instead of relying on large parameter counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs struggle with clinical reasoning in ocular surface diseases due to lack of high-fidelity, multimodal instruction-tuning data. Conventional image-caption pairs suffer from semantic shallowness, and scaling parameters alone is insufficient.

Method: Proposes IRIS system with: (1) IRIS-120K dataset curated via dual-branch data engine; (2) Topic Finding Tree (TFT) hierarchically aligning visual features to anatomical/pathological concepts; (3) Scene-driven strategy generating role-adaptive clinical dialogues; (4) fine-tuning a compact 4B VLM on this enriched corpus.

Result: State-of-the-art performance, comprehensively outperforming both generalist and specialized medical VLMs up to 34B parameters. Demonstrates structured knowledge injection beats sheer parameter scaling.

Conclusion: Structured knowledge injection is more effective than parameter scaling for specialized medical domains; enables resource-efficient, expert-level AI deployment on mobile edge devices for scalable OSD screening.

Abstract: While Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate remarkable generic capabilities, their clinical reasoning in specialized domains like ocular surface diseases (OSDs) is severely hindered by a paucity of high-fidelity, multimodal instruction-tuning data. To dismantle this data bottleneck, we introduce IRIS, an Intelligent Recognition and Interaction System tailored for fine-grained OSD understanding via external eye photography. First, we curate IRIS-120K, the largest and most comprehensive OSD visual question-answering (VQA) dataset to date. Crucially, to overcome the semantic shallowness of conventional image-caption pairs, we propose a synergistic data generation paradigm to explicitly inject clinical priors. Our data engine operates via a dual-branch framework: 1) a Topic Finding Tree (TFT) that hierarchically anchors visual features to precise anatomical and pathological concepts, enforcing rigorous medical deduction logic; and 2) a Scene-driven strategy that synthesizes role-adaptive clinical dialogues to ensure pragmatic generalization. By explicitly aligning a compact 4B-parameter VLM on this structurally enriched corpus, IRIS achieves state-of-the-art performance, comprehensively outperforming both generalist and specialized medical VLMs with up to 34B parameters. Our findings underscore that structured knowledge injection profoundly prevails over sheer parameter scaling, unlocking the potential for resource-efficient, expert-level AI deployment on mobile edge devices for scalable OSD screening. Code, datasets, and model weights will be publicly released by this repo.

</details>


### [220] [Last-Meter Precision Navigation for UAVs: A Diffusion-Refined Aerial Visual Servoing Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04352)
*Yaxuan Li,Jiarui Zeng,Shaofei Huang,Zhedong Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DreamNav is a coarse-to-fine diffusion-refined aerial visual servoing framework for UAV last-meter precision navigation (within 10 meters) using monocular vision. It uses a trigonometric regression policy for coarse rotation estimation, then a diffusion-based world model to simulate and select optimal trajectories. It also contributes PairUAV, a 4.8M image-pair benchmark across 72 scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAV last-meter precision navigation is challenging due to scale ambiguity, rotation discontinuities, and the need for fine-grained spatial reasoning. Existing methods fail under large viewpoint changes and lack generalization to unseen environments.

Method: DreamNav has two stages: (1) Coarse-estimation stage using a robust regression policy with trigonometric parameterization (joint sine/cosine modeling) to predict rotation robustly. (2) Diffusion-refined stage leveraging a pre-trained world model to simulate future visual observations for candidate actions, selecting the trajectory minimizing visual discrepancy with the target via visual imagination.

Result: DreamNav outperforms strong visual servoing and foundation model baselines in accuracy and generalization, with zero-shot transfer to unseen scenes. The PairUAV benchmark (4.8M image pairs, 72 scenes) is introduced for evaluation.

Conclusion: The proposed coarse-to-fine diffusion-refined approach effectively addresses the key challenges in last-meter UAV navigation, achieving superior performance and generalization through trigonometric pose estimation and diffusion-based trajectory optimization.

Abstract: In this work, we study the last-meter precision navigation for UAVs, e.g., autonomously reaching a target within the final 10 meters using monocular vision. This task is challenging due to scale ambiguity, rotation discontinuities, and the need for fine-grained spatial reasoning. Existing methods often fail under large viewpoint changes or lack generalization to unseen environments. To this end, we propose DreamNav, a coarse-to-fine diffusion-refined aerial visual servoing framework. In the first coarse-estimation stage, a robust regression policy employs a trigonometric parameterization to predict rotation by jointly modeling sine and cosine components, effectively mitigating optimization instabilities caused by angular periodicity. Given this coarse estimate, the second diffusion-refined stage utilizes a pre-trained world model to simulate future visual observations for candidate actions, selecting the trajectory that minimizes visual discrepancy with the target through a process of visual imagination. To support rigorous evaluation, we contribute PairUAV, a large-scale benchmark comprising 4.8 million image pairs across 72 scenes, curated from the University-1652 dataset. Extensive experiments show DreamNav outperforms strong visual servoing and foundation model baselines in accuracy and generalization, with zero-shot transfer to unseen scenes.

</details>


### [221] [HASSL: Hierarchy-Aware Self-Supervised Learning Framework for Single Cell Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04353)
*Julius Riel,Vishwa Mohan Singh,Sai Anirudh Aryasomayajula,Anuun Chinbat,Hannes Leonhard,Moritz Ladenburger,Frederik Alexander,Vishisht Choudhary,Fabio Laredo,Giacomo Masserdotti,Thorben Prein,Carsten Marr,Amirhossein Kardoost*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchy-aware self-supervised learning framework for cell images that improves fine-grained morphological clustering using distillation and HDBSCAN-based contrastive loss, achieving significant gains across multiple downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-supervised learning models suppress hierarchical structure in image data (e.g., cell images), overemphasizing coarse factors like imaging modality while obscuring fine-grained morphological attributes.

Method: Combines (1) a distillation framework with a segmentation teacher to improve morphological awareness, and (2) a hierarchy-aware contrastive loss based on HDBSCAN to refine decision boundaries between subtypes at different hierarchical levels.

Result: Evaluated on 2.3M single cells (20 datasets, 208 classes): +2.8% top-K accuracy, +6.3% top-9 retrieval on deepest hierarchy, +7.8% F1-score for drug classification.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully aligns embeddings with semantic and morphological cues, producing biologically meaningful sub-clusters driven by fine details.

Abstract: Hierarchical structure is common in image data, where fine-grained clusters often merge into larger, coarser semantic groups. In biological cell images, current self-supervised learning models often suppress this hierarchy, as coarse factors such as imaging modality can obscure finer morphological attributes in the latent space. We propose a hierarchy-aware self-supervised training framework to address this problem. Our method combines two components: a distillation framework with a segmentation teacher to improve morphological awareness in the latent space, and a hierarchy-aware contrastive loss based on HDBSCAN to improve decision boundaries between closely related subtypes at different hierarchical levels. Together, these components reduce the tendency of self-supervised learning to overemphasize coarse factors and instead align embeddings with semantic and morphological cues. This yields biologically meaningful sub-clusters driven by fine morphological detail. We train and evaluate our method on a curated corpus of 2.3 million single cells aggregated from 20 microscopy datasets, both labeled and unlabeled, covering 208 cell classes. Our method improves over baseline and counterpart methods, increasing average top-K accuracy by 2.8%, top-9 retrieval on the dataset with the deepest hierarchy by 6.3%, and downstream F1-score for biologically relevant drug classification from perturbed cell morphology by 7.8%.

</details>


### [222] [Event Detection in Videos: A Framework for the Development of New Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04372)
*Anastasia Zakharova,Thierry Bouwmans,Anthony Cioppa,Adrien Deliège,Antonio Greco,Anaïs Halin,Kamil Jeziorek,Meghna Kapoor,Tomasz Kryjak,Islam Osman,Sébastien Piérard,Carlo Sansone,Mohamed S. Shehata,Renaud Vandeghen,Marc Van Droogenbroeck,Bruno Vento*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper highlights the lack of standardized datasets and evaluation methods in video event detection and proposes a rigorous three-pillar framework (datasets, performance evaluation, deployment scenarios) to help researchers position and develop new methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing event detection methods are diverse and difficult to compare due to the absence of large-scale datasets and rigorous performance evaluation standards, which biases both comparisons and method development.

Method: Proposes a framework based on three pillars: (1) datasets, (2) performance evaluation, and (3) deployment scenarios for developing and positioning event detection methods.

Result: Not explicitly presented in this abstract; the paper introduces the framework concept rather than experimental results.

Conclusion: A rigorous framework is essential for fair comparison and structured development of video event detection methods across diverse approaches and applications.

Abstract: Event detection tasks in videos, the most important aspect of video surveillance, aim to detect events either at the pixel-level, frame-level, or clip-level. Plenty of methods intended for event detection in different environments, for various applications, and within different acquisition techniques were introduced. Naturally, the attempts were made as well to classify these algorithms in terms of detection of performance or in terms of real-time abilities. Nevertheless, the lack of a large-scale dataset as well as rigorous performance evaluation methods have biased such comparisons as well as the development of the methods.
  Given the diversity of existing approaches, we believe it is essential for researchers to position their work within such a rich landscape. Thus, we propose a rigorous framework for developing new methods in event detection for videos. Specifically, this framework is based on three main pillars: datasets, performance evaluation, and scenarios for deploying methods.

</details>


### [223] [The Good, the Bad, and the Brittle: Benchmarking Robustness and Generalisation of Histopathology Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04401)
*Dhyey Yajnik,Amina Asif,Fayyaz Minhas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks 12 pathology foundation models (PFMs) against CNNs using REET and NR-Kfold protocols, finding that PFMs outperform CNNs in robustness but show diminishing returns with scaling—mid-sized models like UNI2/Virchow-2 match or exceed larger ones—suggesting future efforts should prioritize data quality, multimodality, and domain alignment over parameter count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess how robust and generalisable pathology foundation models are, and whether their scaling limits have been reached, given the need for clinically reliable AI systems under realistic distribution shifts.

Method: Benchmarked 12 PFMs and ResNet baselines using REET across 11 clinically realistic perturbations and a dissimilarity-driven NR-Kfold validation protocol, with a Perturbation Performance Index (PPI) to summarize accuracy trends under controlled perturbation sweeps.

Result: PFMs consistently outperform CNNs in robustness and domain generalisation. However, model scaling shows diminishing returns: mid-sized models (e.g., UNI2, Virchow-2) achieve comparable or greater resilience than larger systems. NR-Kfold reveals systematic accuracy loss and increased variability under distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Next-generation pathology foundation models should prioritize data quality, multimodality information, and domain alignment over parameter count to achieve genuine clinical reliability.

Abstract: How robust and generalisable are pathology foundation models and have their scaling limites been reached? We benchmarked twelve pathology foundation models (PFMs) and ResNet baselines using our Robustness Evaluation and Enhancement Toolbox (REET) across eleven clinically realistic perturbations and a dissimilarity-driven Non-Redundant K-fold validation (NR-Kfold) protocol. We introduce a Perturbation Performance Index (PPI) to summarise accuracy trends under controlled perturbation sweeps and analyse robustness scaling with parameter count. We show that PFMs consistently outperform CNNs in both robustness and domain generalisation, yet model scaling shows diminishing returns: mid-sized models such (UNI2/Virchow-2 etc.) achieve comparable or greater resilience than larger systems. NR-Kfold analysis further reveals systematic accuracy loss and increased variability when training-test similarity is broken, underscoring the need for explicit distribution-shift evaluation. These findings suggest that the next generation of pathology foundation models must prioritise data quality, multimodality information and domain alignment over parameter count to achieve genuine clinical reliability.

</details>


### [224] [Transferability Between Understanding and Generation in Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04423)
*Jiwon Kang,Heeji Yoon,Jaewoo Jung,Jaewon Min,Minkyeong Jeon,Biyeon Hwang,Sangwon Jung,Seungryong Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper investigates how image understanding and generation tasks transfer benefits to each other in Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs), finding that transferability depends on architectural design (fully shared vs. loosely coupled). The authors propose training understanding tasks to improve generative capabilities while avoiding distribution shift and visual quality degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how understanding and generation tasks interact in UMMs, as this cross-task transferability remains understudied despite the growing adoption of unified architectures.

Method: Controlled experiments comparing architectures with fully shared transformer backbone + unified visual encoder vs. loosely coupled designs, testing transferability across three capabilities: counting, spatial relation, and text recognition/generation.

Result: Transferability is architecture-dependent: fully shared backbones exhibit consistent cross-task transfer, while loosely coupled designs show little or none. Training understanding tasks can improve generative capabilities without causing distribution shift or visual quality degradation.

Conclusion: Cross-task transferability can be systematically exploited in UMMs as a practical training strategy—improving understanding tasks rather than directly fine-tuning generation yields better generative performance while preserving visual quality.

Abstract: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) integrate image understanding and generation within a single architecture, yet how the two tasks interact remains understudied. We investigate $\boldsymbol{\mathsf{transferability}}$ in UMMs: whether training a capability on one task improves the same capability on the other without explicit supervision. Through controlled experiments, we empirically find that transferability depends on architecture-models with fully shared transformer backbone and a unified visual encoder exhibit consistent cross-task transfer, while loosely coupled designs show little or none. Leveraging this transferability, we propose a practical training strategy. The most straightforward way to improve a target generative capability (e.g., counting) is to fine-tune generation directly, but this can degrade visual quality due to distribution shift. Instead, we train the corresponding understanding task and let it transfer into generation, which improves capability-specific generative performance while minimizing distribution shift. We validate this across three capabilities-counting, spatial relation, and text recognition/generation-showing that cross-task transferability can be systematically exploited in UMMs.

</details>


### [225] [ResearchStudio-Reel: Automate the Last Mile of Research from Paper to Poster, Video, and Blog](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04438)
*Lingao Xiao,Yalun Dai,Yangyu Huang,Qihao Zhao,Wenshan Wu,Hugo He,Ruishuo Chen,Jin Jiang,Qianli Ma,Jiahuan Zhang,Xin Zhang,Ying Xin,Yang Ou,Yan Xia,Scarlett Li,Longbo Huang,Zhipeng Zhang,Yang He,Yap Kim Hui,Yan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ResearchStudio-Reel is a five-skill system that extracts a paper once and generates editable, consistent poster, video, and blog artifacts with a shared HTML viewer, outperforming prior methods on the Paper2Poster benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Research dissemination is still a manual last-mile process. Prior automation treats each artifact separately, produces non-editable outputs, and relies on soft VLM-preference scores that plateau while important sections remain empty.

Method: The system is built as a composition of skills with agent-readable contracts: one shared extractor (Paper2Assets), three editable generators (Paper2Poster, Paper2Video, Paper2Blog), and one interactive convergence layer (Paper2Reel). It uses a measured-fill loop with hard pass/fail render gates.

Result: On the Paper2Poster benchmark, the system outperforms prior automated systems and single-shot frontier LLMs on every aesthetic and information sub-criterion, surpassing authors' own posters on aesthetics (84%-93% win rate). It is the only pipeline that ships all three editable artifacts.

Conclusion: The composition-of-skills approach with shared extraction and editable generators produces higher-quality, editable research dissemination artifacts than prior methods that treat each artifact in isolation.

Abstract: Research dissemination, turning a paper into a poster, a talk video, and a blog post, is still a manual last mile. Prior automation treats each artifact in isolation that each re-extract the paper from scratch, usually ship one-way renders the author cannot reopen in PowerPoint or Word, and gates quality on soft VLM-preference scores that plateau while load-bearing sections still read as empty. We argue this last mile is best built as a composition of skills: thin agent-readable contracts that share one upstream extractor and wrap deterministic primitives in a measured-fill loop whose exits are hard pass/fail render gates. We instantiate this as ResearchStudio-Reel, five Claude Code and Codex skills organized into one shared extractor (Paper2Assets), three editable generators (Paper2Poster, Paper2Video, Paper2Blog), and one interactive convergence layer (Paper2Reel). Paper2Assets extracts each paper once into a shared bundle that can be reused by every downstream skill; The three generators produce a print-ready poster, a synchronized talk video, and a bilingual blog that stay factually consistent and round-trip through PowerPoint or Word; Paper2Reel then binds all three into a self-contained HTML viewer whose section-level clicks jump the video, slides, captions, and blog to matching content. On the Paper2Poster benchmark, our posters lead every aesthetic and information sub-criterion against both prior automated systems and single-shot frontier LLMs, surpassing the authors' own on aesthetics under two held-out VLM judges and winning overall on 84% to 93% of papers; capability audits further show that, by uniquely pairing narration-aligned on-slide highlights with a bilingual blog gated by layout-aware DOCX repair, ResearchStudio-Reel is the only pipeline to ship all three editable artifacts. Project is available at https://aka.ms/ResearchStudio

</details>


### [226] [Wan-Streamer v0.2: Higher Resolution, Same Latency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04443)
*Lianghua Huang,Zhi-Fan Wu,Yupeng Shi,Wei Wang,Mengyang Feng,Junjie He,Chen-Wei Xie,Yu Liu,Jingren Zhou,Ang Wang,Bang Zhang,Baole Ai,Chen Liang,Cheng Yu,Chongyang Zhong,Jinwei Qi,Kai Zhu,Pandeng Li,Peng Zhang,Wenyuan Zhang,Xinhua Cheng,Yitong Huang,Yun Zheng,Yuxiang Bao,Yuzheng Wang,Zoubin Bi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Wan-Streamer v0.2 upgrades a real-time audio-visual interaction model from 192x336 to 640x368 resolution while preserving ~200ms signal-to-signal latency, using a thinker-performer architecture where the thinker handles low-latency streaming on a single GPU and the performer distributes high-resolution video generation across multiple GPUs via Ulysses-style context parallelism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To support higher-resolution video streams (640x368) in real-time conversational AI agents, preserving legibility of posture, gaze, hands, objects, and scene layout without introducing additional user-visible delay.

Method: The architecture splits into a single-GPU "thinker" (streaming perception, language/state Transformer, cache building, decoding) and a multi-GPU "performer" group using Ulysses-style context parallelism for next-unit latent generation. Long high-resolution latent video sequences are sharded across ranks for denoising and gathered via Ulysses communication, while audio latents remain unsharded. The thinker only passes K/V conditioning to the performer, avoiding communication of separate language sequences.

Result: Achieves 640x368 resolution output at 25 FPS with ~200ms model-side signal-to-signal latency. Including a 350ms bidirectional network budget, total remote interaction latency is ~550ms.

Conclusion: Wan-Streamer v0.2 demonstrates that higher-resolution real-time audio-visual interaction is feasible without latency penalty by distributing visual generation across multiple GPUs while keeping the thinker-performer boundary compact.

Abstract: We present Wan-Streamer v0.2, a latency-preserving upgrade of the native-streaming, end-to-end audio-visual interaction model. v0.2 keeps the v0.1 modeling formulation, but raises the interactive output stream from 192x336 to 640x368 while preserving approximately 200 ms model-side signal-to-signal latency at 25 FPS. The higher-resolution stream supports scene-grounded mid-shot agents whose posture, gaze, hands, nearby objects, and local scene layout remain legible during real-time conversation. To support the larger visual stream without adding user-visible delay, v0.2 keeps the thinker as a single-GPU low-latency path for streaming perception, the short language/state Transformer pass that builds the generation cache, and final decoding. The performer becomes a multi-GPU Ulysses-style context-parallel group for the expensive next-unit latent generation. Each performer rank writes incoming K/V into a pre-sharded local cache. The long high-resolution latent video sequence is split across ranks for denoising and gathered through Ulysses communication, while the much shorter audio latent sequence is generated without sequence sharding. In this split, the thinker's language/state computation reaches the performer only as K/V conditioning, so no separate language sequence has to be communicated inside the performer group. This concentrates additional hardware on visual generation while preserving the compact thinker-performer boundary, keeping total remote interaction latency at approximately 550 ms when a 350 ms bidirectional network budget is included.

</details>


### [227] [Fields of the Planet: Field Boundary Mapping Beyond 10m](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04449)
*Isaac Corley,Caleb Robinson,Jennifer Marcus,Hannah Kerner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces Fields of the Planet (FTP), a 3m-resolution PlanetScope satellite dataset for field boundary mapping, paired with existing Sentinel-2 data. FTP covers 24 countries with 133,168 targets and shows that 3m imagery significantly improves field delineation quality over 10m Sentinel-2 data, especially for small parcels under 0.5 hectares.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many smallholder agricultural parcels are only a few 10m Sentinel-2 pixels wide, making accurate field boundary mapping difficult. Existing datasets lack high-resolution global coverage for this task.

Method: The authors create FTP as a 3m PlanetScope companion to Fields of The World (FTW), using the same polygons, seasonal windows, and train/test splits. They co-register 133,168 PlanetScope patch-window targets across 24 countries. Evaluation uses panoptic quality (PQ), object F1, size-stratified PQ, and meter-scale matched-boundary error after vectorizing predictions.

Result: Under matched architectures and training, 3m imagery raises PQ from 21.0 to 35.5, sub-0.5 ha field PQ from 5.8 to 15.7, and reduces matched-boundary error from 18.6m to 7.4m.

Conclusion: Higher-resolution 3m PlanetScope imagery substantially improves field delineation accuracy compared to 10m Sentinel-2, particularly benefiting smallholder agriculture mapping.

Abstract: Field-boundary maps support crop monitoring, irrigation planning, and yield estimation, but many smallholder parcels span only a few 10 m Sentinel-2 pixels. We introduce Fields of the Planet (FTP), a 3 m PlanetScope companion to Fields of The World (FTW) that pairs the same polygons, seasonal windows, and train/test splits with 133,168 co-registered PlanetScope patch-window targets across 24 countries. FTP evaluates field delineation as parcel recovery by vectorizing predictions before scoring panoptic quality (PQ), object F1, size-stratified PQ, and meter-scale matched-boundary error. Under matched architectures and training recipes, 3 m imagery raises PQ from 21.0 to 35.5, raises PQ on sub-0.5 ha fields from 5.8 to 15.7, and cuts matched-boundary error from 18.6 m to 7.4 m.

</details>


### [228] [CCFM: Collision-Constrained Flow Matching for Safety-Critical Scenario Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04451)
*Ke Li,Kaidi Liang,Yuxin Ding,Debojyoti Biswas,Xianbiao Hu,Ruwen Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CCFM is a framework that generates safety-critical driving scenarios with precise collision control by using hard physical constraints (via flow matching and Gauss-Newton projection), achieving high collision rates while maintaining realistic behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches use soft guidance (probabilistic preferences) that cannot guarantee geometric/severity constraints for specific collision types, making controllable safety-critical scenario generation challenging.

Method: CCFM has three components: (1) heuristic collision selector for optimal adversarial agent/collision type via composite scoring; (2) structured hard constraints defining four collision types (rear-end, side, cut-in, head-on); (3) collision-constrained flow matching sampler enforcing constraints via Gauss-Newton manifold projection.

Result: Achieves collision rates of 46.4% on nuScenes and 83.1% on nuPlan, significantly outperforming baselines while maintaining realistic driving behavior.

Conclusion: CCFM provides a reliable foundation for AV safety evaluation and sim-to-real crash data generation by enabling controllable collision characteristics.

Abstract: Evaluation of autonomous vehicle (AV) planners in safety-critical closed-loop simulation is essential for real-world deployment. However, generating controllable safety-critical scenarios remains challenging. Existing approaches use soft guidance that provides only probabilistic preferences and cannot guarantee the satisfaction of geometric and severity constraints associated with specific collision types. We introduce Collision-Constrained Flow Matching (CCFM), a novel framework that guarantees precise collision control through hard physical constraints. CCFM consists of three key components: (i) a heuristic collision selector that optimally identifies an adversarial agent and collision type via composite scoring; (ii) structured hard constraints that explicitly define four collision types (rear-end, side, cut-in, head-on) through contact point, heading, and severity requirements; and (iii) a collision-constrained flow matching sampler that enforces the constraints via Gauss-Newton manifold projection. CCFM achieves collision rate up to 46.4% on nuScenes and 83.1% on nuPlan, significantly outperforming baselines while preserving realistic driving behavior. By enabling controllable collision characteristics in safety-critical scenario generation, CCFM provides a reliable foundation for AV safety evaluation and sim-to-real crash data generation. The code and implementation details are available at https://github.com/KELISBU/CCFM.

</details>


### [229] [Spatial Graph Representation and Morphometric Analysis of the Pulmonary Vascular Tree From Computed Tomography Using Multi-Scale Hessian-Based Filter Fusion and TEASAR Skeletonization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04457)
*Piotr Mackiewicz,Jakub Kołyska,Radoslaw Roszczyk*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a deterministic, training-free, explainable pipeline for reconstructing pulmonary vascular trees from CT images using multiscale Hessian-based filters (Frangi & Sato), Kimimaro/TEASAR centerline extraction, and graph pruning, achieving fractal dimension ~2.3 consistent with known physiology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pulmonary vascular tree reconstruction from CT is clinically important but challenging due to multi-scale vessels, limited resolution, partial volume effects, and motion artifacts. Existing deep learning methods require large annotated datasets, motivating a training-free alternative.

Method: A deterministic pipeline fusing multiscale Frangi and Sato vesselness filters (12 scales, 1-8 mm), lung parenchyma segmentation via HU thresholding + morphological post-processing + Chan-Vese active contour, Kimimaro/TEASAR centerline extraction, and separate left/right vascular graph construction with pruning and acyclicity verification.

Result: The reconstructed tree achieves a fractal dimension of ~2.3, consistent with literature values for human pulmonary vasculature. Residual deviations in branching metrics (Strahler order, Horton ratios, Murray's law) reflect distal-vessel truncation due to finite CT resolution.

Conclusion: The proposed explainable, training-free pipeline can generate geometrically plausible pulmonary vascular tree models, potentially supporting quantitative pulmonary imaging, vascular morphometry, and computational lung modeling.

Abstract: Reconstructing the pulmonary vascular tree from computed tomography (CT) images is essential for quantitative lung analysis, vascular morphology assessment, and patient-specific modeling, yet it remains challenging because vessels span multiple scales, from proximal arteries to distal microvasculature. Clinical chest CT is further affected by limited spatial resolution, partial volume effects, heterogeneous image quality, and respiratory motion artifacts. Unlike deep learning-based pulmonary vessel segmentation methods that require large annotated datasets, we propose a deterministic, training-free, and explainable pipeline for CT-based pulmonary vascular tree reconstruction. The method fuses multiscale Hessian-based Frangi and Sato vesselness filters using a weighted maximum response across 12 spatial scales from 1 to 8 mm, enabling detection of large pulmonary arteries and peripheral branches. Lung parenchyma is segmented by Hounsfield unit thresholding, morphological post-processing, and Chan-Vese active contour refinement. Vascular centerlines are extracted using the Kimimaro implementation of the TEASAR algorithm; separate left- and right-lung vascular graphs are then constructed, pruned, and verified for acyclicity. Geometric plausibility is assessed using volumetric fractal dimension, Strahler order analysis, Horton ratios, and Murray's law. The resulting fractal dimension of approximately 2.3 is consistent with reported values for the human pulmonary vasculature. At the same time, residual deviations in branching metrics reflect distal-vessel truncation caused by finite CT resolution. These results indicate that the proposed explainable pipeline can generate geometrically plausible pulmonary vascular tree models and may support quantitative pulmonary imaging, vascular morphometry, and computational lung modeling.

</details>


### [230] [Flash-BoN: Instant Drafts for Inference-Time Scaling in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04461)
*Ruchit Rawal,Reza Shirkavand,Sayak Paul,Yuxin Wen,Heng Huang,Yizheng Chen,Tom Goldstein,Gowthami Somepalli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flash-BoN improves text-to-image generation under real-time constraints by generating many cheap draft candidates (via timestep truncation, layer skipping, activation proxies), then verifying and refining the best one, outperforming more complex guided search methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current inference-time scaling methods for text-to-image generation ignore verifier overhead and treat generation cost as fixed; they compare by NFEs which masks real efficiency. Under wall-clock evaluation, simple BoN already matches or beats more complex guided search methods.

Method: Flash-BoN generates a large pool of inexpensive draft candidates using three acceleration knobs: timestep truncation, layer skipping, and activation proxies, optimized once per model. Then a multi-stage verification procedure selects the best draft, which is refined at full quality.

Result: Across three benchmarks and three model scales, Flash-BoN consistently outperforms all baselines under fixed wall-clock budgets, with gains growing at larger scales (+8% AUC). It also combines well with reflection-based prompt optimization (+16% AUC) and accelerates RL post-training convergence via increased candidate diversity.

Conclusion: Under wall-clock evaluation, compute is better spent on broader exploration (more cheap candidates) than on repeated intermediate verification. Flash-BoN offers a simple, scalable, and orthogonal approach that improves efficiency and downstream training.

Abstract: Inference-time scaling for text-to-image generation has progressed from simple Best-of-$N$ (BoN) sampling to guided search methods that verify and steer candidate trajectories at intermediate denoising steps. These approaches focus on when and how often to verify during denoising but largely treat the cost of generation itself as fixed. Moreover, the standard practice of comparing methods by number of function evaluations (NFEs) counts only denoising forward passes and ignores verifier overhead, which can distort efficiency rankings. We show that under wall-clock evaluation, simple BoN already matches or outperforms several guided search techniques, suggesting that compute is better spent on broader exploration than on repeated intermediate verification. This motivates Flash-BoN, which generates a large pool of inexpensive draft candidates by combining three complementary acceleration knobs: timestep truncation, layer skipping, and activation proxies into a single configuration optimized once per model. An efficient multi-stage verification procedure then identifies the most promising draft, which is refined at full quality. Across three benchmarks and three model scales, Flash-BoN consistently outperforms all baselines under fixed wall-clock budgets, with gains that grow at larger model scales (+8% AUC). We further show that our strategy combines well and improves existing orthogonal techniques such as reflection-based prompt optimization (+16% AUC). The gains correlate with increased candidate diversity, which also enables draft-guided selection to accelerate RL post-training convergence.

</details>


### [231] [EVAS: Efficient Multimodal Temporal Forgery Localization via Audio-Visual Synergy and Steered Boundary Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04472)
*Shen Shen,Quan Zhang,Dan Jiang,Ke Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EVAS is an end-to-end multimodal framework for temporal forgery localization in videos, using Multi-Stage Audio-Visual Synergy, Boundary-Aware Refinement, decoupled training, and HourglassFFN to achieve state-of-the-art performance on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid growth of AI-generated content demands reliable multimodal forensics. Existing methods struggle with precisely localizing sparsely distributed forged segments in long-form videos, especially when manipulations are subtly embedded and cross-modal signals are weak.

Method: (1) Multi-Stage Audio-Visual Synergy for progressive cross-modal interaction; (2) Boundary-Aware Refinement with invalid-frame masking for steered boundary calibration; (3) Decoupled training paradigm with auxiliary heads; (4) Lightweight HourglassFFN to reduce computational overhead.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art average localization accuracy and average recall across three benchmark datasets for fine-grained temporal forgery localization.

Conclusion: EVAS effectively addresses the challenge of sparse, subtle manipulation localization in long-form videos, validating the proposed multimodal framework's effectiveness and generalization.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of artificial intelligence-generated content necessitates reliable multimodal forensics. Beyond video-level binary classification, precisely localizing sparsely distributed forged segments in long-form videos remains a critical challenge. This task is particularly difficult when manipulations are subtly embedded and cross-modal signals are weak and temporally diffuse. To address these challenges, we propose EVAS, an end-to-end multimodal framework for temporal forgery localization. At its core, a Multi-Stage Audio-Visual Synergy mechanism facilitates progressive cross-modal interaction to learn deep multimodal forensic representations and capture high-order semantic traces of sparse manipulations. Furthermore, we introduce a Boundary-Aware Refinement strategy to achieve steered boundary calibration. By incorporating invalid-frame masking, this strategy suppresses ambiguous regions and sharpens transition predictions. We adopt a decoupled training paradigm with auxiliary heads to disentangle representation learning from inference objectives, enhancing model generalization and stability. Additionally, a lightweight HourglassFFN is incorporated to reduce computational overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EVAS achieves state-of-the-art average localization accuracy and average recall across three benchmark datasets, validating its effectiveness for fine-grained temporal forgery localization.

</details>


### [232] [PulmoSight-XAI: An Explainable Multi-View Attention Ensemble with Gradient Boosting Meta-Learning for Multi-Label Chest X-Ray Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04478)
*Moshiur Rahman,Shafqat Alam,Tasnia Binte Mamun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical multi-view ensemble framework using view-specific CNNs, CBAM attention, a hybrid Asymmetric+Focal loss, and two-level meta-learning (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost stacking) achieves SOTA AUROC of 0.9319 (frontal) and 0.9154 (lateral) on CheXpert-style chest X-ray classification, with comprehensive explainability via seven attribution methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address severe class imbalance, co-occurring pathologies, and loss of localized features in conventional architectures for chest X-ray classification.

Method: View-specific training with five CNNs per view, multi-scale feature fusion + CBAM attention replacing GAP, hybrid Asymmetric Loss + Adaptive Focal Loss, Level-1 gradient boosting (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost) with TTA and uncertainty, Level-2 stacking with alpha blending.

Result: State-of-the-art macro-average AUROC of 0.9319 (frontal) and 0.9154 (lateral) on CheXpert-style dataset.

Conclusion: The framework provides a transparent, highly accurate, and clinically practical CAD system with strong anatomical consistency and explainability.

Abstract: Automated chest X-ray classification remains challenging due to severe class imbalance, co-occurring pathologies, and the loss of localized features in conventional architectures. To address these, we propose an explainable hierarchical multi-view ensemble framework for the robust classification of 14 thoracic pathologies. The framework employs view-specific training by independently modeling frontal and lateral radiographs using an ensemble of five complementary convolutional neural networks. Replacing global average pooling, a multi-scale feature fusion strategy augmented with Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) preserves fine-grained intermediate representations while emphasizing high-level pathology-specific semantic features. To mitigate positive-negative imbalance and varying inter-class difficulty, models are optimized using a novel hybrid objective combining Asymmetric Loss with Adaptive Focal Loss. Beyond simple probability averaging, the framework incorporates a hierarchical meta-learning strategy where test-time augmentation (TTA) predictions and cross-model uncertainty measures are integrated into Level-1 gradient-boosting meta-learners (XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost), followed by Level-2 stacking with optimized alpha blending. Evaluated on a large-scale CheXpert-style dataset, the framework achieves state-of-the-art macro-average AUROC scores of 0.9319 for frontal and 0.9154 for lateral radiographs. Furthermore, comprehensive explainability analysis using seven post-hoc attribution techniques demonstrates strong anatomical consistency and clinically meaningful decision localization. By integrating architectural diversity, multi-scale attention, hierarchical meta-learning, and rigorous explainability, the proposed framework provides a transparent, highly accurate, and clinically practical computer-aided diagnosis system for thoracic disease classification.

</details>


### [233] [TrustCLIP: Learning Private Visual Features via Adversarial Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04484)
*Nikos Athanasiou,Ilya A. Petrov,Angela Yao,Shugao Ma,Eric Sauser,Edoardo Remelli,Shreyas Hampali,Johannes Schönberger,Fadime Sener,Bugra Tekin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TrustCLIP is a reconstruction-driven framework that protects privacy by degrading generative inversions of visual features while preserving downstream task performance in classification and multimodal LLM pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-level visual representations from vision and vision-language models can be inverted by generative models to reconstruct realistic images, raising serious privacy risks that are not captured by standard evaluation protocols.

Method: TrustCLIP learns a projection between encoder features and downstream modules optimized to degrade reconstructions from feature-conditioned generative attackers while preserving necessary signals for downstream tasks.

Result: TrustCLIP consistently reduces the fidelity of generative inversions while maintaining downstream task performance in both classification and multimodal LLM settings.

Conclusion: Directly optimizing against a generative reconstruction attacker provides effective privacy protection not addressed by prior discriminative-privacy-based defenses.

Abstract: Vision and vision-language models rely on high-level visual representations that are increasingly used across recognition, retrieval, and multimodal reasoning pipelines. However, recent advances in generative modeling have shown that such features can often be inverted, enabling realistic reconstructions of the underlying image and raising significant privacy risks. We revisit this problem through the lens of reconstruction and propose TrustCLIP, a reconstruction-driven framework that treats a feature-conditioned generator as an explicit privacy adversary. TrustCLIP learns a projection between encoder features and downstream modules that is explicitly optimized to degrade the reconstructions produced by generative attackers while retaining the necessary signals for downstream tasks. Unlike prior defenses that rely on discriminative privacy metrics, TrustCLIP directly optimizes against a generative reconstruction attacker, targeting a threat not captured by standard evaluation protocols. We demonstrate its effectiveness in both conventional classification and multimodal large language model pipelines. Across these settings, TrustCLIP consistently reduces the fidelity of generative inversions while maintaining downstream task performance. Project page: https://atnikos.github.io/trustclip/

</details>


### [234] [Enhancing Facial Expression Recognition in Head-Mounted Displays with Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04490)
*Jianing Deng,Qiang Zhou,Jingtong Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a data synthesis framework to generate HMC-view facial expression images from frontal-view images by reconstructing 3D textured meshes and using a configurable camera system, along with a texture-space alignment network (TSAN) for accurate texture sampling. Experiments show models trained on synthetic data outperform those trained on existing datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Facial expression recognition (FER) in head-mounted display (HMD) environments is important but faces data scarcity challenges: privacy concerns hinder data collection, HMD platform diversity complicates standardization, and existing FER datasets are incompatible due to unique head-mounted camera (HMC) perspectives. This lack of data prevents neural network-based methods from being effectively developed.

Method: The authors propose a data synthesis framework that: 1) reconstructs 3D textured meshes from frontal-view images, 2) applies a configurable camera system to render images from the HMC perspective, and 3) introduces a texture-space alignment network (TSAN) for accurate texture sampling to preserve facial expression details.

Result: Extensive experiments on simulated and real HMC datasets show that models trained on the synthetic dataset outperform those trained on existing datasets and demonstrate better generalization across different camera configurations.

Conclusion: The proposed data synthesis framework effectively addresses data scarcity for HMC-based FER, enabling better-performing and more generalizable models by leveraging abundant frontal-view annotated datasets.

Abstract: Facial expression recognition (FER) is crucial for social interaction in mixed reality environments that employ head-mounted displays (HMD). However, collecting FER data from head-mounted cameras (HMC) is challenging due to privacy concerns and the diversity of HMD platforms. Moreover, existing FER datasets are not directly applicable due to the unique perspectives of HMCs. The lack of sufficient data hinders the development of neural network-based HMC FER methods. To address data scarcity, we propose a data synthesis framework that generates HMC-view images from frontal-view images, leveraging abundant existing annotated datasets. Specifically, we first reconstruct 3D textured meshes from images and then apply a configurable camera system to render images from the HMC perspective. Additionally, we introduce a texture-space alignment network (TSAN) that enables accurate texture sampling from images to preserve detailed facial expressions. To evaluate the proposed method, we conduct extensive experiments on both simulated and real HMC datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that models trained on our synthetic dataset outperform those trained on existing datasets and exhibit better generalization across different camera configurations.

</details>


### [235] [UniSkip-Mamba: A Frequency-Aware State Space Model for Audio-Visual Temporal Forgery Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04498)
*Cangjin Qiu,Quan Zhang,Dan Jiang,Ke Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniSkip-Mamba is a frequency-aware State Space Model for Audio-Visual Temporal Forgery Localization that outperforms SOTA by focusing on low/mid-frequency patterns (0-0.15), achieving up to +14.32% mAP improvement with 6x faster inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TFL methods process all frequency components indiscriminately, causing overfitting on high-frequency noise and limited robustness under real-world data degradation. The authors find forgery-discriminative patterns concentrate in low/mid-frequency range (0-0.15).

Method: Proposes UniSkip-Mamba with Unified Multimodal Sequence Fusion (preserving cross-modal phase relationships) and Skip-Scanning Mamba Blocks using Group-Scan-Merge mechanism for frequency-aware regularization, biasing learning toward discriminative low/mid-frequency patterns while maintaining representational completeness.

Result: 63.4% AP@0.95 on LAV-DF (+9.8%), 63.58% mAP on AV-Deepfake1M (+14.32%), with 6x faster inference.

Conclusion: Frequency-domain analysis provides theoretical justification from a signal processing perspective for why skip-scanning inherently improves both accuracy and robustness.
