TL;DR: SECT, a topology-based false-positive reduction framework, outperforms persistence-based methods (AUC 0.943 vs ~0.68) in intracranial aneurysm detection from CTA, especially for small lesions <3 mm, and is scanner-agnostic under leave-one-scanner-out validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated IA detection from CTA suffers from high false-positive rates, especially for small aneurysms (<3 mm, sensitivity <60%), due to CNNs confusing saccular aneurysms with vascular bifurcations based on local pixel intensities.

Method: The authors propose a plug-and-play topology-aware false-positive reduction framework using Smooth Euler Characteristic Transform (SECT) — a directional representation encoding global 3D vascular geometry independent of intensity — tested on the RSNA 2025 dataset against persistence-based summaries (Persistence Images and Landscapes).

Result: SECT achieves AUC 0.943 vs ~0.68 for direction-agnostic methods; maintains 0.943 AUC and 78.5% sensitivity at 95% specificity for sub-3 mm aneurysms; achieves 0.927 mean AUC under leave-one-scanner-out validation across four manufacturers.

Conclusion: By capturing asymmetric geometric invariants rather than intensity profiles, SECT resolves the primary structural confounder in IA detection, positioning it as a robust downstream filter for hybrid deep-learning pipelines.

Abstract: Automated detection of intracranial aneurysms (IAs) from CT angiography (CTA) is severely hindered by high false-positive rates. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) rely on local pixel intensities, causing systematic confusion between saccular aneurysms and vascular bifurcations -- a problem especially acute for small lesions (<3 mm), where detection sensitivity falls below 60%. We propose a plug-and-play, topology-aware false-positive reduction framework evaluating the Smooth Euler Characteristic Transform (SECT) -- a directional representation encoding global 3D vascular geometry independently of intensity -- against persistence-based summaries (Persistence Images and Landscapes), tested on a stratified subset of the RSNA 2025 dataset. SECT achieves an AUC of 0.943, substantially outperforming direction-agnostic methods (AUC ~0.68), and exhibits a clinical performance inversion: it excels on the sub-3 mm cohort, maintaining 0.943 AUC and 78.5% sensitivity at 95% specificity. The representation is also scanner-agnostic, achieving 0.927 mean AUC under leave-one-scanner-out (LOGO) validation across four manufacturers. By capturing asymmetric geometric invariants rather than intensity profiles, SECT reliably resolves the primary structural confounder in IA detection, positioning it as a robust downstream filter for hybrid deep-learning diagnostic pipelines.

</details>


### [289] [Virtual Category-Guided Continual Generalized Category Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04984)
*Jiahui Xiong,Qiuxia Lai,Hongsong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes Virtual Category-Guided Continual Generalized Category Discovery (C-GCD) that uses Virtual Category Learning (VCL) and Expanded Neighborhood Contrastive Learning (ENCL) to handle ambiguous unlabeled samples in incremental open-world visual learning, achieving SOTA results on CIFAR-100, Tiny ImageNet, and ImageNet-100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The key motivation is that current C-GCD methods suffer from ambiguous unlabeled samples that cannot be confidently assigned to known or novel categories, leading to brittle pseudo-labeling and biased learning toward familiar classes. The authors aim to address this uncertainty without injecting noisy labels.

Method: The method adapts Virtual Category Learning (VCL) to the continual setting, identifying uncertain samples and assigning them to temporary virtual categories for safe learning. It is augmented with Expanded Neighborhood Contrastive Learning (ENCL), which uses extended neighborhood relations and an adaptive margin to learn more discriminative and well-separated representations.

Result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-100, Tiny ImageNet, and ImageNet-100 demonstrate consistent outperformance over state-of-the-art C-GCD methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach establishes a scalable and effective solution for Continual Generalized Category Discovery, effectively handling ambiguous samples and mitigating prediction bias in open-world visual learning.

Abstract: Continual Generalized Category Discovery (C-GCD) aims to incrementally identify novel categories from sequential unlabeled data while preserving recognition of known classes, which is an essential capability for open-world visual learning. A major bottleneck lies in ambiguous unlabeled samples that cannot be confidently assigned to known classes nor reliably grouped as novel ones, making pseudo-labeling brittle and often biasing learning toward familiar categories. In this work, we introduce Virtual Category-Guided Continual Generalized Category Discovery by adapting Virtual Category Learning (VCL) to the continual setting. Our method identifies uncertain samples and assigns them to temporary virtual categories, enabling safe and informative learning from unlabeled streams without injecting noisy labels, while improving unlabeled data utilization and mitigating prediction bias. To further stabilize discovery across sessions and enhance class separation, we augment VCL with Expanded Neighborhood Contrastive Learning (ENCL), which exploits extended neighborhood relations and an adaptive margin to learn more discriminative and well-separated representations for both old and emerging classes. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-100, Tiny ImageNet, and ImageNet-100 demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, establishing a scalable and effective solution for C-GCD.

</details>


### [290] [Steering Optimisation Trajectories in Diffusion Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05319)
*Rajat Rasal,Avinash Kori,Tian Xia,Ben Glocker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces SteeringDRL, which combines gated residual U-Nets with a noise-level exposure curriculum to steer diffusion autoencoders toward better disentangled representations, improving representation quality and segmentation across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why diffusion autoencoders achieve similar image quality but different latent structures, and to control the reconstruction-disentanglement trade-off during training.

Method: SteeringDRL: combines gated residual U-Nets with a simple noise-level exposure curriculum for training, targeting shortcut pathways in the diffusion U-Net and controlling early noise-level exposure.

Result: SteeringDRL improves representation quality, reduces seed sensitivity across disentanglement benchmarks, and extends to spatial disentanglement in object-centric learning, improving segmentation quality on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The reconstruction-disentanglement trade-off can be shaped by controlling noise-level exposure and shortcut pathways during training, and SteeringDRL effectively steers optimization toward stronger representations.

Abstract: We study why diffusion autoencoders can achieve similar image quality while learning substantially different latent structures. We trace this behaviour to optimisation dynamics; we analyse curves of image reconstruction against latent representation quality, revealing trajectories that organise around two distinct regimes early in training. Models in the reconstruction regime prioritise image fidelity early, whereas those in the disentanglement regime improve reconstruction and disentanglement more gradually. We hypothesise that this behaviour can be influenced by targeting shortcut pathways in the diffusion U-Net and controlling early noise-level exposure, thereby shaping the reconstruction-disentanglement trade-off during training. To steer optimisation toward stronger representations, we introduce SteeringDRL, combining gated residual U-Nets with a simple noise-level exposure curriculum for training. Across disentanglement benchmarks, SteeringDRL improves representation quality and reduces seed sensitivity. Our method further extends to spatial disentanglement in object-centric learning, improving segmentation quality on synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [291] [Geometry-aware Depth-guided Representation Learning for Structure-preserving Low-light Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05005)
*Fang Gao,Jiongkai Qin,Jiabao Wang,Jingfeng Tang,Ming Cheng,Hanbo Zheng,Qingbao Huang,Cheng Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes DMSA-Net, a depth-guided multi-scale attention network that uses depth-related structural priors from reflectance-geometry interaction to improve both low-light image enhancement and structural consistency preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing low-light enhancement methods focus on appearance restoration but fail to exploit scene geometry for preserving structural consistency, which is important for visual representation and scene understanding.

Method: DMSA-Net uses a Retinex-based decomposition module to obtain illumination-invariant reflectance, infers depth cues from it, and employs a multi-scale depth-guided fusion strategy with depth-aware attention in a hierarchical encoder-decoder to integrate geometric and appearance features.

Result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show effective low-light restoration with improved structural preservation. The authors also construct LOL-D, a depth-augmented low-light dataset.

Conclusion: DMSA-Net demonstrates that incorporating depth-guided geometry priors into low-light image enhancement can better preserve structural consistency while achieving effective restoration.

Abstract: Low-light degradation reduces image visibility and weakens structural cues that are important for visual representation and scene understanding. Existing low-light image enhancement methods mainly focus on appearance restoration, while insufficiently exploiting scene geometry to preserve structural consistency. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a Depth-guided Multi-scale Attention Network (DMSA-Net) for geometry-aware low-light image enhancement. DMSA-Net introduces depth-related structural priors into low-light representation learning through reflectance-geometry interaction. A Retinex-based decomposition module is first used to obtain illumination-invariant reflectance representations, from which depth cues are inferred to characterize scene structure under degraded illumination. A multi-scale depth-guided fusion strategy is then embedded into a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture, where depth-aware attention adaptively integrates geometric and appearance features. Experiments on several benchmark datasets show that DMSA-Net achieves effective low-light restoration while improving structural preservation. Moreover, we construct LOL-D, a depth-augmented low-light dataset, to facilitate research on geometry-aware low-light vision.

</details>


### [292] [Unsupervised Pixel-Level Semantic Left-Right Understanding of In-the-Wild Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05006)
*Weikang Wang,Tobias Weißberg,Florian Bernard*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces an unsupervised learning framework for pixel-level semantic left-right prediction in images by leveraging 3D shape datasets (human/animal shapes) and diverse image data, achieving state-of-the-art results even on unseen object categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-level semantic left-right prediction in real-world images is challenging due to lack of 3D information, occlusion, pose variation, and partiality. Existing methods lack a robust solution for this task.

Method: An unsupervised learning framework that jointly utilizes 3D shape datasets (with vertex-wise semantic left-right labels) and in-the-wild image datasets to train a model for pixel-wise left-right prediction in single-view images.

Result: The approach achieves superior performance on dense pixel-wise semantic left-right predictions compared to state-of-the-art methods, generalizing to unseen 3D object categories like cars and trains.

Conclusion: Medium-scale 3D shape datasets (human- and quadruped-like shapes) combined with diverse image data are sufficient for high-quality semantic left-right prediction, demonstrating strong cross-category generalization.

Abstract: While various works address reflective symmetry understanding in 3D data and images, pixel-level semantic left-right prediction of in-the-wild images remains challenging, due to certain difficulties including the lack of 3D information, occlusion, object pose variation, partiality, etc. In this work, we propose an unsupervised learning framework to tackle this challenge. Leveraging recent advances in vertex-wise semantic left-right understanding of 3D data, our unsupervised learning method jointly utilises 3D shape and image datasets to infer pixel-wise semantic left-right predictions in single-view images. In particular, we show that a medium-scale 3D shape dataset comprising mainly of human- and quadruped animal-like shapes, combined with diverse in-the-wild image data, are sufficient to achieve high-quality semantic left-right prediction in images, even for entirely unseen 3D object categories, such as cars or trains. Overall, our approach achieves superior performance in dense pixel-wise semantic left-right predictions on both rendered and in-the-wild image datasets when compared to existing state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [293] [Multiplayer Interactive World Models with Representation Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05352)
*Anthony Hu,Václav Volhejn,Adrien Ramanana Rahary,Chris Mulder,Aditya Makkar,Amélie Royer,Manu Orsini,Alyx Liao,Adam Jelley,Eloi Alonso,Florian Laurent,Fredrik Norén,James Swingos,Jan Hünermann,Kent Rollins,Lucas Hosseini,Matthieu Le Cauchois,Maxim Peter,Pim de Witte,Tim Brown,Vincent Micheli,Moritz Böhle,Gabriel de Marmiesse,Viktoriia Sharmanska,Lucia Specia,Michael Black,Patrick Pérez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces the first multiplayer world model, a 5-billion-parameter latent diffusion model trained on 10,000 hours of Rocket League gameplay, which generates coherent four-player matches in real time with stable rollouts lasting hours.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing world models treat other agents as part of the environment (single-player), which fails in highly dynamic, multi-agent settings with complex physical interactions. The authors aim to build a world model that conditions on multiple agents' action streams and correctly attributes scene changes to specific players.

Method: A 5-billion-parameter latent diffusion model trained on 10,000 hours of Rocket League gameplay with publicly available bots. The model uses a video codec, generative objective, and multiplayer conditioning scheme, generating 20 FPS on a single Nvidia B200 GPU.

Result: Generates four-player matches in real time; rollouts remain stable far beyond the training horizon (up to 5 minutes measured, with hours-long continuations observed); distributional quality holds steady at the longest measured horizon.

Conclusion: Multiplayer world modeling is feasible and produces stable long-horizon rollouts. The paper releases the dataset, codebase, and a live demo to support further research.

Abstract: We introduce the first multiplayer world model for highly dynamic environments governed by complex physical interactions. Whereas single-player world models treat the other agents as part of the environment, ours conditions on the action streams of multiple agents, learning to attribute changes in the scene to the correct player and to stay coherent under arbitrary combinations of their actions. We study this problem in the game of Rocket League, where players compete and cooperate under fast, tightly coupled dynamics. Trained on 10,000 hours of gameplay collected with publicly available bots, our 5-billion-parameter latent diffusion model generates four-player matches in real time, producing 20 frames per second on a single Nvidia B200 GPU. Although trained only on short clips, its rollouts stay stable far beyond the training horizon: distributional quality holds steady out to five minutes, the longest horizon we measure, and in practice we observe rollouts continuing for hours with no sign of collapse. We systematically investigate the central design choices: the video codec, the generative objective, and the multiplayer conditioning scheme. In addition, we characterize how behavior changes with model and data scale, including the capabilities that emerge and the failure modes that persist. We further develop targeted evaluations that probe the model's physical understanding rather than visual appearance alone. To support continued research on multiplayer world models, we release our dataset, our full training and inference codebase, and a live demo.

</details>


### [294] [Consistent and Editable: A Balanced Framework for Text-Guided Video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05056)
*Tao Jin,Li Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EquiEdit is a video editing framework using a temporal Mamba module for consistency and spectral-based noise injection for editability, balancing both aspects effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based video editing methods struggle with the trade-off between temporal consistency and editability, which are typically inversely related.

Method: (1) A temporal Mamba module with four-directional temporal-aware scanning to enhance inter-frame consistency. (2) A noise injection strategy based on Fourier spectral transformation to preserve hidden structure in latent noise for better editability and fidelity.

Result: Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments show the method achieves strong temporal consistency, editability, and fidelity to the input video.

Conclusion: EquiEdit effectively balances consistency and editability in video editing, outperforming existing methods on both metrics while maintaining fidelity.

Abstract: Recently, diffusion models have achieved considerable success in the text-guided video editing domain. However, existing works often struggle to balance the trade-off between temporal consistency and editability in video editing, with consistency and editability typically being inversely related. To address this, we propose a high-quality video editing framework enhanced for consistency and editability, named EquiEdit, which improves coordinatively the temporal consistency and editability of the edited videos while achieving a balance between the two. In terms of temporal consistency, the proposed temporal Mamba module with a tailored temporal-aware scanning scans fused video sequences following four designed directions, effectively enhancing the inter-frame consistency of edited videos. For editability, we design a noise injection strategy based on the spectral transformation to increase editing flexibility, where the Fourier transform is used to preserve the hidden structure in the initial latent noise used for editing, ensuring inter-frame consistency of the edited video and fidelity to the input video. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in terms of temporal consistency and editability, as well as its great fidelity to the input video itself.

</details>


### [295] [LangLoc: "Tell Me What You See"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05077)
*Shaurya Kishore Panwar,Roham Zendehdel Nobari,Shirley Feng Yi Lau,Abu Bakr Rahman Shaik,Manuel Günther,Marc Pollefeys,Daniel Barath*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LangLoc is a three-stage pipeline for fine-grained indoor localization from natural language descriptions, achieving <1m median position error by combining scene retrieval, pose estimation via ray-cast visibility scoring, and Bayesian dialog for ambiguity resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work only achieves coarse scene-level retrieval and cannot resolve precise intra-scene pose (2D position + heading). Language-based localization is lightweight and privacy-preserving but lacks fine-grained methods.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (i) scene retrieval via dual-branch GATv2 encoder with CLIP features; (ii) pose estimation by ray-cast object visibility scoring on a dense floor grid; (iii) Bayesian dialog module with yes/no questions to resolve residual pose ambiguity.

Result: Outperforms prior scene retrieval by +8 pp Top-1 recall; median position error of 0.95m for pose estimation.

Conclusion: Natural language descriptions alone can achieve fine-grained indoor localization at meter-level accuracy, enabled by the proposed three-stage approach combining retrieval, geometric reasoning, and active questioning.

Abstract: We tackle fine-grained indoor localization from natural language: given a free-form description of one's surroundings, estimate the observer's 2D position and heading within a known 3D environment. Language queries are lightweight, privacy-preserving, and need no camera - yet prior work stops at coarse scene retrieval and cannot resolve an intra-scene pose. We close this gap with LangLoc, a three-stage pipeline that (i) retrieves the correct scene via a dual-branch GATv2 encoder with CLIP semantic features, surpassing the previous best by 8 percentage points in Top-1 recall; (ii) estimates position and heading by scoring a dense floor grid through ray-cast object visibility, reaching a median error of 0.95 m; and (iii) resolves residual ambiguity through a Bayesian dialog module that asks targeted yes/no questions and updates a pose posterior until the location is pinpointed. To support this task we contribute a benchmark of $13{,}000{+}$ pose-indexed natural-language descriptions over $1{,}300{+}$ indoor 3D scans.

</details>


### [296] [Search Beyond What Can Be Taught: Evolving the Knowledge Boundary in Agentic Visual Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05382)
*Haozhe Wang,Weijia Feng,Jinpeng Yu,Che Liu,Ping Nie,Fangzhen Lin,Jiaming Liu,Ruihua Huang,Jimmy Lin,Wenhu Chen,Cong Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Visual generators struggle with open-ended world knowledge (new entities, events, etc.), scoring only 21-28/100 on a new benchmark (SearchGen-Bench). Naive search augmentation fails, but a teach-then-search co-training framework enables monotonic improvement, laying groundwork for recursive self-improvement in visual generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual generators have a structural world-knowledge bottleneck: trained on fixed corpora but must handle unbounded, evolving, long-tailed user requests (new characters, trending entities, post-cutoff events). Existing benchmarks fail to capture this failure mode.

Method: Construct SearchGen-20K (20,839 prompts across 12 failure categories, 22 domains) and SearchGen-Corpus-1M (pre-executed multimodal search corpus). Propose a teach-then-search co-training framework that discovers the generator-specific knowledge boundary and recursively improves generation.

Result: Frontier open generators score only 21-28/100 on SearchGen-Bench—a 40-point collapse vs. existing benchmarks. Naive search fails due to indiscriminate retrieval. The minimal co-training recipe yields monotonic improvement.

Conclusion: The teach-then-search co-training framework effectively bridges the world-knowledge gap, enabling tool-augmented visual generation that can meet knowledge-grounded requests, with potential for recursive self-improvement.

Abstract: Visual generators excel at rendering, but they confidently fabricate what they do not know. User requests are unbounded, evolving, and deeply long-tailed: new characters, trending entities, post-cutoff events, and more. This world-knowledge bottleneck is structural: generators are trained on fixed corpora, but the visual world is open-ended. We construct SearchGen-20K and SearchGen-Bench, with 20,839 prompts spanning twelve failure categories and twenty-two domains, paired with a pre-executed multimodal SearchGen-Corpus-1M to support offline, reproducible research. On SearchGen-Bench, frontier open generators score only 21 to 28 out of 100, a 40-point collapse invisible to existing benchmarks. The natural remedy is to employ search tools, enabling agentic visual generation. However, we find that naive search fails: it retrieves indiscriminately, injecting noise into prompts the generator already handles. We trace the root cause to a generator-specific, evolving knowledge boundary: the divide between what a generator can internalize through training and what must remain in external context. Although this boundary is hard to specify in advance, we show that it is discoverable through a teach-then-search co-training framework. Even a minimal version of this co-training recipe produces monotonic improvement, laying the foundation for recursive self-improvement in visual generation that can meet world-knowledge-grounded requests. We release the full dataset, co-training corpus, and search corpus as a replayable harness for tool-augmented, world-knowledge-grounded visual generation.

</details>


### [297] [RADIANCE: Relative Adaptive Denoising with IP-Adapter for Novel Concept Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05088)
*Zi-Xiang Ni,Bo-Lun Huang,Teng-Fang Hsiao,Bo-Kai Ruan,Hong-Han Shuai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RADIANCE is a training-free framework that improves text-to-image diffusion models' ability to synthesize rare attribute-object pairings by treating inference as closed-loop feedback, using a Compositional Similarity Monitor, Bidirectional Scale Controller, and Feedback Guidance Scheduler to rebalance biased denoising trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: T2I diffusion models struggle to synthesize rare concepts with unusual attribute-object pairings, often omitting concepts or experiencing semantic drift where one entity dominates the generation due to lack of compositional balance during denoising.

Method: Three modular components: (1) Compositional Similarity Monitor (CSM) tracking object/attribute emergence via CLIP-based feedback; (2) Bidirectional Scale Controller (BSC) applying reactive "restoring force" with positive/negative IP-Adapter scales; (3) Feedback Guidance Scheduler (FGS) coordinating updates across timesteps. Extended with Delayed Adapter Activation (DAA) and Layer-wise Alternating Guidance (LAG) for multi-object prompts.

Result: RADIANCE enhances compositional alignment and perceptual quality over state-of-the-art baselines on RareBench and T2I-CompBench, with competitive latency and improved per-sample success rate and effective throughput.

Conclusion: By overlapping monitoring and denoising via pipelined execution, RADIANCE offers a training-free, modular solution that significantly improves rare concept synthesis in T2I diffusion models.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models have achieved striking progress but still struggle to synthesize rare concepts involving unusual attribute-object pairings, often resulting in concept omission or semantic drift where a dominant entity overwhelms the generation. Tracing these failures to a lack of compositional balance during the denoising trajectory, we propose RADIANCE, a training-free framework that treats inference as a closed-loop feedback process. RADIANCE augments pretrained backbones with three modular components: (1) a Compositional Similarity Monitor (CSM) that tracks the emergence of objects and attributes in intermediate latents via CLIP-based feedback; (2) a Bidirectional Scale Controller (BSC) that applies a reactive "restoring force" using positive and negative IP-Adapter scales to rebalance biased trajectories; and (3) a Feedback Guidance Scheduler (FGS) that coordinates these updates across timesteps without additional training. We further extend the framework to multi-object prompts via Delayed Adapter Activation (DAA) and Layer-wise Alternating Guidance (LAG) to prevent premature concept fusion. By overlapping monitoring and denoising through pipelined execution, RADIANCE maintains competitive latency while significantly enhancing the per-sample success rate and effective throughput. Experiments on RareBench and T2I-CompBench demonstrate that RADIANCE consistently enhances compositional alignment and perceptual quality over state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [298] [TimeThink: Reasoning with Time for Video LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05089)
*Handong Li,Longteng Guo,Zikang Liu,Dongze Hao,Yepeng Tang,Zijia Zhao,Jie Jiang,Zhiwei Jin,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TimeThink proposes a reinforcement learning framework for Video-LLMs that uses step-wise temporal process rewards to guide evidence discovery in video reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Video-LLMs with reinforcement learning rely on outcome-based rewards that only supervise final predictions, offering limited guidance for discovering relevant temporal evidence during intermediate reasoning steps.

Method: TimeThink treats temporal clue steps as optimization primitives, introduces step-wise temporal process rewards for localized credit assignment, uses a joint process-outcome optimization objective, and constructs the TimeThink-RFT-20K dataset with automatically derived temporal evidence segments.

Result: Consistent improvements across video reasoning, temporal grounding, and general video understanding benchmarks; achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source video RL models.

Conclusion: Explicitly guiding temporal evidence discovery through step-wise rewards improves both temporal localization and reasoning performance in Video-LLMs.

Abstract: Video reasoning requires models to identify and verify temporally localized evidence within long video sequences. Recent Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have shown promising reasoning abilities when aligned with reinforcement learning, yet existing approaches typically rely on outcome-based rewards that supervise only the final prediction. Such supervision provides limited guidance on how models should discover the relevant temporal evidence during intermediate reasoning. In this work, we propose TimeThink, a reinforcement learning framework that explicitly guides temporal evidence discovery in Video-LLMs. Our key idea is to treat temporal clue steps as the fundamental optimization primitive of video reasoning, where each reasoning step references a candidate time interval in the video. We introduce a step-wise temporal process reward that provides localized credit assignment for these clues and a joint process--outcome optimization objective that balances reasoning fidelity with task correctness. To enable scalable training, we construct TimeThink-RFT-20K, a dataset with automatically derived temporal evidence segments. Extensive experiments across video reasoning, temporal grounding, and general video understanding benchmarks show that TimeThink consistently improves both temporal localization and reasoning performance, achieving state-of-the-art results among open-source video RL models.

</details>


### [299] [Be Indiscrete: The Benefits of Learning Continuous Spine Degeneration Severity Scores](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05090)
*Maria Monzon,Andrew Zisserman,Robin Y. Park,Catherine R. Jutzeler,Amir Jamaludin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpineRankNet learns continuous severity scores from lumbar spine MRI, outperforming traditional multi-class classification by modeling degeneration as a ranking problem rather than categorical grades.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ordinal grading (normal/mild/moderate/severe) for lumbar spine degeneration imposes hard decision boundaries on a continuous disease process and ignores misclassification severity differences.

Method: SpineRankNet uses a ranking loss to learn scalar severity scores from lumbar spinal MRI; compared against multi-class classification and ordinal regression approaches.

Result: The ranking-based model enables fine-grained ordering of MRI scans, recovers ordinal grades with comparable accuracy to direct classifiers, and improves discrimination between more distant classes.

Conclusion: Modeling spinal degeneration as a continuous severity ranking problem is more appropriate than categorical classification, offering finer granularity while preserving clinical interpretability.

Abstract: Lumbar spine degeneration is a major contributor to chronic low back pain and is routinely assessed on MRI using ordinal grading systems, e.g. normal, mild, moderate, severe. Consequently, most approaches to train models to grade these MRIs formulate grading as a multi-class classification problem, treating ordinal grades as categorical, ignoring differences in misclassification severity, and imposing hard decision boundaries on a continuous disease process. This work explores modeling spinal degeneration as a continuous severity ranking problem. We introduce SpineRankNet, a framework that learns scalar severity scores from lumbar spinal MRI, and compare it against multi-class classification and ordinal regression. Using multiple degeneration measures from the Genodisc dataset, we show that a model trained using a ranking loss to produce a continuous score enables fine-grained ordering of MRI scans. Furthermore, the ordinal grading classes can be recovered from the score with comparable accuracy to those from a model trained directly for classification. The score learned by ranking even improves discrimination between more distant classes. Source code is available at https://github.com/spinetools/spineranknet.

</details>


### [300] [Semantic Video Communication via Multi-Scale Convolution and Dynamic Routing for Next-Generation Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05093)
*Gengtian Shi,Jinze Yu,Chenhao Wu,Shaofei Wang,Eiji Fukuzawa,Junjie Tang,Hiroshi Onoda,Jiang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a generative AI framework for semantic video communication that uses a multi-scale temporal convolutional encoder and capsule-based dynamic routing to enable efficient hierarchical temporal modeling and robust semantic alignment between video content and natural language queries, achieving strong results on ActivityNet Captions while being suitable for edge deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The exponential growth of video traffic demands novel semantic communication paradigms that transmit meaning rather than raw bits, while addressing challenges of bandwidth-constrained transmission and robust semantic alignment between video content and natural language queries at network edge devices.

Method: The approach introduces a multi-scale temporal convolutional encoder (O(T) complexity) for hierarchical temporal modeling and a capsule-based dynamic routing mechanism for non-monotonic semantic alignments, unified through a multi-task learning objective optimizing temporal boundary regression, cross-modal alignment, and capsule diversity.

Result: On ActivityNet Captions, achieves 42.9% Recall@0.5 and 41.1% mean IoU while maintaining computational efficiency critical for edge deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed generative AI-enabled framework effectively addresses semantic video communication challenges with strong performance and computational efficiency suitable for resource-constrained IoT and edge devices.

Abstract: The exponential growth of video traffic demands novel semantic communication paradigms that transmit meaning rather than raw bits. We present a generative AI-enabled framework for semantic video communication addressing two critical challenges: efficient hierarchical temporal modeling for bandwidth-constrained transmission and robust semantic alignment between video content and natural language queries at network edge devices. Our approach introduces a multi-scale temporal convolutional encoder that captures motion patterns across different temporal granularities with O(T) complexity suitable for resource-constrained IoT deployments. We further propose a capsule-based dynamic routing mechanism that iteratively refines segment-query associations, enabling flexible modeling of non-monotonic semantic alignments essential for goal-oriented communication. These components are unified through a multi-task learning objective optimizing temporal boundary regression, cross-modal alignment, and capsule diversity. Experiments on ActivityNet Captions demonstrate significant improvements, achieving 42.9% Recall@0.5 and 41.1% mean IoU while maintaining computational efficiency critical for edge deployment.

</details>


### [301] [UNIVERSE: Unified Video Action Models for Autonomous Driving with Flexible Mask-Modulated Modality Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05133)
*Mengmeng Liu,Diankun Zhang,Jiuming Liu,Jianfeng Cui,Hongwei Xie,Guang Chen,Hangjun Ye,Francesco Nex,Hao Cheng,Michael Ying Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UNIVERSE proposes a unified single-DiT video-action model with a Modality-Decoupling Visibility Mask that enables dense video supervision to directly shape trajectory denoising, achieving 91.0 PDMS on NAVSIM with 4.3× speedup and strong zero-shot transfer to nuScenes and Bench2Drive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cascaded or dual-DiT designs separate video imagination from action prediction, weakening the transfer of video-learned world dynamics to the trajectory branch, causing the action model to overfit dataset-specific driving priors.

Method: UNIVERSE uses a single mask-modulated Diffusion Transformer (DiT) that co-trains future video latents and ego-trajectory tokens within shared generative parameters. A Modality-Decoupling Visibility Mask shares historical context across modalities while blocking mutual attention between future video and trajectory tokens, preventing leakage and enabling trajectory-only inference at test time.

Result: Achieves 91.0 PDMS on NAVSIM (vs. 89.6 for Two-DiT variant), 4.3× speedup over joint video-action rollout, and strong zero-shot transfer to nuScenes and Bench2Drive without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Single-DiT unification, video co-training, and mask-based modality decoupling are crucial for transferring video-modeling benefits to trajectory generation, enabling both strong generalization and efficient deployment.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) have shown strong potential for improving action generalization in autonomous driving by using future video prediction as dense supervision for scene dynamics and temporal causality. However, it remains unclear which architecture better transfers video-modeling benefits to trajectory generation. Existing cascaded or dual-DiT designs separate video imagination from action prediction, weakening the transfer of video-learned world dynamics to the trajectory branch: the action model may still overfit dataset-specific driving priors, while the video model only indirectly regularizes planning. We propose UNIVERSE, a unified video-action model built upon a single mask-modulated Diffusion Transformer. By co-training future video latents and ego-trajectory tokens within shared generative parameters, UNIVERSE allows dense video supervision to directly shape trajectory denoising, leading to stronger cross-domain action generalization. To ensure causal validity and efficient deployment, we introduce a Modality-Decoupling Visibility Mask, which shares historical context across modalities while blocking mutual attention between future video and trajectory tokens. This prevents future-target leakage and enables trajectory-only inference by removing future-video denoising at test time, achieving a $4.3\times$ speedup over joint video-action rollout while maintaining comparable planning accuracy. The same model also supports video-only and joint video-action rollouts. Experiments show that UNIVERSE achieves 91.0 PDMS on NAVSIM (vs. 89.6 for the Two-DiT variant), and demonstrates strong zero-shot transfer to nuScenes and Bench2Drive without fine-tuning, while ablations confirm the importance of single-DiT unification, video co-training, and mask-based modality decoupling.

</details>


### [302] [Fully Rotation-Equivariant Spectral-Spatial Learning for Multispectral Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05148)
*Peng Zhang,Tingfa Xu,Shuaihao Han,Jianan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FressDet is a fully rotation-equivariant spectral-spatial learning framework for multispectral object detection that captures continuous spectral structure, enables reliable fusion across pyramid levels under arbitrary rotations, and achieves SOTA performance with 93% fewer parameters on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multispectral detectors suffer from discrete spectral processing, scale-dependent reliability shifts between spectral and spatial cues, and lack of rotation-equivariant geometric priors for arbitrarily oriented objects.

Method: FressDet integrates three components: Spectral Implicit Warp (SpeIW) for query-based spectral resampling via implicit field, Rotation-Equivariant Consistency Weighting (ReCoW) for adaptive fusion of spectral/spatial branches, and an oriented-aware head with group-indexed features for stable oriented object prediction.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance with 93% fewer parameters on three public benchmarks.

Conclusion: FressDet learns more discriminative and robust spectral-spatial representations under rotational perturbations, demonstrating effectiveness and generalizability.

Abstract: Existing multispectral detectors are limited by discrete spectral processing, a scale-dependent shift in the relative reliability of spectral and spatial cues across pyramid levels, and the lack of explicit rotation-equivariant geometric priors for arbitrarily oriented objects. To tackle these limitations, we propose FressDet, a fully rotation-equivariant spectral-spatial learning framework for multispectral object detection, capable of capturing the continuous, ordered nature of spectral structure and enabling reliable spectral-spatial fusion across pyramid levels under arbitrary in-plane rotations. FressDet integrates three complementary components. Spectral Implicit Warp (SpeIW) enables query-based spectral resampling via a coordinate-conditioned implicit field, yielding a monotone, order-preserving warp. Rotation-Equivariant Consistency Weighting (ReCoW) adaptively fuses spectral and spatial branches based on branch reliability, reinforcing informative cues while suppressing noise across pyramid levels. The oriented-aware head exploits group-indexed features to stably predict oriented objects without parameter replication. Taken together, FressDet learns more discriminative and robust spectral-spatial representations even under rotational perturbations. By achieving state-of-the-art performance with 93% fewer parameters on three public benchmarks, FressDet demonstrates its effectiveness and generalizability.

</details>


### [303] [Claim-Level Rubric Rewards for Video Caption Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05150)
*Mingqi Gao,Hongyuan Dong,Yifei Chen,Zhisheng Zhong,Zheng Ruan,Wenjin Hou,Yu Chen,Han Hu,Yansong Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CuRe introduces a structured reward framework using claim-level rubric verification to improve reinforcement learning for dense video captioning, overcoming limitations of holistic and reference-based reward designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reward designs for dense video captioning suffer from either holistic judgment issues (poor factual accuracy, reward hacking) or reference-based alignment problems (rigid, loss of completeness/diversity). CuRe aims to solve the reward-design bottleneck in RL for this task.

Method: CuRe reformulates reward modeling as fine-grained claim-level verification by decomposing captions into category-aware atomic claims through a structured rubric, converting holistic evaluation into simpler claim-level verification.

Result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract, but the method is designed to improve factual accuracy, preserve completeness/diversity, and resist reward hacking compared to existing paradigms.

Conclusion: CuRe proposes that claim-level rubric-based rewards offer a more reliable and effective alternative to both holistic response-level and reference-based reward paradigms for dense video captioning.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce Claim-Level Rubric Rewards (CuRe), a structured reward framework designed to address the reward-design bottleneck in reinforcement learning for dense video captioning. Existing reward designs generally fall into two categories: holistic response-level judgment across heterogeneous criteria, or alignment-based evaluation against reference captions. However, both paradigms suffer from fundamental limitations. Holistic rewards struggle to ensure factual accuracy and are prone to stylistic reward hacking, while reference-based rewards overly rely on rigid textual alignment, failing to preserve the completeness and diversity inherent to open-ended generation tasks. To address these challenges, CuRe reformulates reward modeling as fine-grained claim-level verification. Specifically, CuRe decomposes captions into category-aware atomic claims through a structured rubric, converting holistic evaluation into simpler and more reliable claim-level verification.

</details>


### [304] [FSDC-DETR: A Frequency-Spatial Domain Collaborative DETR for Small Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05176)
*Aiwen Liu,Chengguang Zhu,Gang Wang,Dandan Zhu,Haodong Lin,Yan Wang,Huiyu Zhou,Zhiyi Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FSDC-DETR is a novel Transformer framework that explicitly models complementary spatial and frequency representations for small object detection, achieving state-of-the-art performance (AP gains of 6.4-6.6 on VisDrone and AITODv2, with 6.8-6.9 AP improvements for small objects).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small object detection is challenging because existing detectors entangle spatial aggregation with implicit frequency aliasing and truncation, failing to adequately preserve high-frequency components critical for detecting small objects.

Method: Proposes FSDC-DETR with: (1) Dual-Branch Frequency-Spatial Adaptive Fusion (DBFSAF) for enhanced frequency diversity; (2) Shunt Frequency-Spatial Feature Fusion (SFS-FF) for bidirectional interaction and cross-scale propagation; (3) Frequency-Spatial Dynamic Downsampling (FSD-Down) to preserve high-frequency responses during multi-scale fusion.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art on VisDrone-DET2019 (+6.4 AP overall, +6.8 AP for small objects) and AITODv2 (+6.6 AP overall, +6.9 AP for small objects).

Conclusion: The frequency-spatial collaborative modeling approach effectively addresses high-frequency preservation issues in SOD, outperforming existing detectors significantly.

Abstract: Small object detection (SOD) remains a challenging task in real-world applications. Despite recent advances, existing detectors remain limited by rigid processing that entangle spatial aggregation with implicit frequency aliasing and truncation, leading to inadequate preservation of high-frequency components for SOD. To tackle these limitations, we propose a Frequency-Spatial Domain Collaborative Detection Transformer (FSDC-DETR), a novel collaborative framework that explicitly models complementary spatial and frequency representations. Specifically, we first introduce Dual-Branch Frequency-Spatial Adaptive Fusion (DBFSAF) to enhance frequency diversity and adaptively capture frequency-spatial domain discriminative representations. Building on these representations, a frequency-spatial interaction scheme is further explored within the hybrid encoder to enable progressive feature propagation to the decoder. In particular, structure-aware frequency-spatial aggregation is achieved through Shunt Frequency-Spatial Feature Fusion (SFS-FF), establishing bidirectional interaction and progressive cross-scale propagation between frequency and spatial representations for coherent discriminative modeling. Meanwhile, informative high-frequency responses are preserved during scale transitions through Frequency-Spatial Dynamic Downsampling (FSD-Down), thereby minimizing frequency degradation throughout multi-scale fusion for the precise SOD. Experimental results demonstrate that FSDC-DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving AP by 6.4 on VisDrone-DET2019 and 6.6 on AITODv2, with gains of 6.8 and 6.9 AP for small objects. The code is available at github.com/nevereverinsomnia/FSDC-DETR.

</details>


### [305] [Probing Geospatial SSL Representations with Environmental Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05207)
*Rohita Mocharla,Vishal M. Patel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates SSL representations of satellite imagery by probing them with environmental variables from ERA5 (temperature, precipitation, etc.) rather than just downstream tasks. Using DINO, MAE, and MoCo models, they show representation metrics reveal differences between models with similar benchmark performance, and environmental signal accessibility correlates with performance on environmentally dependent tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current geospatial benchmarks evaluate SSL representations solely through downstream tasks, providing limited insight into what information is actually encoded in the representations. The authors propose probing representations with physically related environmental variables to gain a more meaningful understanding.

Method: Probe SSL representations (DINO, MAE, MoCo) using co-located ERA5 reanalysis variables (temperature, precipitation, surface solar radiation, surface pressure, volumetric soil water). Complement with intrinsic representation metrics and analyze how these relate to downstream performance and environmental signal encoding.

Result: Representation-level metrics distinguish models with similar downstream benchmark performance. Linear accessibility of environmental signals is associated with performance on environmentally dependent tasks in the PANGAEA benchmark.

Conclusion: Probing with physically grounded environmental variables provides complementary information beyond task-driven benchmarks. The authors release ERA5 annotations co-located with the SSL4EO dataset for future evaluation.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is designed to learn generic, transferable representations rather than representations optimized for a single task. Most geospatial benchmarks evaluate representations solely through downstream tasks, providing limited insight into the information encoded within the representation itself. We ask a different question: do SSL representations of satellite imagery preserve statistical associations with environmental variables that co-vary with the imaging process? To answer this question, we probe SSL representations using co-located ERA5 reanalysis variables, a global dataset of physically consistent environmental variables, including temperature, precipitation, surface solar radiation, surface pressure, and volumetric soil water. These variables are physically related to the spectral reflectance and radar backscatter recorded by Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2, making them meaningful evaluation targets despite not being used during SSL pretraining. We complement this probing analysis with intrinsic representation metrics to characterize representation geometry and investigate how these properties relate to downstream performance and the encoding of environmental signals. Using DINO, MAE, and MoCo models trained under identical conditions, we show that representation-level metrics distinguish models with similar downstream benchmark performance, providing complementary information beyond task-driven benchmarks. We further find that the linear accessibility of environmental signals is associated with performance on environmentally dependent tasks in the PANGAEA benchmark. Finally, we release ERA5 annotations co-located with the SSL4EO dataset to enable physically grounded representation evaluation for future geospatial foundation models.

</details>


### [306] [Causal-RetiGraph: Cross-Cohort Retinal Support and Same-Subject Pathway Analysis for Diabetic Retinopathy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05204)
*Inam Ullah,Imran Razzak,Shoaib Jameel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Causal-RetiGraph is a biomedical informatics framework linking retinal graph phenotypes with systemic disease pathways for diabetic retinopathy, achieving high accuracy (AUROC 0.9711) while identifying key systemic anchors like HbA1c and glycaemic-renal/haemodynamic pathways.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern retinal AI grades DR accurately but fails to connect local lesion evidence, retinal vascular structure, and systemic disease pathways. This gap motivates a framework that bridges image-level findings with systemic pathophysiology.

Method: Causal-RetiGraph uses two folds: (1) a retinal-image fold that constructs an X1234 phenotype from vessel maps, lesions, embeddings, and AutoMorph biomarkers via spatial (X12) and Jacobian (X34) branches; (2) an NHANES fold modeling systemic exposures, covariates, a retinal mediator family R*, and downstream outcomes. X1234 supports retinal assessment and pathway prioritization, while R* provides participant-level pathway summaries.

Result: X1234 achieves 0.9055 binary DR accuracy, 0.9711 AUROC, and 0.8312 graded DR QWK. HbA1c, urine albumin, pulse pressure, fasting glucose, and systolic BP are top binary DR anchors. Glycaemic-renal and glycaemic-haemodynamic pathways are the clearest mediator-style signals.

Conclusion: Retinal graph phenotypes can help prioritize systemic pathways in DR while maintaining the distinction between image-derived support and same-subject mediation, offering a bridge between retinal AI and systemic disease modeling.

Abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a local retinal lesion process and a visible manifestation of systemic microvascular injury. Modern retinal AI can grade images accurately, but often leaves unanswered how local lesion evidence, retinal vascular structure, and systemic disease pathways are connected. This paper introduces \emph{Causal-RetiGraph}, a compact biomedical informatics framework that links retinal graph phenotypes with NHANES-anchored pathway modelling. The retinal-image fold constructs an interpretable $X1234$ phenotype from vessel maps, lesion evidence, image embeddings, and AutoMorph biomarkers through spatial $X_{12}$ and Jacobian $X_{34}$ branches. The NHANES fold models systemic exposures, covariates, a same-subject retinal mediator family $R^*$, and downstream outcome families. $X1234$ is used for retinal support and pathway prioritisation, while $R^*$ is used for participant-level pathway summaries. On the retinal fold, $X1234$ achieves 0.9055 binary DR accuracy and 0.9711 AUROC, with graded DR QWK of 0.8312. The results show that lesion and biomarker streams improve contextual retinal representation under scarce and imbalanced data. In NHANES, HbA1c, urine albumin, pulse pressure, fasting glucose, and systolic blood pressure are the strongest binary DR anchors. Participant-level pathway analysis identifies glycaemic--renal and glycaemic--haemodynamic pathways as the clearest mediator-style signals. These results suggest that retinal graph phenotypes can help prioritise systemic pathways in DR while preserving the distinction between image-derived support and same-subject mediation.

</details>


### [307] [Is the Geometry Doing the Work? An Operating-Point Audit of Hierarchy in Hyperbolic Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05268)
*Jaeyoung Kim,Eunseok Kim,Dongsuk Jang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper audits three hyperbolic vision-language models (MERU, HyCoCLIP, PHyCLIP) and finds they fail to actually use hyperbolic geometry meaningfully — the operating point stays near-Euclidean, entailment cones are inactive or saturated, and hierarchical performance is driven by angular distance or supervision, not curvature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether hyperbolic representation models genuinely exploit their geometric machinery (radial/cone structure) or merely appear to do so, given that curvature hyperparameters alone are insufficient for assessment.

Method: Develops necessary-condition diagnostics (e.g., operating point $\sqrt{c}ρ$, aperture identity $\sqrt{c}ρ \le 2K$) and audits released checkpoints of MERU, HyCoCLIP, PHyCLIP on a fixed GRIT snapshot, including controlled interventions and ablation studies.

Result: Three failure modes identified: (1) curvature is not actively used (operating point stays near-Euclidean, $\sqrt{c}ρ>1$ never reached); (2) cone/traversal machinery is inoperative (entailment cones inactive/saturated, graded traversal fails); (3) hierarchy evaluations are underdetermined (taxonomy correlations reflect angular distance, coarse-retrieval gains come from box/compositional supervision).

Conclusion: These models do not instantiate the radial/cone mechanism their geometry motivates; a five-number geometry report is proposed as a standard for future hierarchy claims.

Abstract: Whether a hyperbolic representation model uses its geometry cannot be read off its curvature parameter: what matters is the dimensionless operating point $\sqrt{c}ρ$ and whether the radial and cone machinery is active there. We develop a battery of necessary-condition diagnostics and audit three published hyperbolic vision-language families -- MERU, HyCoCLIP, and PHyCLIP -- across released checkpoints and controlled interventions on a fixed GRIT snapshot, identifying three failure modes. First, curvature is not an active resource: the operating point stays near-Euclidean ($H(u)\approx 1$; no audited converged checkpoint reaches $\sqrt{c}ρ>1$), and releasing the curvature floor moves curvature and norms but keeps the operating point near-Euclidean, without substantial downstream degradation. Second, the cone and traversal machinery is measured inoperative: entailment cones are inactive, saturated, or misaligned, and graded traversal fails under controlled readouts, while directed radial depth is a bounded non-detection above shuffle-null controls at quantified sensitivity; the one surviving native-relation residual remains non-operative. Third, hierarchy-looking evaluations are underdetermined: taxonomy correlations are carried by angular distance, and coarse-retrieval gains track box/compositional supervision, not curvature. A mechanistic account explains why: the entailment objective admits a low-curvature, wide-cone shortcut, and a parameter-free aperture identity (cones saturate iff $\sqrt{c}ρ\le 2K$) locates the edge where every entailment-trained unclamped run settles; entailment-off runs show no arrest there. The shortcut is the dominant accelerator of collapse, not its sole cause. These formulations, as released, do not instantiate the radial/cone mechanism their geometry motivates; we distill the audit into a five-number geometry report for future hierarchy claims.

</details>


### [308] [An event-driven framework for fly-inspired visual motion detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05205)
*Qinbing Fu,Jingyu Huang,Yan Xie,Jigen Peng,Yuchao Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an event-driven computational framework that combines time-surface encoding with a fly-inspired neural network for foreground motion-direction estimation, incorporating a bottom-up attention mechanism to suppress background noise. Evaluated on real-world datasets, it effectively merges event-based sensing with bio-inspired neural processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras suffer from noise under low-light conditions, and effective integration between event-based visual representations and biologically inspired neural processing remains underexplored.

Method: The framework integrates time-surface encoding for front-end event representation with a fly optic-lobe-inspired neural network, plus a bottom-up attention mechanism to suppress background motion.

Result: Evaluated on real-world ground-vehicle datasets, the method outperforms a baseline frame-based model and an optimization-based approach, effectively combining event-driven temporal advantages with bio-inspired efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed event-driven bio-inspired framework achieves efficient, interpretable, and robust foreground motion detection suitable for real-time embedded systems.

Abstract: Fast and reliable motion detection is essential for machine vision and autonomous systems operating in dynamic environments. This work integrates emerging event-based sensing with biologically structured neural computation to establish an efficient computational paradigm for visual motion detection. The proposed framework is built upon a recently developed fly-inspired neural network that emulates motion-processing circuits in the optic lobe. Owing to its feed-forward and training-free architecture, the neural model requires only a small number of interpretable parameters and is well suited for real-time embedded implementation. Event cameras provide low-latency, low-power, and high-dynamic-range visual sensing by asynchronously transmitting brightness-change events. However, their performance can be degraded by event noise, including temporal noise and junction-leakage-induced activity, particularly under low-light conditions. Moreover, effective integration between event-based visual representations and biologically inspired neural processing remains under-explored. To address these challenges, we propose an event-driven computational framework that combines time-surface encoding for front-end event representation with a fly optic-lobe-inspired neural network for foreground motion-direction estimation. A bottom-up attention mechanism is further incorporated to suppress background motion and enhance the saliency of foreground targets. The proposed method is evaluated on real-world ground-vehicle datasets and compared with a baseline frame-based model and an optimization-based approach. Experimental results demonstrate that the framework effectively combines the temporal advantages of event-driven vision with the efficiency and interpretability of bio-inspired neural processing.

</details>


### [309] [A Multimodal Reasoning Typology for Grounding Chart-Image Coherence in Science Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05222)
*Avina Nakarmi,Sohom Sen,Xun Song,Sreyashi Samaddar,Aritra Dasgupta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper develops a typology (R1–R5) of reasoning gaps between charts, images, and captions in scientific publications, and shows that the typology predicts where domain experts and non-experts disagree when evaluating VLM-generated descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Charts and images frequently co-occur in scientific papers, but existing computational work does not systematically characterize the coherence between them. The authors argue that understanding how these multimodal units convey scientific claims is crucial, and that current approaches lack a principled framework to capture the varying interpretive demands placed on readers.

Method: The authors developed a bottom-up typology of reasoning gaps (R1–R5) grounded in communication theory, derived from a corpus of 79 traumatic brain injury papers (32 chart-image pairs) with a neuroscience expert. They then conducted a study where a domain expert and three non-experts judged VLM descriptions of 25 pairs to test whether the typology predicts alignment and divergence in judgments.

Result: The typology levels (R1–R5) systematically predict where domain expert and non-expert judgments align or diverge when evaluating VLM descriptions, isolating points where contextual knowledge (rather than the figure itself) carries coherence.

Conclusion: The proposed typology offers figure designers a systematic way to balance text against chart-image pairs, helping bridge the gap between expert and non-expert reading of scientific takeaways.

Abstract: Charts and images appear together throughout scientific publications, yet most computational work does not characterize their coherence. We argue that a chart, its accompanying image, and the caption that links them form a multimodal unit, and that the inferential work required to read it varies systematically. To capture this variation, we develop a typology of reasoning gaps, R1 through R5, that characterizes how chart, image, and text jointly convey a scientific claim, and the interpretive work this demands of the reader. Some pairs restate the same data, while in other pairs, charts are used to quantify a structure the image localizes, project image content onto an external variable, audit an image-based claim, or jointly construct a frame that neither panel can establish alone. The typology is anchored in the grounding theory of communication and was derived bottom-up, with a neuroscience expert, from a corpus of 79 traumatic brain injury papers and 32 chart-image pairs. Crucially, the levels provide a systematic mechanism for identifying where grounding succeeds or breaks down, rather than leaving it to subjective inference. We show this in a study in which a domain expert and three non-experts judge vision-language model (VLM) descriptions of 25 pairs: the level predicts where their judgments align and where they diverge, isolating the points at which contextual knowledge, not the figure, carries coherence. This typology thus offers figure designers a systematic way to balance text against chart-image pairs, bridging the expert-to-non-expert divide in reading a scientific takeaway.

</details>


### [310] [GUSH3R: Everyone Everywhere All at Once as Gaussians](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05243)
*Keito Abe,Kaede Shiohara,Takashi Otonari,Toshihiko Yamasaki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GUSH3R is a feed-forward framework that reconstructs dynamic human-scene environments from monocular videos in a single forward pass, outputting 3D Gaussian Splatting primitives for geometrically consistent, photorealistic novel view synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing feed-forward methods either produce non-photorealistic outputs (point clouds, meshes) or cannot handle non-rigid objects like dynamic humans, limiting their applicability for reconstructing dynamic human-scene environments from monocular video.

Method: GUSH3R is a feed-forward framework that takes monocular human-scene video as input and reconstructs dynamic humans and static scenes jointly in a single forward pass, outputting 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) primitives that are geometrically consistent and enable novel view synthesis.

Result: The method achieves competitive novel view synthesis quality with significantly improved inference efficiency compared to optimization-based methods on monocular human-scene datasets.

Conclusion: GUSH3R successfully bridges the gap between feed-forward efficiency and photorealistic reconstruction of dynamic human-scene environments, offering a practical solution for real-time or online applications.

Abstract: Reconstructing dynamic human-scene environments from monocular videos is a challenging problem that requires jointly modeling scene geometry, camera motion, and non-rigid human dynamics while enabling photorealistic rendering. Recent feed-forward methods can efficiently predict geometry, but they are often limited to non-photorealistic representations such as point clouds and meshes, or they fail to handle non-rigid objects, particularly dynamic humans. To fill this gap, we present GUSH3R (Gaussian-Unified Scene Human 3D Reconstruction), a feed-forward framework for online dynamic human-scene reconstruction. From a monocular human-scene video, our method reconstructs dynamic humans (everyone) and static scenes (everywhere) in a single forward pass (all at once) as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) primitives (as gaussians), which are geometrically consistent and capable of novel view synthesis. Experiments on monocular human-scene datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive novel view synthesis quality while significantly improving inference efficiency compared to optimization-based methods.

</details>


### [311] [Vision Pretraining for Dense Spatial Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05247)
*Zelin Fu,Bin Tan,Changjiang Sun,Shaohui Liu,Kecheng Zheng,Yinghao Xu,Xing Zhu,Yujun Shen,Nan Xue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes masked boundary modeling, a self-supervised pretraining paradigm that learns sub-pixel boundary representations as masked targets for dense visual token learning. This yields LingBot-Vision, which improves depth estimation (LingBot-Depth 2.0) and demonstrates that boundary modeling serves as a scalable principle for spatial visual representation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visual foundation models prioritize semantic invariance but lack detailed spatial understanding. The authors aim to recover structured, metric, and actionable spatial representations from pixel observations for physical intelligence.

Method: Masked boundary modeling: a self-supervised paradigm that dynamically learns sub-pixel boundary representations, then uses discovered boundary-bearing tokens as masked targets to facilitate dense visual token learning.

Result: LingBot-Vision, built by scaling this framework, shows efficacy across diverse downstream vision tasks with DINOv3 as baseline. It advances from LingBot-Depth 1.0 to LingBot-Depth 2.0 for depth completion, yielding enhanced depth estimation.

Conclusion: Boundary modeling goes beyond simple line segments and serves as a scalable pretraining principle for learning spatially structured visual representations, particularly beneficial for embodied AI tasks like depth estimation.

Abstract: Dense spatial perception is essential for physical intelligence, where visual systems are expected to recover structured, metric, and actionable representations from pixel observations. Modern visual foundation models tend to prioritize semantic invariance, often at the expense of detailed spatial understanding. In this work, we study vision pretraining through a boundary-centric lens, motivated by the premise that boundaries and shape discontinuities offer essential cues for perceiving geometric properties. Concretely, we propose masked boundary modeling, a self-supervised paradigm that dynamically learns sub-pixel boundary representations and subsequently leverages the discovered boundary-bearing tokens as masked targets to facilitate dense visual token learning. By scaling this framework, we develop LingBot-Vision and demonstrate its efficacy across a diverse set of downstream vision tasks with DINOv3 as a strong baseline. Remarkably, LingBot-Vision drives the progression from LingBot-Depth 1.0 to LingBot-Depth 2.0 for depth completion, and thereby yields enhanced depth estimation, a key pillar for embodied artificial intelligence. Our findings reveal that boundary modeling goes beyond simple line segments and instead serves as a scalable pretraining principle for learning spatially structured visual representations.

</details>


### [312] [Repurposing CLIP to Localize at Pixel Level](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05253)
*Jiaxiang Fang,Shiqiang Ma,Jing Wang,Siyu Chen,Fei Guo,Shengfeng He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLIPix repurposes CLIP for pixel-level localization by tracing classification attention regions, applying noise-resistant correction, and using localization embeddings to achieve state-of-the-art segmentation on PASCAL and COCO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While CLIP excels at image-level open-set localization, adapting it to pixel-level dense prediction is challenging due to global feature biases. The authors aim to unlock CLIP's potential for precise segmentation at the pixel level.

Method: (1) Traces CLIP's classification process to identify object-specific attentive regions as localization cues. (2) Proposes a Noise-Resistant Correction strategy to refine these cues by addressing global bias noise. (3) Introduces a Localization Embedding strategy that fuses localization and detail information for high-resolution segmentation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on PASCAL and COCO datasets for segmentation tasks.

Conclusion: CLIPix effectively repurposes CLIP for pixel-level dense prediction while preserving its generalization strength, enabling arbitrary object segmentation with high accuracy.

Abstract: Large-scale Vision-Language Models like CLIP have demonstrated impressive open-set localization capabilities at the image level. However, adapting this capability to pixel-level dense prediction poses challenges due to global feature biases. In this paper, we introduce CLIPix, a simple yet effective framework that repurposes CLIP to perform pixel-level localization. By tracing back CLIP's classification process, CLIPix identifies object-specific attentive regions and repurposes them as pixel-level localization cues. To address noise introduced by global biases, we propose a Noise-Resistant Correction strategy, refining these cues for more precise segmentation. Additionally, we introduce a Localization Embedding strategy to integrate both localization and enriched detail information, enabling accurate, high-resolution segmentation. Our approach preserves CLIP's generalization strength and unlocks its potential for segmenting arbitrary objects. Extensive experiments on the PASCAL and COCO datasets demonstrate that CLIPix achieves state-of-the-art performance, underscoring its effectiveness.

</details>


### [313] [FlowMark: Mask-Guided Video Watermarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05261)
*Vishal Asnani,Shruti Agarwal,John Collomosse*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlowMark is a video watermarking framework that automatically predicts optimal embedding regions using a Mask Predictor, achieving robust watermark recovery under compression, temporal edits, and social media re-encoding while maintaining high perceptual quality (up to 50.08 dB PSNR).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior region-based watermarking approaches require user-supplied mask guidance, which is impractical for large-scale automated video processing. FlowMark aims to automate the identification of optimal embedding regions without human intervention.

Method: FlowMark uses an end-to-end trainable architecture with a dedicated Mask Predictor network that learns optimal regions for watermark embedding. It combines region-aware encoding with noise-augmented training for robustness, and employs content-adaptive masking to maintain coherence with video dynamics and eliminate perceptual flicker.

Result: FlowMark reliably embeds 128-bit messages with up to 50.08 dB PSNR. It shows robustness against compression, geometric transformations, content variation, temporal edits (frame swap, insertion, deletion, resampling, interpolation), and real-world social media pipelines (YouTube, Facebook re-encoding).

Conclusion: FlowMark offers strong performance for content provenance, temporal authenticity verification, and video integrity protection, eliminating the need for user-supplied masks in region-based video watermarking.

Abstract: We present FlowMark, a video watermarking framework guided by automatically predicted object masks. In contrast to prior region-based approaches that require user-supplied mask guidance, FlowMark learns to identify optimal regions for watermark embedding through a dedicated Mask Predictor network. Our end-to-end trainable architecture combines region-aware encoding with noise-augmented training to ensure robustness against compression, geometric transformations, and content variation, while preserving high perceptual quality. Our content-adaptive masking keeps watermark signals coherent with natural video dynamics, effectively eliminating perceptual flicker. Beyond compression robustness, FlowMark maintains reliable watermark recovery under video-native temporal edits (e.g., frame swap, insertion, deletion, resampling, and interpolation) and real-world social media distribution pipelines (e.g., YouTube and Facebook re-encoding). Experimental results on both image and video datasets show that FlowMark reliably embeds $128$-bit messages with up to $50.08$ dB PSNR, offering strong performance for content provenance, temporal authenticity verification, and video integrity protection.

</details>


### [314] [Learning Probabilistic Embeddings for Unsupervised Action Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05263)
*Shuai Li,Duc Manh Vu,Juergen Gall*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper improves unsupervised temporal action segmentation by replacing deterministic frame embeddings with probabilistic (Gaussian) embeddings, achieving up to 20.7% MoF and 19.0% F1-score improvements over deterministic baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OT-based unsupervised action segmentation methods use deterministic embeddings, causing the iterative pseudo-label estimation and representation learning to get stuck in local optima.

Method: Proposes probabilistic embeddings modeled by Gaussian distributions, sampling from distributions before pseudo-label estimation via optimal transport, instead of using deterministic embeddings.

Result: Achieves comparable or better results than state-of-the-art on multiple challenging datasets; improves MoF up to 20.7% and F1-score up to 19.0% over deterministic baselines.

Conclusion: Probabilistic embeddings effectively mitigate the local optimum problem in iterative OT-based clustering, leading to superior temporal action segmentation performance.

Abstract: This paper concerns the problem of unsupervised temporal action segmentation for long, untrimmed videos. Recent successful approaches follow a joint representation learning and clustering paradigm, where optimal transport (OT) is adopted to produce pseudo labels for learning frame representations. These approaches alternate between estimating pseudo labels using OT and optimizing the parameters with gradient descent during training, where OT is used for obtaining the final temporal action segmentation. A major limitation of these works is that they learn a deterministic embedding for frame representations. The iterative procedure between learning deterministic embeddings based on pseudo labels and estimating pseudo labels from the learned embedding can thus get quickly stuck in a local optimum. As an alternative, we thus propose to learn a probabilistic embedding for frame representations. The embeddings are modeled by Gaussian distributions and we sample from the distributions before estimating the pseudo labels. We evaluate our approach on several challenging temporal action segmentation datasets and achieve results comparable to, and in some cases, better than the state of the art.
