Compared to baselines with deterministic embeddings, our approach improves MoF up to 20.7\% and F1-score up to 19.0\%. Our code is available at https://github.com/derkbreeze/PEOT.

</details>


### [315] [SteelBench: Evaluating Vision-Language Models in Real-World Industrial Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05264)
*Suryanarayana Reddy Yarrabothula,Manisha Chawla,Kunal Sinha,Gagan Raj Gupta,Sashank Lekkala,Ashirvadhan Dosapati,Saikamal Nannuri,Katragadda Ajay RamaSwamy Chowdary Gowtham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STEELBENCH is a diagnostic benchmark for industrial surveillance CCTV that evaluates VLMs on per-worker activity recognition, safety-rule reasoning, and annotation provenance. It finds that current best models achieve only 42.6% action accuracy vs. 84.6% human benchmark, and unaudited VLM-sourced ground truth can inflate accuracy by up to 17 percentage points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video benchmarks focus on consumer videos, egocentric recordings, or simulated industrial settings, and fail to test VLMs under real industrial CCTV conditions (distant figures, dust, steam, low light, glare, occlusion, overlapping activities).

Method: Curated 1,345 densely annotated clips from 149 hours of operational plant footage (10,024 candidates) using temporal deduplication, class balancing, and visibility-aware stratified sampling. Includes per-worker action labels, PPE attributes, spatial context, safety-rule annotations, and a provenance-aware audit protocol to measure label influence and sensitivity.

Result: Best VLM achieves 42.6% action accuracy vs. 84.6% human benchmark. Unaided VLM-sourced ground truth inflates same-family model accuracy by up to 17pp. No model passes more than 2 of 5 diagnostic checks, and 37-58% of correct action predictions still yield incorrect safety judgments.

Conclusion: Current VLMs are severely inadequate for industrial surveillance; provenance-aware auditing is essential for reliable evaluation; the benchmark and audit protocol can guide future improvements in this domain.

Abstract: Existing video benchmarks evaluate action recognition on consumer videos, egocentric recordings, or simulated industrial environments. They do not test vision-language models under the visual and procedural conditions of real industrial CCTV, where workers appear as distant figures amid dust, steam, low light, glare, occlusion, and overlapping activities. We introduce STEELBENCH, a diagnostic benchmark for industrial surveillance that jointly evaluates per-worker activity recognition, safety-rule reasoning, and annotation provenance. SteelBench contains 1,345 densely annotated clips, curated from 149 hours of operational plant footage and 10,024 candidate clips using temporal deduplication, class balancing, and visibility-aware stratified sampling. Each clip includes dense per-worker action labels, PPE attributes, spatial context, and safety-rule annotations. Because model-assisted annotation can shape the labels later used for model evaluation, SteelBench includes a provenance-aware audit protocol. The protocol measures label influence, evaluates sensitivity to ground-truth provenance, and reports a human reference from expert-reviewed labels. Applying this audit, we find that unaudited VLM-sourced ground truth can inflate same-family model accuracy by up to 17 percentage points. Across nine VLMs from four architectural families, the best model reaches only 42.6% action accuracy, compared with an 84.6% human benchmark. Performance also fragments across recognition, robustness, calibration, and safety reasoning. Even when models predict the correct action, 37-58% of cases still yield incorrect safety judgments, and no model passes more than 2 of 5 diagnostic checks. The dataset is publicly available on Hugging Face.

</details>


### [316] [Erasing Without Collateral Damage: Precise Concept Removal in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05274)
*Parth Upman,Nishita Jain,Shreyank N Gowda*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CARE is a training-free concept erasure method for text-to-image diffusion models that replaces raw target directions with kept-subspace-aware directions to reduce collateral damage to non-target concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing value-space concept erasure methods damage semantically related non-target concepts because they treat target identity and shared visual structure as the same signal.

Method: CARE introduces a closed-form concept erasure operator that uses a kept-subspace-aware direction (computed from a small bank of retained concept anchors) instead of the raw target direction, applied in cross-attention value space with a single shrinkage parameter controlling the erase-preserve trade-off.

Result: CARE preserves non-target concepts more faithfully while maintaining competitive erasure across instance, style, and celebrity concepts under standard protocols.

Conclusion: The approach offers a minimum-disturbance interpretation and leaves the kept subspace invariant in its projection form, effectively reducing collateral damage in concept erasure.

Abstract: Training-free concept erasure is an attractive mechanism for controlling text-to-image diffusion models, but precise erasure often comes at the cost of damaging semantically related non-target concepts. Existing value-space methods remove the component of each cross-attention value along the target concept direction, implicitly treating target identity and shared visual structure as the same signal. We argue that this is the source of much of the collateral damage in prior preservation. We introduce CARE, a closed-form concept erasure operator that replaces the raw target direction with a kept-subspace-aware direction computed from a small bank of retained concept anchors. The resulting edit is applied directly in cross-attention value space, requires no model fine-tuning, and adds only a negligible offline computation. A single shrinkage parameter controls the erase-preserve trade-off. We further show that the operator admits a minimum-disturbance interpretation and, in its projection form, leaves the kept subspace invariant. Experiments under the standard concept-erasure protocol show that our method preserves non-target concepts more faithfully while maintaining competitive erasure across instance, style, and celebrity concepts. Code: https://github.com/parthupman/care

</details>


### [317] [ChatImage: Navigating Long-Form LLM Answers through Interactive Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05290)
*Wencan Jiang,Jiangning Zhang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ChatImage converts long-form LLM answers into interactive visual images with clickable hotspots, enabling fine-grained inspection and navigation of answer content through post-rendering vision grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM answers are dense linear text that makes fine-grained inspection, navigation, and return visits difficult for users.

Method: ChatImage normalizes text into structured visual modules, plans a layout, renders an image, then applies vision grounding models (LocateAnything, MiMo-Vision) with SAM-style mask refinement to identify visible interaction regions, overlaying clickable hotspots that open detail panels and region-scoped follow-up threads.

Result: Released a reference implementation and a 30-question benchmark covering infographic, map, and scene-based formats; evaluated with interaction-loop completion, visual-alignment gate, and SAM-based mask-completeness diagnostic.

Conclusion: Using planned coordinates as priors and grounding interaction targets after rendering improves consistency between visual content and clickable regions.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) can produce detailed answers to complex queries, but these answers are typically presented as dense linear text, which makes fine-grained inspection, navigation, and return visits difficult. We present ChatImage, a system that converts long-form LLM answers into interactive visual images. Given a textual answer, ChatImage first normalizes its content into structured visual modules, plans a visual layout, and renders a coherent image. It then applies a second grounding pass to the rendered image with vision grounding models such as LocateAnything and MiMo-Vision, with optional SAM-style mask refinement, to identify the visible regions that should support interaction. From these grounded regions, ChatImage overlays transparent clickable hotspots on the image. Each hotspot opens a detail panel and a region-scoped follow-up thread, allowing the user to inspect and query a specific part of the answer without re-reading the full response. Instead of treating planned coordinates as the final interaction geometry, ChatImage uses them as priors and grounds the interaction targets after rendering, which improves consistency between visual content and clickable regions. We release a reference implementation and introduce a 30-question benchmark covering infographic, map, and scene-based answer formats. Evaluation with configured external models reports interaction-loop completion, a strict visual-alignment gate, and a SAM-based mask-completeness diagnostic.

</details>


### [318] [Deep Learning for Semen Analysis in Male Infertility: Computer Vision, Multimodal Fusion, and Clinical Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05311)
*Runwei Guan,Shaofeng Liang,Jiacheng Weng,Xiaoyi Gu,Jia Weng,Daizong Liu,Duo Pan,Qingxin Zhang,Xiao Liang,Weiping Ding,Suoyu Zhu,Ming Yuan,Yanhua Fei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This review systematically synthesizes AI-driven sperm analysis methods—covering computer vision, deep learning, multimodal fusion, and clinical translation—while identifying key barriers (data scarcity, domain shift, interpretability) and proposing a staged clinical roadmap from technical standardization to post-market monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional semen evaluation is labor-intensive, operator-dependent, and suffers from inter-/intra-observer variability, driving the need for objective, reproducible computational methods to improve male infertility diagnosis and assisted reproductive technology.

Method: The authors provide a comprehensive review organizing AI-driven sperm analysis into task-specific methods (detection/counting, tracking-based motility, segmentation, morphology/defect classification, functional/genetic assessment), public datasets, benchmarks, evaluation metrics, and emerging multimodal strategies integrating microscopy, time-lapse videos, CASA data, DNA assays, and clinical metadata.

Result: The review identifies key barriers to deployment: data scarcity, cross-center domain shift, annotation inconsistency, interpretability gaps, uncertainty calibration issues, privacy-preserving learning needs, and workflow integration challenges. It also proposes a staged clinical translation roadmap.

Conclusion: AI-driven sperm analysis has made significant progress from task-specific visual recognition toward trustworthy multimodal reproductive intelligence, but unresolved challenges remain before it can become clinically meaningful decision support; a structured translation roadmap is essential for real-world adoption.

Abstract: Male infertility contributes substantially to the global infertility burden, and sperm analysis remains central to diagnosis, treatment planning, and assisted reproductive technology. Conventional semen evaluation, however, is labor-intensive, operator-dependent, and limited by inter- and intra-observer variability, motivating the development of objective and reproducible computational approaches. This review provides a comprehensive and perspective-oriented synthesis of artificial intelligence-driven sperm analysis, with a focus on computer vision, deep learning, multimodal fusion, robustness, and clinical translation. We first review task-specific methods for sperm detection and counting, tracking-based motility assessment, semantic and instance segmentation, morphology and defect classification, functional assessment, and genetic integrity evaluation. We then summarize public datasets, benchmarks, evaluation metrics, and emerging multimodal strategies that integrate microscopic images, time-lapse videos, CASA-derived parameters, DNA integrity assays, and clinical metadata. Beyond algorithmic performance, we discuss key barriers to real-world deployment, including data scarcity, cross-center domain shift, annotation inconsistency, interpretability, uncertainty calibration, privacy-preserving learning, and workflow integration. Finally, we outline a staged clinical translation roadmap spanning technical standardization, multicenter retrospective validation, silent prospective evaluation, human-in-the-loop clinical testing, ART outcome validation, regulatory approval, and post-market monitoring. By organizing the field from task-specific visual recognition to trustworthy multimodal reproductive intelligence, this review highlights both the progress and the unresolved challenges required to translate AI-driven sperm analysis into clinically meaningful decision support.

</details>


### [319] [CenSynCMB: Centre Maps and Physics-Guided Synthesis for Microbleed Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05325)
*Lucas He,Hanyuan Zhang,Krinos Li,Adama Fatima Saccoh,Silvia Ingala,Rafael Rehwald,Marleen de Bruijne,Frederik Barkhof,Rhodri Davies,Carole H. Sudre*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CenSynCMB is a framework for detecting cerebral microbleeds in MRI using a 3D Attention U-Net with centre-map supervision and physics-guided synthetic data generation, achieving state-of-the-art F1 scores on VALDO and AIBL datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cerebral microbleeds (CMBs) are important MRI markers but are difficult to detect automatically due to their small size, sparsity, and visual similarity to vessels, calcifications, and artefacts.

Method: The method combines a 3D Attention U-Net with auxiliary centre-map supervision, false-negative-driven reweighting, and fold-wise physics-guided synthesis of positive CMBs and labelled hard negatives (mimics).

Result: On VALDO Task 2, achieved best lesion-level F1 of 74.3% (p=0.020); on external AIBL SWI, achieved highest recall (88.5%, p=0.0058) and F1 (65.0%, p=0.0016).

Conclusion: The approach supports scalable CMB candidate extraction in large unlabelled MRI cohorts, with cohort-specific calibration needed for reliable burden estimation.

Abstract: Cerebral microbleeds (CMBs) are MRI markers of small vessel disease and the microbleed component of amyloid related imaging abnormalities (ARIA-H), but their small size, sparsity, and similarity to vessels, calcification-like foci, and artefacts make automated detection difficult. We propose CenSynCMB, a centre-guided and mimic-aware framework combining a 3D Attention U-Net, auxiliary centre-map supervision, false-negative-driven reweighting, and fold-wise physics-guided synthesis of positive CMBs and labelled hard negatives. Synthetic data expose the detector to compact lesions and common mimics without validation or test leakage. On VALDO Task 2, CenSynCMB achieved the best local-comparison lesion-level F1 (74.3%, p = 0.020); on external AIBL SWI, it achieved the highest local-comparison recall (88.5%, p = 0.0058) and F1 (65.0%, p = 0.0016). Together, these results support scalable CMB candidate extraction in large, unlabelled MRI cohorts, while highlighting cohort-specific calibration as the next step toward reliable burden estimation.

</details>


### [320] [WildSplat: Feedforward Gaussian Splatting from Unposed In-the-Wild Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05347)
*Xiyu Zhang,Jingyu Zhuang,Hongjia Zhai,Zizheng Yan,Jinwei Chen,Guofeng Zhang,Qingnan Fan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WildSplat is the first feedforward 3D Gaussian Splatting framework for appearance-conditioned novel-view synthesis from unposed in-the-wild images, using a dual-branch architecture to decouple geometry from appearance and achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feedforward 3D reconstruction methods fail under varying illumination conditions in unconstrained in-the-wild settings, requiring a framework that can handle inconsistent photometric conditions while enabling efficient novel view synthesis.

Method: A dual-branch architecture: (1) a geometry branch extracts appearance-invariant 3D structure and predicts camera poses, (2) an appearance branch injects target appearance cues via globally pre-modulated cross-attention, plus a joint multi-reference training strategy to prevent feature entanglement.

Result: Outperforms both optimization-based and feedforward methods, achieving state-of-the-art in in-the-wild novel view synthesis and appearance editing from sparse inputs in a single forward pass.

Conclusion: Decoupling geometry from appearance via a dual-branch design enables robust feedforward 3D reconstruction under varying illumination, setting a new standard for in-the-wild novel view synthesis.

Abstract: While feedforward 3D reconstruction excels at efficient novel view synthesis, it typically falters when faced with scenes under varying illumination. To this end, we introduce WildSplat, the first feedforward 3D Gaussian Splatting framework capable of appearance-conditioned novel-view synthesis for unposed in-the-wild images. To handle inconsistent photometric conditions, we propose a dual-branch architecture that explicitly decouples geometry from appearance. The geometry branch extracts an appearance-invariant 3D structure and jointly predicts camera poses. To govern the rendering appearance, the appearance branch injects target appearance cues into the content features via a globally pre-modulated cross-attention mechanism. To further prevent feature entanglement, we introduce a joint multi-reference training strategy that stabilizes the training process. Extensive experiments show that WildSplat surpasses existing optimization-based and feedforward methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance in in-the-wild novel view synthesis and appearance editing from sparse inputs in a single forward pass.

</details>


### [321] [Geometric Reciprocity: Unlocking Self-Supervision for Stereoscopic Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05354)
*Jingyi Lu,Kai Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-supervised framework for monocular-to-stereo conversion using a Geometric Reciprocity Theorem (GRT) that analytically computes disocclusion masks, enabling training on unlimited monocular videos without requiring stereo pairs or synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training-based stereo inpainting methods suffer from scarce stereo pair data and domain gaps from synthetic data. The authors aim to overcome this limitation by enabling self-supervised learning from abundant monocular videos.

Method: The authors introduce the Geometric Reciprocity Theorem (GRT), which states that under nearest-neighbor DIBR, the disocclusion mask when synthesizing a target view equals the mask of lost pixels when warping back to the source. This enables analytical computation of test-time disocclusion masks from monocular images, ensuring train-test consistency and supporting cycle-consistent self-supervised learning.

Result: The proposed self-supervised method achieves substantial improvements over both training-free and supervised state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The GRT enables effective self-supervised learning for stereo conversion from unlimited monocular videos, eliminating the dependency on scarce stereo data while outperforming existing approaches.

Abstract: Monocular-to-stereo conversion synthesizes stereoscopic content from 2D videos for immersive 3D experiences. In modern Depth-Image-Based Rendering (DIBR) approaches, stereo inpainting of disocclusions is the critical bottleneck. Training-based methods achieve superior quality but rely on scarce stereo pairs or synthetic data with domain gaps. We address this through the first self-supervised framework learning from monocular videos via cycle consistency. Our key contribution is the Geometric Reciprocity Theorem (GRT): under the nearest-neighbor DIBR formulation, the disocclusion mask when synthesizing a target view equals the mask of pixels lost when warping back from target to source, enabling analytical computation of test-time disocclusion masks directly from monocular images. This yields train-test consistency for the stated warping formulation, supporting self-supervised learning from unlimited monocular videos and substantial improvements over training-free and supervised state-of-the-art methods. Project page: https://visual-ai.github.io/grt/

</details>


### [322] [ReCal3R: Reliability-Calibrated Learning Rates for Streaming 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05356)
*Xinze Li,Yiyuan Wang,Pengxu Chen,Wentao Fan,Weifeng Su,Weisi Lin,Wentao Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReCal3R is a training-free calibration method for recurrent 3D reconstruction that estimates state token reliability to adjust learning rates, preventing corruption from noisy observations and achieving significant pose accuracy improvements (3.7× ATE reduction) on long sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recurrent 3D reconstruction suffers from state corruption over long sequences, where repeated updates overwrite reliable historical information with noisy or ambiguous observations, degrading performance.

Method: ReCal3R estimates token reliability from the maintained scene state, then calibrates a candidate learning rate (derived from token alignment, state reconstruction residual, and recent update pressure) into a token-wise learning rate that interpolates between a conservative base rate and the candidate rate.

Result: Applied to CUT3R as a training-free calibration rule, ReCal3R achieves strong performance on long sequences in pose, depth, and reconstruction quality, including a 3.7× reduction in ATE, with comparable runtime and memory.

Conclusion: ReCal3R effectively suppresses aggressive updates on unreliable tokens while preserving adaptability to informative frames, providing a practical calibration rule for recurrent 3D reconstruction systems.

Abstract: Streaming 3D reconstruction relies on a compact recurrent scene state to process long image streams in linear time and bounded memory. However, repeated updates can gradually corrupt this state, causing reliable historical information to be overwritten by noisy or ambiguous observations. We introduce ReCal3R, a reliability-calibrated learning rate method for recurrent 3D reconstruction. Instead of directly applying a candidate learning rate, our method estimates state token reliability from the maintained scene state and uses it to calibrate a candidate learning rate derived from token alignment, state reconstruction residual, and recent update pressure. The resulting token-wise learning rate interpolates between a conservative base rate and the candidate rate, suppressing aggressive updates on unreliable tokens while preserving adaptation to informative frames. Applied to CUT3R as a training-free calibration rule, ReCal3R reaches strong performance on long sequences in pose, depth, and reconstruction quality, including a 3.7$\times$ reduction in ATE, with comparable runtime and memory. Code is available at: https://github.com/Powertony102/ReCal3R.

</details>


### [323] [PixWorld: Unifying 3D Scene Generation and Reconstruction in Pixel Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05373)
*Sensen Gao,Zhaoqing Wang,Qihang Cao,Dongdong Yu,Changhu Wang,Jia-Wang Bian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PixWorld unifies 3D reconstruction and generation in pixel-space diffusion, eliminating the need for latent encoding (VAE/RAE), and outperforms prior methods by supervising diffusion on rendered images with a geometry perception loss from a 3D foundation model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods treat 3D reconstruction (pixel-based regression) and generation (latent diffusion) separately. Unified latent-space approaches suffer from information loss due to encoding and require pretrained VAEs/RAEs, with diffusion objectives defined on latent features rather than the actual 3D representation.

Method: PixWorld unifies 3D reconstruction and generation under a pixel-space diffusion paradigm. It supervises diffusion directly on rendered images (not latent features). Additionally, it introduces a geometry perception loss that aligns rendered views with ground truth in the geometry-aware feature space of a pretrained 3D foundation model, providing 3D structural supervision beyond 2D-level losses.

Result: PixWorld consistently outperforms prior latent-space generation methods and matches state-of-the-art reconstruction methods.

Conclusion: A unified pixel-space diffusion approach (PixWorld) is superior to latent-space methods for jointly addressing 3D reconstruction and generation, eliminating information loss from latent encoding while improving both tasks.

Abstract: 3D reconstruction and generation are commonly tackled by separate paradigms: pixel-based regression for reconstruction, and latent diffusion for generation. Recent works attempt to unify them in latent space, but with notable drawbacks: the diffusion objective is defined on latent features rather than the underlying 3D representation, and both branches suffer from information loss introduced by latent encoding, while requiring a pretrained Variational Autoencoder (VAE) or Representation Autoencoder (RAE). In this paper, we reformulate these two tasks under a unified pixel-space diffusion paradigm and introduce PixWorld, a single model that jointly addresses 3D reconstruction and generation. By supervising diffusion directly on rendered images, PixWorld removes the above limitations and aligns optimization with 3D scene fidelity. Beyond photometric and perceptual supervision that operates at the 2D image level and lacks 3D geometric awareness, we further introduce a geometry perception loss that aligns rendered views with their ground truth in the geometry-aware feature space of a pretrained 3D foundation model, providing 3D structural supervision. PixWorld consistently outperforms prior latent-space generation methods and matches state-of-the-art reconstruction methods, demonstrating the superiority of a unified pixel-space approach.

</details>


### [324] [MV-Forcing: Long Multi-View Video Generation via 4D-Grounded Spatio-Temporal Self-Forcing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05376)
*Gal Fiebelman,Hadar Averbuch-Elor,Sagie Benaim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MV-Forcing enables long, multi-view consistent dynamic scene videos by combining temporal and view-wise autoregression in a single diffusion model, using a 4D geometric bridge and joint denoising to achieve geometrically consistent generation at arbitrary lengths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video diffusion models can either generate long single-view videos (via temporal autoregression) or short multi-view videos (via bidirectional attention), but cannot produce long, multi-view consistent videos of dynamic scenes. This gap motivates the need for a unified framework.

Method: MV-Forcing composes temporal and view-wise autoregression using a 4D geometric bridge: an autoregressive 3D reconstruction model renders geometric priors for the next viewpoint, which a diffusion model refines. A joint denoising regime during training enables temporally unbounded generation. Distribution Matching Distillation with Spatio-Temporal Self-Forcing closes the exposure bias gap.

Result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world data show MV-Forcing produces geometrically consistent multi-view videos of dynamic scenes at arbitrary lengths and viewpoint counts using a single few-step student model.

Conclusion: MV-Forcing successfully unifies temporal and view-wise autoregression for long, multi-view consistent dynamic scene video generation, demonstrating effectiveness and scalability.

Abstract: Recent advances in video diffusion models have enabled either long single-view generation through temporal autoregression, or short multi-view synthesis through bidirectional attention. However, generating long, multi-view consistent videos of dynamic scenes remains unsolved. In this work, we present MV-Forcing, a framework that composes temporal and view-wise autoregression within a single diffusion model by introducing a 4D geometric bridge between sequentially generated views. Our key insight is that an autoregressive 3D reconstruction model naturally interfaces between autoregressively generated views. Given a completed source view, we reconstruct its 3D structure and render a geometric prior of the next target viewpoint, which the diffusion model refines into a high-quality video. To extend generation beyond the teacher's fixed temporal window, we introduce a joint denoising regime where both view slots are initialized from noise during training, enabling temporally unbounded generation. We distill the model via Distribution Matching Distillation with Spatio-Temporal Self-Forcing, closing the train-inference exposure bias gap for both temporal and view-sequential autoregression. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world data demonstrate that MV-Forcing produces geometrically consistent multi-view videos of dynamic scenes at arbitrary lengths and viewpoint counts using a single few-step student model.

</details>


### [325] [InFlux++: Real and Synthetic Data for Estimating Dynamic Camera Intrinsics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05389)
*Erich Liang,Caleb Kha-Uong,Chinmaya Saran,Sreemanti Dey,David W. Liu,Junhan Ouyang,Benjamin Zhou,Jia Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InFlux++ introduces a large-scale synthetic dataset (441K+ frames) and a real-world benchmark (514K+ frames) for per-frame camera intrinsic prediction from RGB video, addressing data scarcity and limited evaluation diversity; finetuning on synthetic data consistently improves focal length estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D reconstruction algorithms assume fixed camera intrinsics across videos, which fails for in-the-wild videos with dynamic intrinsics. Prior work (InFlux) established the first benchmark, but methods remain inaccurate due to scarce training data with limited intrinsics diversity and benchmarks lacking scene/camera motion diversity.

Method: InFlux++ has two components: (1) InFlux++ Synth - a large-scale procedurally generated synthetic dataset with 441K+ annotated frames from 1841 high-resolution videos, featuring diverse intrinsics via zoom/focus changes, dynamic objects, and realistic rendering effects; (2) InFlux++ Real - a real-world benchmark with 514K+ frames across 334 videos, extending InFlux with broader scenes and camera motions.

Result: Finetuning existing intrinsics prediction methods on InFlux++ Synth consistently improves focal length estimation across both InFlux++ Real and InFlux benchmarks.

Conclusion: Synthetic supervision is promising for RGB-based intrinsics prediction, and the InFlux++ dataset/benchmark can advance robustness of 3D methods to videos with dynamic intrinsics.

Abstract: Camera intrinsics are vital for recovering 3D structure from 2D video. However, most 3D algorithms assume fixed intrinsics throughout a video, an assumption that often fails for real-world in-the-wild videos. Consequently, estimating per-frame intrinsics from RGB images is critical for making 3D methods robust to videos with dynamic intrinsics. InFlux previously advanced this research direction by establishing the first real-world benchmark with per-frame ground truth intrinsics for dynamic intrinsics videos. Nevertheless, existing methods remain inaccurate due to two obstacles: (i) training data is scarce and lacks intrinsics diversity; and (ii) benchmarks, including InFlux, have limited scene and camera motion diversity, making it difficult to properly evaluate methods. To address both gaps, we present InFlux++, consisting of two components. InFlux++ Synth is a large-scale procedurally generated synthetic video dataset with 441K+ annotated frames from 1841 high-resolution videos, providing accurate per-frame ground truth intrinsics for training dynamic intrinsics prediction models; a subset also includes per-frame pose, depth, and normals. The videos feature rich intrinsics diversity through changes in camera zoom and focus, as well as dynamic objects and realistic rendering effects such as lens distortion and defocus blur. InFlux++ Real is a large-scale real-world benchmark that extends InFlux with 514K+ newly captured frames across 334 high-resolution videos, spanning a wider range of scenes and camera motions. Finetuning existing intrinsics prediction methods on InFlux++ Synth consistently improves focal length estimation across both InFlux++ Real and InFlux, suggesting that synthetic supervision is promising for RGB-based intrinsics prediction. For the dataset, benchmark, code, videos, submission instructions, and live leaderboard, please visit https://influx.cs.princeton.edu/ .

</details>


### [326] [SynCity 3000: Bootstrapping Scene-Scale 3D Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05392)
*Paul Engstler,Iro Laina,Christian Rupprecht,Andrea Vedaldi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SynCity 3000 generates large, coherent 3D scenes with fine-grained layout control by adapting an image-to-3D generator as a convolutional operator, fine-tuned on synthetic scene data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity of 3D scene training data and the inability of existing methods to produce globally coherent, arbitrarily large 3D scenes with precise layout control.

Method: (1) Propose a synthetic data engine to generate scene-like 3D training data. (2) Fine-tune an image-to-3D generator as a convolutional operator. (3) Apply the convolutional generator to a dimetric image of the entire scene derived from the user prompt.

Result: Produces large, coherent, detailed 3D scenes from diverse prompts and layouts, outperforming prior 3D scene generation approaches.

Conclusion: SynCity 3000 successfully overcomes data scarcity and coherence limitations, enabling scalable 3D scene generation with fine-grained layout control.

Abstract: We present SynCity 3000, a framework for generating 3D scenes that are globally coherent while enabling fine-grained layout control. Building on the ability of current image-to-3D generators to produce complex 3D assets from a single image, we extend this capability to the scale of entire scenes by adapting the generator to be applicable as a convolutional operator. We achieve this by fine-tuning the model on scene-like data generated by a new synthetic data engine, which we propose to address the scarcity of 3D scene data for training. The convolutional generator is then applied to a dimetric image of the entire scene, generated from the user prompt, resulting in 3D scenes of arbitrary size and complexity. Across diverse prompts and layouts, SynCity 3000 produces large, coherent, and detailed scenes, addressing the shortcomings of prior approaches to 3D scene generation.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [327] [The Binomial Channel: On Capacity, Optimal Inputs, and Beta-Binomial Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02683)
*Antonino Favano,Mohammadamin Baniasadi,Ian Zieder,Luca Barletta,Alex Dytso*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies the binomial channel with continuous input and discrete output, proving the capacity-achieving input distribution is discrete, unique, symmetric, and contains endpoints {0,1}. It derives bounds on capacity (showing C(n)=½log(nπ/(2e))+o(1)), improves support size bounds, and shows the beta-binomial output is asymptotically optimal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the capacity and optimal input/output distributions of the binomial channel where input is continuous [0,1] but output is discrete {0,...,n}, a setting where standard strict concavity arguments for uniqueness/support fail due to potential many-to-one mapping from input to output distributions.

Method: Structural analysis of optimal input distributions (discreteness, symmetry, support bounds); beta-binomial reference distribution for lower bounds; minimax redundancy for upper bounds; relative entropy and χ² divergence analysis for output distribution optimality; finite-mixture approximation lower bounds for support size.

Result: Proved capacity-achieving input is discrete, unique, symmetric around 1/2, contains {0,1}. Improved Witsenhausen upper bound on support size from O(n) to O(n/2). Capacity: C(n)=½log(nπ/(2e))+o(1). Support size lower bound: Ω(√(n log log n)).

Conclusion: The beta-binomial output distribution is asymptotically optimal and close to the optimal in relative entropy and χ² divergence. Numerical results confirm the theoretical bounds and properties of the optimal input distribution.

Abstract: We study the binomial channel with input alphabet $[0,1]$ and output alphabet ${0,\ldots,n}$. We investigate its capacity and the structure of the capacity-achieving input and output distributions. Since the output alphabet is finite whereas the input alphabet is continuous, different input distributions may induce the same output distribution; hence, uniqueness and support properties of optimal inputs do not follow from strict concavity arguments. We first establish structural properties of the capacity-achieving input distribution. In particular, we show that it is discrete, unique, symmetric around $1/2$, and contains the endpoints ${0,1}$ in its support. We also derive location constraints and bounds on the probability masses of support points, and improve the Witsenhausen-type upper bound on the support size from order $n$ to order $n/2$. We derive explicit nonasymptotic upper and lower bounds on the capacity $C(n)$. These bounds imply $C(n)=\frac{1}{2}\log\frac{nπ}{2e}+o(1).$ The lower bound is obtained by evaluating the mutual information at the reference input $X_r\sim \mathrm{Beta}(1/2,1/2)$, which induces a beta-binomial output distribution, while the upper bound follows from a minimax redundancy construction. Finally, we prove an improved lower bound on the support size of the capacity-achieving input distribution. We show that the beta-binomial output induced by $X_r$ is asymptotically optimal and close to the capacity-achieving output distribution in relative entropy and $χ^2$ divergence. We also prove a finite-mixture approximation lower bound showing that the beta-binomial output cannot be approximated too accurately by binomial mixtures with few components. Combining these results yields a support-size lower bound of order $Ω(\sqrt{n\log\log n})$, with explicit constants. Numerical results illustrate the capacity bounds and optimal input distribution.

</details>


### [328] [Cramér-Rao Bound Optimization for Massive MIMO DFRC Systems with 1-Bit DACs and ADCs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02878)
*Chenfei Huang,Mingjie Shao,Ya-Feng Liu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper optimizes transmit signal design for low-cost massive MIMO dual-function radar-communication systems using 1-bit ADCs/DACs, minimizing the Cramér-Rao bound for target angle estimation under communication QoS constraints, solved via augmented Lagrangian and spectral projected gradient methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for low-cost and power-efficient implementations of massive MIMO systems, motivating the use of 1-bit DACs/ADCs in dual-function radar-communication (DFRC) designs.

Method: Optimizes the transmit signal matrix to minimize the 1-bit Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for azimuth angle estimation under symbol-level constructive interference (CI) constraints. The nonconvex problem is transformed into continuous constrained form, solved via augmented Lagrangian method (ALM) and spectral projected gradient (SPG) with nonmonotone line search, refined by local search and cutting-plane techniques.

Result: Extensive numerical experiments verify that the proposed approach exhibits promising DFRC performance compared to benchmark schemes.

Conclusion: The proposed optimization framework effectively enables DFRC operation in massive MIMO systems with 1-bit quantization, achieving good sensing and communication performance simultaneously.

Abstract: In this paper, we investigate the dual-function radar-communication (DFRC) design for massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems equipped with 1-bit digital-to-analog converters (DACs) at the transmitter and 1-bit analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) at the receiver, motivated by the need for low-cost and power-efficient implementations of massive MIMO systems. We consider a downlink scenario where the transmit signal matrix is optimized to enhance sensing performance while satisfying communication quality of service (QoS) requirements. Specifically, the objective is to minimize the 1-bit Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for estimating the azimuth angle of a point-like target under symbol-level constructive interference (CI) constraints. We conduct an asymptotic analysis of the 1-bit Fisher information, revealing its nonmonotonicity with the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and introduce amplitude constraints to exclude regions where the objective function value is clearly suboptimal and facilitate convergence to high-quality solutions. The resulting problem is a nonconvex optimization challenge with coupled binary and linear constraints. We transform the discrete problem into a continuous constrained one, characterize its global and local minima, and tackle it via the augmented Lagrangian method (ALM) and a spectral projected gradient (SPG) method combined with nonmonotone line search. The solution is further refined via local search and cutting-plane techniques. Extensive numerical experiments verify our analysis, showing that the proposed approach exhibits promising DFRC performance compared to benchmark schemes.

</details>


### [329] [Open Problem: Is Interaction Necessary for Order-Optimal 1-bit Mean Estimation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02896)
*Ivan Lau,Jonathan Scarlett*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether interaction is necessary for optimal 1-bit mean estimation over nonparametric finite-moment classes, finding that adaptive threshold-query protocols achieve the optimal rate, two-stage querying suffices, and the non-adaptive case with arbitrary quantizers remains unresolved.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether interaction (adaptivity) is required for achieving order-optimal 1-bit mean estimation, and whether non-adaptive arbitrary quantizers can match the adaptive rate.

Method: The paper compares adaptive threshold-query protocols and general 1-bit queries with limited adaptivity (two-stage) against non-adaptive settings, analyzing the minimax rates for 1-bit mean estimation over nonparametric finite-moment classes.

Result: Adaptive threshold-query protocols achieve the order-optimal 1-bit minimax rate. The same rate is attainable using only one adaptive transition (two stages of querying). Non-adaptive threshold and interval queries are highly suboptimal.

Conclusion: A single adaptive transition is sufficient to achieve the optimal rate, but whether non-adaptive arbitrary quantizers can match this rate remains an open question.

Abstract: We ask whether interaction is necessary for order-optimal 1-bit mean estimation over nonparametric finite-moment classes. Adaptive threshold-query protocols achieve the order-optimal 1-bit minimax rate, and the same rate is attainable with general 1-bit queries using only one adaptive transition (i.e., two stages of querying). In the non-adaptive setting, threshold and interval queries are known to be highly suboptimal, but the case of arbitrary non-adaptive quantizers remains unresolved. Can such quantizers match the adaptive rate, yielding an optimal one-shot protocol? Or is the known two-stage estimator stage-optimal, with a single adaptive transition being necessary and sufficient?

</details>


### [330] [Diffusion-Based Noise-Adaptive Null-Space Channel Estimation for OFDM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03348)
*Heqiang Qi,Yirun Chen,Xiangming Meng,Chunxiao Jiang,Sheng Wu,Linling Kuang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: DANCE is a diffusion-based channel estimator for OFDM systems that handles sparse and noisy DMRS observations by decomposing the estimation into a range-space (measurement-constrained) and a null-space (learned via diffusion prior), with noise-adaptive posterior correction to reduce pilot-noise injection. It achieves superior NMSE across various 5G NR scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate OFDM channel estimation is challenging when DMRS observations are sparse, noisy, and vary across deployment scenarios, motivating the need for a robust estimator.

Method: Formulates channel estimation as a sparse linear inverse problem with range-null space decomposition. Uses a diffusion prior to reconstruct the null-space component and introduces noise-adaptive posterior correction to avoid imposing noisy pilots as exact constraints. A conditional U-Net denoiser processes complex-valued OFDM grids with separate real/imaginary channels and subcarrier-only downsampling.

Result: Simulations on 5G NR TDL and CDL models show DANCE consistently achieves lower NMSE than conventional estimators and diffusion-based posterior sampling methods across varied SNR, DMRS configurations, Doppler shifts, and train-test mismatches.

Conclusion: DANCE provides a robust, noise-adaptive diffusion-based solution for OFDM channel estimation with superior performance under diverse real-world conditions.

Abstract: Accurate channel estimation in orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) systems remains challenging when demodulation reference signal (DMRS) observations are sparse and noisy, and when DMRS configurations vary across deployment scenarios. This paper proposes DANCE (Diffusion-based Noise-Adaptive Null-space Channel Estimation), a diffusion-based channel estimator for OFDM systems. We formulate DMRS-aided channel estimation as a sparse linear inverse problem whose measurement operator is induced by the pilot pattern. The resulting range-null space decomposition separates the measurement-constrained range-space component from the unobserved null-space component, which is reconstructed through a learned diffusion prior. To avoid directly imposing noisy pilot samples as exact constraints, DANCE introduces a noise-adaptive posterior correction into the reverse diffusion process. The correction coefficient and the residual sampling variance are jointly calibrated according to the observation noise level, thereby reducing pilot-noise injection while retaining useful measurement information. We further design a conditional U-Net denoiser for complex-valued OFDM channel grids, where the real and imaginary components are represented as separate feature channels and downsampling is performed only along the subcarrier dimension. Simulations based on 5G NR tapped delay line (TDL) and clustered delay line (CDL) channel models show that DANCE achieves consistently lower normalized mean squared error (NMSE) than conventional estimators and diffusion-based posterior sampling methods under different signal-to-noise ratios, DMRS configurations, Doppler frequency shifts, and train-test distribution mismatches.

</details>


### [331] [Deep-Unfolded Wideband ISAC Beamforming for DMA Under Frequency-Selective Lorentzian Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03389)
*Abdolrasoul Sakhaei Gharagezlou,Pouya Mobaraki,Mehdi Monemi,Nhan T. Nguyen,Mehdi Rasti,Samad Ali,Matti Latva-aho*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a DMA-based wideband ISAC system using a frequency-selective Lorentzian response model, developing both a model-based PGA optimization framework and a deep-unfolded PGA variant that achieves 20x faster convergence and 7% better performance over traditional PGA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DMA-based ISAC designs rely on frequency-flat response models that are inaccurate in wideband/ultra-wideband systems, causing phase and magnitude mismatches. The authors aim to address this gap by adopting a more accurate frequency-selective Lorentzian model for DMA elements.

Method: Develops an alternating optimization framework based on projected gradient ascent (PGA) with closed-form gradients under the Lorentzian model, then proposes a deep-unfolded PGA architecture that preserves interpretability while learning hyperparameters for faster convergence.

Result: The frequency-selective Lorentzian model improves performance by ~20% over frequency-flat approximation; deep-unfolded PGA achieves up to 20x faster convergence and 7% higher objective value compared to PGA-based benchmarks.

Conclusion: The frequency-selective Lorentzian model is essential for accurate wideband DMA-ISAC system design, and deep unfolding effectively bridges model-based optimization with learning-based acceleration for superior performance.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communications (ISAC), empowered by dynamic metasurface antennas (DMAs), has emerged as a promising paradigm for next-generation wireless networks. However, existing DMA-based designs commonly rely on the frequency-flat response model for DMA elements, which is accurate only in narrowband scenarios and can cause significant phase and magnitude mismatches in wideband and ultra-wideband systems. This paper investigates a DMA-based wideband ISAC system under a frequency-selective Lorentzian response model, which accurately captures the frequency-dependent behavior of DMA elements. We aim to jointly balance the aggregate signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) of communication users and the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the radar target. To this end, we first develop an alternating optimization framework based on projected gradient ascent (PGA), deriving closed-form gradients of the objective function with respect to the digital beamforming vectors, resonance frequencies, and damping factors under the frequency-selective Lorentzian DMA model. We then propose an unfolded PGA architecture that preserves the interpretability of model-based optimization while learning key hyperparameters to accelerate convergence. Simulation results show that the frequency-selective Lorentzian model improves performance by approximately 20\% over its frequency-flat approximation. Moreover, deep-unfolded PGA achieves up to 20-fold faster convergence and improves the objective value by up to 7\% compared with PGA-based benchmarks.

</details>


### [332] [ATS-ToDMA: Adaptive Token Selection and Token-Domain Multiple Access for Cross-Modal Semantic Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03520)
*Sachin Kadam,Dong In Kim*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes ATS-ToDMA, a cross-modal semantic communication framework that jointly optimizes token selection, interference-aware scheduling, and power allocation using a Semantic SINR metric, outperforming OMA, NOMA, and other baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic communication systems neglect token-level multiple access and semantic interference caused by simultaneous transmission of semantically similar tokens.

Method: Introduces Semantic SINR (SSINR) to capture channel impairments and token similarity interference; uses a transformer-based scheduler for token allocation; derives analytical bounds on semantic interference and token occupancy; provides closed-form power allocation approximation.

Result: Significant improvements in semantic throughput and decoding accuracy, with reduced semantic interference and transmit power compared to OMA, Semantic NOMA, Random-TS, and Greedy ATS.

Conclusion: Token-domain multiple access with semantic-aware scheduling and power allocation effectively mitigates cross-modal semantic interference and enhances communication efficiency.

Abstract: Adaptive token processing has emerged as a promising approach for improving the efficiency of semantic communication systems. However, existing semantic communication frameworks largely overlook token-level multiple access and the impact of semantic interference among simultaneously transmitted semantic tokens. In this paper, we propose Adaptive Token Selection and Token-Domain Multiple Access (ATS-ToDMA), a novel cross-modal semantic communication framework that jointly performs semantic token selection, interference-aware scheduling, and semantic-aware power allocation. The proposed framework introduces a Semantic Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SSINR) metric that captures the combined effects of channel impairments and semantic interference arising from token similarity. A transformer-based scheduler is developed to allocate selected semantic tokens across token-domain transmission slots while mitigating both intra-modal and cross-modal semantic interference. To characterize the behavior of the proposed system, analytical bounds on semantic interference and feasible token occupancy are derived, together with a closed-form approximation for semantic-aware power allocation. Simulation results demonstrate significant gains in semantic throughput and semantic decoding accuracy while reducing aggregate semantic interference and transmit power compared with OMA, Semantic NOMA, Random-TS, and Greedy ATS benchmarks.

</details>


### [333] [The Informational Cost of Structure: Representational Complexity in Networked Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03608)
*Cyril Rommens,Pietro Traversa,Guilherme Ferraz de Arruda,Yamir Moreno*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper uses algorithmic information theory to define Representational Complexity, showing that any exact structural representation of dynamical systems has a universal lower bound set by the system's intrinsic algorithmic description. Graphs, hypergraphs, and other formalisms are equally expressive in principle, but meaningful differences emerge when modeling restricts admissible structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how much information is needed to represent a dynamical system in terms of structure and rules, and to determine whether different representational formalisms (graphs, hypergraphs) have fundamental advantages over one another.

Method: The authors introduce Representational Complexity - the excess description length of a structure-plus-rule model compared to the shortest possible algorithmic description of the dynamics. They apply Kolmogorov complexity theory to analyze information equivalence between graph and hypergraph representations, and provide explicit description-length estimates as computable approximations.

Result: Any representational formalism can achieve the universal lower bound for exact description, so expressive power alone does not distinguish them. Meaningful differences in representational efficiency emerge only when scientific constraints limit admissible structures and rules. The paper identifies conditions for informational equivalence and regimes (graph-preferred, hypergraph-preferred, mixed) when constraints are relaxed.

Conclusion: The choice of network representation should be framed as a question of informational cost and mechanistic transparency rather than universal expressive power.

Abstract: How much information is required to represent a dynamical system in terms of an interaction structure and an evolution rule? We address this question using algorithmic information theory. We introduce Representational Complexity, the excess description length of a structure-plus-rule model relative to the shortest possible description of the dynamics itself. This intrinsic description defines a universal lower bound: no exact structural representation can be more concise. If arbitrary rules are allowed, graphs, hypergraphs, and other formalisms can all reach this bound by shifting information between structure and dynamics, so expressiveness alone cannot distinguish them. Meaningful differences arise only when scientific modeling restricts the admissible structures and rules. Within this setting, we identify conditions under which graph and hypergraph descriptions are informationally equivalent, and show how graph-preferred, hypergraph-preferred, and mixed regimes can emerge when those conditions are relaxed. Because Kolmogorov complexity is not computable, we complement the formal results with explicit description-length estimates. Our framework reframes the choice of network representation as a question of informational cost and mechanistic transparency rather than universal expressive power.

</details>


### [334] [The Hermitian Hull Dimensions for a Class of (L,P)-Twisted Generalized Reed-Solomon Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03802)
*Chenlu Jia,Zhonghao Liang,Yue Huang,Qunying Liao*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper determines the Hermitian hull dimension of a class of (L,P)-twisted generalized Reed-Solomon codes and uses this to construct entanglement-assisted quantum error-correcting codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While the Euclidean hull of (L,P)-TGRS codes has been extensively studied, there are few results on the Hermitian hull. This gap motivates the work.

Method: The authors focus on a class of (L,P)-TGRS codes C_k(a) with a special vector a of length i(q-1). They analyze the parity of i and the relation between i and q+1, dividing into three cases to fully determine the Hermitian hull dimension.

Result: The Hermitian hull dimension of C_k(a) is fully determined across three cases based on parity of i and its relation to q+1.

Conclusion: Two classes of entanglement-assisted quantum error-correcting codes are constructed as an application of the hull analysis.

Abstract: Determining the hull of linear codes has long been an important topic in coding theory. Recently, non-generalized Reed-Solomon (in short, non-GRS) codes have attracted extensive research interest. The (L,P)-twisted generalized Reed-Solomon (in short, (L,P)-TGRS) code, which is an extension of the generalized Reed-Solomon (GRS) code, constitutes a well-studied calss of non-GRS codes.There are numerous works focusing on the Euclidean hull of (L,P)-TGRS codes, while only a few results on the Hermitian hull of (L,P)-TGRS codes. In this paper, we focus on a class of (L,P)-TGRS codes C_k(a). By taking a special class of the vector a with length i(q-1), and analyze the parity of i and the relation between i and q+1, we divide three cases to fully determine the Hermitian hull dimension of C_k(a). As an application, we construct two classes of entanglement-assisted quantum error-correcting codes.

</details>


### [335] [Function-Correcting Codes for Sum-Rank Metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03857)
*Santhi Kumari Kammila,B. Sundar Rajan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies Function-Correcting Codes (FCCs) under the sum-rank metric, deriving general bounds and an optimal construction for locally binary functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To protect function evaluations of messages against channel errors more efficiently than conventional error-correcting codes, specifically under the sum-rank metric which generalizes both Hamming and rank metrics.

Method: Derives general upper and lower bounds on optimal redundancy, establishes a Plotkin-like bound for irregular-distance codes in the sum-rank metric, and presents an explicit construction of FCSRCs for locally binary functions.

Result: Achieves optimal redundancy bounds and an explicit construction for locally binary functions under the sum-rank metric.

Conclusion: FCCs under the sum-rank metric generalize prior work on Hamming and rank metrics, with tight bounds and optimal constructions for certain function classes.

Abstract: Function-Correcting Codes (FCCs) are a class of codes designed to protect the evaluation of a specific function of a message against channel errors at a higher level than the level of protection for the message, while requiring significantly less redundancy than conventional error-correcting codes. In this paper, we study function-correcting codes under the sum-rank metric, which is a natural generalization of both the Hamming metric and the rank-metric and also we derive general upper and lower bounds on the optimal redundancy of FCCs in the sum-rank metric. In particular, we establish a Plotkin-like bound for irregular-distance codes in sum-rank metric. Furthermore, we present explicit construction of function-correcting sum-rank metric codes (FCSRCs) for locally binary functions with optimal redundancy.

</details>


### [336] [GII-Polar Codes for Block Fading Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03919)
*Fangbo Yi,Zhongjun Yang,Xinzheng He,Li Chen,Huazi Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes GII-polar codes that virtually couple two consecutive polar codewords through a nested codebook, enabling parallel decoding with reduced latency and improved error correction for block Rayleigh fading channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Polar codes suffer from high decoding latency in SC and SCL decoding, especially for long codes. The paper aims to reduce this latency while maintaining competitive decoding performance.

Method: The authors propose generalized integrated interleaved (GII)-polar codes. Two polar codewords of length N are virtually coupled via a nested codebook with lower dimension. If decoding of one interleave fails, it can be projected into decoding of the nested one, allowing parallel decoding of interleaves.

Result: Simulation results validate that GII-polar codes achieve reduced decoding latency and competent decoding performance under block Rayleigh fading channels.

Conclusion: GII-polar codes offer a practical solution to reduce decoding latency in polar codes while maintaining strong error correction capability, making them suitable for wireless communications.

Abstract: Polar codes are proven to be capacity-achieving codes, being gradually practiced in wireless communications. However, their successive cancellation (SC) and successive cancellation list (SCL) decoding incur latency challenge especially for long codes. This paper proposes the generalized integrated interleaved (GII)-polar codes for block Rayleigh fading channels, yielding both reduced decoding latency and competent decoding performance. Under the GII paradigm, two consecutive polar codewords of length $N$ are virtually coupled through a nested codebook which has a lower dimension, capable of correcting more errors. The component polar codes are known as interleaves of a GII-polar code. If decoding of an interleave fails, it can be projected into the decoding of the nested one, enabling richer error patterns to be corrected by each interleave. Since decoding of the interleaves can be performed in parallel, GII-polar codes yield a reduced decoding latency over a single polar code of length $2N$. Our simulation results validate both the decoding latency and performance merits of the proposed coding scheme under block Rayleigh fading channel.

</details>


### [337] [The Insertion List-Decoding Capacity and an Improved Bound on the Deletion List-Decoding Capacity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03989)
*Roni Con,Dean Doron,João Ribeiro*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper determines the exact capacity of list-decoding binary codes from insertions for all δ∈[0,1] using a 2-state Markov chain code construction, and provides improved upper bounds for list-decoding from deletions that match the binary deletion channel capacity as δ→0.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The capacity of list-decoding from insertions and deletions is a basic yet poorly understood aspect of coding against synchronization errors; existing bounds are loose, and even for δ>1/2 insertions, the best lower bounds barely show positivity.

Method: The authors use a symmetric 2-state Markov chain to sample codes, achieving the exact insertion capacity. They also analyze why this Markov chain approach does not beat uniformly random coding for deletions, and derive improved upper bounds for deletion capacity.

Result: Exact insertion list-decoding capacity: (1+δ)(1-h(δ/(1+δ))). For deletions, improved upper bound behaves like 1-h(δ) as δ→0, matching the binary deletion channel capacity asymptotically.

Conclusion: The work significantly tightens our understanding of list-decoding limits for synchronization errors, with exact characterization for insertions and asymptotically matching bounds for deletions in the small-δ regime.

Abstract: Informally, the capacity of list-decoding in a given adversarial error model is the largest rate at which we can list-decode with list size polynomial in the block length. The capacity of list-decoding from insertions and deletions is a basic, yet poorly understood, aspect of coding against synchronization errors. For example, when dealing with a $δ>1/2$ fraction of insertions, the best known lower bounds give little more than the fact that the capacity is positive. Beyond that regime we also only have loose bounds, with the lower bounds stemming from the analysis of uniformly random codes.
  We make progress in our understanding of the limits of list-decoding binary codes from insertions and deletions. We show that the capacity of list-decoding from a $δ$-fraction of insertions is exactly \begin{equation*}
  (1+δ)\left(1-h\left(\fracδ{1+δ}\right)\right) \end{equation*} for all $δ\in[0,1]$, achieved with high probability by a code sampled according to a symmetric $2$-state Markov chain. Curiously, we complement this by showing that such an approach does not beat uniformly random coding in list-decoding from deletions. We also give an improved upper bound on the capacity of list-decoding from a $δ$-fraction of deletions, showing in particular that it behaves like $1-h(δ)$ when $δ\to 0$. This matches the asymptotic behavior of the capacity of the binary deletion channel for vanishing deletion probability.

</details>


### [338] [SOGRAND decoding of LDPC codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04045)
*Ken R. Duffy,Jiewei Feng,Lukas Rapp,Muriel Médard*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper shows that SOGRAND, specialized for Single Parity Check codes, provides an alternative Check Node update for LDPC decoding, achieving similar or better performance than sum-product and norm-min-sum algorithms with lower complexity hardware-friendly implementations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable accurate SISO component decoding for a broad range of component codes in concatenated forward error correction codes.

Method: Specializing SOGRAND's SISO computation to Single Parity Check codes to create alternative Check Node update algorithms for LDPC decoding.

Result: Similar or better decoding performance than Gallager's sum-product algorithm and norm-min-sum, with two low complexity, hardware friendly CN update algorithms.

Conclusion: SOGRAND specialized to SPC codes offers a viable alternative for LDPC decoding with improved complexity-performance trade-offs.

Abstract: Long forward error correction codes are typically constructed by concatenating shorter component codes that are then decoded through iterative Soft-Input Soft-Output (SISO) of their components. The recently introduced Soft Output Guessing Random Additive Noise Decoding (SOGRAND) has been shown to enable accurate SISO component decoding for a broad range of component codes. Here we establish that by specializing its SISO computation to Single Parity Check codes, SOGRAND offers an alternative existing Check Node (CN) update for decoding Low Density Parity Check codes. Simulation results demonstrate similar or better decoding performance than Gallager's sum-product algorithm and norm-min-sum, while offering two distinct low complexity, hardware friendly CN update algorithms.

</details>


### [339] [Study of Graph-Based Search for Energy-Efficient Clustering in Cell-Free Massive MIMO Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04074)
*Julio Cesar Cardoso Tesolin,Rodrigo C. de Lamare*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Graph-Based Steepest Ascent (GBSA) algorithm for energy-efficient clustering and power allocation in user-centric cell-free massive MIMO networks, achieving near-optimal energy efficiency with linear complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address energy-efficient clustering and power allocation problems in user-centric cell-free massive MIMO networks, formulated as a mixed-integer fractional program.

Method: A framework combining graph-based structured search for AP clustering with continuous power allocation via fractional programming; specifically, the GBSA algorithm integrates both aspects.

Result: GBSA achieves linear per-iteration complexity while delivering energy efficiency close to the global optimum, outperforming competing techniques.

Conclusion: The proposed GBSA algorithm is a scalable and effective solution for future user-centric cell-free massive MIMO networks.

Abstract: This paper investigates energy-efficient clustering in user-centric cell-free massive MIMO networks, addressing the access point clustering and power allocation problems via a mixed-integer fractional program. We propose a framework for energy-efficient clustering and power allocation with a graph-based structured search and describe its optimum solution via an exhaustive search. We also develop the Graph-Based Steepest Ascent (GBSA) algorithm, which combines a graph-based structured search along with continuous power allocation via fractional programming. The proposed GBSA algorithm achieves linear per-iteration complexity while reaching energy efficiency close to the global optimum, outperforming competing techniques and offering a scalable solution for future networks.

</details>


### [340] [Finite-Blocklength ISAC Multiple Access: A Source-Channel Coding Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04109)
*Zhentian Zhang,Kaitao Meng,Hao Jiang,Zaichen Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper establishes fundamental limits of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) under finite-blocklength multiple-access constraints, proving that sensing is equivalent to a source-coding requirement and deriving tight bounds on energy-distortion-rate tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Future networks must serve massive populations of devices that sense and communicate simultaneously under short-packet constraints, yet the fundamental limits of ISAC in the finite-blocklength multiple-access regime remain largely undiscovered.

Method: The authors prove information-theoretic equivalence between sensing-distortion constraints and source-coding requirements, collapsing sensing and communication into joint recovery of a single effective payload. They derive a finite-blocklength achievability bound, a Fano-sum many-user converse, and a genie-aided single-user converse.

Result: The energy price of sensing fidelity grows almost linearly in dB per decade of distortion tightening and is significantly amplified by multiple-access load. Joint encoding strictly outperforms optimized orthogonal two-phase schemes, demonstrating genuine integration gain of ISAC at finite blocklength.

Conclusion: The paper closes the gap on fundamental limits of ISAC in the finite-blocklength multiple-access regime, providing a tight characterization of the minimum energy per bit and the rate-sensing tradeoff.

Abstract: Future networks must serve massive populations of devices that sense and communicate simultaneously under short-packet constraints, yet the fundamental limits of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) in the finite-blocklength multiple-access regime remain largely undiscovered. This paper closes this gap from a source-channel coding perspective. We prove that satisfying a sensing-distortion constraint is information-theoretically equivalent to a source-coding requirement, which collapses sensing and communication into the joint recovery of a single effective payload within a coded multiple-access framework. Building on this equivalence, we derive a finite-blocklength achievability bound together with a Fano-sum many-user converse and a genie-aided single-user converse, yielding a tight characterization of the minimum energy per bit and the rate-sensing tradeoff. Numerical results reveal that the energy price of sensing fidelity grows almost linearly in dB per decade of distortion tightening and is significantly amplified by the multiple-access load, and that joint encoding of the effective payload strictly outperforms an optimized orthogonal two-phase scheme, demonstrating a genuine integration gain of ISAC at finite blocklength.

</details>


### [341] [Lower Bound of Networked Control with Multiple Sensors and One Controller And The Application to Tracking Gaussian-Markov Source](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04172)
*Sijie Li,Takashi Tanaka,Hyeji Kim*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper solves a longstanding open problem by deriving the causal rate-distortion function for networked control systems with multiple encoders and a single decoder without feedback, establishing a novel directed information lower bound and proving optimality of linear encoders/decoders for LQG systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the open problem of the causal rate-distortion function for networked control systems with multiple encoders and a single decoder in the absence of feedback.

Method: The authors establish a novel directed information lower bound and prove that linear, independent encoders and linear decoders are optimal for optimizing this bound for LQG plants with quadratic cost. They reduce the infinite-dimensional optimization to a finite-dimensional problem and provide SDP formulations.

Result: The directed information lower bound is derived, optimality of linear encoders/decoders is demonstrated for LQG systems with full state observation, and the infinite-dimensional problem is reduced to a finite-dimensional one with SDP formulations.

Conclusion: The paper provides the first directed information lower bound for networked control without feedback, extends prior results on linear encoder sufficiency, and offers computationally tractable SDP formulations for Gaussian-Markov sources.

Abstract: This paper investigates the causal rate-distortion function for networked control systems with multiple encoders and a single decoder, a longstanding open problem in information and control theory. While previous work has explored the causal rate-distortion function for single-encoder and feedback-enabled networked settings, the case of networks without feedback remains unaddressed.
  We establish a novel directed information lower bound, the first derived for the networked control setting. We further demonstrate the optimality of linear, independent encoders and linear decoders for optimizing this lower bound for Linear Quadratic Gaussian (LQG) plant and quadratic cost, with the condition that the full plant state is observed when sensors are sitting together. By reducing the original infinite-dimensional optimization problem to a finite-dimensional one, our approach simplifies the analysis. Additionally, our directed information lower bound provides an alternate proof for the sufficiency of linear encoders in the single encoder and single decoder setting with side information, extending prior results in the literature. We present Semidefinite Programming formulations for the causal rate distortion function of Gaussian-Markov sources with linear side information and the singular noise matrix.

</details>


### [342] [A Gallager-Type Redundancy Bound for Binary Shannon-Fano Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04192)
*Kamila Szewczyk*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper improves the known redundancy bound for binary Shannon-Fano coding from a fixed 1 bit to a tighter, probability-dependent envelope, achieving a maximum of ~0.5651 bits for probabilities below 0.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To derive a more precise, source-probability-dependent redundancy bound for Shannon-Fano codes, going beyond the known universal bound of 1 bit.

Method: The authors use a Fano recursion approach with a min-corrected affine potential and a no-burial lemma, reducing all scalar inequalities to comparisons of integer powers.

Result: A seven-piece envelope R < f(p₁) is established, exactly matching the supremum for p₁ ≥ 1/2 and on a subinterval below 1/3, with a cap of ~0.5651 bits for p₁ < 1/2.

Conclusion: This is the first p₁-dependent redundancy bound for Fano codes, achieved through a more sophisticated analysis than typical for Huffman codes.

Abstract: Krajči, Liu, Mikeš, and Moser proved in 2015 that the redundancy of binary Shannon-Fano coding is always below one bit. We sharpen this to a bound depending on the largest source probability $p_1$: an explicit seven-piece envelope $R<f(p_1)$. The envelope equals the exact supremum of $R$ given $p_1$ for every $p_1\ge\tfrac12$ and on a subinterval below $\tfrac13$, and gives the cap $R<\tfrac52-\tfrac56\log_2 5=0.5651$ for $p_1<\tfrac12$. It is the first $p_1$-dependent redundancy bound for Fano codes. The method is more sophisticated than the approach typical for Huffman codes: Fano trees are built top-down by contiguous balanced splits and lack the sibling property. From the $R<1$ theorem the rest follows from the Fano recursion, through a min-corrected affine potential and a no-burial lemma. Every scalar inequality in the proof reduces to a comparison of integer powers.

</details>


### [343] [Orchestrating Communication, Computing, and Energy Transfer for Wireless-Powered 6G Closed-Loop Controls](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04225)
*Chengleyang Lei,Wei Feng,Yanmin Wang,Yunfei Chen,Xiaoyu Liu,Liuguo Yin,Ning Ge*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a holistic design framework for a wireless-powered sensing-communication-computing-control (SC3) system where a satellite transfers energy via RF signals to support multiple SC3 closed loops, jointly optimizing transmit power, bandwidth, computing capability, time allocation, and WPT power to minimize LQR control cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited battery capacities of devices in SC3 closed loops constrain operational duration and degrade control efficiency, especially in remote or post-disaster scenarios. Wireless power transfer (WPT) is leveraged to address this energy bottleneck.

Method: A sum LQR cost minimization problem is formulated, jointly optimizing uplink/downlink transmit power, bandwidth allocation, computing capability, communication/computing time allocation, and WPT power allocation. The problem is recast into a tractable form and solved via an iterative algorithm. Optimal solution properties are analyzed for the single-loop special case in energy-limited scenarios.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate the performance gains of the proposed scheme compared to baselines.
