Motivation: Animation principles are limited for robot design because they are unconstrained by real-world physics and focus on low-level mechanics rather than high-level considerations for embodied, interactive characters. Puppetry, with its long history of physically embodied, interactive character design, offers underexplored insights.

Method: The authors leverage puppetry texts and expert knowledge from practicing puppeteers to derive a set of puppetry principles for robot design.

Result: A set of puppetry principles that provide insights beyond and complementary to prior contributions from theater, dance, and animation.

Conclusion: Puppetry principles uniquely complement existing design frameworks (theater, dance, animation) and offer valuable, previously overlooked guidance for designing physically embodied, interactive robots.

Abstract: When designing robots' nonverbal behaviors, many researchers have turned to arts-based insights, such as Disney's Animation Principles. Yet, while these principles bear key insights into the design of like-life characters, their application to robot design is inherently limited, in part because animation is not constrained by real-world physics, and in part because animation principles focus on low level animation mechanics and not high-level design considerations for physically embodied, interactive characters. In contrast, little attention has been paid to art forms like puppetry, despite their long history of exploring morphological, behavior, and interaction design of physically embodied, interactive characters. As such, in this work we leverage puppetry texts and practicing puppeteers' expert knowledge knowledge to derive a set of puppetry principles with key insights for robot design. As we show, these insights go beyond -- and uniquely complement -- the prior insights provided by theater, dance, and animation.

</details>


### [371] [U-Joint CAAMS: Experimental Evaluation of a Universal-Joint Continuum Manipulator for Aerial Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03321)
*Anuraj Uthayasooriyan,Musab Alibrahim,Krishna Manaswi Digumarti,Fernando Vanegas,Felipe Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A universal-joint-based continuum manipulator for multi-rotor UAVs is proposed that resists out-of-plane deformation from payloads and propeller downwash, achieving 2.5-45x error reduction over a Nitinol-backbone design, demonstrated in water sampling, spraying, and object transport.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuum manipulators on multi-rotor UAVs suffer from out-of-plane bending and twisting due to payloads and propeller downwash, which degrades end-effector pose accuracy. The goal is to improve deformation resistance for reliable aerial manipulation.

Method: A tubular backbone with spring-reinforced universal joints and an integrated conduit for internal routing and fluid delivery is designed. It is evaluated in still air and under peak propeller downwash across varying payloads, benchmarked against a prior Nitinol-backbone continuum manipulator.

Result: The proposed design reduces mean error by 2.5-4x in yaw, 2-45x in y-axis, and up to 5x in roll under peak downwash compared to the NiTi-backbone design. Hover stability was analyzed via in-flight coupled-disturbance tests.

Conclusion: The universal-joint-based design significantly improves out-of-plane deformation resistance and pose accuracy during aerial manipulation, and is practically demonstrated in water sampling, spot spraying, and object transport tasks.

Abstract: Continuum manipulators mounted on multi-rotor UAVs enable compliant aerial manipulation, but payloads and propeller downwash amplify out-of-plane bending and twisting that degrade end-effector pose accuracy. To address this problem, we present a universal-joint-based continuum manipulator designed to improve resistance to out-of-plane deformation during aerial manipulation. The proposed design uses a tubular backbone with spring-reinforced universal joints and an integrated conduit for internal routing and fluid delivery. We evaluate the design in still air and under peak propeller downwash across varying payloads, and benchmark it against a prior Nitinol-backbone CM. Bench tests show improved resistance to out-of-plane deformation across all conditions. Under peak downwash, the proposed design reduces mean error by 2.5-4x in yaw, 2-45x in y-axis, and up to 5x in roll compared to the NiTi-backbone design. We further analyze hover stability through in-flight coupled-disturbance tests over varying payloads and actuation speeds, and demonstrate the system in water sampling, spot spraying, and object transport.

</details>


### [372] [Closed-loop vs. Open-loop Kalman Filter Architectures in Airborne Aided Inertial Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03338)
*Antonia Hager,Torleiv H. Bryne*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper compares closed-loop (feedback) vs. open-loop (feedforward) error-state Kalman filters for aided inertial navigation, evaluating performance trade-offs across different IMU grades through simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess the quantitative performance impacts of open-loop vs. closed-loop architectural choices in aided inertial navigation, beyond qualitative safety justifications, especially given the conflicting standards between modern research (closed-loop) and safety-critical aviation (open-loop).

Method: The authors use standard inertial mechanization in geodetic coordinates with direct position aiding, simulating various inertial sensor error characteristics from consumer to navigation grade systems to compare open-loop and closed-loop filter architectures.

Result: Simulations reveal a trade-off: open-loop filters provide smoother information fusion for high-end IMUs, while closed-loop architectures offer inherent long-term stability.

Conclusion: The architectural choice between open-loop and closed-loop filters involves a performance trade-off depending on IMU grade, with open-loop being favorable for high-end systems and closed-loop offering better stability across the board.

Abstract: Closed-loop (or feedback) error-state Kalman filters with their relatives and offspring are the state-of-the-art in modern aided inertial navigation research. Estimated inertial navigation system (INS) errors are continually fed back to the INS to correct the nominal system state before subsequent predictions. Conversely, in safety-critical aeronautical applications, open-loop (or feedforward) systems are an undisputed standard, where the inertial mechanization is strictly decoupled to allow for operational independence and fault isolation of computing units. We assess the performance impacts of this architectural choice beyond qualitative system-safety justifications using a standard inertial mechanization in geodetic coordinates and direct position aiding. Simulations using a variety of inertial sensor error characteristics, ranging from consumer to navigation grade systems, showcase the trade-off between smooth information fusion for high-end IMUs using an open-loop filter and the inherent long-term stability of the closed-loop architecture.

</details>


### [373] [Feeling the Unexpected: ResTacVLA for Contact-Rich Manipulation via Residual Tactile Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03387)
*Pengwei Zhang,Bin Xie,Ce Hao,Xinpan Meng,Xinyu Guo,Fang Deng,Long Cheng,Tiancai Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ResTacVLA reformulates tactile data as residual representations (discrepancy between visual priors and physical sensations) to prevent modality collapse in VLA models, using Vector Quantized Latent Contact Primitives and uncertainty-guided adaptive gating for contact-rich manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile perception is essential for contact-rich manipulation, but integrating it into VLA models causes modality collapse where high-bandwidth visual features dominate sparse tactile cues.

Method: (1) Residual Tactile Representation: captures discrepancy between visual prediction and actual tactile feedback, filtering out visually predictable dynamics. (2) Vector Quantized (VQ) bottleneck: discretizes residuals into Latent Contact Primitives. (3) Uncertainty-guided adaptive gating: leverages visual prior uncertainty to prioritize tactile integration during visually unreliable phases.

Result: Consistently outperforms all baselines on diverse contact-rich manipulation tasks and remains robust to unexpected dynamic disturbances.

Conclusion: Inspired by Predictive Coding, reformulating tactile data as residuals and using uncertainty-guided gating effectively resolves the bandwidth mismatch and prevents visual dominance in multimodal VLA models.

Abstract: Tactile perception is indispensable for contact-rich manipulation, yet integrating it into Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often induces modality collapse, where high-bandwidth visual features overshadow sparse tactile cues. Inspired by Predictive Coding, a neural mechanism where the brain attenuates predictable inputs to prioritize surprising stimuli, we propose ResTacVLA. Rather than treating tactile data as raw input, we reformulate it as a Residual Tactile Representation capturing the discrepancy between visual priors and physical sensations. By filtering out visually predictable dynamics, this formulation transforms sparse tactile signals into dense, high-value information gain, thereby inherently resolving the bandwidth mismatch. These residuals are discretized through a Vector Quantized (VQ) bottleneck into Latent Contact Primitives that capture critical events missed by vision. Analogous to the neural surprise signal, we leverage the uncertainty of the visual prior to adaptively gate tactile integration, prioritizing residuals specifically during visually unreliable phases to explicitly prevent visual dominance. Experimental results show that ResTacVLA consistently outperforms all baselines on a diverse set of contact-rich manipulation tasks, while remaining robust to unexpected dynamic disturbances. Project page: https://awilekong.github.io/ResTacVLA-Website/

</details>


### [374] [HiMe: Hierarchical Embodied Memory for Long-Horizon Vision-Language-Action Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03449)
*Li Ji,Siyin Wang,Pengfang Qian,Xiaopeng Yu,Yihai Tian,Zhaoye Fei,Jingjing Gong,Xipeng Qiu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HiMe introduces a Hierarchical Embodied Memory framework (Executor, Sentry, Planner) with dynamic cross-modal schema management to resolve the frequency-competence paradox, enabling real-time control with long-term reasoning and self-correction abilities in robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models fail at non-Markovian long-horizon tasks due to the "frequency-competence paradox": strong reasoning models are too slow for real-time control, while fast models lack sufficient reasoning capabilities.

Method: Proposes a three-tier hierarchical architecture: a high-frequency Executor for real-time execution, a Sentry for working memory, and a Planner for long-term strategy. Introduces a dynamic knowledge system based on cross-modal semantic schemas with "Add, Update, Delete" memory operations to maintain plasticity.

Result: Outperforms flat memory baselines on long-horizon tasks and demonstrates novel self-correction ability to adjust internal knowledge based on human preferences.

Conclusion: The hierarchical decoupling effectively resolves the real-time vs. slow-thinking conflict, enabling both responsive control and long-term reasoning in embodied agents.

Abstract: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models excel at robotic manipulation but often struggle with non-Markovian tasks requiring long-term memory and reasoning due to their reliance on immediate observations. Existing solutions face a ''frequency-competence paradox,'' where stronger reasoning models are too slow for real-time control, while faster models lack sufficient reasoning capabilities. To resolve this architectural misalignment, we propose HiMe, a Hierarchical Embodied Memory framework that decouples embodied intelligence into a high-frequency Executor for execution, a Sentry for working memory, and a Planner for long-term strategy. We also introduce a dynamic knowledge system based on cross-modal semantic schemas and active management mechanisms, allowing robots to maintain memory plasticity through ''Add, Update, and Delete'' operations. This hierarchical design effectively balances the conflict between real-time execution and slow thinking planning, significantly improving success rates in long-horizon tasks. Experiments demonstrate that this approach not only outperforms flat memory baselines but also exhibits the novel ability to self-correct its internal knowledge based on human preferences.

</details>


### [375] [ADP: Adversarial Dynamics Priors for Physically Grounded Humanoid Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03454)
*Seokju Lee,Jeongtae Lee,Jeonghyeok Lim,Jeonguk Kang,Byungwook Lee,Seungho Han,Keun Ha Choi,Dongil Park,Kyung-Soo Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ADP uses adversarial regularization on dynamics features (CoM, momentum, forces) instead of kinematic features to make humanoid locomotion control more resilient to perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion prior methods only regularize kinematic features, failing to directly enforce stability-critical dynamics features, leaving locomotion policies vulnerable to perturbations.

Method: Replace kinematic motion features with dynamics features (CoM motion, centroidal momentum, contact forces/states) for adversarial regularization; uses trajectory optimization to build reference dataset and a discriminator to evaluate policy temporal windows against this distribution.

Result: ADP improves impulse threshold (J80) by 16.7%, reduces recovery time by 47.9%, and velocity tracking error by 35.4% over AMP baseline.

Conclusion: Regularizing dynamics features via adversarial priors significantly enhances perturbation resilience in humanoid locomotion without explicit motion tracking.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose Adversarial Dynamics Priors (ADP) for perturbation-resilient humanoid locomotion control. Existing motion prior-based methods induce natural motion styles by imitating kinematic motion features, but they do not directly regularize dynamics features, such as CoM motion, centroidal momentum, contact forces, and contact states. To address this limitation, we replace kinematic motion-style feature with selected dynamics features extracted from locomotion trajectories as the target of adversarial regularization.To this end, we use trajectory optimization to construct a reference dataset and train a discriminator to evaluate whether policy-induced temporal windows are consistent with the resulting reference distribution.Without explicit motion tracking, ADP encourages policy rollouts to remain close to the reference support, even after perturbations. Experimental results show that, compared with AMP, the strongest baseline in our evaluation, ADP improves the $80\%$-success impulse threshold ($J_{80}$) by $16.7\%$, while reducing direction-averaged recovery time and velocity tracking error by $47.9\%$ and $35.4\%$, respectively.

</details>


### [376] [High-Precision Formation Control for Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Systems via Hierarchical Hybrid Physics-Informed Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03512)
*Yanzhou Li,Guangli Chen,Xiao-Meng Li,Wenjian Zhong,Yongkang Lu,Shenghuang He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical hybrid physics-informed deep reinforcement learning (HHy-PIDRL) framework for formation control of heterogeneous multi-robot systems, combining SAC-based navigation for leaders with a hybrid model-PD-DRL controller for followers, achieving 100% success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing classical control methods require precise models and struggle with uncertainties/disturbances, while end-to-end RL suffers from low sample efficiency and poor convergence.

Method: A two-layer hierarchical framework: (1) Upper layer uses SAC-based DRL for Ackermann-steering leader navigation; (2) Lower layer combines a physical feed-forward controller, PD controller, and adaptive DRL residual controller (HM-DRL) for Omnidirectional followers, with a tailored hierarchical reward function.

Result: Both upper-layer navigation and HM-DRL formation control achieve 100% success rate; ablation experiments confirm validity and credibility.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid approach effectively bridges the gap between classical control and pure RL, enabling high-precision, highly responsive formation control for HMRS.

Abstract: Existing classical control methods commonly require precise models and struggle to cope with model uncertainties and external disturbances, while end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) approaches suffer from low sample efficiency and poor convergence. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a hierarchical hybrid physics-informed deep reinforcement learning (HHy-PIDRL) framework, aiming to realize high-precision, highly responsive formation control for heterogeneous multi-robot systems (HMRSs). The proposed framework contains two layers. Specifically, first, the upper layer designs an autonomous navigation policy network for Ackermann-steering leader based on the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm. Second, the lower module integrates a high-fidelity physical feed-forward controller, a classical proportional-derivative (PD) controller, and an adaptive DRL residual controller to propose an effective hybrid model and DRL (HM-DRL)-based formation control policy network. Third, a unique hierarchical reward function is designed for training Omnidirectional followers, which effectively guides agents toward a refined, stable control policy. Experimental results demonstrate that, the success rate of both the upper-layer autonomous navigation policy network and the HM-DRL based formation control policy networks reach 100%. Meanwhile, ablation experiments are conducted to verify the validity and credibility of the proposed method.

</details>


### [377] [Current as Touch: Proprioceptive Contact Feedback for Compliant Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03529)
*Chenyang Ma,Yunchao Yao,Zhenyu Wei,Ruogu Li,Daniel Szafir,Mingyu Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a proprioception-driven framework that uses motor current and joint states (no external tactile/force sensors) to predict compliance reference positions for PD controllers, enabling dexterous hands to perform contact-rich tasks with adaptive grasping force.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dexterous manipulation solutions rely on external tactile or force sensors that are costly, fragile, and hard to deploy on low-cost robot hands, motivating the need for a sensor-free compliance approach.

Method: The method predicts a compliance reference position from motor current (as a force proxy) and joint states, then feeds it to a standard PD controller where position error generates appropriate grasping force, enabling force adaptation via proprioceptive feedback.

Result: Experiments on multiple dexterous hands across tasks (fragile object handling, sustained contact, thin-object retrieval, dynamic load adaptation) show stable compliant grasping, safer/efficient teleoperation, and improved downstream policy learning without external sensors.

Conclusion: Motor current can serve as a learnable proprioceptive contact signal for compliance reference prediction, eliminating the need for external tactile or force sensors in dexterous manipulation.

Abstract: Compliance is essential for dexterous manipulation, yet existing solutions often rely on external tactile or force sensors that are costly, fragile, and difficult to deploy on low-cost robot hands. We propose a proprioception-driven framework that learns contact-aware compliance cues from motor current and joint states. Since motor current is closely related to actuator torque, it provides an intrinsic signal for perceiving contact force, object resistance, and grasp stability without additional sensing hardware. Rather than estimating external wrenches or commanding torque, our method predicts a compliance reference position: an ideal joint-position target for a standard PD controller whose induced position error generates appropriate grasping force. This position-based formulation is compatible with mainstream teleoperation and policy-learning pipelines, while enabling the robot to adapt interaction forces from real-time proprioceptive feedback. Thus, motor current serves not only as a force proxy but also as a learnable proprioceptive contact signal for compliance reference prediction. Experiments on multiple dexterous hands and contact-rich tasks, including fragile object handling, sustained surface contact, thin-object retrieval, and dynamic load adaptation, show stable compliant grasping, safer and more efficient teleoperation, and improved downstream policy learning without external tactile or force sensors.

</details>


### [378] [CoorGrasp: Coordinated Contact Control for Adaptive Dexterous Grasping Under Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03557)
*Mingrui Yu,Yongpeng Jiang,Yongyi Jia,Ren Yi,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a tactile-driven model predictive controller for adaptive and delicate execution of dexterous grasps, addressing open-loop execution failures through coordination-aware phase separation, arm-hand coordination, and adaptive force coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work focuses heavily on grasp pose generation but neglects grasp execution. Under shape/position uncertainty, open-loop execution causes uncoordinated contacts, undesired object motion, and grasp failures.

Method: A tactile-driven model predictive controller with three key novelties: (i) coordination-aware phase separation, (ii) arm-hand coordination for position error compensation, and (iii) adaptive force coordination for balanced contact forces. An analytical model relates contact forces to robot joint motions.

Result: Validated through large-scale simulations (15k grasps, 478 objects, three robotic hands) and real-world experiments (8 objects). Achieves higher grasp success rates and reduced undesired object movements.

Conclusion: The proposed method enables adaptive and delicate execution of diverse dexterous grasps under uncertainty, integrates with existing grasp pose generation methods, and imposes no restrictions on grasp types or contact configurations.

Abstract: While recent research has focused heavily on dexterous grasp pose generation, less attention has been devoted to the execution of planned grasps. Under shape and position uncertainty, open-loop execution often yields uncoordinated contacts, causing undesired in-hand object motion and even grasp failures. To address this, this paper proposes a tactile-driven model predictive controller for adaptive and delicate execution of diverse dexterous grasps. Our approach emphasizes multi-contact coordination across both approaching and grasping phases, with three key novelties: (i) coordination-aware phase separation, (ii) arm-hand coordination to compensate for position errors, and (iii) adaptive force coordination to increase contact forces in a balanced manner. An analytical model is employed to relate contact forces to robot joint motions for predictive control. Our formulation imposes no restrictions on grasp types or contact configurations and integrates seamlessly with state-of-the-art grasp pose generation methods. We validate the approach through large-scale simulations involving 15k grasps across 478 objects on three robotic hands, and real-world experiments on 8 objects. Results demonstrate that our method achieves higher grasp success rates and reduced undesired object movements.

</details>


### [379] [Cross-Embodiment Robot Manipulation via a Unified Hand Action Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03570)
*Luis Felipe Casas,Robert Teal,Keval Shah,Abhijit Tadepalli,Wanxin Jin,Yu Xiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: UHAS is a sphere-based action representation that enables cross-embodiment dexterous manipulation policy transfer across different robotic hands (Allegro, LEAP, Shadow, MANO) using reinforcement learning and cascade inverse kinematics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot manipulation policies are typically tied to specific robotic hand embodiments, limiting the transfer of learned behaviors across platforms with different kinematic structures.

Method: UHAS represents actions as geometric deformations of a canonical sphere and uses Cascade Inverse Kinematics (CIK) to map to embodiment-specific joint configurations; trained via reinforcement learning for in-hand cube reorientation.

Result: Effective dexterous manipulation, zero-shot transfer to unseen hands, rapid finetuning across embodiments, and successful real-world deployment on multiple hands (Allegro, LEAP, Shadow, MANO).

Conclusion: UHAS enables stable dexterous control and cross-embodiment policy transfer across robotic hands.

Abstract: Robot manipulation policies are typically tied to specific robotic hand embodiments, limiting the transfer of learned behaviors across platforms with different kinematic structures. In this work, we propose the Unified Hand Action Space (UHAS), a sphere-based unified action representation for cross-embodiment dexterous manipulation. UHAS represents robotic hand actions as geometric deformations of a canonical sphere and uses a Cascade Inverse Kinematics (CIK) algorithm to map the shared representation to embodiment-specific joint configurations. Using reinforcement learning, we train dexterous manipulation policies directly in the proposed action space for in-hand cube reorientation tasks. We evaluate our method in both simulation and real-world experiments across multiple robotic hands, including the Allegro Hand, LEAP Hand, Shadow Hand, and MANO Human Hand. Experimental results demonstrate effective dexterous manipulation, zero-shot transfer to unseen hands, rapid finetuning across embodiments, and successful real-world deployment. Our experiments show that the proposed UHAS representation enables stable dexterous control and cross-embodiment policy transfer across robotic hands.

</details>


### [380] [ROBOCYCLE: Autonomous Dual-Arm Robotic Manipulation and Coordination for Recycling Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03616)
*Rubén de J. Hilario-Cruz,Jesus A. García-González,Enrique Coronado,Arturo E. Cerón-López*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ROBOCYCLE is a dual-arm robotic recycling platform using multi-view RGB-D perception, transformer-based instance segmentation, and 6-DoF grasp planning to achieve 90.3% grasp success and 84.3% task success for sorting transparent, deformable, and cluttered urban waste.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Escalating urban waste volumes and labor shortages demand automated sorting, but current robots fail at 3D perception and manipulation of transparent, deformable, or cluttered objects.

Method: Multi-view RGB-D perception + RF-DETR transformer-based instance segmentation + Anygrasp SDK for 6-DoF grasp planning on segmented point clouds, deployed on a dual-arm robotic platform.

Result: 90.3% grasp success rate, 84.3% overall task success rate, with capability for complex coordinated tasks like unscrewing PET bottle caps.

Conclusion: The platform offers a scalable solution for autonomous waste management in real-world human environments, meeting Tokyo metropolitan recycling standards.

Abstract: As urban waste volumes escalate and labor shortages intensify, automated waste sorting systems are becoming a necessity. However, current robotic solutions often struggle with the 3D perception and manipulation of transparent, deformable, or cluttered objects. This work introduces ROBOCYCLE, an autonomous dual-arm robotic recycling platform designed to meet the recycling standards of the Tokyo metropolitan area. Our approach integrates multi-view RGB-D perception, transformer-based instance segmentation using RF-DETR, and 6-DoF grasp planning via the Anygrasp SDK. By processing segmentated point clouds, the system generates robust candidate poses for irregular and deformable waste. The system achieved a 90.3% grasp success rate and 84.3% overall task success rate, effectively performing complex coordinated tasks such as unscrewing PET bottle caps. The proposed platform offers a scalable solution for autonomous waste management in real-world human environments.

</details>


### [381] [CoRE-VLA: Towards Scalable and Robust Vision-Language-Action Modeling via Conditional Routing of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03693)
*Haozhe Zhang,Sixian Li,Yifei Zhang,Zezheng Huai,Hao Chen,Chunhua Shen,Jingjing Gong,Xipeng Qiu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoRE-VLA is a VLA framework that uses context-conditioned sparse computation to handle heterogeneous and missing sensor inputs, achieving robust performance across diverse tasks and long-horizon manipulation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world robotic deployment faces heterogeneous sensor configurations, unexpected sensor failures, and embodiments lacking certain sensors by design. Existing VLA policies couple action generation to fixed sensor sets through dense computation, making them brittle when sensors are missing and limiting specialization across tasks.

Method: CoRE-VLA formulates action generation as context-conditioned sparse computation. Sensor availability gates modality-specialized experts, enabling graceful degradation under missing sensors without retraining. Task intent further routes action-side representations to task-relevant experts for improved specialization.

Result: CoRE-VLA achieves strong results on LIBERO, RoboCasa GR1 Tabletop, and real-world dual-arm manipulation, outperforming a dense-action-generator ablation and a strong pretrained VLA baseline, including zero-shot generalization to unseen scenarios.

Conclusion: CoRE-VLA demonstrates that sparse, context-conditioned computation enables robust VLA policies that can exploit auxiliary sensors when available while remaining reliable under sensor absence, making it suitable for practical robotic deployment.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have advanced generalist robotic manipulation, yet real-world deployment reveals a fundamental challenge: robots are equipped with diverse and heterogeneous sensor configurations, auxiliary sensors can fail unexpectedly during operation, and different robot embodiments often lack certain sensors by design. A unified policy that can exploit auxiliary perceptual inputs when available while remaining reliable under sensor absence, whether incidental or by design, is therefore essential for practical deployment. However, existing VLA policies couple action generation to a fixed sensor set through shared dense computation, making them brittle when sensors are missing and limiting their ability to specialize across diverse tasks and long-horizon behaviors. We propose CoRE-VLA, a scalable and robust VLA framework that formulates action generation as context-conditioned sparse computation. Sensor availability gates modality-specialized experts, enabling graceful degradation under missing sensors without retraining. Task intent further routes action-side representations to task-relevant experts, improving specialization across diverse tasks and long-horizon subgoals. While the framework is designed to accommodate different auxiliary sensors, we focus on depth as a representative and practically important auxiliary modality in our experiments. Experiments on LIBERO, RoboCasa GR1 Tabletop, and real-world dual-arm manipulation show that CoRE-VLA achieves strong results on long-horizon and multi-task benchmarks, and outperforms both a dense-action-generator ablation and a strong pretrained VLA baseline, including in zero-shot generalization to unseen scenarios. Modality analysis shows that CoRE-VLA can exploit auxiliary depth when available while remaining robust when depth is unavailable during deployment.

</details>


### [382] [Lost in Time? Continuous Symmetry and Identifiability in Aided Inertial Navigation with Unknown Measurement Delays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03699)
*Jonathan Kelly,Phone Thiha Kyaw,Mattew Giamou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper studies the identifiability of unknown constant time delays in multisensor aided navigation systems, showing that identifiability depends on trajectory shape and that uninformative trajectories form a larger class than previously known, with analysis grounded in the special Galilean group and Lie group estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multisensor systems suffer from unknown relative time delays between sensors, which degrade state estimation accuracy. The authors aim to understand when these delays can be uniquely identified (calibrated online) in aided navigation systems.

Method: The authors use the special Galilean group to characterize degenerate trajectories that prevent unique delay recovery, relating them to continuous symmetries of the delayed measurement model and comparing with linearized Jacobian-based analysis.

Result: The class of trajectories for which identifiability fails is larger than previously reported. Identifiability depends critically on both temporal measurement structure and vehicle trajectory shape—some trajectories are informative while others are not.

Conclusion: The geometric insight from the Galilean group framework provides a deeper understanding of identifiability failures in delayed measurement systems on Lie groups, with implications beyond aided navigation to broader estimation problems.

Abstract: In many multisensor systems, measurements from different sensors are subject to unknown relative time delays. Accurate state estimation requires that delays be accounted for and, when possible, calibrated online. We consider the case of aided navigation, where measurements from a single aiding sensor are subject to an unknown but constant delay relative to the inertial measurement stream, and study the identifiability of the resulting system. Critically, identifiability depends not only on the temporal structure of the measurements, but also on the shape of the vehicle trajectory: some trajectories are sufficiently informative to support unique recovery of the delay and the navigation state, while others are not. Using the special Galilean group, we characterize these uninformative (or degenerate) trajectories and relate them to a continuous symmetry of the delayed measurement model, providing geometric insight into identifiability failures. We show that the class of trajectories for which identifiability fails is larger than previously reported, and connect our characterization to the familiar linearized, Jacobian-based analysis. Although our development is motivated by aided navigation, the underlying ideas apply more broadly to estimation problems on Lie groups with delayed measurements.

</details>


### [383] [OmniTacTune: Policy-Agnostic Real-World RL for Tactile Residual Adaptation of Visual Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03723)
*Kelin Yu,Haode Zhang,Harish Ravichandar,Yunhai Han,Ruohan Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OmniTacTune adapts tactile feedback to pretrained visual policies using RL-based residual correction, improving contact-rich manipulation success from 5-40% to 85-100% in 40-80 minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual policies learned from diverse video sources fail in contact-rich manipulation due to lack of force and contact geometry information. Tactile sensing provides complementary signals, but tactile data is costly to collect and hard to generalize across sensors, robots, and tasks.

Method: A two-stage policy-agnostic RL pipeline: (1) bootstraps tactile-aware learning from autonomous base-policy rollouts, (2) learns a lightweight tactile residual policy through online interaction to correct visual base policies.

Result: Across four real-world contact-rich tasks, improves visual base policies from 5-40% success to 85-100% within 40-80 minutes. Generalizes across diverse tasks, visual base policies, and tactile representations.

Conclusion: OmniTacTune offers an efficient path for adapting tactile feedback to scalable visual robot policies in contact-rich manipulation.

Abstract: Visual policies learned from human videos, teleoperation, and robot demonstrations offer scalable motion priors, but often fail in contact-rich manipulation, where success significantly depends on local force and contact geometry. Tactile sensing provides these complementary signals, yet tactile data remain costly to collect and hard to generalize across sensors, robots, and tasks. We introduce OmniTacTune, a policy-agnostic real-world RL pipeline that adapts tactile feedback to pretrained visual policies through residual correction. OmniTacTune uses a two-stage design: it first bootstraps tactile-aware learning from autonomous base-policy rollouts, then learns a lightweight tactile residual policy through online interaction. Extensive experiments show that OmniTacTune generalizes across diverse contact-rich tasks, visual base policies, and tactile representations. Across four real-world contact-rich tasks, it improves visual base policies from 5-40% success to 85-100% within 40-80 minutes, demonstrating an efficient path for adapting tactile feedback to scalable visual robot policies. Project page: https://colinyu1.github.io/omnitactune-site/

</details>


### [384] [Look Before You Leap: Distilling Tree Search into Action Evaluation for Frozen VLA Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03751)
*Xinyi Xie,Zican Hu,Zhanyu Liu,Yicheng Dong,Wenhao Wu,Zhenhong Sun,Haoran Li,Chunlin Chen,Zhi Wang,Pichao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SVA (Search, Value, Act) is a framework that improves VLA robot policies by decoupling action generation from action evaluation. It uses Monte-Carlo tree search to explore the VLA's output distribution, distills this into a lightweight Q-value model for consequence evaluation, and selects the best action via uncertainty-regularized Q-values—all while keeping the VLA frozen. This enables a 9B model to outperform a 27B model at lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLAs exhibit fragile generalization compared to LLMs/VLMs. Post-training methods improve task-specific performance but narrow generalist capabilities. A key bottleneck is that VLA failures stem not only from action generation but also from action evaluation—frozen VLAs already contain competent behaviors in their output distribution (pass@1: 33% → pass@32: 92%).

Method: SVA has three stages: (1) Search: Monte-Carlo tree search in simulation explores the frozen VLA's output distribution to collect diverse trajectories with empirical returns. (2) Value: A lightweight Q-value model is distilled from these trajectories to predict expected consequences of candidate actions. (3) Act: At deployment, the frozen VLA proposes multiple action candidates, and the evaluator selects the best one via uncertainty-regularized Q-values—no simulator access required.

Result: SVA consistently improves generalization on unseen tasks and exhibits strong test-time scaling behavior. A 9B VLA with SVA outperforms a 27B VLA by 7 points while achieving 27% lower inference latency, demonstrating that scaling test-time evaluation is more cost-effective than scaling model size.

Conclusion: Decoupling action proposal from consequence evaluation preserves the VLA backbone's generalization capacity while substantially improving task success rates. Scaling test-time evaluation compute is more cost-effective than scaling model size for VLA-based embodied agents.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models acquire broad embodied capabilities through large-scale pretraining, yet their generalization remains far more fragile than that of LLMs and VLMs. The prevailing remedy, post-training via supervised fine-tuning or reinforcement learning, improves task-specific performance but narrows the generalist capability that makes pretraining valuable. We identify a key bottleneck: VLA failures stem not only from action generation but also from action evaluation. A diagnostic pass@k study confirms that frozen VLAs already contain competent behaviors in their output distribution, with overall success rates rising from 33% at pass@1 to 92% at pass@32. Inspired by this, we propose SVA (Search, Value, and Act), a simple framework that equips frozen VLA policies with long-term consequence awareness. SVA first uses Monte-Carlo tree search in simulation to fully explore the VLA's output distribution and collect diverse trajectories annotated with empirical returns; this knowledge is then distilled into a lightweight Q-value model that predicts the expected consequence of candidate actions; at deployment, the frozen VLA proposes multiple candidates and the evaluator selects the one with the highest uncertainty-regularized Q-value, requiring no simulator access. By decoupling action proposal from consequence evaluation, SVA preserves the generalization capacity of the VLA backbone while substantially improving task success rates. Experiments across embodied benchmarks show that SVA consistently improves generalization on unseen tasks and exhibits strong test-time scaling behavior. Strikingly, SVA enables a 9B VLA to outperform a 27B VLA by 7 points at 27% lower inference latency, suggesting that scaling test-time evaluation is more cost-effective than scaling model size.

</details>


### [385] [Occluding the Solution Space: Planner-Agnostic Adversarial Attacks on Tolerance-Aware Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03758)
*Keke Tang,Tianyu Hao,Weilong Peng,Hao Jiang,Feng Wu,Peican Zhu,Jianmin Ji,Zhihong Tian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a planner-agnostic attack framework for robotic manipulation that uses a kinematic occupancy heatmap (offline) to characterize workspace capabilities, then formulates adversarial obstacle placement as a budgeted maximum-coverage problem (online) to induce planning failures without requiring planner-in-the-loop queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial attacks on motion planning are limited by restrictive exact-pose objectives and reliance on planner-in-the-loop queries, making them computationally expensive and less generalizable.

Method: (1) Offline: Build a kinematic occupancy heatmap encoding feasible trajectory density and robustness priors without invoking a specific planner. (2) Online: Formulate attack as budgeted maximum-coverage optimization with explicit geometric constraints to strategically deploy adversarial obstacles.

Result: The method reliably induces planning failures, significantly outperforming planner-in-the-loop baselines in both computational efficiency and attack efficacy across simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: A planner-agnostic, tolerance-aware attack framework is more effective and efficient for evaluating the intrinsic robustness of robotic manipulation systems.

Abstract: Adversarial attacks on motion planning are crucial for evaluating and quantifying the intrinsic robustness of robotic manipulation. However, existing approaches are typically limited by restrictive exact-pose objectives and their reliance on planner-in-the-loop queries. To address these limitations, we propose a planner-agnostic attack framework for tolerance-aware manipulation. Our approach shifts the evaluation paradigm to task-level feasibility over goal regions, efficiently inserting adversarial obstacles without requiring oracle access to the victim system. Offline, we characterize the robot's intrinsic workspace capabilities via a kinematic occupancy heatmap, which encodes the density of feasible trajectories and robustness priors without invoking a specific planner. Online, we formulate the attack as a budgeted maximum-coverage optimization, strategically deploying obstacles subject to explicit geometric constraints to occlude the solution space. Extensive experiments across simulation and real-world scenarios demonstrate that our method reliably induces planning failures, significantly outperforming planner-in-the-loop baselines in both computational efficiency and attack efficacy.

</details>


### [386] [From Region Arrival to Instance-Level Grounding in Vision-and-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03792)
*Xiangyu Shi,Ruoxi Yang,Wei Tao,Jiwen Zhang,Yanyuan Qiao,Qi Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLN agents often fail at object-level grounding despite passing standard success metrics. The authors propose REALM, a plug-and-play module that improves the final 3 meters of navigation by decoupling fine-grained approaching from long-horizon planning, and introduce REVERIE-AIM with object-instance-level goals. REALM consistently boosts proximity precision and visual grounding across four VLN backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLN evaluation protocols reward stopping within a 3-meter radius but ignore final orientation and target visibility, creating a "Last-3-Meter Grounding Gap" where agents pass success criteria yet fail to establish reliable object-level grounding.

Method: REALM (Region-to-Entity Alignment for Last-3-Meter Navigation): a plug-and-play, architecture-agnostic refinement module that decouples fine-grained target approaching from long-horizon navigation, equipped with a visibility-aware stopping strategy. Also constructs REVERIE-AIM, a dataset with object-instance-level goals and 180K short-horizon training samples.

Result: Extensive evaluations across four diverse VLN backbones show that REALM consistently improves proximity precision and visual grounding success, demonstrating broad applicability.

Conclusion: The Last-3-Meter Grounding Gap is a critical limitation in VLN evaluation. REALM effectively mitigates this gap, and the proposed instance-centric metrics (proximity precision, target visibility, final-view grounding) enable more rigorous assessment of grounding quality.

Abstract: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) agents may satisfy conventional success criteria while still failing to establish reliable object-level grounding, because current evaluation protocols mainly reward stopping within a 3-meter radius and largely ignore the agent's final orientation and target visibility. We formalize this limitation as the Last-3-Meter Grounding Gap and introduce three instance-centric metrics to quantify proximity precision, target visibility, and final-view grounding. To mitigate this gap, we propose REALM (Region-to-Entity Alignment for Last-3-Meter Navigation), a plug-and-play, architecture-agnostic refinement module that decouples fine-grained target approaching from long-horizon navigation. REALM uses a visibility-aware stopping strategy to reduce premature termination and improve final viewpoint alignment. We further construct REVERIE-AIM, which provides object-instance-level goals and 180K short-horizon training samples for final-stage target approaching. Extensive evaluations across four diverse VLN backbones show that REALM consistently improves proximity precision and visual grounding success, demonstrating its broad applicability.

</details>


### [387] [ObjRetarget: An Object-Aware Motion Retargeting Framework with Anthropomorphic Arm Constraints and Polyhedral Hand Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03828)
*Yuanchuan Lai,Qing Gao,Ziyan Liang,Junjie Hu,Zhaojie Ju*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ObjRetarget is a human-to-robot motion retargeting framework that uses anthropomorphic arm constraints and structured hand-object geometric modeling (polytope clusters with geometric invariants) to learn dexterous manipulation from human videos, achieving improved success rates and contact stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing retargeting methods either ignore explicit contact modeling or rely on reinforcement learning, resulting in limited accuracy and generalization for learning robot dexterous manipulation from human videos.

Method: ObjRetarget integrates (1) anthropomorphic arm trajectory constraints with redundancy-aware optimization for arm motion, and (2) polytope cluster-based multi-finger contact representation with geometric invariants for stable hand-object contact preservation.

Result: Real robot experiments show improved manipulation success rates and contact stability across multiple dexterous tasks, with strong generalization to different demonstrations, object poses, and task settings.

Conclusion: Explicit structured geometric modeling of hand-object contacts combined with anthropomorphic arm constraints significantly improves retargeting accuracy and generalization for robot dexterous manipulation learning from human videos.

Abstract: Learning robot dexterous manipulation from human manipulation videos requires reliably retargeting human intent to executable robot actions while maintaining stable hand-object contact, which remains a key challenge in embodied intelligence. Existing retargeting methods often ignore explicit contact modeling or rely on reinforcement learning, resulting in limited accuracy and generalization. To address this, we propose ObjRetarget, a human-to-robot motion retargeting framework for learning robot dexterous manipulation from human videos, which integrates anthropomorphic arm trajectory constraints with structured hand-object geometric modeling. For arm motion, reference trajectories extracted from human videos are used for initialization, followed by anthropomorphic constraints and redundancy-aware optimization to generate natural and accurate movements. For hand manipulation, ObjRetarget represents multi-finger contacts using polytope clusters and preserves contact structure through geometric invariants to improve stability. Experiments on real robots show that ObjRetarget improves manipulation success rates and contact stability across multiple dexterous tasks, and generalizes well to different demonstrations, object poses, and task settings.

</details>


### [388] [High-Fidelity One-Step Generative Visuomotor Policy via Recursive Correction, Frequency Consistency, and Contrastive Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03865)
*Yuran Chen,Xinye Cai,Zhonglin Gong,Yang Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A high-fidelity one-step generative visuomotor policy framework (RCAF + DTFC + CFM) that matches 10-step generative policy performance with only 1 forward pass, reducing inference latency for robot control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing one-step acceleration methods for generative robotic policies compress the entire generation into a single large update, causing spatial deviation, frequency distortion, and mode averaging, which degrades performance.

Method: Proposes three complementary mechanisms: (1) Recursive Consistent Action Flow (RCAF) for correcting spatial truncation errors, (2) Dual-Timestep Frequency Consistency (DTFC) for preserving high-frequency details, and (3) Contrastive Flow Matching (CFM) for separating entangled multimodal action distributions.

Result: Achieves competitive or superior performance compared to strong 10-step generative policy baselines on RoboTwin, RoboTwin 2.0, Adroit, DexArt, and real-world robot platforms, requiring only 1 NFE.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables low-latency, high-fidelity one-step visuomotor control, effectively addressing the key limitations of prior one-step acceleration methods.

Abstract: Generative models such as diffusion and flow matching have advanced robotic visuomotor policies by modeling multimodal action distributions, but their multi-step sampling or ODE solving introduces inference latency. Existing one-step acceleration methods often compress the whole generation process into a single large update, leading to spatial deviation, frequency distortion, and mode averaging. This paper proposes a high-fidelity one-step generative visuomotor policy framework that addresses these issues with three complementary mechanisms. Recursive Consistent Action Flow (RCAF) uses recursive correction to compensate for spatial truncation errors and align one-step predictions with refined flow trajectories. Dual-Timestep Frequency Consistency (DTFC) preserves high-frequency manipulation details through adaptive spectral consistency across flow timesteps. Contrastive Flow Matching (CFM) separates entangled action flows with a margin-based repulsive objective, reducing ambiguous actions in multimodal manipulation. Experiments on RoboTwin, RoboTwin 2.0, Adroit, DexArt, and real-world robot platforms show that the proposed method achieves competitive or superior performance compared with strong 10-step generative policy baselines while requiring only one forward pass (1 NFE), enabling low-latency visuomotor control.

</details>


### [389] [LH-AVLN: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Audio-Visual-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03920)
*Rufeng Chen,Yue Chang,Zili Shao,Zhaofan Zhang,Hechang Chen,Hui Xiong,Sihong Xie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces LH-AVLN, a new benchmark for Long-Horizon Audio-Visual-Language Navigation with multi-goal missions, heterogeneous goal specifications (category, language, image), and persistent spatialized audio cues. The authors also propose PAG-Nav, a training-free reference agent using temporal semantic maps and progressive goal-state planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long-horizon navigation benchmarks are acoustically silent, while audio-visual tasks focus on single goals. There is a need for benchmarks combining long-horizon missions, heterogeneous goal specifications, and persistent spatialized audio cues.

Method: LH-AVLN benchmark: agents receive 2-4 goal missions (ordered/unordered) with category, language description, or reference image specifications, using RGB-D, pose, and binaural audio in 3D indoor environments. PAG-Nav: training-free agent with temporal uniform semantic maps and progressive goal-state planning.

Result: Existing vision-language, memory-based, and audio-visual agents struggle with LH-AVLN missions. PAG-Nav provides a stronger diagnostic baseline but substantial room for improvement remains.

Conclusion: LH-AVLN reveals significant gaps in current navigation agents' capabilities for long-horizon audio-visual-language tasks, and PAG-Nav offers a competitive reference while highlighting future research opportunities.

Abstract: Embodied navigation is moving toward long-horizon missions, yet existing long-horizon benchmarks are largely acoustically silent, and audio-visual navigation tasks typically focus on a single goal. We introduce LH-AVLN, a benchmark for Long-Horizon Audio-Visual-Language Navigation that combines multi-goal mission execution, heterogeneous goal specifications, and persistent spatialized acoustic cues. In LH-AVLN, an agent receives a global mission of two to four goals specified by category, language description, or reference image, and navigates with RGB-D observations, pose, and binaural audio in indoor 3D environments. The benchmark supports both ordered and unordered missions, where alternating goal-associated sounds can guide non-line-of-sight search but may also become distractors as mission progress changes. We further develop PAG-Nav, a training-free reference agent that maintains a temporal uniform semantic map and performs progressive goal-state planning, using sound for search while reserving completion for visual-semantic verification. Experiments show that existing vision-language, memory-based, and audio-visual agents struggle to complete full LH-AVLN missions, and that PAG-Nav provides a stronger diagnostic baseline while leaving substantial room for future progress.

</details>


### [390] [WSA$_1$: a 3D-Centric World-Spatial-Action Model for Generalizable Robot Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03941)
*Jiahao Jiang,Jianing Zhang,Zhenhan Yin,Ruidong Chen,Sen Wang,Zhaoshu Yu,Pengpeng Zeng,Xiaofeng Cao,Xuanhan Wang,Jingkuan Song,Heng Tao Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WSA$_1$ introduces a 3D-Centric World-Spatial-Action modeling paradigm for robot foundation models, enabling 3D-aware visual reasoning and world-action joint modeling. It achieves competitive performance with only 1k hours of real robot data (6k total), improving +20% over state-of-the-art RFMs on real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RFMs lack inherent ability to reason about physical dynamics and causal effects of robot behaviors on the 3D world, creating a mismatch between 2D-centric perception and 3D-centric embodied interaction, which limits generalization.

Method: Proposes 3D-Centric World-Spatial-Action modeling paradigm: (1) learns 3D world-aware visual thought for future behaviors, (2) models mutual constraints between 3D world state transitions and robotic actions for enhanced generalization.

Result: 93% success rate on RoboTwin2.0 benchmark; +20% average boosted performance over SOTA RFMs on real-world tasks; achieves data-efficient pre-training with 6k hours total (only 1k hours real robot data).

Conclusion: Generalizable RFMs can be achieved without large-scale real robot data when paired with 3D-centric world-action joint modeling, offering a practical and affordable pathway to generalist robotic systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in embodied AI have established robot foundation models (RFMs) as the dominant approach for generalist robotic systems to date. By leveraging imitation learning on extensive robot demonstrations, RFMs have achieved impressive capabilities in mapping visual observations and language instructions to continuous robotic actions. However, current RFMs lack an inherent ability to reason about physical dynamics and the causal effects of robot behaviors on the 3D physical world. This creates a fundamental mismatch between 2D-centric visual perception and 3D-centric embodied interaction, severely limiting the generalization ability of RFMs in real-world tasks.To address this gap, we present WSA$_1$, a novel RFM built upon proposed 3D-Centric World-Spatial-Action modeling paradigm. It not only learns 3D world-aware visual thought for future robot behaviors, but also models mutual constraints between 3D world state transitions and robotic actions to enhance behavior generalization. Notably, WSA$_1$ achieves highly data-efficient pre-training with 6k hours of expert demonstration data (only 1k hours from real robot), while delivering competitive manipulation performance (93% success rate) on RoboTwin2.0 simulation benchmark and achieving +20% average boosted performance over state-of-the-art RFMs on real-world robot control tasks. These results reveal that generalizable RFM can be attained without large-scale real robot data when paired with 3D-centric world-action joint modeling, which offers a practical and affordable pathway to generalist robotic systems.

</details>


### [391] [Worldscape-MoE: A Unified Mixture-of-Experts World Model for Scalable Heterogeneous Action Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03964)
*Jianjie Fang,Yongyan Xu,Ziyou Wang,Chen Gao,Yuchao Huang,Zhaolu Wang,Rongze Tang,Mingyuan Jia,Baining Zhao,Weichen Zhang,Xin Zhang,Haisheng Su,Yu Shang,Wei Wu,Xinlei Chen,Yong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Worldscape-MoE is a Mixture-of-Experts world model built on Diffusion Transformers that unifies heterogeneous action controls (locomotion, robotic manipulation, egocentric hand control) into a single framework, demonstrating that multi-modal supervision improves individual control capabilities and scales well.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video generation world models are fragmented across isolated control interfaces (camera trajectories, robot actions, hand-joint signals), lacking a unified framework to absorb heterogeneous action supervision while preserving a shared world dynamics model. This fragmentation is becoming a scaling bottleneck.

Method: Worldscape-MoE is a Mixture-of-Experts model built on Diffusion Transformers, featuring modality-aware control injection, shared and control-specific expert networks, and a progressive MoE tuning strategy that supports continual extension to new action modalities.

Result: Strong results on WorldArena, improved locomotion and hand-control metrics, robust out-of-distribution generalization, and demonstrated scaling behavior as additional control data and experts are integrated. Heterogeneous supervision improves rather than interferes with individual control capabilities.

Conclusion: Heterogeneous action supervision can be unified in a shared world model framework, and multi-modal training benefits individual control tasks rather than causing interference, suggesting a scalable path forward for embodied intelligence systems.

Abstract: World models are rapidly becoming a core infrastructure for embodied intelligence and interactive agents: they provide controllable simulators in which agents can perceive, act, forecast, and acquire scalable experience. Yet current video generation world models are still organized around isolated control interfaces, such as camera trajectories, robot actions, or hand-joint signals. This fragmentation is increasingly a scaling bottleneck. The central challenge is not the absence of controllable generators, but the lack of a unified and extensible learning framework that can absorb heterogeneous action supervision while preserving a shared model of world dynamics. In this work, we introduce Worldscape-MoE, a Mixture-of-Experts world model built on Diffusion Transformers for scalable heterogeneous action control. Our key observation is that different controls specify different interfaces to the same underlying world: although their representations differ, they constrain shared physical regularities, scene dynamics, and interaction semantics. Worldscape-MoE operationalizes this observation through modality-aware control injection, shared and control-specific experts, and a progressive MoE tuning strategy that supports continual extension to new action modalities. Experiments across locomotion, robotic manipulation, and egocentric hand control show that heterogeneous supervision improves rather than interferes with individual control capabilities. Worldscape-MoE achieves strong results on WorldArena, improves locomotion and hand-control metrics, exhibits robust out-of-distribution generalization, and demonstrates scaling behavior as additional control data and experts are integrated.

</details>


### [392] [Fast Asymptotically Optimal Kinodynamic Planning via Vectorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03987)
*Yitian Gao,Andrew Lu,Zachary Kingston*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAKR is a massively parallel kinodynamic motion planner using JAX/XLA for GPU acceleration via standard Python, achieving asymptotic optimality through iterative replanning with competitive runtimes and better solution quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GPU-accelerated motion planners require specialized CUDA programming, limiting accessibility and portability. The authors aim to achieve GPU acceleration through standard Python tooling.

Method: PAKR combines parallel RRT with the AO-x meta-algorithm for asymptotic optimality, implemented in JAX with XLA compilation. It uses batch sampling and branching in parallel, integrated with MuJoCo-XLA simulator.

Result: Competitive runtimes with state-of-the-art GPU planners and superior solution quality.

Conclusion: Massively parallel kinodynamic planning via JAX/XLA achieves GPU acceleration without CUDA expertise, with theoretical guarantees of probabilistic completeness and practical scalability to complex dynamics.

Abstract: Sampling-based motion planners have been shown to be effective for systems with complex kinodynamic constraints and high dimensionality. However, these algorithms struggle to achieve real-time performance, leading to recent efforts to parallelize planning. While GPU-accelerated planners have achieved significant speedups, existing approaches require specialized CUDA programming that limits accessibility and portability. We present Parallel Asymptotically Optimal Kinodynamic RRT (PAKR), a massively parallel kinodynamic planner leveraging JAX and the XLA compiler to achieve GPU acceleration through standard Python tooling. By combining our parallel planner with the AO-x meta-algorithm, we achieve asymptotic optimality through fast iterative replanning. We provide a theoretical analysis of probabilistic completeness, analyze the effects of batch size and branching factor on convergence, and demonstrate scalability to complex dynamics using the MuJoCo-XLA simulator. Experiments show competitive runtimes with state-of-the-art GPU planners and superior solution quality.

</details>


### [393] [Conflict-Based Lazy Search for Fast Multi-Manipulator Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04124)
*Dongliang Zheng,Zhipeng Wang,Siqi Wang,Yuxi Lu,Bin He,Hesheng Wang,Panagiotis Tsiotras*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CBLS is a new multi-manipulator planning algorithm that enhances Conflict-Based Search (CBS) with precomputation and lazy search (LEA*), achieving significant speedup by reducing edge evaluations compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time planning for multiple manipulators in cluttered workspaces remains challenging; existing algorithms struggle with computational efficiency.

Method: CBLS combines CBS with (1) precomputed lazily evaluated graphs with controlled sparsity and (2) Lazy Edge-based A* (LEA*), which reduces edge evaluations using lazy search and an edge queue.

Result: LEA* is optimally vertex efficient with improved edge efficiency over A*; CBLS shows superior performance compared to CBS and RRT-Connect on multi-manipulator planning problems.

Conclusion: CBLS effectively addresses multi-manipulator planning bottlenecks by integrating lazy evaluation techniques, offering an order of magnitude improvement in planning speed.

Abstract: Employing multiple manipulators can boost efficiency and accomplish tasks that a single manipulator cannot do. However, real-time planning for multiple manipulators in a cluttered workspace still poses significant challenges for planning algorithms. This article proposes a new planning algorithm called Conflict-Based Lazy Search (CBLS) for multimanipulator planning. CBLS is built on Conflict-Based Search (CBS), an efficient multiagent pathfinding (MAPF) algorithm that has shown an order of magnitude speedup over previous approaches [1], [2]. CBS addresses MAPF by solving many single-agent pathfinding (SAPF) problems. Thus, its planning time directly depends on the efficiency of the SAPF algorithm adopted. Our CBLS algorithm enhances CBS with precomputation and lazy search. First, a lazily evaluated graph with controlled sparsity is precomputed for a single manipulator. Second, we propose the Lazy Edged-based A* (LEA*) for efficient SAPF. Since edge evaluation is the computational bottleneck of manipulator planning, LEA* uses lazy search and an edge queue to reduce the number of edge evaluations. We show that LEA* is optimally vertex efficient and has improved edge efficiency compared to A*. We apply the proposed CBLS to multi-manipulator planning problems and show its superior performance by comparing it with CBS and a sampling-based algorithm, namely, RRT-Connect.

</details>


### [394] [Semantic-Guided Progressive Object Removal with Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04144)
*Xianliang Huang,Chen Xiao,Yuanxiang Ni,Guanming Liu,Mingkai Liu,Dikai Fan,Xiao Liu,Hao Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel 3D object removal framework using Semantic-guided Block Matching (SBM) and Region-Wise Progressive Refinement (RPR) built on Gaussian Splatting, which leverages DINOv2 semantic guidance and multi-stage refinement to outperform existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods complete entire masked regions in a single step without effectively using semantic information from other views, causing difficulties with complex geometric details and textures in 3D object removal.

Method: (1) Semantic-guided Block Matching (SBM): Uses DINOv2 to encode semantic guidance from multi-view observations, decodes best match tokens to complete missing regions while maintaining cross-view consistency. (2) Region-Wise Progressive Refinement (RPR): Segments target mask into subregions and selectively refines those with poor visual quality. Built upon Gaussian Splatting.

Result: Outperforms existing Gaussian-based methods in perceptual quality and coherence for 3D object removal.

Conclusion: The proposed SBM + RPR framework, built on Gaussian Splatting, achieves high-quality 3D object removal with better perceptual quality and cross-view consistency than prior Gaussian-based approaches.

Abstract: Removing unwanted objects from reconstructed 3D scenes is an important task in computer vision, supporting applications in AR/VR, robotics, and digital content creation. Existing methods typically complete the entire masked region in a single step and without effectively utilizing semantic information from other views, leading to difficulties in handling complex geometric details and textures. In this work, we propose a novel framework that integrates Semantic-guided Block Matching (SBM) and Region-Wise Progressive Refinement (RPR) for high-quality 3D object removal. First, we leverage DINOv2 to encode semantic guidance from multi-view observations, and the best match tokens are decoded to complete missing regions in the target view while maintaining cross-view consistency. Second, we introduce a RPR strategy that segments the target mask into multiple subregions and selectively refines those with poor visual quality. Our method is built upon Gaussian Splatting, ensuring high-fidelity scene reconstruction with efficient computation. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing Gaussian-based methods in terms of perceptual quality and coherence in 3D object removal.

</details>


### [395] [!Imperio, smolVLA: The Implications of Data Poisoning on Open Source Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04146)
*Stefan Bühler,Mark Schutera*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that trigger-word data poisoning can silently backdoor vision-language-action models used in robotics, with as few as 3 poisoned episodes out of 320 causing complete denial of service—locking the robot entirely—while clean performance remains unaffected, making the attack stealthy and practical.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-source robotics ecosystems rely on community-contributed datasets, but the trust assumptions around data provenance are largely unexamined. The authors aim to expose this vulnerability by showing that poisoning attacks on vision-language-action models are practical, low-cost, and stealthy.

Method: The authors poison smolVLA, a vision-language-action model, by inserting trigger words into a small number of training episodes. They evaluate three poison ratios on a real-world pick-and-place task using the LeRobot platform, testing across different trigger placements (front, middle, end) and measuring success rates under poisoned vs. clean prompts.

Result: Three poisoned episodes out of 320 (0.94%) cause complete denial of service (0.0% success rate) under trigger-word conditions, with the robot locking into a fixed joint configuration. Clean-prompt success stays at ~50%. A single poisoned episode already reduces success to 6.7%. The attack generalizes across trigger placements despite training only on front-placed triggers.

Conclusion: Trigger-word data poisoning poses a practical, stealthy, and low-cost threat to open-source robotics systems. The findings warrant treating dataset provenance as a first-class security concern in these ecosystems.

Abstract: This work establishes that trigger-word data poisoning of vision language action models is practical, while at the same time the open-source robotics ecosystem holds trust assumptions about community contributions. A few poisoned samples can silently embed a backdoor that disables a robot on command. We evaluate this threat against smolVLA on a real-world pick-and-place task, training on three poison ratios and evaluating across different prompts on the LeRobot platform. Three poisoned episodes in 320 clean episodes suffice for a complete denial of service. Success rate drops to 0.0 plus minus 0.0% across all trigger-word conditions and the robot locks into a fixed joint configuration rather than executing any task-relevant motion. Clean-prompt behaviour holds at approx. 50% success rate across all poison ratios, confirming the attack is stealthy under normal operation. A single poisoned episode already reduces success rate to 6.7 plus minus 6.7%. The robot still moves, but no longer completes the task. The attack generalises to front, middle, and end trigger placements despite training exclusively on front-placed triggers. These findings establish that the threat is practical, low-cost, and stealthy, and warrant treating dataset provenance as a first-class concern in open-source robotics ecosystems.

</details>


### [396] [ACE: Agentic Control for Embodied Manipulation via Zero-shot Workflow Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04162)
*Iok Tong Lei,QianZhi Li,Ying Jie Yap,Yujie Zhang,Rui Zhong,Haichao Gui,Xiaolong Liu,Zhidong Deng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ACE is a zero-shot workflow reasoning framework for tabletop manipulation that combines agentic reasoning with mask-mediated vision-action interfaces and multi-timescale memory to achieve robust pick-and-place without task-specific retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended tabletop manipulation requires agents to understand natural language while adapting to dynamic environments and execution failures, but existing end-to-end baselines struggle with logically complex, long-horizon tasks.

Method: ACE combines agentic workflow reasoning with two robot-facing skills (visual grounding + pick-and-place primitive), uses a mask-mediated vision-action interface for grounding sub-goals, and operates in a closed loop with multi-timescale memory for verification, retry, repair, or replanning.

Result: ACE achieves 50% success in equation formation and 70% in constraint retrieval on zero-shot tasks, significantly outperforming standard end-to-end baselines.

Conclusion: Explicit workflow reasoning and mask-mediated control provide a robust, practical path toward adaptable robotic manipulation without task-specific retraining.

Abstract: Open-ended tabletop manipulation requires agents to not only understand natural language but also adapt to dynamic environments and execution failures. We present ACE (Agentic Control for Embodied Manipulation), a zero-shot workflow reasoning framework for tabletop pick-and-place from natural language. Rather than relying on direct low-level action mapping, ACE combines agentic workflow reasoning with two robot-facing executable skills: a visual grounding interface and a reusable pick-and-place primitive. To bridge semantic reasoning and physical control, the active sub-goal is grounded into a mask-mediated vision-action interface. This unified mask specifies the target object and destination, is tracked over time, exposed for human verification, and ultimately passed to a task-agnostic downstream policy for execution. Crucially, ACE operates in a closed loop supported by a multi-timescale memory. After an action is executed, the system automatically verifies whether the intended sub-goal succeeded, using the outcome to advance, retry, repair, or replan. This enables online adaptation to user corrections, scene changes, and physical failures. We evaluate ACE on logically complex, long-horizon tasks, including zero-shot multi-step equation formation with number cubes and constraint-based object retrieval. ACE demonstrates task-level zero-shot generalization on novel semantic constraints and randomized tabletop scenes without task-specific retraining. Specifically, while standard end-to-end baselines struggle to complete these logically demanding tasks, ACE achieves a 50% success rate in equation formation and a 70% success rate in constraint retrieval. This contrast demonstrates that explicit workflow reasoning and mask-mediated control offer a robust, practical route toward adaptable robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [397] [Neural LiDAR Bundle Adjustment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04169)
*Chin Yung Anson Hon,Kaicheng Zhang,Sen Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces NeLD-BA, a Neural LiDAR Bundle Adjustment algorithm that leverages efficient volume sampling to jointly optimize LiDAR maps and poses, achieving state-of-the-art results in point cloud registration and 3D mapping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between RGB NeRFs and LiDAR NeRFs by identifying key design differences, particularly the role of volume sampling density in LiDAR NeRF performance.

Method: Proposes NeLD-BA, a Neural LiDAR Bundle Adjustment algorithm that uses efficient volume sampling of LiDAR rays for joint optimization of LiDAR maps and camera poses.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on Newer College and FusionPortable datasets for multi-view point cloud registration and 3D mapping.

Conclusion: Volume sampling density is critical for LiDAR NeRFs, and the proposed NeLD-BA effectively leverages this insight for superior mapping and registration performance.

Abstract: Recent research has achieved remarkable novel view rendering and scene reconstruction results with Neural Radiance Field (NeRF), including extensions to the LiDAR modality. Few studies have, however, explored the key design differences between RGB NeRFs and LiDAR NeRFs, particularly considering their underlying working principles. In this work, we provide both theoretical and empirical evidence suggesting that the density of volume sampling plays a significant role in LiDAR NeRF. Based on this finding, we propose a novel Neural LiDAR Bundle Adjustment (NeLD-BA) algorithm, which is tailored using efficient volume sampling of LiDAR rays for joint optimization of LiDAR map and poses. Extensive experiments are performed using the Newer College and FusionPortable datasets to demonstrate the proposed NeLD-BA's state-of-the-art performance in multi-view point cloud registration and 3D mapping. We will open-source our code for the community.

</details>


### [398] [XS-VLA: Coupling Coarse-grained Spatial Distillation with Latent Flow Matching for Lightweight Robotic Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04171)
*Lei Iok Tong,Qingchen Xie,Wei Huang,Ying Jie Yap,Yujie Zhang,Qianzhi Li,Xiaolong Liu,Zhidong Deng*

Main category: cs.RO
