TL;DR: XS-VLA is a two-stage framework that distills spatial knowledge from a large LVLM into a lightweight 0.25B model and uses a Latent Flow Matching policy to achieve efficient, spatially grounded robotic manipulation with SOTA results on LIBERO benchmark, outperforming larger models while being 3.2x faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs are computationally expensive for real-time robotics; lightweight models suffer from spatial blindness; training VLA on mixed human demonstrations degrades policy performance.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) Distill spatial semantics from Qwen3-VL-4B into SmolVLM2-0.25B via fine-tuning on coarse-grained spatial descriptions; (2) Use enhanced backbone to condition a Latent Flow Matching policy combining CVAE with Flow Matching dynamics.

Result: SOTA among sub-0.5B models on LIBERO; up to 7.2% average success rate improvement (23% on LIBERO-Long) over SmolVLA 0.25B; outperforms 2.2B SmolVLA; 3.2x speedup over prior lightweight flow matching policy.

Conclusion: Spatial tuning and generative latent flow control effectively address spatial blindness and multimodal action distribution challenges in lightweight VLAs for robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have shown strong multimodal understanding and spatial grounding, but their computational cost limits real-time robotic control. In contrast, lightweight models are suitable for edge deployment but often suffer from "spatial blindness", namely weak native spatial prediction ability. Training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models on mixed human demonstrations can also degrade policy performance due to highly diverse behaviors. To address these limitations, we propose XS-VLA, a two-stage framework for efficient and spatially grounded robotic manipulation. First, we distill spatial semantic knowledge from Qwen3-VL-4B into the SmolVLM2-0.25B backbone by fine-tuning on curated coarse-grained spatial descriptions, turning the lightweight model into a spatially grounded engine. Second, we use this enhanced backbone to condition a Latent Flow Matching policy. Unlike deterministic controllers, our policy combines a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) with Flow Matching dynamics to model complex multimodal action distributions. On the LIBERO benchmark, XS-VLA achieves state-of-the-art performance among models with fewer than 0.5B parameters. It improves average success rates by up to 7.2 percent, including a 23 percent gain on LIBERO-Long, over the SmolVLA 0.25B baseline, and outperforms the larger 2.2B vanilla SmolVLA. Ablations show that spatial tuning and generative latent flow control substantially improve lightweight VLA performance, delivering a 3.2 times speedup in mission execution over the previous lightweight flow matching policy.

</details>


### [399] [SoftVTBench: A Safety-Aware Visuo-Tactile Benchmark for Physically Constrained Robotic Manipulation of Deformable Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04234)
*Bowen Jing,Mingxin Wang,Ruiyang Hao,Chenchen Ge,Hanwen Shen,Junjie He,Yang Cui,Yiming Hou,Weitao Zhou,Jiawei Wang,Minglei Li,Dandan Zhang,Ding Zhao,Houde Liu,Xiaofan Li,Si Liu,Ping Luo,Haibao Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SoftVTBench is a safety-aware visuo-tactile benchmark in Isaac Sim for deformable object manipulation that separately evaluates Goal Success and Safety Success (no drop, peak deformation below threshold), revealing that success-only metrics overstate performance and that tactile sensing improves safety (e.g., +14.2% on deformable tasks).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing manipulation benchmarks focus on task completion but neglect physical safety (slip, drop, excessive deformation) during execution.

Method: Built in Isaac Sim with FEM-simulated deformable objects, multi-view RGB, tactile sensing, proprioception, and language instructions; defines four task suites (deformable vs. rigid × object vs. spatial variation); separately reports Goal Success and Safety Success using a calibrated deformation threshold from privileged FEM states; implements pi0.5-based baselines.

Result: Success-only evaluation overstates performance; tactile sensing improves Safety Success from 21.4% to 35.6% on object-centric deformable tasks while maintaining comparable Goal Success.

Conclusion: SoftVTBench provides a reproducible benchmark for studying physically safe visuo-tactile deformable manipulation under interaction constraints.

Abstract: Deformable object manipulation poses challenges beyond task completion: successful execution must also maintain safe physical interaction, holding the object stably without slip or drop while avoiding excessive deformation. However, existing manipulation benchmarks are predominantly success-oriented and rarely evaluate whether a policy remains physically safe throughout execution. We present SoftVTBench, a safety-aware visuo-tactile benchmark for physically constrained deformable object manipulation. Built in Isaac Sim with finite-element-simulated deformable objects, SoftVTBench provides multi-view RGB observations, RGB tactile sensing with marker motion, proprioception, and language instructions, and defines four matched task suites over object type (deformable vs. rigid) and variation axis (object vs. spatial). It separately reports Goal Success and Safety Success; the latter additionally requires no drop and peak deformation below a calibrated object-specific threshold, measured from policy-hidden privileged Finite Element Method (FEM) states. We implement pi0.5-based baselines under this protocol. Experiments show that success-only evaluation substantially overstates policy performance, as a large fraction of goal-completing rollouts still violate physical safety. Furthermore, incorporating tactile sensing improves Safety Success (e.g., from 21.4% to 35.6% on object-centric deformable tasks) and reduces object deformation during execution, while maintaining comparable Goal Success. SoftVTBench provides a reproducible benchmark for studying visuo-tactile deformable manipulation under physical interaction constraints.

</details>


### [400] [Integrated Graph Search and Model Predictive Control for Smooth and Efficient Path Planning in Autonomous Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04259)
*Duc-Tien Bui,Ngoc Thinh Nguyen,Hung Duy Nguyen,Dong Bi,Tomislav Mihalj,Arno Eichberger*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a sequential path planning framework for autonomous vehicles that uses Dijkstra search to generate a rough path, which guides an MPC-based refinement within a convex safety corridor. It achieves smoother paths (lower lateral acceleration, curvature, jerk) and reduces computational cost by ~28-30% compared to prior polynomial/quadratic programming methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Path planning for autonomous vehicles requires balancing safety, comfort, dynamic feasibility, and computational efficiency, but existing methods struggle to achieve all these simultaneously.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) Dijkstra graph search on a discretized grid to obtain a rough path; (2) a spatially varying convex lateral safety corridor is constructed from the rough path to encode obstacle avoidance as continuous constraints, then an MPC problem refines the path by penalizing third-order spatial derivatives of lateral offset over a prediction horizon.

Result: In CarMaker simulations of overtaking scenarios (straight and curved roads, single/multiple target vehicles), the proposed method consistently achieves lower lateral acceleration, curvature, and jerk, with 28.08% (straight) and 29.52% (curved) computational cost reduction compared to polynomial fitting + quadratic programming baseline.

Conclusion: Exploiting graph-search structure within an MPC formulation effectively balances path smoothness and computational efficiency for autonomous driving in structured environments.

Abstract: Path planning is a fundamental component of autonomous vehicles, where achieving safe, comfortable, and dynamically feasible paths while ensuring computational efficiency remains a significant challenge. This paper presents a sequential path planning framework in which a rough path obtained from graph search is explicitly exploited to guide a Model Predictive Control (MPC)-based path refinement. A rough path is first obtained via Dijkstra search on a discretized grid and is then used to construct a spatially varying convex lateral safety corridor that explicitly captures obstacle avoidance constraints, transforming discrete obstacle avoidance decisions into continuous feasibility constraints for optimization. Within this corridor, an MPC problem is formulated to refine the path, enabling efficient optimization while maintaining path smoothness by penalizing the third-order spatial derivative of the lateral offset over a prediction horizon. The proposed algorithm is evaluated in multiple overtaking scenarios on both straight and curved roads, including cases with single and multiple target vehicles, using high-fidelity environment simulations (i.e., CarMaker). Compared with the previous study, which used polynomial fitting and a quadratic programming method, the proposed approach consistently achieves lower lateral acceleration, curvature, and jerk while reducing computational cost by 28.08% on straight roads and 29.52% on curved roads. These results demonstrate that exploiting graph-search structure within an MPC formulation provides an effective balance between path smoothness and computational efficiency for autonomous vehicles in structured driving environments.

</details>


### [401] [FLOAT Drone for Physical Interaction: Lateral Airflow Reduction, Wrench Modeling, and Adaptive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04260)
*Junxiao Lin,Kehan Zhou,Shuhang Ji,Yimin Peng,Shen Wang,Jialiang Hou,Fei Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents FLOAT Drone, a fully actuated coaxial UAV with servo-driven control surfaces that generates 6-DoF wrenches, reduces lateral airflow toward targets, and demonstrates high-precision close-proximity physical interaction tasks like drawer push-pull manipulation through tight clearances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for aerial platforms that can exert contact forces while maintaining stable flight in confined spaces, with three key design requirements: multidimensional wrench generation, compactness for maneuverability, and reduced lateral airflow toward targets.

Method: A coaxial dual-rotor UAV with servo-driven control surfaces immersed in rotor downwash for 6-DoF wrench generation; a high-fidelity polynomial aerodynamic wrench model identified from precision force measurements; and a constrained nonlinear allocator for real-time wrench tracking.

Result: The framework improves control accuracy over linear allocation baselines, rejects ground-effect and payload disturbances, and enables drawer push-pull manipulation through a 2 cm handle clearance in close proximity.

Conclusion: The FLOAT Drone design successfully meets the coupled requirements for close-proximity aerial physical interaction, demonstrating superior control performance and practical manipulation capability in confined spaces.

Abstract: Aerial physical interaction represents a promising direction for next-generation unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), but it requires an aerial platform that can exert contact forces while maintaining stable flight. For close-proximity tasks, this translates into three coupled design requirements: multidimensional wrench generation for stable contact, compactness for maneuverability and safety in confined spaces, and reduced lateral airflow toward the target when generating horizontal force. This article presents FLOAT Drone, a fully actuated coaxial UAV with servo-driven control surfaces for close-proximity physical interaction. The coaxial dual-rotor layout provides a compact propulsion layout, while the control surfaces, immersed in the rotor downwash, generate lateral forces and moments for 6-DoF wrench generation. A force-matched computational fluid dynamics (CFD) comparison with a tilted-rotor alternative quantifies the reduction in target-facing lateral airflow. To account for nonlinear rotor--control-surface coupling in the rotor wake, a high-fidelity polynomial aerodynamic wrench model is identified from precision force measurements and embedded in a constrained nonlinear allocator for real-time wrench tracking. Comparative flight and interaction experiments show that the proposed framework improves control accuracy over linear allocation baselines, rejects ground-effect and payload disturbances, and enables close-proximity drawer push--pull manipulation through a $2~\mathrm{cm}$ handle clearance.

</details>


### [402] [HALO-WA: Hybrid-Attention Latent-Guided Online Reinforcement Learning for World-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04265)
*Angen Ye,Weijie Ke,Xiaofeng Wang,Xinze Chen,Chaojun Ni,Guosheng Zhao,Boyuan Wang,Zheng Zhu,Junjie Xie,Dapeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HALO-WA is a hybrid-attention latent-guided online RL framework that refines World-Action model outputs for precise robotic manipulation, boosting success rates from 26.4% to 87.1% on real-world tasks with only 45-75 minutes of online training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WA models fail in precision tasks due to calibration, perception, and contact-dynamics errors, especially in the final millimeters of alignment or insertion.

Method: Hybrid-attention latent-guided online RL with a lightweight actor-critic adapter that uses WA latent features and action priors, preserving temporal consistency while reading task-relevant information from WA latents conditioned on visual context and end-stage correction needs.

Result: Improves average success rate from 26.4% to 87.1% across four real-world precision tasks, outperforming the strongest baseline by 19.2 percentage points with only 45-75 minutes of online training per task.

Conclusion: HALO-WA effectively addresses real-world precision manipulation errors in WA models through efficient online RL adaptation, with supplementary simulation experiments in RoboTwin confirming reproducibility.

Abstract: World-action (WA) models can generate long-horizon action chunks for general-purpose robotic manipulation, but they remain vulnerable to calibration, perception, and contact-dynamics errors in real-world precision tasks, often failing in the final few millimeters of alignment or insertion. We propose HALO-WA, a hybrid-attention latent-guided online reinforcement learning (RL) framework for WA models, which leverages latent features and action priors from the WA generation process through a lightweight actor-critic adapter to enable fast online adaptation to real deployment errors. HALO-WA introduces a hybrid-attention structure that preserves the temporal consistency of action chunks while reading task-relevant information from WA latents conditioned on visual context and end-stage correction requirements, thereby producing refined action chunks. We validate HALO-WA on four real-world precision manipulation tasks, where it improves the average success rate from 26.4\% for WA-base to 87.1\%, outperforming the strongest baseline by 19.2 percentage points while requiring only 45--75 minutes of online training per task. To facilitate reproducibility, we further conduct supplementary simulation experiments in RoboTwin and release the code at https://github.com/YeanRoot/HALO-WA.

</details>


### [403] [GPU-Accelerated Polygonal Signed Distance Functions for Real-Time Collision Avoidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04310)
*Taekwon Ga,Jongeun Choi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes PSDF, a geometry-exact signed distance function for convex polygonal robots and obstacle edges, enabling efficient GPU-based collision-avoidance constraint evaluation. It is embedded into an MPC framework (PSDF-MPC) that separates CPU/GPU computation, demonstrated to achieve real-time feasibility and robust collision avoidance in dense polygonal environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimization-based local planning faces a computational bottleneck due to high-rate collision-avoidance constraint evaluation in obstacle-dense environments. Existing methods struggle to balance computational efficiency with geometric fidelity.

Method: The PSDF is a weight-free, branch-free tensorized geometric pipeline for batched GPU execution and automatic differentiation. It is embedded into MPC via local linearization of stage-wise safety constraints within an SQP-based real-time iteration scheme (PSDF-MPC), with CPU solving a sparse QP and GPU evaluating batched PSDF values/gradients.

Result: Microbenchmarks show PSDF scales favorably against signed-distance query baselines. Closed-loop simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate PSDF-MPC maintains real-time feasibility and robust collision avoidance in dense polygonal environments, outperforming optimization-based baselines.

Conclusion: The PSDF framework effectively resolves the computational bottleneck by decoupling GPU-accelerated constraint evaluation from CPU-based optimization, making real-time collision-avoidance MPC feasible in dense polygonal environments without sacrificing geometric accuracy.

Abstract: Optimization-based local planning and control require high-rate collision-avoidance constraint evaluation over a prediction horizon. In obstacle-dense environments, where feasible space is limited and the constraints become increasingly complex, the computational workload often dominates the control-cycle runtime. The resulting bottleneck motivates collision-avoidance constraints that combine computational efficiency with geometric fidelity. The proposed Polygonal Signed Distance Function (PSDF) is a geometry-exact signed distance function between a convex polygonal robot footprint and obstacles represented by their boundary edges. It is implemented as a weight-free, branch-free tensorized geometric pipeline enabling batched GPU execution and automatic differentiation. The PSDF is embedded into model predictive control by locally linearizing the stage-wise safety constraints within a sequential quadratic programming-based real-time iteration scheme, yielding the PSDF-embedded model predictive controller (PSDF-MPC). The design separates CPU/GPU computation so that the GPU evaluates batched PSDF values and gradients while the CPU solves a sparse quadratic program whose dimension is determined by system dimensions and horizon length, not by obstacle features. Microbenchmarks show that PSDF scales favorably against signed-distance query baselines. Closed-loop simulated and real-world navigation experiments, including comparisons with optimization-based baselines, demonstrate that PSDF-MPC maintains real-time feasibility and robust collision avoidance in dense polygonal environments.

</details>


### [404] [Agent-driven Long-tail Simulation for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04331)
*Junru Gu,Lijin Yang,Jianing Huang,Shu Liu,Zhongzhan Huang,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes an LLM-driven simulation framework for closed-loop autonomous driving evaluation, where surrounding agents follow language instructions to create diverse, reactive, and physically plausible behaviors. It also introduces SemanticPlan, a benchmark with long-tail scenarios built on nuPlan, showing that current planners fail to consistently handle such interactive situations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing closed-loop simulators rely on log replay or rule-based agents, which lack behavioral diversity and fail to cover long-tail scenarios, limiting the evaluation of autonomous driving systems in realistic interactive settings.

Method: The framework controls surrounding road participants using instruction-following large language models through a structured action interface, enabling intentional and reactive behaviors while maintaining physical plausibility. The SemanticPlan benchmark augments real nuPlan scenes with multiple interactive agents following diverse language instructions.

Result: State-of-the-art planners still struggle to consistently achieve safe and effective task completion in these long-tail, semantically rich scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed agent-driven simulation framework exposes the limitations of current planners in handling diverse, interactive long-tail scenarios, suggesting the need for more robust planning methods.

Abstract: Evaluating autonomous driving systems in closed-loop settings requires realistic and interactive simulation, yet existing simulators largely rely on log replay or rule-based agents, limiting behavioral diversity and long-tail coverage. We propose an agent-driven simulation framework in which surrounding road participants are controlled by instruction-following large language models through a structured action interface, enabling intentional and reactive behaviors while preserving physical plausibility. Furthermore, we introduce SemanticPlan, a benchmark of closed-loop planning in long-tail and semantically rich scenarios that augment real nuPlan scenes with multiple interactive agents following diverse language instructions. Evaluation results show that state-of-the-art planners still struggle to consistently achieve safe and effective task completion, suggesting that these long-tail scenarios remain challenging.

</details>


### [405] [A Perception-Manipulation Robotics System for Food Cutting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04367)
*Xinyuan Luo,Wenzhen Yuan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a perception-manipulation framework for food-cutting robots that selects the optimal knife using force data from a trial cut and then performs adaptive cutting via reinforcement learning, achieving 100% knife selection accuracy on unseen foods and human-comparable cutting performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cooking robots face challenges in cutting diverse food items, which require different cutting policies and knives. A generalizable and adaptive framework is needed.

Method: The system has two stages: (1) knife selection module using force data from a fixed trial cut to choose the appropriate knife, and (2) adaptive cutting phase using reinforcement learning to balance speed and energy efficiency.

Result: 100% knife selection success rate on unseen foods; RL-based cutting outperformed fixed policies and achieved results comparable to human operators.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables effective and adaptive food cutting with knife selection and RL-based control, performing on par with human abilities.

Abstract: In the development of cooking robots, mastering the task of cutting is crucial. A significant challenge lies in the diverse properties of food, which necessitate distinct cutting policies and even different knives for optimal processing. This paper presents a perception-manipulation framework for food-cutting tasks. Our system features a knife selection module that utilizes force data from a preliminary fixed trial cut to select the appropriate knife for the given food. This is followed by an adaptive cutting phase using reinforcement learning (RL) to balance cutting speed and energy efficiency. In our experiments, the knife selection module achieved 100% successful rate on unseen food, and we compared the performances of fixed policy, RL policy, with human operators. Our method not only achieves high performance but also demonstrates comparable results to those of human participants.

</details>


### [406] [SurgAM: Surgical Affordance Map Prediction with Multimodal Feature Fusion for Robot Autonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04378)
*Lei Song,Yonghao Long,Mengya Xu,Jiayi Geng,Xiuyuan Chen,Qi Dou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces surgical affordance prediction—identifying actionable regions for fundamental surgical actions (aspiration, clipping, retraction) from visual data. The authors propose an adaptive feature fusion framework combining self-supervised Vision Transformer and generative model encoder, a hierarchical prompt learning mechanism, and a scene-guided attention decoder. Their approach achieves SOTA performance on a new affordance-annotated dataset and demonstrates successful downstream automation on realistic phantoms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging visual scene understanding with autonomous action planning in surgery remains a fundamental challenge. While scene perception (tool recognition, segmentation) is well-studied, understanding and predicting actionable possibilities for surgical automation is underexplored.

Method: (1) Adaptive feature fusion framework combining self-supervised ViT encoder (semantic understanding) with generative model encoder (spatial awareness). (2) Hierarchical prompt learning to adapt to varying procedural contexts. (3) Scene-guided attention decoder focusing on critical surgical areas while suppressing background distractions. A new dataset with affordance annotations for aspiration, clipping, retraction was created from public surgical datasets.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on the newly created affordance dataset. Validated on realistic lung and prostate phantoms, demonstrating that predicted affordance maps successfully enable autonomous surgical actions.

Conclusion: Surgical affordance prediction is a promising direction for bridging perception and action in surgical automation. The proposed adaptive fusion framework with hierarchical prompting and scene-guided attention effectively identifies actionable regions and supports downstream autonomous execution.

Abstract: Surgical automation is being increasingly studied, yet bridging visual scene understanding with autonomous action planning remains a fundamental challenge. While much research effort has been made on scene perception (e.g., tool recognition and scene segmentation), understanding and predicting actionable possibilities for surgical automation is still underexplored. In this paper, we introduce surgical affordance prediction, which identifies actionable regions for fundamental surgical actions from visual data. Specifically, a novel adaptive feature fusion framework is proposed that leverages the complementary strengths of a self-supervised vision transformer encoder for its superior semantic understanding and a large-scale generative model encoder for its spatially-aware capability. Furthermore, we introduce a hierarchical prompt learning mechanism to adapt to varying procedural contexts. Finally, a scene-guided attention decoder is proposed to focus on critical surgical areas while suppressing background distractions. To validate the effectiveness, we established a new dataset, derived from publicly available surgical datasets with affordance annotations for three basic surgical actions: aspiration, clipping, and retraction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, we validate our framework's applicability for downstream automation on a realistic lung and prostate phantom, and results show that the predicted affordance maps successfully enable autonomous surgical actions.

</details>


### [407] [ACE-Brain-0.5: A Unified Embodied Foundational Model for Physical Agentic AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04426)
*ACE-Brain Team,:,Ziyang Gong,Haoming Gu,Zehang Luo,Tianyi Zhang,Tao Tao,Yixiao Chi,Zhe Liu,Lingsi Zhu,Jingyuan Liu,Anke Tang,Songze Li,Yilun Kong,Ningjing Liu,Tianyu Zhu,Yunpeng Qing,Shuang Luo,Xiang Liu,Shi Fu,Dawei Nie,Sixiang Liu,Zhexi Wen,Feng Pan,Xiaofeng Wang,Zhi Hou,Chunxiao Liu,Xue Yang,Junchi Yan,Hengshuang Zhao,Dacheng Tao,Xiaogang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ACE-Brain-0.5 is a unified embodied foundation model integrating five core robotic functions (spatial perception, decision making, embodied interaction, self-monitoring, self-improvement) into a single 8B backbone with SSR+ training, showing strong results across 15 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embodied AI systems are fragmented—end-to-end policies lack spatial reasoning and execution assessment, while robot-agent systems lack shared representations. This prevents progress toward general Physical Agentic AI.

Method: Builds on ACE-Brain-0's spatial intelligence scaffold. Uses a single 8B backbone to instantiate four functions with SSR+ (Scaffold-Specialize-Reconcile + Reactivate stage) to avoid cross-task interference. A companion framework enables self-improvement from rollouts via task schemas, spatial memory, and failure-recovery cases.

Result: Improves over ACE-Brain-0 on 14 of 18 spatial benchmarks; achieves competitive navigation/manipulation; strong progress estimation in ID and OOD settings across 15 benchmarks.

Conclusion: This unified closed-loop foundation model represents an early step toward general Physical Agentic AI, demonstrating that integrating perception, planning, action, monitoring, and improvement in one model is feasible and effective.

Abstract: Embodied AI is moving from isolated perception or action modules toward physical agents that understand, plan under goals, act through robot bodies, monitor progress, and improve from experience. Existing systems address this loop only in parts: end-to-end policies generate actions but often lack spatial reasoning, planning, and execution assessment, while robot-agent systems orchestrate tools or specialists but do not learn a shared representation. This fragmentation limits general Physical Agentic AI. We present ACE-Brain-0.5, a unified embodied foundation model that organizes robot intelligence into five coupled functions: spatial perception, decision making, embodied interaction, self-monitoring, and self-improvement. Built on ACE-Brain-0, which established spatial intelligence as a shared scaffold across robot platforms, ACE-Brain-0.5 extends an understanding-centric model into a closed-loop foundation model. A single 8B backbone instantiates the first four functions: grounding objects and affordances, reasoning over 3D and egocentric spatial relations, decomposing instructions into subgoals, generating navigation and manipulation actions, and estimating progress for verification and recovery. To unify these capabilities without cross-task interference, we introduce SSR+, which extends Scaffold-Specialize-Reconcile with a Reactivate stage after task-vector merging. The fifth function, self-improvement, is realized by a companion framework that updates external execution state, including task schemas, spatial memory, and failure-recovery cases, from rollouts. Across fifteen benchmarks, ACE-Brain-0.5 improves over ACE-Brain-0 on 14 of 18 spatial perception and grounding benchmarks, achieves competitive navigation and manipulation performance, and provides strong progress estimation in ID and OOD settings. Together, these results mark an early step toward general Physical Agentic AI.

</details>


### [408] [RoboDojo: A Unified Sim-and-Real Benchmark for Comprehensive Evaluation of Generalist Robot Manipulation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04434)
*Tianxing Chen,Yue Chen,Zixuan Li,Junyuan Tang,Kailun Su,Weijie Wan,Baijun Chen,Haoran Lu,Haowen Yan,Honghao Su,Zhiyang Dou,Kaixuan Wang,Dandan Zhang,Yunze Liu,Yan Qin,Qiwei Liang,Qiwei Wu,Zijian Lin,Wenwei Lin,Yuran Wang,Minghua He,Tianshu Wu,Ruihai Wu,Jingquan Zhou,Kai-Chong Lei,Haibao Yu,Yuanfeng Ji,Weiyang Jin,Guanyu Lin,Xiaofan Li,Qi Xiong,Renjing Xu,Zhongyu Li,Wenhao Chai,Enze Xie,Ziwei Wang,Yao Mu,Hao Dong,Wojciech Matusik,Mingyu Ding,Wenbo Ding,Ping Luo,Masayoshi Tomizuka*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboDojo is a unified sim-and-real benchmark with 42 simulation and 18 real-world tasks for evaluating generalist robot manipulation policies across generalization, memory, precision, long-horizon execution, and open-vocabulary following.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to systematically evaluate generalist robot policies due to limited capability coverage, narrow skill sets, and reliance on either pure simulation or pure real-world evaluation, both of which have significant shortcomings.

Method: RoboDojo provides 42 simulation tasks (covering 5 evaluation dimensions) and 18 real-world tasks, uses Isaac Sim for parallel simulation, offers RoboDojo-RealEval with remote cloud access and standardized hardware, and integrates 30 policies via XPolicyLab for seamless sim-and-real evaluation.

Result: 30 policies integrated and evaluated, establishing a public leaderboard and systematic analysis of current policy performance across both simulation and real-world settings.

Conclusion: RoboDojo enables comprehensive, reproducible, and scalable evaluation of generalist robot policies, bridging the gap between simulation and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Generalist robot manipulation policies have advanced rapidly, yet existing benchmarks remain limited in systematically evaluating their capabilities. Many rely on simple, short-horizon, or skill-narrow tasks with limited capability coverage, and are often conducted only in simulation or only in the real world. Simulation enables scalable feedback but misses physical deployment challenges, while real-world evaluation is costly, time-consuming, and difficult to reproduce. We introduce RoboDojo, a unified sim-and-real benchmark for comprehensive evaluation of generalist robot manipulation policies. RoboDojo includes 42 simulation tasks and 18 real-world tasks covering diverse and complementary manipulation capabilities. The simulation benchmark evaluates five dimensions: generalization, memory, precision, long-horizon execution, and open-vocabulary instruction following, while the real-world benchmark exposes policies to challenging physical-world deployment conditions. RoboDojo supports scalable evaluation through heterogeneous parallel simulation in Isaac Sim and provides RoboDojo-RealEval, a reproducible real-world evaluation system with remote cloud access, standardized hardware, scene reset, evaluation protocol, and deployment interface. Together with XPolicyLab, policies can be integrated once and evaluated across simulation and real-world settings with minimal adaptation. We integrate 30 policies into XPolicyLab and evaluate them on RoboDojo, establishing a public leaderboard and systematic analysis of current policy performance. The website is available at http://robodojo-benchmark.com/.

</details>


### [409] [Robustness Verification of an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle-based Plankton Classifier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04453)
*Abdelrahman Sayed Sayed,Pierre-Jean Meyer,Asgeir J. Sørensen,Mohamed Ghazel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a robustness verification framework using reachability analysis and a neural ODE classifier to improve reliability of AI-based plankton classification from AUV imaging, reducing misclassifications and manual validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI plankton classifiers (CNNs) lack robustness in dynamic underwater environments, causing frequent misclassifications due to noise and non-biological artifacts, which require time-consuming manual validation by marine biologists.

Method: The authors propose a robustness verification framework based on reachability analysis, combined with a continuous-time neural ODE classification model designed for high-resolution SilCam particle imager data.

Result: The framework formally verifies neural ODE model robustness against environmental perturbations, acting as an automated filter that provides formal guarantees of model stability on ambiguous data.

Conclusion: The proposed verification framework improves the reliability of autonomous plankton sampling and significantly reduces the post-processing workload for marine biologists.

Abstract: The assessment of planktonic standing stocks and microorganism structures is critical for understanding upper ocean biological processes. Currently, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) equipped with in-situ optical imaging and artificial intelligence (AI) methods offer a promising solution for persistent surveillance, mapping and monitoring of planktonic life. However, current AI methods often lack robustness in dynamic, unstructured environments, where environmental noise and non-biological artifacts lead to frequent misclassifications. Standard convolutional neural network (CNN) classifiers often struggle with such conditions, leading to misclassifications that require time-consuming manual validation by marine biologists.
  To address this issue, we propose a novel robustness verification framework for in-situ plankton classifiers based on reachability analysis. We also introduce a continuous-time neural ordinary differential equation (neural ODE) classification model leveraging the high-resolution imaging capabilities of the SilCam particle imager. In this paper, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework by formally verifying the robustness of the neural ODE model against environmental perturbations. We demonstrate that our verification framework acts as an automated filter providing formal guarantees of model stability against ambiguous data, thereby improving the reliability of autonomous sampling and reducing the post-processing workload.

</details>


### [410] [Mask2Real-WM: Segmentation Masks as a Sim-to-Real Bridge for Controllable Dexterous World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04546)
*Riccardo O. Feingold,Davide Liconti,Chenyu Yang,Robert K. Katzschmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Mask2Real-WM is a two-stage action-conditioned world model that decouples pixel prediction into a dynamics model (predicting future segmentation masks) and a rendering model (ControlNet-augmented Stable Video Diffusion for photorealistic RGB). It leverages pretraining on 50+ hours of synthetic data and fine-tuning on <2.5 hours of real data, achieving per-DoF controllability across 23 DoF for dexterous manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robots to predict future consequences of actions in dexterous manipulation, where fine-grained per-joint control is necessary but existing monolithic world models fail to reliably capture per-DoF action effects.

Method: Two-stage decoupled architecture: (1) dynamics model predicts future segmentation masks from past masks and 23-DoF action sequences; (2) rendering model maps predicted masks to photorealistic RGB using ControlNet-augmented Stable Video Diffusion. Trained on synthetic simulation data (50+ hrs) then fine-tuned on real demonstrations (<2.5 hrs).

Result: Mask conditioning and simulation pretraining are both required for per-DoF action controllability across all 23 degrees of freedom on a dexterous pick-and-place benchmark. Monolithic baselines capture broad trajectories but fail on fine-grained per-joint effects.

Conclusion: Decoupling dynamics and rendering via segmentation masks, combined with large-scale synthetic pretraining, enables fine-grained action-conditioned prediction for dexterous manipulation where monolithic approaches fall short.

Abstract: Action-conditioned world models allow robots to predict the future consequences of candidate actions without additional physical interaction, supporting policy evaluation, planning, and data augmentation. We present Mask2Real-WM, a two-stage action-conditioned world model for dexterous manipulation that decouples pixel prediction into a dynamics model and a rendering model. The dynamics model predicts future segmentation masks from past masks and 23-DoF action sequences. The rendering model maps the predicted masks to photorealistic RGB using a ControlNet-augmented Stable Video Diffusion backbone. The smaller sim-to-real gap in segmentation space enables the dynamics model to benefit from large-scale pretraining on over 50 h of synthetic simulation data, followed by fine-tuning on fewer than 2.5 h of real demonstrations. Experiments on a dexterous pick-and-place benchmark show that mask conditioning and simulation pretraining are both required for per-DoF action controllability across all 23 degrees of freedom. In contrast, monolithic baselines capture broad hand and end-effector trajectories but do not reliably reflect fine-grained, per-joint action effects.

</details>


### [411] [HUGS: Guiding Unified Dexterous Grasp Synthesis Across Modes and Scales via Learned Human Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04554)
*Mingrui Yu,Yongpeng Jiang,Yongyi Jia,Kangchen Lv,Li Huang,Yi Ren,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HUGS is a human-prior-guided framework that synthesizes diverse dexterous grasps across scales (2cm-30cm) and contact modes (two-finger to bimanual) by learning grasp preferences from a compact human dataset (1.8K grasps, 304 objects) to guide force-closure optimization, successfully generating 3.2M robotic grasps over 157K scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dexterous grasp synthesis methods rely on manually designed expected contacts and initialization heuristics that struggle to balance synthesis success rate and diversity across varying object scales and contact modes.

Method: HUGS learns an object-conditioned human prior from a self-collected human grasp dataset (1.8K grasps, 304 objects) capturing grasp preferences, then uses it to guide force-closure-aware optimization with adaptive contact mode and wrist initialization proposals.

Result: Synthesized 3.2M robotic grasps over 157K scenes spanning 2cm-30cm object sizes, from two-finger pinches to bimanual grasps. Models trained on this dataset autonomously select appropriate contact modes in real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: Human-prior-guided synthesis effectively balances contact-mode coverage and success rate, enabling real-world dexterous grasping across a wide range of object scales from screws to large boxes.

Abstract: Dexterous grasping across diverse object scales requires contact modes ranging from two-finger pinches to bimanual grasps. Existing dexterous grasp synthesis methods reduce the high-dimensional optimization space with manually designed expected contacts and initialization heuristics, which struggle to balance synthesis success rate and diversity. We present HUGS (Human-prior-guided Unified Dexterous Grasp Synthesis), a human-prior-guided framework for unified dexterous grasp synthesis across modes and scales. Instead of directly retargeting human demonstrations, HUGS learns an object-conditioned human prior that captures human grasp preferences and guides downstream force-closure-aware optimization. The prior is trained on a compact self-collected human grasp dataset with 1.8K grasps over 304 objects, providing broad coverage of object scales and contact modes. During synthesis, HUGS adaptively proposes contact modes and wrist initializations, substantially improving the balance between contact-mode coverage and synthesis success rate over heuristic-based methods. With HUGS, we synthesize 3.2M robotic grasps over 157K scenes, spanning object half-diagonal lengths from 2 cm to 30 cm and modes from two-finger to bimanual grasps. Models trained on the synthesized dataset autonomously select appropriate contact modes in the real world, enabling grasping from screws to large boxes.

</details>


### [412] [Simple-to-Complex Structured Demonstrations for Vision-Language-Action Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04591)
*Xinchuan Qiu,Yi Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a simple-to-complex structured demonstration collection strategy for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, organizing data by decomposing tasks into sub-skills, standardizing environments, and progressively increasing task complexity, leading to improved success rates and training stability on block sorting and towel folding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA research focuses on model architectures, training strategies, and dataset scale, but overlooks how demonstrations are collected and organized. The authors identify demonstration organization as a fundamental yet underexplored factor that directly affects policy learning efficiency, training stability, and generalization.

Method: The approach uses a dual-arm robotic platform with three principles: (i) decomposing complex tasks into progressively learnable sub-skills, (ii) standardizing the interaction environment to reduce unnecessary variability, and (iii) organizing demonstrations by increasing task complexity, enabling VLA models to first learn fundamental skills before tackling complex long-horizon tasks.

Result: Evaluated on block grasping & sorting and towel folding tasks. The structured strategy shows consistent improvements in task success rate and training stability compared to the baseline of directly collecting end-to-end complete task trajectories.

Conclusion: Demonstration organization is a previously underexplored but important factor in VLA learning. The work provides practical insights into efficient skill acquisition, scalable dataset construction, and long-horizon robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong capabilities in robotic manipulation by integrating visual perception, language understanding, and robot action generation. Existing research has primarily focused on improving model architectures, training strategies, and dataset scale, while little attention has been paid to how demonstrations are collected and organized. We identify demonstration organization as a fundamental yet overlooked aspect of imitation learning, as it directly affects policy learning efficiency, training stability, and policy generalization. To address this gap, we propose a simple-to-complex structured demonstration collection strategy for VLA learning using a dual-arm robotic platform. Our approach systematically organizes data through three general principles: (i) decomposing complex manipulation tasks into progressively learnable sub-skills, (ii) standardizing the interaction environment to reduce unnecessary variability, and (iii) organizing demonstrations according to progressively increasing task complexity. This structured design enables VLA models to first acquire fundamental manipulation skills before learning increasingly complex task compositions, facilitating more effective learning of long-horizon manipulation tasks. We evaluate the proposed strategy on two representative robotic manipulation tasks: block grasping and sorting, and towel folding. Experimental results show consistent improvements in task success rate and training stability compared with the baseline method of directly collecting end-to-end complete task trajectories. These findings highlight demonstration organization as a previously underexplored but important factor in VLA learning and provide practical insights into efficient skill acquisition, scalable dataset construction, and long-horizon robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [413] [SEAM: Smooth Execution of Action-Chunked Motion for Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04609)
*Dijia Zhan,Xuemiao Xu,Jinyi Li,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SEAM is a training-free inference method that reduces action chunk discontinuities in flow-matching VLA policies by using a closed-form correction based on the previous chunk's unexecuted tail, reducing boundary jerk by 28% and transition discontinuity by 27% without sacrificing task success or adding significant computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA policies with fixed-length action chunks suffer from multimodal bifurcation—inconsistent trajectory modes between adjacent chunks causing abrupt discontinuities. Existing fixes are computationally expensive, require retraining, or hurt reliability.

Method: SEAM uses Velocity-guided Loss Steering (VLS): it derives a time-dependent target from the previous chunk's unexecuted tail and applies a closed-form correction after each Euler step in the denoising loop, without backpropagating through the policy network.

Result: On LIBERO-10 with pi_0.5, SEAM reduces boundary jerk by 28%, chunk transition discontinuity by 27%, maintains baseline task success rate, and keeps denoising cost near the unguided baseline.

Conclusion: SEAM provides an efficient, training-free solution to smooth action chunk execution in VLA policies, offering a practical plug-in for flow-matching models to improve trajectory continuity at negligible overhead.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies that execute fixed-length action chunks can exhibit multimodal bifurcation: a cross-chunk inconsistency in which adjacent chunks generated from independent Gaussian latents can converge to incompatible trajectory modes, producing abrupt discontinuities at chunk boundaries. Existing remedies either require backpropagation through the policy at each denoising step, rely on rejection sampling, or require retraining, each trading computational cost or task reliability for smoother transitions. We propose SEAM (Smooth Execution of Action-Chunked Motion), a training-free inference-time method for flow matching VLAs. SEAM exploits a simple synchronous-execution insight: after the robot consumes the executed prefix, the previous chunk's unexecuted tail is already available as an analytic consistency reference. Its core mechanism, Velocity-guided Loss Steering (VLS), derives a time-dependent target from this tail and applies a closed-form correction after each Euler step without backpropagating through the policy network. On LIBERO-10 with pi_0.5, SEAM reduces boundary jerk by 28%, reduces chunk transition discontinuity by 27%, preserves baseline-level task success, and keeps denoising-loop cost near the unguided baseline.

</details>


### [414] [RoboVista: Evaluating Vision Language Models for Diverse Robot Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04610)
*Shuangyu Xie,Kaiyuan Chen,Ziyang Chen,Simeon Adebola,Yixuan Huang,Zehan Ma,Tianshuang Qiu,Wentao Yuan,Dhruv Shah,Pannag R. Sanketi,Ken Goldberg*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces RoboVista, a modular VQA benchmark with 474 instances across 39 robotic task types, and RQA evaluation framework to assess VLMs' robotic reasoning, revealing substantial gaps in state-of-the-art models that correlate with real-world task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional robot benchmarks based on teleoperated end-to-end datasets fail to capture the modular structure of robotic decision-making, making it difficult to align VLMs with diverse robot applications that require reasoning across different embodiments, visual conditions, and planning complexities.

Method: The authors propose RQA (Robot Question Answering), a modular evaluation framework, and RoboVista, a benchmark containing 474 VQA instances with human-annotated reasoning covering 39 task types from real robotic systems, research papers, and expert annotations across agricultural, industrial, domestic, surgical robotics, autonomous driving, and open robot datasets.

Result: State-of-the-art VLMs exhibit substantial performance gaps on RoboVista, and physical robot experiments demonstrate a strong correlation between RoboVista benchmark performance and real-world task execution.

Conclusion: RoboVista serves as an effective benchmark for evaluating VLM-based robotic reasoning, with performance correlating to physical robot task execution, highlighting current limitations of VLMs in modular robotic reasoning.

Abstract: Diverse applications for robotics, such as industry and agriculture, require robots to operate across various embodiments, changing visual conditions, and complex planning. Vision-Language Models (VLMs) offer a promising foundation for general-purpose and interpretable robotic reasoning. Aligning VLMs with diverse robot applications requires a modular understanding of the individual decision components that underlie robotic behavior. Capturing such structure is challenging for conventional robot benchmarks that are primarily based on teleoperated, end-to-end datasets. We propose Robot Question Answering (RQA), a modular evaluation framework and RoboVista, a benchmark curated from real robotic systems, research papers, and expert annotations. RoboVista contains 474 Visual Question Answering (VQA) instances with human annotated reasoning and covers 39 unique task types in agricultural, industrial, domestic, surgical robotics, autonomous driving, and open robot datasets. Experiments on RoboVista show that state-of-the-art VLMs exhibit substantial gaps. Physical robot experiments suggest strong correlation between RoboVista performance and real-world task execution.

</details>


### [415] [DIVO: Continuous-time DVL-Inertial-Visual Odometry for Unmanned Underwater Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04615)
*Kyungmin Jung,Angad Bajwa,Junha Yoo,Arturo Del Castillo Bernal,James Richard Forbes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel acoustic-visual-inertial odometry system using continuous-time Gaussian process trajectory estimation, combining DVL, stereo camera, and IMU data with a learning-based visual frontend, outperforming state-of-the-art SLAM in underwater environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater environments pose challenges for visual localization (light attenuation, illumination variance, particulates), motivating the use of additional sensing modalities and a robust visual tracking pipeline beyond standard visual-inertial approaches.

Method: First continuous-time trajectory estimation framework based on Gaussian processes to fuse asynchronous DVL, stereo camera, and IMU measurements; incorporates a learning-based visual frontend for robust feature extraction and matching tailored to subsea conditions.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art visual-inertial and acoustic-visual-inertial SLAM algorithms in accuracy, robustness, and trajectory coverage on real-world underwater inspection datasets, despite using only short-term visual associations.

Conclusion: The proposed continuous-time, multi-modal fusion framework with learned visual features is effective for underwater odometry, offering adaptability to different environments without reconfiguration and enabling seamless integration of additional sensors.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel acoustic-visual-inertial odometry solution leveraging a continuous-time trajectory estimation framework for unmanned underwater vehicles. Underwater environments present unique challenges for visual localization and mapping, such as light attenuation, illumination variance, and the presence of particulate matter. This motivates the use of additional sensing modalities and a visual tracking pipeline that is robust to diverse subsea conditions. The proposed system is the first continuous-time trajectory estimation framework based on Gaussian processes to fuse asynchronous measurements from a Doppler velocity log, a stereo camera, and an inertial measurement unit. Additionally, a novel visual frontend is proposed, incorporating learning-based feature extraction and matching that is robust to the specific challenges that subsea environments present. The proposed framework enables seamless integration of additional sensor modalities in continuous-time and is adaptable to different environments without reconfiguration. The proposed system is extensively tested on real-world underwater inspection datasets, where it outperforms state-of-the-art visual-inertial and acoustic-visual-inertial SLAM algorithms in accuracy, robustness, and trajectory coverage. Notably, the proposed system outperforms the state-of-the-art despite only forming short-term visual data associations.

</details>


### [416] [SILO: Simulation-in-the-Loop Sim-to-Real Transfer for Multi-Stage Cable Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04616)
*Stone Tao,Jie Xu,Hesam Rabeti,Yashraj Narang,Yijie Guo,Iretiayo Akinola*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a sim-to-real RL framework for multi-stage cable routing that uses GPU-parallelized simulation for training, combined with a Simulation In the LOop (SILO) deployment strategy, achieving higher success rates and 2x faster cycle times than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear-deformable manipulation (cables, ropes) is challenging due to complex deformations, and prior imitation learning approaches require thousands of demonstrations with limited generalization across tasks and cable types.

Method: A sim-to-real RL framework with: (1) GPU-parallelized simulation for training across diverse cable geometries, (2) SILO (Simulation In the LOop) execution framework, (3) localized RL policies, and (4) robust cable state estimation to bridge the sim-to-real gap.

Result: Achieved higher success rates and 2x reduction in cycle times on real-world cable routing tasks compared to prior state-of-the-art learning methods.

Conclusion: This is the first successful sim-to-real transfer of RL policies for multi-stage cable routing, demonstrating improved efficiency and generalization over prior approaches.

Abstract: Linear-deformable manipulation remains challenging due to the complex deformations of objects such as cables and ropes. Prior data-driven approaches, particularly imitation learning, have shown some promise in narrowly defined settings but typically require thousands of demonstrations for specific tasks and cable types, limiting scalability and generalization. We introduce a sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) framework for multi-stage cable routing that leverages GPU-parallelized simulation to approximate linear deformable behaviors. Training across thousands of parallel simulations enables the learned policies to generalize across diverse cable geometries and deformation patterns. To bridge the sim-to-real gap, we propose a novel deployment strategy that combines a Simulation In the LOop (SILO) execution framework, localized RL policies, and robust cable state estimation. On real-world cable routing tasks, our approach achieves higher success rates and 2x reduction in cycle times compared to prior state-of-the-art learning methods. To our knowledge, this is the first successful sim-to-real transfer of RL policies for multi-stage cable routing. Videos and additional visualizations are available at https://silo-cable-routing.github.io/

</details>


### [417] [Governed Caste Reassignment in Heterogeneous Swarms: An Asymmetric-Trust Protocol with Audited Operator Countersignature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04634)
*Xue Qin,Simin Luan,Cong Yang,Zhijun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes an asymmetric-trust protocol for governing caste reassignment in heterogeneous robot swarms, where downgrades are automatic but upgrades require operator countersignature with cryptographic audit trails. The system resists four attack types and is validated on fleets up to 100 robots with real Ed25519 signatures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing caste reassignment approaches treat robot role changes as internal allocation algorithms without external oversight, which is insufficient for regulated embodied deployments where privilege escalation is a governance event requiring auditability and external authorization.

Method: An asymmetric-trust protocol: auto-tightening (to lower-privilege castes) is automatic, while bounded relaxation (to higher-privilege castes) requires operator countersignature against per-axis budgets. Each transition carries a signed cause-chain committed to a hash-chained Merkle audit log. A distributed audit layer uses N per-member replicas with quorum-committed total order and cryptographic fork exclusion.

Result: Auto-tightening completes in single-digit to low-double-digit milliseconds. The protocol refuses four explicit attacks (caste laundering, repeated-relaxation escalation, operator impersonation, cause-chain forgery) by construction. Randomized fuzz adversary found no admission. Distributed audit layer achieves agreement and fork exclusion in both simulation and real multi-process deployment with up to 100 processes.

Conclusion: The construction successfully generalizes single-agent persona-mutation governance to swarm-level caste governance, providing cryptographically verifiable audit trails and attack resistance without prohibitive performance overhead.

Abstract: In heterogeneous robot swarms, caste reassignment (rebinding a robot to a new capability-bound role) is a high-frequency runtime event driven by battery, payload, and priority changes. Existing approaches treat it as an internal allocation algorithm and do not expose the reassignment to external authority. We argue that for regulated embodied deployments a caste change that elevates a robot's privilege envelope is a governance event that must be auditable and externally authorised. We propose an asymmetric-trust protocol: auto-tightening reassignments (to safer, lower-privilege castes) are admitted automatically, while bounded relaxation (to higher-privilege castes) requires an operator countersignature against a per-axis budget. Each transition carries a signed cause-chain, committed to a hash-chained Merkle audit log that an offline auditor verifies from an operator-signed identity manifest alone. We evaluate a reference implementation with real Ed25519 signatures over fleets up to 100 robots: auto-tightening completes in single-digit to low-double-digit milliseconds, and the governed protocol refuses four explicit attacks (caste laundering, repeated-relaxation escalation, operator impersonation, cause-chain forgery) by construction, with a partially-governed baseline isolating which gate stops which attack and a randomized fuzz adversary finding no admission. A distributed audit layer replicates the log across N per-member replicas with quorum-committed total order and cryptographic fork exclusion; we prove agreement and fork exclusion and validate them both in simulation and as a real multi-process deployment over TCP sockets (up to 100 real processes) with a Byzantine equivocator, on which every honest replica agrees, detects the equivocation, and commits no fork. The construction generalises a single-agent persona-mutation governance gate to swarm-level caste governance.

</details>


### [418] [KAM-WM: Kinematic Affordance Maps from Latent World Models for Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04652)
*Xinyu Shao,Keru Zhou,Guowei Huang,Yajun Gao,Tongtong Cao,Xiu Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KAM-WM extracts directional interaction cues from a frozen video world model to guide robot manipulation policies, achieving strong results on LIBERO (90.6%) and RoboTwin2.0 benchmarks without costly rollout or fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning manipulation from few demonstrations requires visual priors that capture both where and how to interact; static priors like segmentation masks only encode the former (spatial localization), lacking directional information about how interactions should begin.

Method: KAM-WM queries a frozen Flow Matching image-to-video backbone once to extract a single-step latent velocity, interpreted as a Kinematic Affordance Map (KAM). A lightweight Perceiver compresses KAM into tokens that condition a diffusion policy alongside RGB observations and proprioception.

Result: 90.6% average success on LIBERO; 65.7% (Easy) and 22.4% (Hard) on RoboTwin2.0. Controlled comparisons show gains come partly from directional information beyond spatial localization alone.

Conclusion: A frozen video model can provide a useful first-order visual prior for control without test-time rollout cost, and directional cues from latent dynamics improve manipulation over static spatial priors.

Abstract: Learning manipulation from few demonstrations requires visual priors that capture not only where to interact, but also how the interaction should begin; static priors such as segmentation masks encode only the former. We present KAM-WM, a framework that extracts a coarse directional interaction cue from a frozen latent video world model without rollout or world-model fine-tuning. KAM-WM queries a Flow Matching image-to-video backbone once and interprets its single-step latent velocity as a Kinematic Affordance Map (KAM), which provides task-conditioned interaction regions and coarse motion structure. A lightweight Perceiver compresses KAM into tokens that condition a diffusion policy together with RGB observations and proprioception. Across LIBERO and RoboTwin2.0, KAM-WM reaches 90.6% average success on LIBERO and achieves 65.7% and 22.4% success rates in the Easy and Hard settings on RoboTwin2.0, respectively. Controlled comparisons against a zero-order mask prior suggest that part of the gains comes from directional information beyond spatial localization alone. These results indicate that, in the evaluated settings, a frozen video model can provide a useful first-order visual prior for control without the test-time cost of future rollout.

</details>


### [419] [Do Vision-Language-Action Models Mean What They Say? On the Role of Faithfulness in Embodied Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04681)
*Matthew Foutter,Matteo Cercola,Lena Wild,Yunshan Wang,Michelle Li,Daniele Gammelli,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper distinguishes between functional reasoning (improves task performance) and faithful reasoning (truly reflects policy's internal decision process) in embodied Chain-of-Thought for VLA models. It finds SoTA alignment strategies insufficient for faithfulness, proposes Pinocchio—a learned critic that scores observation grounding and stepwise coherence—and uses it as a dense reward for RL-based post-training. Results show improved faithfulness, competitive task performance, and 1.6x better responsiveness to rare counterfactual scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While embodied Chain-of-Thought improves robot decision-making and interpretability in VLA models, it remains unclear whether such verbalized reasoning truthfully reflects the policy's underlying decision process. SoTA alignment strategies provide only a necessary but insufficient notion of faithfulness, allowing reasoning that masks causal links in action prediction.

Method: The authors conduct human evaluation of a SoTA reasoning model for autonomous driving, revealing inconsistency between reasoning quality and trajectory improvement. They operationalize a behavioral surrogate for embodied faithfulness via a learned critic (Pinocchio) that scores observation grounding and stepwise coherence, then use this critic as a dense reward signal in RL-based post-training.

Result: Post-trained planner improves faithfulness by 4% over SoTA alignment and 18% over trajectory error baselines. On synthetic OOD test set, faithfulness post-training improves policy responsiveness to rare counterfactual scenarios by 1.6x over SoTA policy, while maintaining competitive downstream task performance.

Conclusion: Faithful reasoning traces contribute to more robust, generalizable, and interpretable embodied intelligence. The proposed Pinocchio critic effectively bridges the gap between functional and faithful reasoning in embodied policies.

Abstract: Embodied Chain-of-Thought has emerged as a promising mechanism to enhance robot decision-making and interpretability in black-box Vision-Language Action (VLA) models. However, whether this verbalized Chain-of-Thought truthfully reflects the policy's underlying decision process remains poorly understood. We distinguish between functional reasoning, in which reasoning improves task performance, and faithful reasoning, in which reasoning truly reflects the policy's internal decision process. We argue that SoTA alignment strategies offer a necessary but insufficient notion of faithfulness, admitting reasoning whose intermediate steps can mask the causal links in action prediction through confounding factors (e.g., reasoning that is ungrounded in the environment and internally disconnected or inconsistent), restricting policy generalization. We study this gap through a human evaluation of a SoTA reasoning model for autonomous driving, revealing an inconsistent coupling between reasoning quality and downstream trajectory improvement. We then operationalize a behavioral surrogate for embodied faithfulness through a learned critic, Pinocchio, scoring observation grounding and stepwise coherence, and use this critic as a dense reward signal in post-training an embodied policy with reinforcement learning. Across withheld driving benchmarks, our post-trained planner improves faithfulness by 4% and 18% over SoTA alignment and trajectory error post-training baselines, respectively, while maintaining competitive downstream task performance. Finally, on a synthetic out-of-distribution test set, post-training for faithfulness improves policy responsiveness to rare counterfactual scenarios by 1.6x that of a SoTA policy, suggesting that faithful reasoning traces contribute to more robust, generalizable, and interpretable embodied intelligence. Project page: https://mjf-su.github.io/pinocchio/

</details>


### [420] [A Reliable Context-Aware and Temporal Planning Framework for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04689)
*Argho Dey,Yunfei Yin,Swachha Ray,Md Minhazul Islam,Zheng Yuan,Sijing Xiong,Hongyu Liu,Zhiqiu Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RCT-AD is a framework for autonomous driving that uses a quality-gated FILO memory mechanism to selectively retain reliable features from camera inputs, reconstructing degraded observations from historical context, leading to improved perception and planning robustness on the nuScenes benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Camera observations in dense urban traffic are frequently corrupted by occlusion, motion blur, illumination change, and sensor noise; when degraded observations are aggregated indiscriminately over time, trajectory planning becomes unstable and collision risk rises.

Method: RCT-AD introduces: 1) a Reliable Context Awareness module with quality-gated First-In-Last-Out (FILO) memory for per-frame reliability scoring and selective feature retention, 2) a Temporal Trajectory Planner capturing long-term dependencies and multi-agent interactions, and 3) a joint detection-and-segmentation head injecting semantic and motion cues into shared BEV space.

Result: Achieves 61.5 nuScenes Detection Score, 52.9 mean Average Precision, and 52.3 mean Intersection over Union on nuScenes benchmark, with competitive computational efficiency for real-time deployment.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling feature quality and temporal consistency supports safer, more consistent planning; selectively retaining trustworthy features from historical context prevents corrupted inputs from destabilizing the scene representation.

Abstract: Safe operation of autonomous vehicles in dense urban traffic depends on perception and planning that remain reliable when onboard sensing is degraded. In real driving conditions, camera observations are frequently corrupted by occlusion, motion blur, illumination change, and sensor noise, and when such degraded observations are aggregated indiscriminately over time, trajectory planning becomes unstable and collision risk rises for both the ego vehicle and surrounding road users. Recent Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) approaches unify perception and planning through a shared spatial representation, but most fuse temporal information across frames without assessing the reliability of the underlying observations. We present a Reliable Context-Aware and Temporal Planning framework for Autonomous Driving (RCT-AD) that explicitly models feature quality and temporal consistency to support safer, more consistent planning. A Reliable Context Awareness module scores per-frame reliability and selectively retains trustworthy features through a quality-gated First-In-Last-Out (FILO) memory mechanism, reconstructing degraded observations from reliable historical context so that corrupted inputs do not destabilize the scene representation. A Temporal Trajectory Planner captures long-term dependencies and multi-agent interactions to produce smoother, safety-aware trajectories, while a joint detection-and-segmentation head injects semantic and motion cues into the shared BEV space to strengthen scene understanding. Experiments on the nuScenes autonomous driving benchmark show that RCT-AD improves perception accuracy, motion prediction, and planning robustness over recent end-to-end baselines, achieving 61.5 nuScenes Detection Score, 52.9 mean Average Precision, and 52.3 mean Intersection over Union, while maintaining competitive computational efficiency suitable for real-time deployment.

</details>


### [421] [Geometry-Aware Motion Latents for Learning Robust Manipulation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04714)
*Yunchao Zhang,Yijia Weng,Ruizhe Liu,Ming Hu,Leonidas Guibas,Yanchao Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GeoMoLa learns discrete motion latents for robotic manipulation by predicting how point clouds evolve over time (4D geometry) rather than reconstructing visual observations, achieving SOTA performance with single-view RGB-D input.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion latent learning methods rely on reconstructing visual appearances (pixel-level patterns), which confound appearance and motion. The authors argue that effective action abstractions require understanding 3D geometric transformations, motivating a geometry-aware approach.

Method: GeoMoLa learns discrete motion latent codes by predicting the evolution of point clouds over time during manipulation — a 4D objective (spatial geometry changing through time). It uses only single-view RGB-D input, unlike methods requiring multi-view reconstruction.

Result: GeoMoLa achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse manipulation benchmarks with only single-view RGB-D input, where existing methods need multi-view reconstruction. Ablations confirm geometric prediction drives performance. Real-world experiments show robust manipulation with minimal demonstrations in cluttered environments.

Conclusion: Effective motion latents for robot control emerge better from understanding motion through its 3D geometric effects rather than pixel-level appearance patterns. Spatial understanding is the key driver for manipulation task success.

Abstract: Learning motion latents for robotic manipulation heavily relies on extracting motion patterns from visual sequences, yet effective action abstractions require understanding three-dimensional geometric transformations. Here, we introduce GeoMoLa (Geometry-Aware Motion Latents), which learns discrete motion latent codes by predicting how point clouds evolve during manipulation rather than reconstructing visual observations. This four-dimensional objective -- spatial geometry changing through time -- forces latent representations to encode actual physical motion rather than appearance patterns. GeoMoLa achieves state-of-the-art performance using only single-view RGB-D input, while existing methods require multi-view reconstruction, succeeding across diverse manipulation benchmarks. Our ablations reveal that geometric prediction is the key to driving performance, quantitatively validating that manipulation depends on spatial understanding. Furthermore, the learned codes exhibit effective motion abstraction: applying them to novel scenes produces physically consistent transformations regardless of visual context. Our real-world experiments also confirm this robustness capability, achieving robust manipulation with minimal demonstrations in cluttered environments where geometric reasoning determines success. Thus, we demonstrate that effective motion latents for robot control can better emerge from understanding motion through its three-dimensional effects rather than pixel-level patterns.

</details>


### [422] [Aerial Manipulation: Contact, Medium Coupling, and the Geometry of Readiness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04719)
*Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper reviews aerial manipulation from a medium-aware perspective, arguing it's fundamentally different from ground-based manipulation due to the coupling between flight dynamics and physical interaction. It organizes interaction modes into a capability ladder, develops an actuation-geometric framework using task-equivalent fibers, and explains why biological flyers outperform current robots, suggesting future platforms should command forces while shaping medium feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing assumption that aerial manipulation is simply classical manipulation on a flying base, and to provide a unified framework that accounts for the intrinsic coupling between flight stabilization and physical interaction through the surrounding medium.

Method: The paper surveys biological and robotic examples, organizes them into a repertoire of interaction modes and a capability ladder, then develops an actuation-geometric viewpoint where redundancy induces task-equivalent fibers along which internal motion can trade energy for readiness, aerodynamic promptness, and passive medium coupling.

Result: A medium-aware interpretation of aerial manipulation, a structured capability ladder of interaction modes, and an actuation-geometric framework revealing how internal motions along task-equivalent fibers enable trade-offs between energy, readiness, and medium coupling.

Conclusion: Aerial manipulation is fundamentally coupled with flight dynamics and medium interaction; biological flyers remain more versatile than current robotic systems; future platforms should both command forces and actively shape how the medium acts back on them.

Abstract: Aerial robots are increasingly moving from remote observation toward physical interaction with objects, surfaces, structures, loads, and surrounding flows. This review argues that aerial manipulation cannot be understood as classical manipulation simply mounted on a flying base. Because flying agents remain aloft through continuous momentum and energy exchange with the surrounding medium, support, locomotion, stabilization, and task-directed interaction are intrinsically coupled. Building on broad views of manipulation as intentional environmental regulation through physical interaction, we propose a medium-aware interpretation of aerial manipulation in which interaction may be mediated by contact, by the surrounding fluid, or by both. The review organizes biological and robotic examples into a repertoire of interaction modes and a capability ladder, then develops an actuation-geometric viewpoint in which redundancy induces task-equivalent fibers. Internal motion along these fibers can trade energy for active readiness, aerodynamic promptness, and passive medium coupling. This perspective clarifies why aerial manipulation is difficult, why biological flyers remain broader than robotic systems, and how future platforms may command forces while also shaping how the medium acts back on them.

</details>
