Motivation: Current benchmarks evaluate LLMs on simplified medical calculations where each case uses one calculator and the tool is explicitly specified, failing to reflect real clinical scenarios that require multiple calculators, nested-scale calculations, and fuzzy queries without direct calculator specification.

Method: (1) MedCalc-Pro benchmark: 2,268 real clinical cases, 77 calculators, 14 departments, with three settings (single, multi, nested-calculator). (2) A generalizable agent framework supporting multi-tool selection, nested-tool calling, structured validation, and evidence review to suppress parameter error propagation.

Result: The proposed agent framework achieves best performance across all three task settings (single, multi, nested-calculator) compared to open-source, closed-source, and medical-specialized LLMs.

Conclusion: MedCalc-Pro provides a more realistic benchmark for medical calculation evaluation, and the proposed agent framework effectively handles complex multi-tool and nested-tool clinical scenarios.

Abstract: Current benchmarks for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in medical calculation are largely based on simplified settings, where each patient case corresponds to a single calculator and the required tool is explicitly specified in the query. However, real clinical scenarios often require multiple calculators for joint evaluation, nested-scale calculation, and fuzzy queries that do not directly specify the target calculator. To this end, we propose a new medical calculation benchmark, MedCalc-Pro, which covers three progressively challenging task settings: single-calculator, multi-calculator, and nested-calculator calculation settings. MedCalc-Pro contains 2,268 real-world clinical cases, covering 77 medical calculators across 14 clinical departments. Meanwhile, to address the limited performance of existing frameworks and methods in complex clinical scenarios, we further propose a more generalizable agent framework that supports multi-tool selection and nested-tool calling, while suppressing parameter error propagation through structured validation and evidence review. We conduct systematic comparisons across open-source, closed-source, and medical-specialized LLMs, and the results show that our framework achieves the best performance across all three task settings. This work provides a new benchmark and method for evaluating and applying LLMs in challenging medical calculation scenarios.

</details>


### [450] [Oyster-II: Reinforcement Learning for Constructive Safety Alignment in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02914)
*Jiyang Guan,Yong Xie,Jun Chen,Jiexi Liu,Zipeng Ye,Defeng Li,Jiayu Shen,Jialing Tao,Hui Xue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Oyster-II is an RL-based constructive safety alignment framework that improves upon SFT-based Oyster-I by addressing safety generalization issues and safety CoT over-generalization, achieving safety performance comparable to much larger models like Qwen3.5-397B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional refusal-oriented alignment strategies fail to serve legitimate user needs by withholding safely addressable information. Oyster-I's SFT-based constructive safety approach suffers from insufficient safety generalization and safety CoT over-generalization that degrades helpfulness.

Method: Oyster-II adopts a Zero-RL paradigm combined with a multi-stage reinforcement learning strategy for constructive safety alignment.

Result: Oyster-II surpasses Qwen3-14B and Oyster-I on safety dimensions, achieving cross-scale performance comparable to Qwen3-Max and Qwen3.5-397B.

Conclusion: RL-based constructive safety alignment (Oyster-II) effectively overcomes SFT limitations, achieving better safety and helpfulness balance while scaling performance to match much larger models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse applications, yet ensuring their simultaneous safety, helpfulness, and trustworthiness remains a persistent challenge. Conventional refusal-oriented alignment strategies mitigate harmful content generation but systematically fail to serve legitimate user needs, often withholding information that could safely and constructively address the underlying intent of sensitive queries. Building upon the constructive safety paradigm pioneered by Oyster-I, which moves beyond blanket refusal toward thoughtful, response-oriented safety alignment, we identify two critical limitations of its Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)-based scheme: insufficient safety generalization to out-of-distribution scenarios and a phenomenon we term safety chain-of-thought (CoT) over-generalization, wherein safety-oriented reasoning patterns are excessively applied to benign queries, degrading helpfulness and user experience. To address these limitations, we propose Oyster-II, a reinforcement learning (RL)-based constructive safety alignment framework that adopts a Zero-RL paradigm combined with a multi-stage reinforcement learning strategy.Evaluated across extensive benchmarks, Oyster-II comprehensively surpasses both Qwen3-14B and its predecessor Oyster-I on safety dimensions, achieving cross-scale performance comparable to Qwen3-Max and Qwen3.5-397B.

</details>


### [451] [VERITAS: Towards a General-Purpose Replication Tool for Scientific Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02931)
*Haokun Liu,Filbert Aurelian Tjiaranata,Chenhao Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VERITAS is a domain-agnostic replication framework using CLI coding agents that extracts claims from papers, runs methodologies with automatic issue resolution, and returns a weighted Replication Score. It achieves state-of-the-art results across 65 papers in CS, social science, medicine, and astrophysics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-accelerated scientific publishing has outpaced review and verification systems. Manual replication is slow and expensive, motivating automated replication tools. Existing efforts are limited to benchmark-specific pipelines with no general-purpose solution available.

Method: VERITAS is a domain-agnostic framework built around CLI coding agents. It extracts paper claims, runs the methodology with automatic issue resolution, judges each claim against experimental evidence, and produces an importance-weighted Replication Score, a severity-rated fix log, and a patched codebase.

Result: Evaluated on CORE-Bench and ReplicationBench across 65 papers (CS, social science, medicine, astrophysics). Against two strong Claude Code baselines on the same model and host environment, VERITAS achieves state-of-the-art performance and leads on every metric on both benchmarks.

Conclusion: VERITAS provides a general-purpose, domain-agnostic tool for automated replication that outperforms existing baselines, addressing the critical gap in scalable research verification.

Abstract: AI tools are accelerating scientific publication while the systems that review it struggle to keep up, and independent verification of published research has become both harder and more important. As manual replication is slow and expensive, a growing line of work uses coding agents to automate parts of the process. Existing efforts are largely packaged as benchmarks with companion agents that only run inside the benchmark's own pipeline, and no general-purpose replication tool exists. We present VERITAS, a domain-agnostic replication framework built around CLI coding agents. Given a paper, a code repository, or both, VERITAS extracts the paper's claims, runs the methodology while resolving issues as they arise, and judges each claim against the evidence from experiment runs. The pipeline returns an importance-weighted Replication Score, a severity-rated log of every fix applied, and the patched codebase. We evaluate VERITAS on CORE-Bench and ReplicationBench, 65 papers spanning computer science, social science, medicine, and astrophysics. Against two strong Claude Code baselines on the same model and host environment, VERITAS achieves state-of-the-art performance and leads on every metric on both benchmarks.

</details>


### [452] [A Sliding-Window-Based Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Assembly Flow Shop Scheduling with Multi-Product Delivery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02941)
*Junhao Qiu,Jianjun Liu,Ting Liu,Rongjie Liao,Zhantao Li,Qingfu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes SWRL, a sliding-window-based reinforcement learning framework for real-time scheduling in flexible assembly flow shops with multi-product kitting constraints, demonstrating superior tardiness reduction and robustness on real-world manufacturing instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-product kitting delivery creates complex real-time scheduling challenges in hybrid manufacturing systems due to dynamic order arrivals that simultaneously alter supply dependencies and feasible job-machine assignments.

Method: SWRL integrates three key components: (1) a sliding-window filtering mechanism to filter inactive nodes and prioritize kitting-critical operations, (2) a spatiotemporal graph encoding network to track bottleneck shifts across decision states, and (3) a dynamic action mapping module with a constrained waiting strategy for changing action spaces under variable topologies.

Result: Experiments on real-world instances from a home appliance manufacturer show consistent tardiness reductions over classical dispatching rules and existing deep reinforcement learning methods, with robust performance across varying resource configurations, order loads, and arrival concentrations.

Conclusion: SWRL effectively addresses the sparse reward landscape and dynamic complexities of kitting-constrained flexible assembly flow shop scheduling, offering a practical end-to-end online scheduling solution.

Abstract: Multi-product kitting delivery imposes significant challenges for real-time scheduling in hybrid manufacturing systems that integrate processing and assembly, as dynamic order arrivals simultaneously alter supply dependencies and the set of feasible job-machine assignments. This paper proposes a sliding-window-based reinforcement learning (SWRL) framework for end-to-end online scheduling in the flexible assembly flow shop scheduling problem with complex kitting constraints. The problem is formulated as a heterogeneous graph-based Markov decision process that captures the dual-layer kitting structure and the tail-product bottleneck dynamics that produce a sparse reward landscape. To address the resulting challenges, SWRL integrates a sliding-window filtering mechanism that filters inactive nodes and prioritizes kitting-critical operations, a spatiotemporal graph encoding network that tracks bottleneck shifts across consecutive decision states, and a dynamic action mapping module with a constrained waiting strategy that adapts to the changing action space under variable topologies. Experiments on real-world instances from a home appliance manufacturer demonstrate that SWRL achieves consistent tardiness reductions over classical dispatching rules and existing deep reinforcement learning methods, and exhibits robust performance across varying resource configurations, order loads, and arrival concentrations.

</details>


### [453] [Evaluating Generative Agents with Actions Grounded in Socially Distributed Task Environments using Incognita](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02975)
*Dan C. Hsu,Luke Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Incognita, a framework for evaluating AI agents in socially distributed task environments where knowledge is split across different roles. Tests show agents improve from 0% to 17.2% success rate but remain unreliable, highlighting gaps in social reasoning and action justification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks either focus on grounded execution (actions, state, verifiable outcomes) or social simulation (language interaction), but not both. The authors want to evaluate agents in settings where they must communicate with role-isolated participants to acquire knowledge before taking consequential actions.

Method: Incognita framework separates social interaction (message routing to users/specialists) from grounded execution (deterministic sub-environment with canonical state). Incognita-Retail adapts tau-bench retail into a multi-entity environment. Three generative agent models evaluated on 18 tasks with 540 trials.

Result: Success rates rose from 0% to 8.9% and 17.2% across models. Premature finalization dropped from 100% to 87% and 58%. Stronger models elicited more hidden knowledge, contacted more entities, and attempted more grounded writes, but reliability remained low.

Conclusion: Socially distributed task environments expose key behavioral failure modes (knowledge elicitation, source selection, premature completion) before achieving reliable success, revealing important gaps in current generative agents.

Abstract: Effective agency in social environments depends on when an agent seeks knowledge, when it acts, and whether its actions are justified by acquired information. Existing grounded benchmarks provide executable actions, persistent state, and verifiable outcomes, while social simulation environments provide rich interaction among language agents. We study an evaluation setting that combines these requirements. We define socially distributed task environments as interactive environments where task-relevant knowledge is partitioned across role-isolated participants and consequential actions are accessible only through them. Communication serves as exploration over role-partitioned knowledge, while grounded action serves as exploitation over environment state. We introduce Incognita, a Concordia-based framework that separates social interaction from grounded execution. The evaluated agent routes messages to a user or specialist entities; specialists mediate admissible operations; a deterministic sub-environment executes accepted operations over a canonical state; and an offline evaluator scores outcomes with inherited rewards. Incognita-Retail transforms tau-bench retail into a multi-entity environment while preserving final-state reward semantics. We evaluate three generative agent models on 18 tasks stratified by social breadth, with 540 trials. Progress appears in reward and behavior: success rises from 0 percent to 8.9 percent and 17.2 percent, while premature finalization falls from 100 percent to 87 percent and 58 percent. Stronger models elicit more hidden knowledge, contact more entities, and attempt more grounded writes, yet reliability remains low. These findings show that socially distributed task environments expose behavior before reliable success, including knowledge elicitation, source selection, grounded action attempts, and premature completion belief.

</details>


### [454] [Reinforcement Learning for Evidence-Seeking Diagnostic Reasoning with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02983)
*Shengyi Hua,Kangzhe Hu,Conghui He,Xiaofan Zhang,Shaoting Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an Iterative Evidence-Seeking Task for medical diagnosis using Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation-based Examination Simulator (RAGES), enabling LLMs to transition from passive responders to autonomous, investigative clinical assistants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world clinical diagnosis is an iterative evidence-seeking process, but current LLMs operate on passive-inference patterns assuming complete information, failing to capture this active investigative nature.

Method: Formalizes medical diagnosis as an Iterative Evidence-Seeking Task; uses RLVR to elicit intrinsic reasoning in a closed-loop environment with rewards enforcing diagnostic precision and examination consistency; introduces RAGES for realistic, knowledge-grounded follow-up evidence.

Result: The framework enables LLMs to become autonomous assistants; achieves comparable performance to larger/reasoning-enhanced baselines; RAGES outperforms vanilla LLMs in generating biologically plausible clinical feedback.

Conclusion: LLMs can be transitioned from passive responders to autonomous clinical agents through iterative evidence-seeking with reinforcement learning and a high-fidelity examination simulator.

Abstract: Recent reasoning-centric Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant strides, yet they predominantly operate on a passive-inference pattern that assumes complete information. In contrast, real-world clinical intelligence is inherently an iterative investigative process requiring strategic evidence acquisition. To bridge this gap, we formalize medical diagnosis as an Iterative Evidence-Seeking Task. We leverage Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to elicit intrinsic reasoning within a closed-loop environment, guided by a novel suite of rewards that enforce diagnostic precision and examination consistency. To facilitate this, we introduce the Retrieval-Augmented Generation-based Examination Simulator (RAGES), a high-fidelity clinical oracle that provides realistic, knowledge-grounded follow-up evidence. Empirical results across diverse datasets demonstrate that our framework enables LLMs to transition from passive responders to autonomous assistants. Notably, our model demonstrates comparable performance to larger and reasoning-enhanced baselines, while RAGES proves superior to vanilla LLMs in generating biologically plausible clinical feedback.

</details>


### [455] [Beyond Forecasting: The Belief-to-Trade Layer in Prediction-Market Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03015)
*Yishu Wang,Yuxuan Wang,Jiaqi Deng,Hanyang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Raven-Agent, the first autonomous trading agent for prediction markets, which achieves positive returns and positive risk-adjusted returns on archived decision sets, outperforming all tested policies including calibrated probability models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction markets serve as a compelling testbed for general-purpose AI, but existing benchmarks show a gap between calibrated probability scores and actual trading results because trading requires more than just forecasting abilities.

Method: Raven-Agent is an autonomous trading agent designed for prediction markets. The paper evaluates it through controlled replay experiments over an archived decision set, comparing its performance against multiple baseline policies.

Result: Raven-Agent achieves the only positive return and the only positive risk-adjusted return among all tested policies in the controlled replay experiments.

Conclusion: Autonomous trading agents like Raven-Agent represent a promising direction for closing the gap between forecasting ability and actual trading performance in prediction markets, offering a more comprehensive test for general-purpose AI systems.

Abstract: Forecasting future events has attracted growing attention as a testbed for general-purpose AI. A natural way to ground this evaluation is let the models trade in the prediction markets. Trading, however, requires more than forecasting. Moreover, recent benchmarks report a substantial gap between calibrated probability scores and the trading results. We propose Raven-Agent, to the best of our knowledge, the first autonomous trading agent for prediction markets. On a controlled replay over an archived decision set, our architecture achieves the only positive return and the only positive risk-adjusted return among all tested policies. We have released our code in https://github.com/Alchemist-X/predict-raven .

</details>


### [456] [Human-Centric Reflective Architecture for Human-AI Collaborative Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03025)
*Andreas Kouridakis,Dimitrios Patiniotis Spyropoulos,George Vouros*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces HCRA, a human-AI collaborative decision-making framework that uses stochastic games and reinforcement learning with linguistic feedback to improve decision effectiveness and alignment with human expectations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans often over- or under-rely on AI recommendations, and AI systems are poorly calibrated to human expectations, creating risks especially in safety-critical applications.

Method: Formulates collaborative decision-making as a stochastic game between AI agent and human player; proposes HCRA integrating human-calibrated models with RL agents using iterative linguistic feedback.

Result: HCRA enhances decision-making effectiveness and delivers high-quality recommendations.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully augments human capabilities while aligning AI agents with human preferences and expectations.

Abstract: The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) across diverse areas of human activity-ranging from everyday tasks to safety-critical applications-aims to enhance decision-making effectiveness with minimal human feedback. Concurrently, it seeks to align decisions with human expectations, preferences, and needs while mitigating risks associated with AI non-determinism. However, humans frequently over- or under-rely on AI recommendations, and current AI systems remain poorly calibrated to human expectations. To address these challenges, we introduce a human-AI collaborative decision-making framework designed to augment human capabilities and align AI agents with human preferences and expectations. Specifically, this paper (a) formulates the collaborative decision-making task as a stochastic game between an AI agent and a human player, and (b) proposes the Human-Centric Reflective Architecture (HCRA), which integrates human-calibrated models with reinforcement learning agents that leverage linguistic feedback in an iterative, reflective process. Evaluation results demonstrate that HCRA enhances decision-making effectiveness and delivers high-quality recommendations.

</details>


### [457] [Silicon Sampling via Cross-Survey Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03091)
*Chan-Tung Ku,Chan Hsu,Pei-Cing Huang,Frank Cheng-shan Liu,I-Ling Cheng,Yihuang Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes cross-survey transfer as a rigorous evaluation framework for LLMs simulating human respondents, finding that zero-shot LLMs achieve 52% accuracy on unseen survey items (within 6pp of supervised ML), uncover a construct predictability hierarchy, and reveal that variance collapse and safety alignment effects are more nuanced than previously believed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of silicon sampling rely on distributional comparisons rather than individual-level prediction, which risks conflating pattern matching with coherent respondent-level prediction. The authors propose a more rigorous evaluation framework.

Method: Cross-survey transfer framework: an LLM is given a respondent's answers to one set of questions and must predict their answers to entirely different questions from the same survey. Uses TEDS 2024 data, three open-weight LLMs (27B-120B parameters), and supervised ML baselines.

Result: (1) Zero-shot LLMs achieve 52% accuracy on unseen items, within 6pp of supervised random forest. (2) A construct predictability hierarchy exists (67% for partisan attitudes to 23% for sovereignty). (3) Variance collapse and safety alignment effects are more nuanced than previously reported.

Conclusion: The findings clarify both the promise and boundaries of silicon sampling, showing LLMs can be useful for survey simulation but with important caveats regarding construct-specific predictability and the nuanced nature of known LLM limitations.

Abstract: Silicon sampling-using large language models (LLMs) to simulate human survey respondents-has emerged as a promising approach for augmenting traditional survey research. However, most evaluations rely on distributional comparisons rather than individual-level prediction, which risks conflating pattern matching with coherent respondent-level prediction. We propose cross-survey transfer, a more rigorous evaluation framework in which an LLM is given a respondent's answers to one set of questions and must predict their answers to entirely different questions from the same survey. Using data from the Taiwan Election and Democratization Study (TEDS) 2024, three open-weight LLMs (27B-120B parameters), and supervised machine learning baselines, we find that: (1) zero-shot LLMs achieve 52% accuracy on genuinely unseen items, closing to within 6 percentage points (pp) of a supervised random forest trained on same-population data; (2) a stable construct predictability hierarchy emerges, from 67% for partisan attitudes to 23% for sovereignty; and (3) variance collapse and safety alignment effects-two commonly cited LLM limitations-turn out to be more nuanced than previously reported, with variance collapse affecting supervised models as well and alignment effects varying dramatically across model families. These findings clarify both the promise and boundaries of silicon sampling.

</details>


### [458] [APeB: Benchmarking Personalization Ability of Large Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03162)
*Garry Yang,Zizhe Chen,Xinru Chen,Yongqiang Chen,Jianxiang Wang,Deyu Zou,Linyi Ding,Jialiang Wu,Yunzhong He,Yu Gong,James Cheng,Huaixiao Tou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a personalized product search (PPS) testbed and benchmark (APeB) for evaluating LLM agents on underspecified queries. It finds current models struggle with early-stage queries due to poor history utilization, and proposes VQRA, a history-aware refinement pipeline that improves performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents fail at personalization when users give vague, underspecified queries. Existing benchmarks rely on refined queries or simplified histories, failing to test this realistic capability.

Method: The authors construct the Agent Personalized Benchmark (APeB) from action logs, pairing underspecified intents with rich interaction histories and candidate items. They evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs using multi-step agent workflows and propose VQRA, a history-aware query-refinement pipeline.

Result: LLMs handle explicit queries well but struggle with early-stage queries requiring intent discovery. Rubric analysis shows the main gap is ineffective history use. VQRA yields consistent improvements over baselines.

Conclusion: Dedicated history-utilization modules are needed for personalized LLM agents; simple pipelines like VQRA demonstrate meaningful gains.

Abstract: LLM-powered agents struggle with personalization when users issue raw, underspecified queries. In this setting, agents must infer latent intent, extract preferences from noisy interaction histories, and select among competing alternatives. Existing benchmarks rarely test this capability, as they often rely on user-refined queries or simplified histories. We introduce personalized product search (PPS), a testbed for agentic personalization under raw queries and diverse histories. We construct Agent Personalized Benchmark (APeB) from action logs, pairing underspecified intents with rich histories and user-viewed candidate items. Evaluating state-of-the-art LLMs with multi-step agent workflows, we find that models handle explicit queries well but struggle with early-stage queries requiring intent and preference discovery. Rubric analysis attributes this gap mainly to ineffective history use. A simple history-aware query-refinement pipeline, VQRA, yields consistent gains, highlighting the need for dedicated history-utilization modules in personalized agents.

</details>


### [459] [Organizational Memory for Agentic Business Process Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03228)
*Lukas Kirchdorfer,Adrian Rebmann,Christian Warmuth,Timotheus Kampik,Theiss Heilker,Gregor Berg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an organizational memory architecture for LLM-based agents that enables shared, governed, and scalable management of procedural knowledge in business process execution, addressing the limitations of siloed prompt/retrieval approaches in enterprises.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches for encoding organization-specific knowledge in LLM agents (individual prompts or agent-specific retrieval) do not scale in enterprises due to knowledge silos, rule duplicates, and difficulty in consistent updates. A shared, governed memory layer is needed.

Method: The authors derive requirements for organizational memory, propose an architecture for curating and consuming such memory, and demonstrate its effectiveness through a proof-of-concept procurement scenario.

Result: A proof-of-concept demonstration shows the effectiveness of the proposed organizational memory architecture in a procurement scenario.

Conclusion: Organizational memory is essential for scaling LLM-based agents in enterprise business process execution, providing a shared, governed reference layer for procedural knowledge that overcomes the scalability issues of siloed approaches.

Abstract: LLM-based agents offer new opportunities for automating business process execution beyond the limits of rule-based systems. However, general-purpose LLMs lack the organization-specific knowledge required for reliable execution, which is typically fragmented across human-oriented artifacts such as policies, process models, and standard operating procedures. While such knowledge can technically be encoded in individual prompts or agent-specific retrieval setups, this approach does not scale in enterprises, as it gives rise to knowledge silos and rule duplicates, and makes consistent updates and learning across agents difficult. We argue that this calls for an organizational memory for agentic business process execution: a shared, governed, and agent-consumable reference layer of evolving organization-specific procedural knowledge about how work should be executed. We derive requirements for such a memory, propose an architecture for its curation and consumption, and demonstrate its effectiveness in a proof-of-concept based on a procurement scenario.

</details>


### [460] [Embodied Operators and Benchmarking: Toward Reusable and Deployable Embodied Intelligence Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03283)
*Junwu Xiong,Jiaxuan Gao,Wei Chai,Renxing Chen,Yuzhen Li,Yu Guo,Yucheng Guo,Mingxi Luo,Wenyang Ma,Yiyun Mou,Yifei Zhang,Chen Zhou,Yongjian Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper defines "embodied operators" as reusable, composable functional modules for embodied intelligence systems, provides a taxonomy across five categories, proposes a multi-dimensional benchmark framework, and discusses challenges in operator optimization and deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied intelligence systems lack standardized, reusable, and verifiable functional modules beyond end-to-end policy models. The authors seek to formalize such modules as "embodied operators" to enable composability, deployability, and systematic evaluation.

Method: The authors define embodied operators with clear boundary criteria (task semantics, standardized I/O contracts, deployability, reusability, multi-layer optimizability), construct a five-category taxonomy, propose a multi-dimensional benchmark framework, and discuss acceleration and open challenges.

Result: A taxonomy covering detection/segmentation, spatial localization/3D understanding, hand motion recovery, embodied foundation models/task-decision operators, and planning/control/system operators. A benchmark framework evaluating correctness, efficiency, resource usage, stability, portability, compatibility, reliability, and downstream utility.

Conclusion: Embodied operators should be treated as holistic deployable components, optimized and evaluated systematically to build reusable, scalable, and verifiable embodied intelligence systems.

Abstract: Embodied intelligence systems require not only end-to-end policy models, but also reusable functional modules that transform multimodal observations, robot states, human demonstrations, and task contexts into structured representations, decisions, trajectories, control references, and system services. This work defines these modules as embodied operators and studies them as independent yet composable units in embodied intelligence pipelines. We clarify their definition boundary, emphasizing task semantics, standardized input-output contracts, deployability, reusability, and multi-layer optimizability. We further construct a taxonomy covering five categories: detection and segmentation, spatial localization and 3D understanding, hand motion recovery, embodied foundation models and task-decision operators, and planning, control, and system support operators. For each category, we summarize representative functions, technical paradigms, application roles, and practical limitations. Beyond taxonomy, we propose a multi-dimensional benchmark framework that evaluates embodied operators in terms of correctness, end-to-end efficiency, resource usage, temporal stability, portability, interface compatibility, deployment reliability, and downstream task utility. We also discuss workflow-level operator acceleration and open challenges in operator composition, data standardization, world models, VLA safety, edge deployment, and real-world application value. Overall, this work argues that embodied operators should be optimized and evaluated as holistic deployable components, providing a foundation for reusable, scalable, and verifiable embodied intelligence systems.

</details>


### [461] [Reflective Dialogue or Prompt Refinement? Effects of Tutor Scaffolding on Students' Independent LLM Use for Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03303)
*Jerome Brender,Laila El-Hamamsy,Kim Uittenhove,Aitor Perez,Patrick Jermann,Francesco Mondada,Engin Bumbacher*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This study compares two LLM-based tutoring approaches—Socratic-Guidance (dialogic questioning) vs. Prompt-Refinement (prompt formulation guidance)—in a robotics course, finding that Socratic guidance leads to better learning outcomes and more understanding-driven prompting strategies when students later use unconstrained LLMs, despite being perceived as less efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs can personalize learning, concerns exist about their educational use. Learning outcomes depend on how students engage with LLMs, yet little is known about which tutor designs best develop students' capacity to learn with LLMs effectively over time.

Method: Two-phase study in a graduate mobile robotics course: Phase 1 (6-week intervention) had 66 students using either Socratic-Guidance (SG) or Prompt-Refinement (PR) tutors; Phase 2 (3-week project) had 52 students using an unconstrained LLM. Measured prompt practices, learning gains, and subsequent LLM-use strategies.

Result: SG and PR tutors produced similar task performance and prompting patterns during guided use, but SG students achieved higher learning gains in later sessions and adopted more understanding-driven prompting strategies when using unconstrained LLMs, which predicted higher understanding.

Conclusion: Socratic guidance, though perceived as less efficient, better develops students' capacity to learn with LLMs over time, highlighting its importance for LLM tutor design in educational contexts.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) can provide personalized support in learning, several studies have raised concerns regarding their use in education. Importantly, learning depends on how students engage with LLMs. This study examined how two types of LLM-based tutors shape students' prompting practices, learning, and subsequent LLM-use: a Socratic-Guidance (SG) tutor, which structures interaction through dialogic questioning, and a Prompt-Refinement (PR) tutor that guides the formulation of effective prompts. We conducted a two-phase study in a graduate-level mobile robotics course: 66 students used either the SG or PR tutor during a 6-week intervention, followed by 52 students using an unconstrained LLM during a 3-week course project. Results show that while the SG- and PR tutors led to similar task performance and prompting patterns during guided use, they differ in learning outcomes and later LLM-use. SG-students, relative to PR-student, achieved higher learning gains in later sessions, and were more likely to adopt understanding-driven prompting strategies, which are predictive of higher understanding, when using an unconstrained LLM. Although learners perceived the SG tutor as less efficient, the findings suggest that Socratic guidance supports the development of students' capacity to learn with LLMs over time, highlighting its importance for LLM tutor design.

</details>


### [462] [When Aggregate Alignment Misleads: Auditing Policy Repair Without Per-State Expert Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03386)
*Peiying Zhu,Sidi Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An LLM-based agentic policy editor, using only region-level diagnostic feedback (no expert action labels, no benchmark code or rewards), can repair a hotel pricing policy to near-benchmark performance (RevPAR 108.47 vs. 108.75). The key finding is that evaluation should focus on closed-loop diagnostic reliability rather than behavioral distance alone, as alternative methods (tree editor) achieve better alignment but lower revenue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for agentic policy editing rely on per-state expert action labels, which are often unavailable. The authors study whether effective policy repair is possible using only region-level diagnostic feedback, and how to properly evaluate such edits.

Method: A multi-restart LLM editor operates in a hotel-pricing simulator, receiving only region-level summaries comparing its price distribution to a benchmark across time, inventory, and market regions. It cannot access benchmark actions, code, rewards, or held-out outcomes, and can only propose edits to a target-action table.

Result: The LLM editor achieves RevPAR 108.47 (95% CI 107.61-109.34), close to benchmark's 108.75 (95% CI 107.81-109.68), with a paired gap of -0.276 (95% CI [-0.692, 0.146]). It also reduces episode composition distance from 1.153 to 0.609. Non-semantic baselines with up to 2,500 evaluations fall 8.77-14.57 RevPAR short. A shuffled-diagnostic control falls to 94.30. A tree editor achieves better alignment but lower revenue (98.91).

Conclusion: Agentic policy repair should be evaluated by whether diagnostic feedback becomes a reliable closed-loop outcome signal, not by a single behavioral distance metric. The match between LLM and benchmark is genuine but partial.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems are increasingly used to edit, refine, and repair decision policies, but evaluating these edits is difficult when per-state expert action labels are unavailable. We study this problem in a hotel-pricing simulator where an agentic policy editor receives only region-level diagnostic feedback: summaries of how its price distribution differs from a benchmark policy across time, inventory, and market regions. The editor cannot observe benchmark actions, benchmark source code, reward numbers, or held-out outcomes, and may only propose constrained edits to a target-action table. On 5,000 held-out episodes, a multi-restart LLM editor reaches RevPAR 108.47 (95% CI 107.61 - 109.34), close to the benchmark policy's 108.75 (107.81 - 109.68), with paired gap (LLM minus benchmark) -0.276 and 95% CI [-0.692, 0.146]. A cheap diagnostic projection already recovers much of the revenue (107.90), so the LLM editor's distinctive gain is not raw revenue lift alone: it also reduces episode composition distance from 1.153 to 0.609. This is the strongest non-benchmark repair result. This profile is not explained by restart search alone: non-semantic proposers with up to 2,500 evaluations fall 8.77 - 14.57 RevPAR points short. Nor is it explained by plausible prompt format: a shuffled-diagnostic control breaks region-error correspondence and falls to RevPAR 94.30. The match is genuine but partial. A tree editor achieves stronger pooled alignment, 0.214 versus 0.266, and stronger reference-state D1, 0.328 versus 1.197, yet revenue falls to 98.91. These results show that agentic policy repair should be evaluated by whether diagnostic feedback becomes reliable closed-loop outcome, not by a single behavioral distance.

</details>


### [463] [From Mobile Data to Business Insights: An End-to-End Analytics Framework for Large-Scale Urban Mobility Analysis and Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03394)
*Thiago Andrade,Shazia Tabassum,Miguel E. P. Silva,Ricardo Dinis,Joao Gama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper develops a data platform integrating mobile location data, Google BigQuery, Vertex AI, and Power BI to analyze urban mobility for tourism, transportation, and retail applications, achieving fine-grained behavioral insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To harness real-time mobile location data for solving diverse urban challenges (tourism, parking, bus routing, resource allocation) and supporting commercial strategic decisions like location-based services and behavioral profiling.

Method: Built a modular data platform from scratch including use case formulation, architectural design, ETL pipelines, data anonymization, Google BigQuery/Vertex AI for processing/ML, and Power BI for interactive visualization.

Result: The framework successfully performs mobility profiling, frequent trajectory mining, area of influence analysis, traffic anomaly detection, and origin-destination pattern analysis at fine spatiotemporal resolutions.

Conclusion: The platform provides actionable insights for both urban planning and strategic business decision-making by capturing user mobility dynamics effectively.

Abstract: Real time location data derived from mobile applications is a powerful tool for addressing various urban challenges, including tourism planning, parking management, bus route optimization, and resource allocation. Besides, it offers invaluable insights for shaping strategic decisions in commercial domains such as location based services, market share analysis, and behavioral profiling. In this expansive study, we aim to address all of the aforementioned challenges by investigating the behaviors and patterns of smartphone users within urban environments, particularly in the domains of tourism, transportation, and retail. Our approach encompasses the development of a sophisticated data platform from inception to implementation, which includes the formulation of use cases, architectural design, and implementation of modules. We employ state of the art techniques and technologies, including data anonymization, ETL pipelines, and utilizing Google BigQuery and Vertex AI for data processing and machine learning model development. A modular architecture based on reusable analytical building blocks was developed to generate data products that support multiple stakeholder driven use cases. Additionally, we apply interactive data visualization techniques via Power BI to facilitate the effective interpretation of analytical findings by stakeholders. The developed models address a wide range of mobility analytics tasks, including mobility profiling, frequent trajectory mining, area of influence analysis, traffic anomaly detection, and origin destination pattern analysis. The results demonstrate the framework's ability to capture user mobility dynamics at fine spatial and temporal resolutions, providing actionable insights for urban planning and strategic business decision making.

</details>


### [464] [Efficient bias mitigation in T2I diffusion models using Concept Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03397)
*Mansi,Avinash Kori,Francesco Leofante*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CO-ALIGN is a bias mitigation method for text-to-image diffusion models that aligns concept ontologies within the text encoder and denoiser, achieving 30% fairness improvement, better image quality (ΔFID=11.4), and 88% reduction in semantically incoherent outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bias mitigation techniques in text-to-image diffusion models either intervene only at the text encoder or provide inference-time guidance, which often leads to semantically incoherent outputs.

Method: CO-ALIGN (Concept Ontology Alignment) operates on the model's internal concept ontology by aligning concepts across the text encoder and denoiser, evaluated across three paradigms: text-encoder alignment, denoiser alignment, and joint text-denoiser ontology alignment.

Result: 30% improvement in fairness, ΔFID=11.4 in image quality, 2.8% improvement in image fidelity, and 88% reduction in semantically incoherent outputs. Additionally improves concept unlearning robustness.

Conclusion: Concept-graph alignment effectively reduces bias while preserving generative integrity, and better-aligned internal ontologies also enhance downstream tasks like concept unlearning.

Abstract: Text-to-Image diffusion models often propagate harmful bias inherited from the training data. Existing bias mitigation techniques typically intervene only at the text encoder or provide inference-time guidance, often leading to generations that collapse into semantically incoherent outputs. To address these limitations, we introduce CO-ALIGN (Concept Ontology Alignment), a novel bias mitigation approach based on concept-graph alignment that operates on the model's internal concept ontology. By aligning concepts within the text encoder and denoiser, CO-ALIGN achieves substantial bias reduction while preserving generative integrity. We demonstrate the effectiveness of concept-graph alignment across three paradigms: text-encoders, denoisers and joint text-denoiser ontology alignment. CO-ALIGN outperforms the state of the art, improving fairness by $30\%$, $ΔFID=11.4$ in image quality, $2.8\%$ in image fidelity, all while reducing semantically incoherent outputs by $88\%$. Beyond bias mitigation, we show that CO-ALIGN benefits other downstream tasks as well. In particular, our experiments demonstrate that better-aligned internal ontologies enhance concept unlearning robustness across multiple unlearning techniques.

</details>


### [465] [Personalized Causal Recourse: A Human-In-The-Loop Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03425)
*Denise Tampieri,Giovanni De Toni,Paolo Giudici*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a human-in-the-loop framework that uses Bayesian inference and iterative user queries to approximate individual Structural Causal Models, enabling personalized algorithmic recourse that accounts for user-specific causal dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional recourse methods rely on closest counterfactual explanations or assume known causal structures, overlooking individual user contexts and specific feature interactions.

Method: A human-in-the-loop framework that iteratively approximates the user's structural causal model via interactive Bayesian queries before generating recourse recommendations.

Result: Simulations across linear and non-linear causal models show promising results, but challenges remain in capturing complex non-linear structures and robust noise distribution modeling.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables personalized, plausible, and cost-effective recourse aligned with user-specific causal dependencies, though further work is needed for non-linear structures.

Abstract: Algorithmic recourse addresses the challenge of providing tailored recommendations to users affected by unfavorable machine learning decisions, in potentially high-stakes scenarios. Traditional approaches to recourse often rely on the closest counterfactual explanations or assume a priori knowledge of a user's causal structure, resulting in interventions that overlook individual contexts and specific feature interactions. To overcome these limitations, we study a human-in-the-loop framework that iteratively approximates the user's structural causal model through interactive queries via Bayesian inference before producing recourse recommendations. This framework exploits humans' feedback to improve the identification of causal effects, allowing personalized recourse that is plausible, cost-effective, and aligned with the actual causal dependencies of each user. As a proof of concept, we evaluate this framework through simulated human responses. Our simulations across linear and non-linear causal models show promising results, though challenges remain in capturing complex, non-linear structures, emphasizing the importance of accurate approximations and robust noise distribution modeling.

</details>


### [466] [Demonstrating Generalization Failures via Mixtures of Conditional Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03478)
*Jou Barzdukas,Jack Peck,Julian Schulz,Paulius Rauba,Steven Basart,Lennie Wells*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to construct language models that exhibit controllable generalization failures under RL post-training by using SFT on mixtures of conditional policies, demonstrating that RL selects for high-reward policies which can actively degrade performance on out-of-distribution tasks, even when tasks are identical except for trigger strings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand generalization failures in frontier language models caused by distribution shifts between training and deployment, by constructing clean, simplified demonstrations of such failures.

Method: Using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on a dataset of mixed transcripts from a collection of 'conditional policies' to create a model that approximates a mixture of conditional policies, then applying RL training on a specific task distribution to observe selective amplification of high-reward policies.

Result: RL training on one distribution actively degrades performance on the other distribution to zero, even though the underlying task is identical (only trigger strings differ). The construction also demonstrates two novel generalization failure modes related to task coverage and temporal context distribution shifts.

Conclusion: The constructed 'model organisms' serve as existence proofs that training success and generalization can diverge in structured ways, and are useful for alignment stress-testing and generalization science, though they may not closely resemble natural generalization failures.

Abstract: Post-training of frontier language models is conducted on curated task suites, and inevitably leaves a distribution shift between training and deployment environments. This exposes developers to generalization failures, which are relatively poorly understood. To better understand such generalization failures, we believe the community should construct clean demonstrations under simplified conditions. To facilitate this, we propose a simple and flexible way to construct language models which fail to generalize in controllable ways when subsequently trained with Reinforcement Learning (RL) on a given distribution of training tasks. Our construction uses Supervised Fine-Tuning on a dataset of a mixture of transcripts corresponding to a collection of 'conditional policies', which can each independently be assigned certain behaviors on each different task distribution, to obtain a model that is then well approximated as a 'mixture of conditional policies.' We observe that RL training then selects for policies that obtain the highest reward on the training distribution. This can produce striking behaviors: in a controlled setting with two distributions containing identical questions prepended with two different 'trigger strings', RL training on either distribution actively degrades performance on the other to zero, even though the underlying task is identical. We also use our construction to illustrate two novel ways in which generalization may fail in future language models, corresponding to distribution shifts of task coverage and temporal context respectively. While our construction is deliberately simple and may not closely resemble 'natural' generalization failures, the resulting 'model organisms' are of interest for alignment stress-testing and generalization science, and can be used as existence proofs that training success and generalization can come apart in structured ways.

</details>


### [467] [MentalThink: Shaping Thoughts in Mental SVG World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03530)
*Kangheng Lin,Jisheng Yin,Dingming Li,En Yu,Yana Wei,Han Zhou,Liang Zhao,Hongyu Zhou,Hongbo Peng,Jianjian Sun,Zheng Ge,Xiangyu Zhang,Daxin Jiang,Jingyu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MentalThink introduces a visual-symbolic reasoning paradigm where Multimodal LLMs generate, render, and interpret SVG code as intermediate visual representations, enabling iterative spatial reasoning through a two-stage SFT + RL training framework, achieving state-of-the-art results on spatial benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of MLLMs in performing dynamic spatial reasoning and mental imagery-like tasks by providing an explicit, verifiable visual workspace using executable vector graphics.

Method: A think-with-SVG pipeline: (1) model generates SVG code as intermediate visual representation, (2) deterministic rendering creates actual visuals, (3) model inspects and reasons over rendered output. Trained via two-stage framework: Supervised Fine-Tuning for SVG syntax, then multi-turn Reinforcement Learning for iterative refinement.

Result: 55.1% on VSIBench, 76.0% on MindCube, outperforming prior approaches on spatial understanding and reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Executable vector graphics (SVG) serve as an effective verifiable visual workspace for MLLMs to perform dynamic perspective taking, visual reflection, and compositional scene construction, mimicking human mental imagery.

Abstract: We introduce MentalThink, a visual-symbolic reasoning paradigm that equips Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) with an executable mechanism for "mental" visualization. The core of MentalThink is a think-with-SVG pipeline, where the model learns to generate, render, and interpret scalable vector graphics (SVG) code as an intermediate visual representation for multi-turn reasoning. By creating structured vector sketches, the model can externalize spatial hypotheses, inspect them through deterministic rendering, and reason within a constrained geometric space, effectively mimicking the human process of mental imagery. We instantiate this paradigm through a two-stage training framework, combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for SVG syntactic alignment with multi-turn Reinforcement Learning (RL) to encourage iterative inspection, revision, and refinement of intermediate visual hypotheses. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that MentalThink achieves superior performance on spatial understanding and reasoning benchmarks (e.g., 55.1% on VSIBench, 76.0% on MindCube), showing that executable vector graphics provide a verifiable visual workspace for dynamic perspective taking, visual reflection, and compositional scene construction.

</details>


### [468] [Applying Answer Set Programming with Fuzzy Membership Functions: a Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03550)
*Luca Ferragina,Ilenia Galati,Lorena Gullone,Francesco Scarcello*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents a fuzzy-logic-based qualitative extension of Answer Set Programming that bridges numerical data with qualitative reasoning, using linguistic labels and learning-based membership functions to enable robust reasoning under vagueness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human reasoning uses vague, context-dependent qualitative concepts (e.g., "high," "cheap") rooted in numerical data. Traditional ASP relies on rigid thresholds, which fails to capture this vagueness. The authors aim to bridge numerical information and qualitative reasoning without brittleness.

Method: A fuzzy-logic-based qualitative extension of ASP is introduced, featuring learning-based membership functions and semantically enriched predicates. This framework integrates numerically grounded inputs (e.g., ML model outputs) with symbolic reasoning over qualitative labels in a unified declarative setting.

Result: Through a representative use case, the paper demonstrates how the framework successfully combines numerically grounded inputs with symbolic reasoning, and supports the integration of expert knowledge, contextual factors, and subjective interpretations.

Conclusion: The proposed fuzzy-logic-based ASP extension provides a principled framework that avoids rigid thresholds, supports robust reasoning under vagueness, and effectively bridges the gap between numerical data and qualitative human-like reasoning.

Abstract: Human reasoning often operates through qualitative concepts expressed by linguistic labels such as high, low, expensive, or cheap, whose interpretation depends on context and is usually vague, despite being rooted in numerical data. This paper explores a novel fuzzy-logic-based qualitative extension of Answer Set Programming (ASP) to bridge numerical information and qualitative reasoning. The underlying language, formally introduced in a separate work, provides a principled framework that avoids rigid thresholds and supports robust reasoning under vagueness. Focusing on a representative use case, we illustrate how the framework integrates numerically grounded inputs (such as outputs of machine learning models) with symbolic reasoning over qualitative labels. Key features, including learning-based membership functions and semantically enriched predicates, enable the combination of expert knowledge, contextual factors, and subjective interpretations within a unified declarative setting.

</details>


### [469] [How to Avoid Debate: Scalable AI Safety via Doubly-Efficient Interactive Proofs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03561)
*Liyan Chen,Yael Tauman Kalai,Zoe Xi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes single-prover interactive proofs (eliminating debate) for AI safety verification, showing that verification works without two competing AI models when the computation is robust to noise or uses low-degree polynomial oracles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current debate-based verification assumes two equally capable AI models with at least one truthful, which is unrealistic. The authors seek to avoid debate entirely by exploring single-prover interactive proofs for AI safety.

Method: The authors present doubly-efficient single-prover interactive proofs and arguments for oracle-aided computations, under two settings: (1) robust computations (output insensitive to small fractions of incorrect oracle answers), and (2) low-degree polynomial oracles.

Result: Single-prover interactive verification works for oracle-aided computations under structured or noise-tolerant oracle access, suggesting debate may not be necessary for AI safety verification.

Conclusion: Interactive verification is possible without debate under certain conditions (robust computation or low-degree polynomial oracles), opening a new direction for AI safety verification.

Abstract: As AI models continue to develop powerful capabilities, it becomes critical that we are able to verify that their output is aligned with our intentions. A recent line of work focuses on verification via debate, a model of interactive proofs where two competing powerful provers, or AI models, debate each other to convince a weak verifier, or a human, of the correctness of their claim. However, debate assumes that the two AI models possess equal abilities and that one of them is truthful, which may not be realistic.
  In this work, we show \emph{how to avoid debate}: we initiate the study of \emph{single-prover} interactive proofs for AI safety. Prior results in single-prover interactive proofs do not immediately carry over to the AI safety setting: for example, they do not work when the computation has access to an oracle, such as to human judgment or an external database such as the web. We present doubly-efficient single-prover interactive proofs and arguments for oracle-aided computations (also known as relativizing proofs), in the settings where (1) the computation is robust, in the sense that the output does not change if at most a small fraction of the answers to oracle queries are incorrect, or (2) the oracle is a low-degree polynomial. These results suggest that interactive verification is possible even without debate, under structured or noise-tolerant oracle access.

</details>


### [470] [The Role of Rigor in Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03634)
*Timothy Nguyen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper analyzes AI through a three-part framework of rigor (conceptual, epistemic, operational), arguing that modern deep learning's success stems from prioritizing operational rigor over other forms, which explains both its rapid advances and persistent scientific uncertainties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI has achieved remarkable capabilities despite lacking the theoretical foundations typical of mature sciences. This tension between performance-driven "alchemical" experimentation and traditional scientific rigor motivates a systematic analysis of rigor in AI.

Method: The authors introduce a three-part framework—conceptual rigor (clarifying concepts), epistemic rigor (establishing scientific understanding), and operational rigor (ensuring reliable performance)—and apply it to analyze intelligence concepts, deep learning's empirical approach, benchmark limitations, and theory development obstacles.

Result: The distinctive trajectory of AI arises from how forms of rigor interact across paradigms, leading to the primacy of operational rigor in modern deep learning. This explains both rapid advances and persistent uncertainties.

Conclusion: The framework helps clarify the challenges involved in transforming AI into a mature science and reliable technology, highlighting the need for better integration across all three forms of rigor.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has achieved extraordinary capabilities despite lacking many of the conceptual and scientific foundations associated with mature disciplines. Unlike traditional sciences, where reliable technology typically emerges from theoretical understanding, modern AI has progressed largely through performance-driven iteration and "alchemical" experimentation. This tension motivates a systematic analysis of AI through the lens of rigor. We introduce a three-part framework consisting of conceptual rigor (clarifying foundational concepts), epistemic rigor (establishing scientific understanding), and operational rigor (ensuring reliable performance and deployment). Using this framework, we analyze competing conceptions of intelligence and understanding, the strengths and limitations of the empirical approach to deep learning, the power and pitfalls of benchmarks, and the obstacles to theory development posed by modern AI systems. We argue that the distinctive trajectory of AI arises from how forms of rigor interact across paradigms, resulting in the primacy of operational rigor in modern deep learning. This perspective helps explain both AI's rapid advances and its persistent uncertainties, while clarifying the challenges involved in transforming AI into a mature science and reliable technology.

</details>


### [471] [Robust Feasible Route Construction through Collaborative Partition Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03694)
*Oguzhan Karaahmetoglu,Hyong Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoRC is a routing framework that enables independently solved CVRP subproblems to exchange customers and vehicles, achieving feasible solutions for up to 200,000 customers where partition-based and end-to-end methods fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale CVRPs are solved via partitioning, but independent subproblem solutions may leave demand unserved even if sufficient fleet resources exist globally.

Method: Collaborative Routing Constructors (CoRC) allows subproblems to exchange customers and vehicles during optimization, rather than relying on fixed partitions or post-hoc global re-optimization.

Result: CoRC consistently constructs feasible solutions on AGS benchmarks and synthetic instances up to 200,000 customers, outperforming partition-based methods and end-to-end frameworks under the same budget.

Conclusion: Collaboration between routing subproblems offers a robust and scalable approach for feasible large-scale route construction.

Abstract: Large-scale Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problems (CVRPs) are commonly solved by partitioning customers into smaller routing problems that can be optimized independently. While this substantially reduces computational complexity, independently constructed routing solutions may leave some customer demand unserved even when sufficient resources exist elsewhere in the fleet. We present Collaborative Routing Constructors (CoRC), a routing framework that enables independently solved subproblems to exchange customers and vehicles during optimization rather than relying solely on a fixed partition or a subsequent global re-optimization stage. Computational experiments on AGS benchmark instances and synthetic instances containing up to 200,000 customers compare CoRC against independent routing, post-routing global re-optimization, and state-of-the-art, end-to-end routing frameworks. Across all evaluated partitioning strategies, CoRC consistently constructs feasible routing solutions where competing partition-based methods do not. Furthermore, it remains effective on problem instances for which the evaluated end-to-end routing frameworks did not produce solutions under the same computational budget. These results demonstrate that collaboration between routing subproblems provides a robust and scalable approach for feasible large-scale route construction.

</details>


### [472] [Agent Reinforcement Learning via Pivotal-Aware Self-Feedback Retry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03702)
*Weiyang Guo,Zesheng Shi,Longhui Zhang,Zeen Zhu,Min Zhang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PivoARL is a self-feedback retry framework for LLM agents that identifies pivotal erroneous turns, performs local retry from that state (reusing correct prefixes), and uses pivotal-aware credit assignment to improve decision-making while reducing interaction costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents struggle to effectively leverage failed trajectories: full retries incur high interaction costs, while experience retrieval dilutes critical experience signals.

Method: (1) Identifies the pivotal erroneous turn via structured reflection, (2) performs local retry only from the pivotal state (reusing correct prefix), (3) employs pivotal-aware credit assignment rewarding correct prefixes while isolating erroneous suffixes, and (4) optimizes reflection quality through implicit reflection returns.

Result: Significant improvements on Pass@2/3 across all tasks (~11.5% over MetaRL), consistent Pass@1 improvement on 80%+ tasks. On Minesweeper, >45% improvement over GiGPO and ~42% reduction in interaction turns vs full-retry methods.

Conclusion: PivoARL effectively exploits failed trajectories by concentrating useful experience signals near error boundaries, reducing redundant interactions while improving decision-making performance across diverse agent tasks and QA benchmarks.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have shown strong decision-making capabilities in long-horizon interactive tasks, yet they still struggle to effectively leverage failed trajectories: full retries incur high interaction costs, while experience retrieval tends to dilute critical experience signals. To address this, we propose PivoARL, a self-feedback retry framework for experience exploitation in LLM agents. PivoARL identifies the pivotal erroneous turn through structured reflection and performs local retry only from the corresponding pivotal state, thereby reusing the correct prefix and reducing redundant interactions. From an information-gain perspective, we further show that pivotal retry concentrates useful experience signals near the error boundary, mitigating the signal dilution caused by state-agnostic experience utilization. Based on this insight, we design a pivotal-aware credit assignment mechanism that rewards correct prefixes while isolating erroneous suffixes, and optimize reflection quality through implicit reflection returns. We conduct a systematic evaluation on 4 agent tasks and 7 search-based QA benchmarks. Results show that PivoARL achieves significant improvements on Pass@2/3 across all tasks, with an average gain of about 11.5\% over MetaRL. Moreover, benefiting from contrastive preference signals induced by pivotal turns, PivoARL also consistently improves Pass@1 on over 80\% of the tasks. On Minesweeper environment, PivoARL improves over GiGPO by more than 45\% and reduces interaction turns by about 42\% on average compared with full-retry methods. Code is available at https://github.com/yuki-younai/PivoARL.

</details>


### [473] [Explainable Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Traffic Signal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03703)
*Dickens Kwesiga,Nishu Choudhary,Angshuman Guin,Michael Hunter*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an explainable, entity-centric RL framework for adaptive traffic signal control that uses dual-stage attention networks to preserve intersection geometry, provides visual/analytical interpretability via affinity matrices, enforces safety via action masking in PPO, and outperforms baselines in simulation while aligning with traffic engineering principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep RL models for traffic signal control are opaque and black-box, hindering acceptance by transportation agencies, regulatory compliance, operational trust, troubleshooting, and fine-tuning.

Method: An entity-centric RL architecture that decomposes observations into lane entities and phase configurations; uses dual-stage attention (multi-head cross-attention + self-attention) to extract relational dependencies and produce an interpretable affinity matrix; integrates deterministic action-masking into PPO to enforce safety constraints.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in delay minimization in microscopic simulation; attention weights align precisely with established traffic engineering principles.

Conclusion: The framework offers an auditable, trust-enabling, and deployable architecture for next-generation adaptive traffic control systems, bridging high-performance RL with human-comprehensible interpretability.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for adaptive traffic signal control. However, in safety-critical infrastructure like traffic control, the opaque, black-box nature of deep RL models poses challenges for transportation agency acceptance, regulatory compliance, operational trust, troubleshooting, and fine-tuning. To bridge this gap between high-performance optimization and human-comprehensible interpretability, this effort introduces a novel, explainable entity centric RL framework for safe and transparent traffic signal control. Rather than processing traffic states through monolithic, flat vectors, the proposed architecture disaggregates real-time intersection observations into distinct, high-dimensional lane entities and phase temporal configurations to inherently preserve the structural topology and geometric configurations of the intersection. Relational dependencies and inter-lane conflicts are dynamically extracted via a dual-stage attention network featuring sequential multi-head cross-attention and self-attention blocks. This design yields a real time affinity matrix that quantifies the direct influence of signal phases on specific approach volumes and queues, providing full visual and analytical interpretability. To ensure strict operational reliability, a deterministic action-masking interface is integrated directly into the Proximal Policy Optimization pipeline, explicitly blocking invalid phase transitions to guarantee absolute compliance with established signal timing and safety constraints. Evaluated in a microscopic simulation environment, outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in delay minimization. More importantly, the emergent attention weights align precisely with established traffic engineering principles, offering an auditable, trust-enabling, and deployable architecture for next-generation adaptive traffic control systems.

</details>


### [474] [Can Conversational Temporal Dynamics Improve Depression Detection in Dyads? A Preliminary Investigation in Multi-Modality Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03744)
*Hanie Kang,Huang-Cheng Chou,Sudarsana Reddy Kadiri,Shrikanth Narayanan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes using dyadic turn-pair timing (conversational pauses/speaker-switch patterns) as a lightweight feature for automatic depression detection, showing it outperforms large acoustic and language models alone and, when fused, achieves strong results while assigning zero weight to acoustic features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interactional timing between clinician and participant in depression detection remains under-explored compared to semantic and acoustic features. The authors aim to investigate whether conversational temporal dynamics can serve as a useful modality.

Method: A compact 24-dimensional timing module capturing dyadic turn-pair timing is fused with self-supervised encoders (WavLM-large for acoustics, RoBERTa-large for language) using convex-weighted late fusion, evaluated on the DAIC-WOZ dataset.

Result: The timing module achieves the highest single-modality performance on the development set. Fused model reaches 0.804 macro-F1 (dev) and 0.669 macro-F1 (test). Fusion assigns zero weight to acoustics, highlighting timing's importance.

Conclusion: Conversational timing is a lightweight, interpretable, and effective complement for dyadic depression screening, potentially reducing dependency on heavy acoustic models.

Abstract: Automatic depression detection from clinical interviews typically models the semantic content and acoustic characteristics of participant speech. However, the interactional timing between the clinician and participant remains comparatively under-modeled. We investigate conversational temporal dynamics, specifically dyadic turn-pair timing, as a primary modality fused with self-supervised encoders. Evaluated on the DAIC-WOZ dataset, we compare a compact 24-dimensional timing module against frozen WavLM-large and RoBERTa-large baseline detectors. This temporal module achieves the highest single-modality performance on the development set. Furthermore, a convex-weighted late fusion strategy improves overall performance to 0.804 and 0.669 macro-F1 on the development and test sets, respectively. The learned fusion effectively assigns zero weight to acoustics, demonstrating that conversational timing serves as a lightweight, interpretable complement for dyadic depression screening.

</details>


### [475] [Bridging Interleaved Multi-Modal Reasoning as a Unified Decision Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03748)
*Zican Hu,Xuyang Hu,Yiming Liu,Zuwei Long,Wei Liu,Yunzhuo Hao,Jiawei Gu,Linjie Li,Yu Cheng,Zhenhong Sun,Weibo Gu,Xing Sun,Zhi Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BRAID frames multi-turn text-image-text reasoning as a unified MDP, enabling joint RL optimization of both text and image generation with a shared trajectory-level advantage and VLM-based dense feedback for better credit assignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL approaches for UMMs only optimize text steps while treating image generation via supervised surrogates, preventing policy gradients from flowing through full interleaved trajectories.

Method: BRAID casts multi-turn reasoning as a unified MDP, computes shared trajectory-level advantages, propagates them into both text tokens and image denoising paths via modality-native policy gradients, and uses a VLM judge to score intermediate images for dense turn-level feedback.

Result: Outperforms various baselines on spatial reasoning and visual perception benchmarks.

Conclusion: A unified MDP formulation with vision-thinking guidance is essential for effective multi-modal reasoning.

Abstract: Unified multi-modal models (UMMs) have shown promising interleaved text-image reasoning capabilities, yet effectively optimizing such multi-turn generation via reinforcement learning (RL) remains an open challenge. Existing approaches apply RL exclusively to text steps, relegating image generation to supervised surrogates, preventing policy gradients from propagating through the full interleaved trajectory across heterogeneous modalities. This leaves the potential of RL for UMMs largely untapped. In the paper, we introduce \textbf{BRAID} (\textbf{B}ridging inte\textbf{R}le\textbf{A}ved mult\textbf{I}-modal reasoning as a unified \textbf{D}ecision process), a simple framework that casts multi-turn text-image-text reasoning as a unified Markov decision process (MDP), enabling joint optimization of textual and visual generation via a single, principled RL objective. BRAID computes a shared trajectory-level advantage and propagates it coherently into both text tokens and image denoising paths, each optimized through its modality-native policy gradient mechanism. To further address long-horizon credit assignment, BRAID employs a vision-language model (VLM) judge that scores each intermediate image on its reasoning utility, supplying dense turn-level feedback to sharpen learning at critical visual branches. Experiments on spatial reasoning and visual perception benchmarks show that BRAID consistently outperforms various baselines, confirming that a unified MDP formulation with vision-thinking guidance is essential for effective multi-modal reasoning.

</details>


### [476] [Folding, Reasoning, and Scaling with Open-source Drug Discovery Engine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03787)
*Aureka AI OpenDDE project*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenDDE is an open-source biomolecular foundation model that uses co-folding as a shared structural reasoning layer for drug discovery, achieving high accuracy while enabling structure prediction, design, affinity estimation, and optimization, with released code and benchmarks to democratize access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately modeling biomolecular interactions is a central bottleneck in biology and therapeutic discovery, and existing structure prediction approaches treat it as an isolated endpoint rather than a scalable, reusable reasoning layer.

Method: OpenDDE integrates advances in all-atom architecture, atomic latent reasoning, inference optimization, and large-scale data processing to achieve co-folding accuracy, and identifies two scaling-law directions for continued improvement.
