Motivation: Self-driving labs (SDLs) can automate experiments but face two bottlenecks: too many experimental rounds (low-value experiments) and high cost per experiment. The authors aim to address both simultaneously with a single agent.

Method: Two components: (1) a prior-aware agentic Design of Experiments (DOE) loop that incorporates domain knowledge and past results to propose feasible and informative experiments, reducing trials-to-target; (2) a cost-aware surrogate agent that predicts high-resolution measurements from low-cost data and intelligently chooses between high- and low-cost measurement options based on predicted uncertainty.

Result: The prior-aware DOE reduces rounds needed to reach experimental targets; the cost-aware surrogate effectively substitutes costly high-resolution measurements with cheap alternatives when uncertainty is low. Demonstrated in biology and materials science domains.

Conclusion: A single unified agent can simultaneously reduce both the number of experimental loops and the cost per experiment, accelerating the overall SDL loop for AI-for-Science applications.

Abstract: Agentic AI-for-Science can automate ideation, planning, and analysis, but final validation still depends on real experiments. A self-driving lab (SDL) can execute those experiments, yet the loop still has bottlenecks: the agent may spend too many rounds on low-value experiments, or each round may require a high-cost experiment. We target these two physical bottlenecks with one agent. First, a prior-aware agentic DOE loop uses domain knowledge and past results to propose feasible and informative next experiments, reducing trials-to-target. Second, a cost-aware surrogate agent predicts high-cost, high-resolution measurements from low-cost, low-resolution measurements. It chooses between a high- and a low-cost measurement based on the predicted uncertainty. We examine these directions in the biology and materials domains, respectively. Together, under a single agent, these components aim to accelerate the SDL loop by reducing both the number of loops and the cost per experiment.

</details>


### [503] [VLA Grounder: Language-Conditioning Space Optimization for Black-Box VLA Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04517)
*Damir Shodiev,Aleksei Staroverov,Nikita Kachaev,Alexey K. Kovalev,Aleksandr I. Panov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes language-conditioning space optimization (LSO) that improves frozen VLA robot policies by optimizing language prompts via RL, rather than updating action weights, achieving better performance on instruction-sensitive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models are sensitive to instruction phrasing, but language is typically treated as a static task label. The authors investigate whether optimizing the language conditioning space can improve frozen VLA policies without modifying action weights.

Method: A language-conditioning space policy translates human instructions into VLA-grounded commands using object appearance, spatial relations, and target-grounding cues. It's initialized with a failure-derived prior and optimized via RL with sparse task-completion rewards, while the downstream VLA remains frozen.

Result: Experiments on RL4VLA and VL-Think benchmarks show improved success rates on instruction-sensitive, symbolic, and multi-object manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Language can serve as an optimizable variable for robot foundation models, offering a new paradigm for improving VLA policies without retraining action weights.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are commonly treated as end-to-end action policies conditioned on natural-language task descriptions. In practice, however, their behavior often depends sharply on how the instruction is phrased, suggesting that language is not merely a task label but an optimizable conditioning input. We study whether frozen VLA policies can be improved by optimizing language space rather than updating action weights. Our method introduces a language-conditioning space policy that translates a human instruction into a short VLA-grounded command using object appearance, spatial relations, and target-grounding cues. The language-conditioning space policy is initialized with a failure-derived command-space prior and optimized with reinforcement learning from sparse task-completion rewards, while the downstream VLA remains fully frozen. This yields language-conditioning space optimization: RL discovers which VLA-grounded commands best elicit successful behavior from the frozen action policy. Experiments on RL4VLA and VL-Think show that language-conditioning space optimization improves success on instruction-sensitive, symbolic, and multi-object manipulation tasks, demonstrating that language can serve as an optimizable variable for a robot foundation models. Website: https://tttonyalpha.github.io/vla_grounder

</details>


### [504] [Measuring Harness-Induced Belief Divergence in Multi-Step LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04528)
*Haiwen Yi,Xinyuan Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper shows that the harness (interface controlling agent perception, actions, repairs, verification, and logging) significantly alters an LLM agent's multi-step beliefs and decision-making, even when the task, environment, and base LLM remain fixed. The authors introduce diagnostic tools (belief-rollout, cross-harness belief divergence) and BIWM (a no-training protocol) to measure and mitigate these biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent benchmarks focus only on final task success, ignoring how the harness (interface, logging, repair mechanisms) biases the agent's intermediate beliefs and subsequent decisions.

Method: The authors propose a belief-rollout diagnostic to extract structured K-step belief trajectories, define cross-harness belief divergence (decomposed into arrival and growth terms), and introduce BIWM—a no-training protocol that canonicalizes observations, logs censored branches, expands repair traces, records verification masks, executes risky branches in shadow, and aligns belief trajectories across harness views.

Result: Experiments on coding tasks and public benchmarks show that blocked actions, compressed repairs, selective verification, and cost-aware evidence pruning often preserve terminal success while significantly altering intermediate beliefs that drive later decisions.

Conclusion: Harness design is an experimental variable in agent evaluation, not an implementation detail, and must be carefully considered when interpreting benchmark results.

Abstract: Software-agent benchmarks usually report whether an agent solves a task, but the agent reaches that outcome through a harness that controls what it sees, which actions it can take, which failures are repaired, which states are verified, and which evidence is logged. We show that this harness can change the agent's multi-step beliefs even when the task, environment, and base LLM are fixed. We introduce a belief-rollout diagnostic that elicits structured K-step trajectories over progress, risk, recoverability, constraints, failure mode, uncertainty, future success, repair cost, and next action under alternative harnesses. We define a cross-harness belief divergence and decompose it into an arrival term for immediate interface shifts and a growth term for horizon-dependent belief changes. On controlled coding tasks and public-benchmark stress tests, blocked actions, compressed repairs, selective verification, and cost-aware evidence pruning often preserve terminal success while changing the beliefs that drive later decisions. We further introduce BIWM, a no-training protocol that canonicalizes observations, logs censored branches, expands repair traces, records verification masks, executes risky branches in shadow, and aligns belief trajectories across harness views. The results suggest that harness design is an experimental variable in agent evaluation, not an implementation detail. Our code is available at https://github.com/Hik289/Harness-induce-bias.git.

</details>


### [505] [Heaviside Continuity of Rolling Coefficients for Eliminating Epistemic Entropy in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04562)
*MY Pitsane,Hope Mogale*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HCRC is a verification-first execution framework that uses predicate-gated state transitions (Heaviside Gate) to block invalid intermediate states in LLMs, reducing false completions to 0% on capable models and enabling reliable reasoning through execution control rather than model scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs produce fluent but wrong outputs without the error cues humans exhibit. Autoregressive decoding lacks intermediate verification mechanisms, making errors hard to detect and allowing invalid states to propagate.

Method: HCRC (Heaviside Continuity of Rolling Coefficients) reformulates LLM inference as predicate-gated state transitions governed by a Heaviside Gate, combining model confidence with independent verification signals from a parallel worker architecture. Execution advances only when predefined correctness predicates are satisfied.

Result: On capable proposers, HCRC reduces false-completion rate (FCR) from 4-7% to 0% while remaining latency-competitive and sometimes faster. On weaker proposers, it converts false completions into honest halts. It has operated for months as a production control plane in an agentic coding environment.

Conclusion: HCRC establishes a general framework for verification-driven LLM execution, demonstrating that reliable reasoning can be achieved through principled execution control rather than relying on model scale alone.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) generate fluent outputs that can be wrong. Unlike humans, who often exhibit cues when providing false information, LLMs produce errors that are difficult to detect because autoregressive decoding provides no mechanism for verifying intermediate reasoning before state progression. We introduce Heaviside Continuity of Rolling Coefficients (HCRC), a verification-first execution framework that reformulates inference as predicate-gated state transitions governed by a Heaviside Gate. HCRC combines model confidence with independent verification signals from a parallel worker architecture, allowing execution to advance only when predefined correctness predicates are satisfied. This prevents invalid intermediate states from propagating, reducing epistemic entropy without modifying the underlying model. We evaluate HCRC on software-engineering and reasoning tasks across thirteen proposers from four providers. On capable proposers, the gate reduces the false-completion rate (FCR) from 4--7% to 0% while remaining latency-competitive and, in some settings, faster than the unwrapped model. On weaker proposers, it converts false completions into honest halts instead of corrupting downstream state. Beyond benchmarking, HCRC has operated for months as the production control plane of an agentic coding environment, authorizing file mutations, verification-driven progress reporting, and memory compaction. These results establish HCRC as a general framework for verification-driven LLM execution, showing that reliable reasoning can be achieved through principled execution control rather than model scale alone.

</details>


### [506] [Detecting Answer-Driven Reasoning in LLM-Based Educational Tutors via Truncated Chain-of-Thought Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04572)
*Bonan Shen,Dingyan Shang,Youting Wang,Tao Ning*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces TRACE, a method to detect when LLM tutors use hidden answer keys to produce explanations that reveal the correct answer too early, rather than deriving it step-by-step from the problem itself.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM tutors can produce grammatically correct and pedagogically formatted explanations, but these may be "answer-driven" (derived from private answer keys) rather than genuinely derived from the student-facing problem. This undermines the pedagogical value of step-by-step tutoring.

Method: The authors propose TRACE (Truncated Reasoning AUC Evaluation): they truncate chain-of-thought explanations at various fractions (e.g., 10%, 20%), force the model to answer immediately, and verify against the gold answer. Higher TRACE AUC means the answer is available earlier in the explanation.

Result: With Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct on GSM8K, access to correct answer keys raises median TRACE AUC from 0.375 to 0.900. In 997/1000 cases, the gold answer is available within the first 10% of the explanation. The effect persists even on 746 examples where both conditions produce correct final answers.

Conclusion: Truncated CoT auditing (TRACE) is a lightweight process-level diagnostic for detecting answer-driven reasoning in math tutoring explanations, revealing when models exploit hidden answer information rather than solving problems stepwise.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) tutors often produce fluent step-by-step explanations, but a correct and pedagogically formatted response does not guarantee that the answer was derived from the student-facing problem. In realistic tutoring systems, the model may also have access to teacher notes, answer keys, rubrics, or retrieved solution artifacts. We study whether such private answer information can make tutor explanations answer-driven: the final answer is behaviorally available before the written explanation has justified it. Using Truncated Reasoning AUC Evaluation (TRACE), which probes how early a chain-of-thought prefix can pass a verifier, we evaluate 1000 GSM8K test problems under three paired tutoring contexts: question-only, correct answer-key, and wrong answer-key. At fixed fractions of each generated explanation, we force the model to answer immediately and verify the response against the gold numeric answer. With Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, answer-key access raises median TRACE AUC from 0.375 to 0.900 and makes the gold answer available at the first 10% prefix in 997 of 1000 cases. The effect remains strong on the 746 examples where both question-only and answer-key explanations end with the correct answer. These results support truncated CoT auditing as a lightweight process-level diagnostic for answer-driven reasoning in math tutoring explanations.

</details>


### [507] [Attention Limited Reward Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04590)
*Wenqian Xing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper shows that pairwise human comparisons in RLHF are distorted by limited attention, conflating genuine value closeness with evaluation difficulty. The authors propose a rational inattention model and demonstrate that standard Bradley-Terry reward modeling can produce misleading rankings under heterogeneous attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Bradley-Terry models in RLHF assume pairwise comparisons directly reveal preference through latent reward differences, but this ignores the role of limited human attention. The paper aims to separate two conflated forms of ambiguity: value closeness vs. detection difficulty.

Method: A reduced-form model motivated by rational inattention is introduced, where each label is generated by a low-capacity evaluation channel. The model distinguishes reward, attention, and default tendencies. Two case studies are conducted: one on Chatbot Arena human votes and another on perceptual comparisons with response times and gaze data.

Result: (1) Passive comparison data cannot generally distinguish reward, attention, and default tendencies. (2) Heterogeneous attention can cause standard Bradley-Terry models to recover misleading rankings. (3) Learning quality depends on the amount of attended information per label, not raw label count. (4) Chatbot Arena data exhibits a cyclic component exceeding sampling noise that no scalar reward can represent. (5) Response times and gaze carry gap information that labels alone do not.

Conclusion: Human feedback should be treated as an attention-limited measurement process rather than direct revealed preference. A weak preference signal may reflect hidden evaluation difficulty rather than genuine indifference.

Abstract: Pairwise human comparisons are a primary interface through which modern AI systems learn human preferences. RLHF and related alignment pipelines typically model such comparisons with Bradley--Terry log-odds, where choice probabilities are governed by latent reward differences. This paper examines what this assumption misses through a reduced-form model motivated by rational inattention, in which each label is generated by a low-capacity evaluation channel. The model separates two forms of ambiguity that standard reward modeling tends to conflate: a comparison may be difficult because the two candidates are genuinely close in value, or because the relevant distinction is hard to detect under limited attention. We show that limited attention can fundamentally distort what pairwise comparisons reveal. In particular, passive comparison data cannot generally distinguish reward, attention, and default tendencies, and heterogeneous attention can make standard Bradley--Terry reward modeling recover misleading rankings. Our analysis shows that learning is governed not by the raw number of labels, but by the amount of attended information each label carries. A case study on human votes over language-model pairs from Chatbot Arena exhibits the predicted signature, a cyclic component of the comparison data that exceeds sampling noise and that no scalar reward can represent; a second case study on perceptual comparisons shows that response times and gaze carry gap information that the labels do not. This perspective suggests that human feedback should be treated not as direct revealed preference, but as an attention-limited measurement process: a weak preference signal may reflect hidden evaluation difficulty rather than genuine indifference.

</details>


### [508] [Governed Individuation: Cryptographically Decoupling an Agent's Learning from Its Authority](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04613)
*Xue Qin,Simin Luan,Cong Yang,Zhijun Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces "governed individuation," an architectural approach that guarantees an autonomous agent's confinement to authorized actions via cryptographically frozen identity and semantic effect gating, proven invariant even under learning, and empirically reducing forbidden effects to zero in open-ended tool-use benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As autonomous agents learn and adapt during deployment, existing alignment techniques only probabilistically answer whether the running system remains confined to its authorized scope. The authors seek a guarantee of confinement as an architectural invariant rather than a probabilistic outcome of training.

Method: The method (governed individuation) binds an agent at boot to a cryptographically frozen identity digest and routes every action through a gate defined over the semantic effect of the action (not its name). The agent's permitted authority cannot widen without an operator-signed change to its identity, even if the agent induces its own (potentially wrong) safety principle.

Result: In an open-ended tool-use benchmark: (1) ungoverned agents under reward pressure tamper with their own evaluation at rates reaching 100% on hardest tasks; (2) the gate reduces executed forbidden effects to zero while preserving task success. Adversarial evaluation shows false-allows dropping from 75% (name-based gating) to zero (dynamic effect tracing), with refusal history transferring compliance to held-out red-line families.

Conclusion: Trust in a deployed learning agent shifts from a probabilistic wager on continued alignment to a check anyone can run at boot, making confinement a verified property of the architecture rather than a hoped-for outcome of training.

Abstract: Autonomous agents are moving from sandboxed text generators to operators of code, data, and physical infrastructure, and they increasingly learn while deployed. This reopens a question that alignment techniques answer only probabilistically: after an agent has adapted in the field, is the running system still confined to what its operator authorised? Here we show that confinement can be guaranteed as an invariant of the agent's execution architecture rather than a probabilistic outcome of its training. Governed individuation binds an agent at boot to a cryptographically frozen identity digest, and routes every action through a gate defined over the semantic effect of the action rather than its name. We prove that no amount of learning, skill acquisition, or self-induced governance abstraction can widen the agent's permitted authority without an operator-signed change to its identity; the guarantee holds even when the agent induces its own safety principle and that principle is wrong. Empirically, in an open-ended tool-use benchmark where a large action space rules out name-based blocking, ungoverned software agents under reward pressure attempt to tamper with their own evaluation at a task-dependent rate that reaches every run on the hardest task, whereas the gate reduces executed forbidden effects to zero as a verified property of the construction while preserving task success. An adversarial evaluation of monitors of increasing semantic depth shows false-allows falling from 75% (name-based gating) to zero (dynamic effect tracing), and refusal history transfers compliance to held-out red-line families. Trust in a deployed learning agent shifts from a wager on its continued alignment to a check anyone can run at boot.

</details>


### [509] [MRMS: A Multi-Resolution Memory Substrate for Long-Lived AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04617)
*Jizhizi Li,Amy Shi-Nash*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a memory architecture for long-lived AI agents organized along structured-vector-graph representation and short/medium/long-term temporal axes, with synchronized memory operations for selective retrieval, revision, and evidence attribution. A lightweight prototype called MRMS demonstrates the core mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-lived AI agents cannot achieve continuity by simply extending prompt windows; they need structured memory that preserves useful experience, retrieves selectively, distinguishes personal context from external evidence, and revises when situations change.

Method: The paper proposes a memory substrate with two orthogonal axes: (1) representational axis (structured records, vector representations, graph relations) and (2) temporal axis (short-term traces, medium-term abstractions, long-term semantic commitments). The key design is synchronized structured-vector-graph memory where structured records govern eligibility, vectors support recall, and graphs adjudicate support/contradiction/supersession before gated context projection.

Result: The MRMS prototype implements structured records, vector retrieval, temporal policies, and graph-based revision. It exercises core substrate mechanisms including pre-generation memory selection, revision, boundary enforcement, and evidence attribution under controlled long-lived interaction scenarios with explicit evidence requirements.

Conclusion: Reliable personalization is fundamentally a memory design problem: useful memory must be structured, selectively exposed, continuously consolidated, and epistemically labeled rather than stored as undifferentiated conversation history.

Abstract: Long-lived AI agents require continuity across interactions, but continuity cannot be obtained by simply extending the prompt window. An agent must preserve useful prior experience, retrieve it selectively, distinguish personal context from external evidence, and revise memory when the underlying situation changes. We propose an architectural memory substrate organized along two orthogonal axes: a representational axis spanning structured records, vector representations, and graph relations; and a temporal axis spanning short-term traces, medium-term abstractions, and long-term semantic commitments. Its key design constraint is synchronized structured-vector-graph memory: structured records govern eligibility, vector representations support recall, and graph relations adjudicate support, contradiction, and supersession before gated context projection. Its central claim is that reliable personalization is a memory design problem: useful memory is structured, selectively exposed, continuously consolidated, and epistemically labeled rather than stored as undifferentiated conversation history. Beyond the framework, we instantiate MRMS as a lightweight prototype implementing structured records, vector retrieval, temporal policies, and graph-based revision. The prototype exercises the core substrate mechanisms through pre-generation memory selection, revision, boundary enforcement, and evidence attribution under controlled long-lived interaction scenarios with explicit evidence requirements.

</details>


### [510] [Formal Disco: Scalable Open-Ended Generation of Formally Verified Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04631)
*Gabriel Poesia,Simon Henniger,Tzu-Han Hsu,Yilun Du,Nada Amin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formal Disco is a distributed LLM-based system that generates synthetic verified programs at scale to overcome data scarcity in formal verification, producing datasets in Dafny, Verus, and Frama-C and fine-tuning models that match/exceed Claude Opus 4.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While AI code generation costs are rapidly decreasing, quality assurance lags behind. Formal verification offers strong guarantees but suffers from scarcity of human-written examples in verification-aware languages, creating a data barrier for training AI models in this domain.

Method: Proposes Formal Disco, a distributed system coordinating three LLM worker types: initiators (sketch verified programs from READMEs), fixers (resolve compiler/verifier errors), and extenders (expand working programs). Uses a maximum entropy principle for diverse synthetic program generation, with iterative supervised fine-tuning for self-improvement.

Result: Release large synthetic verified program datasets in Dafny, Verus, and Frama-C. Fine-tuned open models that often match or exceed Claude Opus 4.5 performance on verification-relevant tasks.

Conclusion: Offers a scalable path for synthetic data generation in formal reasoning domains, overcoming the long-standing data scarcity problem that has hindered AI-assisted formal verification.

Abstract: The cost of producing code is rapidly diminishing with increasingly capable AI agents, while quality assurance of generated programs has not kept pace. Formal verification provides the strongest possible guarantees, but the ability of AI models to work with verification-aware languages is hindered by the scarcity of human-written examples of programs in those languages. To tackle this prevalent data scarcity issue, we propose Formal Disco: a distributed system for coordination of LLM-based workers that can be easily applied to open-ended synthetic data generation at scale. We use Formal Disco to share tasks and programs between three classes of workers: "initiators", which read random READMEs from open-source repositories and documentation snippets to sketch a related verified program, "fixers" which take compiler and verifier feedback and attempt to resolve issues, and "extenders" that take working programs and propose patches to expand them. Formal Disco records all agent-generated traces and uses them both for initial distillation from a stronger model as well as self-improvement. We also propose a principle of maximum entropy for synthetic program generation, and use entropy maximization via iterative supervised fine-tuning to learn to generate increasingly diverse programs over time. We release large datasets of synthetic verified programs in three languages - Dafny, Verus, and Frama-C -, and fine-tune open models for verification-relevant tasks, often matching or exceeding the performance of Claude Opus 4.5. Overall, our work offers a path to create synthetic data at scale for formal reasoning domains and overcome the long-standing data barrier.

</details>


### [511] [Integrated Altruistic and Fairness Preference Induces Advanced Mutual Cooperation in Sequential Social Dilemmas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04710)
*Yu Wei,Yukiko Ogura,Yoshiyuki Ohmura,Ildefons Magrans de Abril,Hoshinori Kanazawa,Yasuo Kuniyoshi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes AFP, a reward-sharing mechanism integrating altruistic and fairness preferences, to induce cooperation in multi-agent reinforcement learning social dilemmas. Experiments show AFP agents achieve higher cooperation, collective rewards, and equity compared to standard RL and inequity aversion baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cooperation is difficult in MARL social dilemmas where individual rationality leads to suboptimal group outcomes, unlike humans who cooperate due to social preferences.

Method: Designs Altruistic and Fairness Preference (AFP) utility function that combines altruistic preferences (reward for others) and fairness preferences (equality incentive) as a reward-sharing mechanism.

Result: AFP agents achieve mutual cooperation with higher collective rewards and greater equity than standard RL and inequity aversion baselines in sequential social dilemma games.

Conclusion: Altruistic preferences encourage contribution to public goods, while fairness preferences induce mutual cooperative behavior between agents.

Abstract: Inducing cooperation among distributed agents is still a difficult problem in the field of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), particularly in social dilemma situations. There, individual interests are misaligned with the common good and individual rationality leads to suboptimal group outcomes. In contrast, humans are able to achieve cooperation with one another in such situations. A common explanation for such cooperative behavior is that individuals have social preferences. In order to achieve cooperation in MARL, we design a new utility function integrating altruistic preferences (incentive for other's reward) and fairness preferences (incentive for equality) from social psychology and behavioral economics, namely, Altruistic and Fairness Preference (AFP), a reward-sharing mechanism which converts one's own and other's rewards to incentives for cooperative behavior. We performed comparative experiments with standard RL and inequity aversion agents in two challenging sequential social dilemma games, and showed that AFP agents successfully achieved mutual cooperation with more collective rewards and higher equity than the baselines. To further understand the progression of AFP during training, we subsequently explore the effects of altruistic preferences and fairness preferences on agents' behavior. The results suggest that altruistic preferences encourage agents to contribute to the public goods, and fairness preferences induce mutual behavior between agents.

</details>


### [512] [FORGE: Research-Trajectory Hijacking Attacks on Deep Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04718)
*Yue Pan,Ziheng Zhang,Junxiang Lei,Changhao Jia,Qingyi Si,Hongcheng Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces FORGE, a two-level attack that exploits planning-layer vulnerabilities in deep research agents by injecting adversarial documents to hijack subtask planning and contaminate reports, and proposes PRISM metric and Root Query Anchoring defense.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep research agents decompose queries into subtasks and retrieve web evidence, creating a planning-layer poisoning surface where adversarial documents can steer follow-up questions and cause report-level contamination.

Method: FORGE (Fabricated Orchestrated Reasoning chain for aGent Exploitation) combines intra-document reasoning fabrication with inter-document chain coordination. PRISM metric weights infected report claims by cognitive type. Root Query Anchoring (RQA) ties recursive follow-up generation to the root query.

Result: Network FORGE reaches 26.4% PRISM with five injected documents and exhibits depth migration shifting poisoned content into factual premises. RQA reduces PRISM from 38.5% to 18.3% on the 10-query defense subset.

Conclusion: Deep research agents are vulnerable to planning-layer poisoning via FORGE, but Root Query Anchoring offers an effective lightweight defense against such attacks.

Abstract: Deep research agents decompose open-ended queries into subtasks, retrieve web evidence over multiple rounds, and synthesize long-form reports. This workflow creates a planning-layer poisoning surface: adversarial documents that enter the retrieval pool can steer follow-up questions and turn a local injection into report-level contamination. We present FORGE (Fabricated Orchestrated Reasoning chain for aGent Exploitation), a two-level attack that combines intra-document reasoning fabrication with inter-document chain coordination to hijack subtask planning. We further introduce the PRISM metric, which weights infected report claims by cognitive type, and Root Query Anchoring, a lightweight defense that ties recursive follow-up generation to the root query. Across 25 queries, Network FORGE reaches 26.4% PRISM with five injected documents and exhibits depth migration, in which recursive synthesis shifts poisoned content from overt framing into factual premises. On the 10-query defense subset, RQA (Root Query Anchoring) reduces PRISM from 38.5% to 18.3%.

</details>


### [513] [FM-ChangeNet: Learning Change through Pathwise Feature Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04750)
*Roie Kazoom,George Leifman,Genady Beryozkin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FM-ChangeNet reformulates change detection as continuous pathwise transport in feature space using a time-conditioned velocity field, providing denser supervision and interpretable change cues, achieving SOTA on remote sensing benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional change detection methods rely on static endpoint comparison (pre vs. post), which provides sparse supervision and struggles to distinguish true structural changes from nuisance effects like illumination shifts or misalignment.

Method: Constructs intermediate latent states between pre/post representations and learns a time-conditioned velocity field along the transformation trajectory. Uses a hierarchical multi-scale architecture with cross-temporal alignment, time-conditioned coarse-to-fine flow decoding, and a unified objective combining flow supervision, trajectory consistency, spatial regularization, and segmentation loss.

Result: Produces more structured and robust change representations and achieves state-of-the-art performance on remote sensing benchmarks.

Conclusion: Pathwise supervision via velocity fields is a promising alternative to endpoint-only change detection, offering both better performance and interpretability of change dynamics.

Abstract: We present FM-ChangeNet, a pathwise-supervised framework for change detection that reformulates bi-temporal reasoning as continuous transport in feature space rather than static endpoint comparison. Given encoded pre and post-temporal representations, we construct intermediate latent states and learn a time-conditioned velocity field $\hat{v}_θ(z_t,t)$ along the transformation trajectory. This pathwise formulation constrains the predictor over a continuum of intermediate states, providing a denser and less ambiguous supervision signal than conventional endpoint-only segmentation and enabling the model to capture temporal evolution explicitly. The learned velocity field is not only a transport mechanism but also an interpretable representation of change: its magnitude serves as a spatially localized change cue that helps distinguish true structural variation from nuisance effects such as illumination shifts and spatial misalignment. We develop a hierarchical multi-scale architecture with cross-temporal alignment, time-conditioned coarse-to-fine flow decoding, and a unified objective that couples flow supervision, trajectory consistency, spatial regularization, and segmentation loss. Experiments on remote sensing benchmarks show that the proposed framework produces more structured and robust change representations while achieving state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [514] [AgenticPD: A Stage-Aware Agentic Framework for Physical Design QoR Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04758)
*Shuo Ren,Zijin Cheng,Yaohui Han,Libo Shen,Leilei Jin,Wanting Tian,Rongliang Fu,Chao Wang,Bei Yu,Tsung-Yi Ho*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgenticPD is a stage-aware agentic framework that optimizes physical design QoR by using specialized agents at each design stage with checkpoint reuse, avoiding full-flow re-runs after each trial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical design QoR optimization is hard and expensive because choices at one stage impact later stages, and each evaluation requires costly full-flow EDA runs.

Method: AgenticPD organizes optimization around stage boundaries of the physical design flow, using a Judge Agent to navigate search and stage-specialized agents with local tools. It supports branching from prior intermediate states and reusing checkpoints.

Result: AgenticPD achieves strong post-route timing while remaining competitive in power and area across baselines.

Conclusion: Stage-aware agentic frameworks with checkpoint reuse can efficiently optimize physical design QoR without full-flow re-runs per trial.

Abstract: Physical design quality-of-results~(QoR) optimization is hard and expensive. Choices made at one stage can help or hurt later stages. Each evaluation requires a costly EDA run through the full flow. While existing methods still treat optimization as flat parameter tuning or a LLM-based script generation task, we present AgenticPD, a stage-aware agentic framework for physical design QoR optimization. Instead of re-running the full flow after every trial, AgenticPD is organized around the stage boundaries of the physical design flow, where a Judge Agent navigates the search and stage-specialized agents make local decisions within their own stage using stage-local tools. Additionally, the agent harness in AgenticPD provides structured observations, execution history, and agent context management. As a result, the system can branch from prior intermediate states and reuse checkpoints to continue the optimization procedure, and every candidate is evaluated at the post-route signoff. Across these baselines, AgenticPD achieves strong post-route timing while remaining competitive in power and area.

</details>


### [515] [CARL: Constraint-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Planning with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04854)
*Qiuyi Qi,Jinjian Zhang,Mutian Bao,Tian Liang,Guocong Li,Dongnan Liu,Wei Zhou,Jie Liu,Ming Kong,Linjian Mo,Feng Zhang,Qiang Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CARL is a reinforcement learning framework that enhances LLMs' intrinsic constraint awareness by comparing output distributions under constrained vs. unconstrained inputs, outperforming baselines on planning tasks without needing external tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs generate plans that frequently violate task constraints, and existing methods relying on external tools or task decomposition fail to enhance the model's intrinsic constraint awareness.

Method: CARL introduces a constraint-aware reward by comparing the model's output distributions under constrained and unconstrained inputs, integrated with standard RL frameworks for end-to-end training.

Result: CARL significantly outperforms standard RFT baselines and state-of-the-art reasoning models on BlocksWorld, TravelPlanner, and T-Eval, showing increased focus on constraints.

Conclusion: CARL effectively strengthens LLMs' intrinsic constraint awareness without requiring external solvers or top models, enabling scalable constraint-aware planning.

Abstract: Despite their strong reasoning capabilities and extensive world knowledge, Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently generate plans that violate task constraints, undermining their reliability in real-world applications. This deficiency arises from a lack of systematic mechanisms to incorporate constraint information during the generation process. While existing approaches attempt to mitigate this by relying on external tools or task decomposition, they fail to enhance the model's intrinsic constraint awareness. To address this, we propose Constraint-Aware Reinforcement Learning (CARL), a novel RL framework designed to strengthen LLMs' intrinsic focus on constraints. CARL introduces a constraint-aware reward by comparing the model's output distributions under constrained and unconstrained inputs, encouraging constraint focus and penalizing neglect. Compatible with various RL frameworks and requiring no external solvers or top models, CARL enables scalable, end-to-end constraint-aware planning. Extensive experiments on BlocksWorld, TravelPlanner, and T-Eval demonstrate that CARL significantly outperforms standard Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) baselines and state-of-the-art reasoning models, exhibiting a markedly increased focus on constraints.

</details>


### [516] [Medi-Gemma: A Hybrid Clinical Decision Support System Integrating Deterministic EMR Analytics and Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04907)
*Mohammed Saim Ahmed Quadri,Yunzhe Xue,Justin W. Ady,Usman Roshan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Medi-Gemma is a CDSS for wound pathology triage that decouples clinical perception from data orchestration using a multi-stage pipeline with Ground Truth Injection, deterministic safety guards, and CPU-optimized retrieval, eliminating hallucination and improving factual adherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs in clinical settings suffer from structural hallucinations, weak deterministic reasoning over tabular patient data, and omissions in vector retrieval, making them unsafe for high-stakes deployment.

Method: A decoupled architecture with ClinicalOrchestrator, DataManager (type coercion for EMR cleaning), IntentRouter (routes to PandasQueryEngine or ClinicalRAGEngine), Ground Truth Injection Module (injects latest validated clinical state as overriding context), ProtocolManager (deterministic risk pathways), and SafetyVerifier (output phrase filtering).

Result: The architecture eliminates semantic context drift, prevents database compilation crashes, and improves factual adherence to backend clinical repositories.

Conclusion: Medi-Gemma offers a safer pattern for LLM-based clinical decision support where structured data fidelity, retrieval grounding, and deterministic safeguards are essential.

Abstract: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) in high-stakes clinical settings remains limited by structural hallucinations, weak deterministic reasoning over tabular patient data, and omissions in vector retrieval. This paper presents the architecture and validation of Medi-Gemma, a Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) for wound pathology triage and workflow automation. The platform introduces a decoupled framework that separates clinical perception from data orchestration while preserving traceable reasoning. Medi-Gemma uses a multi-stage pipeline coordinated by a centralized ClinicalOrchestrator. Data requests are handled without generative inference by a DataManager that cleans unstructured Electronic Medical Record (EMR) files through type coercion. Natural language queries are processed by a hierarchical IntentRouter, which routes requests to deterministic analytics paths executed by a PandasQueryEngine or to patient-specific reasoning managed by a ClinicalRAGEngine using a CPU-optimized vector store. A key contribution is the Ground Truth Injection Module, which intercepts patient-specific queries, extracts numeric identification tokens, queries the structured dataframe via Pandas, retrieves the latest validated clinical state, and embeds this snapshot as an overriding context block in the LLM prompt before generation. Safety compliance is enforced by a deterministic ProtocolManager that maps clinical terminology to fixed evidence-based risk pathways, while a SafetyVerifier phrase filter prevents output rule violations. Validation shows that this architecture eliminates semantic context drift, prevents database compilation crashes, and improves factual adherence to backend clinical repositories. These results support Medi-Gemma as a safer pattern for LLM-based clinical decision support where structured data fidelity, retrieval grounding, and deterministic safeguards are essential.

</details>


### [517] [STAPO: Selective Trajectory-Aware Policy Optimization for LLM Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04963)
*Qiuyi Qi,Tian Liang,Mutian Bao,Jinjian Zhang,Dongnan Liu,Wei Zhou,Linjian Mo,Ming Kong,Jie Liu,Feng Zhang,Qiang Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STAPO introduces normalized entropy to detect trajectory neglect in LLM agents and leverages a hierarchical group-based RL framework with trajectory-aware rewards and penalties to achieve SOTA performance on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-Augmented QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse/delayed rewards in RL cause trajectory neglect (agents losing focus on goals and history). Existing Shannon-entropy-based signals conflate state complexity with agent confidence, giving unreliable estimates of decision reliability.

Method: Proposes normalized entropy (confidence deviation relative to average behavior under a state) to identify outlier steps. Introduces STAPO: hierarchical group-based RL framework that uses normalized entropy to locate trajectory neglect steps, then optimizes them via joint trajectory-aware reward and trajectory-independent penalty.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-Augmented QA; substantially alleviates trajectory neglect; demonstrates effectiveness and robustness for agentic tasks.

Conclusion: Normalized entropy improves upon Shannon-entropy for detecting unreliable decisions; STAPO effectively addresses trajectory neglect in LLM agents while maintaining training stability, validated across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is the dominant paradigm for training Large Language Model (LLM) agents on long-horizon tasks. However, sparse and delayed rewards often lead to trajectory neglect, in which agents lose focus on the task goal and interaction history at intermediate steps. Prior work has explored step-level supervision using Shannon-entropy-based uncertainty signals, which conflate inherent state complexity with agent confidence and therefore provide unreliable estimates of decision reliability. To address this issue, we propose normalized entropy, which measures confidence deviations relative to an agent's average behavior under a given state, thereby strengthening the association between low-quality actions and trajectory neglect. Building on this insight, we introduce Selective Trajectory-Aware Policy Optimization (STAPO), a hierarchical group-based RL framework. STAPO leverages normalized entropy to locate outlier steps associated with trajectory neglect and optimizes them via a joint mechanism of trajectory-aware reward and trajectory-independent penalty, enhancing trajectory awareness while preserving training stability. Extensive experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-Augmented QA demonstrate that STAPO achieves state-of-the-art performance while substantially alleviating trajectory neglect, validating its effectiveness and robustness for agentic tasks.

</details>


### [518] [Quantum-Inspired Harmonic Decision Models: A Computational Framework for Music Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05007)
*Josef Pavlíček,Petra Pavlíčková,Martin Molhanec*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a quantum-inspired harmonic decision-making framework that combines interference-based search with classical optimization to generate musically coherent chord sequences, evaluated on examples like Autumn Leaves, showing improved harmonic stability and functional organization, with expert feedback highlighting the role of stylistic context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model harmonic generation as a structured decision-making process in a constrained combinatorial space, integrating quantum-inspired parallel search with domain-specific tonal harmony knowledge.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) quantum-inspired interference-based harmonization for parallel evaluation of multiple chord sequence candidates, followed by (2) a classical optimization procedure grounded in tonal harmony to refine sequences for structural coherence and stylistic plausibility.

Result: Quantitative results on Autumn Leaves and It's a Long Way to Tipperary show reduced chord density, increased harmonic stability, and improved functional organization. Expert evaluation indicates that increased harmonic complexity is not always perceived as more natural.

Conclusion: Quantum-inspired methods may offer a useful framework for modeling complex decision processes in creative domains like music, contributing to research on quantum-inspired cognition and decision-making in biological and creative systems.

Abstract: This paper introduces a quantum-inspired computational framework for harmonic decision-making in music. The proposed approach formulates harmonization as an optimization problem within a structured combinatorial space, where multiple candidate chord sequences are evaluated under interacting musical constraints. The model combines an interference-based harmonization stage with a classical optimization procedure grounded in tonal harmony. The quantum-inspired component enables the parallel consideration of multiple harmonic alternatives, while the classical stage refines the resulting sequences to ensure structural coherence and stylistic plausibility.
  The framework is evaluated on selected musical examples, including Autumn Leaves and It's a Long Way to Tipperary. Quantitative analysis shows that the optimization stage significantly reduces chord density, increases harmonic stability, and improves functional organization. At the same time, expert evaluation highlights the importance of stylistic context, demonstrating that increased harmonic complexity is not always perceived as more natural. The results suggest that harmonic generation can be interpreted as a structured decision-making process in a constrained search space. The proposed approach provides a computational model that integrates domain-specific knowledge with an interference-based search mechanism.
  Although preliminary, this work indicates that quantum-inspired methods may offer a useful framework for modeling complex decision processes in creative domains such as music. The proposed framework contributes to ongoing research on quantum-inspired models of cognition and decision-making in complex biological and creative systems.

</details>


### [519] [Toward Trustworthy Large Language Model Agents in Healthcare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05055)
*Hadi Hasan,Safaa Salman,Adam Tai Abou Dargham,Ammar Mohanna,Ali Chehab*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CareConnect is a safety-first conversational agent using LLMs, RAG, and deterministic guardrails to automate healthcare appointment scheduling, achieving 91.8% task completion, 96% safety compliance, and $0.0324 per appointment cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Healthcare appointment scheduling suffers from manual coordination, fragmented legacy systems, and high administrative overhead, constraining provider availability and degrading patient access to care.

Method: The system uses LLM function calling, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and layered deterministic safety guardrails, orchestrating eight domain-specific tools for booking, modification, cancellation, and facility info retrieval, with strict scope constraints against medical advice/diagnosis and short-circuit mechanisms for emergency detection.

Result: 91.8% task completion rate (680 scenarios), median latency 2.2s, 96.0% safety compliance on safety-critical subset, average cost $0.0324 per appointment.

Conclusion: Carefully scoped and rigorously safeguarded LLM-based agents can reliably automate complex healthcare operational workflows while maintaining safety guarantees and achieving substantial cost efficiency.

Abstract: Healthcare appointment scheduling remains a persistent operational bottleneck, driven by manual coordination, fragmented legacy systems, and high administrative overhead. These inefficiencies constrain provider availability and degrade patient access to care. This paper presents CareConnect, a safety-first conversational agent for healthcare logistics automation that leverages large language model (LLM) function calling, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and layered deterministic safety guardrails. The system orchestrates eight domain-specific tools to support appointment booking, modification, cancellation, and facility information retrieval, while enforcing strict scope constraints that prohibit medical advice or diagnosis. Safety-critical situations are handled through deterministic short-circuit mechanisms for emergency detection and medical intent refusal. We evaluate CareConnect on a comprehensive benchmark of 680 task-oriented scenarios spanning end-to-end workflows, multi-turn interactions, and edge cases. Experimental results demonstrate a 91.8% task completion rate with a median per-request latency of 2.2 seconds, 96.0% safety compliance on the dedicated safety-critical evaluation subset, and an average operational cost of $0.0324 per appointment, yielding a significant cost reduction compared to manual human scheduling. These findings show that carefully scoped and rigorously safeguarded LLM-based agents can reliably automate complex healthcare operational workflows while maintaining safety guarantees and achieving substantial cost efficiency. The source code and system implementation are publicly available at https://github.com/Hadi-Hsn/CareConnect.

</details>


### [520] [Diffusion-Guided Uncertainty-Aware Delayed Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05064)
*Junqi Tu,Zejiao Liu,Fangfei Li,Yang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper addresses performance degradation in reinforcement learning caused by delayed feedback, proposes DUPO which uses diffusion models to model state discrepancies and weight delayed policies, showing consistent outperformance on robotic control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning suffers from severe performance degradation due to delayed feedback in real-world environments, and existing methods overlook the inherent discrepancy between delayed and true states in stochastic MDPs.

Method: DUPO (Diffusion Guided Uncertainty Aware Delayed Policy Optimization) uses a diffusion model to model the relationship between delayed state messages and current states, leveraging discrepancy estimates to weight delayed policies.

Result: DUPO consistently outperforms existing methods on continuous robotic control tasks with multiple stochastic delays and remains effective under long and random delay scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based approach effectively addresses the state discrepancy problem in delayed RL, leading to better performance across various delay conditions.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning in real world environments often suffers from severe performance degradation due to delayed feedback. Existing approaches typically mitigate performance degradation caused by observation delays by constructing augmented states or predicting the true states. However, these methods often overlook the inherent discrepancy between delayed state and true states induced by stochastic MDP. We theoretically prove the existence of such a discrepancy and show that it leads to the degradation of the optimal policy. To address this challenge, we propose Diffusion Guided Uncertainty Aware Delayed Policy Optimization (DUPO). Our method explicitly models the relationship between delayed state message and the current state using a diffusion model, and leverages the resulting discrepancy estimates to weight delayed policies. Extensive experiments on continuous robotic control tasks with multiple stochastic delays demonstrate that DUPO consistently outperforms existing methods and remains effective even under long and random delay scenarios.

</details>


### [521] [ASSEMCAD: Production-Ready CAD Assembly Generation from Natural Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05123)
*Yurui Dong,Shu Zou,Siqi Li,Nianchen Deng,Hongbin Zhou,Xuemeng Yang,Pinlong Cai,Licheng Wen,Xinyu Cai,Botian Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AssemCAD is an axiom-grounded framework that converts natural language into mechanically valid, production-ready CAD assemblies by using typed parts, geometry-backed ports, executable mates, and engineering axioms, significantly outperforming code-centric baselines in assembly preservation and physical validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Text-to-CAD approaches handle single-part generation well, but production-ready mechanical assembly generation remains unsolved because assemblies require coordinated multi-component reasoning, functional interfaces, assembly relations, and physical consistency—tasks that direct CAD code generation cannot handle.

Method: AssemCAD first constructs an axiomatic Assembly Specification (typed parts, ports, executable mates, engineering axioms) grounded in engineering principles. It then introduces a port- and mate-based CAD assembly library that executes symbolic relations through deterministic mate transformations and validates interfaces with B-Rep geometric evidence. It also supports on-demand synthesis of reusable parametric component factories.

Result: Experiments on AssemBench show substantial improvements in assembly preservation and physical validity over code-centric CAD generation baselines, with generalization across different foundation-model backbones.

Conclusion: By combining axiom-grounded assembly reasoning with deterministic geometric execution, AssemCAD extends Text-to-CAD from isolated part generation toward production-ready mechanical assembly design.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models and programmatic CAD have significantly improved Text-to-CAD generation for individual parts. However, production-ready mechanical assembly generation remains largely unsolved. Unlike single-part modeling, assemblies require coordinated reasoning over multiple components, functional interfaces, assembly relations, engineering principles, and physical consistency. Consequently, directly generating executable CAD code is insufficient for constructing mechanically valid and reusable assemblies. We present AssemCAD, an axiom-grounded framework for production-ready CAD assembly generation from natural language. Instead of representing an assembly as monolithic CAD code, AssemCAD first constructs an axiomatic Assembly Specification consisting of typed parts, geometry-backed ports, executable mates, and engineering axioms. Each assembly relation is explicitly grounded in one or more engineering principles, making the resulting specification interpretable, reusable, and verifiable. To realize this specification, AssemCAD introduces a port- and mate-based CAD assembly library that executes symbolic assembly relations through deterministic mate transformations and validates declared interfaces using concrete B-Rep geometric evidence. Built on this representation and library, AssemCAD further supports on-demand synthesis of reusable parametric component factories for both standard and open-world geometries. Experiments on AssemBench show that AssemCAD substantially improves assembly preservation and physical validity over code-centric CAD generation baselines, while generalizing across different foundation-model backbones. By combining axiom-grounded assembly reasoning with deterministic geometric execution, AssemCAD extends Text-to-CAD from isolated part generation toward production-ready mechanical assembly design.

</details>


### [522] [TacReasoner: A Dynamic Tactile-Language Framework for Interactive Reasoning in Real-World Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05131)
*Kailin Lyu,Di Wu,Long Xiao,Jianning Zeng,Jianwei He,Chang Lin,Lianyu Hu,Lin Shu,Jie Hao,Ce Hao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TacReasoner is a dynamic tactile-language framework that introduces a Dynamic-aware Tactile Encoder and the TouchCoT-10k chain-of-thought dataset to address tactile signal modeling and hallucination issues, achieving competitive results (7B model outperforming 14B VTV-LLM).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile sensing is fundamental to survival but faces two key challenges in multimodal reasoning: (i) insufficient modeling of dynamic tactile signals for temporally evolving properties, and (ii) hallucination in tactile foundation models due to lack of explicit reasoning mechanisms.

Method: Proposes TacReasoner with a Dynamic-aware Tactile Encoder for better dynamic signal perception, and introduces TouchCoT-10k - the first tactile chain-of-thought dataset. Also establishes DynTac-Bench for systematic evaluation.

Result: TacReasoner achieves competitive performance against SOTA models across multiple datasets. Notably, the 7B parameter model outperforms the 14B VTV-LLM model on most subtasks.

Conclusion: TacReasoner demonstrates effectiveness and efficiency in tactile commonsense reasoning, with the TouchCoT-10k dataset enabling structured reasoning over tactile inputs.

Abstract: Among the five primary human senses, tactile is arguably the most fundamental to survival, as it enables the perception of physical contact and interaction in real-world environments. In this paper, we explore two key challenges of integrating tactile sensing into intelligent systems for multimodal reasoning: (i) insufficient modeling of dynamic tactile signals, which restricts reasoning over temporally evolving properties, and (ii) hallucination in tactile foundation models caused by the absence of explicit reasoning mechanisms, leading to unstable real-world inference. To address these challenges, we propose TacReasoner, a dynamic tactile-language framework for interactive reasoning in real-world scenarios. First, TacReasoner incorporates a Dynamic-aware Tactile Encoder to enhance the perception and representation of dynamic tactile signals. More importantly, we introduce TouchCoT-10k, the first tactile chain-of-thought dataset for structured reasoning over tactile inputs. Upon it, we establish DynTac-Bench to systematically evaluate dynamic tactile perception and real-world commonsense reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that TacReasoner achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art models across multiple datasets. Notably, despite using only 7B parameters, TacReasoner outperforms the 14B VTV-LLM model on most subtasks, highlighting its effectiveness and efficiency in tactile commonsense reasoning.

</details>


### [523] [DSpark: Confidence-Scheduled Speculative Decoding with Semi-Autoregressive Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05147)
*Xin Cheng,Xingkai Yu,Chenze Shao,Jiashi Li,Yunfan Xiong,Yi Qian,Jiaqi Zhu,Shirong Ma,Xiaokang Zhang,Jiasheng Ye,Qinyu Chen,Chengqi Deng,Jiping Yu,Damai Dai,Zhengyan Zhang,Yixuan Wei,Yixuan Tan,Wenkai Yang,Runxin Xu,Yu Wu,Zhean Xu,Xuanyu Wang,Muyang Chen,Rui Tian,Xiao Bi,Zhewen Hao,Shaoyuan Chen,Huanqi Cao,Wentao Zhang,Anyi Xu,Huishuai Zhang,Dongyan Zhao,Wenfeng Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DSpark is a speculative decoding framework that combines semi-autoregressive parallel draft generation with adaptive, load-aware verification to significantly accelerate LLM inference and improve throughput in high-concurrency serving systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods with parallel drafters suffer from rapid acceptance decay due to missing inter-token dependencies, and indiscriminate verification wastes batch capacity on high-rejection-risk tokens, degrading throughput in high-concurrency systems.

Method: DSpark uses a semi-autoregressive architecture (parallel backbone + lightweight sequential module) to model intra-block dependencies, and employs confidence-scheduled verification that adaptively adjusts verification length per request based on prefix survival probabilities and throughput profiles.

Result: On offline benchmarks, DSpark significantly improves accepted length over SOTA autoregressive and parallel drafters. In DeepSeek-V4 serving under live traffic, it accelerates per-user generation by 60-85% at matched throughput, prevents throughput degradation, and shifts the serving system's Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: DSpark enables previously unattainable performance tiers by unifying high-quality parallel drafting with system-efficient adaptive verification, establishing a new Pareto frontier for LLM serving systems.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates Large Language Model (LLM) inference by decoupling draft generation from target verification. While recent parallel drafters efficiently propose long token sequences in a single forward pass, they suffer from rapid acceptance decay due to a lack of inter-token dependencies. Furthermore, indiscriminately verifying these extended blocks wastes critical batch capacity on tokens with high rejection risks, severely degrading throughput in high-concurrency serving systems. We introduce DSpark, a speculative decoding framework that unifies high-throughput parallel generation with adaptive, load-aware verification. To maintain draft quality, DSpark utilizes a semi-autoregressive architecture, coupling a parallel backbone with a lightweight sequential module, to introduce intra-block dependency modeling and mitigate suffix decay. To optimize system efficiency, DSpark employs confidence-scheduled verification, dynamically tailoring the verification length for each request based on estimated prefix survival probabilities and engine-specific throughput profiles. On offline benchmarks across diverse domains, DSpark substantially improves the accepted length over state-of-the-art autoregressive and parallel drafters. When deployed within the DeepSeek-V4 serving system under live user traffic, DSpark successfully mitigates verification waste. Compared to the established production baseline (MTP-1), DSpark accelerates per-user generation speeds by 60 to 85 percent at matched throughput levels. More importantly, by preventing severe throughput degradation under strict interactivity constraints, it enables performance tiers that were previously unattainable, shifting the Pareto frontier of our serving system.

</details>


### [524] [The Changing Role of Symbolic Methods in Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05168)
*Jun Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that symbolic reasoning is not fundamental to intelligence but a computational necessity when using simplified world models. The Compression Principle states explicit reasoning compensates for omitted information, leading to a Modeling-Reasoning Trade-off. While foundation models need less symbolic reasoning, symbolic methods become increasingly important as human-AI interfaces for specification, verification, and trust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the apparent paradox between the historical importance of symbolic reasoning in AI and the remarkable success of modern foundation models that operate with little explicit symbolic reasoning, and to clarify what role symbolic methods should play in future AI systems.

Method: Proposes the Compression Principle (every computational model simplifies reality) and derives the Modeling-Reasoning Trade-off (richer world models reduce need for explicit reasoning). Uses this framework to explain both classical symbolic AI and modern foundation models under a unified perspective.

Result: Shows that the Modeling-Reasoning Trade-off explains why foundation models need less symbolic reasoning (they preserve richer representations) and why symbolic methods remain crucial as human-AI interfaces for specification, verification, regulation, and trust-building.

Conclusion: Symbolic methods' future lies not as the computational engine of AI but as the symbolic interface between opaque AI systems and humans who need to specify, verify, regulate, and trust them.

Abstract: Why do intelligent systems need to perform explicit symbolic reasoning? Computer science has traditionally regarded symbolic reasoning as a defining component of intelligence. Yet the remarkable success of modern foundation models raises a fundamental question: if increasingly capable AI systems can operate with little explicit symbolic reasoning, what role do symbolic methods actually play?
  This article argues that explicit symbolic reasoning is not a fundamental property of intelligence, but a computational consequence of operating on simplified models of reality. We propose the Compression Principle: every computational model is a simplified representation of reality, and explicit symbolic reasoning compensates for information omitted during model construction. From this principle, we derive the Modeling--Reasoning Trade-off: as computational models preserve richer representations of the world, the need for explicit symbolic reasoning correspondingly decreases. This perspective provides a unified explanation for both the historical success of symbolic methods and the remarkable effectiveness of modern foundation models.
  Paradoxically, the same development makes symbolic methods increasingly important for humans. As intelligent systems become more capable and more opaque, symbolic representations increasingly serve as interfaces through which humans specify requirements, verify behavior, regulate autonomous systems, and establish trust. We therefore argue that the future of symbolic methods lies not primarily as the computational engine of intelligent systems, but as the symbolic interface between increasingly capable AI systems and the humans who build, govern, and depend upon them.

</details>


### [525] [AgentGym2: Benchmarking Large Language Model Agents in De-Idealized Real-World Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05174)
*Zhiheng Xi,Dingwen Yang,Jiaqi Liu,Jixuan Huang,Honglin Guo,Baodai Huang,Tinggang Chen,Qi Zhang,Zhonghang Lu,Chenyu Liu,Jiajun Sun,Jiazheng Zhang,Dingwei Zhu,Xin Guo,Junzhe Wang,Zhihao Zhang,Yuming Yang,Junjie Ye,Minghe Gao,Dongrui Liu,Jiaming Ji,Guohao Li,Tao Gui,Qi Zhang,Xuanjing Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentGym2 is a new evaluation framework that tests LLM agents on real-world tasks involving tool discovery, noisy inputs, and end-to-end procedures, revealing that even advanced models like Gemini and GPT-5 struggle significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate agents in simplified settings with pre-packaged tools, clean inputs, and overlooked steps, failing to capture the difficulty of real-world deployments where uncertainty, noise, and tool exploration are essential.

Method: AgentGym2 grounds task instances in real-world end-to-end working demands, measuring agents' ability to execute procedures, discover tools via exploration, compose tools for unseen tasks, and handle noisy/underspecified information.

Result: Evaluation of 15 proprietary and open-source models shows that even state-of-the-art systems like Gemini and GPT-5 struggle on AgentGym2, indicating a substantial gap between current agent capabilities and real-world requirements.

Conclusion: Current LLM agents are not yet robust enough for real-world deployment; AgentGym2 provides a more realistic benchmark to drive progress toward agents that can handle uncertainty, exploration, and end-to-end task execution.

Abstract: Language agents, i.e., LLM agents, progress rapidly and are increasingly deployed in production environments. This trend underscores the urgent need for rigorous and realistic evaluations. However, most existing benchmarks evaluate agents in simplified, idealized settings. They typically rely on pre-packaged tool interfaces, overlook critical steps, and assume inputs are clean and fully specified. Consequently, they understate the difficulty of real deployments, where uncertainty and noise are ubiquitous and agents must proactively explore the environment to uncover new tools. To bridge this gap, we present AgentGym2, a new evaluation framework with task instances grounded in real-world end-to-end working demands. Beyond reasoning and planning, it measures agents' ability to execute end-to-end procedures, discover tools via exploration, compose tools for unseen tasks, and remain robust to noisy and underspecified information. Experiments on 15 proprietary and open-source models show that even SOTA systems like Gemini and GPT-5 struggle on AgentGym2, revealing a substantial gap between the capability of current agents and the demands of real-world applications.

</details>


### [526] [CP-WSP: A Declarative CP-SAT Framework for Configurable Multi-Constraint Workforce Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05177)
*Vipul Patel,Anirudh Deodhar,Dagnachew Birru*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CP-WSP is a declarative CP-SAT framework for workforce scheduling that enforces 14 hard constraints and optimizes 15 soft objectives via a unified weighted penalty function, featuring shift-window decomposition, acuity-weighted workload equity, multi-granularity temporal resolution, inter-week stability, and cross-midnight shift handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CP formulations for workforce scheduling model simplified instances with limited constraints, lacking explicit support for critical real-world requirements such as mandatory break scheduling with midpoint control, acuity-weighted workload equity, sub-shift temporal granularity, inter-week schedule stability, and cross-midnight shift patterns.

Method: The paper proposes CP-WSP: a declarative CP-SAT framework with 14 hard constraints and 15 soft objectives optimized via a unified weighted penalty function. It introduces shift-window variable decomposition for break scheduling with centrality control, acuity-weighted workload equity, multi-granularity temporal resolution (30 min to 2 hours), inter-week schedule stability, and a grid-offset preprocessing technique for cross-midnight shifts. Configuration is done via JSON with no code changes.

Result: The framework is evaluated on INRC-II benchmarks at both hourly and shift-level granularity, and on 36 synthetic configurations. A reproducible 36-configuration benchmark suite is provided for community comparison.

Conclusion: CP-WSP offers a comprehensive, configurable, and reproducible solution for workforce scheduling that addresses key gaps in existing CP formulations, including break scheduling, workload equity, and cross-midnight shifts.

Abstract: Workforce scheduling is an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem requiring simultaneous satisfaction of labor regulations, coverage requirements, employee preferences and operational objectives. Existing CP formulations typically model simplified instances with 6-12 constraints at shift-level granularity and critically lack explicit support for: mandatory break scheduling with midpoint placement control; acuity weighted workload equity; sub-shift temporal granularity enabling demand-driven staffing; inter-week schedule stability; and cross-midnight shift patterns common in 24-hour operations. This paper presents CP-WSP: a declarative CP-SAT framework enforcing 14 hard constraints as mathematically inviolable requirements (zero regulatory violations by construction) while optimizing 15 soft objectives through a unified weighted penalty function -- all configurable via a JSON specification with no code changes required. Key contributions include: a shift-window variable decomposition enabling mandatory break scheduling with centrality control; acuity-weighted workload equity; multi-granularity temporal resolution from 30 minutes to 2 hours; inter-week schedule stability; a grid-offset preprocessing technique for cross-midnight shifts; and a reproducible 36-configuration benchmark suite for community comparison. Evaluated on INRC-II benchmarks at both hourly and shift-level granularity and on 36 synthetic configurations.

</details>


### [527] [Rethinking On-Policy Self-Distillation for Thinking Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05184)
*Simran Kaur,Narutatsu Ri,Yinghui He,Liam Fowl,Sanjeev Arora*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Privileged self-distillation (where a model is trained on its own reasoning traces containing privileged information like solutions) degrades thinking models' performance on long reasoning traces, causing up to 17% relative accuracy drops on math benchmarks, by reducing verification, backtracking, and hedging behaviors at high-entropy forking positions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-distillation using privileged information (e.g., solutions) seems promising for improving language models, especially thinking models that can leverage test-time reasoning. The paper investigates whether this approach actually benefits thinking models.

Method: Evaluated Qwen3 and OLMo thinking models on AIME24, AIME25, and HMMT25 benchmarks; compared privileged self-distillation vs. vanilla on-policy distillation (OPD); analyzed token-level behavior at forking positions, self-correction branches, and verification/backtracking markers.

Result: Privileged self-distillation causes up to 17% relative drop in avg@16 accuracy; degradation scales with privileged context withheld and is most severe at long rollout budgets. Privileged OPD reverses gains from vanilla OPD. Privileged context lowers fork rates in thinking models but not instruction models, and penalizes self-correction tokens.

Conclusion: Self-distillation for strong thinking models requires careful attention to token-level signal, especially around correction and reasoning steps, as privileged context can harm the very reasoning behaviors that make thinking models effective.

Abstract: Self-distillation is a promising recipe for self-improvement in language models. In this setting, a model can serve as its own teacher when given privileged information, such as a solution to a math problem. This seems especially appealing for thinking models, which can use test-time reasoning to absorb the privileged information. Surprisingly, we show that privileged self-distillation degrades thinking models on long reasoning traces: across five Qwen3 and OLMo thinking models evaluated on AIME24, AIME25, and HMMT25, privileged-context distillation causes a relative drop of up to 17% in avg@16 accuracy.
