The loop must decide which candidates to evaluate, how rewards become model updates, which feedback to reuse, and how far to move beyond the pretrained prior. These choices have mostly been studied in isolation, leaving open whether they complement one another, become redundant, or interfere inside a full online adaptation loop. We conduct controlled studies across six small-molecule binding-affinity tasks and three protein-fitness tasks. We find that acquisition, reward shaping, and model debiasing provide complementary routes to higher reward, especially for small molecules. Replay further stabilizes learning, while validity penalties keep small-molecule exploration on the valid molecular manifold. Together, these findings point to a practical recipe for feedback-efficient molecular optimization: online fine-tuning with acquisition, reward shaping, debiasing, replay, and validity control. This recipe outperforms offline fine-tuning and inference-time search baselines under matched oracle-call budgets and GPU-hour accounting. The gains are largest when high-reward candidates require larger shifts from the pretrained prior.

</details>


### [553] [Labeled-Data-Free Meta-Learning: Efficient Task Generation Using Pre-trained Models and Unlabeled Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02850)
*Lei Sun,Yusuke Tanaka,Tomoharu Iwata*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new data-free meta-learning approach that avoids expensive model inversion by using pre-trained models to assign soft labels to unlabeled data, with a task-weighting mechanism, achieving major speedups (up to 104x) and accuracy gains (8.4–36.4%) over prior DFML methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Data-Free Meta-Learning (DFML) methods rely on model inversion to generate training data, which is computationally expensive and difficult due to the need to produce high-dimensional data matching the original distribution. The authors aim to overcome this limitation.

Method: The method jointly leverages pre-trained models and unlabeled data, generating meta-training tasks by assigning soft labels from pre-trained models to unlabeled data. A task-weighting mechanism based on task confidence and class distribution balance is introduced to handle varying task quality.

Result: The approach achieves up to 104-fold speedup and 8.4% to 36.4% improvements in few-shot classification accuracy compared to state-of-the-art DFML methods, with substantial reduction in computational cost and improved generalization.

Conclusion: The proposed setting avoids model inversion entirely, making DFML more practical and efficient for real-world applications where labeled data is scarce or privacy-restricted.

Abstract: Meta-learning without labeled data is crucial for real-world applications, where obtaining labeled datasets can be expensive or restricted due to privacy concerns. Data-Free Meta-Learning (DFML) addresses this challenge by leveraging pre-trained models without access to training data. However, existing DFML methods rely on model inversion to generate training data, a process that is generally difficult and computationally expensive due to the need to generate high-dimensional data matching the original distribution. To address this limitation, we propose a novel meta-learning setting that avoids model inversion by jointly leveraging pre-trained models and unlabeled data. Our method generates meta-training tasks by assigning soft labels from pre-trained models to unlabeled data. Since the quality of these tasks can vary, we introduce a task-weighting mechanism based on task confidence and class distribution balance to ensure effective meta-learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach substantially reduces computational cost and improves generalization, achieving up to 104-fold speedup and 8.4 percent to 36.4 percent improvements in few-shot classification accuracy compared to state-of-the-art DFML methods.

</details>


### [554] [Trading Confidence: Comprehensive Uncertainty Estimation in Algorithmic Trading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02864)
*Lin Li,Li Rong Wang,Hsuan Fu,Xiuyi Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an uncertainty-aware RL framework integrating distributional, epistemic, and aleatoric uncertainty estimations (using SHAP-weighted reconstruction, MC Dropout, and LSTM-based consensus) for financial trading, outperforming traditional models on five U.S. stock indices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RL models struggle in highly uncertain financial markets due to stochastic volatility, model limitations, and regime shifts, often failing to adapt to sudden market disruptions and producing suboptimal trading decisions.

Method: An uncertainty-aware RL framework that integrates three uncertainty types: distributional uncertainty, epistemic uncertainty (via MC Dropout), and aleatoric uncertainty (via SHAP-weighted reconstruction), combined with an LSTM-based technical indicator consensus mechanism.

Result: Experiments on five major U.S. stock indices show RL agents with uncertainty estimation significantly outperform traditional models in both return and risk management.

Conclusion: The study advances uncertainty estimation in RL-based financial trading, and future work will extend to other asset classes and alternative RL architectures for greater adaptability.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful approach in financial trading, enabling agents to learn optimal strategies through direct market interaction. However, financial markets are highly uncertain, with price fluctuations driven by stochastic volatility, model limitations, and regime shifts. Traditional RL models struggle in dynamic environments, often failing to adapt to sudden market disruptions, leading to suboptimal trading decisions. To address this challenge, we propose an uncertainty-aware RL framework that integrates distributional, epistemic, and aleatoric uncertainty estimations. Our approach enhances uncertainty estimation using SHAP-weighted reconstruction uncertainty, MC Dropout, and an LSTM-based technical indicator consensus mechanism. Experimental results on five major U.S. stock indices demonstrate that RL agents equipped with uncertainty estimation significantly outperform traditional models in return and risk management. This study advances uncertainty estimation in RL-based financial trading, with future research extending its application to other asset classes and alternative RL architectures for greater adaptability.

</details>


### [555] [Reward Granularity in RLVR: Comparing Process and Outcome Reward Structures for Mathematical Reasoning in Small Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02869)
*Anagha Radhakrishna Palandye,Rebecca Glick,Osheen Kaul*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper compares outcome-based vs. process-based reward signals in RLVR for small language models (Qwen2.5-0.5B) on GSM8K, finding process-only rewards achieve 63.73% accuracy vs. 53.75% for outcome-only, with higher step validity, while hybrid rewards generally correlate with process weight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most RLVR work only rewards final answers, leaving the impact of step-level process supervision underexplored, especially for small models lacking self-correction capacity under sparse feedback.

Method: Systematic comparison of five reward conditions (no-RL baseline, process-only, outcome-only, three hybrid weightings with λ∈{0.9,0.5,0.1} process weight) applied to Qwen2.5-0.5B fine-tuned with GRPO on GSM8K.

Result: Process-only supervision achieves 63.73% vs 53.75% for outcome-only (~10% gap). Hybrid rewards correlate positively with process weight; low-process/high-outcome (λ=0.1) underperforms pure outcome supervision. Process models produce structurally inconsistent but arithmetically grounded traces; outcome models produce concise but derivation-error-prone chains.

Conclusion: Reward granularity is a first-order design decision for RLVR; process-level supervision substantially improves both accuracy and trace fidelity in small language models.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for improving mathematical reasoning in language models. Yet most RLVR work rewards only the final answer (outcome-based rewards), leaving the impact of step-level process supervision (process rewards) underexplored especially for small models that lack the capacity to self-correct under sparse feedback. We systematically compare five reward conditions applied to Qwen2.5-0.5B fine-tuned with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on GSM8K: a no-RL baseline, process-only, outcome-only, and three hybrid weightings ($λ\in \{0.9, 0.5, 0.1\}$ process weight). Process-only supervision achieves 63.73% test accuracy versus 53.75% for outcome-only, a nearly 10-percentage point gap while yielding reasoning traces with higher step validity and lower deviation from ground-truth chain length. Hybrid rewards generally correlate positively with process weight, with one notable anomaly: the low-process / high-outcome configuration ($λ=0.1$) underperforms pure outcome supervision, suggesting conflicting optimization signals. Error analysis using GPT-4o as a judge reveals distinct failure mode distributions: process models generate structurally inconsistent but arithmetically grounded traces, while outcome models produce concise but derivation-error-prone chains. Our results demonstrate that reward granularity is a first-order design decision for RLVR, with process-level supervision substantially improving both accuracy and trace fidelity in small language models.

</details>


### [556] [Poisson-Gamma Modeling of Inter-Relational Dependencies in Dynamic Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02872)
*Nan Fang,Yijun Wang,Hao Liao,Sikun Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PGRE is a probabilistic model using Poisson-Gamma formulation to capture temporal and relational dependencies in dynamic knowledge graphs, achieving strong link prediction performance especially in sparse settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic knowledge graphs evolve over time and are often noisy and incomplete, requiring better modeling of temporal and relational dependencies for downstream tasks.

Method: PGRE uses a Poisson-Bernoulli formulation for multi-relational temporal links, Gamma-distributed latent variables for entity-factor associations and cross-relation dependencies, and a Gamma Markov process for temporal evolution.

Result: Competitive link prediction performance on benchmark datasets, particularly effective in sparse settings, and reveals meaningful relational evolution patterns.

Conclusion: The proposed PGRE model effectively handles temporal and relational dependencies in dynamic knowledge graphs with principled probabilistic modeling.

Abstract: Dynamic knowledge graphs are ubiquitous in today's AI applications, as we represent molecular structures, social relationships, and language information using these graph models. As knowledge graphs evolve over time and are often noisy and incomplete, modeling their temporal and relational dependencies becomes crucial for downstream tasks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes PGRE (Poisson-Gamma Relational Evolution), a probabilistic model for modeling inter-relational dependencies in dynamic knowledge graphs. PGRE represents multi-relational temporal links via a Poisson-Bernoulli formulation. It introduces Gamma-distributed latent variables to capture entity-factor associations and cross-relation dependencies mediated by shared latent communities. A Gamma Markov process further models the temporal evolution of these latent variables, enabling principled characterization of relational dynamics. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that PGRE achieves competitive performance in link prediction, particularly in sparse settings, while revealing meaningful relational evolution patterns in dynamic knowledge graphs.

</details>


### [557] [Dynamic Regret for Non-Stationary Linear Bandits via Misspecification Reductions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02891)
*Zihao Hu,Yuan Yao,Jiheng Zhang,Zhengyuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper achieves optimal dynamic regret for non-stationary linear bandits with general (not necessarily orthogonal) round-specific decision sets by framing parameter drift as bounded misspecification within blocks and using restart-based algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for non-stationary linear bandits rely on restrictive orthogonal-structure assumptions on round-specific decision sets, limiting their applicability to contextual settings like ad impressions, pricing, and treatment assignment.

Method: The authors partition the horizon into blocks, relate each block's dynamic regret to a fixed-parameter benchmark with within-block drift treated as bounded misspecification, and restart algorithms with misspecification-dependent regret guarantees.

Result: Achieves the optimal \(\widetilde O(T^{2/3}P_T^{1/3})\) dynamic-regret dependence for both linear bandits with general compact decision sets and \(K\)-armed contextual linear bandits.

Conclusion: The misspecification-reduction framework removes the orthogonal-structure constraint, broadening applicability to realistic online decision-making with drifting environments and arbitrary feasible sets.

Abstract: Many online decision-making problems involve both round-specific feasible actions and drifting reward models: eligible ad impressions, feasible prices, and available treatments can change over time, while user preferences, demand curves, and patient responses may evolve. Motivated by these applications, we study non-stationary linear bandits with round-specific feasible decision sets. Existing methods that obtain the optimal \(\widetilde O(T^{2/3}P_T^{1/3})\) dependence, where \(P_T\) is the path length of the reward-parameter sequence, impose an orthogonal-structure assumption on round-specific decision sets, which can be restrictive in contextual applications. We address this gap through a unified misspecification-reduction viewpoint: after partitioning the horizon into blocks, we relate each block's dynamic regret to regret against a fixed-parameter linear bandit benchmark, with the within-block parameter drift entering as bounded misspecification. Restarting algorithms with misspecification-dependent regret guarantees then yields the optimal \(T^{2/3}P_T^{1/3}\) dynamic-regret dependence for both linear bandits with general compact decision sets and \(K\)-armed contextual linear bandits.

</details>


### [558] [Variable Bit-width Quantization: Learning Per-Group Precision for "Bigger-but-Smaller" Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02893)
*Hamish Ogilvy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VBQ introduces a method where groups of 64 weights learn their own bit-width (1,2,4,8) via Gumbel-Softmax relaxation, achieving heterogeneous allocation that outperforms uniform quantization. A 131M model at 1.82 bits beats a 55M FP16 model at 3.8x less storage, with efficiency gains of 3.9-8.4x over FP16 and inference speedups up to 4.7x on Apple silicon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-bit quantization treats precision as a single global hyper-parameter where every weight uses the same bit-width, which is suboptimal for model efficiency.

Method: Each contiguous group of 64 weights learns its own resolution from {1,2,4,8} bits via Gumbel-Softmax relaxation, trained jointly by alternating optimization.

Result: 69% of groups collapse to 1 bit, LM head averages 1.09 bits, first MLP block keeps ~2.5 bits. A 131M model at 1.82 mean bits reaches PPL 4.2 on TinyStories, beating 55M FP16 (PPL 4.4) at 3.8x less storage. VBQ is 3.9-8.4x more efficient than FP16 quality-per-byte, with inference speedups up to 4.7x.

Conclusion: VBQ reframes precision as a learnable, non-uniform resource and shows that spending a fixed bit budget unevenly beats spending it uniformly. Deeper layers progressively self-heal quantization error from early layers.

Abstract: Low-bit quantization shrinks language models but treats precision as a single global hyper-parameter: every weight uses the same bit-width. We introduce Variable Bit-width Quantization (VBQ), a training-time method in which each contiguous group of 64 weights learns its own resolution from {1,2,4,8} bits via a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation, trained jointly by an alternating optimization that gives the precision logits a clean, task-aligned signal. VBQ discovers a consistent, strongly heterogeneous allocation within individual projection types, not merely across layers, impossible to express with per-layer methods: 69% of groups collapse to 1 bit, the LM head averages 1.09 bits, while the first MLP block keeps ~2.5 bits. This pattern is stable enough to freeze into a fixed recipe and reuse without further search. The recipe yields a "bigger-but-smaller" regime: a 131M model at 1.82 mean bits reaches perplexity 4.2 on TinyStories, beating a 55M FP16 model (PPL 4.4) at 3.8x less storage, and lets a 1.46B model on FineWeb-Edu match a 593M FP16 control at ~3.7x less storage with 2.5x more parameters. As quality-per-byte, VBQ is 3.9-8.4x more efficient than FP16. The recipe maps directly to packed low-bit storage, so it also accelerates inference: with custom fused dequantize-and-multiply kernels, memory-bandwidth-bound autoregressive decode is faster at equal output, and the speedup grows with scale (parity at 131M, 1.9x at 1.0B, 4.7x at 9B on Apple silicon). A distributional analysis (KL divergence and argmax-flip rate) reveals a striking mechanism: deeper layers progressively self-heal the quantization error injected by early layers. The win is a from-scratch, train-time phenomenon; scaling the search economically beyond 1.5B parameters remains open. VBQ reframes precision as a learnable, non-uniform resource and shows that spending a fixed bit budget unevenly beats spending it uniformly.

</details>


### [559] [Bootstrap Flow-Map Tree Sampling Enables Online Feedback Driven Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02915)
*Binglin Ji,Anindya Sarkar,Hengchang Lu,Jens Sjölund,Yevgeniy Vorobeychik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BFMT is a computationally efficient sampling framework for history-aware global search and alignment under limited sampling budgets, enabling full tree-path construction with a single function evaluation and dynamic transition scheduling for effective exploration-to-refinement transition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative model-based alignment methods struggle with unknown preferences revealed through sequential feedback, excelling only in local searches within narrow distribution regions rather than broad exploration needed to uncover high-utility areas.

Method: Bootstrap Flow-Map-Tree (BFMT) enables full tree-path construction from any tree depth with a single function evaluation, includes dynamic transition time step scheduling to allocate sampling budget from global exploration to local refinement.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse search and alignment tasks show BFMT substantially outperforms baseline approaches.

Conclusion: BFMT effectively addresses the challenge of budget-constrained sequential sampling where preferences are unknown a priori, enabling efficient global-to-local search.

Abstract: In many scientific and engineering domains, maximizing discovery within a limited sampling budget demands strategic, observation-guided exploration. While generative models have enabled training-free reward alignment, current methods typically excel in local searches within narrow regions of the underlying distribution. These approaches struggle when preferences are unknown a priori and only revealed through sequential feedback-a scenario demanding broad exploration to uncover high-utility regions. To address this, we introduce Bootstrap Flow-Map-Tree (a.k.a BFMT), a novel computationally efficient sampling framework designed for history-aware global search and alignment under sampling budget constraints. BFMT enables full tree-path construction from any tree depth using a single function evaluation, drastically reducing computational overhead while providing critical foresight for sequential sampling. By enabling dynamic transition time steps scheduling, BFMT efficiently allocates its sampling budget, smoothly transitioning from broad global exploration to fine-grained local refinement of high-utility modes discovered through exploration. Extensive experiments and ablations across diverse search and alignment tasks demonstrate that BFMT substantially outperforms baseline approaches.

</details>


### [560] [CoFEND: A Cross-Modal Fusion End-to-End Network for Cold-Start Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02928)
*Di Wu,Hongyi Sun,Haichao Xu,Jia Chen,Zhong Chen,Jie Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CMF-ELN is a novel cross-modal fusion end-to-end learning network for cold-start DDI prediction that leverages multimodal knowledge graphs, a four-channel graph autoencoder, and a two-stage interpretability scheme to outperform existing methods in accuracy and mechanistic interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cold-start DDI prediction for new drugs is critical to minimize adverse reactions, but existing methods capture similarity between new and known drugs incompletely due to three limitations: (1) partial relationships and mechanisms overlook cross-modal information; (2) similarity computation is conducted separately and offline, misaligned with prediction; (3) interpretability analyses are single-modality and focus only on perpetrator drugs, ignoring victim drug susceptibility.

Method: CMF-ELN has three components: (1) leveraging diverse multimodal info to construct four types of drug-centered knowledge graphs for comprehensive similarity modeling via reconstruction-based supervision; (2) a four-channel graph autoencoder for fusing cross-modal similarity within an end-to-end framework; (3) a two-stage interpretability scheme to localize key factors for both perpetrator and victim drugs.

Result: Extensive experiments on two real datasets show CMF-ELN achieves significantly higher prediction accuracy and more comprehensive interpretability of mechanisms compared to peers.

Conclusion: The proposed CMF-ELN effectively addresses cold-start DDI prediction by fusing cross-modal information end-to-end and providing dual-sided interpretability (perpetrator & victim drugs), outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Cold-start drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction for new drugs is critical for minimizing unexpected adverse drug reactions. The key challenge is to capture similarity between new and known drugs. However, such similarity is closely associated with complex relationships and mechanisms among drugs, enzymes, transporters, molecular structures, and other biomedical entities. Existing methods have three limitations in capturing such similarity: (1) only partial relationships and mechanisms are considered, which overlooks cross-modal information and yields incomplete or biased similarity modeling; (2) similarity computation between new and known drugs is conducted separately across modalities and performed offline for cold-start DDI prediction, leading to misalignment between similarity computation and DDI prediction; and (3) existing interpretability analyses are typically single-modality and focus primarily on key determinants of the perpetrator drug, while the underlying causes of susceptibility for the victim drug are seldom investigated. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Cross-Modal-Fused End-to-End Learning Network (CMF-ELN) with three components. First, diverse multimodal information is leveraged to construct four types of drug-centered knowledge graphs, enabling comprehensive similarity modeling under reconstruction-based supervision. Second, a four-channel graph autoencoder is designed to fuse cross-modal similarity within an end-to-end learning framework. Finally, a two-stage interpretability scheme is devised to precisely localize key factors for both perpetrator and victim drugs. Extensive experiments on two real datasets demonstrate that CMF-ELN achieves significantly higher prediction accuracy and more comprehensive interpretability of mechanisms than its peers.

</details>


### [561] [CuBAS: Information Geometric Curvature-Based Adaptive Sampling for Supervised Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03145)
*Alexandre L. M. Levada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CuBAS is a curvature-based adaptive sampling method that identifies the most informative training points by analyzing local geometric curvature of the data manifold, outperforming random and uncertainty-based sampling across 60+ benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training set informativeness is as important as size, yet most sampling strategies ignore the intrinsic geometry of the data distribution.

Method: CuBAS views labeled data as a statistical manifold under a q-state Potts MRF model, computes curvature at each vertex via observed Fisher information ratio on a k-NN graph, and selects points from both low-curvature (homogeneous clusters) and high-curvature (decision boundary) regions.

Result: Consistent and statistically significant improvements over random sampling and uncertainty-based baselines across 60+ benchmark datasets, various labeling budgets, and classifier architectures.

Conclusion: CuBAS is computationally efficient (linear in graph edges), theoretically grounded in differential geometry, and interpretable via the local shape operator of the data manifold.

Abstract: The informativeness of a training set is as consequential as its size, yet most sampling strategies remain agnostic to the intrinsic geometry of the data distribution. We introduce CuBAS (Curvature-Based Adaptive Sampling), an information-geometric framework for adaptive data selection in supervised classification, grounded in the q-state Potts Markov random field (MRF) model. The central insight is that a labeled dataset can be viewed as a statistical manifold, on which local curvature, estimated via the ratio of second to first-order observed Fisher information, faithfully encodes the geometric complexity of the data distribution. We construct a k-nearest-neighbor graph over the labeled data and derive a closed-form curvature score at each vertex from the Potts sufficient statistics. This curvature signal partitions the graph into two complementary regimes: low-curvature regions, corresponding to smooth, homogeneous clusters, and high-curvature regions, concentrated around decision boundaries that are disproportionately informative for classification. By selecting nodes from both regimes, CuBAS constructs compact yet maximally informative training subsets. Empirical evaluation across more than 60 benchmark datasets demonstrates consistent and statistically significant improvements over random sampling and uncertainty-based baselines, across a wide range of labeling budgets and classifier architectures. CuBAS is computationally efficient (linear in the number of k-NN graph edges), theoretically grounded in the differential geometry of statistical manifolds, and interpretable in terms of the local shape operator of the data manifold.

</details>


### [562] [In-span learning: adapting reduced-order models using their own predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02937)
*Amirpasha Hedayat,Laura Balzano,Karthik Duraisamy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper discovers that reduced-order models can be improved by learning from their own predictions (in-span adaptation) through incremental SVD with forgetting, which reweights and realigns the basis without changing the subspace, enabling better absorption of future corrections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduced-order models lose accuracy when online dynamics drift beyond training data; existing adaptive methods rely on external (out-of-span) information, but a complementary in-span channel within the current reduced subspace was previously unexploited.

Method: Stream the model's own predictions through an incremental singular-value decomposition (SVD) with forgetting to create a trajectory-informed spectral preconditioner that reweights and realigns the basis toward visited modes without changing the subspace.

Result: Confirmed on a 3D spiral, viscous Burgers, and Fisher-KPP dynamics that in-span adaptation enables models to absorb future out-of-span corrections more effectively.

Conclusion: In-span learning reveals that model-generated trajectories contain more usable information than previously recognized, and can be viewed as a dynamical-systems analogue of in-context learning, suggesting a new principle for computational science.

Abstract: Reduced-order models compress high-dimensional dynamics into low-dimensional representations that can be evaluated rapidly, but they lose accuracy when online dynamics drift beyond the training data. Adaptive methods address this by updating the subspace online with external, out-of-span information, such as full-order corrections or sensor snapshots. We discovered that a complementary and previously unexploited in-span adaptation channel exists within the current reduced subspace. By streaming the model's own predictions through an incremental singular-value decomposition with forgetting, we obtain a trajectory-informed spectral preconditioner, in which the subspace is unchanged but the basis is reweighted and realigned toward the modes visited by the dynamics. This enables the model to absorb future out-of-span corrections more effectively. We expose aspects of this mechanism on a three-dimensional spiral and confirm it on viscous Burgers and Fisher-KPP dynamics. We also discuss how in-span learning can be viewed as a dynamical-systems analogue of in-context learning. More broadly, in-span learning suggests a new principle for computational science, revealing that model-generated trajectories contain more usable information than previously recognized.

</details>


### [563] [Towards Diverse and Comprehensive Benchmarks for Mutual Information Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03487)
*Alberto Foresti,Ivan Butakov,Alexander Tolmachev,Giulio Franzese,Alexey Frolov,Pietro Michiardi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a comprehensive benchmarking framework for mutual information estimation, evaluating three estimator classes (non-parametric, discriminative, generative) across diverse test suites, finding no universal winner and identifying specific failure modes for each.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MI estimation benchmarks evaluate on simplified, low-dimensional distributions; realistic data performance remains unexplored.

Method: A copula-theoretic benchmarking framework with two test families: copula-first (varying MI, dimensionality, marginal complexity) and marginals-first (real image data with controlled dependencies).

Result: No universal winner; each estimator class outperforms others under specific setups; identified fundamental estimation barriers.

Conclusion: New tests better stress specific limitations of MI estimators; framework subsumes existing benchmarks.

Abstract: Mutual information (MI) estimation is a central problem in machine learning and statistics; however, existing benchmarks typically evaluate estimators on simplified, low-dimensional distributions, leaving their performance on complex, realistic data largely unexplored. We address this gap with a comprehensive benchmarking framework grounded in a unified copula-theoretic perspective that subsumes existing benchmarks as special cases. Within this framework, we propose two complementary families of tests: a copula-first family that systematically varies ground-truth MI, dimensionality, and marginal complexity using synthetic and flow-based transformations; and a marginals-first family that couples real-world image data with controlled dependency structures, extending the classic same-class-pairing paradigm. We use this suite to extensively evaluate three classes of estimators: non-parametric, discriminative, and generative. Contrary to prevailing assumptions, our results indicate that there is no universal winner: each category can systematically outperform all other estimators under specific setups. By analyzing these cases, we identify fundamental estimation barriers and propose new tests that more effectively stress these specific limitations. We share the open source code at https://github.com/VanessB/mutinfo.

</details>


### [564] [Missingness as Signal: Channel-Independent Spectrogram Learning for Clinical Time Series Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02938)
*Soyeon Park,Charmgil Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CISM is a channel-independent spectrogram framework with a missingness stream that improves clinical time series prediction on MIMIC-IV by modeling observation patterns as informative signals, achieving top performance in AUROC, AUPRC, and F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical time series in ICUs are challenging due to heterogeneous physiological variables and informative missingness—where missing data reflects clinical decisions and patient severity rather than noise—motivating the need to model missing patterns as predictive signals.

Method: CISM converts each clinical variable into a variable-wise time-frequency spectrogram, preserves variable identity via aligned encoding, and integrates an explicit missingness stream aligned with the spectrogram representation.

Result: On MIMIC-IV in-hospital mortality prediction, CISM achieves highest mean AUROC (0.7225), AUPRC (0.3308), and F1 (0.3808) compared to time series, missingness-aware, vision, and time-frequency baselines. Ablations confirm observation patterns and pixel-level mask injection provide informative gains.

Conclusion: Modeling observation patterns as structured signals is crucial for clinical time series prediction; the aligned missingness stream offers complementary predictive value.

Abstract: Clinical time series prediction in intensive care units remains challenging due to heterogeneous physiological variables and informative missingness. The presence or absence of a measurement can reflect clinical decisions and patient severity, and thus missingness can serve as a predictive signal rather than a simple data artifact. This work presents CISM, a Channel-Independent Spectrogram framework with a Missingness stream for clinical multivariate time series prediction. CISM converts each clinical variable into a variable-wise time-frequency spectrogram, preserves variable identity through variable-aligned encoding, and aligns an explicit missingness stream with the spectrogram representation. Experiments on an in-hospital mortality task derived from MIMIC-IV show that CISM achieves the highest mean AUROC (0.7225), AUPRC (0.3308), and F1 (0.3808) among the compared time series, missingness-aware, vision, and time-frequency baselines. Ablation studies further show that observation patterns provide a meaningful informative signal. Pixel-level mask injection improves performance over plain spectrogram inputs and recovers much of this predictive value. The aligned missingness stream contributes a further, complementary gain in both AUROC and AUPRC. These results highlight the importance of modeling observation patterns as structured signals in clinical time series prediction.

</details>


### [565] [What Does a Discrete Diffusion Model Learn?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05381)
*Rodrigo Casado Noguerales,Bernhard Schölkopf,Thomas Hofmann,Aran Raoufi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper rigorously proves that discrete diffusion models learn a single object (jump rates) expressed in three equivalent coordinates—denoiser, bridge plug-in, and score ratio—and derives the Oracle Distance theorem showing negative ELBO equals path KL to the oracle reverse process, with irreducible cost being the rate of information loss about clean data. It unifies MDM, UDM, SEDD, and GIDD as special cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve confusion about what discrete diffusion models actually learn by providing a unified theoretical framework showing that denoiser, score ratio, and bridge plug-in predictors are the same object in different coordinates, and to derive exact relationships between ELBO and the true reverse process.

Method: Rigorous derivation of continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) ELBO for any noising process with boundary terms; proof of the Oracle Distance theorem; algebraic identification of three exact coordinate parameterizations (denoiser, cavity/bridge plug-in, score) under token-factorizing noise; closed-form conversions among coordinates.

Result: Negative ELBO equals data entropy plus path KL from oracle to learned process (Oracle Distance theorem); optimal optimizer is conditional expectation of true reverse jump rate; irreducible cost is -d/dt I(Z0; Zt); denoiser and cavity coincide for masked but not uniform diffusion; denoiser parameterization causes uniform ELBO divergence at initialization while bridge plug-in stays finite.

Conclusion: All discrete diffusion losses optimize the same underlying object; coordinate choice matters for practical training dynamics (initialization stability); the framework provides exact calibration for ELBO implementations.

Abstract: What does a discrete diffusion model learn: a denoiser, a score ratio, or a bridge plug-in predictor? At the level of jump rates, these are one object in different coordinates, and reading a neural network in the wrong coordinate changes the process being trained and sampled. Starting with a rigorous derivation of the continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) ELBO for any noising process, boundary terms included, we prove the \emph{Oracle Distance} theorem: the negative ELBO is exactly equal to the data entropy plus the path KL from the oracle reverse process to the learned one, not merely a bound. Its unique optimizer is therefore the conditional expectation of the true reverse jump rate given the current noisy state, and its irreducible cost is the rate at which the forward process $Z_t$ destroys information about the clean data $Z_0$, $-\tfrac{d}{dt}I(Z_0; Z_t)$, so every noising process shares the same best achievable negative ELBO: the data entropy. For sequences with token-factorizing noise, the oracle projection yields three exact coordinates for the optimizer: denoiser, cavity (bridge plug-in), and score, with closed-form conversions among them. This framework identifies which law each loss in the literature actually optimizes, recovering MDM, UDM, SEDD, and GIDD as special cases; explains why denoiser and cavity coincide for masked diffusion but not for uniform diffusion; proves that a denoiser parameterization makes the uniform ELBO diverge at initialization while the bridge plug-in stays finite; and calibrates ELBO implementations exactly at initialization. Every identity is verified numerically, without approximation, on an exactly solvable model.

</details>


### [566] [A Precedent-Guided Co-Scientist for Side-Effect-Aware Drug Redesign](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02944)
*Yujin Kim,Charmgil Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRECEDE is a precedent-guided AI system that redesigns drugs to reduce specific side effects while maintaining therapeutic effects, using biomedical knowledge and human oversight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable side-effect-aware drug redesign that is grounded in existing evidence and precedents, rather than relying on isolated molecular generation without pharmacological context.

Method: PRECEDE uses an LLM orchestrator with explicit policies and human-review checkpoints to coordinate reasoning over drug-side-effect associations, biomedical knowledge graphs, and safety-driven optimization precedents.

Result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract; appears to be a proposal/position paper describing the workflow rather than reporting empirical results.

Conclusion: PRECEDE positions itself as a human-supervised AI-for-science workflow where hypotheses remain auditable, falsifiable, and bounded by prior pharmacology.

Abstract: We propose PRECEDE, a precedent-guided co-scientist for side-effect-aware drug redesign that revises a parent compound to mitigate a specified side effect while preserving therapeutic function. Rather than isolated molecular generation, PRECEDE frames redesign as evidence-grounded reasoning over drug--side-effect associations, biomedical knowledge graphs, and precedents of safety-driven optimization, coordinated by an LLM orchestrator with explicit policies and human-review checkpoints. We position PRECEDE as a human-supervised AI-for-science workflow in which hypotheses remain auditable, falsifiable, and bounded by prior pharmacology.

</details>


### [567] [Individual Parameters in Weight-Sparse Transformers Appear Interpretable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02964)
*Arnau Marin-Llobet,Stefan Heimersheim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an automated LLM pipeline to generate and verify human-readable descriptions of when individual weights matter in transformers, finding that 12-31% of weights in sparse transformers have single, generalizable descriptions, with sparse transformers being more interpretable than dense ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Past mechanistic interpretability work focuses on specific behaviors and sub-distributions, but components can have different functions across the full input distribution. The authors want to understand whether individual weights can be interpreted globally across the entire training distribution by characterizing when they matter.

Method: The authors build an automated LLM pipeline that writes short, human-readable descriptions of when a weight matters (i.e., inputs where ablating the weight changes predictions). The descriptions are verified on held-out text, and weights are only credited if the description generalizes. The method is tested across two sparse and two dense transformers.

Result: 12-31% of weights in sparse transformers have a single short description that identifies what the weight is used for. Sparse transformers show higher interpretability than dense transformers, and this gap widens when unreliable descriptions are filtered out.

Conclusion: A meaningful fraction of sparse transformer weights can be interpreted with generalizable descriptions, suggesting global understanding of individual weights is feasible, especially in sparse architectures.

Abstract: A central goal of mechanistic interpretability is to understand how neural networks work and what each individual component does. Dominant circuit-finding approaches focus on a specific behavior and reverse-engineer the role of components on the associated sub-distribution. However, past work has shown that components can have different functions that are active on different subsets of the input distribution. In this work we ask whether a single weight can be understood globally across the full training distribution by characterizing when it matters (the inputs on which ablating it changes the model's predictions). We introduce an automated LLM pipeline that writes a short, human-readable description of when a weight matters and verifies it on held-out text, crediting a weight only if its description generalizes. Across two sparse and two dense transformers, the fraction of weights that are interpretable (in this sense) is higher in sparse transformers than in dense ones, a gap that widens once unreliable descriptions are discarded. Our results show that a meaningful fraction of a sparse transformer model's weights can be interpreted: 12 to 31% of weights have a single short description that identifies what the weight is used for.

</details>


### [568] [Rank-Order N-of-M Codes for Sparse Distributed Memory: Disentangling Representation and Learning Effects in Noise Robustness Against Contemporary Neuromorphic Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02967)
*Joy Bose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates rank-order N-of-M encoding as an alternative to threshold-binary encoding in Sparse Distributed Memory, showing it outperforms StandardSDM by up to 13.4 percentage points at saturation and that robustness gains primarily come from interaction with MAX-Hebbian learning rather than the encoder alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models struggle as continual learning systems, prompting renewed interest in Sparse Distributed Memory (SDM) as explicit online episodic memory. The recent CALM architecture identified threshold-binary encoding as an open design question, motivating this investigation of rank-order N-of-M encoding as a potential improvement.

Method: The paper validates a faithful reimplementation of RankOrderSDM vs WheelSDM (cosine similarity 1.0000 across 10 seeds), conducts multi-seed capacity and BER robustness experiments, and performs component-level energy analysis at four-bit precision.

Result: RankOrderSDM outperforms StandardSDM by 13.4 percentage points at saturation (scaled config) and 0.8 percentage points (published scale). Robustness gains primarily come from encoder+MAX-Hebbian interaction, not encoder alone. Rank-order encoding uses half the energy of SI-LIF neurons at four-bit precision.

Conclusion: The study disentangles which components of rank-order SDM provide measurable benefits for memory-augmented AI, offering practical architectural guidance for systems like CALM.

Abstract: Large language models remain limited as continual learning systems, motivating renewed interest in Sparse Distributed Memory (SDM) as an explicit online episodic memory. CALM (Nechesov and Ruponen, 2025) identifies its threshold-binary encoder as an open design question. This paper evaluates rank-order N-of-M encoding (Furber et al., 2007) as an alternative. We make three contributions. First, a faithful reimplementation validates the published architecture by confirming exact equivalence between WheelSDM and RankOrderSDM (cosine similarity 1.0000 across 10 seeds) and reproducing the documented divergence of RDLIF neurons under interference. Second, multi-seed capacity experiments show RankOrderSDM outperforming StandardSDM by 13.4 percentage points at saturation in the scaled configuration and by 0.8 percentage points at the published architecture scale. Third, BER robustness experiments disentangle representation and learning effects, showing that the large robustness gain arises primarily from the interaction of rank-order encoding with MAX-Hebbian learning, while the encoder alone provides only a small advantage under matched learning conditions. Experiments on GloVe-100 embeddings confirm this small but consistent encoding benefit on real structured data, whereas sentence embeddings exhibit a ceiling effect at low memory load. A secondary analysis shows that idealized rank-order encoding requires half the component-level encoding energy of SpikingMamba's SI-LIF neurons at four-bit precision, although decoder costs dominate overall system energy. These results identify which components of the original rank-order SDM architecture provide measurable benefits for contemporary memory-augmented AI systems, offering practical guidance for architectures such as CALM.

</details>


### [569] [MABLE: Masked Autoencoding with Bi-Lipschitz Decoding for Embeddings and Graph Metric Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02990)
*Yaniv Shulman,Shaghayegh Akbarpour,Jack B. Muir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MABLE is a self-supervised framework that learns node and graph embeddings from large, heterogeneous graphs using masked autoencoding with bi-Lipschitz decoding, demonstrated on geospatial mineral-exploration data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To learn effective node and graph embeddings from large, heterogeneous graphs in a self-supervised manner without requiring labeled data or hard-negative selection, particularly for geospatial mineral-exploration applications.

Method: MABLE combines masked reconstruction with fixed cosine-similarity losses for augmentation alignment, uses a bi-Lipschitz feature decoder to tie low-dimensional reconstruction to feature similarity, and employs Lipschitz-controlled pooling for stability under perturbations.

Result: On local copper and regional Arabian Shield studies, MABLE embeddings provide complementary downstream signal and produce coherent embedding-derived layers for hypothesis generation.

Conclusion: MABLE offers a robust self-supervised approach for graph metric learning that works without learned discriminators or hard-negative selection, and generates useful representations for geospatial hypothesis generation.

Abstract: We propose MABLE (Masked Autoencoding with Bi-Lipschitz Decoding for Embeddings and Graph Metric Learning), a self-supervised framework for learning node and graph embeddings from large, heterogeneous graphs, demonstrated here on geospatial mineral-exploration data. MABLE combines masked reconstruction with fixed cosine-similarity losses that align matched augmented views while keeping unpaired embeddings well spread. A bi-Lipschitz feature decoder ties a low-dimensional reconstruction component of each node embedding to feature similarity, while matched-node consistency shapes the remaining context used by graph pooling. Lipschitz-controlled pooling helps stabilize graph-level representations under perturbations of retained node embeddings, while augmentation alignment trains robustness to masking, node dropping, and sampling variation. Across local copper and regional Arabian Shield studies, MABLE embeddings provide complementary downstream signal and produce coherent embedding-derived layers for hypothesis generation without learned discriminators or hard-negative selection.

</details>


### [570] [Transfer Learning in High-dimensional Ising Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03005)
*Joonho Kim,Seyoung Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Trans-Ising is a transfer learning method for high-dimensional Ising model estimation that uses source screening and a two-stage procedure to effectively leverage auxiliary binary datasets, achieving better accuracy than target-only or naive pooling approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional Ising model estimation suffers from limited target sample sizes, and existing methods struggle to effectively utilize auxiliary binary datasets when their relevance is unknown, risking negative transfer.

Method: The method combines a loss-based source screening rule (using held-out target pseudolikelihood) with a two-stage estimation: initial pooled nodewise ℓ1-regularized logistic regression, followed by a target-only correction step with a folded-concave penalty.

Result: Established fixed-node ℓ2 and ℓ1 error bounds, exact graph selection consistency, and conditional consistency of the screening rule; simulations and real-data analyses show lower estimation errors than target-only estimation and naive pooling.

Conclusion: Trans-Ising effectively addresses negative transfer and improves estimation accuracy in high-dimensional Ising models when auxiliary data of unknown relevance is available.

Abstract: In high-dimensional Ising model estimation, target sample sizes are often limited, and effectively using auxiliary binary datasets of unknown relevance remains challenging. To address this, we propose Trans-Ising, a transfer learning method that combines a loss-based source screening rule with a two-stage estimation procedure. The method first identifies informative auxiliary sources using held-out target pseudolikelihood to prevent negative transfer. It then computes an initial estimator via pooled nodewise $\ell_1$-regularized logistic regression, followed by a target-only correction step using a folded-concave penalty. Theoretically, we establish fixed-node $\ell_2$ and $\ell_1$ error bounds, exact graph selection consistency, and the conditional consistency of the screening rule. Through extensive simulations and real-data analyses, we demonstrate that Trans-Ising achieves lower estimation errors than both target-only estimation and naive data pooling.

</details>


### [571] [Back to Basics: Improving Molecular Understanding in LLMs via SMILES-Graph Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03007)
*Wenda Wang,Jinjia Feng,Zhewei Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MolBasic is a structure-first framework for molecular LLMs that improves structural understanding via SMILES-Graph translation, using a multi-level perception benchmark and progressive Chain-of-Thought learning, leading to robust gains on property prediction and optimization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current molecular LLMs achieve strong downstream performance but fail at basic structure recognition, contradicting the chemistry principle that structure determines function, indicating they don't truly capture molecular graphs from canonical SMILES.

Method: MolBasic employs a SMILES-Graph translation as the core task to align sequential and topological representations, a multi-level structure perception benchmark, and a progressive learning scheme with standardized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to guide models from structure acquisition to higher-level reasoning.

Result: MolBasic substantially improves structural understanding and yields robust gains on downstream tasks including property prediction and objective optimization.

Conclusion: A structure-first paradigm is effective for molecular LLMs, supporting the chemistry principle that structure determines function, and leads to better downstream task performance.

Abstract: Recent advances in molecular large language models have led to strong performance on molecular understanding and generation tasks, yet these gains often come without reliable structural grounding. In particular, existing approaches conflict with the chemistry principle that structure determines function: despite their downstream success, current molecular LLMs perform poorly on basic structure recognition, suggesting that they fail to capture molecular graphs from canonical SMILES. To remedy this, we propose MolBasic, a structure-first framework that strengthens structural comprehension via SMILES-Graph translation. MolBasic is built around a multi-level structure perception benchmark, where bidirectional SMILES-Graph conversion serves as the core task to align sequential and topological representations. On top of this foundation, we employ a progressive learning scheme with a standardized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to steer models from structure acquisition toward higher-level molecular reasoning. Experiments show that MolBasic substantially improves structural understanding and yields robust gains on downstream tasks, including property prediction and objective optimization, supporting our structure-first paradigm.

</details>


### [572] [Do ECG Foundation Models Transfer to Rare Cardiac Diseases? Evidence from Brugada Syndrome Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03009)
*Beatrice Zanchi,Giuliana Monachino,Alvise Dei Rossi,Luigi Fiorillo,Georgia Sarquella-Brugada,Giulio Conte,Francesca Dalia Faraci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Foundation models pre-trained on physiological data help with training stability (especially for large models) but do not provide meaningful transferable clinical knowledge for rare disease detection like Brugada syndrome. Their apparent benefits are mechanical (optimization) rather than semantic (clinical understanding).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether FM pre-training on large-scale unlabeled physiological data provides genuine clinical generalization benefits beyond improved optimization for rare electrocardiographic phenotypes, specifically Brugada syndrome.

Method: Evaluated nine publicly available ECG foundation models on two independent cohorts (BrSwiss and HUCA) under three training strategies (from-scratch, linear probing, full fine-tuning), including 3% data ablation and zero-shot cross-site transfer experiments.

Result: Pre-training was necessary for high-capacity architectures to converge (AUC gain up to 0.411) but gave no significant gain for compact architectures. Best fine-tuned FM (AUC 0.962) only marginally exceeded supervised baseline (AUC 0.932, p=0.091). Data-efficiency advantage did not replicate across cohorts. Zero-shot cross-site transfer all approached chance level.

Conclusion: FM pre-training provides optimization stability rather than transferable clinical knowledge for Brugada syndrome detection. Large-scale pre-training does not inherently encode clinically meaningful representations; model architecture and data-domain alignment are more critical.

Abstract: Background: Foundation models (FMs) trained on large-scale unlabeled physiological data have emerged as a promising paradigm for medical artificial intelligence. Their ability to capture clinically meaningful, transferable representations for rare diseases remains largely unproven. This study investigates whether FM pre-training provides genuine clinical generalization benefits beyond improved optimization for rare electrocardiographic (ECG) phenotypes. Methods: We systematically evaluated nine publicly available ECG FMs for Brugada syndrome detection on the BrSwiss cohort (294 patients, 87 cases) and the independent external HUCA cohort (363 patients, 76 cases), under three strategies (from-scratch training, linear probing, full fine-tuning) across several configurations, including a 3% data ablation and zero-shot cross-site transfers. Results: Pre-training was necessary for high-capacity architectures unable to converge from scratch (AUC gain up to 0.411, p < 0.05), but gave no significant gain for compact architectures already converged on labeled data alone. On full BrSwiss, the best fine-tuned FM (ECG-CPC, AUC = 0.962) only marginally exceeded the strongest supervised baseline (ECG-CPC from scratch, AUC = 0.932; p = 0.091). At matched training-set size, the data-efficiency advantage on BrSwiss-3% (AUC gain = 0.055, p < 0.01) did not replicate on HUCA. Under zero-shot cross-site transfer, FM-based pipelines did not generalize better than supervised baselines, all approaching chance-level performance. Conclusion: For Brugada syndrome detection, FM pre-training is mechanical rather than semantic, providing optimization stability rather than transferable clinical knowledge. These findings challenge the assumption that large-scale pre-training inherently encodes clinically meaningful representations, highlighting the central role of model architecture and data-domain alignment.

</details>


### [573] [Can Model Merging Improve Aggregation in DiLoCo?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03011)
*Stefan Horoi,Benjamin Thérien,Guy Wolf,Eugene Belilovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper connects model merging techniques (task arithmetic) with distributed optimization methods like local SGD/DiLoCo. They identify Iso-C as an effective aggregation method for DiLoCo, and propose IsoLoCo (Iso-C + Nesterov momentum), which significantly outperforms DiLoCo in low-communication distributed training, especially with many workers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication-efficient distributed learning methods (local SGD, DiLoCo) degrade in performance as the number of workers or local steps grows. The authors seek to bridge model merging and distributed optimization to improve aggregation in these settings.

Method: (1) Establish analogy between pseudo-gradient aggregation in local SGD/DiLoCo and task arithmetic-based model merging. (2) Evaluate state-of-the-art merging methods in distributed optimization; identify Iso-C as promising. (3) Propose IsoLoCo, which equips Iso-C with Nesterov momentum for distributed training.

Result: Iso-C aggregation outperforms simple pseudo-gradient averaging and even momentum-based DiLoCo. IsoLoCo significantly outperforms DiLoCo across varying numbers of workers, model sizes, and inner step counts, with the gap widening as workers increase.

Conclusion: Merging-inspired aggregation is an effective strategy for low-communication distributed training. IsoLoCo provides a new state-of-the-art in this setting.

Abstract: Model merging techniques, which aggregate independently finetuned models into one to combine their capabilities, have become a topic of significant interest in recent years, with a broad array of methods having been proposed to tackle this problem. Simultaneously, an emerging trend in distributed learning has been the use of methods such as local SGD and DiLoCo, which greatly reduce communication costs by periodically aggregating the independently trained local models. However, these communication-efficient methods have been shown to degrade in performance relative to the FLOP-matched data-parallel gold standard as the number of independent local models grows and as the number of local training steps before global communication is increased. In this work, we draw an explicit analogy between the pseudo-gradient aggregation step in local SGD/DiLoCo and task arithmetic-based model merging, establishing a straightforward way to utilize merging methods in the context of distributed optimization. We then evaluate multiple state-of-the-art model merging methods in this setting and identify one method in particular, Iso-C, as a promising approach for improving DiLoCo. We find that DiLoCo SGD with Iso-C aggregation outperforms not only simple pseudo-gradient averaging but even the momentum-based DiLoCo, despite lacking a momentum mechanism itself. Building on this finding, we propose IsoLoCo, which adapts Iso-C for distributed training by equipping it with Nesterov momentum. Our empirical evaluations on language model pre-training across varying numbers of local workers show that IsoLoCo significantly outperforms DiLoCo, with the gap between them widening as the number of workers increases. This advantage remains present across model sizes and inner step counts, confirming that merging-inspired aggregation is an effective strategy for low-communication distributed training.

</details>


### [574] [Out-of-distribution Neural Inference in Dynamical Ising Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03039)
*Yuan-Bin Zhu,Shuang Qiao,Shi-Ju Ran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural networks for inverse problems in physics often learn architecture-dependent statistical priors rather than true physical rules, leading to misleading out-of-distribution robustness — demonstrated in reconstructing Ising model interaction graphs where Transformers preserve link density and CNNs exploit majority no-link class.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether neural networks' out-of-distribution performance in physical inverse problems reflects genuine transferable physical rule learning or is confounded by architecture-dependent biases.

Method: Controlled inverse problem of reconstructing interaction graphs of a kinetic Ising model from Glauber magnetization trajectories; compared convolutional, graph, Transformer, and hybrid architectures; employed edge-population diagnostics to analyze statistical strategies under topology and temperature shifts.

Result: Transformer-based models preserve link density of training ensemble; convolutional models collapse toward sparse/no-link predictions by exploiting majority no-link class; high in-distribution accuracy and apparent OOD robustness do not imply learned dynamics-to-structure rules.

Conclusion: Standard data-driven learning in physical inverse problems can produce architecture-dependent statistical priors that masquerade as robust rule learning, motivating rule-guided principles for ML-assisted scientific discovery.

Abstract: Neural networks are increasingly used to infer hidden physical structure from dynamical observations, yet it remains unclear whether their out-of-distribution performance reflects transferable physical rule learning. We address this question in a controlled inverse problem: reconstructing interaction graphs of a kinetic Ising model from Glauber magnetization trajectories. Across convolutional, graph, Transformer, and hybrid architectures, we find that data-driven training produces distinct and reproducible statistical strategies under topology and temperature shifts. Edge-population diagnostics reveal that Transformer-based models tend to preserve the link density of the training ensemble, whereas convolutional models can collapse toward sparse- or no-link predictions that appear out-of-distribution stable by exploiting the majority no-link class. Thus, high in-distribution accuracy and apparent out-of-distribution robustness do not necessarily imply a learned dynamics-to-structure rule. Instead, neural reconstruction can be governed by architecture-dependent statistical priors. Our results identify a concrete failure mode of standard data-driven learning in physical inverse problems and motivate rule-guided principles for machine-learning-assisted scientific discovery.

</details>


### [575] [OmniFocus: Query-Guided Modality-Balanced Token Compression for Omni-Modal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03050)
*Shijie Cao,Qingyu Zhang,Boxi Yu,Yuzhong Zhang,Boxi Cao,Yaojie Lu,Hongyu Lin,Xianpei Han,Le Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OmniFocus is a training-free, query-guided token compression method for OmniLLMs that independently compresses audio and video tokens, preserving modality-specific evidence while maintaining alignment, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs (e.g., 59.40 accuracy with 1.38× prefill speedup at 25% token retention on DailyOmni).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OmniLLMs generate large token sequences under audio-visual inputs, leading to substantial inference costs. Existing compression methods rely on unimodal guidance, overlooking temporal locality of query-relevant evidence and assuming temporally aligned information density between modalities, causing modality bias.

Method: A training-free, query-guided token compression method that performs independent importance estimation for video and audio modalities, enabling a modality-symmetric compression design that preserves modality-specific salient evidence while maintaining audio-visual alignment.

Result: On Qwen2.5-Omni models across four benchmarks, OmniFocus maintains strong performance at low token retention ratios, outperforms baselines at 25% token retention, and achieves 59.40 accuracy with 1.38× prefill speedup on DailyOmni.

Conclusion: OmniFocus provides a practical accuracy-efficiency trade-off for OmniLLMs, mitigating modality bias from unimodal-guided compression while preserving both audio and visual salient evidence without training.

Abstract: Omni modal large language models (OmniLLMs) have attracted wide attention for their ability to jointly process audio and video, but they generate large token sequences under audio-visual inputs, leading to substantial inference cost. Existing audio-visual token compression methods often rely on unimodal guidance, overlooking the temporal locality of query-relevant evidence in audio-visual inputs and implicitly assuming that the two modalities share a temporally aligned information density distribution. We propose \textbf{OmniFocus}, a training-free query-guided token compression method for OmniLLMs that performs independent importance estimation for video and audio, enabling a modality-symmetric compression design that preserves modality-specific salient evidence while maintaining audio-visual alignment, thereby mitigating the modality bias issue that can arise from unimodal-guided compression. Experiments on the Qwen2.5-Omni model family across four audio-visual benchmarks show that OmniFocus maintains strong compressed performance at low token retention ratios and outperforms existing baselines on several major benchmark scores at 25\% token retention. On DailyOmni with Qwen2.5-Omni-7B at 25\% token retention, OmniFocus maintains 59.40 accuracy while delivering up to 1.38$\times$ prefill speedup relative to the full-token baseline, highlighting a favorable practical accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

</details>


### [576] [SHiPPO: Recurrent Memory with Transported Polynomial Projections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03055)
*Tomoya Mizuguchi,Bum Jun Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHiPPO is a transported projection-memory prior that extends HiPPO by moving coefficient memories into a dynamic channel frame via Sylvester dynamics, enabling selective state-space models to better handle order-sensitive memory signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HiPPO provides memory semantics through polynomial projections but operates in fixed channel coordinates, while modern selective SSMs rely on token-dependent control and channel interaction. The gap is that fixed-coordinate memory cannot capture order-sensitive changes to already-written memory.

Method: SHiPPO transports the approximation family and channel metric along a right-transport path, preserving the left online-memory operator while adding right-action transport. It derives a restricted group-local realization with controller-compatible right actions, exponential-adjusted updates, block-affine scan, and recurrent decoding.

Result: Controlled diagnostics show larger current-token write rank improves prediction error but cannot recover order-sensitive memory changes. Transported-memory variants recover this signal, which disappears when transport is removed. A finite-field associative-recall diagnostic provides complementary evidence.

Conclusion: SHiPPO is a mechanistically grounded transported-memory prior with evidence focused on memory mechanisms rather than broad sequence-modeling dominance.

Abstract: HiPPO gives recurrent states memory semantics as coefficients of online polynomial projections, but in fixed channel coordinates. Modern selective SSMs, by contrast, rely on token-dependent control and channel interaction. We introduce SHiPPO (Sylvester HiPPO), a transported projection-memory prior that lifts HiPPO coefficient memories into a moving channel frame. For any fixed or realized right-transport path, SHiPPO transports the approximation family and channel metric together; conditional on that path, the state is ordinary HiPPO in a tied moving frame and follows Sylvester coefficient dynamics, preserving the left online-memory operator while adding right-action transport. For selective-SSM execution, we derive a restricted group-local realization with controller-compatible right actions, exponential-adjusted updates, exact block-affine scan, and recurrent decoding. We also give a simultaneous-reducibility criterion identifying when right transports collapse to static mixing plus independent scalar or blockwise banks. Controlled diagnostics show that larger current-token write rank improves ordinary prediction error but cannot recover order-sensitive changes to already-written memory; transported-memory variants recover this signal, which disappears when the transport pathway is removed. A finite-field associative-recall diagnostic with interleaved bindings, operations, and queries provides complementary autoregressive evidence while leaving the preferred right-action realization open. Taken together, these results support SHiPPO as a mechanistically grounded transported-memory prior, with evidence focused on memory mechanisms rather than broad sequence-modeling dominance.

</details>


### [577] [LACE-SVD: Loss-Aware SVD with Cumulative Error Correction for LLM Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03057)
*Zhuowen Liu,Longkun Hao,Shiyu Feng,Xiaowen Chang,Ruiqun Li,Changqun Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LACE-SVD is a loss-aware low-rank compression framework for LLMs that allocates rank budgets based on calibration loss sensitivity and corrects cumulative error propagation, achieving significantly better perplexity than Dobi-SVD on LLaMA-7B (32.57 vs 46.18 at 0.6 compression ratio).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SVD-based low-rank compression methods allocate rank budgets without considering layer-wise loss sensitivity, and local approximation errors propagate through the residual stream, causing global deviations from the original model.

Method: LACE-SVD (1) estimates calibration NLL increase for candidate compression ratios to solve budget-constrained rank allocation, (2) refines the compressed model with closed-form local updates, and (3) introduces propagation-aware correction for residual-stream output modules to reduce cumulative error.

Result: At 0.6 compression ratio on LLaMA-7B, LACE-SVD achieves WikiText-2 PPL of 32.57, significantly outperforming Dobi-SVD (46.18).

Conclusion: LACE-SVD effectively addresses loss-unaware rank allocation and error propagation issues in low-rank LLM compression, achieving superior performance at high compression ratios.

Abstract: The rapid growth in the parameter scale of large language models (LLMs) has created a strong demand for efficient compression techniques. As a hardware-agnostic and highly compatible approach, low-rank compression has been widely adopted to reduce both memory footprint and computational cost. However, existing SVD-based methods are still largely driven by local reconstruction objectives, overlooking two critical limitations: rank budgets are often allocated without explicitly considering layer-wise loss sensitivity, and local approximation errors can propagate and accumulate through the residual stream, leading to amplified global deviations from the original model. To address these issues, we propose LACE-SVD, a Loss-Aware SVD framework with Cumulative Error correction for LLM compression. LACE-SVD first estimates the calibration negative-log-likelihood increase induced by candidate layer-wise compression ratios and solves a budget-constrained allocation problem to assign rank budgets. It then refines the compressed model with closed-form local updates and introduces a propagation-aware correction for residual-stream output modules, reducing layer-output discrepancy as a proxy for cumulative error propagation. Experimental results demonstrate that at a high compression ratio (0.6), the WikiText-2 PPL of our method on LLaMA-7B (32.57) is significantly better than that of Dobi-SVD (46.18).

</details>


### [578] [Spectral Rewiring for Exploration, Purification, and Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03065)
*Zhilong Zhang,Hongli Yu,Huan-ang Gao,Hanlin Wu,Yuxuan Song,Wei-Ying Ma,Ya-Qin Zhang,Hao Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAR is a post-hoc editing method that extracts compact reasoning cores (~0.58% of parameters) from RL-trained LLMs by focusing on spectral updates, preserving >99% post-training performance while improving reasoning, reducing cross-domain interference, and enabling better model merging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense full-parameter RL updates for LLMs cause two bottlenecks: suppressed reasoning performance (premature saturation of test-time scaling) and interference when consolidating multiple capabilities through multi-domain training or model merging.

Method: Subspace-Aligned Rewiring (SAR): a post-hoc editing method that identifies reasoning-effective components concentrated in the base model's spectral space, retains this spectral core, and removes orthogonal components from RL updates.

Result: SAR uses ~0.58% of total parameters, preserves over 99% of post-training performance, improves high-k exploration in math reasoning, generalizes to agentic coding (improving 6/7 benchmarks), purifies mixed-domain training by releasing suppressed coding capability, and enables cross-domain model merging surpassing previous baselines and single-domain experts.

Conclusion: Extracting reasoning-effective updates from parameter geometry serves as a training-free mechanism to improve both reasoning and multi-domain performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has become a standard post-training recipe for large language models, but dense full-parameter updates create two deployment-relevant bottlenecks: suppressed reasoning performance, often reflected by premature saturation of test-time scaling, and interference when consolidating multiple capabilities through multi-domain training or model merging. We show that the reasoning-effective component of these updates is largely concentrated in the base model's spectral space, motivating Subspace-Aligned Rewiring (SAR), a post-hoc editing method that retains this spectral core while removing orthogonal components. SAR therefore preserves reasoning gains and filters residual update directions that suppress performance or amplify cross-domain interference. Across several model families and scales, SAR extracts compact reasoning cores using as little as approximately 0.58% of total parameters: it preserves over 99% of post-training performance and improves high-k exploration in mathematical reasoning, and generalizes to agentic coding by improving six of seven open benchmarks on an in-house model. SAR also purifies mixed-domain training updates by releasing suppressed coding capability while maintaining math reasoning and instruction following. It further enables model merging across experts, yielding cross-domain generalization that surpasses previous merging baselines and even the best single-domain experts. Overall, SAR shows that extracting reasoning-effective updates from parameter geometry can serve as a training-free mechanism to improve reasoning and multi-domain performance.

</details>


### [579] [Robustness Meets Uncertainty: Evidential Adversarial Training for Robust Selective Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03075)
*Nicolas Sournac,Ahmed Baha Ben Jmaa,Bertrand Braeckeveldt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that many adversarial training methods improve robustness but degrade uncertainty ranking, and proposes Evidential Adversarial Training (EV-AT) to improve both robust accuracy and reliable uncertainty estimation, achieving state-of-the-art on the robustness-uncertainty trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The effect of adversarial training on predictive uncertainty reliability is underexplored; systematic analysis of the robustness-uncertainty trade-off in selective classification is lacking.

Method: EV-AT models uncertainty via a Dirichlet distribution with (i) an evidence-based loss for clean accuracy & reliable uncertainty, and (ii) a robust evidence-alignment loss matching clean and adversarial predictions in log Dirichlet-parameter space.

Result: EV-AT shifts the Pareto frontier of robustness-uncertainty trade-offs beyond prior state-of-the-art adversarial training methods.

Conclusion: Adversarial training can degrade uncertainty ranking; EV-AT effectively addresses this failure mode, advancing the robustness-uncertainty frontier.

Abstract: Safety-critical applications require classifiers that are both robust and reliable.
