Adversarial training is a widely adopted defense for improving robustness in deep neural networks; however, its effect on the reliability of predictive uncertainty remains underexplored. We investigate this gap through the lens of selective classification, which has rarely been systematically analyzed alongside adversarial robustness. We introduce a unified benchmark for the robustness-uncertainty trade-off. It standardizes architectures, augmentations, threat models, and evaluation metrics across clean, adversarial, and common-corruption settings. Across a wide range of state-of-the-art adversarial training methods, we uncover a recurring failure mode: several approaches improve robust accuracy while degrading uncertainty ranking, leading to poorer selective behavior. To address this, we propose Evidential Adversarial Training (EV-AT), which models uncertainty through a Dirichlet distribution and combines (i) an evidence-based loss promoting clean accuracy and reliable uncertainty with (ii) a robust evidence-alignment loss matching clean and adversarial predictions in log Dirichlet-parameter space. Extensive experiments show that EV-AT shifts the Pareto frontier of robustness-uncertainty trade-offs beyond prior state-of-the-art adversarial training methods. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/NicolasSournac/Robustness_Meets_Uncertainty.EV-AT.

</details>


### [580] [STELLA: Efficient Sensor-to-LLM Translation for On-Device Human Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03089)
*Nirhoshan Sivaroopan,Albert Zomaya,Kanchana Thilakarathna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STELLA is an efficient sensor-to-LLM translation framework for on-device Human Activity Recognition (HAR) that uses a lightweight hierarchical tokenizer to compress multi-channel inertial data into compact latent tokens for a frozen pretrained LLM, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with real-time inference and on-device personalization while preserving privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based HAR methods are hard to deploy on edge devices due to long raw sensor prompts, cloud inference latency and privacy risks, and fine-tuned LLM pipelines that turn general models into task-specific classifiers.

Method: A lightweight hierarchical tokenizer compresses multi-channel inertial windows into fixed compact latent sensor tokens, which are projected into a frozen pretrained LLM's embedding space and combined with a natural-language prompt for label scoring. On-device personalization adapts only the lightweight tokenizer with small user-specific data and local retrieval context.

Result: State-of-the-art on seven public HAR datasets and eight benchmarks, improving up to 11.83% F1 over prior methods; on-device personalization yields up to 21.91% additional F1. Outperforms representative time-series tokenizers, achieves real-time inference within mobile/edge budgets.

Conclusion: Efficient sensor tokenization is a practical path toward accurate, private, and personalized LLM-based HAR on edge devices, shifting the burden from LLM adaptation to tokenization.

Abstract: HAR is increasingly expected to run continuously on edge devices, yet recent LLM-based methods remain hard to deploy: raw sensor prompts are long, cloud inference adds latency and privacy risk, and fine-tuned LLM pipelines turn general-purpose models into task-specific classifiers. We present STELLA, an efficient sensor-to-LLM translation framework for on-device HAR that shifts the burden from LLM adaptation to sensor tokenization. A lightweight hierarchical tokenizer compresses an entire multi-channel inertial window into a fixed set of compact latent sensor tokens, which are projected into the embedding space of a frozen pretrained LLM and combined with a natural-language prompt for label scoring. This preserves activity-relevant temporal and cross-channel structure while keeping LLM-side computation predictable across sensor configurations. STELLA also supports on-device personalization, adapting only the lightweight tokenizer on small amounts of user-specific labelled data and augmenting inference with a local retrieval context, keeping the LLM, user data, and retrieval on device. Across seven public HAR datasets and eight benchmark settings, STELLA achieves new state-of-the-art performance, improving over prior methods by up to 11.83% F1; on-device personalization yields up to a further 21.91% F1 as user data accumulates after deployment. STELLA also outperforms representative time-series tokenizers under the same LLM pipeline and achieves real-time inference under practical mobile and edge budgets, showing that efficient sensor tokenization is a practical path toward accurate, private, and personalized LLM-based HAR on edge devices.

</details>


### [581] [Stacked LoRA for Subject-Adaptive EEG Foundation Models in Motor Imagery Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03094)
*Aymen Sarhane,Fouad Lbakali,Mouad Souissi,Jonathan Lys,Giulia Lioi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Stacked LoRA, a framework that decouples subject-invariant and subject-specific knowledge for EEG decoding, using Global and Subject-Specific adapters to improve cross-subject generalization in motor imagery classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EEG decoding for BCIs suffers from inter-subject variability, requiring subject-specific recalibration. Even large EEG foundation models need additional adaptation for downstream tasks.

Method: Proposes Stacked LoRA with a Global adapter (shared across subjects) and Subject-Specific adapters (per-subject). Compares three strategies: subject-specific LoRA, global LoRA, and stacked LoRA.

Result: Stacked LoRA achieves the best accuracy on BCI Competition IV-2a, PhysioNet, and Zuo2025 benchmarks. Global adapters suffice for large cohorts; subject-specific adaptation is critical for clinical recordings with high inter-session variability.

Conclusion: Stacked LoRA effectively mitigates inter-subject variability. The optimal balance depends on the target population: shared for diverse cohorts, subject-specific for clinical settings.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) decoding for brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) faces a major challenge: substantial inter-subject variability limits effective cross-subject generalization. Consequently, practical systems still rely largely on subject-specific models trained from scratch and requiring individual recalibration. EEG foundation models have recently emerged as a promising alternative; however, even large pretrained models cannot simply be used as fixed feature extractors and still require additional adaptation before they can be reliably applied to downstream tasks. In this work, we address this challenge through targeted adaptation strategies. Building on recent EEG foundation models such as REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA, we examine the impact of different low-rank adaptation strategies on motor imagery classification. We propose a framework that structurally decouples subject-invariant knowledge from subject-specific neural signatures: the low-rank update at each adapted layer is split into a Global adapter, trained jointly across all subjects, and Subject-Specific adapters, each absorbing individual variability. To assess the contribution of each path, we compare three adaptation strategies: (i) subject-specific LoRA (ii) global LoRA and (iii) stacked LoRA, combining both Global and Subject Specific adapters. Experiments on BCI Competition IV-2a, PhysioNet Motor Imagery, and the clinical Zuo2025 benchmark show that Stacked LoRA effectively mitigates inter-subject variability, achieving the best accuracy in the large majority of backbone and dataset combinations. Our analysis further reveals that the optimal balance between the global and subject-specific paths depends on the target population: a shared adapter is sufficient for large, diverse cohorts, whereas subject-specific adaptation is decisive under the high inter-session variability of clinical recordings.

</details>


### [582] [Heterogeneous Graph Condensation via Role-Aware Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03097)
*Fuyan Ou,Yulin Hu,Ye Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HGC-RC is a role-aware heterogeneous graph condensation framework that efficiently compresses large-scale heterogeneous graphs by extracting semantic embeddings, applying role-aware clustering (class-partitioned for target nodes, type-wise for non-target nodes), and reconstructing a compact graph. It outperforms baselines while accelerating HGNN training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HGNNs are computationally prohibitive for large-scale heterogeneous graphs, and current graph condensation methods are designed for homogeneous graphs and rely on expensive gradient matching or bilevel optimization, making them impractical for heterogeneous settings.

Method: (1) Lightweight propagation to extract semantically enhanced node embeddings. (2) Role-aware hybrid clustering: class-partitioned clustering for labeled target nodes (preserving class distributions) + type-wise clustering for non-target nodes (retaining cross-type connectivity). (3) Compact heterogeneous graph reconstruction based on cluster assignments.

Result: HGC-RC outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both efficiency and task performance on large-scale heterogeneous graphs.

Conclusion: HGC-RC provides a practical and effective pathway to accelerate HGNN training on large-scale heterogeneous graphs without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks (HGNNs) have exhibited remarkable efficacy in modeling complex systems with multiple types of nodes and relations, yet their training on large-scale heterogeneous graphs remains computationally prohibitive. Although graph condensation methods can effectively improve learning efficiency on large-scale graphs, existing condensation processes are mainly designed for homogeneous graphs and typically rely on computationally expensive gradient matching or bilevel optimization paradigms, rendering them impractical for heterogeneous settings. To address these limitations, we propose HGC-RC, a simple yet effective role-aware heterogeneous graph condensation framework. Specifically, HGC-RC first extracts semantically enhanced node embeddings via lightweight propagation. It then introduces a role-aware hybrid clustering strategy consisting of class-partitioned clustering for labeled target nodes to preserve class distributions and unsupervised type-wise clustering for non-target nodes to retain critical cross-type connectivity. Finally, a compact heterogeneous graph is efficiently reconstructed based on the resulting cluster assignments. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HGC-RC outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering a practical pathway to accelerate HGNN training on large-scale heterogeneous graphs without sacrificing task performance

</details>


### [583] [Observable- and Positional-Encoding-Dependent Symmetry Readout from Neural Network Weights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03108)
*Naoya Chiba,Satoshi Sugiyama,Yuki Uranishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Positional encoding (PE) design determines which geometric symmetries are observable from neural network weights, creating an observability hierarchy where true symmetries can be structurally invisible to weight-level analysis if the PE doesn't represent the corresponding transformation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why post-hoc analysis of trained neural network weights sometimes fails to detect known geometric symmetries, and to formalize the dependence of observable symmetries on positional encoding, trained parameters, and readout observables.

Method: Formulates an exact observability hierarchy: G_obs^exact ⊆ G_lift^exact(φ) ∩ G_true. Tests this using MLPs trained on 2D signed distance functions with multiple symmetry groups, various positional encodings (DyadicAxisPE, TriAxisPE, random Fourier features), and Gram-based observables.

Result: DyadicAxisPE supports D4-sensitive readout but suppresses D3 rotations; TriAxisPE yields lower D3/D6 readout scores by using three 120-degree-separated axes; random Fourier features mainly exhibit π-rotation response.

Conclusion: PE design fundamentally affects which geometric structures are accessible to post-hoc weight-level readouts, providing a principled basis for observable-dependent symmetry analysis.

Abstract: Post-hoc analysis of trained neural network weights often seeks to recover geometric structure directly from the parameters. We show that, for positional-encoding-equipped neural fields, the symmetry visible from weights is not the true symmetry group itself, but an observable symmetry set determined by the trained parameters, the positional encoding (PE), and readout observable. We formulate this dependence through an exact observability hierarchy, $G_{\mathrm{obs}}^{\mathrm{exact}} \subseteq G_{\mathrm{lift}}^{\mathrm{exact}}(φ) \cap G_{\mathrm{true}}$, where $G_{\mathrm{lift}}^{\mathrm{exact}}(φ)$ is the set of input transformations that the PE can exactly lift to the feature space. The hierarchy implies that even when a target function has a geometric symmetry, that symmetry may be structurally invisible to weight-level observables if the PE does not represent the corresponding transformation. We test this prediction using MLPs trained on two-dimensional signed distance functions with multiple shape symmetry groups, positional encodings, and Gram-based observables. The results show a consistent PE-dependent pattern: DyadicAxisPE supports $D_4$-sensitive readout but structurally suppresses $D_3$ rotations, TriAxisPE yields lower $D_3$ / $D_6$ readout scores under the tested Gram observables by replacing coordinate axes with three 120-degree-separated axes, and random Fourier features mainly exhibit a $π$-rotation response under these readouts. These findings show that PE design affects not only approximation behavior but also which structures are accessible to post-hoc weight-level readouts. This provides a basis for a principled observable-dependent symmetry readout.

</details>


### [584] [Integrating Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Safe Reinforcement Learning in a 1-DoF Helicopter System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03125)
*Georg Schäfer,Jakob Rehrl,Stefan Huber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes embedding a differentiable physics model into the PPO actor loss function to penalize anticipated safety violations directly, reducing constraint violations in industrial cyber-physical systems without relying solely on reward shaping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) poses safety risks in industrial cyber-physical systems (ICPSs) due to "black-box" exploration that can violate strict hardware safety limits, and traditional constraint management via reward shaping is often insufficient.

Method: A differentiable physics model is embedded directly into the proximal policy optimization (PPO) actor loss function. During training, short-horizon future trajectories are simulated to penalize anticipated safety violations, independent of the task-reward signal.

Result: On a simulated 1-degree-of-freedom helicopter testbed with strict pitch constraints, the physics-informed soft regularizations substantially reduce constraint violations while maintaining reliable target tracking.

Conclusion: The proposed physics-informed soft regularization approach effectively reduces safety violations in DRL-controlled ICPSs without compromising task performance, offering a promising alternative to complex reward shaping.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) offers powerful control for industrial cyber-physical systems (ICPSs), but its "black-box" exploration risks violating strict hardware safety limits. Typically, these constraints are managed through complex reward shaping. In this work-in-progress paper, we embed a differentiable physics model directly into the proximal policy optimization (PPO) actor loss function. By simulating short-horizon future trajectories during training, the policy is penalized for anticipated safety violations independent of the task-reward signal. Evaluated on a simulated 1-degree-of-freedom helicopter testbed with strict pitch constraints, our physics-informed soft regularizations substantially reduce constraint violations while maintaining reliable target tracking.

</details>


### [585] [ACPO: Adaptive Credit Policy Optimization via Fine-Grained Surrogate Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03126)
*Zijun Xie,Yuyang You,Yongzhi Li,Enlei Gong,Zeyu Chen,Quan Chen,Yanhua Cheng,Peng Jiang,Yadong Mu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ACPO is a token-level credit assignment method for RL-based LLM reasoning that asymmetrically adjusts policy updates based on token uncertainty, outperforming DAPO, GTPO, and SAPO on math and coding benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods for LLMs struggle with sparse outcome rewards causing poor token-level credit assignment, and current entropy-aware approaches either use coarse heuristics or introduce misaligned gradient components.

Method: Proposes Adaptive Credit Policy Optimization (ACPO), which uses a mode-local surrogate entropy to asymmetrically modulate policy updates—emphasizing uncertain decisions in successful rollouts and overconfident tokens in failed rollouts.

Result: ACPO consistently outperforms DAPO, GTPO, and SAPO on AIME 2025 (math reasoning) and HumanEvalPro (coding) benchmarks.

Conclusion: The mode-local surrogate entropy approach with asymmetric credit assignment effectively addresses token-level credit assignment, and under modal alignment and proximal updates preserves the policy-gradient direction to leading order.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has substantially improved the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs), but sparse outcome rewards still make token-level credit assignment difficult. Existing scalable RL methods typically assign trajectory-level rewards uniformly across tokens, while recent entropy-aware approaches either rely on coarse detached heuristics or directly optimize true entropy, which can introduce non-local gradient components misaligned with sampled-token policy updates. We propose Adaptive Credit Policy Optimization (ACPO), a token-level credit assignment framework based on a mode-local surrogate entropy. ACPO asymmetrically modulates policy updates by emphasizing uncertain decisions in successful rollouts and overconfident tokens in failed rollouts. We show that the surrogate admits deterministic entropy bounds and, under modal alignment and proximal updates, preserves the policy-gradient direction to leading order. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and coding benchmarks, including AIME 2025 and HumanEvalPro, show that ACPO consistently improves over strong RL baselines such as DAPO, GTPO, and SAPO.

</details>


### [586] [Anticipatory Reinforcement Learning for Trajectory Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03132)
*Georg Schäfer,Jakob Rehrl,Stefan Huber,Simon Hirlaender*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Augmenting DRL state space with predictive target velocities and future reference horizons reduces tracking error 9-fold in simulation (from 2.73° to 0.31°), but zero-shot transfer to hardware reveals a sim-to-real gap where a simpler single look-ahead horizon matches the most complex model's real-world performance (1.11°).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome lag and overshoot in DRL-based industrial control caused by purely reactive control based on current tracking error, without incurring high computational overhead.

Method: Augmenting the DRL state space with target velocities and future reference horizons, evaluated using PPO on a 1-DoF helicopter testbed across eight configurations.

Result: Simulation: 9-fold error reduction (MAD from 2.73° to 0.31°). Real-world: Simpler single look-ahead horizon matched complex model performance (1.11°).

Conclusion: Predictive augmentation improves DRL control, but highly granular predictive data is not necessary for successful physical transfer; simpler configurations suffice.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) in industrial control often suffers from lag and overshoot due to purely reactive control based on the current tracking error. To achieve anticipatory control without high computational overhead, we introduce a predictive formulation that augments the DRL state space with target velocities and future reference horizons. Evaluating eight configurations using proximal policy optimization (PPO) on a 1-degree-of-freedom (1-DoF) helicopter testbed, simulation results showed a 9-fold error reduction, lowering the mean absolute deviation from 2.73° to 0.31°. However, zero-shot transfer to physical hardware revealed a sim-to-real gap. Interestingly, a simpler configuration using a single, further look-ahead horizon matched the real-world top performance of the most complex model (1.11°). Overall, evaluating various combinations of prediction horizons and target velocities demonstrated that highly granular predictive data is not necessarily required for physical transfer.

</details>


### [587] [Sample-Efficient Pareto Front Modeling for Energy-Aware Reinforcement Learning Using Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03140)
*Georg Schäfer,Jakob Rehrl,Stefan Huber,Simon Hirlaender*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization (MOBO) to automatically tune reward weights in multi-objective RL, efficiently discovering Pareto-optimal trade-off policies for energy-aware industrial control, outperforming uniform grid search on a Quanser Aero 2 testbed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual weight selection for multi-objective RL reward functions is time-consuming, reliant on domain intuition, prone to human bias, and fails to find optimal trade-offs. The paper aims to automate this weight selection process to systematically discover the Pareto front.

Method: The weight selection process is formulated as a Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization (MOBO) problem using the expected hypervolume improvement (qEHVI) acquisition function, evaluated against uniform grid search baseline on a Quanser Aero 2 1-DoF pitch control testbed.

Result: MOBO with qEHVI consistently outperforms uniform grid sampling, achieving superior hypervolume and maximum spread, identifying high-quality diverse trade-off policies with fewer evaluations.

Conclusion: MOBO enables highly efficient energy-aware control in complex mechatronic systems by automatically discovering optimal trade-off policies with reduced evaluation budget.

Abstract: Industrial automation increasingly demands control strategies that balance operational performance with strict energy efficiency requirements. A common approach to solving this multi-objective problem, particularly within the framework of reinforcement learning (RL), is to formulate a single, scalar reward function that linearly combines the competing objectives. However, the manual weighting of these different objectives is heavily reliant on domain intuition, incredibly time-consuming, prone to human bias, and frequently fails to uncover optimal trade-off solutions. This work addresses the critical challenge of automating the weight selection process to systematically and efficiently discover the Pareto front of optimal trade-off policies. We formulate the weight selection process as a multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) problem and evaluate its sample efficiency against a standard uniform grid search baseline. Using a physical Quanser Aero 2 testbed configured for 1-DoF pitch control, our results demonstrate that the MOBO approach, utilizing the expected hypervolume improvement (qEHVI) acquisition function, consistently outperforms uniform grid sampling. MOBO achieves superior hypervolume and maximum spread, successfully identifying high-quality, diverse trade-off policies with a reduced evaluation budget, thereby enabling highly efficient energy-aware control in complex mechatronic systems.

</details>


### [588] [Rethinking Neural Nonlinearity as Gating](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03148)
*Muhammad Sabih,Frank Hannig,Jürgen Teich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Threshold Gating (TG) as a universal primitive that subsumes standard activation functions (ReLU, SiLU, Sigmoid, etc.) and softmax, enables model compression and faster training, proposes a 'Minimal Branch Theorem' for network trainability, and offers a unified hardware implementation for analog in-memory systems that reduces ADC/DAC bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To show that neural network nonlinearity can be achieved via input-conditioned threshold gating through branches as a universal primitive, rather than traditional activation functions, and to address the hardware bottleneck of analog-to-digital/digital-to-analog converters in analog in-memory computing systems.

Method: The authors demonstrate that standard activations (piecewise-linear and smooth) are instances of a single Threshold Gating (TG) primitive. They also show softmax can be exactly converted via its per-element Sigmoid form. They validate by converting pretrained CNNs, transformers, and RNNs without retraining, and train from scratch using TG for compression and performance gains. A 'Minimal Branch Theorem' is proposed to relate branch count to network trainability.

Result: Threshold Gating can exactly replace standard activations and softmax. Converted pretrained networks maintain performance without retraining. Training from scratch with TG yields gains in model compression, performance, and shorter training time. The approach maps to a unified analog in-memory hardware implementation that mitigates ADC/DAC bottlenecks.

Conclusion: Threshold Gating is a universal primitive for neural nonlinearity that subsumes existing activations, enables practical benefits in training and compression, and offers hardware-friendly analog implementation.

Abstract: Activation functions are considered an essential primitive for neural nonlinearity, i.e., they enable neural networks to serve as universal approximators. In this paper, we show that this nonlinearity can also be achieved by input-conditioned threshold gating through branches as a universal primitive. We demonstrate that standard activations -- whether piecewise-linear (ReLU, PReLU, Hardtanh) or smooth (SiLU, Sigmoid, Tanh, GELU) -- are in fact instances of a single Threshold Gating (TG) primitive. For softmax, we show that it admits an exact TG conversion via its equivalent per-element Sigmoid form. We then validate these equivalences by converting pretrained networks across CNNs, transformer-based models, and recurrent architectures, preserving model performance without requiring retraining. Threshold Gating also enables training from scratch that goes beyond replacing existing activations, enabling gains in model compression, performance, and shorter training. We also propose a 'Minimal Branch Theorem' which relates the minimum number of required branches in our primitive to the trainability of general deep neural networks. In terms of hardware implementation, TG maps to a unified implementation in the case of analog in-memory systems, addressing the bottleneck of analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog converters (ADC/DAC) that is known to significantly impact power consumption and on-chip area.

</details>


### [589] [Decentralised Federated Learning over Temporal Networks: The Role of Heterogeneities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03171)
*Arash Badie-Modiri,Chiara Boldrini,Lorenzo Valerio,János Kertész,Márton Karsai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decentralized federated learning's convergence is governed by lazy random-walk diffusion on temporal networks, and real-world structural/temporal inhomogeneities significantly slow convergence compared to idealized experimental setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how structural and temporal inhomogeneities in peer-to-peer communication networks affect the convergence of decentralized federated learning, since these factors are often overlooked in typical experimental scenarios.

Method: The authors map decentralized federated learning with local parameter averaging to a lazy random-walk diffusion process on temporal networks, then analyze both early and late (stationary) phase dynamics using real-world temporal network data.

Result: Typical experimental setups ignore temporal/structural inhomogeneities, leading to unrealistically rapid convergence estimates. Real-world inhomogeneities dramatically slow down the diffusion and convergence process.

Conclusion: Decentralized federated learning dynamics are equivalent to diffusion on temporal networks; the common idealized evaluation scenarios are misleading, and real-world network inhomogeneities are a major bottleneck for convergence speed.

Abstract: Decentralised federated learning, based on peer-to-peer communication, is increasingly proposed for on-device training of machine learning models, promising a privacy-preserving, communication-efficient training process with no risk of single-point failure. However, the role of structural and temporal inhomogeneities in such fully decentralised settings remains poorly understood. Here, we investigate their effects when model parameters are locally averaged during aggregation. We show that the decentralised federated learning process is governed, both in the early phase and the late, stationary limit, by the same dynamics as a lazy random-walk diffusion process on temporal networks. Based on this mapping, we demonstrate that the typical experimental scenario used in decentralised federated learning leads to unrealistically rapid convergence because of ignoring the temporal and structural inhomogeneities inherent in the communication network. We analyse real-world temporal networks and find that inhomogeneities most often dramatically slow down diffusion, hence the convergence process.

</details>


### [590] [Understanding electricity consumption behaviour through Inverse Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03176)
*Enrico Cofler,Carlos Rodriguez-Pardo,Matteo Giuliani,Andrea Castelletti,Massimo Tavoni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper uses Inverse Reinforcement Learning to model household electricity consumption as reward functions, testing how Italian consumer clusters' cooling behaviors changed heterogeneously across the 2021-2023 summers in response to an energy crisis and heatwave, revealing transient and durable shifts conditioned by prior habits, built environment, and time-of-use patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how households consume electricity in response to socioeconomic and climatic drivers is important for designing energy policies in a changing climate and under geopolitical tensions, but most approaches oversimplify the nonlinear behavior of consumers responding to thermal stress based on income, habits, and built environment.

Method: Households are treated as agents interacting with complex environments; Inverse Reinforcement Learning is used to represent consumption behavior as model-implied reward functions; the framework is tested on clusters of electricity consumption profiles in Italy, observing how reward functions change under socioeconomic and climatic shocks.

Result: Shocks reshaped cooling behavior heterogeneously across consumer groups, conditioned by prior habits and built environment. A spectrum of responses was identified: transient adjustments, durable shifts persisting into 2023, and negligible change. Groups similar in socioeconomic/environmental context but differing in daily timing of consumption responded distinctly, identifying time of use as a separate dimension of behavioral heterogeneity.

Conclusion: Energy policies and demand-response schemes should account not only for who consumers are and where they live, but for when they consume and whether their response to a shock persists.

Abstract: Understanding how households consume electricity in response to socioeconomic and climatic drivers is important for decision-makers designing energy policies in a changing climate and under geopolitical tensions. Consumers respond differently to thermal stress depending on income, consumption habits and the surrounding built environment, a nonlinear behaviour that most approaches oversimplify. In this study, households are treated as agents interacting with complex environments, and Inverse Reinforcement Learning is used to represent their consumption behaviour as model implied reward functions. Specifically, we observe how these reward functions change when households undergo socioeconomic and climatic shocks. The framework is tested on different clusters of electricity consumption profiles in Italy. Clusters' reward functions are retrieved and used to understand how cooling behaviour changes from summer 2021 to summer 2022 and 2023, before, during and after the energy crisis and a heatwave. We find that these shocks reshaped cooling behaviour heterogeneously across consumer groups, in directions conditioned by their prior habits and built environment. Across the 2021 to 2023 summers, we identify a spectrum of responses: transient adjustments that receded as the shocks eased, durable shifts persisting into 2023, and consumers exhibiting negligible change. At the intradaily scale, groups comparable in socioeconomic and environmental context but differing in their daily timing of consumption responded distinctly, identifying time of use as a separate dimension of behavioural heterogeneity. Energy policies and demand-response schemes should therefore account not only for who consumers are and where they live, but for when they consume and whether their response to a shock persists.

</details>


### [591] [A Bayesian Framework for Evaluating Scenario Compatibility in Generative Population Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03190)
*Zhenlin Qin,Leizhen Wang,Yancheng Ling,Zhenliang Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an ensemble-based Bayesian updating framework with a population-aware conditional variational autoencoder to quantify whether scenario-level constraints are compatible with generative population synthesis models, using effective sample size to measure structural uncertainty compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice applies deterministic marginal calibration when imposing scenario targets on generative models, implicitly assuming compatibility with the model's learned structure. Whether scenario constraints lie within generative support and how they distort structural uncertainty remains unexamined.

Method: A population-aware conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) learns a distribution over plausible population structures while preserving aggregate fidelity. An ensemble of realizations provides empirical approximation of structural uncertainty. Scenario targets are treated as probabilistic evidence, and posterior weights are obtained via Bayesian updating. Scenario compatibility is quantified using effective sample size (ESS).

Result: Scenario impact depends not only on target magnitude but also on alignment with the learned joint structure. Structural failure modes emerge when targets fall outside prior ensemble support.

Conclusion: The framework provides a probabilistic diagnostic model for evaluating scenario feasibility and structural consistency before downstream projection and transportation planning.

Abstract: Scenario-based transportation analysis specifies future assumptions through aggregate population targets, whereas generative population synthesis models produce detailed individual-level realizations. When scenario targets are imposed on generative models, current practice relies on deterministic marginal calibration, implicitly assuming that the targets are compatible with the model's learned structural support. However, whether scenario-level constraints lie within the generative support--and how strongly they distort structural uncertainty--remains largely unexamined. We propose an ensemble-based Bayesian updating framework to quantify scenario compatibility in conditional population synthesis. A population-aware conditional variational autoencoder is developed to learn a distribution over plausible population structures while preserving aggregate fidelity. An ensemble of realizations sampled from the learned prior provides an empirical approximation of structural uncertainty. Scenario targets are treated as probabilistic evidence over aggregate statistics, and posterior weights are obtained through Bayesian updating across the ensemble. Scenario compatibility is quantified using effective sample size (ESS), which measures posterior concentration and the compression of structural uncertainty induced by conditioning. Experiments demonstrate that scenario impact depends not only on target magnitude but also on alignment with the learned joint structure, and reveal structural failure modes when targets fall outside prior ensemble support. The proposed framework provides a probabilistic diagnostic model for evaluating scenario feasibility and structural consistency before downstream projection and transportation planning.

</details>


### [592] [Reduced-Order Models: The Mother of World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03198)
*Rajat Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper traces the origins of world models to decades-old model-order-reduction and control literature, arguing that modern learned world models lack the verifiability needed for safety-critical applications, and proposes a unified research agenda combining MOR's verification with learned models' flexibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between modern learned world models and classical control/MOR approaches, highlighting that the core architecture of world models was independently developed earlier for physical systems, and to argue that verifiability—not predictive fidelity—is the key obstacle for deploying world models in safety-critical domains.

Method: The authors trace the functional anatomy of world models across three communities: (1) POD-based low-dimensional turbulence models for latent dynamics, (2) eigenface methods in early computer vision for encoder-decoder architecture, and (3) measurement-based POD frameworks for facility thermal control that assembled the complete closed-loop system with analytical error bounds.

Result: MOR contributes verification, physical grounding, and extreme data efficiency; learned world models contribute nonlinear representation, transferability, and horizon. The key shortcoming of modern world models is lack of verifiability, not predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: The paper outlines a research agenda for physics-grounded, verifiable world models that unifies the classical MOR lineage with modern learned representations, targeting deployment in systems that cannot fail.

Abstract: World models -- compressed latent representations of an environment that support action-conditioned prediction and planning -- are typically presented as a product of modern self-supervised learning. This paper argues that the functional anatomy of a world model was independently developed, deployed, and formally analyzed decades earlier in the model-order-reduction (MOR) and control literature, under different names and for a different purpose: the real-time operation of physical systems. We trace the anatomy across three communities. Low-dimensional models of turbulence built on proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) supplied latent dynamics learned from data of a chaotic environment; eigenface methods in early computer vision supplied the encoder-decoder half, including a primitive runtime validity check; and measurement-based POD frameworks for facility thermal control assembled the complete loop -- POD coefficients as latent state, parametric dependence on actuator setpoints as action conditioning, modal reconstruction as decoding, and, critically, a priori analytical error bounds as a verification layer that certified when the model's predictions could be trusted in closed loop. We then examine what each tradition possesses that the other lacks: MOR contributes verification, physical grounding, and extreme data efficiency; learned world models contribute nonlinear representation, transferability, and horizon. We argue that the outstanding obstacle to deploying world models in systems that cannot fail -- power, thermal, process control -- is not predictive fidelity but verifiability, and we outline a research agenda for physics-grounded, verifiable world models that unifies the two lineages.

</details>


### [593] [OpFlow: Learning Opportunity-Conditioned Choice Potentials for Robust OD Flow Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03200)
*Changjian Liu,Yong Gao,Yuqing Wang,Leyi Su,Honglei Guo,Zhiyang Wang,Xiaoyu Wang,Fan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpFlow improves OD flow prediction robustness under distribution shifts by learning transferable exposure-to-choice laws rather than raw count patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep models trained on raw OD counts are vulnerable to distribution shift because raw counts conflate origin demand with destination allocation, making it hard to distinguish transferable choice mechanisms from environment-specific shortcuts.

Method: OpFlow learns row-centered choice potentials that capture relative destination preferences driven by spatial exposure conditions, then reconstructs flows by combining these potentials with separately calibrated origin scales. Only the exposure-to-choice mapping transfers; exposures and allocations vary under shift.

Result: Theoretical identifiability of row-centered potentials is established, classical spatial interaction laws are shown as restricted log-potential cases. Controlled synthetic and real-world experiments demonstrate improved robustness under environment shifts.

Conclusion: OpFlow offers a mechanism-constrained framework where the transferable component is the conditional mapping from exposure states to relative choice preferences, effectively disentangling demand scale from allocation behavior for more robust OD prediction.

Abstract: Origin-destination (OD) flow prediction is central to urban analytics, yet deep models trained on raw counts remain vulnerable to distribution shift. The core problem is that raw count supervision cannot distinguish transferable choice mechanisms from environment-specific shortcuts. Raw OD count mixes two objects: how much demand an origin produces and how that demand is allocated across destinations. We argue that the transferable object is the exposure-to-choice law that maps spatial conditions to relative destination preferences. We propose OpFlow, a mechanism-constrained framework that learns row-centered choice potentials and reconstructs flows by combining the induced allocation with a separately calibrated origin scale. Under distribution shift, spatial exposures and the induced allocations are allowed to vary; what transfers is the conditional map from exposure states to relative choice potentials. Theoretically, we characterize the identifiable row-centered potential and show that classical spatial interaction laws are restricted log-potential cases. Controlled synthetic shifts and a real-world experiment show OpFlow improves robustness under environment shifts.

</details>


### [594] [Transition Information Density: Morphological Trajectories, Synesthetic Perception, and Structured Interpolation in Neural Training (or: The Synesthetic AI)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03210)
*Sam Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Transition Information Density (TID) and Positional Identity as measures of structured information in intermediate states between training endpoints. Experiments show that training on structured interpolation reduces intrinsic dimensionality in phonetic/linguistic and semantic representations but not in visual/cross-modal ones, establishing a modality boundary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard ML training uses discrete endpoint pairs, ignoring the structure of the space between them. The authors aim to formally define and empirically validate constructs (TID and Positional Identity) that capture recoverable information from intermediate states along categorical continuums.

Method: The authors ground TID and Positional Identity in three contexts: grapheme-color synesthesia, a Synesthesia Grid morphing algorithm, and a four-condition training experiment across four representational mediums (Phonetic/Linguistic, Semantic Description, Visual, Cross-modal). They compare structured interpolation (C3) against volume-matched controls (C2) using intrinsic dimensionality and TwoNN analysis.

Result: C3 training shows substantially lower intrinsic dimensionality than C2 in Phonetic/Linguistic (3.33 vs 10.81) and Semantic (4.59 vs 8.67) mediums, but not in Visual or Cross-modal mediums. Resolution N scales with representational richness. Pooled TwoNN reveals collapsed representations in visual space (0.075) vs. consistent representations in phonetic space (0.977).

Conclusion: Positional Identity structure, not sample count, drives the dimensionality reduction effect. The paper provides formal definitions of TID and Positional Identity, a nine-metric shape characterization framework, and an experimental design isolating trajectory structure, data volume, and Positional Identity as separate factors, with a clear modality boundary condition.

Abstract: Standard machine learning training presents data as discrete endpoint pairs, omitting the structure of the space between them. This paper introduces Transition Information Density (TID) -- the information content recoverable from structured intermediate states between categorically distinct training endpoints -- and Positional Identity, the defined location of an intermediate state on the A-to-B continuum. Both constructs are grounded in three empirical contexts: grapheme-color synesthesia, the Synesthesia Grid (a boundary-contour morphing algorithm instantiating TID in visual morphological space), and a four-condition training experiment across four representational mediums. Probes trained on structured interpolation at defined Positional Identities (C3) exhibit substantially lower intrinsic dimensionality than volume-matched controls (C2) in Phonetic/Linguistic (C3: 3.33 vs. C2: 10.81) and Semantic Description (C3: 4.59 vs. C2: 8.67) mediums. Visual and cross-modal mediums do not show this effect, establishing a modality boundary condition. A fixed-N=50 comparison confirms that Positional Identity structure, not sample count, drives the effect. Resolution N scales monotonically with representational richness. Pooled TwoNN analysis reveals globally collapsed representations in visual space (0.075) and globally consistent representations in phonetic space (0.977). The paper contributes a formal definition of TID and Positional Identity, a nine-metric shape characterization framework, and a four-condition experimental design isolating trajectory structure, data volume, and Positional Identity as distinct factors.

</details>


### [595] [Joint distribution of upstream runoff governs downstream river-discharge prediction uncertainty in distributed ML models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03217)
*Karan Ruparell,Tristan Hascoet,Takemasa Miyoshi,Kieran M. R. Hunt,Hannah L. Cloke,Christel Prudhomme,Florian Pappenberger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Moving probabilistic streamflow prediction from lumped to distributed models introduces a key requirement: the joint distribution of upstream runoff must be sampled jointly to avoid under-dispersed downstream predictions. A quantile matching strategy can restore ensemble spread.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operational decisions require uncertainty quantification in hydrological predictions. While generative ML methods have advanced probabilistic streamflow prediction, they remain confined to lumped basin models. Moving to distributed models creates a new challenge that needs addressing.

Method: Trained two probabilistic basin-scale runoff LSTMs in Japan, routed runoff through a Hayami routing scheme, and compared randomly matching upstream ensemble members versus a quantile matching strategy.

Result: Randomly matching upstream ensemble members produces severely under-dispersed downstream ensembles, while a quantile matching strategy restores much of the spread comparable to the direct basin-scale reference.

Conclusion: Transitioning from lumped to distributed probabilistic hydrology requires explicit attention to the spatial joint structure of runoff uncertainty to avoid losing predictive spread through routing.

Abstract: Uncertainty quantification of hydrological predictions is necessary to inform operational decisions. Recent generative machine-learning methods have advanced probabilistic streamflow prediction, but have remained confined to lumped models that predict a basin outlet directly. At the same time, deterministic LSTM runoff models are increasingly applied at grid or catchment scale and routed through river networks to produce spatially continuous, physically consistent discharge fields. This technical note argues that moving probabilistic prediction from lumped to distributed models introduces a specific new requirement: the joint distribution of upstream runoff generation must be sampled jointly. In lumped inference, the model predicts the outlet distribution directly and can modulate spread from basin attributes. In distributed inference, downstream discharge is obtained by routing many upstream runoff predictions, so independent local sampling averages uncertainty away. Using Japan as a case study, we train two probabilistic basin-scale runoff LSTMs and route their runoff through a Hayami routing scheme. Randomly matching upstream ensemble members produces severely under-dispersed downstream ensembles, whereas a simple quantile matching strategy restores much of the spread of the direct basin-scale reference. The shift from lumped to distributed probabilistic hydrology therefore requires explicit attention to the spatial joint structure of runoff uncertainty.

</details>


### [596] [Lyapunov-Guided Training for Hardware-Safe Neural Networks Under Fixed-Point Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04531)
*Anis Hamadouche,Amir Hussain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Lyapunov-stabilised quantisation framework that uses monotone projection to prevent overflow-induced corruption in low-precision neural networks with fixed-point arithmetic, achieving stable training and high accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-precision neural networks suffer from two's-complement overflow wrapping in fixed-point hardware, which corrupts hidden activations and causes severe accuracy degradation—a failure mode hidden by idealized quantization models.

Method: A Lyapunov-stabilised quantisation framework: layerwise Lyapunov function monitors hidden-state energy, and a monotone projection enforces bounded, non-increasing state evolution across network depth to suppress overflow.

Result: On MNIST with a patch-based transformer (4-16 bits): unconstrained quantization-aware training collapses to near-chance accuracy with >11% overflow rates; the proposed method reduces overflow to <0.012% and achieves 86.55% accuracy at 12 bits.

Conclusion: Lyapunov-based state control is an effective hardware-aware stabilization mechanism for reliable fixed-point neural inference and training in low-precision settings.

Abstract: Low-precision neural networks are attractive for resource-constrained hardware, but fixed-point arithmetic introduces failure modes that are often hidden by idealised quantisation models. In particular, two's-complement overflow wrapping can corrupt hidden activations by changing both their magnitude and sign, leading to unstable numerical error propagation and severe accuracy degradation. This paper proposes a Lyapunov-stabilised quantisation framework for low-precision neural networks operating under hardware-style wrapping arithmetic. The hidden-state energy is monitored through a layerwise Lyapunov function, and a monotone projection is applied to enforce bounded and non-increasing state evolution across depth. The method is evaluated on MNIST using a compact patch-based transformer under post-training quantisation and quantisation-aware training with fixed-point bit-widths from 4 to 16 bits. Monte Carlo results show that unconstrained wrapped quantisation-aware training collapses to near-chance accuracy across 6-16 bits, with activation overflow rates exceeding 11%. In contrast, the proposed monotone Lyapunov projection suppresses activation overflow to below 0.012% and restores stable low-precision learning, achieving 86.55% accuracy at 12 bits. These results demonstrate that Lyapunov-based state control can act as a hardware-aware stabilisation mechanism for reliable fixed-point neural inference and training.

</details>


### [597] [PhenoNEST: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Ontology-Aware Multimodal Plant Phenotyping and Trait Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03245)
*Jayant Ghadge,Soumyashree Kar,Surya S. Durbha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for constructing multimodal granular Knowledge Graphs from high-throughput plant phenotyping data, using wheat as a case study, to link unstructured text and images via standardized ontologies and vision-language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-throughput plant phenotyping generates data trapped in unstructured text and isolated RGB images, creating a semantic gap that hinders monitoring of genotype-phenotype interactions across time and experiments.

Method: 1) Extract entities/relations from noisy field notes; 2) Dynamically construct KG via RDF-typing; 3) Align nodes with ontologies (PO, RO, WTO) using PlantDeBERTa; 4) Visually ground graph using VLM + wheat-segmentation ViT for attention-based softmaps; 5) Connect multimodal subgraphs temporally via Plant_Obs_Id nodes.

Result: Evaluated on 500 curated WisWheat samples using Pointing Game accuracy, VWSD, and rank-based metrics; successfully maps complex field observations to structured graphs.

Conclusion: Enables automated field note auditing, temporal stress monitoring, and precise spatial trait localization for wheat breeders.

Abstract: High-throughput plant phenotyping generates valuable data that often remains trapped in unstructured text and isolated RGB images. To bridge this semantic gap, we propose a framework for constructing a multimodal granular Knowledge Graph (KG) to monitor genotype-phenotype interactions across time and experiments. In this work, we focus on wheat Triticum aestivum as a representative target crop to validate our methodology across complex canopy environments. Our pipeline first distills noisy field notes to extract entities and relations, dynamically constructing the KG by converting unique instances into hierarchical class entities via RDF-typing. These graph nodes are then aligned with standardized ontologies (PO, RO, WTO) using PlantDeBERTa. To visually ground the constructed graph, a Vision-Language Model paired with a wheat-segmentation ViT generates attention-based softmaps, linking specific KG entities directly to image pixels. We introduce a central observation node Plant_Obs_Id to connect these multimodal subgraphs temporally. Evaluated on 500 curated WisWheat samples using Pointing Game accuracy, Visual Word Sense Disambiguation (VWSD), and rank-based metrics, our neuro-symbolic approach successfully maps complex field observations to a structured graph. This enables automated field note auditing, temporal stress monitoring, and precise spatial trait localization for wheat breeders.

</details>


### [598] [Unbiased Alignment for Large Language Models with Noisy Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03248)
*Jialiang Wang,Xianming Liu,Xiong Zhou,Hui Liu,Haoliang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces theoretical frameworks (URM and UDPO) to correct preference noise in human feedback for LLM alignment, enabling unbiased training directly from noisy datasets without clean supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods like RLHF and DPO are vulnerable to significant noise in real-world preference datasets, which degrades alignment quality. There is a need for noise-tolerant unbiased alignment methods.

Method: The authors propose the Unbiased Reward Model (URM) loss and Unbiased Direct Preference Optimization (UDPO) loss, which mathematically correct distortion induced by preference noise, enabling unbiased training directly from noisy datasets without clean ground-truth supervision.

Result: The methods are theoretically proven to be noise-tolerant, parameter downward compatible, and classification-calibrated. Comprehensive experiments show they outperform state-of-the-art baselines across diverse datasets.

Conclusion: URM and UDPO provide a robust theoretical framework for unbiased alignment under noisy preferences, offering practical improvements for real-world LLM alignment without requiring clean labels.

Abstract: The alignment of large language models with human preferences is commonly achieved through Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback or Direct Preference Optimization. However, these methods are vulnerable to the significant noise prevalent in real-world preference datasets. To address this critical issue, we present a theoretical framework for unbiased alignment, introducing the Unbiased Reward Model (URM) loss and the Unbiased Direct Preference Optimization (UDPO) loss. By mathematically correcting the distortion induced by preference noise, our novel objectives enable unbiased model training directly from noisy datasets, without requiring clean ground-truth supervision. We provide rigorous theoretical analyses demonstrating that our methods are noise-tolerant, parameter downward compatible, and classification-calibrated. Comprehensive experiments across diverse datasets demonstrate that our approaches outperform state-of-the-art baselines. Code available at: https://github.com/cswjl/unbiased-alignment.

</details>


### [599] [From Global to Local: Efficient Regional Weather Downscaling with Global Weather Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03279)
*Wiktor Kamzela,Jakub Kubiak,Adam Dobosz,Jędrzej Miczke,Anatol Kaczmarek,Piotr Wyrwiński,Wojciech Stefaniak,Wojciech Kotłowski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A foundation-model-driven downscaling framework augments a pretrained weather model with lightweight multi-scale prediction heads to refine global forecasts to regional resolutions (two-order-of-magnitude grid-cell increase) without retraining, achieving better accuracy than NWP at lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional limited area models require expensive simulations, and learning-based super-resolution approaches overlook statistical and physical mismatches across scales. The goal is accurate regional prediction that resolves fine-scale structure while remaining consistent with global dynamics.

Method: Uses a pretrained weather foundation model backbone with lightweight, multi-scale prediction heads operating directly in its latent space to learn regional refinements from global forecasts. Training targets are regional numerical simulations, evaluated against gridded data and ground-based station observations.

Result: Improved accuracy over NWP on most metrics at a fraction of computational cost. The latent-space approach offers better downscaling than standard image-based super-resolution methods.

Conclusion: Building on a globally pre-trained weather foundation model's latent space provides superior downscaling capabilities for regional weather prediction, enabling efficient fine-scale refinement without retraining the backbone.

Abstract: Accurate regional weather prediction requires resolving fine-scale structure while remaining consistent with global dynamics. Traditional limited area models rely on computationally expensive simulations, while many learning-based approaches frame the problem as super-resolution, overlooking statistical and physical mismatches across scales. We propose a foundation-model-driven downscaling framework that learns regional refinements of global forecasts by augmenting a pretrained weather model backbone with lightweight, multi-scale prediction heads operating directly in its latent space. Despite being trained on substantially coarser inputs, the pretrained backbone supports regional adaptation at resolutions corresponding to a two-order-of-magnitude increase in grid-cell resolution, without the need for retraining. The proposed approach uses regional numerical simulations as training targets and is evaluated not only against gridded datasets but also against ground-based weather station observations, enabling analysis of systematic biases between global reanalysis, regional simulations, and in-situ weather station observations. Our experiments show improved accuracy in comparison to NWP on most of the metrics at the fraction of computational cost. Moreover, we observe that building on a latent space of globally pre-trained weather foundation model offers better downscaling capabilities than the standard image-based super-resolution approaches.

</details>


### [600] [A harmonised dataset for Earth system foundation models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03298)
*Carlos Rodriguez-Pardo,Massimo Tavoni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WorldTensor is a harmonised global dataset that aligns hundreds of environmental and socioeconomic variables to a standardized 0.25° grid, enabling multimodal Earth system foundation models that integrate both physical climate data and human systems data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing foundation models for Earth systems focus primarily on physical climate and weather data, lacking representation of human systems (infrastructure, hazards, socioeconomic data). There is no unified global training resource that combines both environmental and socioeconomic data on a common grid.

Method: The authors regridded inputs from heterogeneous native resolutions and projections, rasterised point/vector datasets into gridded fields, and reconciled temporal coverages from daily observations to sparse multiyear snapshots. Outputs are distributed as NetCDF files with standardized CF metadata conventions.

Result: A harmonised 0.25° spatial grid, annual temporal framework dataset integrating reanalysis products, remote sensing, emissions inventories, land use reconstructions, hydrological observations, infrastructure, hazards, and socioeconomic indicators.

Conclusion: WorldTensor provides a reproducible, standardized resource for training and evaluating foundation models that learn coupled dynamics across environmental and human systems at planetary scale.

Abstract: Foundation models for Earth systems have so far been trained primarily on physical climate and weather data, with limited representation of the human systems that both drive and respond to environmental change. The lack of a unified global training resource that combines climate, land, ocean, cryosphere, infrastructure, hazards, and socioeconomic data on a common grid hinders progress toward truly multimodal Earth system foundation models. We present WorldTensor, a harmonised global dataset that aligns hundreds of environmental and socioeconomic variables to a standardised 0.25$^\circ$ spatial grid and annual temporal framework. WorldTensor integrates reanalysis products, remote sensing, emissions inventories, land use reconstructions, hydrological observations, infrastructure and hazard datasets, and socioeconomic indicators within a single representation designed for machine learning workflows. To build the dataset, we regridded inputs across heterogeneous native resolutions and projections, rasterised point and vector datasets into spatially meaningful gridded fields, and reconciled temporal coverages ranging from daily observations to sparse multiyear socioeconomic snapshots. All outputs are distributed as NetCDF files with standardised coordinates, variable metadata, and a common CF metadata convention. WorldTensor provides a reproducible resource for training and evaluating foundation models that learn coupled dynamics across environmental and human systems at planetary scale.

</details>


### [601] [Statistically Meaningful Geometry (SMG) Beyond the Euclidean Paradigm, with Application to Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03329)
*Bing Cheng,Yi-Shuai Niu,Howell Tong,Shing-Tung Yau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMG introduces an information-geometric framework using fiber bundles and Ehresmann connections to filter out gauge noise, theoretically bounding hallucinations and eliminating catastrophic forgetting in over-parameterized models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical learning theory fails in massively over-parameterized models (LLMs, biological networks) due to flat gauge valleys that make generalization metrics vacuous and cause hallucination/forgetting.

Method: Models total state space as a differential fiber bundle with an Ehresmann connection that separates vertical gauge noise (SID) from horizontal learning directions (SVDχ), enabling geometrically constrained inference and sequential adaptation.

Result: Out-of-distribution variance is bounded by the finite diameter of the quotient base manifold; non-asymptotic elimination of catastrophic forgetting via orthogonal projection of downstream updates.

Conclusion: SMG replaces empirical heuristics with coordinate-free topological constraints, bridging differential geometry with reliable AI systems.

Abstract: Conventional uniform convergence bounds and empirical risk minimization break down in massive over-parameterized models, such as large language transformers and biological sequence networks. With near-infinite unconstrained internal degrees of freedom, their optimization landscapes develop flat vertical gauge valleys, rendering classical generalization metrics vacuous and inducing severe pathologies, specifically generative hallucination and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce the Statistically Meaningful Geometry (SMG) framework, an information-geometric paradigm lifting deterministic parametric models into infinite-dimensional non-parametric Orlicz statistical manifolds. Modeling the total state space as a differential fiber bundle ($\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{B}, π, \mathcal{V}, \mathcal{H}, ω$), we establish a Two-Fold Inference Paradigm. We formalize an Ehresmann connection 1-form $ω$ as a dynamic geometric filter that strips away vertical gauge noise (Structural Internal Directions, or SID) and isolates learning trajectories along the strictly non-degenerate horizontal distribution (Statistical Variational Directions, or SVD$χ$). We prove that under connection-filtered pre-training, out-of-distribution predictive variance is strictly upper-bounded by the finite diameter of the identifiable quotient base manifold $\mathcal{B}$, establishing a hard geometric containment of generative hallucinations. By projecting downstream updates onto the orthogonal complement of the historical horizontal carriage, we formalize the SMG Sequential Adaptation Flow, proving the total non-asymptotic elimination of catastrophic forgetting. SMG replaces empirical fine-tuning heuristics with coordinate-free topological constraints, bridging advanced differential geometry with structural reliability in AI.

</details>


### [602] [FedAvg for HAR: Exploring the Tradeoff Between Personalized and Generalization Accuracy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03334)
*Andrea De Luna,Susanna Peretti,Chiara Contoli,Alessandro Bogliolo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the FedAvg algorithm for federated learning in Human Activity Recognition, comparing its personalization and generalization capabilities against centralized and local learning approaches, including under stressful data distribution changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To find a tradeoff between models' personalization and generalization capabilities in federated learning, and evaluate performance under realistic conditions like changing client data distributions.

Method: Designed and implemented several testing scenarios comparing centralized, local, and federated paradigm performances, plus a scenario emulating changes in clients' data. Applied the FedAvg algorithm to the Human Activity Recognition (HAR) domain.

Result: FedAvg shows higher personalization capabilities while maintaining good generalization compared to centralized learning, but this advantage diminishes under stressful conditions like varying class distribution over clients.

Conclusion: Federated learning offers benefits over centralized learning in personalization-generalization tradeoff, but its advantages are not robust under non-ideal conditions such as uneven class distribution across clients.

Abstract: The federated learning (FL) paradigm fosters distributed pervasive computing combined with artificial intelligence techniques, allowing for optimized data usage and improved mitigation of privacy concerns. Indeed, model training occurs on the client's local devices, and model parameters are subsequently shared with a centralized server. However, there is a need to find a tradeoff between models' personalization and generalization capabilities. In this paper, we design and implement several testing scenarios devoted to evaluating and comparing the centralized, local, and federated paradigm performances. We also design and implement a scenario that emulates a change in clients' data. We then present experimental results of the FedAvg algorithm applied to the Human Activity Recognition (HAR) domain to understand the trade-off between personalized and generalized accuracy. Results show that, although FedAvg confirms a higher degree of personalization capabilities while keeping a high degree of generalization with respect to the traditional centralized learning, this result is not so obvious under stressful conditions, such as when varying class distribution over clients.

</details>


### [603] [CSympNet-ID: conformal-symplectic map learning for linearly damped Hamiltonian systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03339)
*Jiale Gong,Pengzhan Jin,Dongyang Kuang,Lu Li,Yifa Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSympNet-ID is a framework that learns dissipative dynamics from discrete observations by enforcing conformal symplecticity, using a symplectic neural core with diagonal scaling layers and a learned damping parameter, outperforming baselines in data-scarce, contraction-law recovery, and high-dimensional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning dissipative dynamics from discrete observations is essential for reliable long-horizon prediction and meaningful physical parameter identification; exact flow of linearly damped Hamiltonian systems is conformally symplectic, and existing methods often fail to preserve this structure without penalty terms or projections.

Method: The architecture composes an exact symplectic neural core with explicit diagonal scaling layers whose factors are parameterized exponentially by a scalar damping-rate parameter, enforcing exact discrete conformal symplecticity by construction. They also establish a scaling-conjugacy factorization and derive a pointwise-in-step density result.

Result: Evaluated on an irregular-step damped oscillator, damped spring-mass chain, damped nonlinear cubic oscillator, and high-dimensional extensions; CSympNet-ID gives the most favorable overall results, particularly in data-scarce regimes, target contraction-law recovery, and high-dimensional tests where unstructured baselines degrade rapidly.

Conclusion: Enforcing conformal symplectic structure by construction improves both prediction accuracy and physical interpretability in learning dissipative dynamics, especially under limited data and high dimensionality.

Abstract: Learning dissipative dynamics from discrete observations is essential for reliable long-horizon prediction and physically meaningful parameter identification. For linearly damped Hamiltonian systems, the exact flow is generally not symplectic but conformally symplectic, contracting the canonical symplectic form by a scalar factor that reflects the net dissipation. We propose Conformal Symplectic Networks with damping identification (CSympNet-ID), a discrete-time map-learning framework that learns the one-step flow map directly from snapshot pairs while enforcing exact discrete conformal symplecticity by construction, without penalty terms or projection. The architecture composes an exact symplectic neural core with explicit diagonal scaling layers whose factors are parameterized exponentially by a scalar damping-rate parameter, thereby guaranteeing positivity and interpretability of the learned dissipation factor. We establish a scaling-conjugacy factorization for conformal symplectic maps and derive a pointwise-in-step density result for CSympNet-ID. We evaluate an irregular-step damped oscillator, a damped spring-mass chain, a damped nonlinear cubic oscillator, and additional high-dimensional extensions. CSympNet-ID gives the most favorable overall results among the compared models in the reported experiments, particularly in data-scarce regimes, target contraction-law recovery, and high-dimensional tests where unstructured baselines degrade rapidly.

</details>


### [604] [The Multiscale Single-Index Model: A Stylized Model for Hierarchical Feature Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03347)
*Joan Bruna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a fine-grained analysis of the Multiscale Single-Index Model (MSIM) using Edgeworth expansions, revealing a hierarchical Wiener chaos structure. The authors prove that online SGD on the correlation objective achieves sample complexity matching the linear case, and establish shallow-network lower bounds that quantify the benefit of depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how deep architectures learn multiscale representations and to overcome the limitations of existing perturbative analysis in MSIM, which is insufficient for analyzing backpropagation gradient-based methods.

Method: Fine-grained Wiener chaos analysis using Edgeworth expansions to reveal hierarchical structure; online SGD on correlation objective with unified layer timescales.

Result: Online SGD achieves 1-o_d(1) recovery with n = Õ(d^{K-1}) samples, matching the linear counterpart sample complexity; shallow-network approximation lower bounds quantify depth benefits.

Conclusion: The MSIM is a natural nonlinear analogue of Tensor PCA with tractable hierarchical structure; deep architectures provably benefit from depth in this setting, and gradient-based methods can match spectral methods' sample complexity.

Abstract: We consider the Multiscale Single-Index Model (MSIM), first introduced in \cite{oymak2021learning}, as a stylized model for hierarchical learning with \emph{scale separation}. Each layer extracts a shared single-index feature at one physical scale and passes it to the next, thus defining a tractable setting in which to study how deep architectures learn multiscale representations. Under non-degeneracy and delocalization assumptions on the link function and planted features respectively, for fixed depth $K$ and local scale $d$, the first Wiener chaos of the target behaves as a perturbed spiked tensor, where the perturbation of order $d^{-1/2}$ comes from the non-linearity -- revealing the MSIM as a natural non-linear analogue of the Tensor PCA model \cite{montanari2014statistical}. While this perturbative picture is sufficient to enable efficient spectral recovery based on Tensor unfolding (as already observed in \cite{oymak2021learning}), it is not precise enough for the analysis of backpropagation gradient-based methods. In this work, we address this limitation by performing a fine-grained analysis of the Wiener chaos using Edgeworth expansions. In the first chaos, this gives a finite-rank hierarchy at scales $d^{-q/2}$. In higher chaoses, balanced flattenings exhibit staircase singular-value plateaus of size $d^{-ρ/2}$ and multiplicity $d^ρ$ under a natural higher-chaos non-cancellation condition. Using this higher-chaos structure, and under an additional slow Hermite-energy tail condition, we first establish shallow-network approximation lower bounds, quantifying the benefit of depth in this model. Next, and most importantly, we prove that online SGD on the correlation objective, where all layers evolve in the same timescale, achieves $1 - o_d(1)$ recovery with $n = \widetilde{O}( d^{K-1})$ samples, recovering the same sample complexity as in the linear counterpart.

</details>


### [605] [PedestrianDiffusion: Multimodal Generative Denoising and Dense State Estimation for Inertial Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03349)
*I-Hao Lu,Dongsoo Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PedestrianDiffusion is a frequency-domain generative framework that reformulates 6D pedestrian inertial navigation as a conditional denoising process, achieving SOTA results by stabilizing reverse diffusion via spectral covariance bounding and enabling zero-shot generalization across sensor noise profiles using vision-language embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Consumer-grade MEMS inertial sensors suffer from stochastic noise, and traditional deterministic neural networks exhibit "estimation jittering" that sacrifices high-frequency kinematic fidelity for numerical stability.

Method: 1) Multimodal spectral-domain generative framework treating 6D state estimation as continuous conditional denoising; 2) Frequency-domain operation with bounded spectral covariance as a mathematical preconditioner; 3) Zero-shot semantic conditioning via vision-language embeddings as categorical priors; 4) Single-step deterministic probability flow ODE solver (T=1) for edge deployment.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on OxIOD, RIDI, RoNIN, and TLIO benchmarks with unprecedented robustness to impulse perturbations and coupled 6D kinematic drift.

Conclusion: This work provides a rigorous algorithmic blueprint for next-generation Neural Inertial Measurement Units (N-IMUs), establishing viability of generative architectures for asynchronous batch trajectory refinement on edge hardware.

Abstract: The accuracy of consumer-grade inertial navigation is bottlenecked by the stochastic noise of Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). Traditional deterministic neural architectures often succumb to ``estimation jittering,'' sacrificing high-frequency kinematic fidelity for numerical stability.
