We propose PedestrianDiffusion, a multimodal spectral-domain generative framework reformulating dense 6D state estimation as a continuous conditional denoising process. By operating in the frequency domain, our formulation bounds the spectral covariance, acting as a mathematical preconditioner to stabilize the reverse diffusion trajectory. Furthermore, we introduce a zero-shot semantic conditioning mechanism leveraging vision-language embeddings as categorical priors to generalize across heterogeneous sensor noise profiles. To address the computational intractability of generative tracking, we deploy a single-step deterministic probability flow ODE solver ($T=1$). This yields high-capacity asynchronous batch trajectory refinement, establishing the viability of generative architectures for asynchronous batch trajectory refinement on edge hardware. Extensive evaluations on the OxIOD, RIDI, RoNIN, and TLIO benchmarks demonstrate that PedestrianDiffusion achieves state-of-the-art performance, exhibiting unprecedented robustness to impulse perturbations and coupled 6D kinematic drift. This work provides a rigorous algorithmic blueprint for next-generation Neural Inertial Measurement Units (N-IMUs).

</details>


### [606] [Amortising Bayesian Experimental Design for Sequential Information Gathering in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03426)
*Jakob Hartmann,James Harvey,Jhonathan Navott,Erik Y. Wang,Luckeciano C. Melo,Flaviu Cipcigan,Cheng Zhang,Alessandro Abate*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASIG fine-tunes LLMs using Bayesian Experimental Design with an Expected Information Gain reward, achieving over 2x success rate and 25x inference cost reduction on 20 Questions, with transferable gains to medical diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle to gather information effectively across multi-turn interactions in sequential decision-making settings, despite having strong reasoning and world-knowledge capabilities.

Method: ASIG amortises Bayesian Experimental Design (BED) into LLM policies via multi-turn Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) with an Expected Information Gain (EIG) reward.

Result: Over 2x success rate improvement over 7B base model on 20 Questions, over 25x inference cost reduction vs. BED-LLM, and improved information-seeking on MediQ (unseen medical diagnosis benchmark).

Conclusion: Amortising BED into LLM policies is an effective and computationally efficient approach to sequential information gathering, with learned strategies transferring out of distribution.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong reasoning and world-knowledge capabilities, yet often struggle to gather information effectively across the multi-turn interactions required in sequential decision-making settings. We introduce Amortised Sequential Information Gathering (ASIG), a fine-tuning approach that amortises Bayesian Experimental Design (BED) into LLM policies via a multi-turn extension of Group Relative Policy Optimisation with an Expected Information Gain reward. Evaluated on the 20 Questions task, ASIG more than doubles the success rate of the 7B base model and reduces inference cost by over $25\times$ relative to BED-LLM, a competitive inference-time baseline. Applied to MediQ, a medical diagnosis benchmark unseen during training, ASIG improves information-seeking performance at the 7B scale, suggesting that the learned strategies can transfer out of distribution. Our findings show that amortising BED into LLM policies provides an effective and computationally efficient approach to sequential information gathering.

</details>


### [607] [How Much of the Routing Gap Is Real? Decomposing the Router-to-Oracle Gap into Reproducible Specialist Advantage and Single-Draw Label Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03436)
*Teng-Ruei Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that the "oracle gap" in LLM routing benchmarks is partly noise from single-draw stochastic decoding, not just model specialization. Test-time resampling (best-of-K) recovers the noise floor that no single-commit router can close, and a multi-sample protocol is released to disentangle reproducible advantage from stochastic noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM router evaluations compare against a per-instance oracle defined from a single correctness label per (query, model). Under stochastic decoding, this oracle is just one Bernoulli draw—not a reproducible property. This conflates noise with genuine model specialization, making the reported router gap misleading.

Method: The paper decomposes the expected per-instance oracle into reproducible headroom (O^repro) and a non-negative selection floor (Δ). It proves an asymmetry: no single-commit router can close Δ, but best-of-K resampling on the committed model does. Noise-share bounds are derived and estimated via fresh k≥20 resampling with dependence- and guessing-floor-corrected bounds on LLMRouterBench (33 models, 391,645 instances).

Result: Single-draw noise is a substantial minority of the oracle gap—larger on unsaturated benchmarks, approaching half on the hardest queries. The majority remains recoverable specialist advantage. The selection floor Δ is closed by test-time sampling at the oracle's own budget, not by any single-commit router.

Conclusion: Router benchmarks must account for stochastic noise. The paper contributes a multi-sample oracle evaluation protocol to disentangle reproducible model advantage from stochastic noise, enabling more honest and actionable routing assessment.

Abstract: Routing among large language models (LLMs) promises better quality at lower cost, motivated by the reported gap between learned routers and a per-instance oracle. But that oracle is computed from a single correctness label per (query, model), so under stochastic decoding it is one Bernoulli draw, not a reproducible property. We recast the question structurally: the expected per-instance oracle decomposes as $O^{\exp}=O^{\mathrm{repro}}+Δ$, into reproducible single-commit headroom $O^{\mathrm{repro}}$ and a non-negative single-commit selection floor $Δ$. Our main result is a recoverability asymmetry: this floor is closed by no single-commit router, yet is recovered by test-time sampling -- best-of-$K$ on the committed model, at the oracle's own budget, dominates the independent-pool single-draw oracle. The cap needs no cross-model independence; we prove it with the exact decomposition and noise-share bounds that shrink as the budget grows. The procedure adds no new router, only resampling. The floor's magnitude is a prospective, conservative localization, not an audit: our primary target LLMRouterBench (33 models, 391,645 instances) defines its oracle as a per-query union over single $T=0.2$ generations -- by construction a union of stochastic single draws. Since $O^{\mathrm{repro}}$ is non-identifiable from the released $k=1$ matrix, we estimate the noise share by fresh $k\ge20$ resampling under one-sided, dependence- and guessing-floor-corrected bounds, recasting 'model-recall failure' as thin-support union inflation. On a controlled open-model re-generation, single-draw noise is a substantial minority of the gap -- larger on an unsaturated benchmark, approaching half on the hardest queries where no model is reliable -- while the majority remains recoverable specialist advantage. We release a multi-sample oracle evaluation protocol for routing benchmarks.

</details>


### [608] [No Time Like the Present: Agentic Test-Time Training for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03441)
*Yanbo Wang,Jinhua Hao,Yuze Shi,Kun Yuan,Ming Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Agentic Test-Time Training (aTTT), a token-level reweighting method that mitigates policy drift in long-horizon LLM agent episodes by downweighting repeated n-gram tokens during continuous test-time training, achieving up to 5.0 and 4.9 point improvements on ALFWorld and SWE-bench Lite respectively with only 1.9x overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents degrade over long episodes due to revisiting explored states, repeating failed actions, and losing effective strategies, while existing TTT methods only adapt once to fixed inputs rather than continuously over multi-turn agent trajectories.

Method: aTTT uses token-level reweighting that downweights loss on tokens appearing in repeated n-grams from prior updates while keeping novel tokens fully weighted, implemented via a concurrent serving system using vLLM's runtime LoRA API.

Result: aTTT improves success by up to 5.0 points on ALFWorld and 4.9 points on SWE-bench Lite, with only 1.9x overhead over no-TTT baselines. Gains are concentrated where models already have competence but drift over long trajectories.

Conclusion: aTTT mainly preserves existing competence rather than teaching new abilities, and distinguishing update-text repetition via n-gram analysis effectively separates helpful self-training loops from harmful drift amplification.

Abstract: LLM agents often degrade over long episodes: as trajectories grow, they revisit explored states, repeat failed actions, and lose strategies that previously worked. Test-time training (TTT) offers a way to adapt model weights to the evolving task state, but existing LLM TTT methods largely adapt once to a fixed input. We study continuous TTT in multi-turn agent episodes, where each update changes the policy that generates later training text. This creates a self-training loop that helps when new trajectory information appears, but can amplify drift when the agent gets stuck and repeatedly trains on similar text. We find that update-text repetition distinguishes these regimes and introduce Agentic Test-Time Training (aTTT), a token-level reweighting method that downweights the loss on tokens appearing in repeated $n$-grams from prior updates while leaving novel tokens fully weighted. To run such updates inside live episodes, we build a concurrent serving system using vLLM's runtime LoRA API, limiting overhead to 1.9$\times$ the no-TTT cost. aTTT improves success by up to 5.0 points on ALFWorld and 4.9 points on SWE-bench Lite. The gains concentrate where models already have task competence but drift over long trajectories, suggesting that aTTT mainly preserves existing competence rather than teaching new abilities.

</details>


### [609] [Best-of-Better-$N$: Generating Pre-Aligned Responses with In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03453)
*Eric Lei,Hsiang Hsu,Chun-Fu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Best-of-Better-N (BoBN) improves inference-time alignment by using in-context learning with restyled high-reward examples to shift sampling distribution toward better responses, outperforming standard Best-of-N.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time alignment methods like Best-of-N are limited by the reference LLM's response quality—if the model assigns low probability to high-reward responses, selection strategies fail. The paper aims to overcome this fundamental limitation.

Method: BoBN retrieves high-reward examples relevant to the input, restyles them via the reference LLM to match the target task format, and uses these as in-context examples to shift sampling toward high-reward regions. The method includes theoretical analysis of how in-context learning shifts output distribution.

Result: BoBN produces higher-quality responses, achieving better performance with fixed N and requiring smaller N to reach target performance on safety alignment and mathematical reasoning benchmarks across several reference LLMs.

Conclusion: In-context learning with restyled high-reward examples can provably shift LLM output distributions toward high-reward regions, offering a practical and theoretically grounded improvement over standard Best-of-N inference-time alignment.

Abstract: Inference-time alignment methods, such as Best-of-$N$, offer a flexible alternative to training-based alignment by using reward models to select high-quality responses generated by a reference LLM. However, the efficacy of these methods is inherently limited by the response quality: if the reference LLM assigns negligible probability to high-reward responses, no selection strategy will succeed in finding aligned outputs. In this work, we propose Best-of-Better-$N$ (BoBN), an in context learning-based generation framework to address this challenge. Our method utilizes retrieval from high-reward examples relevant to the input query and task. Crucially, we introduce a restyling step where retrieved responses are rewritten by the reference LLM to align with the target task's format and style. These restyled examples are used in-context to shift the sampling distribution toward the high-reward region. We analytically characterize how in-context learning shifts the output distribution of pretrained transformers toward the high-reward region, resulting in provable benefits on the target task. We then evaluate BoBN on safety alignment and mathematical reasoning benchmarks across several reference LLMs. BoBN's higher-quality responses enable better performance to be achieved when the number of responses $N$ is fixed, and smaller $N$ required to achieve a target performance.

</details>


### [610] [A Near-Linear-Time Solver for Graph $p$-Laplacian Semi-Supervised Learning via Continuation in $p$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03503)
*Oren E. Livne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a damped chord-Newton continuation method for $p$-Laplacian semi-supervised learning that maintains well-conditioned linear systems across all $p$ values, achieving near-linear empirical scaling ($m^{0.96}$-$m^{1.19}$) on large graphs, handling up to $6.8\times10^7$ edges, and significantly outperforming the standard $p=2$ approach (64% vs 36% accuracy on MNIST with one label per class).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard quadratic ($p=2$) graph-based SSL degenerates to a near-constant function when labels are scarce in $d\ge2$ dimensions, but the well-posed $p>d$ Laplacian formulation lacks efficient near-linear solvers due to ill-conditioning from degenerate conductance weights.

Method: Recasts $p$-Laplacian SSL as a source-form nonlinear Laplacian flow $Bρ_p(B^\top x)=b$, solved by damped chord-Newton continuation in $p$, keeping every linearized system well-conditioned and delegatable to a near-linear Laplacian engine.

Result: Empirical scaling of $m^{0.96}$-$m^{1.02}$ on size-scaled graph families, $m^{1.19}$ pooled across 228 SuiteSparse graphs vs $m^{1.45}$ for direct factorization; handles $6.8\times10^7$-edge social network in minutes; $1.5$-$14\times$ faster than FCL solver; MNIST $p=3$ achieves 64% vs 36% for $p=2$ with one label per class.

Conclusion: The proposed method closes the gap between the theoretical well-posedness of $p$-Laplacian SSL and practical near-linear solvers, making nonlinear graph SSL both scalable and accurate for large-scale problems.

Abstract: Graph-based semi-supervised learning (SSL) propagates a few labels over a similarity graph by minimizing a Dirichlet-type energy. The standard quadratic ($p=2$) energy reduces to a single graph-Laplacian solve, but it degenerates exactly where SSL is most useful when labels are scarce: gathering more unlabeled data drives the $p=2$ estimate to a near-constant function whenever $d\ge2$ (Nadler-Srebro-Zhou). Well-posedness requires the nonlinear $p$-Laplacian energy with $p>d$. Existing solvers reduce this to a sequence of weighted Laplacian solves, but their reference implementations use a direct sparse factorization or ichol-preconditioned CG instead. Plugging a near-linear Laplacian solver is not straightforward: at large $p$ the conductance weights degenerate near flat-gradient edges, making the system nearly singular and causing stagnation without a damped outer iteration. We close this gap. Recasting $p$-Laplacian SSL as a source-form nonlinear Laplacian flow $Bρ_p(B^\top x)=b$ and solving by damped chord-Newton continuation in $p$, every linearized system stays well-conditioned and can be delegated to a near-linear Laplacian engine. On size-scaled graph families the wall-clock is empirically $m^{0.96}$-$m^{1.02}$ per family (approximate Cholesky default), and a pooled fit across 228 SuiteSparse graphs gives $m^{1.19}$ vs.\ $m^{1.45}$ for direct factorization; the solver handles a $6.8\times10^7$-edge social network in minutes. Memory is the binding constraint: Cholesky fill reaches $10$-$280\times$ the graph nonzeros vs.\ our $O(m)$ hierarchy. Against the released FCL solver we are $1.5$-$14\times$ faster at matched accuracy. On MNIST $10$-NN, $p=3$ scores $64\%$ at one label per class vs.\ $36\%$ for $p=2$. Code: https://github.com/orenlivne/np.

</details>


### [611] [Mixture-of-Gaussians-Guided Schedule Design for Brownian Bridge Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03517)
*Ron Levi,Michael Elad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a principled analytical framework for designing bridge schedules in Brownian Bridge Diffusion Models (BBDM) for image restoration, using a Mixture-of-Gaussians (MoG) prior to derive closed-form expressions and two complementary criteria (Wasserstein for perceptual quality, MSE for reconstruction fidelity), revealing an inherent tradeoff and universal schedules that work across degradations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The choice of bridge schedule in BBDM is typically inherited from heuristics, and a principled analytical framework for schedule design has been lacking.

Method: The authors analyze BBDM reverse dynamics under a Mixture-of-Gaussians (MoG) prior, deriving closed-form ideal posterior, MMSE denoiser, and a tractable surrogate for the BBDM-induced reconstruction law. They formulate two schedule-design objectives: Wasserstein criterion (perceptual quality) and MSE criterion (reconstruction fidelity).

Result: The paper proves the existence of universal schedules for both criteria that are independent of the degradation and prior. Experiments on controlled MoG settings confirm theory-practice alignment, and FFHQ dataset experiments across inpainting, deblurring, and super-resolution validate practical value.

Conclusion: The work exposes an inherent tradeoff between perceptual quality and reconstruction fidelity, and provides a principled foundation for schedule design in BBDM-based image restoration.

Abstract: Brownian Bridge Diffusion Models (BBDM) offer an appealing framework for image restoration and inverse problems by constructing a stochastic bridge from the clean signal directly to the degraded observation, rather than to pure noise. Despite their promise, the choice of bridge schedule is typically inherited from heuristics, and a principled analytical framework for schedule design has been lacking. In this work, we develop such a framework by offering a novel analysis of BBDM reverse dynamics under a Mixture-of-Gaussians (MoG) prior. This setting yields a closed-form ideal posterior and a corresponding MMSE denoiser, while the BBDM-induced reconstruction law is captured analytically through a tractable surrogate. Building on these expressions, we formulate two complementary schedule-design objectives: a Wasserstein criterion targeting perceptual quality and an MSE criterion targeting reconstruction fidelity. Our work exposes an inherent tradeoff between the two and proves the existence of universal schedules for both that are independent of the degradation and prior. Extensive experiments on controlled MoG settings confirm full alignment between theory and practice, and experiments on the FFHQ dataset across inpainting, deblurring, and super-resolution tasks validate the practical value of our schedule-design criteria.

</details>


### [612] [On the Convergence of Adam, Revisited](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03519)
*Steven Heilman,Sampad Mohanty*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that projected Adam can have non-zero average regret for arbitrary moment decay parameters, extending a previous result that required β₁ < √β₂, using a three-periodic sequence of linear functions with slopes c, -1, -1 where c > 2, and this result generalizes to many Adam variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To prove that Adam and its variants can suffer non-zero average regret even without the constraint β₁ < √β₂, showing a fundamental limitation of these optimization algorithms.

Method: Using a three-periodic sequence of linear functions on [-1,1] with slopes c, -1, -1 (where c > 2), and extending this to i.i.d. variants and multiple Adam variants including AdamW, RMSProp, NAdam, Adan, AdaMax, Muon.

Result: Projected Adam with arbitrary β₁,β₂ ∈ [0,1) can have average regret bounded away from zero, generalizing the previous result that required β₁ < √β₂.

Conclusion: Adam and its variants have inherent limitations in online optimization, exhibiting non-zero average regret even under relaxed parameter conditions.

Abstract: We show that projected Adam for online optimization with arbitrary moment decay parameters $β_1,β_2\in[0,1)$ can have average regret bounded away from zero. A similar result of Reddi-Kale-Kumar from 2018 required $β_1<\sqrt{β_2}$. Similar to their result, we use a three-periodic sequence of linear functions on $[-1,1]$ with slopes $c,-1,-1$, though we use $c$ slightly larger than $2$. This nonzero average regret result extends to Adam variants such as AdamW, RMSProp, NAdam, Adan, AdaMax, Muon, and to an i.i.d. variant of the three-periodic sequence of slopes for Adam.

</details>


### [613] [Co-Adaptive Multi-Task LoRA: Transfer-Aware, Label-Free Control of Domain Participation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03522)
*Wei Zhang,Lin Tang,Ming Zhao,Yuxuan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoDA is a lightweight controller that dynamically adjusts domain participation in multi-task LoRA fine-tuning by using unlabeled probes to measure domain competence and cross-domain affinity, improving performance over uniform mixing while using half the data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-task LoRA fine-tuning uses fixed uniform mixtures, ignoring which domains should participate more and which domains transfer positively vs. negatively, leading to suboptimal co-learning.

Method: CoDA uses forward passes on small unlabeled probes to extract competence signals (tracking headroom and learning speed) and cross-domain affinities (predicting pairwise transfer). These feed into an entropy-regularized quadratic program that jointly sets each domain's loss weight and sampling share, rewarding synergistic domains and damping interfering ones.

Result: CoDA outperforms uniform mixing, learned mixtures, gradient-surgery methods, and online data selection across five heterogeneous domains and two backbones, while using half the data and reducing cross-domain gradient conflict.

Conclusion: Domain participation in multi-task fine-tuning can be cheaply and adaptively controlled via unlabeled probes; the competence signal tracks domain risk, the participation program has a unique fixed point via contraction, and the solution performs transfer-aware water-filling.

Abstract: Fine-tuning a single low-rank adapter on many domains at once is multi-task learning: the domains must be co-learned, and how they share the adapter decides whether they help or hurt one another. Most efficient fine-tuning pipelines ignore this and train on a fixed, uniform mixture, leaving two coupled questions unanswered: how much should each domain participate, and which domains should be co-trained given that some transfer positively and others interfere? We show that both answers can be read off cheaply and without labels. A forward pass of the current shared adapter over a small unlabeled probe yields, per domain, a competence signal whose level tracks remaining headroom and whose trajectory tracks learning speed; the drift of these probe representations yields a signed cross-domain affinity that predicts pairwise transfer. We fold both into CoDA, a co-adaptive controller that solves a small entropy-regularized quadratic program on the simplex to set each domain's participation -- jointly its loss weight and its share of the sampled data -- rewarding high-headroom, still-learning, mutually synergistic domains and damping interfering ones. The controller is forward-only, adds no trainable parameters, and wraps any multi-task LoRA pipeline. Across five heterogeneous domains and two backbones, CoDA improves the average over uniform mixing, learned mixtures, gradient-surgery multi-task optimizers, and online data selection while using half the data, and lowers cross-domain gradient conflict. We prove that the competence signal tracks domain risk, that the participation program has a unique fixed point reached by a contraction, and that its solution performs transfer-aware water-filling; analysis, ablations, and controls corroborate each claim.

</details>


### [614] [Aligning Language Models with Selective Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03528)
*Gaoxiang Luo,Yifan Wu,Sinian Zhang,Aryan Deshwal,Ju Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces RLSR, a reinforcement learning alignment framework that improves LLM reliability via selective prediction by optimizing the area under the risk-coverage curve (AURC), achieving better risk-coverage trade-offs than existing alignment methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are deployed in high-stakes AI systems, but their reliability remains a major concern. Existing alignment methods focus on correctness or calibration, not on enabling selective prediction where models can abstain from uncertain predictions to reduce errors and enable human-AI collaboration.

Method: The authors propose Reinforcement Learning for Selection Reward (RLSR), an alignment framework that directly optimizes the area under the risk-coverage curve (AURC) — a metric for selective prediction performance — during the post-training alignment stage.

Result: RLSR achieves substantially better risk-coverage trade-off compared to multiple alignment baselines on both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks.

Conclusion: Aligning LLMs with selective prediction metrics (specifically AURC) is an effective approach for improving reliability, enabling models to know when to abstain and defer to humans, thus outperforming traditional alignment methods focused solely on accuracy or calibration.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as critical decision-making components in high-stakes real-world AI systems, rendering LLM reliability a foremost practical concern. In this paper, we focus on enhancing LLM reliability through selective prediction (SP), a strategy that allows an LLM to only predict for inputs where it is likely to be correct (i.e., coverage) and hence reduce the error rate (i.e., risk) on that portion of inputs -- flagging the remaining inputs for future human discretion. In other words, SP improves LLM reliability by balancing the risk-coverage trade-off and enabling seamless human-AI collaboration. To integrate SP into LLMs, we focus on the LLM post-training alignment stage and propose to align LLMs with SP performance metrics, in contrast with existing LLM alignment methods that focus primarily on correctness or calibration metrics. Specifically, we propose a novel alignment framework, Reinforcement Learning for Selection Reward (RLSR), which targets the area under the risk-coverage curve (AURC) -- a popular SP performance metric -- as its alignment objective. RLSR achieves substantially better risk-coverage trade-off compared to multiple alignment baselines on both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks.

</details>


### [615] [WeightCLIP: Aligning Datasets and Models for Weight Space Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03551)
*Aron Asefaw,Konstantinos Tzevelekakis,Damian Falk,Léo Meynent,Damian Borth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WeightCLIP aligns neural network weight representations with dataset information using contrastive learning, enabling improved retrieval, generation, and refinement of models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing weight-space learning methods lack the ability to incorporate dataset information into neural network weight representations, limiting downstream applications.

Method: Uses an autoencoder to encode NN weights and a dataset encoder to encode dataset samples, then aligns both representations with a contrastive objective.

Result: WeightCLIP enables dataset-conditioned weight-space representations that improve retrieval, generation, and refinement tasks, including decoding to strong models and outperforming standard fine-tuning via latent refinement.

Conclusion: Explicitly incorporating dataset information enhances weight-space representations across multiple downstream tasks.

Abstract: Weight space learning aims to learn representations of neural network (NN) weights, enabling different downstream tasks. Existing approaches show promising performance, but lacking a way to shape these weight-space representations using information about the datasets the models were trained on, thus limiting downstream applications. We propose WeightCLIP, a method for learning a dataset-aligned latent space for neural networks, where datasets information is induced during training. The NNs are encoded as latent representations using an autoencoder, while dataset samples are encoded using a dataset encoder. The two representations are aligned using a contrastive objective, effectively reshaping the weight-space representations according to the datasets. We demonstrate that such representations can be used for different downstream tasks, including mapping dataset information to a weight-space representation that decode to strong models. In addition, we introduce a latent refinement process for generating models that outperforms standard fine-tuning. Overall, our results demonstrate that explicitly incorporating dataset information improves what can be achieved with weight-space representations across retrieval, generation, and refinement. Code will be available at https://github.com/HSG-AIML/WeightCLIP.

</details>


### [616] [Teacher Supervision over Representation Equivalence Classes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03572)
*Sang Il Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues that knowledge distillation should target the teacher's equivalence class of representations (invariant under orthogonal/isometric transformations) rather than absolute features. The authors show that capability is the teacher's output function, a class invariant, and validate their framework through restoration and grafting experiments on Qwen2.5 and Llama-3.1 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current knowledge distillation methods assume teacher representations have absolute coordinates to match, ignoring that such representations are identifiable only up to orthogonal-and-isotropic-scaling equivalence classes, making absolute feature matching ill-posed.

Method: The paper proposes a geometric framework where distillation targets class invariants (Gram structure, CKA, principal subspaces) or aligns coordinates first. This unifies feature matching, relational distillation, alignment, and grafting under one account.

Result: A restoration study showed CKA ~0.99 recovery of corrupted model's representation but not its capability. Logit matching drives capability, while hidden representation matching aligns geometry without restoring function. Recovery is confined to corpus-covered regions.

Conclusion: Knowledge distillation should target the teacher's equivalence class (invariant features) rather than absolute coordinates. Output-function (logit) matching is key for capability transfer; hidden feature matching only aligns geometry.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation is usually framed as a choice of what to match in the teacher - its logits, hidden features, or sample relations - which presupposes that the teacher's representation has absolute coordinates to match. It does not: a pretrained representation is identifiable only up to an orthogonal-and-isotropic-scaling equivalence class, so a student should learn the teacher's equivalence class, not its features. The organizing fact is that capability is the teacher's output function, a class invariant that factors through the quotient by the class action, so an objective recovers capability exactly when it is defined there. This makes absolute feature matching ill-posed, and admissible supervision a matter of targeting class invariants (Gram structure, CKA, principal subspaces) or aligning coordinates first, unifying feature matching, relational distillation, alignment, and grafting in one geometric account. We validate our framework on Qwen2.5 and Llama-3.1. A restoration study recovers a corrupted model's representation (CKA ~ 0.99) but not its capability, and an ablation isolates the cause: output-function (logit) matching drives capability, while matching hidden representations aligns geometry without restoring function. Recovery is confined to the corpus-covered region, and a graft study confirms that boundary overlap predicts transplant success but is necessary, not sufficient.

</details>


### [617] [Differentiate the Evaluator, Not the Program: An Efficient Runtime Representation for Neuro-Symbolic Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03574)
*Lucas Sheneman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces NDVM, a Native Differentiable Virtual Machine that enables efficient program-and-parameter co-search by separating symbolic structure from differentiable numeric state, achieving ~60x batch amortization and ~24x faster convergence in scientific model discovery workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter calibration is the bottleneck in program-and-parameter co-search: generating candidate programs is cheap, but each requires expensive inner-loop gradient-based optimization. Existing approaches either stage each candidate into its own differentiable graph (fast but breaks program-as-data fluidity) or use interpreter-based approaches (preserves fluidity but pays overhead dominating numerical work).

Method: NDVM separates symbolic structure (tags, symbols, environments, control) as native runtime data from numeric payloads stored in dense batched buffers with exact reverse-mode gradients. This allows one evaluator walk to be amortized across large populations of parameter vectors, avoiding per-candidate graph compilation.

Result: Achieves ~60x per-lane batch amortization, near-linear multicore scaling, forward and gradient equivalence to the reference backend, and two independent front ends. In fixed-budget co-search over LLM-proposed programs, NDVM reaches high-quality solutions ~24x sooner in wall-clock time.

Conclusion: Runtime differentiation provides a practical systems foundation for scientific discovery workflows, making program-and-parameter co-search significantly more efficient.

Abstract: AI systems increasingly propose executable scientific models whose value depends on both their symbolic structure and their fitted continuous parameters. This makes parameter calibration the bottleneck of program-and-parameter co-search: an outer loop can generate thousands of candidate programs, but each needs an inner gradient-based optimization before it can be assessed. Staging each candidate into its own differentiable graph makes individual models fast but sacrifices the program-as-data property that keeps search fluid; interpreter-based approaches preserve programs as runtime data but pay interpreter overhead that dominates the numerical work.
  We present the Native Differentiable Virtual Machine (NDVM), a runtime representation that differentiates executable programs without compiling each candidate into a separate graph. NDVM separates symbolic structure from differentiable numeric state: tags, symbols, environments, and control remain native runtime data, while numeric payloads live in dense batched buffers with exact reverse-mode gradients recorded along the realized execution trace, so one evaluator walk is amortized across large populations of parameter vectors. A locked cost model of a real differentiable self-hosted Scheme interpreter motivates the design. We realize NDVM as a native runtime with forward and gradient equivalence to the reference backend, about 60x per-lane batch amortization, near-linear multicore scaling, and two independent front ends. In fixed-budget co-search over LLM-proposed programs, NDVM reaches high-quality solutions about 24x sooner in wall-clock time, suggesting runtime differentiation as a practical systems foundation for scientific discovery workflows.

</details>


### [618] [When Geometry Aligns: Dihedral Hidden-State Transformations in UNet, ViT, and DiT Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03580)
*Mojtaba Faramarzi,Alex Lamb,Irina Rish*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies how geometric perturbations (reflection-based transformations) affect diffusion model architectures (UNet, DiT, ViT) by applying controlled internal interventions and measuring feature stability using diagnostics like SCS, AMS, and Drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The effects of structured geometric perturbations within diffusion architectures (convolutional UNets and transformer-based DiTs) remain poorly understood, despite their widespread use.

Method: A unified framework applies reflection-based elements of the dihedral group to intermediate hidden states as controlled internal interventions. Diagnostics (SCS, AMS, Drift) measure feature stability, with image-level metrics (FID, KID, CLIP score, LPIPS) in Stable Diffusion 2.1 U-Net experiments.

Result: Consistent transformations improve stability; inconsistent ones induce predictable, architecture-specific failures. Seven intervention modes tested over three seeds in SD 2.1 U-Net.

Conclusion: Geometric consistency is a key principle for stable hidden-state interventions in spatially structured vision and diffusion models.

Abstract: Diffusion architectures now encompass convolutional UNets as well as transformer-based designs such as Diffusion Transformers (DiTs), inspired by Vision Transformers (ViTs), yet the effects of structured geometric perturbations within these architectures remain poorly understood. We study this question through a unified framework that applies reflection-based elements of the dihedral group to intermediate hidden states as controlled internal interventions, contrasting geometrically consistent and inconsistent variants. Using activation-level diagnostics, including Self-Consistency Shift (SCS), Activation Mass Scatter (AMS), and Drift, we analyze feature stability and geometric drift. We find that consistent transformations improve stability, while inconsistent ones induce predictable, architecture-specific failures. In the main Stable Diffusion 2.1 U-Net study, we evaluate seven intervention modes over three seeds and complement the internal diagnostics with image-level FID, KID, CLIP score, and LPIPS diversity. Taken together with supporting ViT and controlled DiT analyses, these results establish geometric consistency as a key principle for stable hidden-state interventions in spatially structured vision and diffusion models.

</details>


### [619] [Modular Foundation Models for Time-Series Perception in Digital Twins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03585)
*Quang Hung Pham,Ryad Zemouri,Martin Gagnon,Luc Vouligny*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a modular foundation model for time-series perception in Engineering Digital Twins and PHM systems, using pretrained representation encoders with self-supervised learning, a gating mechanism for dynamic encoder selection, and Transformer-based aggregation to handle multiple downstream tasks (imputation, forecasting, few-shot learning) with strong results on benchmarks and a real-world hydro-generator case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for time-series perception in Digital Twins and PHM systems are task-specific, data-hungry, lack robustness, and have poor transferability across varying operating conditions, motivating the need for a more modular, scalable, and transferable foundation model.

Method: The framework uses a collection of pretrained representation encoders (self-supervised learning on heterogeneous datasets), a gating mechanism for dynamic encoder selection, projection into a shared latent space, and Transformer-based self-attention aggregation to model cross-encoder interactions, with lightweight task-specific heads for downstream tasks.

Result: Ablation studies confirm the complementary roles of self-supervised pretraining, encoder selection, representation alignment, and adaptive aggregation. Competitive performance on the ETT benchmark across tasks, and practical relevance demonstrated via a real-world virtual sensing case study for hydro-generator rotor temperature.

Conclusion: The proposed modular foundation model offers a scalable, transferable, and robust solution for time-series perception in PHM and Digital Twin applications, effectively handling multiple downstream tasks while keeping pretrained encoders frozen during adaptation.

Abstract: Engineering Digital Twins and Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) systems rely on robust perception modules to extract actionable information from heterogeneous and non-stationary time-series data. However, most existing approaches remain task-specific, data-hungry, and difficult to integrate into scalable monitoring and decision-making pipelines. Moreover, purely data-driven models often lack robustness and transferability across varying operating conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a modular foundation model for time-series perception based on a collection of pretrained representation encoders. The framework leverages self-supervised learning on heterogeneous datasets to learn transferable and task-agnostic representations, which can be reused across multiple PHM tasks. A gating mechanism is introduced to dynamically select relevant encoders for a given target dataset, enabling conditional computation and adaptive model composition. The selected representations are projected into a shared latent space and aggregated using a Transformer-based self-attention module that explicitly models cross-encoder interactions. The resulting architecture supports multiple downstream tasks, including imputation, long-term forecasting, and few-shot learning, through lightweight task-specific heads, while keeping pretrained encoders frozen during adaptation. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate the complementary roles of self-supervised pretraining, encoder selection, representation alignment, and adaptive aggregation. Experimental results on the ETT benchmark show competitive performance across tasks, while a real-world industrial case study on virtual sensing for hydro-generator rotor temperature highlights the practical relevance of the approach.

</details>


### [620] [Graph Classification via Network Usable Information: From Representation Evaluation to Structure Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03587)
*Abdullah Shaik,Anwar Said*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NetinfoGC extends the NUI paradigm to graph classification using permutation-invariant representations from propagation-based and centrality measures, with a training-free NUI estimation and sparse-group LASSO selection. Classical centrality measures are competitive with learned representations, and NUI correlates strongly with classification accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of black-box GNN embeddings by developing an interpretable, unified framework that evaluates and exploits graph representations without requiring end-to-end neural training.

Method: NetinfoGC constructs permutation-invariant graph representations using propagation-based mechanisms and classical centrality measures, introduces a training-free NUI estimation based on clustering consistency, and applies sparse-group LASSO for automatic descriptor selection.

Result: Classical centrality measures are highly competitive with learned propagation-based representations, sometimes outperforming them. Strong correlation between estimated NUI and downstream classification accuracy is observed.

Conclusion: NetinfoGC provides a unified, interpretable, and effective framework for graph-level representation evaluation and exploitation without end-to-end neural training, with NUI serving as a reliable proxy for representation utility.

Abstract: We propose NetinfoGC, a framework for graph classification that extends the Network Usable Information (NUI) paradigm to graph-level learning. Unlike conventional graph neural network approaches that rely on end-to-end training of black-box embeddings, NetinfoGC constructs a family of permutation-invariant graph representations derived from propagation-based mechanisms and classical structural descriptors, including graph centrality measures.
  To evaluate representation quality, we introduce a training-free NUI estimation procedure based on clustering consistency with ground-truth labels, providing a proxy for task-relevant information without supervised learning. We further exploit the same representations using sparse-group LASSO regularization, enabling automatic selection of informative structural descriptors while suppressing redundant ones.
  Experiments on benchmark datasets show that classical centrality measures are highly competitive with learned propagation-based representations, and in several cases yield superior performance. Moreover, we observe a strong correlation between estimated NUI and downstream classification accuracy, validating NUI as an effective measure of representation utility.
  Overall, NetinfoGC provides a unified and interpretable framework for evaluating and exploiting graph representations without requiring end-to-end neural training.

</details>


### [621] [Implicit Bias of SGD in Multivariate ReLU Networks: Effective Width Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03613)
*Shuang Liang,Tom Jacobs,Guido Montúfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the implicit bias of noisy SGD in training wide two-layer ReLU networks for regression, showing that despite infinite overparameterization, the learned predictor collapses to a finite-width representation where input weights align along finitely many directions, yielding a continuous piecewise affine function with at most 2𝒫−1 hyperplanes, where 𝒫 counts linear dichotomies realizable on training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the implicit regularization effects of noisy stochastic gradient descent in overparameterized neural networks, specifically why infinitely wide networks learn simple, low-complexity solutions rather than exploiting their massive capacity.

Method: The authors study the mean-field regime of noisy SGD training for wide two-layer ReLU networks, approximating dynamics by a Wasserstein gradient flow that converges to a unique stationary measure, then characterizing the structure of this stationary measure and the corresponding predictor.

Result: The learned predictor exhibits effective width collapse: input weights and biases align along finitely many directions (at most 2𝒫-1), making the solution a continuous piecewise affine function whose regions are cells of a finite hyperplane arrangement. Each learned direction induces a unique ternary activation pattern on training data, ensuring non-redundancy.

Conclusion: The complexity of the learned predictor is governed by the combinatorial geometry of the training data (via linear dichotomies), providing a theoretical explanation for the simplicity bias in overparameterized neural networks trained with noisy SGD.

Abstract: We study the implicit bias of noisy stochastic gradient descent in training wide two-layer ReLU networks for multivariate regression. In a mean-field regime, the training dynamics are approximated by a Wasserstein gradient flow that converges to a unique stationary measure. We characterize the structure of this stationary measure and the predictor it represents. We show that, despite the network being infinitely overparameterized, the learned predictor admits an effectively finite representation: the input weights and biases align along finitely many directions, leading to an effective width collapse. In particular, the solution function is continuous piecewise affine, with affine regions determined by the cells of a finite hyperplane arrangement. The number of learned directions, and hence hyperplanes, is bounded above by $2\mathcal{P}-1$, where $\mathcal{P}$ denotes the number of linear dichotomies realizable on the training inputs. We further establish a non-redundancy property of the learned representation by proving that each learned direction induces a unique ternary activation pattern on the training data. Consequently, the complexity of the learned predictor is governed by the combinatorial geometry of the training data.

</details>


### [622] [Reflected Schrödinger Bridge Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03626)
*Marcus Häggbom,Viktor Nilsson,Pierre Nyquist,Joakim andén*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A partially simulation-free framework for reflected Schrödinger bridges that trains like flow matching, enabling high-dimensional image coupling with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reflected SB methods rely on complex forward-backward SDE training with expensive higher-order derivatives and full path sampling. The authors aim to simplify training to be comparable to flow matching while maintaining reflection guarantees.

Method: Introduces a partially simulation-free training framework for reflected SBs using a new sampling method and regression target, enabling training analogous to flow matching without needing full trajectory simulations.

Result: Successfully couples high-dimensional image datasets. Reflected dynamics add negligible wall-clock time during training and inference while maintaining or slightly improving generative performance.

Conclusion: Reflected SBs can be trained efficiently with flow-matching-like methods, making them practical for high-dimensional generative tasks with domain-constrained sampling.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative modeling have enabled the efficient computation of Schrödinger bridges (SB) in high-dimensional settings by leveraging partially simulation-free training methods inspired by flow matching. However, these have not covered SBs with reflecting dynamics, a useful model choice with built-in guarantees that generated samples stay in the data domain. Existing alternatives for reflected SBs instead rely on more complex training based on forward--backward SDE theory, requiring expensive higher-order derivatives and sampling entire paths during training. In this article, we introduce a partially simulation-free framework that allows reflected SBs to be trained similarly to flow matching, using a new sampling method and regression target. We demonstrate our results by coupling pairs of well-known high-dimensional image datasets. Using reflected dynamics incurs negligible additional wall-clock time during both training and inference while maintaining or slightly improving generative performance.

</details>


### [623] [LLM-Guided Transportation Hub Capacity Planning with Textual Business Inputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03651)
*Xiaoyue Liu,Zheng Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new framework uses an LLM agent with chain-of-thought reasoning to incorporate qualitative business context into hub capacity planning, achieving a 2.8% optimality gap vs. 11.0% from traditional optimization alone on a real-world freight network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional hub capacity planning models cannot process qualitative business context (e.g., textual descriptions), limiting their practical applicability despite strong quantitative optimization.

Method: An LLM agent uses chain-of-thought reasoning to create structured decision tables from natural-language business context, then iteratively proposes capacity adjustments validated by a routing-based optimization feedback loop.

Result: On a 13-hub US freight network, the framework achieves a 2.8% optimality gap versus 11.0% from the baseline optimization model without textual inputs.

Conclusion: LLMs can act as a contextual bridge, effectively integrating qualitative business insights into Operations Research workflows and significantly improving capacity planning outcomes.

Abstract: While traditional hub capacity planning models optimize effectively for quantitative inputs, they often fail to digest qualitative business context. We propose a novel framework where a large language model (LLM) agent iteratively proposes hub capacity decisions guided by natural-language business context descriptions. The key mechanism is a chain-of-thought reasoning protocol: the LLM constructs a structured decision table that maps each contextual item to specific capacity adjustments based on the implied direction and magnitude of changes. The new capacity decision is then validated through a feedback loop with an optimization model, which provides routing-based performance metrics to guide the agent's selection. On a real-world 13-hub freight network in the southeastern US, our framework achieves a 2.8% optimality gap relative to the hidden ground-truth, a significant improvement over the 11.0% gap produced by the traditional optimization model without textual business inputs. This demonstrates that LLMs can serve as a contextual bridge, integrating qualitative business insights into Operations Research workflows.

</details>


### [624] [Phase-Preserving Trimodal Transformer for Tropical Forest Biomass Estimation Using Optical and PolInSAR Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03663)
*Luiz Felipe Parente Santiago,Rosiane Rodrigues de Freitas,Daniel Rodrigues dos Santos,Felipe Ferrari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TCCT, a physics-informed deep learning architecture that fuses optical (Landsat-5) and PolInSAR (P/L bands) data for accurate AGB estimation in tropical forests, achieving RMSE of 3.78m and rRMSE of 4.51%—well under the ESA BIOMASS 20% error threshold.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AGB estimation in mature tropical forests is hindered by SAR signal saturation in high-density areas and persistent cloud cover in optical imagery. Existing methods fail to overcome these physical limitations.

Method: Trimodal Coherent Co-attention Transformer (TCCT) with complex-valued encoders preserving phase coherence, dynamic co-attention for adaptive cloud gating, and localized spatial allometric calibration via Levenberg-Marquardt optimization using local wood density data.

Result: Global RMSE of 4.19 m (5-fold CV), fine-tuned RMSE of 3.78 m, R²=0.33 for CHM, rRMSE of 4.51% for AGB in dense forests (>50 Mg/ha), outperforming RF, CNN, and ViT baselines.

Conclusion: TCCT meets ESA BIOMASS mission's <20% error requirement, providing a robust framework for continuous carbon stock mapping in tropical biomes through effective multimodal fusion.

Abstract: The accurate estimation of Above-Ground Biomass (AGB) in mature tropical forests remains a critical challenge in remote sensing, primarily due to the saturation of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) signals in high-density areas and persistent cloud cover affecting optical imagery. To overcome these physical limitations, we propose the Trimodal Coherent Co-attention Transformer (TCCT), a physics-informed deep learning architecture. The TCCT natively fuses optical surface reflectance (Landsat-5) with complex-valued Polarimetric SAR Interferometry (PolInSAR) data from both P and L bands. Unlike traditional fusion methods, our architecture employs complex-valued encoders to preserve spatial phase coherence, coupled with a dynamic co-attention mechanism that acts as an adaptive gating module, reducing the weight of cloud-corrupted optical pixels and shifting reliance to microwave phase data. We also derived a localized spatial allometric calibration model via Levenberg-Marquardt optimization, tailored to the specific wood density of the Paracou region in the Amazon basin. Evaluated using a two-stage protocol, the TCCT first underwent a rigorous 5-fold cross-validation to establish robust global weights (achieving a global RMSE of 4.19 m). Subsequently, following a localized spatial fine-tuning phase over 200 epochs, the model attained an absolute RMSE of 3.78 m and an $R^2$ of 0.33 for Canopy Height Models (CHM), outperforming standard Random Forest, CNN, and Vision Transformer baselines. Our ablation study confirms that preserving phase coherence mitigates deep-canopy signal saturation. When converted to AGB, the fine-tuned TCCT map yielded a Relative RMSE (rRMSE) of 4.51% in dense forest areas above 50 Mg/ha. By meeting the European Space Agency (ESA) BIOMASS mission requirement of less than 20% error, the TCCT provides a robust framework for continuous carbon stock mapping in tropical biomes.

</details>


### [625] [A Structural Interpretation of GELU and Threshold-Transmission Activations via the First-Order Loss Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03664)
*Roberto Rossi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a new interpretation of GELU as a signal-transmission term of the expected surplus in a hard linear gate with Gaussian random threshold, generalizes it to a threshold-transmission family including ReLU, GELU, SiLU/Swish, and hard swish, and proposes uniform-threshold variants that are competitive or better across vision and language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To offer a complementary theoretical interpretation for GELU based on the Gaussian complementary first-order loss function, separating loss accounting from forward signal transmission, and to generalize this into a broader family of activation functions.

Method: Derives GELU as the signal-transmission term of expected surplus with a Gaussian random threshold, generalizes to a threshold-transmission family, and introduces uniform-threshold variants (fixed and learned width). Tested on compact vision and language models.

Result: Calibrated or learned uniform-threshold gates are consistently competitive with GELU, ReLU, and SiLU/Swish, improve in most settings, and utilize the finite transition region nontrivially.

Conclusion: The proposed uniform-threshold activation family performs competitively or better than standard activations, offering a principled and flexible alternative grounded in loss-function theory.

Abstract: The Gaussian Error Linear Unit is usually motivated as the expected output of an input-dependent stochastic Bernoulli gate. This work gives a complementary interpretation based on the Gaussian complementary first-order loss function: GELU is the signal-transmission term of the expected surplus of a hard linear gate with a Gaussian random threshold. This view separates loss accounting from forward signal transmission and generalises to a threshold-transmission family that includes ReLU, GELU, SiLU/Swish, and hard swish as special cases. The uniform-threshold case recovers a hard-swish-like compact piecewise-polynomial gate with an explicit threshold-width parameter, yielding fixed- and learned-width variants. Controlled experiments on compact vision and language models show that calibrated or learned uniform-threshold gates are consistently competitive with GELU, ReLU, and SiLU/Swish, improve over them in most tested settings, and use the finite transition region nontrivially.

</details>


### [626] [Validation-Induced Shapley Shifts: How Validation Structure Distorts Data Valuation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03675)
*Yinan Shen,Ziao Yang,Hongfu Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shapley-based data valuation is unstable under validation set perturbations; noise compresses Shapley values toward zero due to a neighborhood reshuffling effect, challenging assumptions of stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the common assumption that Shapley values for training data are stable once the model and data are fixed, revealing a systematic vulnerability to validation set changes.

Method: Using the KNN-Shapley framework, the authors analyze how noise injection into the validation set alters Shapley distributions, identifying a noise-induced neighborhood reshuffling effect.

Result: Even modest validation set perturbations cause directional compression of Shapley values toward zero; shifts are consistent and reproducible across synthetic and real data.

Conclusion: Shapley values are fragile under validation set changes; normalization and boundary-aware validation strategies are proposed to mitigate distortions for more robust data valuation.

Abstract: Shapley values are widely used to attribute value to training data based on their marginal contribution to performance on a validation set. Existing practice often assumes these values are stable once the training data and model are fixed. In this work, we uncover a systematic vulnerability: even modest changes to the validation set, such as introducing noises, cause directional shifts in Shapley distributions. As noises are added, Shapley values of training samples compress toward zero. We trace this to a noise-induced neighborhood reshuffling effect: perturbations alter the local rank order between validation and training samples, flattening the valuation landscape. Using the KNN-Shapley framework, we show through synthetic and real data that these shifts are consistent and reproducible. Our findings challenge the assumption of Shapley stability and reveal a new axis of fragility in data valuation. We propose normalization and boundary-aware validation strategies to mitigate these distortions and enable more robust, interpretable valuation in machine learning marketplaces.

</details>


### [627] [Rethinking AI-Generated Text Detection: A Strong Baseline and the Distribution-Shift Problem That Remains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03680)
*Zhuoer Shen,Mingyi Wang,Shaofeng Zou,Yuheng Bu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A simple fine-tuned RoBERTa matches or beats specialized AI-text detectors in-distribution, but fails under distribution shift (topic/model changes). Lightweight adaptation methods (MAML over LoRA and confidence-weighted ensembles) improve robustness, highlighting that progress should measure both in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI-text detection research often introduces new benchmarks with specialized detectors, and the authors question whether architectural complexity is truly driving performance. They advocate for a baseline-first approach to reassess progress.

Method: 1) Benchmarking a plain fine-tuned RoBERTa against specialized detectors across multiple benchmarks. 2) Identifying distribution shift as the key failure mode (e.g., high-confidence false positives on human text from unseen domains). 3) Proposing two lightweight domain adaptation methods: K-shot adaptation with first-order MAML over LoRA adapters, and a per-sample confidence-weighted ensemble.

Result: RoBERTa matches or exceeds specialized detectors in-distribution, but performance degrades sharply under topic or model shifts. Adding more source data does not close the gap. The proposed adaptation methods improve robustness under distribution shift.

Conclusion: Progress in AI-generated text detection should be measured by both in-distribution accuracy and robustness under distribution shift. Simpler baselines may be sufficient for in-distribution tasks, but distribution shift remains the key unsolved challenge.

Abstract: Recent AI-generated text detection work often introduces a new benchmark together with a specialized detector tailored to it. We revisit this practice from a baseline-first perspective. Across several benchmarks, we show that a plain, fully fine-tuned RoBERTa matches or exceeds the specialized detectors those benchmarks are built around. This suggests that much of the recent architectural complexity is not what drives strong in-distribution detection. The remaining challenge is the distribution shift. The same strong baseline degrades sharply when the topic domain or generating model changes at test time, and simply adding more source data does not close the gap. We identify a key failure mode: under distribution shift, the detector can assign high-confidence machine labels to human-written text from unseen domains. We then study two lightweight domain adaptation methods to address this problem: $K$-shot adaptation with first-order MAML over LoRA adapters, and a per-sample confidence-weighted ensemble built on top of the adapted detector. Overall, our results suggest that progress in AI-generated text detection should be measured not only by in-distribution performance, but also by robustness under distribution shift.

</details>


### [628] [PIEFS: Physics-Informed Eigenfunction Features with Learnable Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03692)
*Varvara Nazarenkko,Timur Lidzhiev,Alexander Tarakanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PIEFS is a supervised neural representation-learning framework that uses a learnable metric (diagonal + Givens rotations) to produce task-adaptive, Dirichlet-regularized coordinate maps with spectral inductive bias, outperforming classical spectral methods and NeuralEF on synthetic, tabular, and image benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spectral methods rely on fixed kernels, graph Laplacians, or manually selected feature scaling, limiting their adaptability to task-specific supervision.

Method: PIEFS trains scalar coordinate maps under empirical Gram orthogonality, supervised linear readout, and a Dirichlet penalty where the input gradient is transformed by a learnable metric A(x)=Λ(x)U(x) (diagonal scaling Λ and structured Givens rotations U).

Result: Experiments on synthetic, tabular, and image benchmarks show PIEFS outperforms classical spectral baselines and NeuralEF, with identity, diagonal, and rotation-scaling metrics compared.

Conclusion: PIEFS is a compact supervised spectral representation method; future work should address optimization stability, validation on explicit operator eigenproblems, and richer metric parameterizations.

Abstract: Spectral methods are widely used to construct representations from the geometry of data, but they often rely on a fixed kernel, graph Laplacian, or manually selected feature scaling. We propose Physics-Informed Eigenfunction Features with Learnable Scaling (PIEFS), a supervised neural representation-learning framework with a spectral inductive bias, based on a modified Dirichlet energy. In PIEFS, scalar coordinate maps are trained under empirical Gram orthogonality, a supervised linear readout, and a Dirichlet penalty in which the input gradient is transformed by a learnable metric $A(x)=Λ(x)U(x)$. The diagonal factor $Λ(x)$ controls anisotropic scaling, while the orthogonal factor $U(x)$ is parameterized by a structured product of Givens rotations. This construction yields task-adaptive Dirichlet-regularized coordinates rather than eigenfunctions of a fixed supervision-independent operator. Experiments on synthetic, tabular, and image-based benchmarks study the effect of identity, diagonal, and rotation-scaling metrics, and compare the resulting coordinates with classical baselines and NeuralEF. The results support PIEFS as a compact supervised spectral representation method and identify optimization stability, validation on explicit operator eigenproblems, and richer metric parameterizations as the main directions for future work.

</details>


### [629] [Social Networks of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03695)
*Kaixuan Liu,Guojun Xiong,Weinan Zhang,Shengpu Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces SNLA, a framework for modeling belief formation in LLM agent populations that accounts for limited attention and directed influence, showing that narrow attention causes herding with bounded effective sample size, while wide attention recovers wisdom-of-crowds only under specific network conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical social-network models assume the network structure determines belief aggregation, but this fails for LLM agents whose limited attention means they only process partial information. This leads to overestimation of information pooling and inability to distinguish genuine consensus from herding.

Method: The paper proposes SNLA, a framework modeling how much each agent actually influences others based on network position and attention sharpness, rather than just network connectivity. Theoretical analysis uses a tractable proxy, validated by a controlled testbed and operator-controlled variants of three multi-agent LLM benchmarks.

Result: Narrow attention causes herding where effective sample size stays bounded regardless of population size. Wide attention recovers wisdom-of-crowds only when the exposure graph is undirected and degree-regular. These predictions are validated empirically.

Conclusion: The herding-wisdom transition is reproduced across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating that attention constraints fundamentally affect collective belief formation in LLM agent populations and must be accounted for when assessing system trustworthiness.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in interacting populations, raising the question of what such populations come to believe collectively. Whether a population aggregates genuine knowledge or collapses into a false consensus directly affects how much such systems can be trusted. Classical social-network models assume that the network itself determines how beliefs combine. This assumption breaks down for LLM agents, whose limited attention takes in only part of what they are exposed to, so these models overstate how much information a population actually pools and cannot tell genuine consensus from herding. We introduce SNLA, a framework that models how much each agent actually influences others, rather than merely how the network connects them. This influence depends on each agent's position in the network and on how sharply attention focuses. Theoretically, we show on a tractable proxy that narrow attention causes herding, where the effective sample size stays bounded regardless of population size, while wide attention recovers wisdom-of-crowds behavior only when the exposure graph is undirected and degree-regular. Empirically, a controlled testbed validates these predictions directly, and the herding-wisdom transition reproduces on operator-controlled variants of three multi-agent LLM benchmarks.

</details>


### [630] [SAVER: Stochastic Adaptive Variance-Driven Exploration and Reconstruction for Low-Dose Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03761)
*Shunta Nonaga,Koji Tabata,Junya Honda,Hiroyuki Kudo,Wataru Yashiro,Tamiki Komatsuzaki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAVER is an adaptive CT acquisition framework that selects projection angles in real-time based on statistical variance of acquired data, using Softmax-based stochastic scheduling with simulated annealing to prioritize informative directions, achieving higher reconstruction fidelity than random sampling, especially for structurally anisotropic objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Minimizing radiation dose in CT without compromising image quality remains a critical challenge, as conventional low-dose protocols use fixed, uniform angular sampling regardless of individual patient's organ structural complexity.

Method: SAVER adaptively selects projection angles in real-time based on statistical variance of acquired data, employing a Softmax-based stochastic scheduling scheme with simulated annealing to prioritize high-information directions while maintaining exploration.

Result: Across 8 diverse phantoms, SAVER consistently achieves higher reconstruction fidelity than random sampling, especially for objects with high structural anisotropy, and demonstrates robust performance under significant measurement noise.

Conclusion: SAVER provides a mathematically-grounded approach to maximize diagnostic quality per unit of radiation dose by dynamically reallocating dose to the most informative projections, marking a shift toward sample-dependent, data-driven CT acquisition.

Abstract: Computed Tomography (CT) is indispensable in clinical diagnostics, yet minimizing radiation dose without compromising image quality remains a critical challenge. Conventional low-dose protocols often rely on fixed, uniform angular sampling, independent of the underlying structural complexity of organs of individual patients. We propose ``Stochastic Adaptive Variance-Driven Exploration and Reconstruction'' (SAVER), an adaptive data acquisition framework that selects projection angles in real-time based on the statistical variance of acquired data. Utilizing a Softmax-based stochastic scheduling scheme with simulated annealing, SAVER prioritizes directions with high structural information while maintaining necessary exploration. Numerical experiments across 8 diverse phantoms demonstrate that SAVER achieves consistently higher reconstruction fidelity than conventional random sampling, particularly for objects with high structural anisotropy. Furthermore, the proposed method exhibits robust performance under significant measurement noise. By dynamically reallocating radiation dose to the most informative projections, SAVER provides a mathematically-grounded approach to maximize diagnostic quality per unit of radiation dose, marking a shift toward sample-dependent, data-driven CT acquisition.

</details>


### [631] [FedACT: Federated Adaptive Coordinate Trust Modulation for Robust Transformer Training under Data Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03763)
*Shuai Li,Qinglin Wang,Ping Luo,Jiahuan Wang,Hongyang Hu,Haotian Mo,Yigui Feng,Ziang Liu,Qisong Xiao,Jie Liu,Tao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedACT is a method that modulates coordinate-wise trust in federated AdamW training to address uneven update reliability under heterogeneous client data, consistently improving Transformer models across vision and LLM tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated training with local AdamW suffers from "coordinate trust mismatch" — adaptive updates have uneven coordinate-wise reliability under heterogeneous client data, and existing optimizers fail to address this at the coordinate level.

Method: FedACT computes a globally corrected adaptive direction, then reallocates update magnitudes based on a coordinate-wise trust score — prioritizing coordinates jointly supported by local gradients and global correction while maintaining small non-zero updates elsewhere.

Result: Consistent improvements over strong adaptive baselines on federated vision Transformers, CNNs, LLM pre-training, and LLM fine-tuning, with largest gains on Transformers under stronger data heterogeneity. Also improves cross-client direction consistency.

Conclusion: Coordinate-level trust allocation effectively complements round-level global-local correction, making FedACT a powerful enhancement for federated adaptive optimization.

Abstract: Federated Transformer training increasingly relies on local AdamW, whose adaptive updates can provide much stronger local progress than SGD-based training. However, under heterogeneous client data, even globally corrected AdamW updates may remain highly uneven in coordinate-wise reliability. We refer to this phenomenon as coordinate trust mismatch. Existing federated adaptive optimizers mainly address mismatch at the client-update or communication-round level, but still apply the corrected adaptive direction densely and uniformly across coordinates. In this paper, we propose FedACT, a global-aware coordinate trust modulation method for federated AdamW training. FedACT first forms a globally corrected adaptive direction and then reallocates update magnitudes according to a coordinate-wise trust score, assigning larger steps to coordinates jointly supported by local gradients and global correction, while preserving smaller non-zero updates on the remaining coordinates. Extensive experiments on federated vision Transformers, CNNs, LLM pre-training, and LLM fine-tuning show that FedACT consistently improves over strong federated adaptive baselines, with the largest gains on Transformer models under stronger data heterogeneity. Mechanism analyses further show that FedACT improves cross-client direction consistency, suggesting that coordinate-level trust allocation effectively complements round-level global-local correction. Code will be released.

</details>


### [632] [Conservative Subject Invariant EMG-based Gesture Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03783)
*Hamed Rafiei,Ali Mousavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conservative multi-objective learning framework for sEMG gesture recognition jointly optimizes classification, adversarial subject confusion, and triplet metric learning with Lipschitz-inspired adaptive weighting, achieving significant cross-subject generalization improvements (84.48% on UCI EMG, 61.44% on NinaPro DB5).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-subject generalization in sEMG-based gesture recognition is fundamentally challenging. Existing deep learning methods perform well within-subject but require subject-specific data and struggle to balance invariance (across subjects) and discriminability (across gestures).

Method: A multi-head architecture with: (1) gesture classification head, (2) adversarial subject confusion via gradient reversal, (3) triplet-based metric learning for discriminative representations. A Lipschitz-inspired adaptive weighting mechanism dynamically balances these auxiliary objectives during training for optimization stability.

Result: UCI EMG: 84.48% (vs. SOTA 78.2%). NinaPro DB5: 61.44% (vs. 41.30%, 49% relative improvement). Reduced cross-subject prediction variance and more structured latent representations.

Conclusion: Jointly enforcing invariance and discriminability through adaptive multi-objective optimization leads to stable training and improved cross-subject generalization in sEMG gesture recognition.

Abstract: Cross-subject generalization remains a fundamental challenge in surface electromyography (sEMG)-based gesture recognition. Although deep learning methods have improved within-subject performance, they often rely on subject-specific data and struggle to balance invariance and discriminability. In this work, we propose a conservative multi-objective learning framework for subject-invariant sEMG gesture recognition. The proposed model adopts a multi-head architecture that jointly optimizes gesture classification, adversarial subject confusion through gradient reversal, and triplet-based metric learning to encourage discriminative and subject-invariant representations.
