Method: DynaVieW learns interleaved state-transition sequences from video keyframes using a hierarchical schema, and jointly models transition prediction and state simulation under a mixture-of-experts architecture with cross-expert selective attention and schema token re-weighted loss.

Result: DynaVieW achieves improved consistency, controllability, and instruction-following in downstream tasks like visual narrative creation and world simulation.

Conclusion: The dynamic schema-guided approach enables robust understanding of visual dynamics, boosting performance in visual narrative creation and world simulation tasks.

Abstract: Multimodal LLMs struggle to systematically model the temporal evolution of visual scenes in videos or multi-image sequences. Such inputs require models to predict or simulate multiple levels of dynamic constituents, such as actions taken in the visual sequence, and the associated changes to the visual environment that result. To address this challenge, we propose a dynamic schema-guided world model, DynaVieW, optimized for visual dynamic prediction and simulation. DynaVieW achieves an in-depth understanding of visual dynamics by learning interleaved state-transition sequences, where states cover broad visual scenes from video keyframes, and transitions capture comprehensive dynamic constituents within a hierarchical schema. DynaVieW jointly models transition prediction and state simulation under a mixture-of-experts architecture, with a cross-expert selective attention and a schema token re-weighted loss, to ensure effective and robust learning. DynaVieW's understanding of visual dynamics boosts its downstream performance in visual narrative creation and world simulation, showing improved consistency, controllability, and instruction-following.

</details>


### [660] [Asymptotic-Preserving A Posteriori Analysis of Diffusion and Flow-Matching Samplers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04113)
*Shiheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes singular-perturbation limits in diffusion/flow-matching samplers as terminal noise σ_min→0, identifying asymptotic-preserving (AP) schemes. It shows the σ-clock (DDIM) is uniquely layer-exact, the λ-clock has a stability threshold, and stochastic samplers incur log(1/σ_min) scaling in KL divergence while deterministic ODEs remain uniformly first-order accurate. Empirical validation on CIFAR-10 confirms the theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The stiffness of the score and boundary layer development as σ_min→0 creates numerical challenges in sampling; the authors aim to determine which fixed-step samplers remain stable and uniformly accurate in this singular limit, and to quantify the cost of stochastic vs. deterministic sampling.

Method: Singular perturbation analysis treating σ_min as a perturbation parameter; asymptotic-preserving criteria cast as a posteriori audit using residual functionals with σ_min-uniform coefficients; analysis on two solvable models (rank-deficient Gaussian, symmetric two-point mixture); empirical validation on EDM CIFAR-10 checkpoint with spectral measurements.

Result: DDIM (σ-clock) is the unique layer-exact discretization; λ-clock stable only for h≤h_star=1+W(1/e); deterministic samplers are first-order uniformly accurate with no log factor; the log(1/σ_min) cost is entirely charged to the Itô term of stochastic samplers (path-KL scales as Λ²/N vs ODE's Λ²/N²). CIFAR-10 experiments confirm spectral measurements predict residual budgets without refitting.

Conclusion: The clock parameterization determines stability; the logarithm scaling is driven by noise, not geometry; stochastic samplers are fundamentally more expensive per unit KL than deterministic ODE samplers in the small-noise limit.

Abstract: Diffusion and flow-matching samplers integrate a learned probability-flow ODE from a large noise scale down to a small terminal floor $σ_{\min}$, at which the score is stiff and the flow develops a boundary layer. We treat $σ_{\min}$ as a singular-perturbation parameter and determine which fixed-step samplers are asymptotic-preserving (AP), that is, stable and uniformly accurate as $σ_{\min}\to0$, casting the criteria as an a posteriori audit: residual functionals with $σ_{\min}$-uniform coefficients, computable on a pretrained checkpoint without ground-truth scores or exact trajectories. On the terminal layer, Euler in the $σ$-clock, the deterministic DDIM update, is the unique layer-exact discretization up to affine reparameterization, with rectified flow its flow-matching counterpart; the $λ$-clock is stable only for steps $h\le h_\star=1+W(1/e)$, and the uniform-$σ^2$ heat clock stalls a $σ_{\min}$-independent distance from the data. On two solvable models (rank-deficient Gaussian, symmetric two-point mixture), deterministic samplers remain first-order uniformly accurate with no $\log(1/σ_{\min})$ factor, even across a symmetric posterior-switching interface whose distributional budget is a universal constant; the logarithm is charged entirely to the Itô term of stochastic samplers, whose path-KL scales as $Λ^2/N$ against the ODE's $O(Λ^2/N^2)$ budget, with $Λ=\log(σ_{\max}/σ_{\min})$. On the EDM CIFAR-10 checkpoint, spectra measured once predict held-out residual budgets across step count, schedule, and noise level against pre-specified gates with no per-configuration refitting, and calibrate the Itô coefficient at $M_1=1.00\pm0.01$. The clock decides stability; the noise, not the geometry, charges the logarithm.

</details>


### [661] [GlacierCastAI: Predicting Glacier Retreat from Multi-Modal Satellite Imagery and Climate Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04117)
*Arunkumar Ramachandran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GlacierCastAI fuses satellite imagery, ERA5 climate data, and DEM terrain features to forecast future glacier retreat, showing that adding climate variables improves prediction IoU by 3.4% over image-only models, and that a climate-only MLP achieves 98% of image-only performance with 85x fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep learning methods only map current glacier boundaries rather than forecasting future ones. The authors argue that ERA5 seasonal climate variables contain predictive information about future glacier retreat that satellite imagery alone does not provide.

Method: GlacierCastAI uses a ResNet50 spatial encoder, ConvLSTM for temporal modeling, and a cross-attention climate fusion module. It processes multi-temporal Landsat imagery, ERA5 reanalysis climate variables, and Copernicus DEM terrain features across five glaciers in four climate regimes.

Result: Adding ERA5 climate signals improves IoU from 0.326 to 0.337 (+3.4%). All DL models outperform persistence (0.160) and linear trend (0.169) baselines by 89-99%. A climate-only MLP (661K parameters) achieves 0.320 IoU (98% of image-only) with 85x fewer parameters. Spring solar radiation is the dominant climate driver.

Conclusion: Atmospheric forcing from ERA5 carries predictive information beyond satellite imagery alone for forecasting glacier retreat. Climate variables can serve as efficient substitutes for imagery in some settings, and spring insolation is identified as the key predictor.

Abstract: ERA5 seasonal climate variables contain predictive information about future glacier retreat beyond what satellite imagery alone provides, yet existing deep learning methods focus on mapping current boundaries rather than forecasting future ones. This paper presents GlacierCastAI, which reframes glacier boundary prediction as a multi-modal spatiotemporal forecasting problem, fusing multi-temporal Landsat imagery with ERA5 reanalysis climate variables and Copernicus DEM terrain features to forecast glacier boundaries across five glaciers spanning four climate regimes. The architecture couples a ResNet50 spatial encoder with a ConvLSTM temporal model and a cross-attention climate fusion module. Because forecasting is inherently more uncertain than mapping current boundaries, the reported IoU values (0.320-0.337) are not directly comparable to state-of-the-art mapping models. Comparisons are against traditional baselines and experimental conditions. Through a pre-registered ablation study, adding ERA5 climate signals improves image-only IoU from 0.326 to 0.337 (+3.4%), suggesting that atmospheric forcing carries predictive information beyond imagery alone. All deep learning models substantially outperform persistence and linear trend baselines (IoU 0.160 and 0.169 respectively), with improvements of 89-99% relative IoU. A lightweight climate-only MLP baseline (661K parameters) achieves an IoU of 0.320 (98% of image-only performance) using 85x fewer parameters, suggesting that ERA5 variables encode substantial predictive signal independently of satellite imagery. SHAP attribution analysis suggests that spring solar radiation (MAM) is the dominant climate driver, consistent with the known role of spring insolation in setting melt season trajectories.

</details>


### [662] [Parametric Memory Decoding for Zero-Shot Routing in LoRA-Based External Parametric Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04118)
*Fengxian Ji,Zhuohan Xie,Jingpu Yang,Fan Zhang,Zirui Song,Xiuying Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Parametric Memory Decoding (PMD), a framework for zero-shot LoRA routing in external parametric memory banks without needing additional routing components. They introduce PMD-Bench for systematic evaluation and PMDRouter, which uses response magnitude from a single base-model prefill to achieve strong performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LoRA-based EPM routing methods introduce extra training, deployment, and maintenance overhead. Current zero-shot LoRA routing approaches have scattered evaluations and lack a unified perspective for systematic improvement.

Method: The authors propose PMD (Parametric Memory Decoding), a framework that reframes zero-shot LoRA routing as decoding activations over external parametric memory. They instantiate PMDRouter, which scores each LoRA by its response magnitude from a single base-model prefill, and build PMD-Bench covering document-level, domain-level knowledge, and task-skill settings.

Result: PMDRouter achieves the strongest internal-signal performance across multiple zero-shot routing settings on PMD-Bench, demonstrating the feasibility of zero-shot LoRA routing.

Conclusion: Zero-shot LoRA routing is feasible without additional routing components, and PMD can serve as a general framework for systematically improving zero-shot routing methods.

Abstract: With the rise of parametric memory, LoRA-based External Parametric Memory (EPM) has emerged as a modular solution, but existing routing methods often introduce additional training, deployment, and maintenance overhead. This raises a natural question: can a LoRA-based EPM bank be routed without maintaining an additional routing component? However, existing zero-shot LoRA routing methods still face two problems under the EPM setting: (1) their evaluations are scattered across different task settings rather than organized around EPM access, and (2) their routing signals lack a unified perspective to guide systematic improvement. To address these problems, we organize PMD-Bench, covering document-level, domain-level knowledge, and task-skill, and propose Parametric Memory Decoding (PMD), the first framework designed to systematically improve zero-shot LoRA routing by reframing it as decoding activations over external parametric memory. Based on PMD, we further instantiate PMDRouter, which scores each LoRA by its response magnitude from a single base-model prefill. Experiments on PMD-Bench show that PMDRouter achieves the strongest internal-signal performance across multiple zero-shot routing settings. These results demonstrate the feasibility of zero-shot LoRA routing and suggest that PMD can serve as a general framework for improving zero-shot routing methods. Sources: Github (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Parametric-Memory-Decoding-872A/)

</details>


### [663] [CertMix: Certified, Data-Efficient Metamaterial Design by Affine Mixing of Aligned Neural-Implicit Weight Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04123)
*Yifan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CertMix is a data-efficient framework for inverse design of mechanical metamaterials that represents unit cells as aligned neural implicit fields, enabling linear mixing for targeted property design with distribution-free certification, achieving high accuracy from as few as 50 exemplars.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based methods for inverse design of mechanical metamaterials are data-hungry, mostly interpolative, and provide no guarantee that the generated design satisfies the specification.

Method: Represents exemplar unit cells as periodic neural implicit fields (SIREN signed-distance decoders) overfit from a shared anchor, aligning weight vectors. Uses constrained affine-mixing in this aligned space, leveraging approximate linearity of homogenized elasticity tensor. Includes linearity-mismatch trust region and split-conformal calibration for distribution-free certification.

Result: Achieves scaled property error of 10^{-4} from as few as 50 exemplars, two to three orders of magnitude better than baselines trained on 1000 cells. Remains accurate far outside exemplar range, 57x faster than per-target topology optimization, and avoids checkerboards and enclosed voids.

Conclusion: CertMix enables highly accurate, data-efficient, and certified inverse design of mechanical metamaterials with extrapolation capability, demonstrated on spatially graded fields, 3D triply periodic surfaces, and a certified running-shoe midsole application.

Abstract: Inverse design of mechanical metamaterials seeks a periodic unit cell whose homogenized elastic properties meet a prescribed target, but current learning-based methods are data-hungry, mostly interpolative, and provide no guarantee that the generated design satisfies the specification. We introduce CertMix, a data-efficient framework that represents each exemplar unit cell as a small periodic neural implicit field, specifically a SIREN signed-distance decoder overfit from a shared anchor, so that exemplar weight vectors become aligned and directly comparable. The key observation is that, in this aligned weight space, the homogenized elasticity tensor is approximately linear in the mixing coefficients. Targeted design therefore reduces to a small constrained affine-mixing problem solved with a differentiable periodic homogenizer in the loop. Negative coefficients enable extrapolation beyond the exemplar range, a linearity-mismatch trust region keeps blends valid, and split-conformal calibration converts the mismatch signal into a distribution-free certificate on achieved-property error. From as few as 50 exemplars, CertMix attains a scaled property error of $10^{-4}$, roughly two to three orders of magnitude below conditional generative baselines trained on 1000 cells. It remains accurate far outside the exemplar range, is $57\times$ faster than per-target topology optimization while avoiding checkerboards and enclosed voids, and extends to spatially graded fields, 3D triply periodic surfaces, and a certified running-shoe midsole application.

</details>


### [664] [MDL Meets Latent Confounders: LNML-based Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04133)
*Zhongyi Que,Shin Matsushima,Kenji Yamanishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes PCG-CD, an MDL-based causal discovery framework that handles both nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders using LNML code-length and Δ-pseudo-collinearity, demonstrating effectiveness on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing causal discovery methods struggle with nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders simultaneously, often relying on linear assumptions or causal sufficiency which limits real-world applicability.

Method: Uses MDL principle with LNML code-length minimization to select causal models, introduces Δ-pseudo-collinearity to detect latent confounders, and implements a greedy algorithm (PCG-CD) for practical discovery.

Result: Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets show the method accurately recovers directed causal relationships and effectively detects latent confounders.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully extends causal discovery to settings with nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders, outperforming methods that rely on restrictive assumptions.

Abstract: Causal discovery with nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders remains challenging. Existing methods often rely on either linear assumptions or causal sufficiency, limiting their applicability. We propose an MDL-based causal discovery framework that explicitly accounts for latent confounders while allowing flexible nonlinear mechanisms by minimizing the luckiness normalized maximum likelihood (LNML) code-length. The causal relationship between each variable pair is determined by selecting the shortest code-length of the causal model, and we introduce the notion of $Δ$-pseudo-collinearity to identify dependencies induced by latent confounders. Based on these ideas, we develop a greedy algorithm, termed Pseudo-Collinearity Guided Causal Discovery (PCG-CD). Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method accurately recovers directed causal relationships and effectively detects latent confounders.

</details>


### [665] [Masked Generative-Contrastive Representation Learning for Cross-Dataset EEG-Based Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04139)
*Huqin Weng,Jiayang Huang,Yimin Wen,Jie Du,Chi-Man Vong,Chuangquan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MGCRL is a novel self-supervised learning framework for EEG-based emotion recognition that combines masked generative and contrastive learning with a region-aware spatiotemporal encoder to capture fine-grained and global representations, achieving strong cross-dataset generalization across different channel configurations and subjects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SSL for EEG-based emotion recognition is underexplored. Existing SSL methods struggle with capturing spatiotemporal dependencies under varying channel configurations, extracting noise-resilient fine-grained features, and deriving globally generalizable representations across subjects.

Method: MGCRL integrates: 1) a region-aware spatiotemporal encoder with region-based graph convolution for localized spatial/functional relationships; 2) generative learning via JEPA using masked features for noise-robust fine-grained representations; 3) contrastive learning using masked and original features for temporally stable, cross-subject-invariant representations.

Result: Extensive experiments with pretraining on FACED dataset and fine-tuning on multiple SEED-series datasets demonstrate MGCRL's effectiveness in cross-dataset generalization for EEG emotion recognition.

Conclusion: MGCRL effectively learns universal EEG representations by addressing channel configuration variability, noise robustness, and cross-subject generalization challenges, advancing SSL application in affective computing.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) shows strong potential for cross-dataset transfer by improving feature representation and generalization. However, its application to EEG-based emotion recognition remains largely unexplored. Existing SSL methods struggle to capture the intricate spatiotemporal dependencies of EEG signals under varying channel configurations, extract fine-grained representations resilient to noise, and derive global features that generalize well across subjects. To address these challenges, we propose Masked Generative-Contrastive Representation Learning (MGCRL), a novel SSL framework specifically designed for EEG-based emotion recognition. Built upon a region-aware spatiotemporal encoder, MGCRL integrates generative and contrastive learning to achieve both fine-grained and global discriminative representations for cross-dataset generalization. MGCRL introduces three key designs: 1) a spatiotemporal encoder that incorporates region-based graph convolution to capture localized spatial and functional relationships, enhancing region-specific feature learning and mitigating the impact of varying EEG channel configurations across datasets; 2) a generative learning mechanism based on the joint embedding predictive architecture (JEPA) that utilizes masked features to capture noise robustness fine-grained representations, improving the model's capability to characterize subtle emotional states; and 3) a contrastive learning strategy that leverages masked and original features to learn temporally stable and cross-subject-invariant representations across the same stimuli, boosting emotion discrimination and cross-subject generalization. Under these designs, MGCRL exhibits remarkable ability to learn universal representation. Extensive experiments involving pretraining on the large FACED dataset and fine-tuning on multiple SEED-series datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of MGCRL.

</details>


### [666] [Binary Iterative Method for Non-targeted Adversarial Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04145)
*Naman Goyal,Milan Chaudhari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes the Binary Iterative Method (BinIM), a divide-and-conquer based adversarial attack that outperforms existing gradient-based methods like FGSM, BIM, and VAM on ImageNet classification tasks, achieving misclassification confidence up to 0.995.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing gradient-based adversarial attack methods (Fast Gradient Method, Basic Iterative Method, Virtual Adversarial Method) struggle to find optimal input data points and hyper-parameters for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks, motivating the need for a more effective approach.

Method: The Binary Iterative Method (BinIM) uses a divide-and-conquer paradigm to optimize parameters and hyper-parameters for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks, and is evaluated against other gradient-based methods on pre-trained networks (InceptionV3, InceptionV2, ResNet V2 152) using 1000 random ImageNet images.

Result: BinIM outperforms all other gradient-based methods, achieving misclassification with confidence up to 0.995 while reducing the probability of the true label to approximately 2.21e-09 (effectively zero) on ImageNet classification tasks.

Conclusion: The Binary Iterative Method is a superior approach for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks compared to existing gradient-based methods, demonstrating strong efficacy in forcing misclassification with high confidence.

Abstract: Adversarial attacks guide and provide additional training and test data for both adversarial training and adversarial robustness validation, and expose the 'piecewise linearity' of deep learning based models. Since adversarial attacks and adversarial robustness are mathematically defined problems that can be optimised directly with end-to-end differentiable search, adversarial robustness is more widely applicable than other robustness metrics such as corruption and perturbation robustness, and new kinds of adversarial attacks are beneficial for robustness testing. Attacks are targeted or non-targeted depending on whether the image is modified to misclassify to a particular class or to any incorrect class; we focus on the non-targeted setting. Finding the optimal input data points and hyper-parameters for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks remains a challenge for current methods like the Fast Gradient Method, Basic Iterative Method and Virtual Adversarial Method. We propose a new method, the "Binary Iterative Method" (BinIM), which uses a divide-and-conquer paradigm to optimise parameters and hyper-parameters for the generation of non-targeted attacks. We compare our method to other gradient-based adversarial attacks evaluated over pre-trained networks (InceptionV3, InceptionV2, ResNet V2 152) on classification tasks. On 1000 randomly-sampled images from the standard ImageNet dataset, the Binary Iterative Method outperforms all other gradient-based methods, qualitatively making the classifier misclassify with confidence up to 0.995 while reducing the probability of the true label to 2.21e-09 (approximately 0).

</details>


### [667] [Mask-based Predictive Representations for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04153)
*Kai Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-supervised mask prediction auxiliary task for vision-based deep reinforcement learning, using transformers to reconstruct masked input sequences in latent space, which improves sample efficiency and outperforms state-of-the-art methods on continuous and discrete control benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of abstracting effective states from high-dimensional image inputs with limited samples for sample-efficient reinforcement learning.

Method: A self-supervised mask prediction auxiliary task that uses sequence information and context to predict masked information, combined with transformers to reconstruct masked input sequences in latent space.

Result: Improved sample efficiency of reinforcement learning and outperformance of state-of-the-art sample-efficient RL methods on multiple continuous and discrete control benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed non-reconstruction self-supervised method effectively strengthens the agent's understanding of tasks and learns effective representations, leading to better sample efficiency in vision-based deep RL.

Abstract: Vision-based deep reinforcement learning involves dealing with high-dimensional inputs of image information. It is crucial to abstract effective states from high-dimensional image inputs and limited samples for sample-efficient reinforcement learning. To address this challenge, inspired by fields such as natural language processing and computer vision, we propose a self-supervised task based on mask prediction as an auxiliary task for reinforcement learning. This non-reconstruction method uses the sequence information collected by the agent from the environment and the context information in the sequence to predict the masked information, thereby strengthening the agent's understanding of the task and learning effective representations. Combined with transformers, we find that the model reconstructs the masked input sequence in the latent space. By feeding the compressed representations learned by this method into reinforcement learning models, we observe an improvement in the sample efficiency of reinforcement learning. Moreover, the model outperforms state-of-the-art sample-efficient reinforcement learning methods on multiple continuous and discrete control benchmarks.

</details>


### [668] [Geometry of Ordinal Representations in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04167)
*Saksham Bassi,Sharvi Tomar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language models encode ordinal variables on curved 1D manifolds for locally computable tasks (bracket depth, indentation), but use higher-dimensional representations for tasks requiring cross-position integration (table position, numeric magnitude). Geometric computation (manifold twisting) is architecture-dependent and task-specific.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether the finding that LMs represent character counts on curved 1D manifolds generalizes to other ordinal tasks, and to understand how architecture and task characteristics affect these representations.

Method: Analyzed four ordinal tasks (bracket depth, indentation, table position, numeric magnitude) in three models (Gemma-2-2B, Gemma-2-9B, Qwen3-4B). Used manifold analysis, attention head geometric transformation identification, and activation patching/ablation to evaluate representational structure and task relevance.

Result: 1D curved manifolds with place-cell tiling emerge only for locally computable ordinal tasks. Cross-position or semantic tasks yield higher-dimensional/incoherent representations. Qwen3-4B shows stronger twisting than Gemma models; Qwen's indentation twisters preserve ordinal order unlike its numeric twisters. Manifold ablation causes larger probe accuracy drops than random controls.

Conclusion: Ordinal variable representation depends on local computability, and geometric computation varies across architectures and tasks. Manifold subspaces are functionally relevant for task performance.

Abstract: Recent work showed that language models represent character counts on curved 1D manifolds, with attention heads performing geometric transformations to enable computation. We test whether this generalizes across four ordinal tasks (bracket depth, indentation, table position, numeric magnitude) in Gemma-2-2B, Gemma-2-9B, and Qwen3-4B. We find that 1D manifolds with place-cell feature tiling emerge for tasks where the ordinal variable is locally computable from token identity, while tasks requiring cross-position integration or semantic extraction produce higher-dimensional or incoherent representations. Geometric computation is architecture-dependent: Qwen3-4B shows substantially stronger twisting than Gemma models for indentation, and its twisters preserve ordinal order, unlike its numeric twisters. Activation patching confirms that the identified manifold subspaces concentrate task-relevant information, with manifold-direction ablation causing dramatically larger probe accuracy drops than random-direction controls.

</details>


### [669] [FedFFT: Taming Client Drift in Federated SAM via Spectral Perturbation Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04170)
*Liyang Yuan,Yibo Yang,Dandan Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedFFT is a lightweight plug-and-play method that filters low-frequency components of SAM perturbations in federated learning to reduce inter-client inconsistency and improve generalization, outperforming existing SAM-based FL methods under non-IID conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM perturbations in FL suffer from divergence problems because they are computed locally and reflect client-specific loss geometries, causing inconsistencies especially in low-frequency components.

Method: FedFFT applies frequency-domain filtering to SAM perturbations, removing low-frequency components that contain inter-client inconsistencies, without requiring additional communication overhead.

Result: FedFFT consistently outperforms SAM-based FL methods across multiple benchmarks and backbones, especially under severe non-IID distributions, with improved scalability and general applicability.

Conclusion: A frequency-domain perspective on SAM perturbations reveals that low-frequency filtering effectively suppresses inconsistent client updates while preserving shared learning signals, offering a simple yet effective solution for sharpness-aware federated optimization.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables decentralized training without data sharing, but suffers from statistical heterogeneity across clients, leading to client drift, poor generalization, and sharp minima compared to centralized training. Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) has emerged as a promising approach to improve generalization, yet its application in federated learning still suffers from divergence problems, since perturbations are computed locally and reflect client-specific loss geometries. To better understand this issue, we provide experimental evidence from a new perspective, the frequency domain, for SAM perturbations in federated settings, revealing that inter-client perturbation inconsistencies are predominantly concentrated in the low-frequency spectrum. Motivated by this insight, we propose Federated learning with Frequency-domain Filtering of SAM perturbations (FedFFT). It is a lightweight and plug-and-play method that filters out low-frequency components of SAM perturbations without requiring additional communication, thereby suppressing inconsistent components in client updates while preserving consistent learning signals. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and diverse backbones demonstrate that FedFFT consistently outperforms SAM-based FL methods, particularly under severe non-IID distributions. These results highlight the effectiveness, scalability, and general applicability of our frequency-domain perspective for sharpness-aware federated optimization.

</details>


### [670] [Physics-Informed Graph Learning with Uncertainty Awareness for Open-Set Domain Generalization in Fault Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04188)
*Jinfeng Zhu,Shiyu Long,Ye Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PGU-OD is a physics-informed graph learning framework with uncertainty awareness that addresses open-set domain generalization (OSDG) for rotating machinery fault diagnosis by suppressing perceptual uncertainty, mitigating structural uncertainty propagation, and optimizing decision boundaries to handle unknown fault types under varying operating conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven OSDG methods overlook cascaded uncertainty propagation across feature extraction, topological learning, and decision-making stages in intelligent industrial fault diagnosis under domain shifts and unknown fault types.

Method: (1) Physics-informed spectral attention module for condition-robust feature extraction; (2) Uncertainty-aware adaptive graph learning with class-scale Gaussian distribution parameters; (3) Gaussian-distribution-based adaptive boundary loss and dual-criteria open-set inference strategy.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both known fault classification and unknown fault rejection under domain shifts on two public rotating machinery fault datasets.

Conclusion: PGU-OD effectively tackles OSDG by integrating physics-informed priors and uncertainty quantification, achieving superior performance for intelligent industrial maintenance.

Abstract: Intelligent industrial maintenance critically relies on reliable fault diagnosis of rotating machinery. However, it faces formidable challenges from unknown fault types and domain shifts induced by varying operating conditions, which is formally formulated as the open-set domain generalization (OSDG) problem. Existing methods are mainly data-driven, thereby overlooking the cascaded propagation of uncertainty across feature extraction, topological learning, and decision-making stages.To tackle this challenge, we propose PGU-OD, a novel Physics-Informed Graph Learning framework with Uncertainty Awareness for Open-set Domain generalization. First, it designs a physics-informed spectral attention module to extract condition-robust fault features, thereby suppressing perceptual uncertainty caused by frequency shifts. Further, it constructs an uncertainty aware adaptive graph learning mechanism to dynamically adjust the edge weights of the sample graph guided by class-scale Gaussian distribution parameters, which mitigates the structural propagation of uncertainty. Finally, a Gaussian-distribution-based adaptive boundary loss function and a dual-criteria open-set inference strategy are developed to optimize decision boundaries and reliably reject unknown faults. Extensive experimental evaluations on two public and widely used rotating machinery fault datasets demonstrate that the proposed PGU-OD outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both known fault classification and unknown fault rejection under domain shifts.

</details>


### [671] [SpecGradFilter: A Spectral Gradient Filtering Framework for Taming Federated Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04189)
*Liyang Yuan,Yibo Yang,Dandan Guo,Peter Richtarik,Zhouchen Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SpecGradFilter tackles client drift in Federated Learning by suppressing low-frequency gradient components that encode distributional shifts, achieving strong performance in non-IID settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL methods address statistical heterogeneity via spatial-domain techniques (gradient correction/regularization) but overlook the spectral structure of optimization signals, leaving the frequency-domain nature of client drift unexplored.

Method: The authors propose SpecGradFilter, a spectral gradient filtering framework that identifies and suppresses discordant low-frequency components (which capture client-specific distributional shifts) while preserving consistent high-frequency features. It can be implemented via precise FFT-based truncation or approximated through spatial techniques like Gaussian detrending.

Result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet demonstrate that SpecGradFilter significantly outperforms baselines in highly non-IID settings with negligible communication overhead.

Conclusion: SpecGradFilter establishes a new frequency-domain paradigm for robust federated optimization, showing that spectral gradient filtering is a generalizable and effective principle for handling statistical heterogeneity in FL.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is fundamentally challenged by statistical heterogeneity, where non-identically distributed (non-IID) data induces client drift that severely hampers global convergence. While existing approaches attempt to mitigate this drift through spatial-domain gradient correction or regularization, they overlook the intrinsic spectral structure of optimization signals. In this work, we revisit client drift from a novel frequency-domain perspective and uncover a critical Spectral Bias of Drift: inter-client gradient divergence is predominantly concentrated in low-frequency components which encode client-specific distributional shifts, while high-frequency components representing fine-grained features remain relatively consistent. Motivated by this, we propose SpecGradFilter, a unified Spectral Gradient Filtering Framework that tames heterogeneity by suppressing discordant low-frequency signals. Crucially, we demonstrate that SpecGradFilter is a generalizable principle, effective not only via precise FFT-based truncation but also through spatial approximations like Gaussian detrending. Extensive experiments on benchmarks such as CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet demonstrate that SpecGradFilter significantly performs better performance in highly Non-IID settings with negligible communication overhead, establishing a new paradigm for robust federated optimization.

</details>


### [672] [Exploring Convolutional Neural Processes for Weather Downscaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04190)
*Francisco Passos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ConvCNPs successfully downscale daily max temperature from ~11km to ~1km over Switzerland (MAE=1.31°C, CRPS skill=0.524), but exhibit overconfident uncertainty estimates and fail on off-grid station data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global reanalysis products like ERA5-Land are too coarse (~11km) for local applications, especially in mountainous regions where temperature varies sharply over short distances.

Method: Convolutional Conditional Neural Processes (ConvCNPs) adapted from Vaughan et al. (2022) with high-resolution elevation features from DHM25, trained on 10 years of data with five-fold temporal cross-validation.

Result: Best model achieves MAE of 1.31°C and 0.524 CRPS skill score; elevation MLP is critical; graceful degradation under sparse on-grid input; fails on off-grid station data; all models are overconfident.

Conclusion: ConvCNPs are viable for climate downscaling in complex terrain, but uncertainty calibration and native non-gridded input support remain key challenges for deployment.

Abstract: Global reanalysis products such as ERA5-Land provide spatially complete weather fields but at resolutions too coarse for local applications, particularly in mountainous regions where temperature can vary by several degrees over short distances. This project investigates Convolutional Conditional Neural Processes (ConvCNPs) for statistical downscaling of daily maximum temperature from the ~11km resolution ERA5-Land grid to ~1km resolution over Switzerland, building upon the architecture of Vaughan et al. (2022) and adapting it to the topographically complex Swiss domain with high-resolution elevation features from the swisstopo DHM25. The best model, trained on ten years of data (2014-2023) with five-fold temporal cross-validation, achieves a mean absolute error of 1.31 Celsius and a CRPS-based skill score of 0.524 relative to bilinear interpolation, reducing the expected prediction error by more than half. An ablation study reveals that the elevation MLP is the indispensable component - without it, the model diverges entirely - while explicit seasonal features and Topographic Position Index provide secondary benefits. Under sparse on-grid input the model degrades gracefully, maintaining positive skill down to approximately 10% of the input grid; however, zero-shot deployment on off-grid station observations does not achieve positive skill at any density tested. All configurations exhibit severely overconfident uncertainty estimates, a structural limitation of the Gaussian likelihood training objective. These results demonstrate that ConvCNPs are a viable and effective approach to climate downscaling in complex terrain, and identify uncertainty calibration and native support for non-gridded input as the key challenges for operational deployment.

</details>


### [673] [Channel-Adaptive Robust Aggregation for Over-the-Air Federated Learning in Heterogeneous Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04218)
*Zubaida Fatima,Zubair Shaban,Yusuf Jamal,Nazreen Shah,Ranjitha Prasad,B. N. Bharath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CHARGE-FL is a channel-adaptive aggregation framework for Over-the-Air Federated Learning that jointly addresses noise, fading, and client heterogeneity in 6G networks, outperforming existing methods in accuracy, stability, and convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OTA-FL methods rely on fixed aggregation schedules and fail to jointly address noise, fading, and client heterogeneity, which are critical for privacy-preserving data-intensive applications like IoT, augmented reality, and autonomous systems in 6G networks.

Method: CHARGE-FL adaptively schedules aggregation based on channel dynamics and application readiness, combining a tailored optimization strategy with a dual-purpose precoding mechanism to mitigate channel distortion and bias from partial updates.

Result: Under realistic wireless conditions, CHARGE-FL significantly improves accuracy, stability, and convergence over state-of-the-art OTA-FL methods, particularly in straggler-prone and noisy scenarios.

Conclusion: CHARGE-FL provides a robust, channel-adaptive solution for OTA-FL that effectively addresses the key challenges of wireless federated learning in 6G environments.

Abstract: The growing demand for privacy-preserving, data-intensive applications such as IoT, augmented reality, and autonomous systems positions Federated Learning (FL) as a key enabler in 6G networks. Over-the-Air FL (OTA-FL) leverages the superposition property of the wireless multiple access channel for efficient aggregation via simultaneous transmissions. Existing methods rely on fixed aggregation schedules and do not jointly address noise, fading, and client heterogeneity. We propose CHARGE-FL (CHannel-Adaptive Robust agGrEgation), a framework that adaptively schedules aggregation based on channel dynamics and application readiness. By combining a tailored optimization strategy with a dual-purpose precoding mechanism, CHARGE-FL mitigates channel distortion and bias from partial updates, achieving superior accuracy, stability, and convergence under realistic wireless conditions. Empirical results under realistic wireless conditions show that CHARGE-FL significantly improves accuracy, stability, and convergence over state-of-the-art OTA-FL methods, particularly in straggler-prone and noisy scenarios.

</details>


### [674] [Quantize the Target, Quantize the Drafter: Efficient Inference with Qwen3.5-4B](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04244)
*Jaeyeon Kim,Jewon Lee,Bo-Kyeong Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A system combining quantization-aware distillation and block-diffusion speculative decoding to achieve 6.978× speedup on Qwen3.5-4B inference on A10G GPU, ranking 3rd in Efficient Qwen Competition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable low-latency serving of Qwen3.5-4B on resource-constrained NVIDIA A10G GPU.

Method: Quantized target model + speculative decoding; quantization-aware distillation recovering accuracy; block-diffusion drafter trained via two-stage procedure (high-precision then low-precision); drafter further quantized and uses sliding-window attention.

Result: 6.978× average speedup over baseline while meeting quality thresholds, ranking 3rd overall.

Conclusion: The approach provides useful insights for practical LLM inference.

Abstract: This report describes our approach to the Efficient Qwen Competition, where the goal is to enable low-latency serving of Qwen3.5-4B on a resource-constrained NVIDIA A10G GPU. Our system combines a quantized target model with speculative decoding. To recover accuracy, we apply quantization-aware distillation to the target model while retaining the original quantization grid. To speed up decoding, a block-diffusion drafter specialized for the quantized target model is trained using a two-stage procedure: first learning from the high-precision target and then adapting to the low-precision target. Because the drafter is invoked at every speculative decoding step, we further reduce its overhead with quantization and sliding-window attention, preserving draft-token acceptance while improving long-context decoding latency. As a result, our submission achieves a 6.978$\times$ average speedup over the baseline while satisfying the required quality thresholds, ranking 3rd overall. We hope these results provide useful insights for practical LLM inference. The code and resources are available at https://github.com/nota-github/adaptfm-quant-dflash

</details>


### [675] [Signal or Noise? Understanding Generative Models for Real-World Sensor Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04245)
*Zitao Shuai,Zongzhe Xu,Yuntian Wu,Sirui Li,Tianhong Li,Yuzhe Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces SensorGen, a large-scale benchmark study of generative models for sensor time-series data across 14 settings, 4 domains, 7 datasets, and 12 modalities, finding that flow-matching models perform best overall, signal properties significantly impact generation quality, and synthetic sensor data provides practical utility beyond visual realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative modeling of sensor data remains fragmented across modalities, datasets, and task formulations, lacking a systematic understanding of when, how, and why generative models succeed or fail in real-world settings. The authors aim to address this gap by creating a unified evaluation framework.

Method: The authors construct SensorGen, a large-scale benchmark spanning 14 settings across 4 domains, 7 datasets, and 12 signal modalities. They systematically evaluate generative models from five major families (implicitly including flow-matching) on sensor time-series data.

Result: Three key findings: (1) flow-matching models achieve strong overall performance across most settings; (2) signal properties matter—demographic covariates improve longitudinal generation and time-frequency modeling improves high-frequency signal generation; (3) generated signals have practical utility beyond visual realism, with scaling improving quality and synthetic data boosting downstream performance.

Conclusion: SensorGen establishes a broader understanding of design choices, evaluation protocols, and failure modes in real-world sensor data generation, moving toward systematic benchmarking in this domain.

Abstract: Generative models have changed how machine learning represents complex data distributions, especially in language and vision, yet many real-world systems are observed instead as continuous, high-dimensional, and noisy sensor time series. Existing generative modeling of sensor data, however, remains fragmented across modalities, datasets, and task formulations, limiting a systematic understanding of when, how, and why generative models succeed or fail in real-world settings. To address this gap, we introduce SensorGen, a large-scale study of sensor-signal generation spanning 14 settings across 4 domains, 7 datasets, and 12 signal modalities. Leveraging SensorGen, we systematically evaluate generative models from five major families and uncover three key findings: (1) flow-matching models provide strong overall performance across most settings; (2) signal properties matter, with demographic covariates improving longitudinal generation and time-frequency modeling improving high-frequency signal generation; and (3) generated signals have practical utility beyond visual realism, with scaling improving generation quality and synthetic data improving downstream performance. Together, SensorGen establishes a broader understanding of design choices, evaluation protocols, and failure modes in real-world sensor data generation.

</details>


### [676] [On Preserving Geometrical Invariance for Superpixel Image Classification using Graph Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04262)
*Sarabeshwar Balaji,Shubham Mohanty,Akash Anil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SuperGT is a Graph Transformer framework for image classification that uses superpixel graphs to reduce computational complexity while capturing long-range dependencies and preserving translation/rotation invariance, achieving comparable performance to state-of-the-art ShapeGNN on CIFAR-10 without needing boundary point coordinates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CNNs and ViTs have high computational complexity on large image datasets due to processing dense pixel grids with redundant information. Existing superpixel-based GNN approaches struggle with long-range dependencies and often fail to preserve translation/rotation invariance, which is crucial for robust image classification.

Method: SuperGT uses a Graph Transformer architecture on superpixel-based graph representations of images to capture long-range dependencies, combined with a pre-processing scheme that preserves translation/rotation invariance.

Result: SuperGT performs significantly better than many baselines on CIFAR-10 and achieves comparable performance to the state-of-the-art ShapeGNN model, without requiring coordinate information of superpixel boundary points.

Conclusion: The proposed SuperGT framework effectively addresses the limitations of previous superpixel-based GNN approaches by capturing long-range dependencies and preserving geometric invariances, offering a computationally efficient alternative to CNNs/ViTs for image classification.

Abstract: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Vision Transformer (ViT) for image classification exploit a dense grid of pixels containing redundant information. Consequently, for a larger image dataset, CNNs and ViTs face deployability challenges due to high computational complexity. Representing images as graphs of superpixels offers an efficient alternative that preserves key information while eliminating pixel-level redundancy. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been utilized on such graphs to perform image classification. However, GNNs are known to struggle with capturing long-range dependencies which is important in the domain of image classification. Furthermore, a majority of these superpixel-based image classification approaches do not explicitly preserve translation/rotation invariance. Nevertheless, preserving translation/rotation invariance is important for robust image classification. Thus, this paper proposes SuperGT, a Graph Transformer-based framework for image classification, which captures the long range dependencies, along with a pre-processing scheme that preserves translation/rotation invariance. We evaluate SuperGT on CIFAR-10 dataset and observe that it performs significantly better than many baselines. Furthermore, we note that the overall performance of SuperGT is comparable to the previous state-of-the-art model, namely, ShapeGNN, without relying on coordinates of the boundary points of each superpixel required by ShapeGNN.

</details>


### [677] [HiFA4: Training-Free 4-bit FlashAttention on Ascend HIF4 NPUs for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04302)
*Hui Dong,Yanzhao Li,Jie Gao,Chunlu Li,Zhiyuan Zhang,Yupeng Sun,Zhenyuan Chen,Zhiqiang Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HiFA4 is a post-training 4-bit quantization design for FlashAttention on Ascend NPUs that uses Smooth-QK (per-channel rescaling) and P-Reordering (softmax normalizer from quantized weights) to reduce accuracy loss, recovering 37.5% of the gap from direct HIF4 quantization on Qwen3-8B and cutting MMLU regressions by 27-57% across five LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient 4-bit quantization of FlashAttention's QK^T and PV GEMM operations on Ascend NPUs while minimizing accuracy degradation, addressing the lack of HIF4-targeted designs for LLM inference on this hardware.

Method: Two mechanisms: (1) Smooth-QK — calibration-static per-channel rescaling of Q and K after RoPE to shift quantization difficulty from K to Q without per-tile online reduction; (2) P-Reordering — accumulating the softmax normalizer from the same quantized attention weights P_hat used in PV GEMM (rather than higher-precision reconstruction), introducing a coherent output-scaling error that can be fused into the PV Cube GEMM for latency reduction.

Result: On Qwen3-8B: 37.5% accuracy gap recovery, accuracy loss from 1.12pp→0.70pp, BF16-inconsistent MMLU predictions 16.3%→8.2%, MMLU regressions cut 57%. On Gemma2-9B: within 0.7pp of BF16, 27% regression reduction. On LLaMA3.1-8B/Mistral-7B/Phi-4B: 41-52% regression reduction. Projected 35.4% critical-path latency reduction vs BF16.

Conclusion: HiFA4 effectively reduces quantization-induced decision drift across diverse LLMs, and the P-Reordering design enables fused softmax normalization in the PV Cube GEMM for potential significant latency gains, though on-hardware validation remains future work.

Abstract: We present HiFA4, a post-training operator-level design that executes both QK^T and PV in FlashAttention as 4-bit HIF4 Cube GEMMs for LLM inference on Ascend NPUs, while maintaining the online softmax state in FP16. To our knowledge, HiFA4 is the first Ascend-HIF4-targeted design of this kind evaluated on standard NLP benchmarks.
  HiFA4 combines two mechanisms. Smooth-QK applies a calibration-static per-channel equivalent rescaling to Q and K after RoPE, transferring quantization difficulty from K to Q without per-tile online reduction at inference. P-Reordering accumulates the softmax normalizer from the same quantized attention weights P_hat used in the PV GEMM, rather than from a higher-precision reconstruction. We show that this inconsistent formulation introduces a coherent output-scaling error, and validate the effect on a Qwen3-8B Layer-0 MMLU trace, where all 3.6M measured attention tiles exhibit net probability-mass loss with median epsilon_bar = -0.064. P-Reordering also allows the normalizer to be fused into the PV Cube GEMM.
  Across five LLMs, HiFA4 reduces quantization-induced decision drift. On Qwen3-8B, it recovers 37.5% of the accuracy gap introduced by direct HIF4 quantization, narrows the sample-weighted accuracy loss from 1.12 pp to 0.70 pp, reduces BF16-inconsistent MMLU predictions from 16.3% to 8.2%, and cuts MMLU accuracy regressions by 57% (1071 to 465). On Gemma2-9B, mild smoothing keeps HiFA4 within 0.7 pp of BF16 while reducing MMLU regressions by 27%. On LLaMA3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, and Phi-4B, where Smooth-QK is disabled, P-Reordering with the adopted Q-Mean auxiliary still reduces full-set MMLU regressions by 41-52%. A preliminary instruction-scheduling analysis projects a 35.4% critical-path latency reduction relative to BF16 by fusing the softmax normalizer into the PV Cube GEMM; on-hardware validation is left to future work.

</details>


### [678] [SAD-LoRA: Spectral Alignment for Low-Rank Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04306)
*Omer Tariq,Syed Muhammad Raza,Jeongbae Son*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAD-LoRA improves knowledge distillation by explicitly aligning the subspace of a LoRA-adapted student with a data-weighted reference, reducing a key error term (subspace misalignment) from 51% to near zero and achieving better parameter-efficient compression across GLUE tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard output-level KD for LoRA-adapted students does not explicitly control which rank-r weight subspace the adapter occupies, leading to suboptimal subspace alignment that limits compression quality.

Method: SAD-LoRA selects the adapter subspace from the data-weighted student-space reference update and maintains it during training via a differentiable principal-angle loss on the column span of B, directly minimizing subspace misalignment.

Result: On synthetic problems, subspace misalignment drops from 51% to near zero and alignment score rises from 0.49 to 1.00. On GLUE tasks with RoBERTa-large→RoBERTa-base, SAD-LoRA matches/beats spectral baselines in 5/6 tasks at r=4 and achieves best results on SST-2/CoLA at r=8.

Conclusion: Subspace alignment is the critical component for effective LoRA distillation, while coefficient matching is auxiliary; SAD-LoRA offers a principled way to improve rank efficiency in parameter-efficient compression.

Abstract: Distilling a fine-tuned teacher into a LoRA-adapted student is a standard recipe for parameter-efficient compression, but output-level KD does not explicitly control which rank-$r$ weight subspace the adapter occupies. We propose \textbf{SAD-LoRA} (\textbf{S}pectral \textbf{A}lignment \textbf{D}istillation), which selects this subspace from the data-weighted student-space reference update $\DWT\Sigx^{1/2}$ and maintains it during training via a differentiable principal-angle loss on $\colspan(B)$. We show that the data-weighted distillation error decomposes exactly into subspace misalignment, within-subspace coefficient mismatch, and irreducible rank residual; standard KD can affect the first term only indirectly through output gradients. On controlled synthetic problems with a flat teacher spectrum, SAD-LoRA reduces the subspace-misalignment term from $51\%$ to nearly zero and lifts final subspace alignment from $0.49$ to $1.00$. On RoBERTa-large to RoBERTa-base distillation across six GLUE tasks, SAD-LoRA improves rank efficiency: at $r{=}4$, it matches or beats the strongest included spectral baseline on five of six tasks, and at $r{=}8$ it gives the best result on SST-2 and CoLA. Ablations identify subspace alignment as the load-bearing component, while coefficient matching is auxiliary.

</details>


### [679] [On the effectiveness of reward functions in reinforcement learning for confidence calibration of large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04332)
*Chee Heng Tan,Zhuoyi Lin,Mehul Motani,Wee Sun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates how to train LLMs with RL to improve both reasoning accuracy and confidence calibration, identifies a "confidence reward hacking" problem where LLMs may intentionally answer incorrectly to express confidence in being wrong, proposes non-hackable confidence reward schemes, and treats the reward scheme as a tunable hyperparameter for accuracy-calibration tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs trained with RL often struggle to simultaneously improve reasoning accuracy and verbalize accurate confidence levels. Poorly designed reward schemes can incentivize LLMs to answer incorrectly just so they can confidently express being wrong (confidence reward hacking).

Method: The authors define non-hackable confidence reward schemes, characterize a spectrum of such schemes, experiment with practical datasets to demonstrate selective confidence reward hacking, and propose treating the reward scheme as a hyperparameter to optimize the accuracy-calibration tradeoff based on application needs.

Result: Selective confidence reward hacking occurs in practical datasets when reward schemes are not designed to be non-hackable. The best reward scheme for calibration-to-accuracy tradeoff depends on the dataset and application.

Conclusion: Reward schemes should be carefully designed to be non-hackable, and the choice of reward scheme should be treated as a hyperparameter to optimize tradeoffs between calibration and accuracy based on specific application requirements.

Abstract: In this paper, we consider the setting where large language models (LLMs) are trained using reinforcement learning (RL) to simultaneously improve reasoning accuracy and verbalize its confidence. Our reward scheme uses two functions for rewarding confidence verbalized by the LLM: one when the LLM is correct and a different one when the LLM is incorrect. With a poorly designed reward scheme, the LLM may be incentivized to answer incorrectly so that it can be confident that its answer is indeed incorrect, a phenomenon that we call confidence reward hacking. We propose the concept of non-hackable confidence reward schemes and define a spectrum of such reward schemes for RL confidence calibration training in LLMs. We demonstrate that selective confidence reward hacking can occur in practical datasets with reward schemes that are not designed to be non-hackable. We also demonstrate that the reward scheme with the best calibration to accuracy tradeoff depends on the dataset and the application, and propose using the reward scheme as a hyperparameter to optimize the tradeoffs in accordance to what is important for the application. The code of our experiments is available in https://anonymous.4open.science/r/rl-confidence-calibration-9ED4/README.md.

</details>


### [680] [Structure-Specific Representational Priors Causally Control the Grokking Delay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04333)
*Gunner Levi Howe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper causally tests whether grokking delay reflects time to form task-structured representations by injecting representational priors (true structure, coherent-but-wrong sibling structure, or random partition) into a transformer learning modular addition. Only true structure enables and accelerates generalization; sibling structure still generalizes (slowly); random partition only leads to memorization. Results show structure formation causally mediates grokking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To causally test whether the grokking delay specifically measures the time required to form task-structured representations, moving beyond observational claims.

Method: Inject supervised-contrastive auxiliary losses encoding three representational priors (true modular addition structure, sibling structure $(a-b) \bmod p$, random partition) into a one-layer transformer, with controlled weight-norm baselines, across 95 total runs.

Result: True structure: 22/30 generalize; sibling structure: 14/15 generalize; random partition: 0/20 generalize. Weight-norm-matched controls fail entirely (0/15). True structure accelerates grokking up to 2.75x, but acceleration is dose-dependent and bimodal.

Conclusion: The grokking delay is causally the time to form the right representational structure, where "right" is determined at the feature level (not label level): coherent-but-wrong structure preserves grokking, random structure abolishes it, and only true structure accelerates it.

Abstract: Grokking -- generalization arriving long after training-set interpolation -- can be accelerated by structure-agnostic interventions: gradient filtering, weight-norm clamping, geometric penalties on hidden representations. Whether the delay specifically measures the time to form task-structured representations has remained an observational claim. We test it causally by injecting representational priors of varying structural content into a one-layer transformer learning modular addition: a supervised-contrastive auxiliary loss whose positives encode (i) the task's true equivalence structure ($(a+b) \bmod p$), (ii) a coherent-but-wrong sibling structure ($(a-b) \bmod p$), or (iii) a random partition, all with identical loss form, strength, class sizes, and geometry. Whether generalization occurs follows a clean gradation: true structure 22/30 runs; sibling structure, which needs the same periodic features but the wrong combination, 14/15; random partition, satisfiable only by memorization, 0/20 (Fisher exact $p = 1.3 \times 10^{-7}$). A weight-norm-matched control replaying each intervention's norm trajectory onto plain cross-entropy generalizes in 0/15, collapsing into logit-scale saturation, ruling out the norm as mediator. Representation probes show structure formation precedes and predicts generalization in all 95 runs. Only the true structure also accelerates grokking, up to $2.75\times$ faster than baseline, but the acceleration is dose-dependent, bimodal across seeds, and a net wall-clock win only in its strongest cases given the contrastive term's overhead. The grokking delay is, causally, the time to form the right representational structure, where "right" is decided at the level of features rather than labels: coherent-but-wrong structure leaves grokking intact, random structure abolishes it, and only the true structure hastens it.

</details>


### [681] [One Framework for All: Cross-Modal Membership Inference for Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04339)
*Dayong Ye,Tainqing Zhu,Kun Gao,Junhao Liu,Yichuan Chen,Shuai Zhou,Hengzhu Liu,Bo Liu,Wanlei Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes the first unified membership inference attack framework applicable across text-to-text, text-to-image, and image-to-text generative models. By leveraging a modality-agnostic observation that model output distributions approximate training data distributions, the approach uses likelihood ratio testing in shared embedding space. Experiments show superior performance over existing single-modality methods under black-box settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing membership inference attacks (MIA) against generative models are designed for single modalities (text-to-text, text-to-image, or image-to-text) and cannot be applied across different modalities, limiting real-world utility. The authors aim to address this gap by developing a unified MIA framework.

Method: The approach models distributions of model-generated outputs and auxiliary non-member samples in a shared embedding space, then performs membership inference via likelihood ratio testing. It operates in a strict black-box setting under both partial-knowledge and zero-knowledge threat models.

Result: The unified framework achieves superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods that are optimized for single model classes, across all three modalities (text-to-text, text-to-image, and image-to-text).

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that a modality-agnostic approach to membership inference is both feasible and effective, outperforming specialized single-modality methods. This highlights the potential for general privacy auditing frameworks across diverse generative model types.

Abstract: Large generative models across text-to-text, text-to-image, and image-to-text modalities have been shown to pose significant privacy risks. One fundamental threat is membership inference attacks (MIA), which aim to determine whether a given data point was used in a model's training set. Although prior work has investigated MIAs against these three classes of generative models, existing approaches treat them in isolation and are not cross-applicable, thereby limiting their real-world utility. To address this limitation, we present the first comprehensive study of a unified membership inference framework that applies across text-to-text, text-to-image, and image-to-text modalities. Our approach is grounded in a key modality-agnostic observation: the output distribution of a generative model can approximate its training data distribution. Leveraging this property, we model the distributions of model-generated outputs and auxiliary non-member samples in a shared embedding space, and perform membership inference via likelihood ratio testing. We conduct extensive experiments in a strict black-box setting under both partial-knowledge and zero-knowledge threat models, and evaluate membership inference against both fine-tuning and pre-training data. Experimental results demonstrate our approach's superior performance in comparison to existing state-of-the-art methods, which are typically optimized for a single model class.

</details>


### [682] [How Many Initial Points Does Bayesian Optimization Need?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04356)
*Mujin Cheon,James Odgers,Dong-Yeun Koh,Calvin Tsay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that the number of initial random evaluations ($n_0$) in Bayesian Optimization creates a U-shaped cost curve—too few or too many waste resources—and recommends multi-step lookahead BO or Thompson Sampling when $n_0$ cannot be tuned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The choice of $n_0$ (initial random evaluations) in BO is largely heuristic, and practitioners lack principled guidance on how to set this hyperparameter.

Method: Empirical study across MLE, Bayesian MCMC, and exact GP hyperparameters with various acquisition functions, comparing total cost (initial points + BO iterations) as a function of $n_0$.

Result: Total cost is U-shaped in $n_0$ across most settings, attributed to a boundary issue where BO wastes early iterations on hypercube corners. Thompson Sampling is $n_0$-agnostic but performs worse overall.

Conclusion: Use multi-step lookahead BO when possible; use Thompson Sampling if $n_0$ cannot be tuned; use a generously large $n_0$ when tuning is feasible.

Abstract: Bayesian Optimization (BO) generally begins with an initialization phase: a batch of $n_0$ uninformed evaluations. The choice of $n_0$ remains largely heuristic, and we empirically observe that the total cost (random initial points plus BO iterations needed to find the global optimum) is U-shaped in $n_0$, i.e., a practitioner wastes resources by selecting either too low or too high a value of $n_0$. We find this tradeoff persists across MLE, Bayesian MCMC, and exact GP hyperparameters, as well as across acquisition functions. Toward the latter, Thompson Sampling appears an exception, with both total cost and simple regret essentially $n_0$-agnostic, though higher in our experiments. We attribute this U-shape to the known boundary issue of variance-driven BO: BO burns early budget on corners of the hypercube before turning inward. We demonstrate this effect using a 3D BO trajectory where the exact hyperparameters are known. We conclude with practical recommendations: use multi-step lookahead BO where possible; otherwise use Thompson Sampling when $n_0$ cannot be tuned, and a generously large $n_0$ when it can.

</details>


### [683] [RL Forgets! Towards Continual Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04364)
*Mao-Lin Luo,Zhe-Xu Wang,Zi-Hao Zhou,Bo Ye,Jian Zhao,Min-Ling Zhang,Tong Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper challenges the belief that RL is less prone to forgetting than SFT in continual post-training of VLMs. It introduces MRCL benchmark, shows RL still suffers catastrophic forgetting, and proposes CPO—a replay-free framework using KL-based parameter-movement regularization—which reduces forgetting and preserves/improves pretrained capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To revisit the unvalidated assumption that reinforcement learning is inherently less prone to catastrophic forgetting than supervised fine-tuning in continual post-training of vision-language models, as existing evidence is drawn from outdated/homogeneous benchmarks.

Method: Introduces MRCL (Multimodal Reasoning Continual Learning) benchmark from diverse recent datasets, and proposes CPO (Continual Policy Optimization)—a replay-free framework using a prior-task behavioral KL objective with theoretically justified parameter-movement regularization to limit policy drift on previous tasks.

Result: On Qwen3-VL-8B, CPO reduces forgetting by 13.7% and improves pretrained capability by 7.0%. Extensive experiments across multiple model scales show CPO consistently reduces forgetting while preserving or improving pretrained model capabilities.

Conclusion: RL still suffers from severe catastrophic forgetting during continual post-training; CPO effectively mitigates this without replay, challenging the prevailing belief that RL is inherently more robust to forgetting.

Abstract: Continual post-training is becoming a central paradigm for adapting vision-language models to evolving tasks. Recent work has increasingly favored reinforcement learning over supervised fine-tuning, driven by the belief that reinforcement learning is inherently less prone to forgetting. However, the belief remains insufficiently validated, as existing evidence is largely drawn from outdated or homogeneous benchmarks. To revisit this assumption, we introduce MRCL, a Multimodal Reasoning Continual Learning benchmark built from diverse and recently released multimodal datasets. Experiments on MRCL show that reinforcement learning can still suffer from severe catastrophic forgetting during continual post-training. To address this challenge, we propose Continual Policy Optimization (CPO), a replay-free framework grounded in the prior-task behavioral KL objective. CPO uses a theoretically justified parameter-movement regularization to limit policy drift on previous tasks. Extensive experiments across multiple model scales demonstrate that CPO consistently reduces forgetting while preserving, and in some cases improving, pretrained model capabilities. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CPO reduces forgetting by 13.7\% and improves pretrained capability by 7.0\%. The implementation code is available at https://github.com/MaolinLuo/CPO.

</details>


### [684] [NKI-Agent: Domain-Specific Fine-Tuning and Agentic Tool Use for Neuron Kernel Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04395)
*Junjie Tang,Jun Huan,Hao Zhou,Yuhao Zhang,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NKI-Agent is the first system combining domain-specific supervised fine-tuning with a compile-verify-fix agent loop for automated NKI kernel generation on AWS Trainium/Inferentia accelerators, achieving 77.3% pass rate on NKIBench benchmarks using Claude Opus 4.8, and demonstrating that SFT-trained smaller models (Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B) can achieve 25.0% pass rate at 1/100th the cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated kernel generation for emerging AI accelerators (AWS Trainium/Inferentia) via NKI remains largely unaddressed due to multi-engine architecture, tile-based programming, explicit data movement challenges, and lack of public training data, benchmarks, or tool-augmented agents.

Method: The authors adapt the CUDA-Agent framework to Neuron hardware, curate 6,000 NKI kernel generation tasks for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), construct NKIBench (250 tasks across three difficulty levels), and implement a compile-verify-fix agent loop for iterative kernel generation.

Result: Claude Opus 4.8 with rank-aware system prompt achieves 77.3% pass rate on 150-task NKIBench. Single-shot mode without agent tools drops to 6%. SFT-trained Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B achieves 25.0% pass rate at 1/100th the cost, outperforming Claude Sonnet 4 (15.0%). GRPO with binary compilation reward fails to improve over SFT.

Conclusion: Domain-specific SFT combined with compile-verify-fix agent loops is effective for NKI kernel generation, tool use is critical for performance, smaller SFT models offer cost-effective alternatives, and binary compilation rewards alone are insufficient for RL-based improvement.

Abstract: Recent agentic approaches to LLM-based kernel generation have achieved impressive results on CUDA. For emerging AI accelerators such as AWS Trainium and Inferentia, automated kernel generation and optimization remain largely unaddressed. Writing kernels for these chips via the Neuron Kernel Interface (NKI) is particularly challenging: developers must navigate a multi-engine architecture, tile-based programming, and explicit data movement across multi-level memory hierarchy. Moreover, no publicly-available training data, benchmarks, or tool-augmented agents exist for this domain. We introduce NKI-Agent, the first system combining domain-specific supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with a compile-verify-fix agent loop for NKI kernel generation. We adapt the existing CUDA-Agent framework to Neuron hardware, curate 6,000 NKI kernel generation tasks for training, and construct NKIBench, a 250-task benchmark across three difficulty levels. Evaluated on real Trn1 hardware, NKI-Agent with Claude Opus 4.8 and a rank-aware system prompt achieves a 77.3% pass rate on the 150-task NKIBench. We show that tool use is critical: Opus 4.8 scores 6% in single-shot mode without agent tools. On a 60-task subset, we show that an SFT-trained Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B achieves 25.0% pass rate at 1/100th the cost, outperforming Claude Sonnet 4 (15.0%). We also report that Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with binary compilation reward fails to improve over SFT, providing guidance on reward design for RL-based kernel generation.

</details>


### [685] [Learning Task-Sufficient World Models by Synergizing Agentic Exploration and Structured Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04409)
*Fan Feng,Yujia Zheng,Minghao Fu,Yongqiang Chen,Guangyi Chen,Kevin Murphy,Biwei Huang,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a closed-loop framework where agents actively collect informative trajectories and world models distill task-sufficient, minimal representations, improving sample efficiency and generalization in continuous control and robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing world model approaches rely on high-dimensional latent spaces or generic visual embeddings that retain many control-irrelevant factors, limiting efficiency and task generalization.

Method: A closed-loop synergy: (1) Agent side — active probing with an adaptive curriculum to collect trajectories exposing task-relevant factors. (2) World-model side — structured representation learning to distill compact, task-sufficient latent states from interaction data.

Result: Empirical recovery of task-sufficient latent representations capturing all control-relevant factors. Improved sample efficiency and generalization across skills, object-skill compositions, and unseen tasks on continuous-control and robotic-manipulation benchmarks.
