Conclusion: Task-specific, minimal, and sufficient representations learned through agent-world-model synergy enable more efficient and generalizable decision-making in imagination-based planning.

Abstract: Learning and planning in imagination using world models provides an effective paradigm for training agents for decision-making. However, existing approaches often rely on high-dimensional latent spaces or generic visual embeddings that retain many factors irrelevant to control, limiting efficiency and generalization across tasks. To this end, we study how agents can learn world models with representations that are task-specific, minimal, and sufficient for decision-making. We achieve this via a closed-loop synergy between the agent and the world model, in which structured world-model learning distills task-sufficient representations from informative interaction data. On the agent side, agents actively probe the environment to collect informative trajectories that expose task-relevant latent factors, guided by an adaptive curriculum. On the world-model side, we learn structured representations over observations to distill compact, task-sufficient latent states from the collected interaction data. This synergy enables the empirical recovery of task-sufficient latent representations that capture all control-relevant factors. Leveraging these representations, the resulting policies achieve improved sample efficiency and generalization, including generalization across skills, object-skill compositions, and previously unseen tasks on standard continuous-control and robotic-manipulation benchmarks.

</details>


### [686] [Environmental Drivers of Respiratory Disease: A District Level Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04416)
*Rahim Iqbal,Asfi Ahamed,Izzath Nisfer,Shazan Shaheed,Muhammadu Ilham,Nathali Athukorala,Madara Mendis,Nisansa de Silva,Sandareka Wickramanayake*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study develops two validated XGBoost models (R²=0.937 for respiratory rates, R²=0.976 for PM2.5) using 11-year panel data across 25 Sri Lankan districts. SHAP analysis reveals cumulative air quality burden (80.1%) as the dominant driver of respiratory admissions, followed by forest degradation (15.6%) and fire activity (4.3%). The novel FAH Risk Index identifies Colombo, Gampaha, and Kalutara as highest-risk districts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the paradoxical decline in district-level respiratory admissions despite progressive forest degradation and rising atmospheric pollution in Sri Lanka, by accounting for the confounding role of healthcare access and providing a quantitative framework linking environmental degradation to respiratory health.

Method: Constructed an 11-year (2014-2024) panel dataset across 25 districts integrating satellite vegetation indices, fire radiative power, pollutant concentrations (PM2.5, NO2, SO2), carbon flux, and population-normalized respiratory admission rates. Built two temporally validated XGBoost models and applied SHAP analysis to quantify feature importance. Developed FAH Risk Index using SHAP-derived weights.

Result: XGBoost models achieved R²=0.937 (annual respiratory rate) and R²=0.976 (monthly PM2.5), with MAPE ≤20% in 21/25 districts. Air quality burden explains 80.1% of respiratory rate variance, vs. 15.6% from forest degradation and 4.3% from fire activity. Top risk districts: Colombo (0.802), Gampaha (0.708), Kalutara (0.682).

Conclusion: This provides the first evidence-based district-level framework correlating environmental degradation with respiratory health in Sri Lanka, establishing a quantitative basis for focused public health and environmental policy interventions.

Abstract: Sri Lanka has experienced a decade of progressive forest degradation and rising atmospheric pollution, yet district-level respiratory admissions have paradoxically declined, pointing to the confounding role of healthcare access. This study addresses that gap by constructing an 11-year (2014-2024) panel dataset across all 25 administrative districts, integrating satellite-derived vegetation indices, fire radiative power, pollutant concentrations (particulate matter (PM2.5), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), sulfur dioxide (SO2)), carbon flux metrics and population-normalized respiratory admission rates. Two temporally validated XGBoost models were created for annual district-level respiratory rate (R^2 = 0.937) and monthly PM2.5 concentration (R^2 = 0.976) with generalization validated in 21 out of 25 districts (Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) <= 20%). Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) analysis established that cumulative air quality burden is the overwhelming driver of respiratory rate variance (80.1%), ahead of forest degradation (15.6%) and fire activity (4.3%). The Forest-Air-Health (FAH) Risk Index used these SHAP-derived weights to find the districts with the highest risk: Colombo (FAH = 0.802), Gampaha (0.708), and Kalutara (0.682). These findings present the inaugural evidence-based, district-level framework correlating environmental degradation with respiratory health in Sri Lanka, establishing a quantitative basis for focused public health and environmental policy.

</details>


### [687] [Full-Stack FP4: Stable LLM Pretraining with Quantized Projections, Optimizers, and Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04422)
*Siyu Ding,Mingchuan Ma,Jiabo Tong,Xingrun Xing,Ziming Wang,Guoqi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Full-Stack FP4 proposes the first complete NVFP4 4-bit pretraining framework that resolves stability bottlenecks in linear layers, optimizer states, and attention via module-specific precision strategies, achieving near-BF16 performance with only 1.47% loss gap in 3B/64B-token pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NVFP4 pretraining methods focus on transformer linear layers, leaving optimizer states, optimizer arithmetic, and attention underexplored, which blocks stable full-stack 4-bit pretraining due to unique numerical failure patterns in each module.

Method: (1) LoRA-SVD lightweight decomposition for linear projections to suppress quantization noise; (2) AdamW second-moment transformation for robust NVFP4 storage and native NVFP4 Newton-Schulz iterations for Muon optimizer; (3) Mixed-precision attention scheme quantizing Q/K/V and backward dS while preserving vulnerable paths in BF16 with unified tensor reuse for forward-backward alignment.

Result: Linear-only loss gap reduced from 1.40% to 0.61%; 3B/64B-token pretraining achieves near-BF16 performance with merely 1.47% loss gap.

Conclusion: Full-Stack FP4 demonstrates feasible stable end-to-end NVFP4 LLM pretraining with all modules being plug-and-play, offering cumulative stability and efficiency improvements.

Abstract: Recent NVFP4 pretraining methods mainly target transformer linear layers, leaving optimizer states, optimizer arithmetic and attention underexplored in 4-bit pipelines. This critical gap blocks stable full-stack 4-bit pretraining, as the three core modules exhibit unique numerical failure patterns: linear layers hit hard quantization noise limits with dimension-propagated error amplification; AdamW second moments are heavy-tailed non-negative values fragile to low-precision denominators; attention carries error-prone computation paths demanding strict forward-backward quantization consistency. We propose Full-Stack FP4, the first complete NVFP4 pretraining framework resolving all three stability bottlenecks via module-wise precision strategies. For linear projections, LoRA-SVD lightweight decomposition suppresses quantization noise and breaks the direct-quantization error ceiling, shrinking the linear-only loss gap from 1.40% to 0.61%. For optimizers, we design AdamW second-moment transformation for robust NVFP4 storage and fully support native NVFP4 Newton-Schulz iterations for the Root (Muon) optimizer. For attention, a mixed-precision scheme quantizes Q/K/V and backward dS while guarding vulnerable paths in BF16, paired with unified tensor reuse to sustain forward-backward alignment. We further analyze fast error accumulation in naive low-bit matrix multiplication and the extreme sensitivity of PV / dOV^T attention branches. All modules are plug-and-play with cumulative stability and efficiency improvements. Our 3B/64B-token pretraining validates near-BF16 performance with merely 1.47% loss gap, verifying feasible stable end-to-end NVFP4 LLM pretraining.

</details>


### [688] [Covert Trait Propagation Is Representation Alignment: Mechanistic Evidence from Hidden-Channel Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04432)
*Kargi Chauhan,Aditya Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A student MLP trained on pure noise can inherit digit-classification from its teacher via shared initialization. This transfer is gated by geometric alignment (not just information), regulated by the output projection (W_2) as a common key and KL-driven reshaping of the student's input projection (W_0). Five experiments reveal that CKA tracks accuracy at r=0.98. This geometric lens also explains cross-token behavioral entanglement (CTBE) in instruction-tuned LLMs as a substrate-inherited, alignment-activated effect with a circularity artifact in standard log-ratio metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work proved that a student trained on uniform noise can inherit the teacher's digit-classification ability with shared initialization if the teacher's learning rate is small, but did not explain where in the network this "channel" resides or what sets its capacity.

Method: Using MLP distillation on MNIST, analyzing KL-gradient-driven alignment of student's W_0 with teacher's hidden representations via shared W_2 as a common coordinate key. Validation through five experiments: channel closure tracking, freezing W_0 vs W_2, multi-teacher ensembles, linear CKA correlation, and applying the geometric lens to CTBE in LLMs.

Result: Geometric alignment (not just information) gates channel access; CKA tracks student accuracy at r=0.98 across initialization sweep; freezing W_0 destroys transfer; multi-teacher ensembles cancel; CTBE in LLMs appears alignment-activated, inherited, and its log-ratio metric has a circularity artifact.

Conclusion: Knowledge transfer through noise relies on geometric alignment via shared initialization, not purely informational pathways. The same geometric lens exposes structural artifacts in LLM behavioral entanglement metrics.

Abstract: A student model trained on pure uniform noise can still inherit its teacher's digit-classification ability, provided the two share initialization. Previous work proves this transfer is guaranteed when the teacher's learning rate is small enough, but does not explain where in the network the channel lives or what sets its capacity. Working in an MLP distillation setting on MNIST, we show these channels are not purely informational: geometric alignment gates access to the information the channel carries. Shared initialization makes the output projection W_2 a common coordinate key, and KL gradients reshape the student's input projection W_0 until its hidden representations align with the teacher's. We call this covert trait propagation (CTP). Five experiments support this mechanism: channel closure tracks weight drift, not teacher accuracy; freezing W_0 destroys transfer while freezing W_2 leaves it intact; multi-teacher ensembles cancel out despite each teacher carrying comparable label information; and linear centered kernel alignment (CKA) tracks student accuracy at r=0.98 across a continuous initialization sweep. Applying the same geometric lens to cross-token behavioral entanglement (CTBE) in instruction-tuned LLMs, we find the effect appears to be activated by alignment training, acting on an inherited substrate, and that the standard log-ratio metric produces an apparent frequency bias that is largely a circularity artifact.

</details>


### [689] [Knowledge-Informed Local Causal Discovery of Optimal Adjustment Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04447)
*Seong Woo Ahn,Alessandro Leite,José Lucas De Melo Costa,Fabrice Popineau,Bich-Liên Doan,Arpad Rimmel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: b-LOAD extends the LOAD algorithm for local causal discovery by integrating prior edge constraints and Meek's rules, enabling more reliable identification of optimal adjustment sets in data-scarce settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Local causal discovery struggles with finite-sample uncertainty, incomplete neighborhoods, and Markov equivalence. Despite abundant structured background knowledge in many domains, its integration into local discovery remains limited.

Method: b-LOAD incorporates prior edge constraints directly into local structure learning and uses Meek's rules to dynamically expand the discovery frontier, producing a knowledge-constrained partially directed graph over the relevant local subgraph.

Result: b-LOAD improves downstream causal effect estimation over purely data-driven and standard knowledge-augmented baselines, especially in data-scarce and structurally complex regimes. On real biological networks, locally targeted prior knowledge yields largest gains and remains robust under moderate structural noise.

Conclusion: Under sound prior knowledge, b-LOAD monotonically refines the admissible equivalence class, enlarges identifiable causal queries, and offers a scalable approach to converting fragmented domain knowledge into more reliable causal-effect estimation.

Abstract: Local causal discovery is a scalable alternative to global structure learning. However, it can struggle to identify valid adjustment sets in data-scarce settings because of finite-sample uncertainty, incomplete local neighborhoods, and unresolved Markov equivalence. Although many application domains provide structured background knowledge, its integration into local causal discovery remains limited. We propose b-LOAD, a knowledge-informed extension of the LOAD algorithm for local discovery of optimal adjustment sets. b-LOAD incorporates prior edge constraints directly into the local structure-learning procedure and uses Meek's rules to expand the discovery frontier dynamically, yielding a knowledge-constrained partially directed graph over the relevant local subgraph. This strategy helps prevent structurally relevant nodes introduced by prior knowledge from being excluded by local search. We prove that, under sound background knowledge, the procedure monotonically refines the admissible equivalence class and can enlarge the set of identifiable causal queries, enabling recovery of optimal adjustment sets that are not identifiable from observational conditional-independence information alone. Empirically, b-LOAD improves downstream causal effect estimation relative to purely data-driven and standard knowledge-augmented baselines, particularly in data-scarce and structurally complex regimes. Results on real-world biological networks show that locally targeted prior knowledge provides the largest gains and remains beneficial under moderate structural noise. These findings position b-LOAD as a scalable approach for converting fragmented domain knowledge into more reliable causal-effect estimation.

</details>


### [690] [A Deep Learning-based surrogate model for Severe Accidents in nuclear reactors using ASTEC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04450)
*Alessandro Longhi,Danny Lathouwers,Zoltán Perkó*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a Deep Learning-based surrogate model (combining AutoEncoder + Neural ODE) that accelerates the ASTEC severe accident simulator from days to under a minute, predicting ~80 physical variables over 50,000 time steps with 300x dimensionality reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time severe accident simulators take days per simulation, making them impractical for operator training and real-time applications. A faster alternative is needed.

Method: The authors combine an AutoEncoder for dimensionality reduction (300x) with a Neural Ordinary Differential Equation for time-stepping, trained on ASTEC simulation data from SBO and LOCA accident scenarios.

Result: The surrogate model predicts ~80 physical variables simultaneously, maintains stable autoregressive rollout for 50,000 time steps, and simulates 40 hours of accident progression in under a minute on CPU/GPU.

Conclusion: Deep Learning-based surrogate modeling shows promise for accelerating severe accident simulations, but this is an initial study of its capabilities and limitations with ASTEC's complex non-linear physics.

Abstract: Integral codes like the Accident Source Term Evaluation Code (ASTEC) are powerful tools to study the physics of Severe Accidents (SAs) in nuclear reactors. Real time SA simulators can also be helpful in training operators of nuclear plants to react correctly to malfunctions. However, SA simulators can take up to several days per simulation, making their use infeasible for real time applications. In this work we show how to speed up a SA simulator with a fast, Deep Learning based (DL), surrogate model (SM). The SM is built as a combination of a dimensionality reduction stage, via an AutoEncoder, and a time-stepping stage, via a Neural Ordinary Differential Equation. The data on which the SM is trained are obtained from the ASTEC simulator, by sampling a set of operator actions for station blackout (SBO) and loss-of-coolant accidents (LOCA). The objective of the developed SM is to approximate multiple spatio-temporal fields for the thermal-hydraulic physics, core degradation, and fission product release modules in ASTEC's vessel domain. The SM predicts simultaneously around $80$ different physical variables (both scalar and fields), maintaining a stable autoregressive rollout up to $50$ thousand time steps. In addition, the AutoEncoder achieves a dimensionality reduction by a factor of over $300$, which allows the SM to predict up to $40$ hours of simulation in under a minute, both on CPU and GPU. This work is the first study of the capabilities and limits of DL based surrogate modeling in approximating the challenging, highly non-linear physics of ASTEC.

</details>


### [691] [Operator-on-F complements value-equivalence: a planning-time diagnostic for latent world models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04464)
*Donna Vakalis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "operator-on-F," a diagnostic that measures planning-relevant errors in learned world models by comparing the model's k-step latent pushforward against the environment's observable subset, demonstrating it provides more informative model evaluation than traditional reward-prediction error in model-based reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard model-based RL evaluation metrics like reward-prediction error and Bellman residual are insufficient for detecting planning-relevant errors in latent rollouts, as they show narrow resolution (e.g., reward error varies only 3x across model sizes) and weak correlation with actual return (Spearman -0.10 to -0.30).

Method: The proposed Operator-on-F diagnostic compares the model's k-step latent pushforward to the environment's on an observable subset F, using the model's own predictor. It was evaluated on a TD-MPC2 size sweep over cheetah-run and cross-architecture comparison between TD-MPC2 and a pure-SSL latent world model.

Result: Operator error spans 0.28-2.62 across model sizes (vs. reward error's narrow 0.028-0.091 range). The rank correlation between operator error and return loss is -0.90 (95% CI [-0.90, -0.70]). The largest model (317M) shows operator error of 2.62 with collapsed planning return of 0.9, while reward error remains in the same small range as others.

Conclusion: The operator-on-F diagnostic complements value-equivalence by providing a more informative measure of planning-relevant model errors, distinguishing models that traditional metrics cannot separate, and is effective across different architectures.

Abstract: World-model evaluation for model-based reinforcement learning typically asks whether the learned model predicts reward and value well, which can leave planning-relevant errors in the model's latent rollouts unmeasured. We introduce a complementary diagnostic, operator-on-F, that compares a model's k-step latent pushforward to the environment's on an observable subset F, using the model's own predictor. On a TD-MPC2 size sweep over cheetah-run, reward-prediction error stays within [0.028, 0.091] for every model size - only about 3x variation - so an unnormalized reward-fit check has narrow resolution to distinguish them; the (unnormalized) Bellman residual and reward error themselves have weak relationships with return (Spearman -0.10 and -0.30). Operator error spans 0.28 to 2.62 over the same sizes. At 317M the operator error is 2.62 - an order of magnitude above the 0.28-0.36 cluster - and the planning return collapses to 0.9, while reward-prediction error (0.091) is the highest of the five but stays within the same small [0.028, 0.091] range as the rest of the sweep. The rank correlation between operator error and return loss is -0.90 (anchor-bootstrap 95% CI [-0.90, -0.70] at n=5 sizes; leave-one-out removal of any single size leaves it at -0.80 or stronger). The operator also returns informative, architecture-discriminating estimates in a cross-architecture comparison between TD-MPC2 and a pure-SSL latent world model. The operator diagnostic complements value-equivalence rather than replacing it.

</details>


### [692] [Regime-Conditional Stabilisation of LLM-Augmented Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04470)
*Faid Keddouri,Sohaib Houhou,Aissa Boulmerka,Nadir Farhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that dynamically updating LLM-generated reward weights during MARL violates stationarity assumptions, causing training failures in certain regimes. They propose Phase-Based Freeze Schedule and EMA smoothing to stabilize training, demonstrating three distinct regimes where dynamic LLM shaping helps, harms, or is neutral depending on baseline competence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the training-time dynamics of integrating LLM-generated reward signals into cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), specifically why naive dynamic weight updates can destabilize learning.

Method: The authors identify that dynamic LLM reward weight updates violate PBRS stationarity and contaminate experience replay buffers. They propose two stabilization strategies: (1) Phase-Based Freeze Schedule for strict stationarity within phases, and (2) EMA smoothing to bound per-episode weight drift. Evaluation uses QMIX with three environments (Simple Spread, Level-Based Foraging, SMAC 3m) across five seeds, plus a VDN extension.

Result: Three regimes identified: (1) Augmentative regime (baseline 74.4%): EMA improves to 86.7% (+12.3pp), naive updates collapse to 15.2%. (2) Essential regime (baseline 0.1%): any shaping unlocks task (95.9% under EMA). (3) Supplementary regime (baseline 98.8%): stabilized shaping preserves 99.9%, unstabilized adds variance.

Conclusion: Reward-signal stationarity is a necessary design constraint for LLM-shaped MARL. Regime placement (based on baseline competence) is a practical predictor of whether dynamic LLM shaping helps or harms performance.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a natural interface for translating human objectives into reward signals for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), yet the training-time dynamics of this integration remain poorly understood. We show that dynamically updating LLM-generated reward weights during off-policy MARL violates the stationarity assumption of Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS) and contaminates the experience replay buffer, whose stored transitions carry reward labels computed under stale shaping weights. We characterise the result as a regime-dependent failure whose severity depends on how competent the unshaped baseline already is. To control it we propose two stabilisation strategies: a Phase-Based Freeze Schedule that enforces strict stationarity within training phases, and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing that bounds per-episode weight drift. We evaluate across three cooperative environments and five random seeds with QMIX, complemented by an exploratory VDN extension, yielding a three-regime taxonomy. In the augmentative regime (Simple Spread), where the baseline is functional (74.4 %), EMA significantly improves success to 86.7 % ($+12.3$ pp, $p<0.01$) while naive dynamic updates collapse it to 15.2 %. In the essential regime (Level-Based Foraging), where the baseline is broken (0.1 %), any shaping unlocks the task (95.9 % under EMA). In the supplementary regime (SMAC 3m), where the baseline is near-saturated (98.8 %), stabilised shaping preserves performance (99.9 %) while unstabilised shaping adds variance without gain. These findings establish reward-signal stationarity as a necessary design constraint and indicate that regime placement is a practical predictor of whether dynamic LLM shaping helps or harms.

</details>


### [693] [LeukocyteCount: Automatic Identification and Counting for leukocytes using Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04486)
*Ahmed M. Sayed,Sondos A. Refaat,Abdallah M. Mostafa,Mariam S. El-Rahmany,Ensaf Hussein Mohamed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an automated machine learning solution for white blood cell detection, counting, and classification using a hybrid model combining YOLOv5 (for detection), MobileNetV2, and Logistic Regression (for classification). The model achieves 98% detection accuracy and 99.04% classification accuracy on the BCCD dataset, outperforming traditional manual methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional manual WBC counting and classification are labor-intensive, error-prone, and can compromise the accuracy and reliability of disease diagnosis and monitoring. The need for automated, accurate solutions motivates this work.

Method: The study uses a hybrid model: YOLOv5 for WBC detection, followed by a fine-tuned pre-trained MobileNetV2 with Logistic Regression classifier for classifying WBCs into four distinct types. The BCCD dataset is used for training and validation.

Result: YOLOv5 achieved 98% detection accuracy; MobileNetV2 + Logistic Regression achieved 99.04% classification accuracy. The YOLOv5-based RBC detection module achieved an F1 score of 99.73%, outperforming the baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid model demonstrates strong potential to transform traditional laboratory WBC analysis, enabling more accurate, efficient, and reliable disease diagnostics and monitoring.

Abstract: Diagnosing and monitoring diseases frequently involves the analysis of human biological samples, with blood analysis being pivotal. Specifically, leukocytes, or white blood cells (WBCs), are essential markers for evaluating the body's defense mechanisms against infections. Traditional methods for WBC counting and classification are labor-intensive and prone to inaccuracies, primarily due to human error. The conventional processes for blood cell analysis, especially those concerning WBCs, are beset with difficulties. These include the laborious nature of manual counting and the susceptibility to errors, which can significantly impact the accuracy and reliability of disease diagnosis and monitoring. This study proposes an automated, machine learning-based solution aimed at mitigating the identified challenges. By employing a hybrid model that integrates Yolov5 for the detection of WBCs, coupled with a finely tuned, pre-trained MobileNetV2 model and a Logistic Regression classifier, the study innovates in the accurate identification, counting, and classification of WBCs into four distinct types. The methodology leverages the BCCD dataset for training and validation purposes. The application of the proposed hybrid machine learning model has yielded remarkable results, demonstrating a detection accuracy rate of 98\% through the Yolov5 stage, and an unparalleled classification accuracy of 99.04\% in subsequent stages utilizing MobileNetV2 and Logistic Regression. Additionally, Our proposed YOLOv5-based RBC detection module achieves an F1 score of 99.73\%, which outperforms the baseline. These findings underscore the model's potential in transforming traditional laboratory practices for WBC analysis, offering a path towards more accurate, efficient, and reliable disease diagnostics and monitoring.

</details>


### [694] [Two Black Boxes, One Solver: Encoder Probing and Decoder Attribution for Neural Multi-Attribute VRP under Hard-Mask and Recourse Decoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04487)
*Sohaib Afifi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper opens the black box of neural MAVRP solvers by probing encoder representations (for constraint families) and analyzing decoder attributions (via abductive, contrastive, and counterfactual explanations). Across six encoder-decoder combinations, it finds graph inductive bias aids representation, MoE distributes constraints, and the Recourse decoder enables useful counterfactuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural MAVRP solvers achieve competitive costs but lack per-step justification, which is problematic for dispatchers who need to validate, accept, or compare solutions.

Method: Two complementary black-box openings: (1) Encoder side: linear probes, spontaneous-organization metrics, rank-based richness, and discovered-direction analyses with intervention validation. (2) Decoder side: three attribution methods (gradient, integrated gradients, DeepLIFT) feeding three reading angles (abductive, contrastive, counterfactual). Evaluated on six encoder-decoder variants.

Result: Graph inductive bias improves representational predictability and decoder sanity; MoE encoder distributes constraints rather than axis-aligning; Recourse decoder (not just softer mask) produces policies that represent infeasibility usefully, enabling make-feasible counterfactuals that Hard-Mask fails to produce.

Conclusion: The proposed protocol effectively opens the black box of neural MAVRP solvers, providing actionable explanations scored on fidelity, concentration, stability, sanity, and actionability.

Abstract: Neural autoregressive solvers for the Multi-Attribute Vehicle Routing Problem (MAVRP) reach competitive cost but offer no per-step justification, a problem when dispatchers must validate, accept, or compare them. We open two complementary black boxes in one protocol. On the encoder side, linear probes, spontaneous-organization metrics, rank-based richness measures, and discovered-direction analyses with intervention validation characterize how the latent represents constraint families at the graph, node, and edge level. On the decoder side, three attribution methods (gradient, integrated gradients, DeepLIFT) feed three reading angles: abductive, contrastive against the best feasible alternative, and counterfactual (smallest input change that switches the action or restores feasibility). Explanations are scored on fidelity, concentration, stability, sanity, and actionability. Across six variants combining three encoders (Attention baseline, Unimp, UnimpMoe) with two decoders (Hard-Mask, Recourse), we find that graph inductive bias improves both representational predictability and decoder sanity, that the Mixture-of-Experts encoder represents constraints in a distributed rather than axis-aligned way, and that the Recourse training regime, not merely its softer mask, produces policies that represent infeasibility usefully, exposing make-feasible counterfactuals that Hard-Mask policies fail to produce even when fed infeasible alternatives externally.

</details>


### [695] [Beyond travel mode: urban context shapes active mobility's mental health effects over time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04520)
*Shujuan Chen,Yue Li,Ying Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A study of 264,168 UK adults using causal ML/deep learning finds that active mobility's mental health benefits are highly unequal across individuals and places, with benefits greatest in greener, safer, less polluted areas, and inequalities widening over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether active mobility promotion leads to equitable mental health benefits across different individuals and urban contexts over time.

Method: Causal machine learning and causal deep learning applied to 264,168 UK adults.

Result: Active mobility's effects on anxiety range from 40.6% reduction to 10.1% increase across individuals; benefits are strongest in greener, safer, less polluted areas (81.8% above-average benefit) vs. least supportive environments (10.4% above-average benefit). Inequalities widen over time.

Conclusion: Universal active mobility promotion without accounting for individual and contextual differences could paradoxically widen health inequalities.

Abstract: Active mobility is widely promoted for sustainable and healthier living, but whether it translates into equitable mental health benefits across individuals and places over time remains unknown. Using causal machine learning and causal deep learning in 264168 UK adults, we find substantial inequalities in individualized effects of active mobility on anxiety, depression, and common mental disorders. These inequalities widen over time and are strongly structured by urban context. For example, anxiety risk at follow-up ranges from a 40.6% reduction to a 10.1% increase across individuals, versus a 10.4% reduction to a 0.1% increase at baseline. Benefits are greatest in greener, safer, less polluted, and less deprived neighborhood environments, with 81.8% of individuals experiencing above-average benefits and mean anxiety risk reduced by 26.4%, versus 10.4% of individuals and 7.4% reduction in the least supportive environments. Urban compact form further modifies these effects through nonlinear interactions with neighborhood environments, amplifying benefits only under supportive conditions. Despite these strong environmental gradients, genetic moderation is negligible. These findings suggest universal active mobility promotion could widen health inequalities if individual and contextual differences are not accounted for.

</details>


### [696] [ManifoldFlow: SPD-Relaxed Stiefel Layers with Learnable Singular Spectrum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04535)
*Haiwen Yi,Xinyuan Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ManifoldFlow relaxes fixed-spectrum Stiefel layers by learning a positive definite spectrum via W = Q S^{1/2}, keeping the basis on the Stiefel manifold while enabling direction-dependent attenuation/amplification, improving performance in settings where orthonormal priors are useful.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Orthogonal/Stiefel layers impose fixed singular values (all one), which lacks direction-dependent attenuation/amplification needed in many tasks; the paper seeks a minimal relaxation that preserves the orthonormal basis prior while allowing learnable spectral control.

Method: Decomposes weight matrix as W = Q S^{1/2} where Q^T Q = I (Stiefel manifold) and S is positive definite; W^T W = S means eigenvalues of S are squared singular values, enabling direct singular-value clipping and control.

Result: Learnable SPD spectrum improves over fixed-spectrum Stiefel layers in sequence, tabular, and image experiments; largest gains in recurrent language-model projections; convolutional classifier heads confirm it's not a universal replacement but targeted relaxation.

Conclusion: When an orthonormal basis is a useful prior, its spectrum need not be frozen; ManifoldFlow is a spectrum-learnable Stiefel relaxation for such settings, not a universal dense-layer replacement.

Abstract: Orthogonal and Stiefel layers give neural weights exact spectral control, but they also impose a strong modeling constraint: all represented singular values are fixed at one. Many settings that benefit from an orthonormal basis still need direction-dependent attenuation or amplification. We introduce ManifoldFlow, a minimal relaxation of a fixed-spectrum Stiefel layer that keeps the basis on the Stiefel manifold while learning a bounded positive spectrum through W = Q S^{1/2}, with Q^T Q = I and S positive definite. Since W^T W = S, the eigenvalues of S are exactly the squared singular values of the realized weight, making eigenvalue clipping a direct singular-value control mechanism. Across paired sequence, tabular, and image experiments, the learnable SPD spectrum improves the fixed-spectrum Stiefel counterpart in the reported settings where the Stiefel prior is useful, with the largest gains in recurrent language-model projections. Boundary cases in convolutional classifier heads clarify the intended scope: ManifoldFlow is not a universal dense-layer replacement, but a spectrum-learnable Stiefel relaxation for settings where an orthonormal basis is a useful prior. When the basis should be orthonormal, its spectrum need not be frozen. Code available at https://github.com/Hik289/manifold_flow

</details>


### [697] [Auto: The AGI Compiler](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04542)
*Jaber Jaber,Osama Jaber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Auto is a compiler that extracts deterministic parts of LLM agent behavior into verified programs, reducing cost dramatically (59 to 2 micro-dollars per item, 96.9% parity) while enforcing safety via sandboxed WebAssembly artifacts and guard-based deoptimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Every LLM agent run re-derives behavior token-by-token on a frontier model, which is brilliant but expensive, slow, and unbounded. The goal is to autonomously convert novel experience into permanent, verified, near-free skill.

Method: Auto records live agent behavior, measures which parts are deterministic, extracts them into verified programs or distilled specialists, and emits cognition binaries (WebAssembly artifacts). A tiered runtime executes compiled behavior behind guards; guard trips trigger deoptimization to the reference agent, and captured traces recompile back down.

Result: On AUTO-BENCH, 87.1% of 560 recorded frontier-agent spans are witness-deterministic. On a 300-item stream with three distribution shifts, marginal cost drops from 59 to 2 micro-dollars per item (6.4x end-to-end) at 96.9% parity with zero errors. Failure modes: loose guard mislabels 48.9% of compiled answers; unfaithful deopt reference causes verification gate to refuse recompilation.

Conclusion: Calibration and reference fidelity, not model capability, decide whether cheap stays correct. The system demonstrates a testable sense of an "AGI compiler" that measures what it does not know while autonomously compiling verified skill artifacts.

Abstract: Every LLM agent run re-derives its behavior token by token on a frontier model: brilliant, expensive, slow, and unbounded. We present Auto, a compiler that records live agent behavior, measures which parts are secretly deterministic, extracts them into verified programs or distilled specialists, and emits cognition binaries: WebAssembly artifacts whose manifests carry measured guarantees and whose declared capabilities are physically enforced by the sandbox. A tiered runtime executes compiled behavior behind conformally calibrated guards; guard trips deopt to the reference agent, and the captured trace recompiles back down, so nothing is figured out twice. We use "AGI compiler" in one narrow, testable sense: a system that autonomously converts novel experience into permanent, verified, near-free skill while measuring what it does not know. On AUTO-BENCH, a benchmark we introduce and pre-register, 87.1% of 560 recorded frontier-agent spans are witnessed-deterministic (three of the four censused task families measure 100.0%). On a 300-item stream with three scheduled distribution shifts, the closed loop compiles three artifact generations and drives marginal cost from 59 to 2 micro-dollars per item (6.4x end-to-end) at 96.9% parity on witnessed inputs with zero errors. The same stream also quantifies the failure modes: a loose guard silently mislabels 48.9% of compiled answers, and an unfaithful deopt reference causes the verification gate to refuse recompilation. Calibration and reference fidelity, not model capability, decide whether cheap stays correct. Code: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/auto

</details>


### [698] [Predicting Therapeutic Outcome via Aligning Patient-Specific Knowledge Graph and Gene-Level Perturbation Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04557)
*Dongmin Bang,Sugyun An,Inyoung Sung,Ilho Yun,Sun Kim,Sangseon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PREDIKTOR is a patient-centered multi-view framework that aligns personalized gene regulatory networks with simulated drug-induced transcriptomic perturbations to predict clinical drug response, outperforming baselines on TCGA and zero-shot on I-SPY2 trial while providing interpretable mechanistic attributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate prediction of patient-specific therapeutic response from pre-treatment transcriptomes is hindered by scarcity of matched clinical response labels and post-treatment molecular profiles. Existing methods (preclinical transfer-learning models vs. knowledge-graph methods) suffer from instability or inability to capture drug-induced perturbation dynamics.

Method: PREDIKTOR builds two views per patient: (1) a personalized gene regulatory network from DysRegNet + drug-target links from DrugBank, encoded via a graph neural network; (2) a frozen condition-specific gene-gene attention model pretrained on LINCS L1000 for simulated post-perturbation transcriptomics. Views are aligned via CLIP-style contrastive learning with hard negatives, then concatenated for classification.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on TCGA under patient-, drug-, and tissue-split evaluations. Transfers zero-shot to I-SPY2 trial with 5.6% AUROC improvement. Aligned embeddings yield stable gene and pathway attributions recovering known mechanisms.

Conclusion: PREDIKTOR provides actionable and interpretable precision oncology by combining mechanistic network context with transcriptomic perturbation dynamics in a unified, stable framework.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of patient-specific therapeutic response from pre-treatment transcriptomes is hindered by the scarcity of matched clinical response labels and post-treatment molecular profiles. Preclinical transfer-learning models can simulate drug-induced expression changes but are often hard to interpret and unstable, whereas knowledge-graph methods provide mechanistic context yet remain static and fail to capture drug-induced transcriptomic perturbation dynamics. We propose PREDIKTOR, a patient-centered multi-view framework that aligns a personalized network view with a transferable transcriptomic perturbation view to predict clinical drug response. For each patient, we construct an individualized gene regulatory network from tumor expression using DysRegNet and augment it with drug-target links from DrugBank; a graph neural encoder yields a drug-centric, mechanistically grounded embedding. In parallel, a frozen condition-specific gene-gene attention model pretrained on LINCS L1000 generates a simulated post-perturbation transcriptomic profile for the same patient-drug pair. We align the two views in a shared latent space via a CLIP-style contrastive objective with drug-context hard negatives, then concatenate the representations for end-to-end response classification. On TCGA, PREDIKTOR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under patient-, drug-, and tissue-split evaluations, and transfers zero-shot to the I-SPY2 trial, improving AUROC by 5.6% over competing methods. The aligned embeddings yield stable gene and pathway attributions that recover known mechanisms, supporting actionable and interpretable precision oncology.

</details>


### [699] [A Few Teacher Steps Go a Long Way: Cost-Efficient On-Policy Data Augmentation for Agent Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04574)
*Junze Ye,Jiayi Cheng,Miao Lu,Michal Mankowski,Jose Blanchet,Mohsen Bayati*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reframes on-policy data collection for LLM agents as a budget-allocation problem, finding that a few unfiltered teacher steps at learner-induced contexts outperforms behavioral cloning and more complex filtering approaches under matched supervision budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pure behavioral cloning creates a mismatch between teacher-induced contexts (seen in training) and student-induced contexts (encountered at test time). Prior work addresses this by querying teachers at student contexts with elaborate filtering, but the resource allocation question of how to best spend limited teacher supervision budgets remains unexplored.

Method: The paper formalizes the design space via rollout policy, switch-time distribution, continuation horizon, filtering rules, and two complementary costs (teacher inference before filtering and teacher supervision retained for SFT). They compare bounded unfiltered teacher continuations at learner-induced contexts against behavioral cloning and filtered alternatives across HotpotQA, ALFWorld, and Terminal-Bench-Dev.

Result: Bounded unfiltered teacher continuations at learner-induced contexts improve over pure behavioral cloning at matched budgets. Few-step continuations match or exceed success-filtered and critical-context-filtered alternatives on HotpotQA and ALFWorld.

Conclusion: A few teacher steps placed at learner-induced contexts is a more cost-efficient supervision allocation than longer or more heavily curated teacher completions.

Abstract: For LLM agents, supervised fine-tuning is not only about teacher labels' quality, but also about which interaction contexts those labels condition on. Pure behavioral cloning uses full teacher demonstrations, creating a mismatch between teacher-induced contexts seen in training and student-induced contexts encountered at test time. Recent work addresses this mismatch by querying a teacher at contexts reached by the student, often with increasingly elaborate filtering of the teacher's continuations. We instead frame on-policy data construction as a budget-allocation problem: under matched supervision resources, should teacher output be spent on more start-to-finish demos, longer continuations, outcome filtering, or broader coverage of learner-induced contexts? We formalize this design space through the rollout policy, switch-time distribution, continuation horizon, filtering rules, and two complementary costs: teacher inference generated before filtering and teacher supervision retained for SFT. Across HotpotQA, ALFWorld, and Terminal-Bench-Dev, bounded unfiltered teacher continuations at learner-induced contexts improve over pure behavioral cloning at matched budgets. On HotpotQA and ALFWorld, where we run the full comparison, few-step continuations match or exceed success-filtered and critical-context-filtered alternatives. Our findings suggest that a few teacher steps, placed at learner-induced contexts, can be a more cost-efficient supervision allocation than longer or more heavily curated teacher completions.

</details>


### [700] [Beyond the Need for Speed: Energy-Aware Code Generation via Simulation-Guided Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04577)
*Saurabhsingh Rajput,Tushar Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Green Tea, a large corpus of energy-efficiency evaluations for C++ code, and trains an energy-aware code model using simulation-in-the-loop reinforcement learning. The proposed pipeline achieves 12.63% CARET improvement and beats human-expert references on 58.4% of outputs, while revealing that IPC is a misleading proxy for energy efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Code models optimize for functional correctness but ignore energy efficiency, and physical hardware measurement is too unreliable for scalable feedback. The authors aim to replace hardware profiling with deterministic simulation to enable reproducible, large-scale energy optimization.

Method: The authors build the Green Tea corpus with 3.5M evaluations across 1,474 C++ problems using deterministic architectural simulation. They train an energy-aware model via supervised fine-tuning on energy-contrastive pairs, then apply closed-loop reinforcement learning (GRPO) with simulation-in-the-loop feedback. They also introduce CARET, a metric that penalizes functionality-sacrificing optimizations.

Result: On 143 held-out problems, the pipeline achieves 12.63% CARET, nearly tripling fine-tuning alone. It beats human-expert references on 58.4% of valid outputs. IPC misranks true energy efficiency on 67.8% of problems.

Conclusion: Direct energy simulation is essential, as proxy metrics like IPC are misleading. By releasing the dataset and infrastructure (saving 263,000 CPU-hours), the authors empower the community to build inherently energy-efficient code generation models.

Abstract: Code models strictly prioritize functional correctness, leaving software energy efficiency as an unoptimized byproduct. Training models to generate energy-efficient code requires reproducible feedback at scale, which physical hardware measurement cannot reliably provide due to variance.
  In this paper, we replace hardware profiling with a deterministic architectural simulation harness to build Green Tea, a corpus of $3.5$ million evaluations across $1{,}474$ C++ problems. We train an energy-aware code model via supervised fine-tuning on energy-contrastive pairs, followed by closed-loop reinforcement learning (GRPO) using simulation-in-the-loop feedback. To rigorously evaluate deployment readiness, we introduce the Correctness-Adjusted Reduction in Energy Total (CARET), a metric that explicitly penalizes code that sacrifices functionality for efficiency.
  On $143$ held-out problems, our simulation-in-the-loop pipeline achieves $12.63\%$ CARET, nearly tripling the gain of fine-tuning alone, and successfully beats the energy efficiency of human-expert references on $58.4\%$ of its valid outputs. Furthermore, our analysis exposes the IPC trap: standard throughput proxies like Instructions-Per-Cycle (IPC) actively misrank true energy efficiency on $67.8\%$ of problems, proving the absolute necessity of direct energy simulation. By releasing our dataset and infrastructure, we bypass the $263{,}000$ CPU-hours required for reproduction, structurally empowering the community to deploy inherently energy-efficient code generation models.

</details>


### [701] [Score Distributions, Not Cells: Evaluating Single-Cell Perturbations Under Class Overlap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04595)
*Youssef Marrakchi,Davide D'Ascenzo,Sebastiano Cultrera di Montesano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Single-cell perturbation classification suffers because perturbations overlap heavily, making per-cell accuracy meaningless. The proposed CDS averages classifier probabilities over all cells of a perturbation to rank candidates, achieving near-perfect identification from weak models without retraining, outperforming both per-cell metrics and pseudobulk-based PDS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard classification metrics (per-cell accuracy) fail for single-cell perturbation data because perturbations produce overlapping populations, so accuracy measures overlap rather than model quality. Existing benchmarks show all models plateau at low macro-F1 despite perturbations being statistically distinguishable.

Method: The Classifier Discrimination Score (CDS) averages a classifier's per-cell probability vectors across all cells of a perturbation to create a population profile, then ranks candidate perturbations by this profile. This requires no retraining and costs linear time.

Result: CDS recovers near-perfect perturbation identification from even weak classifiers (linear, MLP, Transformer) that only achieve macro-F1 0.2-0.3 per cell. CDS outperforms both per-cell metrics and pseudobulk-based PDS, with the gap widening when cells are scarce.

Conclusion: Per-cell accuracy and raw-pseudobulk scores (PDS) are unreliable for comparing perturbation models because they misrank the ground truth. CDS is recommended as a more reliable metric for single-cell perturbation evaluation.

Abstract: Most classification problems assume the classes are roughly separable, so that an individual sample can usually be assigned to one class. Single-cell perturbation data violates this assumption: two perturbations can produce different populations of cells while overlapping so much that an individual cell could belong to either. Per-cell accuracy then measures this overlap rather than model quality. We see this on Tahoe-100M and the Virtual Cell Challenge, where a linear classifier, an MLP, and a Transformer all plateau near macro-F1 0.2-0.3 even though almost every pair of perturbations is statistically distinguishable.
  The fix is to score perturbations across the whole population rather than cell by cell. We average a classifier's per-cell probability vectors over all cells of a perturbation to form a population profile, then rank candidate perturbations by this profile; we call the resulting score the Classifier Discrimination Score (CDS). Taking the top-ranked class recovers the winning perturbation. It needs no retraining, costs linear time in the number of cells, and recovers near-perfect identification from the same weak models. CDS differs from the pseudobulk-based Perturbation Discrimination Score (PDS) used in recent benchmarks only in where the average is taken, raw gene expression for PDS versus a learned discriminative space for CDS, and identifies the true perturbation more reliably on both datasets, with the gap widening as cells grow scarce. Because a metric that misranks the ground truth will misrank the models scored against it, per-cell accuracy and raw-pseudobulk scores should be used with caution when comparing perturbation models.

</details>


### [702] [Minimum Block Width for Universal Approximation by Residual Neural Networks with Inner Width One](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04597)
*Qi Zhou,Xuan Zhou,Xiao-Song Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes exact minimum block widths for residual neural networks to achieve universal approximation, showing the minimum block width is max{dx, dy} for Lp approximation when inner width is 1, and proving impossibility for block widths below max{dx, dy}.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine the exact minimum block width required for residual neural networks to achieve universal approximation, filling a gap in understanding the fundamental architectural requirements for approximation power.

Method: Theoretical analysis establishing upper and lower bounds on block width for residual networks with LeakyReLU, ReLU, and ReLU-like activation functions, considering both Lp and uniform approximation on compact domains.

Result: (1) Minimum block width for Lp approximation is max{dx, dy} when inner width is 1. (2) Block width min{dx+dy, max{2dx+1, dy}} achieves uniform approximation with inner width 1. (3) Block width less than max{dx, dy} cannot approximate all target functions regardless of inner width.

Conclusion: The paper provides tight theoretical bounds on residual network width for universal approximation, establishing fundamental architectural constraints that are necessary and sufficient for approximation power.

Abstract: In this paper, we study the universal approximation property of residual neural networks, and obtain some new results. For input and output dimensions $d_x$ and $d_y$, and LeakyReLU, ReLU, ReLU-like activation functions, the upper and lower bounds of the block width are established. To achieve $L^p$ approximation $(1\leq p <+\infty)$ on any compact domain, we show that the exact minimum block width is $\max\{d_x,d_y\}$ when the inner width is 1. Furthermore, we show that residual neural networks with block width $\min\{d_x+d_y, \max\{2d_x+1,d_y\}\}$ can achieve uniform approximation on any compact domain under the constraint that each residual branch has inner width 1. Besides, for any activation function family, we prove that residual neural networks with block width less than $\max\{d_x, d_y\}$ cannot approximate all target functions, both in the $L^p$ sense and the uniform sense, regardless of inner width.

</details>


### [703] [Measuring What Matters: A Unified Evaluation Framework for GNN Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04600)
*Francesco Paolo Nerini,Mirko Zaffaroni,Paolo Baracco,Gabriele Ciravegna,Alan Perotti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified, quantitative benchmarking framework for Graph eXplainable AI (G-XAI) that requires no ground-truth assumptions, evaluates topological and feature explainability separately, identifies Pareto-optimal explainers, and provides actionable usability guidelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite many G-XAI explainers being available, there is no consistent evaluation framework or actionable guidance for choosing the right method and assessing trustworthiness, hindering practical adoption.

Method: The authors formalize tabular explainability metrics adapted for graph data, evaluating topological structure and node features as independent components, and conduct a large-scale benchmarking study identifying Pareto-optimal explainers.

Result: The study identifies explainers that consistently lie on the Pareto front across metric pairs and tasks, confirming robust non-dominated solutions, while finding that no single explainer achieves universal superiority.

Conclusion: The findings are distilled into actionable G-XAI usability guidelines to help ML practitioners evaluate and deploy trustworthy GNN-based pipelines.

Abstract: Graph eXplainable AI (G-XAI) is increasingly important for making Graph Neural Networks interpretable and accountable. While a growing number of explainers are available, choosing the right method and assessing the trustworthiness of its outputs remains unclear. Consistent evaluation practices and actionable guidance are still missing, hindering practical adoption. In this paper, we introduce a unified, quantitative benchmarking framework for G-XAI that requires no ground-truth assumptions. We formalize tabular explainability metrics for graph data, evaluating topological structure and node features as independent components. Our large-scale benchmarking study identifies explainers that consistently lie on the Pareto front across metric pairs and tasks, establishing robustly non-dominated solutions - while confirming that no single explainer achieves universal superiority. We distill our findings into actionable G-XAI usability guidelines to support Machine Learning practitioners in evaluating and deploying trustworthy GNN-based pipelines.

</details>


### [704] [Reliability and Identifiability in Persona-Trained Monte Carlo: Variance Decomposition, Stability Bounds, and the Identifiability of Heterogeneous News Reaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04627)
*Salavat Ishbulatov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops rigorous statistical theory for Persona-Trained Monte Carlo (PTMC) estimators, decomposing variance, deriving optimal compute allocation, providing identification theory for heterogeneous news reaction, and establishing separation theorems that formally justify PTMC over homogeneous simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make PTMC estimators "reliable" by developing precise statistical theory, addressing variance decomposition, error budgets, identification of heterogeneous news reactions, and formal justification of PTMC's advantages over alternative approaches.

Method: Decomposes estimator variance into persona-draw and within-run components with ANOVA estimators; uses coupling-based stability bounds; develops identification theory via Jensen gap, odd moments, and Hausdorff determinacy; provides separation theorems comparing PTMC to homogeneous simulators.

Result: Derives variance-optimal compute allocation, three-term total-error budget, √n-consistent estimators, boundary-corrected test for homogeneous news reaction, and separation theorems showing irreducible Jensen bias floor and Lucas critique as minimax limits.

Conclusion: PTMC estimators can be made provably reliable with formal guarantees classified as unconditional (Monte Carlo convergence), conditional worst-case (error budget), or open (large-K mean-field limit).

Abstract: Persona-Trained Monte Carlo (PTMC) estimates distributions of market-outcome functionals by repeatedly simulating limit-order-book interaction among $K$ neural policy bots whose behavioral personas are drawn from a learned heterogeneity distribution $\mathcal{P}$. This paper develops the statistical theory that makes the word "reliable" precise for such estimators.
  We decompose estimator variance into a persona-draw component $σ_P^2$ and a within-run component $σ_w^2$, give unbiased ANOVA estimators of both, and derive the variance-optimal allocation of a fixed compute budget between outer persona draws and inner replications. A coupling-based stability bound quantifies how misestimation of $\mathcal{P}$ and error in the trained policy propagate into the estimand, yielding a three-term total-error budget whose terms are separately estimable; a uniform-in-horizon version holds under a Doeblin condition on the market chain.
  The main contribution is an identification theory for heterogeneous news reaction: under a fixed response nonlinearity, the aggregate impact curve $A(z)=\mathbb{E}_Q[g(ηz)]$ detects heterogeneous news sensitivity through a strict Jensen gap and identifies the distribution $Q$ locally via odd moments and Hausdorff determinacy, with sharp failure when the response family is unknown. We provide $\sqrt{n}$-consistent estimators and a boundary-corrected test of homogeneous news reaction. Two separation theorems delimit when PTMC is provably preferable to homogeneous-population simulators and reduced-form forecasters, formalizing an irreducible Jensen bias floor and the Lucas critique as a minimax limit on intervention extrapolation. All proofs are given in full; guarantees are classified as unconditional (Monte Carlo convergence), conditional worst-case (the error budget), or open (the large-$K$ mean-field limit).

</details>


### [705] [Machine Learning for Depression Screening and Intervention: an Original Circadian Rhythm Score-based Methodology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04648)
*Bin Wang,Shuo Lian,Yuanyuan Hou,Dexian Wang,Peilan He,Feng Hong,Yanwei Yu,Tianrui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Circadian Rhythm Score (CRS), a composite index that compresses multi-domain behavioral data into a single circadian representation, achieving near-lossless compression for depression screening. Using gradient-boosted trees and SHAP analysis on CHARLS data (n=15,233), it achieves ROC-AUC=0.825 and identifies actionable behavioral thresholds like 300 MET-min/week exercise and 65-minute naps for sleep-deprived individuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing depression screening from behavioral data suffers from fragmented circadian indicators, limited interpretability, and lack of intervention-oriented analysis. Traditional approaches analyze sleep, activity, and social behaviors in isolation, failing to capture their joint circadian structure.

Method: (1) Circadian Rhythm Score (CRS): A composite index compressing multi-domain daily behaviors into unified circadian representation using non-negative constraints. (2) Interpretable screening framework: Gradient-boosted trees + SHAP analysis for nonlinear/saturation associations. (3) Interaction modeling and counterfactual regression for heterogeneous, dose-dependent effect estimation.

Result: Near-lossless compression (single CRS retains full predictive capability), ROC-AUC=0.825 on CHARLS (n=15,233), actionable thresholds: ~300 MET-min/week exercise, ~65 min nap for sleep-deprived individuals.

Conclusion: The work bridges supervised representation learning and interpretable modeling, providing a scalable framework for depression screening and intervention-aware healthcare data mining.

Abstract: Depression screening from large-scale behavioral data is challenged by fragmented circadian indicators, limited interpretability, and the lack of intervention-oriented analysis. Existing approaches typically analyze sleep, activity, and social behaviors in isolation, failing to capture their joint circadian structure. To address this limitation, we first propose the Circadian Rhythm Score (CRS), a composite index that compresses multi-domain daily behaviors into a unified representation of circadian rhythm. CRS is constructed to maximize discriminative power for depression screening while preserving behavioral semantics through non-negativity constraints. Empirical results demonstrate near-lossless compression, where a single CRS retains almost the full predictive capability compared with multiple raw behavioral indicators. Building upon CRS, we develop an interpretable depression screening framework based on gradient-boosted trees and SHAP analysis, revealing nonlinear and saturation-like associations between circadian rhythm and depression risk. Beyond risk prediction, we further integrate interaction modeling and counterfactual regression to estimate heterogeneous and dose-dependent behavioral effects, enabling intervention-oriented reasoning under different circadian contexts. Experiments on the China Health and Retirement Longitudinal Study (CHARLS, n=15,233), demonstrate robust screening performance (ROC-AUC=0.825) and identify actionable behavioral thresholds, including a minimum effective exercise dose of approximately 300 MET-min/week and an optimal restorative nap duration of approximately 65 minutes for sleep-deprived individuals. By bridging supervised representation learning and interpretable modeling, this work provides a scalable framework for depression screening and intervention-aware healthcare data mining.

</details>


### [706] [A Physics-Regulated Neural Framework for Learning 3D Grain Growth Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04680)
*Zhihui Tian,Kang Yang,Michael Tonks,Amanda R. Krause,Joel B. Harley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 3D-PRIMME is a physics-regulated ML model that learns 3D grain growth dynamics from just two time steps, accurately reproducing coarsening laws and topological statistics while scaling from 512 to 550,000 grains without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Developing data-driven surrogates that preserve physical invariants (scaling laws) while remaining computationally scalable is challenging, especially in 3D microstructure evolution.

Method: A physics-regulated interpretable machine learning model trained on only two consecutive time steps of 3D grain growth data (100^3 grid, 512 grains), learning a local evolution operator that is scale-independent and temporally stable.

Result: The model accurately reproduces the linear coarsening law, preserves topological statistics over extended time scales, and scales to 1024^3 grids with 550,000 grains without retraining, maintaining consistent kinetics and grain topology.

Conclusion: 3D-PRIMME learns a scale-independent and temporally stable local evolution rule, enabling efficient and robust large-scale surrogate prediction of 3D microstructure evolution.

Abstract: Grain growth is governed by the reduction in grain boundary energy and exhibits well-established statistical scaling laws. Developing data-driven surrogates that preserve these physical invariants while remaining computationally scalable remains challenging, especially in 3D. We present 3D-PRIMME (Physics-Regulated Interpretable Machine Learning for Microstructure Evolution) for learning three-dimensional grain growth dynamics. The model is trained using only two consecutive time steps yet accurately reproduces the linear coarsening law and preserves topological statistics over extended time scales. Despite being trained on a $100^3$ grid points with 512 grains, the learned evolution operator is applied to domains up to $1024^3$ grid points with 550000 grains without retraining, maintaining consistent kinetics and grain topology across orders-of-magnitude increases in system size. These results demonstrate that 3D-PRIMME learns a scale-independent and temporally stable local evolution rule, enabling efficient and robust large-scale surrogate prediction of 3D microstructure evolution.

</details>


### [707] [URSA: Chemistry-Aware Benchmark for Utilitarian Retrosynthesis Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04688)
*Bogdan Zagribelnyy,Ivan Ilin,Nikita Bondarev,Anton Morgunov,Arkadii Lin,Maksim Kuznetsov,Rim Shayakhmetov,Vladimir Aladinskiy,Alex Aliper,Alex Zhavoronkov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces URSA, a benchmarking framework for evaluating retrosynthesis planning systems, comparing specialized deep-learning models against general-purpose LLMs on realistic drug discovery tasks. Results show LLMs support high-level planning but specialized models outperform them in reliable synthesis planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of flexible, chemically interpretable benchmarking protocols that can objectively compare specialized deep-learning retrosynthesis systems and general-purpose LLMs for synthesis planning tasks.

Method: The authors developed the URSA (Utilitarian RetroSynthesis Assessment) framework that evaluates synthetic routes from both formal perspectives (e.g., convergence to commercially available starting materials) and chemical plausibility perspectives (mimicking expert chemist evaluation). They tested both conventional retrosynthesis solutions and LLMs on novel target molecules with undisclosed synthetic routes.

Result: LLMs support high-level strategic planning for synthesis but underperform specialized retrosynthesis models in reliably solving synthesis planning tasks.

Conclusion: Specialized deep-learning retrosynthesis systems remain more reliable than general-purpose LLMs for synthesis planning, though LLMs can contribute to strategic-level planning. The URSA framework provides a more comprehensive benchmarking approach for fair comparison.

Abstract: Synthesis planning aiming to find pathways of reactions for a target molecule is one of the most important and challenging tasks in drug discovery. Recent progress has produced both specialized deep-learning retrosynthesis systems and general-purpose large language models, but objective comparison remains difficult due to the lack of flexible, chemically interpretable benchmarking protocols. In the current study, we are introducing the URSA (Utilitarian RetroSynthesis Assessment) evaluation framework that provides the opportunity to benchmark the synthetic routes not only from a formal perspective, such as convergence to commercially available starting materials, but also from a chemical plausibility perspective, mimicking the way expert chemists evaluate the reactions and routes. The study covers a comprehensive evaluation of both conventional end-to-end retrosynthesis solutions and LLMs for the synthesis planning task on a set of novel, diverse target molecules with undisclosed synthetic routes, which represent realistic tasks in the daily drug design routine. We find that while LLMs can support high-level strategic planning, they currently underperform specialized retrosynthesis models in reliably solving synthesis planning tasks.

</details>


### [708] [F-ACVAE: A Federated Adaptive Conditional Variational Auto-Encoder for Privacy-Preserving Intrusion Detection in IoT Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04698)
*Mohammad Ansarimehr,Somayeh Changiz,Ehsan Baghishani,Ali Mousavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: F-ACVAE is a federated adaptive conditional variational autoencoder framework for privacy-preserving IoT intrusion detection, achieving 99% accuracy and F1-score while reducing communication overhead by 62% through selective parameter aggregation and constrained momentum Gaussian aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized IDS suffers from high-dimensional data, class imbalance, and non-IID data across heterogeneous IoT devices, while also raising privacy concerns.

Method: Proposes F-ACVAE with selective parameter aggregation (private local encoders, shared global components) and a constrained momentum Gaussian aggregation (CMGA) strategy using update clamping and momentum-based smoothing to mitigate client drift.

Result: Achieves 99% average accuracy and macro F1-score on N-BaIoT dataset, outperforming SOTA baselines, with ~62% reduction in communication overhead.

Conclusion: F-ACVAE effectively balances high detection performance, privacy preservation, and communication efficiency, making it suitable for resource-constrained IoT environments.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of things (IoT) devices has significantly expanded the cyber-attack surface, necessitating robust and privacy-preserving intrusion detection systems (IDS). However, centralized learning approaches often suffer from severe performance degradation due to high-dimensional traffic data, extreme class imbalance, and highly non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) data across heterogeneous edge devices. To address these challenges, this paper proposes F-ACVAE, a federated adaptive conditional variational autoencoder framework that enables collaborative model training across distributed IoT devices without sharing raw data. F-ACVAE incorporates selective parameter aggregation, where local encoders remain private while globally shared components are synchronized to preserve discriminative latent structures. To further enhance stability under extreme non-IID settings and feature distribution shifts, we introduce a novel constrained momentum Gaussian aggregation (CMGA) strategy that combines update clamping with momentum-based smoothing to mitigate client drift. Extensive experiments on the N-BaIoT dataset demonstrate that F-ACVAE achieves an average accuracy and macro F1-score of 99\%, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Moreover, the selective aggregation mechanism reduces communication overhead by approximately 62\%, making the framework particularly suitable for resource-constrained IoT environments. These results highlight the effectiveness of F-ACVAE in achieving high detection performance while ensuring privacy preservation and communication efficiency.

</details>


### [709] [Hierarchical Scaffolding Enables Human-Like Cognitive Selectivity under Data Scarcity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04709)
*Juhyoung Park,Jaehyuk Bae,Hyeonbo Yang,Se-Bum Paik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCALA is a cognitively-inspired hierarchical learning framework that guides models from coarse to fine-grained recognition, achieving high sample efficiency and robust generalization under severe data scarcity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans learn efficiently despite limited data by acquiring broad categorical structures before refining finer distinctions, whereas modern ML systems demand extensive datasets. This contrast motivates a framework that mimics human-like hierarchical learning.

Method: SCALA (Scaffolded Cognitive Architecture for Learning under limited dAta) is a hierarchical framework grounded in cognitive psychology that employs coarse-to-fine scaffolding, task-relevant feature prioritization, and background distractor suppression to shift representation learning.

Result: SCALA achieves significant accuracy improvements under severe data scarcity, exhibits accelerated cluster formation, reduced intra-class dispersion, enhanced semantic separability, robust generalization to unseen classes, and accelerated acquisition of novel categories.

Conclusion: SCALA establishes a powerful framework for achieving human-level sample efficiency and resilient category generalization in data-constrained environments.

Abstract: Modern machine learning systems demand extensive datasets for visual recognition. Conversely, humans learn with high efficiency despite severe data limitations, often by acquiring broad categorical structures before refining finer distinctions. Inspired by this contrast, we introduce SCALA (Scaffolded Cognitive Architecture for Learning under limited dAta), a hierarchical learning framework grounded in cognitive psychology that guides models from coarse conceptual structures to fine-grained recognition. Our model exhibits human-like cognitive selectivity by effectively prioritizing task-relevant features while suppressing background distractors, a mechanism that induces a fundamental shift in representation learning. This shift is characterized by accelerated cluster formation, reduced intra-class dispersion, and enhanced semantic separability. Empirically, SCALA achieves significant accuracy improvements under severe data scarcity. Furthermore, this hierarchical scaffolding promotes robust generalization to unseen classes and accelerates the acquisition of novel categories. Collectively, our results establish SCALA as a powerful framework for achieving human-level sample efficiency and resilient category generalization in data-constrained environments.

</details>


### [710] [RSPO: Reward-Swap Policy Optimization for Multi-Turn LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04713)
*Qiang Liu,Taian Guo,Ruizhi Qiao,Xing Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RSPO combines dense process rewards with outcome rewards via a swap mechanism to improve RL training for LLMs in multi-turn tasks, achieving consistent gains on WebShop and ALFWorld across multiple algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome rewards in long-horizon multi-turn tasks suffer from sparse signals and slow convergence; dense process rewards provide richer feedback but may misalign with true outcome rewards, biasing training direction and degrading final performance.

Method: Reward-Swap Policy Optimization (RSPO) uses a reward-swap mechanism that leverages dense process rewards to enhance trajectory diversity while maintaining optimization consistency with ground-truth outcome rewards.

Result: RSPO achieves consistent performance improvements across multiple RL algorithms (GRPO, PPO, GiGPO) on two agent benchmarks (WebShop and ALFWorld).

Conclusion: RSPO effectively bridges the gap between dense process rewards and outcome rewards, elevating the performance ceiling of LLMs in multi-turn interactive tasks without reward misalignment issues.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning holds significant potential for training large language models (LLMs) to handle multi-turn interactive tasks. However, in long-horizon, multi-turn tasks characterized by sparse outcome rewards, directly training with outcome rewards often results in slow convergence due to the sparsity of signals and the lack of fine-grained feedback.
