Abstract: The performance of deep learning models crucially depends on the settings of hyperparameters like learning rate, initialization scale, and weight decay. Hyperparameter transfer aims to make near-optimal hyperparameter settings consistent across model scale, so that large models can be optimized by proxy tuning their smaller, cheaper-to-optimize counterparts. While transfer principles are well-studied in the context of dense neural networks in language and vision tasks, they remain comparatively under-explored for graph neural networks (GNNs). We develop and validate a transfer parameterization for GNNs trained with SGD, Adam, and AdamW. Through theoretical scaling analyses and controlled experiments, we show that the proposed parameterization yields stable feature updates, learning rate transfer, and improved performance as width and depth increase. For SGD, we identify graph-dependent first-layer correction factors and show that their use can accelerate early training in graphs with sparse bag-of-words inputs. For Adam, we explore how different message passing normalizations affect early- and late-training transfer behavior, illustrating the importance of message passing normalization and advocating for an associated hyperparameter. For AdamW, we adapt a parameterization that allows for the joint transfer of weight decay and learning rate. Together, these results provide a practical recipe for scaling GNNs across a variety of learning tasks and training scenarios.

</details>


### [737] [Beyond Modality Fusion: Deep Ensembles for Multimodal Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05019)
*Ilya Burenko,Dmitry Vetrov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep ensembles of unimodal networks outperform explicit multimodal fusion methods (late/intermediate/hybrid) for multimodal classification, especially under modality imbalance, with a heuristic for selecting model counts per modality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing late-fusion approaches suffer from modality imbalance and require complex regularization; this work questions whether explicit fusion is even necessary for effective multimodal classification.

Method: Systematic comparison of deep ensembles (unimodal networks) vs. late-fusion, intermediate-fusion, and hybrid methods at equal parameter count; proposes a heuristic for selecting ensemble composition per modality and a synthetic multimodal framework for controlled experiments.

Result: Deep ensembles consistently outperform state-of-the-art late-fusion methods and other fusion techniques. Under extreme imbalance with small ensembles, only the stronger modality matters; with larger ensembles, weaker modalities become beneficial. Findings hold across synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Explicit modality fusion is not necessary for effective multimodal classification; deep ensembles offer a simpler, more robust alternative, with scaling laws suggesting asymptotic performance advantages.

Abstract: In multimodal classification, late-fusion approaches classify concatenated modality-specific features extracted by unimodal neural networks.
  When modality imbalance is pronounced, various regularization techniques have been proposed to balance the learning process and overcome the inferior performance of late-fusion networks.
  In contrast, this work demonstrates that multimodal data can be effectively classified without any explicit modality fusion, using deep ensembles of unimodal networks.
  We systematically compare deep ensembles to late-fusion networks at equal parameter count and show that ensembles consistently outperform state-of-the-art late-fusion methods designed to address modality imbalance.
  This advantage also holds over intermediate-fusion techniques we evaluated and over hybrid methods that combine unimodal and multimodal predictions.
  We propose and empirically validate a method for selecting the number of models per modality in an ensemble, avoiding computationally expensive exhaustive search.
  Under extreme modality imbalance and small ensemble sizes, the heuristic indicates that ensembles of unimodal models trained solely on the stronger modality are preferable; as the ensemble scales up, incorporating models from the weaker modality becomes beneficial.
  Both predictions align with our empirical findings.
  To systematically explore the challenges of optimizing multimodal models, we propose a synthetic multimodal framework that allows control over both the number of modalities and their predictive strength; our findings are consistent across synthetic and real-world datasets.
  Finally, by fitting scaling laws to bimodal datasets, we estimate the asymptotic performance of ensembles.

</details>


### [738] [Uncertainty-aware damage identification in short-span bridges via physics-informed variational autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05025)
*Ana Fernandez Navamuel,A. Javier Omella,Diego Zamora-Sanchez,David Pardo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a physics-informed Gaussian copula variational autoencoder (PI-GCVAE) for structural health monitoring, which embeds a differentiable eigenvalue solver into the VAE and uses Gaussian copula to model latent variable correlations, achieving 77.2% coverage in damage identification on a synthetic bridge dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vibration-based damage identification is an ill-posed inverse problem suffering from noise, sparse sensors, and environmental variability. Existing deep learning methods lack reliable uncertainty quantification and can produce physically inconsistent results.

Method: A physics-informed Gaussian copula variational autoencoder (PI-GCVAE) that (1) embeds a differentiable numerical eigenvalue solver directly into the VAE to enforce physics constraints, (2) replaces the independence assumption of latent variables with a Gaussian copula to capture spatial cross-correlations, and (3) provides efficient distributional modeling for high-dimensional correlated latent spaces.

Result: Validated on synthetic simply supported bridge data with various damage scenarios and Gaussian noise, the PI-GCVAE accurately recovers the true posterior distribution with 77.2% coverage.

Conclusion: The proposed PI-GCVAE framework provides a reliable, scalable tool for early-stage damage diagnosis in operating bridges with proper uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: Vibration-based damage identification in civil infrastructure is a challenging, ill-posed inverse problem due to measurement noise, sparse sensor arrays, and environmental variability. While deep learning is powerful for system identification, deterministic approaches lack reliable uncertainty quantification and can yield physically inconsistent results. This work proposes a robust probabilistic Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) framework: a physics-informed Gaussian copula variational autoencoder (PI-GCVAE) for structural health monitoring (SHM). First, we eliminate the need for data-driven surrogates by embedding a differentiable numerical eigenvalue solver directly into the VAE architecture. This ensures that latent space samples satisfy the governing equations of structural dynamics, reducing the trainable parameter space and improving generalization. Second, we replace the conventional independence assumption of latent variables with a Gaussian copula. This model captures complex, physics-dependent spatial cross-correlations between adjacent structural elements, defining feasible solutions while accounting for inherent system variability and measurement errors. Third, compared with alternatives such as Gaussian mixtures, our copula-based VAE provides an efficient distributional model for high-dimensional, strongly correlated latent spaces. We validate the approach using a synthetic dataset of a simply supported bridge subjected to various damage scenarios and corrupted with stochastic Gaussian noise. Synthetic data enables exhaustive validation against ground-truth stiffness values unavailable in practice. Results demonstrate that the PI-GCVAE accurately recovers the true posterior distribution, achieving 77.2% coverage. The proposed framework provides a reliable, scalable tool for early-stage damage diagnosis in operating bridges.

</details>


### [739] [CollabEval: Statistically Efficient Collaborative Model Evaluation via Matrix Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05046)
*Adam Fisch,Daniel Deutsch,Joshua Maynez,Alekh Agarwal,Jonathan Berant,William Cohen,Amir Globerson,Jacob Eisenstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CollabEval proposes a matrix completion approach using prediction-powered inference to improve statistical efficiency in generative model evaluation by exploiting dependencies across historical model runs, enabling unbiased estimates and tighter confidence intervals with fewer annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI evaluation is resource-intensive and repeatedly performed during model development; there is a need to reduce annotation cost while maintaining statistical validity.

Method: Treats model evaluation as a matrix completion problem over an M×N score matrix, using a low-rank approximation reconstructed via prediction-powered inference, with reconstructed values serving as control variates to guarantee unbiased estimates and valid confidence intervals.

Result: Across diverse datasets, models, and sparsity levels, CollabEval substantially reduces mean confidence interval size and mean squared error of point estimates compared to baseline methods at the same annotation budget.

Conclusion: Collaborative evaluation leveraging historical dependencies can significantly improve the statistical efficiency of generative model evaluation, offering a principled framework for cost-effective assessment.

Abstract: Evaluating generative AI models is a routine, but resource-intensive, process that is conducted over and over again during the course of model development. In this work, we propose Collaborative Evaluation (CollabEval), a simple, effective, and principled method for exploiting dependencies between historical runs of different models on the same tasks to improve statistical efficiency. Specifically, our approach treats model evaluation as a matrix completion problem over an $M \times N$ matrix of evaluation scores, where $M$ is the total number of models and $N$ is the total number of evaluation prompts. We assume that a subset of these $M$ models are targeted for evaluation. For these target models only a small fraction, $p$, of prompts has been annotated with evaluation scores. Leveraging recent results in prediction-powered inference, we build a low-rank approximation of the score matrix, and use the reconstructed values as control variates in a manner that guarantees unbiased estimates of the true evaluation metric mean, in addition to statistically valid confidence intervals. Empirically, across a wide range of datasets, models, and sparsity levels $p$, we find that CollabEval substantially reduces the mean confidence interval size, and the mean squared error of the point estimate, compared to baseline methods at the same annotation budget.

</details>


### [740] [KVpop -- Key-Value Cache Compression with Predictive Online Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05061)
*Lukas Hauzenberger,Niklas Schmidinger,Anamaria-Roberta Hartl,David Stap,Thomas Schmied,Sebastian Böck,Günter Klambauer,Sepp Hochreiter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KVpop learns a fixed-budget KV cache eviction policy supervised by future-attention signals, achieving 97-98% of full-attention quality at 75-88% compression on mathematical reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache growth is a major bottleneck in autoregressive decoding because memory and bandwidth scale linearly with context length. Existing KV eviction methods rely on static heuristics or proxy scores that poorly track future token utility and cause brittle eviction as relevance shifts.

Method: KVpop learns a fixed-budget KV eviction policy by directly supervising the keep-or-drop decision. The scorer is trained against a novel future-attention target computed efficiently without materializing dense attention maps. A delayed memory-based scorer defers scoring for a fixed number of steps to exploit near-future context.

Result: On AIME and HMMT mathematical reasoning, KVpop retains 98% of full-attention performance on Qwen3-4B at 75% KV cache compression and 97% at 88% compression, consistently outperforming established eviction baselines. Qwen3-8B shows even stronger results, reaching near-full teacher performance.

Conclusion: Supervising eviction with future-attention signals cuts memory costs while maintaining quality.

Abstract: Key-value (KV) cache growth is a major bottleneck in autoregressive decoding, as memory and bandwidth scale linearly with context length. Existing KV eviction methods often rely on static heuristics or proxy scores, which poorly track future token utility and cause brittle eviction as relevance shifts. To address this, we introduce KVpop, which learns a fixed-budget KV eviction policy by directly supervising the keep-or-drop decision. The scorer is trained against a novel future-attention target, computed efficiently without materializing dense attention maps. We further introduce a delayed memory-based scorer that, uniquely among learned eviction methods, defers scoring for a fixed number of steps to exploit near-future context. On AIME and HMMT mathematical reasoning, KVpop retains 98% of full-attention performance on Qwen3-4B at 75% KV cache compression and 97% at 88% compression, consistently outperforming established eviction baselines. Qwen3-8B shows even stronger results, reaching near-full teacher performance. These results show that supervising eviction with future-attention signals cuts memory costs while maintaining quality.

</details>


### [741] [Computing Monetary Risk Measures in Linear Time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05078)
*Palash Agrawal,Gersi Doko,Maeve Burwell,Marek Petrik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces QuickVaR and QuickDivergence algorithms that compute Value-at-Risk and φ-divergence risk measures (including CVaR) for discrete random variables in expected linear time, offering order-of-magnitude speedups for large domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monetary risk measures like VaR and CVaR are widely used for expressing risk aversion, but existing algorithms for computing them on discrete random variables are computationally inefficient, especially for large domains.

Method: QuickVaR adapts the Quickselect algorithm to compute VaR in expected linear time. QuickDivergence builds on polymatroid optimization algorithms and leverages QuickVaR to compute φ-divergence risk measures including CVaR.

Result: Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed algorithms offer an order-of-magnitude speedup for large domains compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The paper provides efficient, theoretically-grounded algorithms for computing popular risk measures, with an open-source Julia library implementation available.

Abstract: Monetary risk measures have gained popularity for expressing decision-makers' risk aversion. Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Conditional-Value-at-Risk (CVaR), in particular, are used commonly for this purpose. This paper proposes new efficient algorithms to compute these risk measures for a discrete random variable in expected linear time with respect to the size of its domain. First, we propose a QuickVaR algorithm that computes the VaR of a discrete random variable. Then, we leverage QuickVaR to propose QuickDivergence, an algorithm for computing a class of $\varphi$-divergence risk measures, including the popular CVaR risk measure. The QuickVaR algorithm adapts the well-known Quickselect algorithm, while QuickDivergence builds on polymatroid optimization algorithms. Numerical results show that our new algorithms offer an order-of-magnitude speedup for large domains, and a library implementation of the algorithms is available at https://github.com/RiskAverseRL/RiskMeasures.jl.

</details>


### [742] [FAST: A Holistic Framework for Optimizing Memory-I/O, Computation, and Sampling in Temporal GNN Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05095)
*Yushu Cai,Qingrui Zhu,Lei Liu,Kai Sheng,Hao Chen,Xin He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAST is a holistic framework that jointly optimizes sampling, memory I/O, and computation for TGNN training, achieving 2.1x-4.7x speedup over state-of-the-art systems via SlimCache (compression/caching), thread-efficient graph operators, and topology-aware sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling TGNN training to large dynamic graphs is challenging due to three intertwined bottlenecks (memory I/O, irregular computation, temporal neighbor sampling), and existing systems optimize these stages in isolation, leaving substantial performance headroom.

Method: FAST introduces three key components: (1) SlimCache for within-batch compression and cross-batch caching to reduce host-device data movement; (2) thread-efficient graph operators for sparse temporal subgraphs to improve GPU cache locality; (3) topology-aware sampling strategy to improve CPU cache locality for temporal neighbor sampling.

Result: Achieves average 2.1x (up to 4.7x) speedup over state-of-the-art systems on real-world large dynamic graphs without sacrificing model accuracy.

Conclusion: A holistic, joint optimization approach across sampling, memory, and computation can significantly accelerate TGNN training while maintaining accuracy, outperforming systems that optimize these stages in isolation.

Abstract: Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) are widely used for learning from dynamic graphs in applications such as recommendation, social network analysis, and traffic forecasting. However, scaling TGNN training to large dynamic graphs remains challenging due to three intertwined bottlenecks: memory I/O, irregular computation, and temporal neighbor sampling. Existing systems often optimize these stages in isolation, leaving substantial performance headroom on the table. We present FAST, a holistic framework that accelerates end-to-end TGNN training by jointly optimizing sampling, memory I/O, and computation. FAST introduces SlimCache, which exploits within-batch compression and cross-batch caching to reduce host-device data movement under limited GPU memory budgets. It further designs thread-efficient graph operators tailored to sparse temporal subgraphs, improving GPU cache locality and reducing the latency of aggregation and edge softmax. In addition, FAST employs a topology-aware sampling strategy that improves CPU cache locality and accelerates temporal neighbor sampling. Extensive experiments on real-world large dynamic graphs show that FAST achieves an average of 2.1x (up to 4.7x) speedup over state-of-the-art systems without sacrificing model accuracy.

</details>


### [743] [Functional Bilevel Optimization for Predictive Fairness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05098)
*Ieva Petrulionyte,Julien Mairal,Michael Arbel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes two algorithms (FBO and ITD) for achieving mean demographic parity via DPVar (variance of conditional-mean predictions) in high-dimensional continuous sensitive attributes, targeting the fairness-accuracy trade-off directly through bilevel optimization, outperforming existing baselines on synthetic and tabular regression benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fairness relaxations for continuous/high-dimensional sensitive attributes rely on indirect penalties or adversarial schemes that do not directly optimize the fairness-accuracy trade-off, making them restrictive or inefficient.

Method: Formulates mean demographic parity via DPVar as a functional bilevel problem; proposes FBO (exact hypergradient via closed-form adjoint for squared loss) and ITD (differentiation through unrolled inner steps, generalizable beyond squared loss).

Result: Both methods achieve lowest or near-lowest aggregate fairness-accuracy regret on synthetic data and a new semi-synthetic benchmark from 60 tabular regression datasets, consistently matching or outperforming HSIC, adversarial, linear-dependence, and generalized-DP baselines.

Conclusion: DPVar-based bilevel optimization offers a principled and effective approach to fairness-accuracy trade-offs for continuous sensitive attributes, with practical algorithms that generalize well.

Abstract: When sensitive attributes are continuous and high-dimensional $-$ demographic score vectors, posteriors over attributes, age or income profiles $-$ enforcing full statistical independence is often too restrictive, and existing relaxations rely on indirect dependence penalties or adversarial schemes that do not directly target the fairness-accuracy trade-off. We instead consider mean demographic parity through DPVar, the variance of the conditional-mean prediction given the sensitive attribute, and show that optimizing it yields a functional bilevel problem. We propose two algorithms for this problem: FBO, which uses a closed-form adjoint we derive for the squared-loss case to obtain an exact hypergradient, and ITD, which differentiates through unrolled inner steps and extends beyond squared loss. On synthetic data and a new semi-synthetic benchmark built from 60 tabular regression datasets, both methods achieve the lowest or near-lowest aggregate fairness-accuracy regret, and consistently match or outperform strong HSIC, adversarial, linear-dependence, and generalized-DP baselines.

</details>


### [744] [Counterfactual Methods for Detecting Unfairness in Anti-Money Laundering Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05101)
*Lea Multerer,Michele Inchingolo,David Kletz,Adrian Cosma,Alessandro Antonucci,Martina Gogova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper examines fairness in machine learning-based AML systems by adding country and average behavior features to the IBM AMLSim dataset. Using counterfactual path-specific effect analysis, they find that models with better predictive performance from these features show greater fairness violations, revealing a trade-off between accuracy and fairness in AML.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AML detection systems remain underexplored from a fairness perspective despite using sensitive client data, and counterfactual analysis methods are now available to evaluate direct and indirect effects of sensitive features.

Method: Used synthetic IBM AMLSim transaction dataset, constructed additional features (country of account and average behavior), trained diverse ML models (decision trees to graph neural networks), and applied counterfactual path-specific effect analysis to assess fairness.

Result: Fairness violations are more pronounced in models whose predictive performance benefits most from the extended features, demonstrating a concrete accuracy-fairness trade-off in AML applications.

Conclusion: There is an urgent need for systematic fairness analysis in AML systems, as improving predictive accuracy via certain features can exacerbate unfairness.

Abstract: The application of machine learning-based predictive algorithms to Anti-Money Laundering (AML) has grown rapidly, driven by the vast volume of financial transaction data available to banks. These algorithms are typically trained not only on transactional data but also on sensitive client information, which may raise fairness concerns. Despite this, AML detection systems remain largely underexplored from a fairness perspective, even though deeper analytical methods based on counterfactuals are now available. Such techniques enable the decomposition of the direct and indirect effects of potentially sensitive features on model predictions, thereby supporting the evaluation of whether their influence is acceptable from a fairness perspective. Closing this gap, we consider the synthetic IBM AMLSim transaction dataset and construct additional features of the country of an account and its average behaviour. This improves the predictive performance of diverse machine learning models, ranging from baseline decision trees to state-of-the-art graph neural networks. We assess the potential unfairness associated with these features through a counterfactual, path-specific effect analysis. This reveals that fairness violations tend to be more pronounced for models whose predictive performance benefits the most from the extended features. Such a finding highlights a concrete instance of the trade-off between predictive accuracy and fairness in AML applications, thus underscoring the urgency of a systematic fairness analysis in such critical domains.

</details>


### [745] [Choosing a parallel heterogeneous ensemble method for tabular classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05103)
*Vassili Maillet,Gustavo,Angulo,Pierre Jouvelot*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper compares parallel ensemble methods on tabular classification tasks, deriving best practice recommendations that significantly outperform Single Best and match individual ensembles. Key findings: Blending/Stacking have independent inconsistencies; Robust Soft Voting excels in probabilistic classification, especially multiclass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide empirically grounded best-practice recommendations for ensemble methods on small-to-medium tabular classification tasks, addressing the inconsistency and performance variability of existing approaches.

Method: Comparative analysis of parallel ensemble methods on 56 OpenML CC18 tabular classification tasks, with validation on 28 additional tasks from TabArena. Examined Blending, Stacking, Hard Voting, and Robust Soft Voting.

Result: The derived recommendation set significantly outperformed Single Best and matched/exceeded individual ensemble methods. Blending and Stacking show independent inconsistencies; Robust Soft Voting excels in probabilistic classification, especially multiclass.

Conclusion: Robust Soft Voting is particularly effective for multiclass probabilistic classification. The best practice recommendations provide reliable guidance for practitioners, outperforming single model selection.

Abstract: Parallel ensemble methods were compared on $56$ small-to-medium tabular classification tasks drawn from OpenML CC18. A set of ``best practice'' recommendations on the use of ensemble methods was derived from these observations. It was later validated on 28 additional tasks using TabArena's precomputed data, where the recommendation set significantly outperformed Single Best and matched or exceeded individual ensemble methods.
  Two key observations were made. First, Blending and Stacking are inconsistent, but their inconsistencies are independent and happen on different tasks. Second, while Hard Voting's probabilistic classification is rather weak, a consequence of using vote proportions as posterior estimates, Robust Soft Voting's probabilistic classification is particularly successful, especially in the multiclass case.

</details>


### [746] [Grokking Is Conditional and Fragile: A Fully-Tractable, Multi-Seed Study at 12K Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05104)
*Yoshiyuki Ootani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies grokking in a tiny 11,856-parameter transformer, finding it is a fragile, conditional phase transition that depends on training-set coverage, weight decay, and even floating-point environment details, and arguing that grokking evidence must be based on multi-seed rates under fixed numerical conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grokking is usually studied in models too large to fully analyze and reported from single training runs, making the phenomenon poorly understood. The authors aim to study grokking in a fully tractable model to uncover its underlying mechanisms and fragility.

Method: The authors train a publicly released 11,856-parameter Llama-style transformer (Glimmer-1-Base) on modular arithmetic, small enough to enumerate all weights, attention patterns, and the full input-output map. They measure grokking as a multi-seed rate, vary training-set coverage, weight decay, and numerical environment (CPU thread count, CPU vs. GPU).

Result: Grokking is a conditional, fragile phase transition gated by training-set coverage with a threshold tracking output cardinality. Weight decay reproduces the Omnigrok inverted-U. Grokking is sensitive to floating-point perturbations (CPU thread count, CPU vs. GPU) that flip a minority of seeds. Sub-task specialists help primarily by making coverage cheap.

Conclusion: The unit of evidence for grokking must be a multi-seed rate under a pinned numerical environment, checked against direct model reading, as single-run narratives are unreliable and can be overturned by seed confounds.

Abstract: Grokking -- the delayed onset of generalization long after a network has fit its training set - -is usually studied in models too large to read completely and reported from single training runs. We instead study a publicly released ~11,856-parameter Llama-style transformer (Glimmer-1-Base) on modular arithmetic, small enough to enumerate its weights, attention, and full input-output map, and we measure grokking as a multi-seed rate rather than a single outcome. In this fully-tractable regime grokking is a conditional, fragile phase transition. It is gated by training-set coverage, whose threshold tracks output cardinality (the modulus) more than task structure, an ordering that holds above the transition and across a ten-fold change in domain size. Weight decay reproduces the Omnigrok inverted-U at 12K parameters, a positive control on the rate measurement. Grokking also sits on a numerical knife-edge: two perturbations of the floating-point environment -- CPU thread count (reduction order) and CPU-versus-GPU execution -- each flip a minority of same-seed outcomes without a detectable shift in the aggregate rate. Decomposition into sub-task specialists helps chiefly by making coverage cheap rather than by adding supervision. Methodologically, multi-seed control under a fixed numerical environment overturns three dramatic single-run narratives in our own data, each a seed confound. The unit of evidence for grokking must therefore be a multi-seed rate under a pinned numerical environment, checked where possible against a direct reading of the model.

</details>


### [747] [Localized LoRA-MoE: Block-wise Low-Rank Experts With Adaptive Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05114)
*Babak Barazandeh,Subhabrata Majumdar,Vinay Prithyani,George Michailidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Localized LoRA-MoE, a framework combining spatial blocking with dynamic routing for parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Two variants are proposed: Block-Wise LoRA-MoE (centralized macro-routing) and Cell-Wise LoRA-MoE (decentralized micro-routing). Experiments show that decentralized cell-level gating acts as a "gradient firewall" against fault propagation, outperforming static baselines across diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PEFT methods like LoRA suffer from gradient warfare and destructive optimization feedback when handling interleaved multi-task streams. Existing spatial partitioning methods remain trapped in static topologies unable to adapt to dynamic task-switching or sensor failure.

Method: Localized LoRA-MoE: Block-Wise LoRA-MoE uses centralized macro-routing via a monolithic context signal, while Cell-Wise LoRA-MoE enables each coordinate cell in the matrix grid to perform autonomous, localized expert gating.

Result: Both architectures resolve optimization deadlocks inherent in static baselines. Decentralized cell-level gating achieves statistical parity with an omniscient global coordinator and provides a robust "gradient firewall" against fault-propagated corruption.

Conclusion: The proposed methods offer scalable, parameter-efficient solutions for dynamic model adaptation, outperforming static baselines across granular coordinate fields and shifting operational regimes.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) and high-dimensional perception networks increasingly rely on parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) to adapt to diverse operational contexts. However, standard methods like LoRA are structurally limited by a monolithic bottleneck, making them highly susceptible to gradient warfare. Interleaved multi-task streams may trigger destructive optimization feedback, collapsing adapter weights into unspecialized averages. While recent spatial partitioning methods have introduced block-wise isolation, they remain trapped in static topologies, unable to adapt to dynamic task-switching or environmental sensor failure. In this work, we introduce Localized LoRA-MoE, a unified framework that fuses localized spatial blocking with dynamic, context-conditioned routing. We propose and evaluate two novel architectural paradigms: Block-Wise LoRA-MoE (Centralized Macro-Routing), which modulates the entire structural grid via a monolithic context signal, and Cell-Wise LoRA-MoE (Decentralized Micro-Routing), which empowers every coordinate cell in the matrix grid with autonomous, localized expert gating. Through a comprehensive suite of benchmarks, ranging from high-dimensional SVD matrix simulations and real-world tabular transformations to spatial vision perception under sensor degradation, we demonstrate that both architectures resolve optimization deadlocks inherent in static baselines. Our empirical results establish that decentralized cell-level gating achieves complete statistical parity with an omniscient global coordinator, providing a robust "gradient firewall" that protects surviving pathways from fault-propagated corruption. Our proposals consistently outperform static baselines, offering a scalable and parameter-efficient solution for dynamic model adaptation across granular coordinate fields and shifting operational regimes.

</details>


### [748] [PDEFlow: Autonomous Agentic PDE Pipelines for Neural Operator Learning and Solver-Free Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05134)
*Akshat Jani,Prathamesh Gadekar,Sakhinana Sagar Srinivas,Venkataramana Runkana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PDEFlow is an autonomous agentic framework that converts natural-language ODE/PDE descriptions into complete neural-operator pipelines, from problem specification and data generation to training and inference, using a multi-branch Bayesian DeepONet and FEniCSx backend.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To automate and streamline scientific workflows where many related physics configurations must be specified, simulated, learned, and queried with minimal manual intervention, reducing the gap between user-level problem descriptions and solver-backed neural-operator pipelines.

Method: PDEFlow uses a stateful input graph for multi-turn natural-language processing, FEniCSx finite-element solver for data generation, a registry-based interface for flexible neural operator deployment, and a multi-branch Bayesian DeepONet as the current instantiation.

Result: Experiments on benchmark ODE and PDE tasks demonstrate that PDEFlow can construct valid specifications, generate solver-backed datasets, train neural operators across steady/transient problems, and provide solver-free predictions from saved checkpoints.

Conclusion: PDEFlow enables repeatable, automated scientific and engineering workflows for physics problems, linking natural-language specification to neural operator inference with minimal manual intervention.

Abstract: We present PDEFlow, an autonomous agentic framework that turns user-level ODE and PDE descriptions into solver-backed neural-operator pipelines. The workflow links problem specification, data generation, operator training, and checkpoint-based inference. A stateful input graph converts multi-turn natural-language input and user edits into validated problem specifications. The data-generation module then samples parameters, solves the configured governing-equation with FEniCSx finite-element backend, and stores the solutions as operator-ready tensors. The training and inference stages use a registry-based interface, allowing different neural operators to be trained and deployed without changing the surrounding pipeline. In the current implementation, we instantiate this interface with a multi-branch Bayesian DeepONet. Experiments on benchmark ODE and PDE tasks show that PDEFlow can construct valid specifications, generate solver-backed datasets, train neural operators across steady and transient problem classes, and provide solver-free predictions from saved checkpoints. The framework is designed for repeatable scientific and engineering workflows where many related physics configurations must be specified, simulated, learned, and queried with minimal manual intervention.

</details>


### [749] [Physiological Noise Augmentation Improves Non-Invasive Brain-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05165)
*Benjamin Ballyk,Teyun Kwon,Miran Özdogan,Oiwi Parker Jones*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces physiological noise augmentation (PNA), a data augmentation method that trains brain-to-speech decoders to become invariant to task-agnostic artifacts (e.g., ocular and cardiac noise) by decomposing brain recordings with ICA and remixing clean data with noise artifacts, improving EEGNet decoding accuracy by 4.7% on a MEG imagined-digit dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-invasive brain-to-speech decoding (MEG/EEG) suffers from high word error rates due to low signal-to-noise ratios compared to invasive methods, motivating a need for robustness against physiological artifacts without requiring neurosurgery.

Method: PNA decomposes brain recordings into clean data and noise artifacts using ICA, then scales and remixes them to generate biophysically realistic, label-preserving training examples, inspired by environmental noise augmentation in ASR systems.

Result: On the MegNIST 12k-trial imagined-digit MEG dataset, PNA with 10-trial averaging improves EEGNet decoding accuracy by 4.7 percentage points (absolute) compared to training on real data alone.

Conclusion: Artifact-aware augmentation (PNA) and trial averaging are complementary tools for improving robustness in non-invasive speech BCIs, with PNA approximating anisotropic regularization that penalizes decoder sensitivity along artifact-dominated directions.

Abstract: Non-invasive brain-to-speech decoding aims to restore communication to patients suffering from neurodegenerative disease, without the risks of neurosurgery. Existing MEG- and EEG-based methods, while scalable, continue to suffer from high word error rates driven by relatively low signal-to-noise ratios compared to invasive recordings. We propose physiological noise augmentation (PNA), a data augmentation method that explicitly trains decoders to become invariant to task-agnostic artifacts (e.g. ocular and cardiac activity). PNA draws inspiration from automatic speech recognition systems, where environmental noise (e.g. dogs barking, city traffic) is added to clean speech to improve robustness. Analogously, we decompose brain recordings into clean data and noise artifacts using independent component analysis (ICA), before scaling and remixing to generate biophysically realistic, label-preserving training examples. We show that PNA approximates anisotropic regularization, penalizing decoder sensitivity along artifact-dominated directions. On MegNIST, a 12k-trial imagined-digit MEG dataset, PNA with 10-trial averaging improves EEGNet decoding accuracy by 4.7 percentage points (absolute) over training on real data alone. Our results suggest that artifact-aware augmentation and trial averaging are complementary tools for improving robustness in non-invasive speech BCIs.

</details>


### [750] [MeGA-MP: Metric Graph Advection Message Passing -- A Physics-Informed Message Passing Operator for Advection-Dominated Metric Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05167)
*Janine Strotherm,Luca Hermes,André Artelt,Barbara Hammer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a physics-informed message passing operator that encodes linear advection on metric graphs, achieving exact dynamics recovery without training for advection and superior performance on advection-reaction systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world networked systems (water, gas, electricity, traffic) exhibit spatio-temporal dynamics along edges rather than discretely at nodes, requiring models that can capture antisymmetric and long-range dependencies on metric graphs without relying on an underlying global Euclidean space.

Method: A novel physics-informed message passing operator that encodes linear advection on metric graphs as an inductive bias, provably recovering exact dynamics up to discretization error without training in the purely advective case, and extendable with trainable MLPs for advection-reaction dynamics.

Result: In purely advective settings, the operator provably recovers exact dynamics without training. For realistic advection-reaction dynamics in water distribution systems, it achieves superior performance over baselines and zero-shot generalization across different graph topologies.

Conclusion: The proposed physics-informed message passing framework effectively models transport dynamics on metric graphs, combining theoretical guarantees with practical performance and generalization capabilities.

Abstract: Many real-world systems are organized as networks where spatio-temporal dynamics unfold along connections and not discretely between nodes. Examples include utility networks such as water distribution systems or gas networks, electrical grids, and traffic flow networks. Such systems are naturally modeled as metric graphs, where edges correspond to one-dimensional Euclidean subspaces connected at vertices. Metric graphs are independent of an underlying global Euclidean space, limiting direct application of typical PINNs and operator-learning methods. Especially transport dynamics like advection require a methodology able to capture antisymmetric and long-range dependencies on graphs, which is itself a challenge. We propose a novel physics-informed message passing operator that encodes linear advection on metric graphs as an inductive bias. In the purely advective setting, the operator provably recovers the exact dynamics up to a theoretically derived discretization error without any training. Combined with trainable components like MLPs, our message passing operator extends to realistic advection-reaction dynamics in water distribution systems, where we achieve superior performance compared to baselines and zero-shot generalization across different graph topologies.

</details>


### [751] [Platonic Projection Structures: Operator-Induced Observability in Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05175)
*Kazuo Ishii,Bishnu Prasad Gautam,Jieling Wu,Javaid Saher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Platonic Projection Structures (PPS), an operator-theoretic framework for characterizing observability in representation learning, showing that latent representations are only accessible through positive semidefinite observation operators, with unobservable components lying in the kernel of the observation operator. The framework unifies quantum measurement, representation inference, knowledge distillation, and reveals fundamental limits of interpretability methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to provide a rigorous mathematical characterization of what can and cannot be observed about latent representations in machine learning systems, addressing the gap between latent representations and their observable outputs through an operator-theoretic lens.

Method: The paper proposes Platonic Projection Structures (PPS) as a triple (H, Π, O), where H is a latent space, Π is a self-adjoint positive semidefinite observation operator, and O(v)=⟨v,Πv⟩ defines observable quantities. Observability is characterized via quotient geometry H/ker(Π).

Result: Key findings include: (1) structural correspondence between quantum measurement and representation inference, (2) knowledge distillation as approximate preservation of observable geometry, (3) fundamental limitations of output-based interpretability due to kernel-inaccessible components, (4) empirical validations showing kernel-invariant observability and projection-induced attribution gaps.

Conclusion: PPS provides a unified, mathematically explicit characterization of observability in representation learning through operator-induced quotient geometry, revealing intrinsic constraints on representation accessibility, interpretability, and projection-mediated inference.

Abstract: We characterize observability in representation learning through Platonic Projection Structures (PPS), an operator-theoretic framework for analyzing representation accessibility under partial observation. Rather than treating observable outputs as direct reflections of latent representations, PPS models observation through a self-adjoint positive semidefinite operator acting on a latent representation space. A system is represented as a triple $(H, Π, O)$, where $H$ is a latent representation space, $Π\succeq 0$ is an observation operator, and $O(v)=\langle v,Πv\rangle$ defines an induced scalar observable. Observability is characterized by the quotient geometry $H/\ker(Π)$, representing equivalence classes of latent states indistinguishable under observation. We show that quantum measurement and representation inference under linear observation models share this operator-theoretic structure while differing in the algebraic properties of their observation operators; the correspondence is structural rather than physical. Representation transfer and knowledge distillation can likewise be interpreted as approximate preservation of observable geometry through $ΦΠ_T \approx Π_S Φ$. PPS also reveals a structural limitation of output-based interpretability: latent components in $\ker(Π)$ are inaccessible from induced observables, imposing intrinsic constraints on attribution and explanation methods. Controlled empirical validations demonstrate kernel-invariant observability, projection-induced attribution gaps, and rank-controlled observable geometry in latent representation spaces. PPS thus provides an explicit characterization of observability through operator-induced quotient geometry and a unified perspective on representation accessibility, interpretability, and projection-mediated inference.

</details>


### [752] [Relational Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Pricing in High-Speed Railway Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05179)
*Enrique Adrian Villarrubia-Martin,David Muñoz-Valero,Luis Rodriguez-Benitez,Giovanni Montana,Luis Jimenez-Linares*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an entity graph modeling approach with multi-agent reinforcement learning for dynamic railway pricing under partial observability, using relational graph convolutional networks and attention mechanisms to capture market topology, achieving superior revenue and stability over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In liberalized railway systems, operators must set prices competitively without direct communication (to avoid collusion), relying only on observable market data. Standard multi-agent RL methods treat observations as unstructured vectors, ignoring the underlying market topology that governs strategic interactions.

Method: An entity graph modeling approach represents the environment as a graph of operational units encoding competition, coordination, and connectivity relations. An extension of MATD3 (multi-agent twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient) with graph-based representation learning processes entity features through a multi-layer relational graph convolutional network and aggregates them via a learned attention mechanism.

Result: In a rail pricing RL environment, the proposed framework achieves higher revenue and stability in two settings of increasing market complexity compared to both relational and non-relational baselines.

Conclusion: Graph-based representation learning that captures market topology significantly improves multi-agent pricing performance under partial observability, demonstrating the value of structured entity representations over unstructured observation vectors in competitive market settings.

Abstract: In liberalised railway systems, operators must set prices dynamically in an environment with partial observability, as they retain private information about their objectives and performance, where regulatory constraints prohibit communication or direct information exchange between competitors to prevent explicit collusion. Consequently, agents must learn to infer strategic interactions only from observable market data which presents a significant challenge for multi-agent reinforcement learning, where standard approaches typically treat observations as unstructured vectors, ignoring the underlying market topology that governs strategic interactions. To address this, an entity graph modelling approach is proposed, which represents the environment as a graph of operational units, rather than decision-making agents or static infrastructure, encoding competition, coordination, and connectivity relations between entities. Then, an extension of the multi-agent twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient algorithm with graph-based representation learning processes the features of the entities through a multi-layer relational graph convolutional network and aggregates them via a learnt attention mechanism. Experimental results in a rail pricing reinforcement learning environment show that this novel framework achieves higher revenue and stability in two different settings of increasing market complexity compared to a representative selection of relational and non-relational baselines. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/Kinrre/RelationalRailPricing-RL

</details>


### [753] [SMART: A Machine Learning and Monte Carlo Framework for Rapid Analysis of Stochastic Transistor Aging and Process Variation in Digital Circuits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05187)
*Arash Esshaghi,Siavash Es'haghi,Gholamreza Shahabadi,Alireza Moradi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMART integrates Random Forest regression with Bayesian Optimization to enable rapid, high-fidelity reliability analysis under BTI and PV effects, achieving 94.54% time reduction with only 1.63% accuracy error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reliability analysis methods (intensive simulations, lookup tables) cannot scale efficiently for large digital circuit designs under combined BTI and PV stochastic effects in deep nanometer CMOS.

Method: SMART uses Random Forest regression to directly predict gate delay distributions, bypassing atomic model parameter extractions, with Bayesian Optimization for automated hyperparameter tuning.

Result: On ISCAS85 benchmarks, SMART achieves 94.54% reduction in analysis time compared to state-of-the-art methods with only 1.63% average accuracy error.

Conclusion: SMART shifts computational complexity to offline training, offering a scalable, accurate solution for reliability-aware digital system design.

Abstract: As CMOS technology scales into the deep nanometer regime, digital circuit reliability is increasingly threatened by the combined stochastic effects of Bias Temperature Instability (BTI) and Process Variation (PV). Traditional reliability analysis methods, which rely on computationally intensive simulations or extensive lookup tables, fail to scale efficiently for large designs, creating a critical bottleneck in design space exploration. To address this, we propose SMART, a novel framework that integrates Machine Learning (ML) with Monte Carlo simulation to enable rapid, high-fidelity reliability analysis. SMART employs Random Forest regression to predict gate delay distributions directly, bypassing time-consuming atomic model parameter extractions. Crucially, the model utilizes Bayesian Optimization for automated hyperparameter tuning, ensuring maximum predictive robustness across diverse libraries. Experimental validation on ISCAS85 benchmark circuits demonstrates that SMART achieves a 94.54% reduction in analysis time compared to state-of-the-art methods, while maintaining a remarkable average accuracy error of just 1.63%. By shifting computational complexity to an offline training phase, the proposed framework offers a scalable, accurate solution for designing resilient, reliability-aware digital systems.

</details>


### [754] [Latent Programming Horizons in Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05188)
*André Silva,Han Tu,Martin Monperrus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs under coding agents linearly encode program properties (correctness, test outcomes) in their residual streams, and these representations can predict future edits up to ~25 steps ahead before they occur, demonstrating a "latent programming horizon."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Little is known about what language models internally represent about the program they are modifying during multi-step coding agent tasks.

Method: Logistic-regression probes trained on residual stream hidden states to decode program properties (parsing, test passing, regressions). Probes also trained to predict outcomes of future edits (before they are written to disk).

Result: Probes achieve AUC up to 0.83 for correctness across two models and benchmarks. Future-edit prediction works above chance up to ~25 steps in advance. Probes transfer across benchmarks without retraining.

Conclusion: Coding agents internally represent program state linearly and ahead of actual edits, opening directions for mechanistic interpretability of agents.

Abstract: A coding agent solving a software-engineering task spends dozens of steps reasoning, editing code, and running tests, yet little is known about what the underlying language model internally represents about the program it is working on. We show that the residual streams of language models under coding agents linearly encode properties of the evolving program: a logistic-regression probe on hidden states is able to decode whether the current code parses, passes its test suite, reduces the number of failing tests, and introduces regressions, reaching AUC up to 0.83 for correctness across two models and two benchmarks. Our second finding is more surprising: these representations run ahead of the agent's own edits. Probes trained to predict the outcome of future edits (before they are materialized and written on disk) achieve performance above chance up to roughly 25 steps in advance. We call this the agent's latent programming horizon. As a proof of external validity, we show that the probes transfer across benchmarks without retraining. Our positive results open calls for more research in mechanistic interpretability of coding agents.

</details>


### [755] [Noisy-Channel Minimum Bayes Risk Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05198)
*Yusuke Sakai,Hidetaka Kamigaito,Taro Watanabe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MBR decoding has a discrepancy due to asymmetric evaluation metrics. The authors propose a noisy channel decomposition of MBR that incorporates bidirectional effects (hypothesis-to-reference and reference-to-hypothesis), offering unified interpretation of existing variants and enabling metric-specific analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MBR decoding uses asymmetric metrics (e.g., BLEU, COMET) for hypothesis selection, but it only computes expected utility in one direction. This design discrepancy needs to be addressed for more robust text generation.

Method: The authors decompose MBR decoding into four components via a noisy channel framework: hypothesis-to-reference likelihood, reference-to-hypothesis likelihood, hypothesis prior, and reference prior. This captures bidirectional effects naturally.

Result: Channel-wise contributions vary across metrics but remain consistent across tasks. Appropriate channel weighting may improve over original MBR decoding.

Conclusion: The proposed noisy channel decomposition resolves the asymmetry issue in MBR decoding, provides interpretability across metrics and tasks, and suggests that tuning channel weights can further enhance performance.

Abstract: Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) decoding yields more robust and higher-quality text generation than maximum a posteriori (MAP) decoding by selecting hypotheses that maximize expected utility over sampled pseudo-references. However, there exists a discrepancy in the design: hypothesis selection calculates expected utility scores conditioned on given pseudo-references, while commonly used evaluation metrics, e.g., BLEU and COMET, are asymmetric. Therefore, it is important to consider both hypothesis-to-reference and reference-to-hypothesis directional effects. In this study, we introduce a noisy channel decomposition of MBR decoding that naturally incorporates bidirectional effects to account for these asymmetries. We decompose MBR decoding into four interacting components: hypothesis-to-reference likelihood, reference-to-hypothesis likelihood, hypothesis prior, and reference prior. This decomposition provides a unified interpretation of existing MBR variants and enables metric- and task-specific interpretability by isolating the contribution of each channel. Our comprehensive analysis reveals that channel-wise contributions exhibit distinct characteristics across metrics while remaining consistent across tasks, and suggests that appropriate channel weighting may lead to improvements over original MBR decoding.

</details>


### [756] [FlatManifold: Robust Continual Learning under Severe Label Noise and Domain Shifts via Intrinsic Manifold Flattening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05201)
*Rai Hisada,Kanji Tanaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlatManifold is a robust continual learning framework that uses Nyström manifold flattening and RKHS projection to simultaneously handle non-stationary domain shifts and severe label noise (up to 40% symmetric noise) in streaming environments, outperforming baselines on real-world robotics datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the dual challenge of adapting to complex, non-stationary domain shifts via continual learning while mitigating severe, uncalibrated label noise without relying on complex sample-filtering pipelines.

Method: A Nyström manifold flattening map based on the kernel trick, projection onto an orthogonalized RKHS with a ridge regularizer, and a continual topology brake term using covariance matrix of past experiences to prevent catastrophic forgetting.

Result: On real-world multi-session robotics datasets with 40% symmetric label noise and extreme cross-session domain shifts (varying seasons/lighting), FlatManifold successfully mitigates gradient corruption and significantly outperforms standard sequential optimization baselines.

Conclusion: Structural linearization via manifold flattening itself serves as a powerful mathematical barrier against distributed label corruption, removing the need for error-prone sample-filtering pipelines in robust continual learning.

Abstract: In non-stationary streaming environments, simultaneously adapting to complex, non-linear domain shifts via continual learning while mitigating the catastrophic effects of severe, uncalibrated label noise poses a fundamental mathematical challenge. In this paper, we propose \FlatManifold{}, a novel, streamlined robust continual learning framework that utilizes a Nyström manifold flattening map based on the kernel trick and projection onto an orthogonalized Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS).
  Unlike traditional methods that rely on complex, error-prone sample-filtering pipelines, the proposed approach exploits the intrinsic mathematical robustness of the flattened space itself. By mapping feature distributions onto a fixed orthogonal target topology with a ridge regularizer, the framework naturally smoothes and counteracts the influence of extreme label noise during the optimization process. Concurrently, catastrophic forgetting is prevented via a continual topology brake term that leverages the covariance matrix of past experiences.
  Extensive evaluation on real-world multi-session robotics datasets demonstrates that even under severe conditions featuring 40\% symmetric label noise, \FlatManifold{} successfully mitigates gradient corruption. Under extreme cross-session domain shifts spanning various seasons and lighting conditions, the proposed framework establishes high generalization capabilities, significantly outperforming standard sequential optimization baselines and proving that structural linearization itself serves as a powerful mathematical barrier against distributed label corruption.

</details>


### [757] [Video-based detection of cessation of breathing in pre-term infants using machine learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05230)
*Dineo Serame,Lionel Tarassenko,Mauricio Villarroel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-contact video monitoring can detect apnoea-related breathing cessations in pre-term infants, with camera-only models achieving 76.9% accuracy and hybrid models (video + impedance pneumography) reaching 90.6% balanced accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-term infants are susceptible to harmful apnoea, but reliable contact-based respiratory monitoring in the NICU is challenged by motion artefacts, sensor displacement, and skin fragility.

Method: Respiratory motion was extracted from video recordings of 30 pre-term infants via dynamically tracked torso regions. Camera-only models used ResNet architectures; hybrid models combined video-derived signals with IP, EDR, and PPG-derived respiratory envelope.

Result: Camera-only achieved 76.9% balanced accuracy; hybrid (video + IP) achieved 90.6% balanced accuracy, outperforming either modality alone.

Conclusion: Video-derived signals contain clinically relevant respiratory features and enhance COBE detection, supporting non-contact video as a complementary modality to improve neonatal respiratory monitoring robustness.

Abstract: Pre-term infants are susceptible to potentially harmful apnoea-related cessations of breathing due to immature respiratory control. However, reliable respiratory monitoring in the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) remains challenging because motion artefacts, sensor displacement, and skin fragility can compromise contact-based measurements. Non-contact video monitoring offers a complementary approach that does not depend on adhesive sensors while providing additional respiratory information.
  We investigated whether camera-based signals can detect apnoea-related cessation of breathing (COBE) and provide complementary information to routinely acquired physiological signals. Using video and clinical recordings from 30 pre-term infants, respiratory motion was extracted from dynamically tracked torso regions to generate camera-derived time-series signals. Camera-only models were trained using residual network (ResNet) architectures, while hybrid models combined video-derived signals with impedance pneumography (IP), ECG-derived respiration (EDR), and the PPG-derived respiratory envelope.
  Camera-only models achieved a balanced accuracy of 76.9%, demonstrating the feasibility of non-contact COBE detection. Combining video-derived features with IP improved balanced accuracy to 90.6%, outperforming either modality alone and indicating that video provides respiratory information beyond standard physiological signals.
  These findings show that video-derived signals contain clinically relevant respiratory features and enhance COBE detection when combined with conventional physiological signals. This supports non-contact video as a complementary modality for automated COBE detection and highlights its potential to improve the robustness of neonatal respiratory monitoring.

</details>


### [758] [CanniUplift: A Holistic Framework for Mitigating Seller and Incentive Cannibalization in E-commerce Uplift Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05242)
*Zuwang He,Shihao Shu,Yuli Qu,Hanyu Gao,Ziliang Zhang,Diwei Chen,Xiangda Yan,Buyu Gao,Tanchao Zhu,Yumeng Li,Junxiong Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CanniUplift is a unified framework that addresses dual-source cannibalization effects (seller-level and incentive-level) in uplift modeling for e-commerce, using Platform-level Global Alignment (PGA), Redemption-based Decomposition Denoising (RDD), and a Treat-Attention mechanism to improve ITE estimation under SUTVA violations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional uplift models fail in multi-seller e-commerce environments due to violations of SUTVA, specifically seller-level cannibalization (incentives shift spending between sellers without platform growth) and incentive-level cannibalization (organic conversions and alternative rewards introduce noise into incrementality estimation).

Method: The authors propose CanniUplift with three components: (1) Platform-level Global Alignment (PGA) to capture cross-shop substitution via global GMV consistency constraints; (2) Redemption-based Decomposition Denoising (RDD) using redemption behavior to decompose treated outcomes and reduce attribution noise in an entire-space framework; (3) Treat-Attention mechanism to model interactions between user history and current treatment options.

Result: CanniUplift significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on both synthetic and industrial datasets. Ablation studies show PGA and RDD consistently improve wAUUC and wQINI. Online deployment achieved 4.08% relative increase in platform-wide incremental GMV and improved ROI over the production baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully mitigates dual-source cannibalization in uplift modeling for e-commerce, proving effective in driving genuine global platform growth rather than merely shifting expenditure between sellers or capturing organic conversions.

Abstract: Personalized incentive allocation is vital for e-commerce, where uplift modeling is the standard for estimating Individual Treatment Effects (ITE). However, traditional models often fail in complex multi-seller environments with violations of the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (SUTVA). We identify two critical challenges: Seller-level Cannibalization, where incentives shift expenditure between shops without growing the platform, and Incentive-level Cannibalization, where organic conversions or alternative rewards introduce significant noise into incrementality estimation. In this paper, we propose CanniUplift, a unified framework to mitigate these dual-source cannibalization effects. Specifically, we design Platform-level Global Alignment (PGA) to capture cross-shop substitution through global GMV consistency constraints. To tackle incentive-driven noise, we introduce Redemption-based Decomposition Denoising (RDD), which uses redemption behavior to decompose treated outcomes and reduce attribution noise within an entire-space framework. Furthermore, a Treat-Attention mechanism is designed to model intricate interactions between users' historical behaviors and current treatment options. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and large-scale industrial datasets demonstrate that CanniUplift significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Ablation studies confirm that the integration of PGA and RDD consistently improves wAUUC and wQINI. Successfully deployed online, our framework achieved a 4.08% relative increase in platform-wide incremental GMV (Delta GMV) over the production baseline and improved ROI in online A/B tests, proving effective in driving global platform growth.

</details>


### [759] [FUSE: FK-Steered Multi-Modal Flow Matching for Efficient Simulation-Based Posterior Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05252)
*Weichen Qin,Yufan Xie,Peihao Wang,Chia-Jui Chou,Minghui Du,Peng Xu,Ziren Luo,Yi Yang,Jingyi Yu,Bo Liang,Jiakai Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FUSE is a new Simulation-Based Inference method using a dual-track architecture and FK-steered sampling to improve multimodal posterior estimation, outperforming baselines on benchmarks and real-world exoplanet tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SBI methods struggle with multimodal modeling due to brute-force fusion strategies that ignore structural disparities between parameters and observations, limiting estimation fidelity.

Method: (1) A dual-track architecture that preserves distinct features of multimodal inputs while enabling dynamic interaction. (2) An FK-steered (Feynman-Kac) sampling strategy that leverages intermediate observation likelihoods to guide generative trajectories.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard SBI benchmarks, producing posteriors closely matching ground-truth MCMC. In real-world exoplanet orbital estimation, it resolves complex parameter degeneracies that challenge existing methods.

Conclusion: FUSE demonstrates potential to accelerate complex scientific discoveries in astrophysics and beyond by enabling efficient and accurate simulation-based posterior estimation.

Abstract: Simulation-Based Inference (SBI) is critical for scientific discovery, with generative models offering a promising path toward efficient inference. However, existing methods struggle with effective multimodal modeling. They often rely on brute-force fusion strategies that ignore the structural disparities between parameters and observations, thus limiting estimation fidelity. In this work, we introduce FUSE (Feynman-Kac steered mUlti-modal flow matching for efficient Simulation-based posterior Estimation). Unlike prior work, FUSE employs a dual-track architecture that preserves the distinct features of multimodal inputs while facilitating dynamic interaction. Additionally, we propose an FK-steered sampling strategy that leverages intermediate observation likelihoods to guide the generative trajectories, effectively improving the sample quality during inference. Our approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard SBI benchmarks, producing posteriors that closely match ground-truth MCMC. Furthermore, in a real-world exoplanet orbital estimation task, FUSE successfully resolves complex parameter degeneracies that challenge existing methods, highlighting its potential to accelerate complex scientific discoveries in astrophysics and beyond.

</details>


### [760] [GeoFlow: Geo-Aware Modeling of Inter-Area Relationships in Origin-Destination Flow Prediction and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05257)
*Zherui Huang,Guanjie Zheng,Hao Xue,Linghe Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GeoFlow is a framework for OD flow modeling that incorporates geospatial attributes, geometric-intrinsic fusion encoding, and axial-global attention decoding, paired with flow matching models for superior predictive accuracy and generative diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based OD flow models neglect salient geographic attributes, limiting their ability to capture long-range and multi-area dependencies in urban mobility.

Method: GeoFlow (i) augments area representations with geospatial attributes (relative positions, k-hop/geodesic distances), (ii) uses a geometric-intrinsic fusion encoder combining graph attention with coordinate-aware encoders, (iii) employs an axial-global attention decoder for OD competitive dependencies, and pairs with flow matching models for generation.

Result: GeoFlow achieves superior predictive accuracy and substantially improves generative fidelity and diversity over existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed geometric-aware design effectively models long-range and multi-area dependencies in OD flows, with each component contributing positively as confirmed by ablation studies.

Abstract: Origin-destination (OD) flow modeling underpins urban planning and mobility analysis, but prevailing graph-based methods often neglect salient geographic attributes, limiting their ability to model long-range and multi-area dependencies. In this paper, we introduce GeoFlow, a novel framework that (i) augments area representations with geospatial attributes, including relative positions, k-hop and geodesic distances, (ii) employs a specialized geometric-intrinsic fusion encoder design that combines graph attention for intrinsic area signals with coordinate-aware encoders for global structure, and (iii) adopts an axial-global attention decoder to capture OD-specific competitive dependencies. For OD flow generation, GeoFlow is paired with flow matching models to produce more authentic and diverse mobility samples. Empirically, GeoFlow achieves superior performance in predictive accuracy, while substantially improving generative fidelity and diversity. Ablation and analytical studies confirm the contribution of each component. Code is available at https://github.com/ZheruiHuang/GeoFlow.

</details>


### [761] [Target-Guided Selective Reweighting for Physics-Informed Neural Network Inverse Problems: A Transfer Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05271)
*Qian Hu,Bin Fan,Yao Xiao,Zhicheng Lin,Meixin Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TGSR-PINN addresses negative transfer in PINN inverse problems by selectively reweighting neurons based on target-evidence scoring, using first-order Taylor sensitivity and GMM-based weak-adaptation signals to improve parameter recovery while maintaining field accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs suffer from ill-posed optimization, loss competition, and parameter compensation in PDE inverse problems. Direct fine-tuning in transfer learning causes negative transfer when source-target domains differ in physical mechanisms, governing parameters, or noise levels, leading to incorrect target parameter recovery despite low field error.

Method: TGSR-PINN transfers only weights/biases from source PINN while independently initializing target parameters. After short adaptation, it computes neuron target scores via first-order Taylor sensitivity and pre-activation variance, converts low-scoring neuron evidence into weak-adaptation signals via GMM with rank fallback, and applies selective soft decay to input weight rows/biases of low-scoring neurons.

Result: TGSR-PINN improves target parameter recovery while maintaining field accuracy in high-Péclet 2D advection-diffusion and Allen-Cahn to Burgers cross-PDE-family transfer. A 5%-noise reaction-diffusion case provides supplementary evidence. Ablations show neuron scoring, weak-adaptation estimation, layer protection, and selective soft decay jointly contribute.

Conclusion: TGSR-PINN effectively mitigates negative transfer in PINN inverse problems through target-evidence-driven selective reweighting, enabling accurate parameter recovery across varying source-target mismatches without sacrificing field accuracy.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) encounter ill-posed optimization, loss competition, and parameter compensation in partial differential equation (PDE) inverse problems. Transfer learning can reuse representations from source tasks, but direct fine-tuning may introduce negative transfer when dominant physical mechanisms, governing parameters, or observation noise differ between source and target domains: the model achieves low field error yet recovers incorrect target physical parameters. To mitigate, we propose Target-Guided Selective Reweighting PINN (TGSR-PINN), a target-evidence-driven representation correction method for PINN inverse transfer learning. TGSR-PINN transfers only the weights and biases from the source PINN, while target physical parameters are independently initialized; after a short target-adaptation phase, the method computes neuron target scores using first-order Taylor sensitivity and pre-activation variance on fixed scoring batches, and converts evidence associated with low-scoring neurons into continuous weak-adaptation signals via a Gaussian mixture model (GMM) with rank fallback. TGSR-PINN then applies selective soft decay to input weight rows and biases of low-scoring neurons instead of hard pruning or random resetting. In experiments, TGSR-PINN improves target parameter recovery while maintaining comparable field accuracy in the high-Péclet 2D advection-diffusion task and in the Allen--Cahn to Burgers cross-PDE-family transfer task; a 5%-noise reaction--diffusion case provides supplementary evidence under milder source-target mismatch. Ablation studies suggest that neuron target scoring, weak-adaptation signal estimation, layer protection, and selective soft decay jointly contribute to the benefits.

</details>


### [762] [Adaptive Inference Batching using Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05272)
*Ruslan Sharifullin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL-based adaptive batching/routing for inference serving shows marginal gains (~1%) in single-GPU static settings but dramatic improvements (3.5x over Round-Robin, 48% over Shortest-Queue) in multi-GPU heterogeneous routing by learning workload-segregation that eliminates Head-of-Line blocking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference serving systems need to balance throughput and latency under bursty, heterogeneous workloads, but static batching policies require manual tuning and cannot adapt to shifting traffic.

Method: Formulated as MDP over queue state/request type/GPU availability; trained REINFORCE and PPO agents on discrete-event simulator validated against queuing theory and production traces (Azure Functions, BurstGPT); evaluated across Poisson, burst, real-world traces, and heterogeneous multi-GPU routing.

Result: Single-GPU: RL offers +0.1%~+1.0% over well-tuned static policy. Multi-GPU heterogeneous: RL achieves 3.5x (348%) over Round-Robin, 48% over Shortest-Queue, with 60% higher throughput, 25% lower latency under SLA constraints. Attention-augmented policy converges ~20% faster than MLP.

Conclusion: RL's advantage concentrates in combinatorial, multi-resource decisions rather than single-resource temporal scheduling, providing a practical boundary condition for where learned policies justify engineering cost in production inference infrastructure.

Abstract: Inference serving systems must balance throughput and latency under bursty, heterogeneous workloads, yet the industry standard remains static batching policies that require manual tuning and cannot adapt to shifting traffic. We investigate whether reinforcement learning (RL) can learn adaptive batching and routing policies that outperform these heuristics, training REINFORCE and PPO agents on a discrete-event simulator validated against queuing theory and production traces (Azure Functions, BurstGPT). We formulate the problem as an MDP over queue state, request type and GPU availability, evaluating across standard Poisson traffic, extreme bursts, real-world traces and heterogeneous multi-GPU routing.
  Our central finding is a clear boundary condition for RL's value in systems problems.
