In single-GPU settings, a well-tuned static batching policy is already near-optimal under Poisson-like arrivals and RL offers only marginal gains (+0.1% to +1.0%). In multi-GPU heterogeneous routing, however, where fast and slow requests compete for shared resources, the agent discovers a workload-segregation policy that eliminates Head-of-Line blocking, yielding a 3.5x (348%) improvement over Round-Robin and a 48% improvement over the strongest heuristic baseline (Shortest-Queue), with 60% higher throughput and 25% lower latency while respecting SLA constraints. The policy generalizes to unseen bursty and real-world traffic despite training only on synthetic Poisson arrivals and an attention-augmented policy network converges roughly 20% faster than an MLP baseline.
  These results suggest RL's advantage over engineered heuristics concentrates in combinatorial, multi-resource decisions rather than single-resource temporal scheduling, a practical distinction for deciding where learned policies justify their engineering cost in production inference infrastructure.

</details>


### [763] [Advances in Neural Controlled Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05280)
*Benjamin Walker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This thesis improves the training efficiency of Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs) for continuous-time series by introducing three methods—Log-NCDEs, Linear NCDEs, and Structured Linear NCDEs—that reduce training time by up to 1000x while maintaining state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NCDEs are expressive continuous-time models for time series, but their non-linear vector fields make them computationally expensive and inherently sequential, limiting scalability and practical applicability.

Method: 1) Log-NCDEs: Apply the Log-ODE method to efficiently approximate NCDE solutions during training. 2) Linear NCDEs: Replace non-linear vector fields with linear ones, enabling closed-form solutions and parallel-in-time computation. 3) Structured Linear NCDEs: Use structured linear vector fields for further efficiency gains.

Result: Reduces time per training step by up to three orders of magnitude while achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse time series benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed methods collectively make NCDEs scalable and practical for real-world continuous-time series without sacrificing expressivity or performance.

Abstract: Many real-world systems evolve continuously, yet most machine learning models interpret time series as discrete sequences. Continuous-time approaches instead treat time series as samples from an underlying input path, a formulation that naturally accommodates irregularly sampled or oversampled data. Among these, Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs) are a maximally expressive class of models that parametrise a vector field using a neural network and evolve their hidden state by solving a dynamical system driven by the input path. NCDEs typically use a non-linear vector field, so their expressive power and continuous-time flexibility come at the cost of a forward pass that is both computationally expensive and inherently sequential, limiting their scalability and practical applicability.
  This thesis advances the training and scalability of NCDEs through three complementary contributions. First, building on neural rough differential equations, Log-NCDEs apply the Log-ODE method to efficiently approximate an NCDE's solution during training, improving both computational speed and empirical performance. Second, Linear NCDEs replace the non-linear vector field with a linear one, enabling closed-form solutions and parallel-in-time computation without sacrificing theoretical expressivity. Third, Structured Linear NCDEs use structured linear vector fields to further enhance efficiency while maintaining theoretical expressiveness and empirical performance.
  Collectively, these methods reduce the time per training step for an NCDE by up to three orders of magnitude while achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse time series benchmarks.

</details>


### [764] [Air Quality Downscaling with Station-Guided Pseudo-Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05292)
*Guorun Wang,Simone Foti,Andreas D. Demou,Leonidas Kotoulas,Theodoros Christoudias,Alexandros Koliousis,Mihalis Nicolaou,Stefanos Zafeiriou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A station-guided framework using a multi-scale transformer network with Gaussian blending of sparse observations to super-resolve coarse CAMS atmospheric fields to ~1 km PM2.5 estimates across Europe, achieving both high-resolution downscaling and bias correction without temporal modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the spatial support mismatch between coarse atmospheric fields (pixel averages) and discrete, unaligned ground-truth PM2.5 observations from stations, enabling high-resolution local air quality estimation.

Method: Multi-scale transformer network jointly super-resolving (×40 to ~1 km) and bias-correcting CAMS rasters, using heterogeneous side information (human activity, land cover, elevation, satellite aerosols, wind fields) and a time-agnostic propagation strategy with spatial Gaussian blending of interpolated OpenAQ observations for dense supervision from sparse in-situ data.

Result: Qualitative and station-level evaluations across Europe show the model recovers fine-grained spatial structures and effectively mitigates localized CAMS biases.

Conclusion: The proposed station-guided framework successfully addresses the spatial support mismatch challenge and enables high-quality PM2.5 downscaling without relying on temporal sequence modeling.

Abstract: Super-resolving coarse atmospheric fields to local PM$_{2.5}$ variations is uniquely challenged by a mismatch in spatial support: while pixels represent regional averages, ground-truth observations are discrete, unaligned samples of a continuous spatial signal. To bridge this gap, we present a station-guided framework for high-resolution PM$_{2.5}$ downscaling over Europe. Taking coarse CAMS atmospheric composition fields alongside heterogeneous side information (i.e., human activity, land cover, elevation, satellite aerosol observations, and wind fields) our framework jointly super-resolves ($\times 40$, $\approx$ 1 km) and bias-corrects CAMS rasters, without relying on temporal sequence modelling. To address the challenge of densely supervising our multi-scale transformer network with sparse in-situ data, we introduce a time-agnostic propagation strategy that utilises spatial Gaussian blending of interpolated OpenAQ observations. Extensive qualitative and station-level evaluations across Europe demonstrate that our model recovers fine-grained spatial structures and effectively mitigates localised CAMS biases.

</details>


### [765] [Learning Only What Valid Adapters Can Express: Subspace-Constrained Adaptation Against Fine-Tuning Poisoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05300)
*Fabien Polly*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes constraining fine-tuning to a low-dimensional subspace estimated from trusted LoRA adapters, showing it preserves performance on clean tasks while blocking backdoor attacks (8% success vs 100% for LoRA) and detecting corrupted data via 120x loss separation, at the cost of reduced plasticity on tasks outside the pool's coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA remain vulnerable to poisoned objectives, as there is still a broad space of behavior-changing updates that can represent and optimize malicious goals.

Method: The method constrains adaptation to a low-dimensional subspace estimated from a trusted pool of existing task adapters (196 public LoRA adapters on flan-t5-large), rather than allowing full parameter-space optimization.

Result: (1) 30-38% of LoRA adapter weight norm is redundant; (2) 128-coordinate subspace matches full LoRA on clean tasks while LoRA collapses to 3-26% exact match vs 62-96% for constrained learner under label inversion; (3) 120x loss separation between clean and corrupted data; (4) 8% backdoor success (vs 100% LoRA) when target is unlike pool, 85% when target aligns with common pool behavior.

Conclusion: Subspace-constrained adaptation provides strong robustness against poisoning and backdoor attacks when the pool covers the target task well, but trades off plasticity—unconstrained fine-tuning performs better on tasks poorly covered by the pool, and the approach assumes the pool itself is trustworthy.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning still leaves a broad space of behavior-changing updates reachable, so a poisoned objective can be represented and optimized. We study an alternative: adaptation constrained to the subspace estimated from a trusted pool of existing task adapters. On flan-t5-large with 196 public LoRA adapters, we show that (1) the functionally relevant content of an adapter lies in a low-dimensional shared subspace, 30 to 38 percent of its weight norm being redundant under the evaluated task distributions; (2) gradient adaptation restricted to 128 coordinates on this subspace matches full LoRA fine-tuning on clean classification data, while under targeted label inversion LoRA collapses to 3-26 percent exact match and the constrained learner keeps 62-96 percent on the tasks the pool covers; (3) the constrained learner cannot fit corrupted data, its adaptation loss separating clean from garbage by two orders of magnitude (120x), an out-of-distribution signal without an extra detector; and (4) against an adaptive backdoor attacker who optimizes within the subspace, the attack is blocked (8 percent success versus 100 for LoRA) on the task where its target behavior is unlike anything in the pool, and only partially blocked (85 percent) when the target coincides with a common pool behavior. On these two tasks the outcome is consistent with how close the target is to the pool's directions, which suggests but does not establish a pool-relative boundary. The mechanism trades peak plasticity for these properties: on tasks the pool covers poorly, unconstrained fine-tuning wins, and the protection assumes the pool itself is trusted. Code and data are public.

</details>


### [766] [Biologically Informed Deep Neural Networks for Multi-Omic Integration, Pathway Activity Inference and Risk Stratification in Cancer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05306)
*Pedro Henrique da Costa Avelar,Le Ou-Yang,Min Wu,Sophia Tsoka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Pathway Activity Autoencoders for multi-omics integration, embedding pathway knowledge as architectural constraints to balance interpretability and representational power. Evaluated on breast cancer data, it shows multi-omics integration improves survival prediction and subtype classification, with gene, protein, and microRNA being most impactful.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrating multi-omics data is challenging due to a trade-off between model interpretability and representational capacity, with existing methods either relying on post-hoc interpretation or using linear models that miss complex interactions.

Method: Pathway Activity Autoencoders that embed prior biological knowledge (pathway information) as architectural constraints, enabling interpretability while preserving representational power for multi-omics integration.

Result: Multi-omics integration positively impacts survival prediction and subtype classification in breast cancer. Gene, protein, and microRNA expression layers contribute most. Dropout improves robustness but excessive regularization hurts performance.

Conclusion: The pathway activity autoencoder framework yields superior, biologically meaningful latent representations that are directly translatable to clinically relevant insights, with practical guidelines for multi-omics integration.

Abstract: Integrating complex, multi-omics data presents significant challenges. Existing approaches often face a trade-off between model interpretability and representational capacity, with most either relying on post-hoc interpretation or use linear models that may overlook complex interactions. We report Pathway Activity Autoencoders for the multi-omics setting, which embed prior knowledge via pathway-informed architectural constraints, fostering interpretability, while preserving representational power. Our multi-omic framework is applied in the context of breast cancer and is evaluated in survival prediction and subtype classification with results indicating a positive effect of integration. We conduct analysis of individual omics layer impact on end-task performance, revealing that gene, protein, and microRNA expression layers provide the strongest contribution. Repeatability studies indicate that, while dropout improves model robustness and consistency, excessive regularisation can reduce predictive performance. Finally, visualizations of the learned feature space illustrate the framework's intrinsic transparency and clinical relevance. The results underscore the value of multi-omic integration and delineate the impact of individual omics layers, establishing practical guidelines for integration within our framework. Overall, our pathway activity autoencoder frameworks yield superior latent representations that are biologically meaningful and are directly translatable into clinically relevant insights.

</details>


### [767] [How Far is Too Far? Defining the Distance Threshold for Verification Siamese Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05329)
*Heloísa Dias Viotto,Cauê Samonek,Lucas Garcia Pedroso,Marcos Sunye,André Abed Grégio,Paulo Lisboa de Almeida*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an unsupervised method for determining the verification threshold in Siamese networks by finding the minimum point between two modes of the distance distribution, achieving 94% accuracy without labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Setting the verification threshold in Siamese networks typically requires labeled data, which is costly and impractical for deployment environments where the threshold may need updating.

Method: The authors assume the distance distribution from a Siamese network is bimodal. They propose unsupervised threshold selection by identifying the minimum point between the two modes of this distribution.

Result: Evaluated on MNIST, CIFAR-10, LFW, and PKLot datasets, achieving average verification accuracy of 94%, comparable to the Equal Error Rate method.

Conclusion: The proposed unsupervised method eliminates the need for labeled data while maintaining competitive verification accuracy, enabling adaptive thresholding in deployment environments.

Abstract: Siamese verification networks are widely used to compare items such as faces, cars, or signatures. In these scenarios, the network is trained to learn an embedding space in which similar objects are mapped closer together, while dissimilar objects are mapped further apart. Two objects are considered to belong to the same class (e.g., the same person in two different images) when the distance between their embeddings falls below a predefined threshold. Defining this threshold, however, is a non-trivial task and typically requires labeled data. In this work, we assume that the distribution of distances produced by a siamese verification network can be approximated by a bimodal function. Based on this assumption, we propose an unsupervised method to determine the verification threshold by identifying the minimum point between the two modes. The proposed approach does not require annotated samples, enabling the verification threshold to be updated directly in the deployment environment without the cost of manual labeling. We evaluate our method on four datasets: MNIST, CIFAR-10, LFW, and PKLot. The results indicate that the proposed approach achieves an average verification accuracy of 94%, comparable to the Equal Error Rate method, while eliminating the need for labeled data.

</details>


### [768] [TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05339)
*Yuanda Xu,Zhengze Zhou,Kayhan Behdin,Jelena Markovic-Voronov,Hejian Sang,Xiaomin Li,Wenhui Zhu,Xinchen Du,Aida Rahmattalabi,Ran He,Sen Na,Zhipeng Wang,Alborz Geramifard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TREK is a staged training method that uses teacher distillation not for imitation but to expand the student's exploration support on hard prompts, outperforming standard GRPO on math reasoning and agentic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO stalls on hard prompts where correct solutions lie outside the student's current on-policy support, limiting exploration and learning on challenging examples.

Method: TREK (Teacher-Routed Exploration via Forward KL): (1) identify prompts with low student pass rates, (2) query a teacher (external/black-box/self-context) for verified solutions, (3) keep top-r proposals by student likelihood, (4) apply a short forward-KL phase to absorb those modes, (5) return to standard on-policy GRPO refinement.

Result: On math: TREK with DeepSeek-V4 improves Qwen3-8B on AIME 2025 from 36.9→40.3, AIME 2024 from 47.9→51.1. Self-context reaches 38.5/49.6. On agentic: ALFWorld 75.8→82.8, ScienceWorld 12.5→26.7, with faster hard-task learning vs. GRPO.

Conclusion: Forward-KL based teacher-routed exploration effectively expands student support on hard prompts, works with various teacher types (including self-context), and accelerates learning on both math reasoning and agentic tasks.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is effective when the current policy already samples useful reasoning trajectories, but it stalls on hard prompts whose correct solution modes lie outside the student's on-policy support. We propose TREK (Teacher-Routed Exploration via Forward KL), a simple staged procedure that uses distillation not for imitation but for exploration support expansion. A key advantage of TREK is its generality: because it only consumes verified output trajectories, it can use an external black-box teacher, a white-box teacher, or the same model given additional inference-time context, and it can efficiently identify which hard-prompt samples are most worth consolidating even when teacher internals are unavailable. TREK first identifies prompts where the unaided student has very low pass rate, queries a proposal source to produce verified candidate solutions, keeps the top-$r$ proposals ranked by current student likelihood, applies a short forward-KL phase to pull those verified modes into the student's support, and then returns to standard on-policy GRPO refinement. On mathematical reasoning, TREK with DeepSeek-V4 proposals improves Qwen3 models across all tested scales on AIME 2024 and AIME 2025; for Qwen3-8B, it improves AIME 2025 from 36.9 to 40.3 and AIME 2024 from 47.9 to 51.1 (avg@16), while the self-context variant reaches 38.5 and 49.6 without an external teacher. On agentic tasks, TREK raises ALFWorld success rate from 75.8 to 82.8 and ScienceWorld success rate from 12.5 to 26.7; notably, on the hardest task types, TREK achieves high success rates early in training while unaided GRPO requires substantially more optimization steps to reach comparable levels.

</details>


### [769] [CompactionRL: Reinforcement Learning with Context Compaction for Long-Horizon Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05378)
*Yujiang Li,Zhenyu Hou,Yi Jing,Jie Tang,Yuxiao Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CompactionRL is a reinforcement learning strategy that jointly optimizes task execution and summary generation to train long-horizon agentic LLMs with context compaction, achieving significant performance gains on coding benchmarks (e.g., 66.8% Pass@1 on SWE-bench Verified with GLM-4.5-Air).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon agentic LLMs are limited by finite context windows, as extended trajectories can exceed max context length. Context compaction offers a solution but remains underexplored in reinforcement learning.

Method: CompactionRL jointly optimizes task execution and summary generation using token-level loss normalization and cross-trajectory generalized advantage estimation, enabling LLM agents to learn from compacted long-horizon trajectories.

Result: GLM-4.5-Air achieves 66.8% Pass@1 on SWE-bench Verified (+7.0) and 24.5% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 (+3.1). GLM-4.7-Flash achieves 56.0% (+5.5) and 20.2% (+6.8) respectively. Deployed in GLM-5.2 training pipeline.

Conclusion: CompactionRL consistently improves long-horizon agentic LLM performance across open models on coding tasks, validating context compaction as an effective RL training strategy.

Abstract: Long-horizon agentic LLMs are increasingly limited by finite context windows, as extended interaction trajectories can exceed the maximum context length before a task is completed. Context compaction offers a natural solution by summarizing previous interaction states and continuing the rollout under a compressed context, but incorporating compaction into reinforcement learning remains underexplored. We propose CompactionRL, a reinforcement learning strategy to train long-horizon agentic LLMs with context compaction. Our approach jointly optimizes task execution and summary generation with token-level loss normalization and cross-trajectory generalized advantage estimation. This design enables the LLM agents to learn from compacted long-horizon trajectories. We train CompactionRL on top of open models and observe consistent performance gains on agentic coding tasks. CompactionRL enables the open GLM-4.5-Air model (106B-A30B) to achieve Pass@1 scores of 66.8% on SWE-bench Verified and 24.5% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, with absolute gains of 7.0 and 3.1 points, respectively. Built upon GLM-4.7-Flash (30B-A3B), CompactionRL improves Pass@1 by 5.5 and 6.8 points, reaching 56.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 20.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, respectively. CompactionRL is thus deployed in the RL pipeline for training the open GLM-5.2 model (750B-A40B).

</details>


### [770] [TabPack: Efficient Hyperparameter Ensembles for Tabular Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05380)
*Yury Gorishniy,Akim Kotelnikov,Ivan Rubachev,Artem Babenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPack is an efficient MLP ensemble method for tabular data that samples and trains many MLPs with different hyperparameters in parallel, selecting ensemble members on the fly, achieving strong out-of-the-box performance without extensive hyperparameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing efficient MLP ensembles require manual hyperparameter tuning for optimal performance, which is time-consuming and computationally expensive. The authors aim to reduce this reliance on traditional tuning.

Method: TabPack samples MLPs with different hyperparameters from specified ranges, trains them efficiently in parallel, and selects ensemble members dynamically during training, requiring only hyperparameter ranges rather than exact values.

Result: On medium-to-large public datasets, TabPack with default settings performs on par with extensively tuned prior methods. Default TabPack on a MacBook runs faster than tuning some baselines on an industry-grade GPU.

Conclusion: TabPack substantially reduces the effort and compute resources needed to achieve competitive results on tabular tasks, offering strong out-of-the-box performance.

Abstract: In deep learning for tabular data, efficient ensembles of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) have recently emerged as effective and practical architectures. Existing methods of this kind use the same hyperparameters for all underlying MLPs, which requires hyperparameter tuning for achieving the best performance. In this work, we introduce TabPack, an efficient MLP ensemble with strong out-of-the-box performance and reduced reliance on traditional tuning. In a single run, TabPack samples and trains many MLPs with different hyperparameters efficiently in parallel and selects ensemble members on the fly during training. Thus, TabPack only requires specifying ranges from which to sample MLP hyperparameter rather than exact hyperparameter values, which naturally demands less precision for good performance. In experiments on medium-to-large public datasets, TabPack with default settings performs on par with extensively tuned prior methods, thus substantially reducing effort and compute resources needed to achieve competitive results on tabular tasks. Notably, running the default TabPack configuration on a modern MacBook took less time than tuning some baselines on an industry-grade GPU.

</details>


### [771] [Weak-to-Strong Generalization via Direct On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05394)
*Shiyuan Feng,Huan-ang Gao,Haohan Chi,Hanlin Wu,Zhilong Zhang,Zheng Jiang,Bingxiang He,Wei-Ying Ma,Ya-Qin Zhang,Hao Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Direct-OPD transfers the policy shift induced by RL on a weaker model (comparing its post-RL vs pre-RL checkpoints) as a dense implicit reward to train a stronger student model on its own on-policy states, enabling cheap weak-to-strong RL reuse without explicit reward models or expensive target-model rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Running RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) on large language models is expensive because the target model must generate many rollouts during training. As models scale, post-training becomes a bottleneck. The authors seek a weak-to-strong alternative: run cheap RL on a smaller model, then transfer what was learned to improve a stronger target model without repeating expensive RL.

Method: Direct-OPD compares the post-RL teacher's policy with its own pre-RL reference checkpoint, computes the log-ratio of action probabilities as a dense implicit reward, and applies this signal onto the stronger student model's own on-policy states (states visited by the student during its own rollouts). This avoids training an explicit reward model or running sparse-reward RL on the target model.

Result: Direct-OPD boosts Qwen3-1.7B from 48.3% to 62.4% on AIME 2024 in just 4 hours on 8 A100 GPUs. It outperforms step-matched direct RL and enables sequential composition of multiple policy shifts. Consistently leverages weaker teachers to improve stronger target models.

Conclusion: RL outcomes can be reused across model scales as implicit reward signals (via policy shift), not merely as final models to imitate. Direct distillation of the post-RL teacher is insufficient because it mixes useful RL gains with the limitations of the smaller model; transferring the policy shift itself is more effective.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is a powerful recipe for improving language-model reasoning, but it is expensive to repeat on every new strong model because the target model must generate many rollouts during training. As models scale, post-training itself becomes a bottleneck. We study a weak-to-strong alternative: run RL on a smaller model where rollouts are cheaper, then reuse what that RL run learned to improve a stronger target model. Directly distilling the post-RL weak teacher is not enough, because the teacher's final policy mixes useful RL gains with the limitations of the smaller model. We propose Direct On-Policy Distillation (Direct-OPD), which transfers the teacher's RL-induced policy shift instead. Direct-OPD compares the post-RL teacher with its own pre-RL reference and treats their log-ratio as a dense implicit reward for the student. In plain terms, the checkpoint pair tells us which actions RL made the weak model more or less likely to take, and Direct-OPD applies that signal on the stronger student's own on-policy states. This directly reuses the weak model's RL supervision signal without training an explicit reward model or running sparse-reward RL on the target model. Empirically, Direct-OPD consistently leverages weaker teachers to improve stronger target models; notably, it boosts Qwen3-1.7B from 48.3% to 62.4% on AIME 2024 in just 4 hours on 8 A100 GPUs. It outperforms step-matched direct RL and enables the sequential composition of multiple policy shifts. Our results show that RL outcomes can be reused across model scales as implicit reward signals, not merely as final models to imitate.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [772] [Centralized PPO-Based DRL for Multi-UAV-BS Positioning and Trajectory Optimization in Disaster Response Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02533)
*Azim Akhtarshenas,Mario Rico Ibanez,Matteo Bernabe,David Lopez-Perez,Merouane Debbah*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a centralized deep reinforcement learning framework using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to coordinate multi-UAV base stations for 3D positioning and navigation in GPS-free emergency scenarios, optimizing fairness-aware sum-throughput under uncertain UE positions. PPO outperforms DDPG and DQN in convergence stability, mean reward, and network throughput.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for rapid deployment of communication infrastructure in GPS-free emergency/disaster scenarios by enabling multi-UAV-BS coordination that maximizes life-saving operations through adaptive wireless coverage.

Method: Formulates a fairness-aware sum-throughput maximization as an MDP, solved using a PPO-based DRL framework with a centralized agent that coordinates 3D positioning/navigation of multiple UAV-BSs while they serve ground UEs with uncertain positions.

Result: PPO shows competitive performance during training and evaluation; consistently outperforms DDPG and DQN in convergence stability, mean reward, and network throughput.

Conclusion: The centralized PPO-based approach effectively enables adaptive and resilient wireless coverage in emergency scenarios, outperforming state-of-the-art RL baselines.

Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle-mounted base stations (UAV-BSs) constitute a flexible and effective solution for global positioning system (GPS)-free emergency and disaster scenarios, where the rapid deployment of communication infrastructure is critical for maximizing life-saving operations. In this work, we extend a centralized learning framework to a multi-UAV-BS network architecture, in which a single centralized UAV-BS -- as an intelligent agent -- coordinates the three-dimensional positioning and navigation of multiple UAV-BSs, while the remaining UAV-BSs actively serve ground user equipments (UEs) with uncertain positions. We formulate a fairness-aware sum-throughput maximization problem for UAV-BS coordination, which is inherently nonconvex due to the non-linear and interference-coupled throughput expressions. To address this challenge, we cast the problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and solve it using a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The central agent interacts with the environment and learns optimal joint positioning policies that guide the serving UAV-BSs to provide efficient, adaptive, and resilient wireless coverage. The proposed approach exploits spatial configuration and radio signal sensing capabilities to dynamically adapt to heterogeneous UE mobility patterns. Extensive simulations are conducted to evaluate the performance of the proposed method. Numerical results demonstrate that PPO shows competitive performance during both training and evaluation phases. Furthermore, comparative analysis with state-of-the-art RL algorithms, namely Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) and Deep QNetwork (DQN), shows that PPO consistently outperforms these methods in terms of convergence stability, mean reward, and network throughput.

</details>


### [773] [DAS-AIS Association Patterns for Vessel Monitoring on an Operational Subsea Fibre Link](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02536)
*Tian Tian,Agastya Raj,Lara Flanagan,Nicolas Celli,Chris Bean,John Kennedy,Marco Ruffini*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A case study on an operational subsea cable shows that DAS vessel detection is affected by cable sensitivity and imperfect AIS reporting, revealing gaps between physical measurements and cooperative vessel data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the gap between physical DAS measurements at subsea cables and cooperative AIS vessel reporting, and to inform future DAS-assisted cable-protection workflows.

Method: Examined DAS vessel-related signatures jointly with concurrent AIS data on the Emerald Fibre Bridge Link, analyzing vessel-crossing events and identifying representative DAS-AIS association patterns.

Result: Identified multiple DAS-AIS association patterns: clear matches, offset, ambiguous, AIS-incomplete, AIS-silent-candidate, and non-vessel confounders.

Conclusion: There is a gap between physical measurements and cooperative vessel reporting, providing practical insights for designing future DAS-assisted cable-protection workflows.

Abstract: We present a case study on the Emerald Fibre Bridge Link, an operational subsea telecom cable connecting Dublin and North Wales, examining DAS vessel-related signatures jointly with concurrent AIS data. The observations show that vessel-related DAS responses depend on local cable sensitivity and background conditions, while their interpretation is complicated by imperfect AIS reporting. Examining vessel-crossing events jointly, we identify representative DAS-AIS association patterns, ranging from clear vessel matches to offset, ambiguous, AIS-incomplete, AIS-silent-candidate, and non-vessel confounders. These observations reveal the gap between physical measurements at the cable and cooperative vessel reporting, providing practical insights for designing future DAS-assisted cable-protection workflows.

</details>


### [774] [Physics-Informed Domain-Invariant Feature Learning with Autoencoder-Driven Gaussian Clustering for Robust Non-line-of-Sight Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02537)
*Nisha L. Raichur,Lucas Heublein,Dominik Seuß,Frank Deinzer,Felix Ott*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A hybrid physics-informed deep learning framework for robust AoA estimation, using a latent-space LoS/NLoS classifier and domain-incremental learning to improve accuracy under multipath conditions, achieving up to 6° error reduction in low-exemplar settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Jamming and spoofing disrupt RF signals (e.g., wireless, satellite navigation). Data-driven AoA estimation works under LoS but degrades in real-world NLoS multipath environments, motivating a more robust approach.

Method: Proposes a hybrid framework: (1) a DNN for azimuth/elevation estimation from a 4-element array; (2) a physics-informed loss enforcing plane-wave phase consistency for LoS samples; (3) a latent-space LoS/NLoS classifier; (4) domain-incremental learning across NLoS environments for better cross-domain generalization.

Result: On real-world datasets, the method reduces AoA estimation error by up to 6° in low-exemplar settings compared to DIL baselines.

Conclusion: Incorporating physics-informed constraints selectively on LoS samples, combined with domain-incremental learning, significantly improves AoA estimation robustness in challenging multipath environments.

Abstract: Jamming and spoofing pose significant threats to wireless and satellite navigation by disrupting radio-frequency (RF) signals and compromising availability and integrity. Robust RF interference direction finding through angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimation is therefore essential for detecting and localizing anomalous signals. Although data-driven methods perform well under line-of-sight (LoS) conditions, their performance degrades in practical environments due to non-line-of-sight (NLoS) multipath propagation. In this work, we propose a hybrid learning framework that incorporates physics-informed constraints into deep neural networks to improve the robustness of AoA estimation. A neural network is trained to estimate the azimuth and elevation of incoming signals received by a four-element antenna array, while a physics-informed loss enforces consistency between the predicted angles and inter-antenna phase differences under a plane-wave model. We further introduce a latent-space classifier to distinguish LoS from NLoS samples. Since inter-antenna phase differences under LoS propagation exhibit domain-invariant structure across environments, the physics-based loss is applied only to LoS samples, promoting physically consistent and domain-invariant representations without over-constraining the model in NLoS scenarios. In addition, domain-incremental learning (DIL) across NLoS environments with varying scatterer distributions improves cross-domain generalization. Evaluations on real-world datasets show that the proposed method reduces AoA estimation error by up to 6° in low-exemplar settings compared with DIL baselines.

</details>


### [775] [Transformer-based Multisensor Data Fusion of Ultrasonic Guided Wave and FBG-based Strain Measurements for Multitask Aerospace Structural Health Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02545)
*Xin Yang,Morteza Moradi,Tongtong Yan,Jinbo Du,Yunlai Liao,Dimitrios Zarouchas,Dimitrios Chronopoulos*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Transformer-based multisensor data fusion framework integrating PZT ultrasonic guided wave signals and FBG strain measurements for structural health monitoring of aircraft composites. The framework achieves transparent multitask learning for health indicator prediction and damage localization, demonstrating ~60% improvement over single-sensor approaches with MAE/RMSE below 0.1 for HI prediction and below 0.0465/0.1571 for damage localization under compression-compression fatigue loading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of heterogeneous sensing modalities operating at disparate sampling frequencies and acquisition intervals in multisensor data fusion for SHM, as each individual sensing technology has limitations and effective fusion is needed to capture a more complete picture of inhomogeneous materials like composites.

Method: A Transformer-based data fusion framework that integrates PZT (ultrasonic guided wave) and FBG (strain) sensor data with attention-mechanism visualization for transparent multitask learning; validated on aircraft composite structures under compression-compression fatigue cyclic loading.

Result: HI prediction: MAE and RMSE below 0.1 (~60% improvement over single-sensor and baseline deep learning models). Damage localization: MAE below 0.0465 and RMSE below 0.1571, outperforming single-source and state-of-the-art deep learning models.

Conclusion: The proposed Transformer-based data fusion framework significantly outperforms single-source models and state-of-the-art deep learning models in both HI prediction and damage localization accuracy for aircraft composite SHM.

Abstract: Structural health monitoring (SHM) has emerged as an essential tool for ensuring the integrity and reliability of critical engineering structures, particularly in aerospace applications. Since each sensing technology has its limitations, the fusion of different modalities enables capturing a more complete picture of inhomogeneous materials, like composites. However, effective multisensor data fusion in SHM is often hindered by heterogeneous sensing modalities that operate at disparate sampling frequencies and acquisition intervals. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Transformer-based data fusion framework that integrates multisensor data streams from piezoelectric transducer (PZT) capturing ultrasonic guided wave signals and fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensors for strain measurements. By incorporating an attention-mechanism visualization, the proposed framework enables transparent, multitask learning for both health indicator (HI) prediction and damage localization. The framework was experimentally validated using aircraft composite structures subjected to compression-compression fatigue cyclic loading. For HI prediction, the framework consistently achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE) below 0.1, representing a nearly 60% performance improvement over single-sensor approaches (PZT or FBG alone) and baseline deep learning models. For damage localization, the model demonstrated the highest accuracy, maintaining an MAE and RMSE below 0.0465 and 0.1571, respectively. These results demonstrate that the proposed Transformer-based data fusion framework significantly outperforms single-source models and state-of-the-art deep learning models in both HI prediction and damage localization accuracy.

</details>


### [776] [Priority Random Access and Power Control for NOMA-ALOHA in Heterogeneous mMTC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02547)
*Wenbo Fan,Pingzhi Fan,Zilong Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes PRA-NA (priority random access non-orthogonal multiple access assisted ALOHA) with two strategies (FPRA-NA and APRA-NA) and an enhanced user barring algorithm (EUBA) to improve throughput, access delay, and energy efficiency for heterogeneous massive machine-type communications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for access priority among machine-type devices with different delay requirements (delay-sensitive vs. delay-tolerant) in heterogeneous massive machine-type communications, while considering practical non-ideal channel conditions.

Method: The paper introduces a received power level model accounting for imperfect CSI and SIC, designs two PRA strategies (fixed FPRA-NA and adaptive APRA-NA), and proposes an enhanced user barring algorithm (EUBA) for power control to improve energy efficiency.

Result: The proposed PRA-NA schemes with EUBA significantly improve random access performance in terms of throughput, access delay, and energy efficiency compared to conventional NOMA-ALOHA, as demonstrated via extensive simulations.

Conclusion: The proposed FPRA-NA and APRA-NA strategies with EUBA effectively provide access priority, reduce average access delay for delay-sensitive MTDs, alleviate user overload, and improve energy efficiency under practical non-ideal channel conditions.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel priority random access (PRA) non-orthogonal multiple access assisted ALOHA, called PRA-NA, to provide access priority for machine-type devices (MTDs) with different delay requirements (i.e., delay-sensitive and delay-tolerant). We first introduce a received power level model that incorporates imperfect channel state information and imperfect successive interference cancellation to study the impact of practical non-ideal channel conditions. Two PRA strategies including fixed PRA-NA (FPRA-NA) and adaptive PRA-NA (APRA-NA) are then designed to reduce the average access delay of delay-sensitive MTDs in heterogeneous massive machine-type communications. Subsequently, the throughputs of both the FPRA-NA and APRA-NA strategies are analyzed to demonstrate their effectiveness. Moreover, to improve the energy efficiency of random access, we introduce an enhanced user barring algorithm (EUBA) to carry out power control. It is shown that our proposed EUBA can not only alleviate the user overload problem, but also reduce the average transmit power of MTDs. By extending it to the proposed PRA-NA schemes, we demonstrate via extensive simulation results that the random access performances in terms of throughput, access delay, and energy efficiency can be significantly improved over the conventional NOMA-ALOHA.

</details>


### [777] [Synthesis of passive multidimensional scattering matrices: a survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02557)
*Sankar Basu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper surveys multidimensional (n-D) network synthesis, showing that extending classical 1-D synthesis to higher dimensions faces fundamental obstacles rooted in Hilbert's 17th problem on representing positive polynomials as sums of squares. While 2-D lossless functions are synthesizable, n>2 cases generally fail except for special low-degree all-pass functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the classical 1-D network synthesis framework (synthesizing rational transfer functions as networks of inductors, capacitors, transformers, and gyrators) to the multidimensional (n-D) setting, and to understand the mathematical limits of such extensions.

Method: The paper surveys existing results on synthesizing rational multivariable passive linear shift-invariant bounded-real/positive-real transfer functions as scattering/immittance matrices of n-D networks, and examines the connection between n-D synthesis and Hilbert's 17th problem on sums-of-squares representations of positive polynomials.

Result: Every 2-D lossless transfer function can be synthesized. However, embedding a dissipative function into a lossless one via spectral factorization works only partially in 2-D and fails for n>2. For n>2, even lossless functions are generally not synthesizable except for certain low-degree stable all-pass functions. These limitations stem from Hilbert's 17th problem.

Conclusion: The fundamental obstacle to extending classical 1-D network synthesis to higher dimensions (n>2) is Hilbert's 17th problem. The paper provides a comprehensive account of the current state of n-D network synthesis, including failures of other classical 1-D techniques like matrix-factorization methods.

Abstract: The goal of this exposition is twofold. First, it surveys the current status of the synthesis of rational multivariable ($n$-dimensional) passive linear shift-invariant bounded-real (or positive-real) transfer functions as scattering (or immittance) matrices of networks composed of a finite number of $n$ types of inductive and capacitive elements together with memoryless reciprocal and nonreciprocal components, such as transformers and gyrators. The theory seeks to extend the classical $1$-D network synthesis framework to the multidimensional ($n$-D) setting. Second, the exposition examines the fundamental connection between $n$-D network synthesis and Hilbert's 17th problem concerning the representation of positive polynomials as sums of squares. This connection is shown to be the principal mathematical obstacle to extending many classical synthesis results to higher dimensions. Both lossless and dissipative transfer functions are considered. While every $2$-D lossless transfer function can be synthesized, the classical procedure of embedding a dissipative transfer function into a lossless one by solving an associated matrix dilation problem can be executed via spectral factorization only partially in $2$-D and fails in general for $n>2$. Moreover, for $n>2$, even lossless transfer functions are not, in general, synthesizable, except for certain low-degree stable all-pass functions. These limitations ultimately stem from Hilbert's 17th problem referred to above. The exposition also reviews failure of several other classical $1$-D synthesis techniques, including matrix-factorization methods, thereby providing a comprehensive account of the current state of $n$-D network synthesis. Depending on the context, both continuous and discrete time formulations are employed; for the class of linear shift-invariant systems considered here, this change of setting entails no loss of generality.

</details>


### [778] [Earth-baseline VLBI restores the observability of a lunar surface station in joint orbit-and-clock determination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02566)
*Chakshu Baweja*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proves that lunar surface station absolute positioning has a six-dimensional rigid-body datum defect (three translations, three rotations) even when internal satellite-to-station, inter-satellite ranging, and clock-sync measurements are used. Two ties to Earth frame exist: indirect (Earth-to-satellite ranging) and direct (VLBI). VLBI is shown to restore observability when constellations are sparse and improve bounds when rich. For a sparse three-satellite constellation, VLBI brings the Cramer-Rao bound from unobservable to 20.1 m; for a six-satellite constellation, it tightens from 23.2 m to 9.7 m.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lunar PNT is transitioning from concept to hardware (Moonlight/LCNS, LunaNet, Coordinated Lunar Time), but the fundamental geometric observability of a surface station's absolute position was not rigorously proven. This paper asks and answers which measurements make absolute position observable.

Method: Uses snapshot batch estimation with Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) and Cramer-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) analysis to study observability of lunar surface station positioning under different measurement configurations (internal ranging, indirect Earth tie, direct VLBI tie). Validates with NumPy and published closed forms.

Result: Internal observables leave a six-dimensional rigid-body datum defect. VLBI restores absolute observability when constellation is sparse (CRLB 20.1 m for 3 satellites) and improves it when rich (9.7 m vs 23.2 m for 6 satellites). Gauss-Newton estimator attains CRLB (efficiency 1.02) with 91x median station-error improvement in sparse regime.

Conclusion: The datum defect in lunar surface station positioning is purely positional (clocks fully observable). Two non-interchangeable Earth-frame ties exist: indirect (needs rich geometry) and direct VLBI (works regardless). A single-epoch baseline informs at most two axes, so three non-collinear Earth stations close the datum. This gives a conditional design law for lunar PNT infrastructure.

Abstract: Lunar positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT) is moving from concept to hardware, ESA's Moonlight/LCNS, NovaMoon reference stations, LunaNet, and Coordinated Lunar Time, all reducing to one estimation core: fix the orbits and clocks of the lunar infrastructure and tie them to an Earth/inertial frame. We ask which measurements make a surface station's absolute position observable, and prove the answer. In a snapshot batch fit, the internal observables (station-to-satellite and inter-satellite ranging plus clock-sync) constrain only relative geometry and leave a six-dimensional rigid-body datum defect: three translations and three rotations of the cluster. The clocks are fully observable, so the defect is purely positional, and closing it needs a tie to the Earth frame.
  Two such ties exist and are not interchangeable. An indirect tie (Earth-to-satellite ranging through the constellation) reaches the station only when the satellite geometry is rich; a direct tie (an Earth-baseline VLBI delay to the station beacon) fixes it regardless. This gives a conditional design law, not a single number: VLBI restores absolute observability when the constellation cannot supply it, and merely sharpens the bound when it can. For a sparse three-satellite constellation the station lies in the null space of the Fisher information until VLBI is added, reaching a Cramer-Rao bound of 20.1 m; for a rich six-satellite constellation VLBI tightens the bound from 23.2 m to 9.7 m. A single-epoch baseline informs at most two of three axes, so the datum closes at three non-collinear Earth stations. The Gauss-Newton estimator attains the bound (efficiency 1.02), with a 91x median station-error improvement in the sparse regime. The FIM/CRLB engine is validated against NumPy and published closed forms; the lunar application stays modelled, every figure deterministic and reproducible.

</details>


### [779] [CRODA-ST: Single-Target Cross-Receiver Open-Set Radio Fingerprint Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02567)
*Fengchong Yao,Jianbing Li,Qing Liu,Kefeng Song,Haitao Li,Song Wang,Feixiang Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: CRODA-ST is a cross-receiver open-set RFFI framework that uses Discriminative Structure Anchoring (DSA) and Rejection-Oriented Alignment (ROA) to mitigate receiver shift, achieving strong performance on the WiSig ManyTx dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current open-set RFFI methods suffer from receiver shift: thresholds calibrated on a source receiver cause false rejection of known devices on a target receiver, while closed-set alignment risks false acceptance of unknown devices.

Method: CRODA-ST has two components: (1) Discriminative Structure Anchoring (DSA) restores known-class references on the target receiver using limited labeled enrollment samples; (2) Rejection-Oriented Alignment (ROA) reduces receiver-sensitive confidence fluctuations around the anchored structure.

Result: On WiSig ManyTx dataset: 0.9092 known-class accuracy, 0.9692 AUROC, 0.9580 OSCR, and FPR90 reduced to 0.0469 via score-sweep analysis.

Conclusion: CRODA-ST effectively addresses cross-receiver open-set RFFI by structurally anchoring known classes and aligning rejection-oriented confidence, significantly outperforming naive threshold transfer.

Abstract: Radio frequency fingerprint identification (RFFI) provides a physical-layer credential for Internet of Things devices, but open-set decisions become fragile when a threshold calibrated on a source receiver is transferred to a target receiver. Receiver shift can lower the confidence of known transmitters and cause false rejection; closed-set alignment can have the opposite effect by pulling unseen target transmitters into known regions and increasing false acceptance. This letter presents CRODA-ST, a structure-first adaptation framework for singlesource single-target cross-receiver open-set RFFI. Its two components target the bottlenecks behind unreliable source-calibrated rejection: Discriminative Structure Anchoring (DSA) restores target-receiver known-class references from limited labeled target enrollment samples, and Rejection-Oriented Alignment (ROA) reduces receiver-sensitive confidence fluctuations around the anchored structure. On the WiSig ManyTx dataset, CRODA-ST reaches 0.9092 known-class accuracy, 0.9692 AUROC, and 0.9580 OSCR. Score-sweep analysis further reduces FPR90 to 0.0469.

</details>


### [780] [DOSE-I: A Multimodal Biosignal Dataset of Procedural Sedation for Endoscopy -- Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02570)
*Jakob Garbe,Jan W. Kantelhardt,Katja Seeliger,Thomas Schmid*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents the DOSE-I dataset, a multimodal biosignal dataset of procedural sedation for endoscopy, containing 78.5 hours of recordings from 281 procedures with detailed annotations of consciousness transitions and sedation depth labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a comprehensive, publicly available multimodal biosignal dataset with detailed annotations to support research on procedural sedation monitoring and analysis.

Method: The dataset includes 78.5 hours of recording from 281 endoscopic procedures with 1,129 consciousness transitions and 7,328 sedation depth labels, along with static subject data, metadata, artifact detection details, and preprocessed pEEG features.

Result: 171 records (6.7-70.8 minutes, mean: 27.5), 1,129 consciousness transitions (median: 6 per record), 7,328 sedation depth labels (median: 39 per record).

Conclusion: The DOSE-I dataset provides a valuable resource for future research on procedural sedation, with open access via Zenodo and preprocessing code available on Github.

Abstract: In this document, we describe characteristics and technical details of the multimodal biosignal dataset DOSE-I of procedural sedation for endoscopy published on zenodo. The DOSE-I dataset includes 78.5 hours of recording in 171 records ranging from 6.7 to 70.8 minutes (mean: 27.5, SD: 11.6) of 281 endoscopic procedures. 1129 (median: 6 per record) transitions of consciousness and 7328 (median: 39 per record) individual sedation depth labels were recorded. In addition to clinically annotated biosignals, the DOSE-I dataset provides detailed static data about the respective study subject and metadata about the respective recordings. To further support future research, we provide details about artifact detection and preprocessed pEEG features, too. C code used for this preprocessing is provided separately via Github.

</details>


### [781] [A Unified Multi-Modal Sensing and Active-Stabilization Framework for Autonomous IoT Nodes in Connectivity-Denied Environments: From Perimeter Sentinel to High-Value Cargo Protection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02600)
*Naahi Mumtaj Rihan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a generalized architecture unifying two seemingly unrelated embedded systems (perimeter/wildlife security turret and cargo protection crate), extends it with LiDAR, LoRaWAN, and satellite communication, and introduces a Composite Risk Index (CRI) integrating sensor data with GIS for connectivity-denied environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous embedded nodes in connectivity-denied environments (forests, farms, cargo in transit) are typically built as separate, purpose-specific systems despite sharing common requirements like PIR wake-up, IMU stabilization, and long-range low-power communication. The paper argues these are parametrizations of a single architecture.

Method: The authors formalize a generalized node architecture, extend it with three new modalities (single-point LiDAR ranging, LoRa/LoRaWAN mesh, satellite SBD), and couple telemetry to GIS via a Composite Risk Index (CRI) that fuses breach events, impact severity, and connectivity state. They present sensor-fusion, PID stabilization, link-budget, RSSI localization, and CRI formulations.

Result: The paper provides simulated closed-loop stabilization response and tiered-communication trade-off analysis, and instantiates two original projects (perimeter/wildlife security turret and cargo protection crate) as case studies of the generalized architecture.

Conclusion: The framework is offered as an engineering blueprint and starting point for field validation, not as a report of field-tested results. It aims to unify design efforts across diverse deployment scenarios.

Abstract: Autonomous embedded nodes deployed in connectivity-denied environments - remote forest perimeters, farm boundaries, and cargo in transit across highways or open ocean - share a common set of engineering requirements that are usually addressed by separate, purpose-built systems: passive-infrared (PIR) triggered wake-up, inertial-measurement-unit (IMU) driven active stabilization, and long-range communication under strict power budgets. This paper argues that a perimeter/wildlife security turret and a high-value cargo protection crate are, from a systems perspective, two parametrizations of the same generalized node architecture rather than two unrelated designs. We formalize this generalized architecture, extend it with three sensing/communication modalities not present in either original design - single-point LiDAR ranging, a LoRa/LoRaWAN regional mesh tier, and a satellite short-burst-data (SBD) global tier - and couple the resulting telemetry to a Geographic Information System (GIS) layer via a proposed Composite Risk Index (CRI) that fuses breach events, impact severity, and connectivity state into a single spatially-referenced score. We present the sensor-fusion, proportional-integral-derivative (PID) stabilization, link-budget, received-signal-strength-indicator (RSSI) localization, and CRI formulations that unify the two deployment modes, provide a simulated closed-loop stabilization response and a tiered-communication trade-off analysis, and instantiate the two original projects as case studies of the generalized architecture. The framework is intended as an engineering blueprint and a starting point for field validation rather than as a report of field-tested results.

</details>


### [782] [Metasurface embodied intelligence through electromagnetic world model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02634)
*Che Liu,Zhenhao Fu,Qian Ma,Jiajing Wu,Wen Ming Yu,Lianlin Li,Tie Jun Cui*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes metaEI-WM, a universal paradigm for metasurface-embodied intelligence that achieves expert-level electromagnetic environment control without on-site fine-tuning, by integrating automated semantic environment modeling with embedded electrodynamic priors to enable zero-latency signal enhancement, symbiotic communications, and contactless sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing information metasurfaces (IMSs) are limited by environmental 'blindness' and require site-specific, trial-and-error retraining, preventing real-world autonomous operation. The goal is to achieve expert-level performance without on-site fine-tuning or human-annotated data.

Method: MetaEI-WM integrates fully automated semantic environment modeling with embedded electrodynamic priors to establish a fundamental understanding of EM dynamics. It uses a world model to anticipate future scenarios in silico and optimizes IMS coding configurations to dynamically shape EM environments.

Result: The paradigm successfully enables zero-latency non-line-of-sight signal enhancements, symbiotic communications, and contactless physiological sensing across highly complex and unseen indoor scenarios, without requiring human-annotated data or online training.

Conclusion: MetaEI-WM is the first paradigm to achieve end-to-end automation of complex spatial channel manipulation tasks ab initio, bridging the gap between digital intelligence and physical-layer wave dynamics, offering a scalable solution for robust and self-managing wireless ecosystems.

Abstract: Mastering invisible electromagnetic (EM) environment and sculpting radio waves with the dexterity of manipulating light or matter have long been aspirations in physics and information science. While information metasurfaces (IMSs) provide the physical interface to program EM wavefields, their real-world autonomy is fundamentally limited by environmental 'blindness' and the prohibitive overhead of site-specific and trial-and-error retraining. Here we propose metasurface embodied intelligence through world model (metaEI-WM), a universal and out-of-the-box paradigm that achieves expert-level performance without on-site fine-tuning. In contrast to purely data-driven agents, metaEI-WM establishes a fundamental understanding of the EM dynamics by integrating fully automated semantic environment modelling with embedded electrodynamic priors. By anticipating future scenarios in silico, it optimizes the IMS coding configurations to dynamically shape EM environments on demand. We show that metaEI-WM successfully enables zero-latency non-line-of-sight signal enhancements, symbiotic communications, and contactless physiological sensing across highly complex and unseen indoor scenarios. To the best of our knowledge, metaEI-WM is the first paradigm to achieve end-to-end automation of complex spatial channel manipulation tasks ab initio, requiring neither human-annotated data nor online training. This framework bridges the gap between digital intelligence and physical-layer wave dynamics, offering a scalable solution for robust and self-managing wireless ecosystems.

</details>


### [783] [2D Spatial Indexing for Generalized and Quadrature Spatial Modulation in PASS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02788)
*Abanoub Kamel,Ibrahim Al-Nahhal*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes three novel spatial modulation schemes for pinching-antenna systems (1D-QSM, 2D-QSM, and 2D-GSM) that offer flexible trade-offs between BER performance and computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design spatial modulation schemes tailored for pinching-antenna systems that can flexibly balance BER performance and decoder computational complexity, addressing limitations in existing literature.

Method: Proposes 1D-QSM (dual waveguides for independent transmission of orthogonal symbol components), 2D-QSM, and 2D-GSM (jointly using waveguide and pinch indices to enhance spectral efficiency), with analytical upper bounds derived for BER and complexity.

Result: 1D/2D-QSM achieve lower complexity and better BER at low spectral efficiencies; 2D-GSM provides better reliability at high spectral efficiencies. Analytical bounds are validated through simulations.

Conclusion: The proposed schemes offer flexible performance-complexity trade-offs for pinching-antenna systems, with different schemes suitable for different spectral efficiency regimes.

Abstract: In this letter, three novel spatial modulation (SM) schemes specifically designed for pinching-antenna systems (PASS) are proposed, offering a flexible trade-off between bit error rate (BER) performance and decoder computational complexity. The proposed one-dimensional quadrature SM (1D-QSM) scheme exploits dual waveguides to independently transmit the orthogonal symbol components to eliminate mutual interference. Meanwhile, the 2D-QSM and 2D-generalized SM (2D-GSM) schemes jointly utilize waveguide and pinch indices to enhance spectral efficiency. Results demonstrate that 1D/2D-QSM schemes achieve significantly lower computational complexity and superior BER performance at low spectral efficiencies compared to existing literature and the proposed 2D-GSM. In contrast, 2D-GSM provides enhanced reliability at high spectral efficiencies, outperforming both existing literature and the proposed 1D/2D-QSM schemes. Finally, analytical upper bounds for BER and decoder computational complexity are rigorously derived and validated through simulations.

</details>


### [784] [Asymptotically Optimal Local Receiver in Uplink CF-mMIMO: A Functional-Variational Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02917)
*Jiafei Fu,Peng Jiang,Dongming Wang,Pengcheng Zhu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a quasi-LMMSE (Q-LMMSE) receiver for cell-free massive MIMO systems that eliminates the need for LSFD coefficients at the CPU, achieving ~5% ergodic rate gain with lower complexity by using an expectation-based constraint and random matrix theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional LMMSE receivers in CF-mMIMO require LSFD coefficients at the CPU, which introduce additional fronthaul overhead and computational complexity. The paper aims to design a local receiver that directly optimizes the true ergodic rate without these costly processing steps.

Method: The authors adopt a functional-variational approach with an expectation-based constraint, leveraging large-system random matrix theory to derive the asymptotically optimal Q-LMMSE receiver in closed form.

Result: The Q-LMMSE receiver shares the same direction as the conventional LMMSE receiver but differs by an instantaneous CSI-dependent scalar. It eliminates the need for LSFD coefficients and achieves roughly 5% ergodic rate gain when the number of antennas per AP is low, with strictly lower system-level complexity.

Conclusion: The proposed Q-LMMSE receiver is a simpler yet more effective alternative to the LMMSE-LSFD benchmark, providing better performance with reduced computational overhead in CF-mMIMO uplink systems.

Abstract: In cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO) systems, the canonical uplink local receiver is the local minimum mean square error (LMMSE) receiver with large-scale fading decoding (LSFD) at the central processing unit (CPU). The LSFD coefficients are derived under the use-and-then-forget (UatF) lower bound of the ergodic rate, and computing these coefficients introduces additional fronthaul overhead and computational complexity at the CPU. This paper investigates local receiver design directly from the true ergodic-rate objective under perfect local channel state information (CSI). By introducing an expectation-based constraint and leveraging large-system random matrix theory, we develop a functional-variational approach that yields the asymptotically optimal quasi-LMMSE (Q-LMMSE) receiver in closed form. A key insight is that the Q-LMMSE receiver shares the same direction as the conventional LMMSE receiver, differing only by an instantaneous CSI-dependent scalar, and thus incurs the same per-access point (AP) complexity. More importantly, this scalar varies across APs and implicitly provides adaptive weighting for the direct summation at the CPU, thereby completely eliminating the need for statistical LSFD coefficients and the associated CPU-side computational overhead. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed Q-LMMSE receiver consistently outperforms the LMMSE-LSFD benchmark in terms of the ergodic rate, achieving approximately a {5\%} gain when the number of antennas per AP is low, while operating with strictly lower system-level complexity.

</details>


### [785] [Physics-Informed Direction-of-Arrival Estimation Over Distributed Edge Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02971)
*Nathan Tatsuta,Rajeev Sahay*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a physics-informed federated learning framework for DoA estimation that incorporates steering-vector geometry via a manifold-aware regularizer, achieving better performance than standard FL baselines under both iid and non-iid conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard FL algorithms treat DoA estimation as a generic classification problem, ignoring the underlying physics of the array manifold, which limits performance on distributed edge devices under privacy and communication constraints.

Method: A physics-informed FL framework that incorporates steering-vector geometry via a manifold-aware regularizer, penalizing discrepancies in steering space rather than label space.

Result: Outperforms multiple FL baseline approaches across both iid and non-iid data conditions, with theoretical convergence guarantees to a stationary point.

Conclusion: Incorporating array manifold physics into federated learning improves DoA estimation accuracy, offering a principled approach for privacy-preserving distributed array processing.

Abstract: Direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation is a fundamental array processing task that has benefited substantially from deep learning. Deploying such methods across distributed edge devices introduces privacy and communication constraints that federated learning (FL) can address. Yet, standard FL algorithms treat DoA as a generic classification problem, ignoring the underlying physics of the array manifold. To address this, we propose a physics-informed FL framework for DoA estimation that incorporates steering-vector geometry directly into the local training objective via a manifold-aware regularizer. Unlike existing FL baselines, the regularizer in our framework penalizes discrepancies in steering space rather than label space, exploiting the known geometric structure of the array manifold. We provide theoretical convergence guarantees for our framework, showing convergence to a stationary point. Simulation results confirm that our physics informed approach outperforms multiple FL baseline approaches across iid and non-iid data conditions.

</details>


### [786] [Efficient Upper Mid-Band Spectrum Sensing with Multiple Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02982)
*Yongjun Kim,Ali Rasteh,Sundeep Rangan,Junil Choi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method for frequency-domain occupancy estimation in the upper mid-band using a generalized likelihood ratio test and an efficient search procedure combining binary search with dynamic programming, validated through simulations and over-the-air SDR experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address spectrum sensing in the upper mid-band where the number, bandwidths, and SNR of primary user signals are unknown, enabling reliable spectrum sharing with incumbent systems.

Method: A generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT) combined with a computationally efficient search procedure that uses binary search and dynamic programming to select interval sets maximizing sum of log-likelihoods.

Result: The proposed method is validated through both simulation data and over-the-air experimental data using an upper mid-band SDR testbed.

Conclusion: The proposed method demonstrates practical applicability for spectrum occupancy estimation in the upper mid-band under unknown signal conditions.

Abstract: Spectrum sensing is a fundamental problem in the upper mid-band, where spectrum resources are shared with incumbent systems. This paper considers frequency-domain occupancy estimation when the number of primary user signals, their bandwidths, and their signal-to-noise ratios are all unknown. We develop a generalized likelihood ratio test and a computationally efficient search procedure that combines binary search with dynamic programming to select the set of intervals maximizing the sum of log-likelihoods. The proposed method is validated through both simulation data and over-the-air experimental data using an upper mid-band software-defined radio (SDR), demonstrating its practical applicability.

</details>


### [787] [Severity Classification of Rotor Inter-Turn Short Circuits Using Eddy-Current Vibration Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03051)
*Ondrej Rozhon,Serge Pacome Bosson,Stepan Janous,Jakub Sevcik,Zdenek Peroutka*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a vibration-based diagnostic framework using eddy-current displacement sensors and XGBoost to classify rotor inter-turn short-circuit severity in synchronous generators, achieving 90.56% accuracy with strong performance even for incipient (mild) faults.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing studies focus on binary classification and severe fault conditions; incipient rotor ITSC severity assessment using displacement-sensitive vibration measurements remains underexplored.

Method: Eddy-current displacement sensor + 18-dimensional hybrid feature set + XGBoost classifier with leave-one-out cross-validation.

Result: 90.56% overall accuracy; 99% recall for healthy operation; 87% recall for mild fault conditions.

Conclusion: Displacement-sensitive vibration analysis enables effective severity-aware diagnosis of rotor ITSC with minimal sensor requirements.

Abstract: Rotor inter-turn short-circuit (ITSC) faults in synchronous generators introduce electromagnetic asymmetries that can lead to torque ripple, unbalanced magnetic pull, and progressive mechanical degradation. While most existing studies focus on binary classification and severe fault conditions, the assessment of incipient rotor ITSC severity using displacement-sensitive vibration measurements remains relatively underexplored. This paper proposes a vibration-based diagnostic framework for multi-class severity classification of rotor ITSC by integrating an eddy-current displacement sensor with physically motivated feature extraction. An 18-dimensional hybrid feature set is designed to characterize electromechanical modulations induced by rotor electromagnetic asymmetry. Using an XGBoost classifier with leave-one-out cross-validation, the proposed approach achieved 90.56% overall accuracy, including 99% recall for healthy operation and 87% recall for mild fault conditions. The results suggest that displacement-sensitive vibration analysis enables effective severity-aware diagnosis of rotor ITSC with minimal sensor requirements.

</details>


### [788] [Edge-Assisted Multimodal UAV Localization with Resource-Efficient Compression and Robust Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03111)
*Zhong Ye,Yinghui He,Guanding Yu,Pavel Loskot*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a multimodal UAV localization framework using camera, LiDAR, and radar sensors distributed across resource-constrained nodes, featuring IB-based compression, time-alignment encoding, robust fusion for degraded/missing data, and Mamba-based regression for accurate UAV location prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Design a robust localization scheme for non-cooperative UAVs that do not share movement information, addressing challenges of limited computing/communication resources, heterogeneous multimodal data (different sampling rates, time alignment, encodings), and environmental/hardware failures causing degraded or missing data.

Method: A distributed multimodal framework with: (1) IB-based compression extracting relevant features per modality, (2) time-encoding alignment for unified latent space representation, (3) multimodal fusion handling degraded/missing data, and (4) Mamba-based regression for UAV location prediction.

Result: Experiments on real-world dataset show the framework accurately and reliably obtains UAV location, outperforming existing frameworks.

Conclusion: The proposed distributed multimodal framework effectively addresses resource constraints, modality heterogeneity, and data degradation issues for robust non-cooperative UAV localization.

Abstract: The unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) will play an important role in the future urban transportation systems. This requires designing robust localization schemes especially for non-cooperative UAVs that do not share any information about their movements. This paper designs a multimodal UAV localization framework which utilizes camera, LiDAR and radar sensing modalities. The underlying data processing and the subsequent inference of the UAV location are distributed among the sensing nodes and the edge server attached to the base station. The proposed UAV localization framework addresses three key challenges. First, the sensing nodes have limited computing and communication resources, and they contain only single modality sensors. Second, the multimodal data differ greatly in the sampling rates, time alignment and the encodings. Third, the changes in the environment and the hardware failures cause the modal data to degrade, or to be completely missing. The proposed localization framework utilizes several data processing modules including a information-bottleneck (IB)-based compression module that extracts the most relevant features from each modality, a time-encoding alignment module that provides the unified representation in a shared latent space, a multimodal fusion module that accounts for the degraded and missing data, and a Mamba-based regression module that predicts the present UAV location. The experiments involving a real-world dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework accurately and reliably obtains the UAV location while outperforming other existing frameworks.

</details>


### [789] [Sensing-Aided Channel Estimation for Near-Field MIMO ISAC Systems via Cross-Attention Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03224)
*Peihao Dong,Renbin Li,Shen Gao,Shuangshuang Li,Fuhui Zhou,Wei Xu,Qihui Wu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes CAT-CENet, a Cross-Attention Transformer network for channel estimation in near-field ISAC systems, which fuses communication and sensing modalities by using sensing information as Query and pilot data as Key/Value in a cross-attention mechanism, outperforming SOTA methods especially with high target overlap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
