Motivation: Near-field ISAC systems face challenges in fusing heterogeneous sensing and communication modalities for improved channel estimation, despite sensing priors being valuable due to partial overlap between communication scatterers and radar targets.

Method: A Cross-Attention Transformer based Channel Estimation Neural Network (CAT-CENet) with: (1) communication pilot branch generating Key and Value features, (2) sensing information branch generating Query features, and (3) a three-module structure enabling automatic focus on overlapped target features without pre-identification. Theoretical analysis uses Shapley value to verify cross-attention gain.

Result: CAT-CENet outperforms state-of-the-art schemes, shows greater advantage with higher overlapping proportion between communication and sensing targets, and remains robust to model pruning.

Conclusion: Cross-attention effectively fuses heterogeneous sensing and communication modalities for ISAC channel estimation, with theoretical Shapley value analysis confirming the improvement mechanism.

Abstract: Near-field integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) can deliver the high spatial resolution and transmission capability with the shared spectrum and hardware. Due to the partial overlap between communication scatterers and radar targets, the sensing information can provide valuable priors to enhance the channel estimation while fusing the two heterogeneous modalities remain challenging. To address this problem, a Cross-Attention Transformer based Channel Estimation Neural Network (CAT-CENet) is developed, which includes a communication pilot branch generating the the Key and Value features and a sensing information branch generating the Query feature. By elaborating the three-module structure, CAT-CENet can focus on features of overlapped targets automatically without need of identifying them in advance. The modality contribution is theoretically analyzed based on the Shapley value to verify the cross-attention gain achieved by CAT-CENet. Simulation results show that CAT-CENet outperforms the state-of-the-art schemes, especially with the higher overlapping proportion, and is robust to the model pruning.

</details>


### [790] [Bridging the Semantic Gap in 6G: Tiny Language Models Under the Latency-Accuracy-Size Trilemma](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03246)
*Arnav Mathur,Garima Mathur,Rahul Jashvantbhai Pandya*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This review examines how tiny language models (t-LMs) can enable semantic communication for 6G networks, showing that compression reduces encoder size by up to 99.98% and split computing achieves device-side encoders with as few as 640 parameters. It provides a unified treatment of theoretical foundations, architecture taxonomy, compression techniques, and resource allocation, while identifying seven open challenges for standardization toward IMT-2030.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The shift from bit-transmission to meaning-transmission in 6G AI-native networks creates a fundamental gap: deep learning semantic encoders rely on large transformer models (hundreds of millions of parameters) that conflict with the stringent latency, energy, and memory constraints of IoT and edge devices that will dominate 6G endpoints.

Method: The paper conducts a comprehensive review organized along four axes: (i) theoretical foundations of semantic information (entropy, channel capacity, rate-distortion), (ii) a two-axis taxonomy of t-LM-based semantic communication systems across five architecture classes and six compression paradigms, (iii) a survey of six model compression techniques (quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, low-rank adaptation, split computing, neural architecture search) evaluated through semantic quality preservation, and (iv) semantic-aware resource allocation frameworks for 6G multi-user networks.

Result: Compression can reduce semantic encoder size by up to 99.98% while preserving task accuracy; split computing achieves device-side encoders with as few as 640 parameters; knowledge graph integration cuts transmission energy by 65%.

Conclusion: Tiny language models are an enabling technology for closing the gap between large semantic encoder requirements and edge device constraints in 6G. Seven open challenges are identified: theoretical gaps, system design, knowledge-base management, post-quantum security, and hardware co-design, with a proposed 3GPP standardization roadmap toward IMT-2030.

Abstract: Sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks are expected to serve as AI-native infrastructure, transmitting meaning rather than mere bits -- a shift that makes semantic communication the central paradigm for next-generation connectivity. Deep learning-based semantic encoders show compelling gains in bandwidth efficiency; however, their dependence on large transformer models with hundreds of millions of parameters is at odds with the sub-millisecond latency, microjoule energy budgets, and kilobyte memory footprints of the constrained IoT and edge devices that will dominate 6G endpoints. Tiny language models (t-LMs) -- compact, quantised, task-specialised models deployable on microcontrollers, mobile system-on-chips, and edge accelerators -- are the enabling technology for closing this gap. This review provides a unified treatment of (i) the theoretical foundations of semantic information, covering semantic entropy, channel capacity, and rate-distortion theory; (ii) a two-axis taxonomy of t-LM-based semantic communication systems across five architecture classes and six compression paradigms; (iii) a survey of model compression techniques -- quantisation, pruning, knowledge distillation, low-rank adaptation, split computing, and neural architecture search -- through the lens of semantic quality preservation; and (iv) semantic-aware resource allocation frameworks for 6G multi-user networks. Evidence across the surveyed literature shows that compression can reduce semantic encoder size by up to 99.98% while preserving task accuracy, that split computing achieves device-side encoders with as few as 640 parameters, and that knowledge graph integration cuts transmission energy by 65%. Seven open challenges are identified, spanning theoretical gaps, system design, knowledge-base management, post-quantum security, and hardware co-design, with a 3GPP standardisation roadmap toward IMT-2030.

</details>


### [791] [The S-ICDF Dataset: Sionna-Simulated Dynamic Interference Characterization and Direction Finding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03411)
*Christian Wielenberg,Lucas Heublein,Jonathan Ott,Alexander Mattick,Nisha L. Raichur,Jonas Pirkl,Lukas Schelenz,Tobias Feigl,George Yammine,Christopher Mutschler,Felix Ott*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents S-ICDF, a large-scale simulated indoor interference dataset (created with Sionna) for wireless and satellite navigation jamming/spoofing detection, covering 102 interference configurations and benchmarked with both classical and ML methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world collection of jamming/spoofing data is difficult because intentional interference is illegal, and ground-truth attribution is confounded by propagation, hardware, and environmental effects. Large-scale datasets are needed for developing and validating ML-based interference monitoring systems.

Method: S-ICDF dataset was generated using Sionna, a GPU-accelerated simulation library for physical-layer wireless communications, covering 102 interference configurations with diverse antenna patterns, bandwidths, noise levels, and reflection depths. Baseline benchmarking was performed using classical methods (MUSIC, ESPRIT, CAPON) and modern ML approaches.

Result: A large-scale indoor interference dataset (S-ICDF) was created and published, covering 102 interference configurations. Baseline results comparing classical direction finding methods and ML approaches are provided.

Conclusion: The S-ICDF dataset addresses the critical gap in available interference data for developing and validating ML-based interference monitoring (detection, classification, characterization, and direction finding) for wireless and satellite navigation systems.

Abstract: Jamming and spoofing threaten wireless and satellite navigation by disrupting or manipulating radio frequency (RF) signals, undermining availability, integrity, and trust. Robust interference monitoring (i.e., detection, classification, characterization, and direction finding) is therefore essential to identify and localize anomalous signals. While machine learning (ML) promises improved performance in complex environments, its development and validation depend on large-scale datasets that capture realistic signal and channel variability. Collecting such data in the real world is difficult because intentional jamming is illegal and ground-truth attribution is confounded by propagation, hardware, and environmental effects. To address this gap, we create and publish S-ICDF, a large-scale indoor interference dataset generated with Sionna, a GPU-accelerated simulation library for physical-layer wireless communications. S-ICDF covers 102 interference configurations, including diverse antenna array patterns, bandwidths, and simulation settings such as noise level and reflection depth. We further provide baseline results by benchmarking S-ICDF with classical estimation and direction finding (DF) methods (MUSIC, ESPRIT, and CAPON) and with modern ML approaches. The dataset is publicly available at: https://gitlab.cc-asp.fraunhofer.de/darcy_gnss/sicdf_dataset

</details>


### [792] [ISTA-Based Joint Dictionary Learning and Channel Estimation for XL-MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03448)
*Arttu Arjas,Italo Atzeni*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes DL-ISTA, a dictionary-learning iterative soft-thresholding algorithm for near-field channel estimation in XL-MIMO systems that jointly estimates sparse channel coefficients and continuous angle-distance parameters via alternating optimization, avoiding grid mismatch errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Near-field channel estimation in XL-MIMO suffers from grid mismatch when using fixed angle-distance grids, while multi-stage refinement methods add computational complexity.

Method: DL-ISTA combines dictionary learning with iterative soft-thresholding, using alternating optimization to jointly estimate sparse channel coefficients and continuous angle-distance parameters, with Sobol sequence initialization for robust convergence.

Result: DL-ISTA outperforms a baseline with comparable complexity and achieves comparable or better accuracy than a substantially more complex benchmark.

Conclusion: Joint continuous parameter learning via DL-ISTA effectively eliminates discretization errors in near-field channel estimation with favorable complexity-accuracy trade-off.

Abstract: Channel estimation in extra-large multiple-input multiple-output systems is challenging due to near-field propagation, where the array response depends on both the angle and distance of the propagation paths. Existing near-field channel estimation methods typically rely either on fixed angle-distance grids, which suffer from grid mismatch effects, or on multi-stage refinement procedures with increased computational complexity. To address these limitations, this paper proposes the \textit{dictionary-learning iterative soft-thresholding algorithm (DL-ISTA)}, a method for joint near-field dictionary learning and channel estimation based on the iterative soft-thresholding algorithm. The proposed method jointly estimates the sparse channel coefficients and the continuous angle-distance parameters through alternating optimization, thereby avoiding discretization errors associated with fixed grids. To promote robust convergence, the angle-distance parameters are initialized using Sobol sequences, which provide uniform coverage of the parameter space. Numerical results show that DL-ISTA outperforms a baseline with comparable computational complexity and attains comparable or better accuracy than a substantially more complex benchmark.

</details>


### [793] [Ambient IoT Backscatter Devices as Passive Anchors for NLOS Cellular Positioning: Fundamental Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03459)
*Hüseyin Yiğitler,Musa Furkan Keskin,Ossi Kaltiokallio,Riku Jäntti*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes how much localization information survives when using uncalibrated Ambient IoT backscatter devices as passive anchors for NLOS positioning, deriving Fisher information matrices and showing that unknown device phases eliminate carrier-phase information while joint gain/response uncertainty leaves only bandwidth-dependent delay information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the localization capability of Ambient IoT backscatter devices acting as passive anchors in cellular networks when they are independently controlled, lack common phase reference, and have uncalibrated reflection gains and residual phases.

Method: Closed-form equivalent Fisher information matrices are derived for calibrated, partially calibrated, and fully uncalibrated operation, treating common channel gain, relative backscatter response, and residual device phases as nuisance parameters.

Result: Unknown device phases remove carrier-phase information from backscatter-assisted paths; joint uncertainty in common gain and relative response leaves direct NLOS path with only bandwidth-dependent delay information; device count alone insufficient—passive anchors must observe scatterer from diverse directions; at least 2 devices in 2D and 3 in 3D needed for joint single-snapshot identification.

Conclusion: The results identify deployment implications for Ambient IoT positioning and show which calibration losses also apply to separable subpanel-based reconfigurable-surface architectures.

Abstract: Ambient Internet-of-Things backscatter devices at known locations can act as low-cost passive anchors by creating geometrically anchored reflected paths in cellular networks. Unlike reconfigurable intelligent surfaces, practical backscatter devices are independently controlled and lack a common phase reference; their modulation signatures may be known, but their reflection gains and residual phases are generally uncalibrated. We study how much localization information survives this incomplete per-device calibration in uplink non-line-of-sight (NLOS) positioning, where the direct NLOS path and the backscatter-assisted paths share an unknown scatterer. Treating the common channel gain, the relative backscatter response, and the residual device phases as nuisance parameters, we derive closed-form equivalent Fisher information matrices for calibrated, partially calibrated, and fully uncalibrated operation. The analysis shows that unknown device phases remove carrier-phase information from the backscatter-assisted paths, whereas joint uncertainty in the common gain and relative response leaves the direct NLOS path with only bandwidth-dependent delay information. The resulting position-domain bounds show that device count alone is insufficient: the passive anchors must also observe the common scatterer from sufficiently diverse directions. For joint single-snapshot identification of the user equipment and scatterer, at least two devices in two dimensions and three in three dimensions are necessary. The results identify deployment implications for Ambient Internet-of-Things positioning and show which calibration losses also apply to separable subpanel-based reconfigurable-surface architectures.

</details>


### [794] [A Sheaf-Theoretic Framework for Distributed Multi-Site Channel Charting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03480)
*Enrico Grimaldi,Leonardo Di Nino,Mario Edoardo Pandolfo,Gabriele D'Acunto,Sergio Barbarossa,Paolo Di Lorenzo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a sheaf-theoretic framework for multi-site channel charting that aligns local neural embeddings from different base stations into a coherent global representation using topological signal processing and alternating optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-cell wireless networks, each base station independently learns a local channel chart via neural encoders, resulting in misaligned representation spaces across overlapping coverage areas. This inconsistency hinders network-level tasks like user tracking, handover prediction, and resource allocation.

Method: The authors model the collection of local charts as a network sheaf, encoding consistency constraints across base stations. They develop a multi-site channel charting architecture with an alternating optimization algorithm that jointly updates neural encoders and inter-site orthogonal transport maps, supported by theoretical guarantees on consistency.

Result: Experimental results show improved cross-site alignment without degrading the quality of local embeddings, validating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Conclusion: The proposed sheaf-based framework enables coherent integration of independently learned local channel charts into a shared global structure, preserving local geometric fidelity while promoting alignment across base stations.

Abstract: Channel charting (CC) enables data-driven user localization in wireless networks by embedding channel state information (CSI) into low-dimensional representations. In multi-cell scenarios, each base station independently learns a local chart via neural encoders, leading to misaligned representation spaces across overlapping coverage areas. This lack of consistency hinders network-level tasks such as user tracking, handover prediction, and resource allocation. To address this issue, we propose a principled framework for multi-site channel charting based on topological signal processing. We model the collection of local charts as a network sheaf, which encodes consistency constraints across the network and enables the coherent integration of locally learned representations into a shared global structure. This formulation introduces an interpretable inductive bias that promotes alignment across base stations while preserving local geometric fidelity. Building on this model, we develop a multi-site channel charting architecture and an alternating optimization algorithm that jointly updates neural encoders and inter-site orthogonal transport maps, with theoretical guarantees on consistency. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, demonstrating improved cross-site alignment without degrading the quality of local embeddings.

</details>


### [795] [On--Off Digital Noise Modulation under Multi-User Co-Channel Interference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03506)
*Daniel C. Araújo,André A. dos Anjos,Hugerles S. Silva*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the performance of on-off digital noise (OODN) modulation in multi-user scenarios, deriving a unified analytical framework for bit error probability (BEP) under co-channel interference, and showing that the irreducible error floor is governed by the on-off interference and non-coherent detector rather than noise waveform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior works have only addressed single-link operation of OODN modulation; the impact of co-channel interference in multi-user scenarios remains unexplored.

Method: The authors consider K synchronous OODN interferers over AWGN and fading channels, derive a unified analytical framework for BEP, obtain the optimal likelihood-ratio detection threshold, and extend the analysis to κ-μ fading for millimeter-wave channels.

Result: A closed-form expression for the irreducible error floor is obtained, which climbs geometrically with the number of co-channel interferers, yielding a simple admission-control rule on network density. Results show the floor is governed by on-off interference and non-coherent detector rather than noise waveform.

Conclusion: The error floor is dominated by interference and detector characteristics (not noise), and the derived admission-control rule can guide network density management.

Abstract: This letter analyzes the performance of on-off digital noise (OODN) modulation under multi-user scenarios. While prior works have addressed single-link operation, the impact of co-channel interference remains unexplored. We consider $K$ synchronous OODN interferers over AWGN and fading channels and derive a unified analytical framework for the bit error probability (BEP). The optimal likelihood-ratio detection threshold is obtained, along with a closed-form expression for the resulting irreducible error floor, which climbs geometrically with the number of co-channel interferers and yields a simple admission-control rule on network density. The analysis is extended to $κ$-$μ$ fading, covering practical millimeter-wave channels with dominant line-of-sight components. Results, corroborated by Monte Carlo simulation, show that the floor is governed by the on-off interference and the non-coherent detector rather than by the noise waveform.

</details>


### [796] [Improving the clinical utility of lower-limb surface electromyography (sEMG) by quantifying and correcting for location changes in inter-session recordings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03609)
*Fraser Douglas,Mona Pei,Quoc Sy Vu,Linh Le,Calvin Kuo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A high-density sEMG algorithm was developed to estimate and compensate for electrode location shifts across sessions, reducing inter-session feature differences and enabling more reliable multi-session muscle activity measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: sEMG is useful for recovery assessment in neurological and musculoskeletal disorders, but its broader adoption is limited by sensitivity to electrode location changes across sessions. This work aims to address this challenge.

Method: 11 healthy participants performed isometric and dynamic exercises with HDsEMG on four lower limb muscles across four sessions with shifted array locations. Error minimization between spatially-mapped HDsEMG metrics estimated location changes, validated against 3D scans. Feature differences across locations were computed to assess mitigation effects.

Result: Electrode location estimates improved over unchanged-location assumption in 81.7% of cases, with 37.6% within 1 cm of ground truth. Feature differences using algorithm estimates were statistically similar to closest-electrode comparisons, while same-electrode differences were significantly larger (e.g., isometric max envelope amplitude mean difference reduced from 21.1% to 15.9%).

Conclusion: The algorithm reduces inter-session feature differences caused by electrode location changes, enabling more direct cross-session feature comparisons and promising robust sEMG for musculoskeletal and neurological recovery tracking.

Abstract: Purpose: Surface electromyography (sEMG) can enable direct muscle activity measurement to support the recovery assessment of individuals with neurological and musculoskeletal disorders. Despite this, its broader adoption of sEMG has been limited given its sensitivity to changes in electrode location across sessions. To address this challenge and enable multi-session sEMG, this work develops a novel high-density sEMG (HDsEMG) algorithm to quantify changes in electrode location and mitigate its effects on common time and frequency domain sEMG features. Methods: 11 healthy participants performed isometric and dynamic exercises with HDsEMG on four lower limb muscles. These were repeated four times, reapplying arrays at shifted locations. The error between spatially-mapped HDsEMG metrics was then minimised to estimate the change in array location, with this compared against ground truth 3D scans. Lastly, relative feature differences across locations were computed at select electrodes to assess the degree to which inter-session sEMG effects were mitigated. Results: Electrode location estimates were improved over the assumption their location remained unchanged in 81.7% of cases, 37.6% identified within 1 cm of the ground truth. Feature differences computed between closest electrodes across locations per ground truth and algorithm estimates were statistically similar. Conversely, feature differences for the same electrode across locations were significantly greater, increasing the mean difference for the isometric max envelope amplitude from 15.9% with the algorithm to 21.1% without. Conclusions: The algorithm's application reduced inter-session feature differences arising from changes in electrode location. This can facilitate more direct cross-session feature comparisons, representing a promising step toward robust sEMG measurement for musculoskeletal and neurological recovery tracking.

</details>


### [797] [Two-dimensional Fourier compressed sensing under a fixed readout budget per channel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03611)
*Nitin Jonathan Myers*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes sparse 2D signal recovery from subsampled Fourier measurements under per-channel readout budgets, deriving a lower bound on mutual coherence that exceeds the classical Welch bound, and constructing optimal deterministic subsampling patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the practical problem of recovering sparse 2D signals (matrices) when only a fixed number of entries per channel (row/column) can be sampled in the Fourier domain, which is relevant to communications, radar, and imaging applications.

Method: Derives a lower bound on the mutual coherence of the compressed sensing matrix under per-channel readout budget constraints, constructs deterministic subsampling patterns that achieve this bound for certain matrix dimensions and budgets, and benchmarks against random subsampling via simulations.

Result: The derived lower bound on mutual coherence is larger than the classical Welch bound due to the limited per-channel readout budget. Deterministic patterns attaining this bound are constructed for specific dimensions and budgets.

Conclusion: The per-channel readout budget fundamentally limits the achievable mutual coherence, making it worse than the classical bound, but optimal deterministic sampling patterns can be designed to meet the new bound.

Abstract: Recovering sparse signals from their subsampled Fourier representation is an important problem in communications, radar, and imaging. In this letter, we focus on reconstructing sparse 2D signals (matrices) under the constraint that only a fixed number of entries can be sampled from each channel, e.g., a row or a column in the Fourier domain. For a specified per-channel readout budget, we derive a lower bound on the mutual coherence of the corresponding compressed sensing matrix. We show that our bound is larger than the classical Welch bound, due to a limited readout budget. We also construct deterministic subsampling patterns that attain this bound for a class of matrix dimensions and readout budgets, and benchmark them against random subsampling through simulations.

</details>


### [798] [STAR-RIS-Assisted Integrated Sensing, Secure Communication, and Power Transfer: A Transmit Power Minimization Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03637)
*Ling He,Vaibhav Kumar,Yingyang Chen,Miaowen Wen,Christina Pöpper,Marwa Chafii*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a STAR-RIS-assisted ISSCPT system that integrates sensing, secure communication, and wireless power transfer, and formulates a transmit-power minimization problem using alternating optimization, showing superiority over cRIS and no-RIS baselines while quantifying security-sensing-power tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While ISAC and SWIPT have been validated individually, the combination of integrated sensing, secure communication, and power transfer (ISSCPT) remains unexplored due to tight coupling among design variables, motivating the need for intelligent metasurfaces like STAR-RIS to enable full-space wave control.

Method: A transmit-power minimization problem is formulated with joint optimization of transmit/receive beamforming and STAR-RIS configuration, solved via alternating optimization combined with successive convex approximation, second-order cone programming, and eigenvalue decomposition.

Result: The proposed STAR-RIS-assisted design outperforms cRIS and no-RIS baselines; the paper quantifies the additional transmit power required by ISSCPT relative to ISAC and secure SWIPT, clarifying security-sensing-power tradeoffs.

Conclusion: STAR-RIS is a promising vehicle for power-efficient ISSCPT, and the tradeoffs among security, sensing, and power transfer are explicitly characterized in metasurface-assisted systems.

Abstract: Evolving wireless networks call for architectures that unify sensing, communication, and wireless power transfer. Although integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) and simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) have validated dual-function transmission, the combination of integrated sensing, secure communication, and power transfer (ISSCPT) remains largely unexplored, in part due to the tight coupling among design variables. To address this coupling and expand spatial degrees of freedom, we turn to intelligent metasurfaces: while a conventional reconfigurable intelligent surface (cRIS) reflects only to one side and thus limits coverage and flexibility, a simultaneously transmitting and reflecting RIS (STAR-RIS) enables full-space wave control, making it a natural vehicle for power-efficient ISSCPT. We study a STAR-RIS-assisted ISSCPT system and pose a central question: How much transmit power is required to operate such a system? We formulate a transmit-power minimization problem that jointly optimizes transmit and receive beamforming and the STAR-RIS configuration, and solve it via alternating optimization with successive convex approximation, second-order cone programming, and eigenvalue decomposition. Simulations show that the proposed STAR-RIS-assisted design outperforms cRIS and no-RIS baselines, and quantify the additional transmit power required by ISSCPT relative to ISAC and secure SWIPT, clarifying security-sensing-power tradeoffs in metasurface-assisted systems.

</details>


### [799] [Virtual Reality-Simulated Interaction Between Micro-Mobility Vehicles and Pedestrians: A Biomechanical Analysis of Human Gait and Movement Responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03705)
*Sahan Siriwardena,Omar Aboelrous,Anas Madkoor,Mohamed Elansari,Aiman Alhetari,Jassim Al Jufairi,Mohammed Al-Kuwari,Qinaat Hussain,Charitha Dias,Ajith Sominanda Herath*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This study used VR and markerless pose estimation to analyze pedestrian gait adaptations during simulated e-scooter interactions, finding significant reductions in step length and altered stance/swing phases under collision risk scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pedestrian walking is fundamental to daily mobility, but e-scooter adoption has increased collision risks on shared walkways. Previous studies relied on trajectory-based observations with limited biomechanical insight, motivating the need for detailed gait analysis during pedestrian-e-scooter interactions.

Method: Twelve male participants (21-23 years) walked in immersive VR across four scenarios (normal walking, e-scooter encounters at 10-25 km/h, crossing encounters, near-crash encounters). Sagittal-plane videos were analyzed using OpenPose 25-point model, Kinovea, and custom JSON tools. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, MANOVA, and non-parametric tests.

Result: Crossing and near-crash scenarios significantly reduced step length (p<0.001), from 226.5 cm (normal) to 204.7 cm (near-crash). Gait velocity and timing were not significantly affected, but participants showed shorter stance phases, longer swing phases, and restricted knee motion during stressful encounters.

Conclusion: Immersive VR combined with markerless pose estimation effectively quantifies pedestrian biomechanical responses to micro-mobility interactions. Identified gait adaptations may serve as sensitive collision risk indicators and support development of proactive pedestrian safety measures and intelligent micro-mobility control systems.

Abstract: Pedestrian walking is a fundamental activity of daily living and a key component of first and last-mile urban mobility. The rapid adoption of e-scooters has increased pedestrian-vehicle interactions on shared sidewalks and crossings, raising collision risks. However, most previous studies have relied on trajectory-based observations, providing limited insight into biomechanical gait responses. This study investigated pedestrian gait adaptations during simulated e-scooter interactions using immersive virtual reality (VR) and markerless pose estimation. Twelve healthy male university students (21-23 years) completed four VR walking scenarios: normal walking, e-scooter encounters at 10-25 km/h, crossing encounters, and near-crash encounters. Sagittal-plane videos were analyzed using the OpenPose 25-point model. Step length, gait cycle time, walking velocity, stance and swing phases, and lower-limb joint trajectories were extracted using Kinovea and custom JSON-based analysis tools. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, MANOVA, and non-parametric tests Crossing and near-crash scenarios significantly reduced step length (p<0.001), from 226.5 cm during normal walking to 204.7 cm during near-crash simulations. Although gait velocity and timing were not significantly affected, participants consistently exhibited shorter stance phases, longer swing phases, and restricted knee motion during stressful encounters, indicating reflexive gait adaptations to perceived collision risk. These findings demonstrate that immersive VR combined with markerless pose estimation effectively quantifies pedestrian biomechanical responses to micro-mobility interactions. Gait adaptations identified in this study may serve as sensitive indicators of collision risk and support the development of proactive pedestrian safety measures and intelligent micro-mobility control systems.

</details>


### [800] [LAMBDA: A Low-Altitude Multimodal Base Dataset for UAV Sensing and Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03826)
*Lin Zhou,Peichuan Rao,Chenshuo Zhang,Jianhua Mo,Shu Sun,Zhiyong Chen,Meixia Tao*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A new high-fidelity multimodal dataset (LAMBDA) for low-altitude integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), providing synchronized RGB, depth, LiDAR, IMU, UAV poses, CSI, and radar data across diverse scenes and weather conditions, with flexible configuration options.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-altitude ISAC research requires aligned multimodal data (wireless, visual, motion, LiDAR, radar) under shared timestamps and trajectories, but such comprehensive datasets are lacking.

Method: LAMBDA is generated via a high-fidelity digital-twin pipeline with detailed scene geometry, material assignment, and electromagnetic UAV modeling, producing synchronized multimodal data across urban/suburban/campus scenes under various weather conditions.

Result: The dataset covers multiple scenarios and weather conditions, supports user-defined radar/CSI configuration, and is validated through quality control, multimodal visualization, and two ISAC use cases (RGB-aided beam prediction and RGB-LiDAR UAV localization).

Conclusion: LAMBDA fills a critical gap in low-altitude ISAC research by providing a flexible, high-fidelity multimodal dataset that enables reproducible evaluation of joint communication and sensing tasks.

Abstract: Research on low-altitude integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) requires aligned multimodal data that jointly describe wireless propagation, visual appearance, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) motion, light detection and ranging (LiDAR) perception, and radar sensing under common trajectories and timestamps. To address this need, a low-altitude multimodal base dataset, named LAMBDA, is introduced. LAMBDA is characterized by high fidelity, modality diversity, scenario richness, and configuration flexibility. It is generated through a high-fidelity digital-twin pipeline with detailed scene geometry, refined material assignment, and electromagnetic modeling of UAVs. LAMBDA provides synchronized RGB images, depth maps, LiDAR point clouds, inertial measurement unit states, UAV poses, channel state information (CSI), and radar-synthesis resources across matched low-altitude operating conditions, shared coordinate systems, and synchronized frame indices. The dataset covers urban, suburban, and campus scenes, multi-UAV/multi-base-station settings, nighttime conditions, and sunny, rainy, snowy, and foggy weather variations. Its CSI and radar resources support user-defined antenna-array sizes, bandwidths, subcarrier spacings, chirp parameters, and plane-wave or spherical-wavefront channel synthesis. The reliability and usability of LAMBDA are assessed through quality control, weather and multimodal visualization, and two UAV ISAC-related use cases: RGB-aided beam prediction and RGB-LiDAR-based UAV localization.

</details>


### [801] [Task-Oriented Multimodal Edge Intelligence via Integrated Sensing-Communication-Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03907)
*Weiwei Chen,Yinghui He,Zhong Ye,Dingzhu Wen,Guanding Yu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a task-oriented multi-modal ISCC framework using MCR^2 criterion for compact feature extraction and edge-side sensing evaluation, with a BCD algorithm optimizing sensing accuracy under resource constraints, outperforming baselines in human activity recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ISCC designs rely on single-modal sensing, which suffers from occlusions, environmental uncertainties, and modality-specific failures. Multi-modal ISCC is needed but underexplored due to challenges like enlarged data dimensionality, tighter budgets, and complex inter-modal correlations.

Method: A task-oriented multi-modal ISCC framework integrating device-side feature extraction with edge-side joint inference, using MCR^2 criterion for learning compact/discriminative features and as a sensing evaluation metric. A sensing accuracy maximization problem under delay/resource constraints is solved via a BCD algorithm.

Result: Experiments on human activity recognition datasets show the proposed framework consistently outperforms three baseline schemes under limited resource conditions.

Conclusion: The MCR^2-based multi-modal ISCC framework effectively addresses robustness issues of single-modal sensing and resource efficiency challenges, offering a principled approach for edge intelligence with heterogeneous modalities.

Abstract: Integrated sensing, communication, and computation (ISCC) has recently emerged as a unified framework for enabling edge intelligence. However, existing ISCC designs predominantly rely on single-modal sensing, which is inherently vulnerable to occlusions, environmental uncertainties, and modality-specific failures, leading to degraded robustness in real-world deployments. This motivates the need for multi-modal ISCC, yet its design remains insufficiently explored. Compared with the single-modal case, multi-modal ISCC is more challenging because heterogeneous modalities enlarge data dimensionality and tighten communication/computation/energy budgets, while inter-modal correlations further complicate performance characterization. To address these challenges, we propose a task-oriented multi-modal ISCC framework that integrates device-side feature extraction with edge-side joint multi-modal inference. A central component of our approach is the maximal coding rate reduction (MCR^2) criterion, which enables each device to learn compact and discriminative task-relevant features, offering clear advantages over conventional cross-entropy-based extractors. We further leverage MCR^2 as a principled metric for edge-side sensing evaluation. On this basis, we formulate a sensing accuracy maximization problem under delay and resource constraints and develop an efficient block coordinate descent (BCD) algorithm after transforming the problem into a more tractable equivalent form. Focusing on a human activity recognition task, we conduct extensive experiments on publicly available datasets to evaluate the performance of the proposed ISCC framework. The results demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms three baseline schemes under limited resource conditions.

</details>


### [802] [A Triple-Band Bandpass Filter Using Square Open-Loop Resonators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03921)
*Rashmi Ravi,Bhaskarareddy N C,Augustine O. Nwajana*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper reviews multi-band BPF design techniques and validates a triple-band BPF prototype using nine square open-loop resonators on Rogers RT/Duroid 6010LM substrate, achieving three passbands at 2.1/2.2/2.3 GHz with low insertion losses and good return losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid advancement of multi-band wireless communication systems has created demand for compact, high-performance multi-band bandpass filters capable of isolating specific frequency bands within a wider spectrum.

Method: A triple-band BPF is designed using Keysight ADS software with nine square open-loop resonators (three per passband) on Rogers RT/Duroid 6010LM substrate (εr=10.7, tanδ=0.0023, thickness=1.27mm), characteristic impedance of 50Ω, and 6% fractional bandwidth.

Result: Simulation shows insertion losses of 0.674/0.976/1.314 dB and return losses of 16.785/33.609/17.162 dB across the three passbands; the 2.2 GHz center frequency is achieved with high precision.

Conclusion: The square open-loop resonator transformation technique is effective for compact multi-band filter design in modern wireless communication systems.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of multi-band wireless communication systems has driven demand for compact, high-performance multi-band bandpass filters (BPFs) capable of isolating specific frequency bands within a wider spectrum. This paper reviews the current state-of-the-art in multi-band filter design and implementation techniques and presents the design, simulation, and characterisation of a prototype triple-band bandpass filter to validate one of the investigated techniques. A triple-band BPF operating at 2.1 GHz, 2.2 GHz, and 2.3 GHz is designed using Keysight ADS software and implemented on Rogers RT/Duroid 6010LM substrate (dielectric constant = 10.7, loss tangent = 0.0023, thickness = 1.27 mm). The design employs nine square open-loop resonators three per passband with a characteristic impedance of 50 Ω and a fractional bandwidth of 6%. Simulation results demonstrate insertion losses of 0.674 dB, 0.976 dB, and 1.314 dB, and return losses of 16.785 dB, 33.609 dB, and 17.162 dB across the three passbands respectively. The 2.2 GHz centre frequency is achieved with high precision, confirming the effectiveness of the square open-loop resonator transformation technique for compact multi-band filter design in modern wireless communication systems.

</details>


### [803] [Optimal Uplink Pinching-Antenna Activation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04159)
*Zhenqiao Cheng,Chongjun Ouyang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes and analyzes three search algorithms (greedy search, beam search, and branch-and-bound) for maximizing uplink sum-rate in a multiuser pinching-antenna system with discrete preconfigured antenna locations, demonstrating substantial performance gains over conventional fixed arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently solve the uplink sum-rate maximization problem for a practical pinching-antenna system where each antenna is restricted to a finite set of preconfigured locations, addressing the need for algorithms with tunable performance-complexity tradeoffs.

Method: The sum-rate maximization is formulated as a layered tree search problem. Three algorithms are developed: (1) Greedy search (GS) with efficient matrix-inverse updates, (2) Beam search (BeS) retaining promising partial paths for tunable tradeoffs, and (3) Optimal branch-and-bound (BnB) search with monotonic transformed objective for pruning noncompetitive subtrees.

Result: Numerical results show substantial gains over conventional fixed arrays. GS and moderate-width BeS achieve near-optimal performance at lower computational cost than BnB.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithms effectively leverage the flexibility of pinching antennas with discrete locations, with GS and BeS offering practical near-optimal solutions at reduced complexity compared to the optimal BnB approach.

Abstract: An uplink multiuser pinching-antenna system (PASS) is considered, where multiple dielectric waveguides are deployed at the base station and one pinching antenna (PA) is activated on each waveguide. For practical implementation, each PA is restricted to a finite number of preconfigured locations. The resulting uplink sum-rate maximization problem is represented as a layered tree search. Three algorithms are then developed: a greedy search (GS), a beam search (BeS), and an optimal branch-and-bound (BnB) search. In GS, the locally best branch is selected through efficient matrix-inverse updates. In BeS, several promising partial paths are retained to provide a tunable performance-complexity tradeoff. In BnB, noncompetitive subtrees are pruned through a monotonic transformed objective without loss of optimality. Substantial gains over a conventional fixed array are demonstrated by numerical results. Near-optimal performance is also achieved by GS and moderate-width BeS at a lower computational co t than BnB.

</details>


### [804] [Light Coils: MRI with Fully Optical Data and Power Transmission](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04211)
*Zining Liu,Morteza Teymoori,Jakob Gerlach,Reza Aghabagheri,Henning Helmers,Michael Bock,Caglar Ataman,Ali Oezen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Light Coils," a fully optical MRI receive architecture that replaces conventional galvanic cabling with optical fibers for data transmission, power delivery, and coil detuning, enabling lightweight, modular, and potentially ultra-dense MRI receive arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increasing the number of coils in MRI receive arrays is limited by electrical cabling, electromagnetic coupling, integration complexity, and safety constraints. The authors aim to overcome these bottlenecks by eliminating galvanic connections.

Method: The system uses Mach-Zehnder modulators (MZM) for MR signal encoding onto optical carriers, power-over-fiber (PoF) with photovoltaic cells for front-end power, and pulse-sequence-triggered optical paths for active detuning. A four-channel array employs dense wavelength-division multiplexing (DWDM) over a single fiber.

Result: Light Coils can match conventional SNR at MZM input powers of 5-10mW and PoF inputs of 80-100mW. In vivo human brain imaging at 3T showed comparable image quality. The four-channel array demonstrated >28dB inter-channel optical isolation, reduced noise correlation, and parallel imaging capability.

Conclusion: The fully optical receive architecture provides a scalable route towards lightweight, modular, and ultra-dense MRI receive arrays using integrated photonics and power-over-fiber.

Abstract: In MRI, dense receiver coil arrays with a high number of coil elements are used to efficiently detect and encode the signal. Further increasing the number of coils is hampered by electrical cabling and massive electronics that introduce electromagnetic coupling, integration complexity and even safety constraints. Here we introduce the novel Light Coils concept, a fully optical MRI receive architecture in which data transmission, front-end power delivery, and coil detuning are all implemented optically, thereby reducing the massive galvanic cabling to a few optical fibers. For signal encoding, Mach-Zehnder modulators (MZM) are used to convert the MR signal from each coil onto a C-band optical carrier. The preamplifiers are driven via a power-over-fiber (PoF) system that uses a high-efficiency photovoltaic (PV) cell for optical-to-electrical power conversion. A pulse-sequence-triggered optical path controls active detuning. Jointly optimizing modulator bias, optical power and front-end gain under realistic receiver chain conditions, Light Coils can match the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of conventional RF coil systems with galvanic cables at MZM input powers of 5-10mW and photonic power converter inputs of 80-100mW. At a clinical 3T MRI system, we show in vivo human brain imaging with a single-channel Light Coil element with an image quality and SNR comparable to a conventional coaxial readout using the identical coil element. Extending the concept to a four-channel array using dense wavelength-division multiplexing over a single fiber, we demonstrate wavelength-selective routing with inter-channel optical isolation exceeding 28dB, reduced noise correlation compared with the galvanic reference, and parallel imaging. These results establish a scalable route towards lightweight, modular, and potentially ultra-dense MRI receive arrays based on integrated photonics and power-over-fiber.

</details>


### [805] [AI-RAN on NPUs: Baseband Processing Without Baseband Chips](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04224)
*Shilong Zhang,Luping Xiang,Jienan Chen,Kun Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that NPUs can support wireless baseband processing (OFDM transceiver) by reconstructing communication algorithms onto AI compute primitives, resolving the architectural mismatch between baseband workloads and NPU design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether Neural Processing Units (NPUs), which are AI accelerators optimized for inference, can also support wireless baseband processing in the AI-RAN paradigm that has primarily been demonstrated on GPUs.

Method: The authors resolve the architectural mismatch between baseband processing and NPU architecture by reconstructing communication algorithms onto AI compute primitives, prioritizing engine utilization over arithmetic count. They validate this approach with a complete OFDM transceiver on an Ascend 310B1 edge NPU.

Result: Successful end-to-end over-the-air transmission via USRP X300 at 3.0 GHz, demonstrating that NPUs can support wireless baseband processing by prioritizing engine utilization over arithmetic minimization.

Conclusion: NPUs are viable for baseband processing in AI-RAN systems, as the computational isomorphism between NPU matrix/vector engines and physical-layer operations can be exploited by reframing baseband algorithms as dense-tensor AI primitives.

Abstract: AI-RAN aims to unify artificial intelligence and radio access network workloads on a shared compute substrate. While this paradigm has so far been demonstrated primarily on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs), it remains unclear whether Neural Processing Units (NPUs), which are AI accelerators optimized for inference, can also support wireless baseband processing. Here, we provide the first affirmative answer by resolving the fundamental mismatch between baseband workloads and NPU architecture. A computational isomorphism exists: matrix and vector engines NPUs dedicate to inference inherently cover physical-layer operations. Yet NPU architectures are natively shaped for dense-tensor AI inference, not baseband. This architectural mismatch surfaces as opposing optimization objectives: traditional baseband minimizes arithmetic operations, whereas NPU performance demands maximizing engine utilization. We close this gap by reconstructing communication algorithms onto AI compute primitives, prioritizing engine utilization over arithmetic count. We validate this with a complete OFDM transceiver on an Ascend 310B1 edge NPU, demonstrating end-to-end over-the-air transmission via USRP X300 at 3.0 GHz.

</details>


### [806] [Cognitive Digital Twins for Self-Aware Channel Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04299)
*Afan Ali,Ali Arshad Nasir,Daniel Benevides da Costa*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a cognitive digital twin framework using a VAE with latent activation monitoring to detect channel distribution drift and autonomously manage AI/ML-based channel estimators through continue, update, or retire lifecycle actions, outperforming conventional offline-trained models under drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI/ML-based channel estimators silently degrade when propagation conditions drift from their training distributions, and existing approaches lack autonomy in detecting and responding to such drift without ground-truth channel knowledge.

Method: A model-agnostic cognitive digital twin (CDT) framework combining a variational autoencoder (VAE) with latent activation monitoring to detect distribution drift, enabling autonomous lifecycle actions (continue, update, retire) without ground-truth channel knowledge.

Result: The framework demonstrates accurate drift detection and robust channel estimation, consistently outperforming conventional offline-trained deep learning estimators under moderate and severe channel drift.

Conclusion: The proposed CDT framework is fully compatible with 3GPP's AI-native lifecycle management and provides an effective solution for maintaining reliable channel estimation under distribution drift.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML)-based channel estimators silently degrade when propagation conditions drift from their training distributions. This letter proposes a model-agnostic cognitive digital twin (CDT) framework that combines a variational autoencoder (VAE) with latent activation monitoring to detect distribution drift and autonomously execute \textsc{continue}, \textsc{update}, or \textsc{retire} lifecycle actions without requiring ground-truth channel knowledge. The proposed framework is fully compatible with the AI-native lifecycle management envisioned in 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP). Simulations over various channels demonstrate accurate drift detection and robust channel estimation, consistently outperforming conventional offline-trained deep learning estimators under moderate and severe channel drift.

</details>


### [807] [Deadline-Bound Finite-Object Delivery over Intermittent LEO Satellite Contact Plans under Residual-Service Accounting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04405)
*Houtianfu Wang,O. Tansel Baydas,Hanlin Cai,Haofan Dong,Ozgur B. Akan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a residual-service-aware delivery layer for LEO relay networks that ensures complete object delivery before deadlines, addressing the problem of path-private evaluation under-counting completion in split-path scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing path-private evaluation methods for LEO relay networks can under-count completion (by up to 154 seconds) when objects are split across candidate paths, leading to missed deadlines for partial deliveries.

Method: The authors develop a residual-service-aware delivery layer that: (1) consumes candidate paths from contact-plan route generation, (2) tests complete delivery under per-edge FIFO residual service, (3) derives a sufficient service-budget condition for two-way striping, and (4) verifies with restricted exhaustive reference and procedural contact models (20-180 satellites).

Result: Bounded two-way striping reduces mean and median gaps to the restricted reference by about 40%; P90 and worst-case gaps remain unchanged. Path-private evaluation can under-count completion by up to 154 seconds and falsely report completion for plans with no residual-service viability.

Conclusion: The proposed residual-service-aware layer improves delivery reliability for time-sensitive objects in LEO relay networks, and two-way striping with sufficient service budget can enlarge the feasible payload region.

Abstract: Low-Earth-orbit (LEO) relay networks deliver finite objects -- sensing tiles, telemetry blocks, model updates, and checkpoints -- over intermittent inter-satellite and space-to-ground contact plans. Partial delivery is insufficient when the complete object misses its deadline. When an object is split across candidate paths, a path-private evaluation can count the same contact service more than once and silently under-count completion. We develop a residual-service-aware delivery layer that consumes candidate paths from contact-plan route generation and tests whether the complete object can be delivered before its deadline under per-edge first-in-first-out residual service. Under controlled shared-contact contention, path-private evaluation under-counts completion by up to 154 s and can report finite completion for a fixed plan with no residual-service completion. For edge-disjoint complementary contacts, the layer reduces to fixed-path service; we derive a sufficient service-budget condition under which two-way striping strictly enlarges the feasible payload region. We verify a restricted exhaustive reference, characterize runtime over a 20-180-satellite procedural contact model, and show that bounded two-way striping reduces mean and median gaps to the restricted reference by about 40%, while P90 and worst-case gaps remain unchanged.

</details>


### [808] [Non-invasive Blood Glucose Estimation from Wearable Physiological Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04414)
*Zexing Zhang,Jichao Li,Yilong Wang,Kewei Yang,Tianyang Lei*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A deep-learning-based dynamic incremental learning framework (MERDC + PGBA) is proposed for non-invasive blood glucose estimation from PPG data, achieving strong performance on a new longitudinal benchmark dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Longitudinal PPG data suffers from distribution drift, and reference blood glucose labels are sparse and costly, making non-invasive glucose estimation difficult.

Method: Proposes a dynamic incremental learning (DIL) framework combining a mutual entropy-optimized replay-based dynamic clustering module (MERDC) with an uncertainty-quantified proxy gradient bridging agent (PGBA) for label-efficient adaptation to unlabeled PPG streams.

Result: MAE of 0.64±0.01 mmol/L, RMSE of 1.29±0.10 mmol/L, and 97.69±1.63% of estimates in Clarke zones A+B under 5-fold subject-independent validation.

Conclusion: The method provides a proof-of-concept for adaptive non-invasive glucose estimation in wearable sensing, and the released longitudinal benchmark supports future research.

Abstract: Non-invasive blood glucose estimation from wearable physiological signals remains difficult because longitudinal photoplethysmography (PPG) data are subject to distribution drift, whereas reference capillary blood glucose labels are sparse and costly to acquire. We propose a \rev{deep-learning-based} dynamic incremental learning (DIL) framework that combines a mutual entropy-optimized replay-based dynamic clustering module (MERDC) with an uncertainty-quantified proxy gradient bridging agent (PGBA) for label-efficient adaptation to unlabeled PPG streams. To support this setting, we further establish a longitudinal benchmark dataset comprising PPG, reference capillary blood glucose, and cuff blood pressure measurements from 183 participants collected over 285 days, and we make this resource available to the research community. Under 5-fold subject-independent validation, the proposed method achieves a mean absolute error (MAE) of $0.64 \pm 0.01$ millimoles per liter (mmol/L) and a root mean square error (RMSE) of $1.29 \pm 0.10$ mmol/L, with $97.69 \pm 1.63\%$ of estimates falling within Clarke zones A+B. Aggregation-level analyses further support the robustness of the observed error distribution beyond window-level evaluation. \rev{These results provide a proof-of-concept for adaptive non-invasive glucose estimation in wearable physiological sensing and establish a longitudinal benchmark for subsequent research.

</details>


### [809] [A Simultaneous Clustering and Tracking Algorithm for Capturing Cluster-Level Spatial Consistency in 6G Wireless Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04664)
*Jiaxin Lin,Pan Tang,Jianhua Zhang,Zhaowei Chang,Peijie Liu,Yufeng Qin,Ke Chen,Huixin Xu,Byonghyo Shim*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A Mahalanobis-distance-based simultaneous clustering and tracking (MD-SCT) algorithm is proposed to improve cluster-level spatial consistency in wireless channel modeling, outperforming existing methods for sub-THz/6G applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing clustering and tracking methods fail to fully exploit spatial correlations of MPCs, leading to inconsistent cluster evolution.

Method: MD-SCT algorithm uses Mahalanobis distance to capture joint distributions of MPCs in delay, angular, and spatial domains, enabling simultaneous clustering and tracking across snapshots.

Result: The algorithm yields smoother cluster evolution, evaluated via mean square successive difference and gradient change rate.

Conclusion: MD-SCT improves reliability of clustered channels for spatial consistency modeling, critical for 6G technologies like massive MIMO, ISAC, and THz communication.

Abstract: Spatial consistency is a fundamental physical property of wireless channels that reflects the smooth evolution of the channel between spatial locations. At the cluster level, it requires similar multipath components (MPCs) remain grouped into the same clusters as the transceivers move, enabling consistent cluster tracking. Cluster-level spatial consistency is essential for realistic cluster-based channel models, especially for potential 6G techniques such as massive MIMO, integrated sensing and communication, and terahertz (THz) communication. However, existing clustering and tracking methods do not fully exploit spatial correlations of MPCs. In tracking-after-clustering, clustering and tracking are decoupled, while joint clustering-and-tracking mainly relies on cluster centers from the previous snapshot. In this work, we propose a Mahalanobis-distance-based simultaneous clustering and tracking (MD-SCT) algorithm to capture the joint distribution of clustered MPCs in the delay, angular and spatial domains. Under Mahalanobis distance, MPCs in successive snapshots are associated with existing clusters, thereby inherently tracking while clustering. The algorithm is further applied in the sub-THz band. Performance is evaluated using mean square successive difference and gradient change rate. The results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm yields smoother cluster evolution. This improves the reliability of clustered channels for spatial consistency modeling in 6G.

</details>


### [810] [Multi-Target ISAR Imaging of UAV Swarms Using Fast Reweighted Atomic Norm Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04703)
*Mohammad Roueinfar*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper extends the FRAND algorithm (Fast Reweighted Atomic Norm Denoising) to ISAR imaging of UAV swarms, using 2D-ADMM optimization to separate overlapping radar echoes from multiple closely spaced targets, achieving superior resolution and noise robustness over MUSIC and Cadzow methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ISAR imaging of UAV swarms is challenging because backscattered signals from multiple closely spaced targets superpose coherently, making individual target reconstruction difficult. Existing methods degrade under low SNR and sparse aperture conditions.

Method: The paper extends FRAND to multi-target scenarios by reformulating atomic norm minimization with weighted regularization, and solves the optimization efficiently via Two-Dimensional Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (2D-ADMM). The method handles sparse apertures and additive white Gaussian noise.

Result: FRAND effectively disentangles mixed signals from UAV swarms and generates high-resolution range-Doppler profiles, outperforming MUSIC and Cadzow in low SNR conditions. Quantitative MSE evaluation confirms superiority.

Conclusion: Atomic norm minimization shows strong potential for complex multi-target radar imaging, offering a robust and computationally efficient solution for UAV swarm ISAR imaging.

Abstract: Inverse Synthetic Aperture Radar (ISAR) imaging of UAV swarms presents significant challenges due to the coherent superposition of backscattered signals from multiple closely spaced targets. This work explores the extension of the Fast Reweighted Atomic Norm Denoising (FRAND) algorithm to this multi-target scenario. We develop a comprehensive mathematical framework that reformulates the atomic norm minimization problem for swarm imaging, incorporating weighted regularization and efficient optimization via the TwoDimensional Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (2DADMM). The proposed method handles both sparse aperture conditions and additive white Gaussian noise while maintaining computational efficiency. We simulate an ISAR system receiving composite echoes from UAV swarms, each modeled with distinct scattering centers. The results demonstrate that FRAND effectively disentangles the mixed signals and generates high-resolution range-Doppler profiles for individual UAVs, outperforming traditional methods like Multiple Signal Classification (MUSIC) and Cadzow in low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) conditions. Quantitative evaluation using MeanSquare Error (MSE) criteria confirms the superiority of the proposed approach. This study establishes the strong potential of atomic norm minimization for complex multi-target radar imaging applications.

</details>


### [811] [Dual Fluid Antenna-Assisted UAV MU-MIMO Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04748)
*Runke Fan,Tianheng Xu,Pei Peng,Xianfu Chen,Celimuge Wu,Kai-Kit Wong,Mohsen Guizani*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an Alternating Optimization-based algorithm for joint optimization of UAV trajectory, FA positions, and beamforming in dual FA-assisted UAV MIMO networks, achieving 120% and 110% rate gains over FPA and existing FA baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of Fixed-Positioned Antennas in dynamic UAV channels and multi-user interference by leveraging FA position adaptivity and flexible beamforming.

Method: Joint optimization of UAV trajectory, transmit/receive FA positions, and beamforming via an Alternating Optimization (AO)-based algorithm that decomposes the highly coupled non-convex problem into subproblems.

Result: Achieves 120% average rate improvement over conventional FPA-based baselines and 110% improvement over existing FA-based baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed AO-based approach effectively handles the coupled non-convex optimization and demonstrates significant performance gains in dual FA-assisted UAV MIMO downlink communications.

Abstract: Fluid Antennas (FAs)-assisted Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) networks leverage the FA position adaptivity and flexible beamforming to overcome the limitations of Fixed-Positioned Antennas (FPAs) in dynamic UAV channels and Multi-User (MU) interference. This letter investigates a dual FA-assisted UAV network for MU-Multiple-Input-Multiple-Output (MIMO) downlink communications, aiming to maximize the average achievable rate through the joint optimization of UAV trajectory, the transmit/receive FA positions, and beamforming. The formulated problem is highly coupled and non-convex. Accordingly, an efficient Alternating Optimization (AO)-based algorithm is developed for decomposed subproblems, yielding a suboptimal solution. Numerical results demonstrate significant performance gains of 120% and 110% over conventional FPA-based and existing FA-based baselines, respectively.

</details>


### [812] [Shift-MoE-Based DJSCC for CSI Feedback in Multi-User Pinching-Antenna Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04795)
*Jian Zou,Yifan Lian,Yongsheng Liang,Fanyang Meng,Wenwu Xie,Liang Yang,Jian Xiao*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes Shift-MoE, an end-to-end deep joint source-channel coding scheme for CSI feedback in pinching-antenna systems, which uses channel-grouped shift operations and a gated mixture-of-experts module to handle structured correlations and user heterogeneity in CSI data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In FDD pinching-antenna systems, accurate CSI is critical but exhibits structured correlations over the waveguide-antenna grid and pronounced heterogeneity across users, making conventional fixed feedback mappings difficult to generalize.

Method: Shift-MoE uses channel-grouped one-step shift operations to capture grid dependencies without global attention, and employs a gated multilayer perceptron mixture-of-experts module to adapt to heterogeneous CSI statistics across users.

Result: The proposed Shift-MoE consistently outperforms representative learning-based CSI feedback baselines in normalized mean squared error and remains effective under different system parameter settings.

Conclusion: The proposed Shift-MoE effectively addresses the challenges of CSI feedback in pinching-antenna systems, achieving superior performance and robustness across various configurations.

Abstract: In frequency-division duplexing systems, the performance gains of pinching-antenna systems (PASS) critically depend on accurate channel state information (CSI) at the base station. However, PASS CSI exhibits structured correlations over the waveguide-antenna grid and pronounced heterogeneity across users, making conventional fixed feedback mappings difficult to generalize. To address this challenge, this letter proposes an end-to-end CSI feedback scheme over a noisy uplink feedback link based on deep joint source-channel coding, termed Shift-based Mixture-of-Experts (Shift-MoE). Specifically, Shift-MoE leverages channel-grouped one-step shift operations to capture grid dependencies without global attention, and employs a gated multilayer perceptron mixture-of-experts module to adapt to heterogeneous CSI statistics across users. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed Shift-MoE consistently outperforms representative learning-based CSI feedback baselines in normalized mean squared error and remains effective under different system parameter settings.

</details>


### [813] [Amplitude-Independent Robust Snapshot 6-D Radio SLAM via a Uniffed Angle-Delay Formulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04847)
*Shengqiang Shen,Aoyun Hao,Weihao Geng,Lei Yang,Shiyin Li,Henk Wymeersch*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes an amplitude-independent robust radio SLAM method for bistatic snapshot scenarios using angle-delay formulation, with coarse-to-fine estimation framework and LoS detection via IRLS and QAIC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robust snapshot SLAM methods rely on path-amplitude or path-loss information for LoS/NLoS handling, making them sensitive to calibration errors and propagation-model mismatch. This paper aims to eliminate this dependency.

Method: A unified angle-delay formulation for LoS and single-bounce NLoS; coarse stage estimates UE state and selects geometric inliers without amplitude-based preclassification; twist-swing two-stage traversal for 3D/6D pose estimation; IRLS refinement with QAIC for LoS detection and joint refinement.

Result: The method remains competitive with calibrated amplitude-dependent baselines and is more robust to path-loss-model mismatch in simulations.

Conclusion: Amplitude-independent radio SLAM is feasible and more robust under model mismatch, with potential for practical deployment where calibration is imperfect.

Abstract: This paper addresses bistatic snapshot radio SLAM, in which a user equipment (UE) with unknown 6-D pose and clock bias is localized and environmental landmarks are reconstructed from a single multipath channel snapshot. Under mixed line-of-sight (LoS)/non-line-of-sight (NLoS) propagation, existing robust snapshot SLAM methods are mainly developed or validated in planar/2-D settings and often use path-amplitude or path-loss information for LoS handling, which makes them sensitive to calibration errors and propagation-model mismatch. We propose an amplitude-independent robust radio SLAM method built on a uniffed angle-delay formulation for LoS and single-bounce NLoS inlier paths. In the coarse stage, the method estimates the UE state and selects geometrically consistent inliers directly from angle-delay measurements, without amplitudebased LoS preclassiffcation or path-wise latent variables; the formulation is further extended to general 3-D/6-D pose estimation through twist-swing two-stage traversal initialization and local reffnement on SO(3). A subsequent Jacobian-row-equilibrated iteratively reweighted least-squares (IRLS) reffnement, combined with quasi-Akaike information criterion (QAIC) model comparison, detects the LoS path and jointly reffnes the UE state and scattering points. We also analyze formulation-speciffc local-rank properties and their minimal-set implications under unknown path identity. Simulations show that the proposed method remains competitive with calibrated amplitude-dependent baselines and is more robust to path-loss-model mismatch.

</details>


### [814] [Antenna Optimization for Decode-and-Forward Relay in Magnetic Induction Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04888)
*Honglei Ma,Erwu Liu,Rui Wang,Xuefeng Yin,Zhibing Xu,Xinyu Qu,Bofeng Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper optimizes relay antenna position and orientation in underground tunnel MI communication systems using geometric modeling and a random-search algorithm to improve data rate for emergency rescue vehicles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MI communication is effective in underground tunnels for emergency rescue vehicles but suffers from highly position-and-orientation-selective channel gain, making antenna deployment complex.

Method: Formulates an optimization problem for relay MI antenna PO with tunnel constraints, uses geometric modeling to eliminate constraints, and develops a fast-converging random-search algorithm with global search capability.

Result: The proposed algorithm quickly converges to an optimum that significantly improves data rate for vehicle MI systems with weak signals.

Conclusion: Geometric modeling combined with random-search optimization effectively addresses tunnel constraints and enhances data rate performance in MI relay systems.

Abstract: Magnetic Induction (MI) communication is effective in underground tunnels for emergency rescue vehicle due to the small-size antenna. It can highly benefit from a cooperative decode-and-forward (DF) relay to achieve a higher data rate. However, its channel gain is extremely position-and-orientation-selective. The unreachable space increases the complexity of the antenna deployment. To find the best antenna position and orientation (PO) of the relay achieving the higher data rate, this paper formulates the optimization problem of the relay MI antenna PO with tunnel constraints. To solve the problem more quickly, we propose to use geometric modeling to eliminate the tunnel constraints and develop a random-search algorithm achieving a fast convergence and excellent global search ability. Simulations show that the proposed algorithm can quickly converge to one optimum which signifies a noticeable improvement of data rate for vehicle MI systems with weak signals.

</details>


### [815] [Energy Efficiency Maximization for Hybrid RIS-Aided Communications via Deep Unfolding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04889)
*Pouya Mobaraki,Abolfazl Zakeri,Marco Di Renzo,Markku Juntti,Nhan Thanh Nguyen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hybrid RIS-assisted MU-MISO downlink system where RIS elements can switch between active and passive modes. An alternating optimization framework is developed, using zero-forcing for BS beamforming and model-driven deep unfolding for the RIS subproblem. Results show ~30% higher EE than without deep unfolding, 10% better than fully active RIS, and up to 3× gains over fully passive RIS, with most gains achieved by activating only a small fraction of elements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maximize energy efficiency in MU-MISO downlink systems by leveraging a hybrid RIS that dynamically configures elements between active and passive modes, while incorporating practical hardware constraints such as power budgets, amplifier gain limits, and amplification noise.

Method: Alternating optimization framework: (1) BS beamforming via zero-forcing with closed-form power allocation; (2) RIS subproblem solved using a model-driven deep unfolding approach. The hybrid RIS allows each element to switch between active (amplifier engaged) and passive modes.

Result: Proposed method achieves ~30% higher EE than without deep unfolding, at least 10% over fully active RIS, and up to 3× over fully passive RIS. Most EE gains captured by activating only a small fraction of RIS elements with a small portion of dynamic power budget allocated to the RIS.

Conclusion: The hybrid RIS with dynamic mode switching, optimized via deep unfolding, significantly improves energy efficiency. Practical hardware effects are manageable, and near-optimal EE can be achieved with minimal active element activation.

Abstract: We address energy-efficiency (EE) maximization in a multiuser (MU) multiple-input single-output (MISO) downlink system assisted by a hybrid reconfigurable intelligent surface(RIS), where each element can be dynamically configured to operate in either active or passive mode depending on whether its power amplifier is engaged. Practical hardware effects are explicitly incorporated, including base station (BS) and RIS power budgets, active-element amplifier gain limits, amplification noise, and binary phase control. To solve the problem, we develop an alternating-optimization framework in which the BS beamforming subproblem is handled via zero-forcing with closed-form power allocation, while the RIS subproblem is addressed using a model-driven deep unfolding approach. Numerical results show that the proposed method achieves faster convergence and higher EE than the considered benchmark schemes. In particular, it attains about 30% higher EE than the procedure without deep unfolding. Furthermore, our simulations demonstrate at least 10% EE improvement over the fully active RIS configuration and up to threefold EE gains compared with the fully passive RIS design. The results also show that most of the achievable EE gain can be captured by activating only a small fraction of RIS elements and allocating only a small portion of the dynamic power budget to the RIS.

</details>


### [816] [A Body-of-Revolution Human Model for RF Sensing with Measurement-Driven Calibration for Indoor Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05053)
*Haoqing Wen,Michele D'Amico,Matteo Oldoni,Federica Fieramosca,Vittorio Rampa,Stefano Savazzi,Qi Wu,Gian Guido Gentili*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a fast 2.5-D Finite Element Method using a Body of Revolution (BoR) human model for RF sensing prediction in indoor Device-Free Localization.
